In March 2025 Apple announced the personal-context features would take 'longer than we thought,' pulled the Bella Ramsey ads that had promoted them, and walked the timeline back. iPhone 16 sales softened in the markets where Apple Intelligence was the differentiator, since the marketed feature had not actually shipped. First introduced: Chapter 8.
  114. application programming interfaces An application programming interface, often shortened to API, is the wire-level contract one piece of software offers another. When Shopify exposes its order data and customer profiles through a REST API, it means Klaviyo can subscribe to that data feed and automatically pull every new order into its email-segmentation engine without a human ever exporting a spreadsheet. APIs are the reason the modern stack works as a system rather than as a pile of disconnected silos. First introduced: Chapter 13.
  115. April 23, 2026 postmortem The April 23, 2026 postmortem is the detailed public engineering write-up Anthropic published walking through three concurrent Claude API bugs that ran from January through March 2026 across Sonnet 4.5, Opus 4.5, and Haiku 4.5. It is the most extensive transparency artifact any frontier-AI vendor has shipped on a multi-bug production incident, because it names the bugs (a context-window routing error, a TPU output corruption, a top-k miscompilation), reports how long each one ran before detection, and names the four permanent platform fixes the company put in place afterward. For a capstone team, the postmortem reads as a one-tenth-scale instruction manual for the team's own eval harness. First introduced: Chapter 27.
  116. architectural Radical innovation is the quadrant where the firm pairs a new technology with a new business model at the same time, which means the firm is asking buyers to accept both an unfamiliar capability and an unfamiliar way of paying for it. The 2007 iPhone launched a multi-touch operating system on a carrier-subsidized hardware contract that combined a phone, a music player, and an internet device. Tesla's 2008 Roadster ran a high-performance lithium-ion battery on a direct-to-consumer sales model that skipped franchised dealers. The original iPad introduced tablet computing on an app-store ecosystem. Radical innovation is the rarest of the four quadrants and the one most likely to reshape an industry when it works. First introduced: Chapter 8.
  117. architecture Lawrence Lessig's four-modalities framework, from Code 2.0 (2006), names the four levers that shape behavior in any social system: law (what the rules say), norms (what the community expects), market (what prices reward or punish), and architecture, which is the physical or software design of the environment itself (in the digital case, the code). Lessig's load-bearing claim is that architecture is the binding constraint at the moment of action because it determines what is even possible to do, while law, norms, and market only operate through it; for a marketer, this means the consent flow's code design constrains the buyer's choices more directly than the privacy disclosure text the lawyer drafted. First introduced: Chapter 26.
  118. ARR Annual recurring revenue (ARR) is the dollar value of a subscription business's contracts run-rated to a full year. A $10,000-per-month software contract has $120,000 of ARR. The relationship to monthly recurring revenue (MRR) is straightforward: ARR equals MRR multiplied by 12, and the two metrics are used interchangeably with the time horizon chosen to match the reporting context. First introduced: Chapter 23.
  119. Article 22 GDPR Article 22 gives every EU data subject the right not to be subject to a decision based solely on automated processing when that decision produces legal effects or similarly significant effects. Translated out of the statute, this means that if an AI system inside the marketing stack decides on its own to deny a person a credit-line offer, reject a job applicant at the screening step, or quote a different price by inferred demographic, the affected person can demand a human be brought into the loop, can express their point of view about the decision, and can contest the outcome. The three rights survive even in the narrow Article 22 exceptions where automated decisions are permitted (consent, contract necessity, authorized law), which is why the seven-step parallel-track architecture §26.5 engineers is the architecture the regulation effectively requires. First introduced: Chapter 26.
  120. Article 5 Article 5 of the EU AI Act lists eight categories of AI practice that are prohibited outright across the EU, regardless of consent. The categories most relevant to marketing teams are subliminal manipulation (techniques operating below the user's conscious awareness), exploitation of vulnerability based on age, disability, or socio-economic situation, social scoring of natural persons by public or private actors, untargeted scraping of facial images from the web to build recognition databases, and workplace or education emotion-recognition systems. Article 99 fines a violation at up to 7 percent of the firm's global annual turnover or €35 million, whichever is higher, which is the largest revenue-percentage fine ceiling in any commercial regulation in force worldwide. First introduced: Chapter 26.
  121. Article 50 Article 50 of the EU AI Act places transparency obligations on the providers and deployers of generative AI, effective 2 August 2026. Providers must mark synthetic image, audio, video, and text outputs in machine-readable formats. Deployers, meaning the brands running campaigns, must disclose any AI image, audio, or video that constitutes a deepfake, and must disclose AI-generated text on matters of public interest unless the text passed human editorial review with named editorial responsibility. First introduced: Chapter 3.
  122. Article 99 Article 99 of the EU AI Act sets the maximum penalties for non-compliance, scaled by the severity of the violation. The harshest tier reaches 35 million euros or 7 percent of worldwide turnover (whichever is higher) for prohibited practices under Article 5. The middle tier reaches 15 million euros or 3 percent of worldwide turnover for transparency and other supplier-obligation breaches. The lowest tier reaches 7.5 million euros or 1 percent of worldwide turnover for providing incorrect or misleading information to regulators. First introduced: Chapter 3.
  123. Artificial Analysis Intelligence Index v4.0 The Artificial Analysis Intelligence Index is a cross-vendor composite leaderboard published at artificialanalysis.ai. The v4.0 release combines ten benchmark results into one composite score, so the marketer can read one number that summarizes a model's overall capability rather than comparing ten benchmarks pairwise. The included benchmarks are GDPval-AA (a general task benchmark), tau-squared-Bench Telecom (multi-turn agent), Terminal-Bench Hard (terminal-task agent), SciCode (scientific code), AA-LCR (long-context reasoning), AA-Omniscience (knowledge breadth), IFBench (instruction following), Humanity's Last Exam (frontier knowledge), GPQA Diamond (graduate-physics QA), and CritPt (critical thinking). The marketer reads the index the same way an investor reads the S&P 500: one composite that summarizes a wide field. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  124. arXiv:2505.05190 The arXiv:2505.05190 preprint, published in May 2025, is the empirical paper that measured how badly paraphrase degrades a sampling-time watermark on AI-generated text. The headline finding is that detection accuracy drops below 50 percent on short paraphrased passages, which is no better than a coin flip. The 'short' qualifier in the result matters: long-form text retains more of the statistical signature the watermark deposited at generation time than a tweet-length passage does, so the most common case (a paraphrased social-media post) is the case the watermark struggles most to detect. This is the empirical finding that justifies why the three-layer provenance stack at §26.2 needs all three layers rather than relying on layer-one watermarking alone. First introduced: Chapter 26.
  125. aSeconds An aSecond, short for active-attention second, is the unit Amplified Intelligence proposes for media planning in place of the older viewability standard. One aSecond is one second during which the panel-tracked viewer's eyes are on the advertisement and her face is squared to the screen, measured by webcam-based eye-tracking and facial detection. A typical thirty-second video on linear television earns roughly 9.7 aSeconds because the viewer is on a sofa with the show in the foreground, while the same spot on a mobile social feed earns only about 1.2 aSeconds because the thumb is already scrolling. First introduced: Chapter 16.
  126. ASO keyword audit Skill A Skill is a folder of instructions, scripts, and reference docs that the model loads on demand when the task at hand matches the Skill's description. The Skill standard was open-sourced by Anthropic on December 18, 2025. The basic pattern is a SKILL.md file with YAML frontmatter (name and description) plus the body that tells the model what to do step by step. The marketer drops the folder at ~/.claude/skills/skill-name/, and Claude Code or Claude.ai loads it automatically whenever a user request matches the Skill's description. First introduced: Chapter 14.
  127. Aspire Aspire is a creator-marketing software platform that lets a brand search a vetted database of creators by follower count, audience demographics, engagement rate, and benchmark price range for each creator, then send the brief, negotiate the fee, and pay the creator all in one place. A brand-side analyst at Stillwater Coffee, for example, could open Aspire on Monday morning, filter to OK State micro-creators in the food vertical, and pull a shortlist of fifteen candidates with verified pricing by lunch. The free tier covers basic search, and the paid tier, which starts around 600 dollars a month in 2026, adds audience-quality analytics and contract templates. First introduced: Chapter 20.
  128. asset-light brand strategy An asset-light brand strategy is a firm design that owns the brand, the product design, and the marketing budget, while contracting out manufacturing, logistics, and sometimes retail to third parties. The 1990s form was Nike, Reebok, and Levi's, who continued to design and market products while their factories operated offshore under contractor arrangements. The 2026 form runs through Shein, Temu, and most direct-to-consumer apparel brands, which operate on the same principle at faster cycle times and larger scale. First introduced: Chapter 7.
  129. Asset specificity Asset specificity is the degree to which the assets supporting a transaction are specialized to one particular channel relationship, so that those assets lose much of their value if the relationship ends and they have to be redeployed somewhere else. The channel-relevant forms are brand-specific knowledge held by sales staff, customer-specific data held by the channel partner, dedicated capacity tooled to one brand, and relationship capital built between two firms over years. First introduced: Chapter 11.
  130. Association of National Advertisers (ANA) The Association of National Advertisers (ANA) is the trade body for large US advertisers, representing the companies that spend the most on advertising. Its 2023 Programmatic Media Supply Chain Transparency Study analyzed 35 billion impressions across its member companies' campaigns to estimate how much of every advertising dollar actually reaches working media (impressions shown to real humans who could plausibly notice them) versus is absorbed by intermediary fees and inventory placed on low-quality sites. First introduced: Chapter 19.
  131. associative network The associative network model treats human memory as a graph of nodes connected by links of varying strength. The brand is a node, and the points of difference, points of parity, sensory cues, use occasions, and category entry points each become linked nodes around it. When a buyer encounters a retrieval cue (the category, an occasion, a competitor's name), activation spreads from that cue through the strongest links first, which is why the brand whose links to the cue are strongest and most numerous gets retrieved first in the moment of purchase. First introduced: Chapter 6.
  132. assortment Assortment provision is the work a retailer does when it aggregates products from many different manufacturers onto one set of shelves so the shopper makes one trip and one transaction instead of driving to a separate store for every supplier. Two valid versions of that work exist side by side, and neither is better than the other: narrow-and-deep retailers like Sephora and the Apple Store carry one product line in great depth, while broad-and-shallow retailers like Costco carry a little of nearly everything a household uses in a week. First introduced: Chapter 12.
  133. assurance Assurance is the SERVQUAL dimension that captures how much the staff’s knowledge and courtesy convey trust and confidence to the buyer, and it is the third-most-weighted of the five dimensions. The everyday way to see this is the difference between a doctor who answers a patient’s question with technical fluency and steady eye contact, and a doctor who hedges, looks at the floor, and refers the question down the hall to someone else. Buyers form their assurance impression from four specific cues: the staff’s grasp of the technical content of the service, their willingness to make eye contact and stay engaged during the question, their professional bearing in posture and dress, and their willingness to take ownership of a problem rather than push it onto a colleague. Assurance is therefore expensive to manufacture because it requires hiring for competence and training for composure, but it is what the buyer reads when the cost of being wrong is high. First introduced: Chapter 9.
  134. asymptotic notation Asymptotic notation is the standard mathematical shorthand for describing how an algorithm's cost grows as the input size grows. The most common form is Big-O notation: O(n) means the cost grows linearly with input size, so ten times the input takes ten times as long; O(n log n) is slightly worse than linear; and O(n^2) is quadratic, meaning ten times the input takes one hundred times as long. Donald Knuth's Art of Computer Programming popularized the notation inside computer science, and every modern engineering team uses the same language to argue about whether an algorithm will scale to production traffic or break under load. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  135. Atlas Atlas is OpenAI's Chromium-based browser, launched on macOS on October 21, 2025 for the Free, Plus, Pro, and Go subscription tiers. The ChatGPT sidebar reads whatever page the user has currently open, and Atlas's Agent Mode (gated to the paid Plus, Pro, and Business subscribers) executes multi-step tasks on the user's behalf, including booking, product comparison, and form filling, without the user having to click through every step manually. First introduced: Chapter 18.
  136. Atlas Reasoning Engine The Atlas Reasoning Engine is the orchestrator layer inside Salesforce Agentforce 360, and it is the part that receives the marketer's task, decomposes it into a sequence of tool calls and sub-tasks, dispatches each piece to a specialist agent or to a language-model call, evaluates the intermediate output against the operator's guardrails, and merges the final result. The Atlas layer is the planning-plus-reflection layer of the architecture rather than the language model itself, which is why a Salesforce-anchored capstone team can swap the underlying model (Claude Opus, Claude Sonnet, or another vendor) without rewriting the orchestration logic. First introduced: Chapter 27.
  137. atomic model The atomic model is de Chernatony's eight-component operational brand specification. The brand team audits one line per component to produce a working spec the firm can budget against. The eight are: functional capabilities (what the product does), distinctive name (the verbal asset), legal protection (registered trademarks), shorthand symbol (the visual identity), risk reducer (how the brand reduces buyer uncertainty), identity system (the planning artifact), image in buyers' minds (what buyers actually believe), and value system (the firm's stated values). Gaps and contradictions across the eight components are the brand-management priority list for the next quarter. First introduced: Chapter 7.
  138. ATT App Tracking Transparency (ATT) is the iOS 14.5 framework Apple shipped in April 2021 requiring apps to ask the user explicit permission before tracking them across other companies' apps and websites. Cross-app opt-in rates in the United States settled at roughly 25 to 33 percent after the prompt rolled out (per Adjust and AppsFlyer 2025 attribution reports), and Meta attributed roughly 10 billion dollars of 2022 revenue impact to the change in its 2023 SEC filings. The global mobile-ad ecosystem reorganized around the assumption that the majority of iOS users are no longer trackable across apps. First introduced: Chapter 14.
  139. attention arousal Attention arousal is the effect, documented by Marco Bertini and Luc Wathieu in a 2008 Marketing Science paper, where tagging a surcharge to a specific attribute (a $52 resort fee labeled as covering amenities, a $9 fee labeled as covering shipping) raises that attribute's cognitive salience in the buyer's mind. Because the surcharge forces the buyer to pay attention to that attribute, the buyer ends up weighing it more heavily in the overall purchase decision. Partitioning lifts demand when the highlighted attribute is something the buyer values, and depresses demand when the highlighted attribute reads as padding the firm should have absorbed. Three moderators decide which direction the effect runs: the perceived value of the attribute, its relative importance to the overall purchase, and how easily the buyer can evaluate it on their own. First introduced: Chapter 10.
  140. attention budget The intuition Anthropic introduced in the September 2025 'Effective context engineering for AI agents' post: a model has a finite ability to track the relationships among the tokens in its window, so every token added depletes that finite capacity. Cited as the operational reason context engineering matters more than prompt-tweaking once an agent runs for more than a few turns. First introduced: Chapter 27.
  141. attention mechanism The attention mechanism is the part of a transformer model that decides, for each new token the model is about to produce, which earlier tokens in the input it should pay attention to. The computation runs every input token against every other input token, so the cost grows with the square of the input length. Plain version: doubling the length of the prompt (say, going from a 2,000-word brief to a 4,000-word brief) quadruples the work the model has to do, and tripling it (a 6,000-word brief) makes the work nine times harder. This quadratic-cost property is the structural reason context windows are bounded at one million tokens or so even on the largest 2026 models, and the same property is why the silent-truncation failure that opened this chapter (the 67-percent shipping-complaint number that came from rows 1 through 3,800 of an 8,000-row file) exists at all. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  142. attribute confounding When two attributes share information so closely that the choice model cannot separate their effects. In a T-shirt study: if the price levels were 20-orange / 25-charcoal / 30-cream and never crossed, the model could not tell whether brothers prefer cream or 30-dollar shirts. Sawtooth's design generator flags this automatically; the prohibition section in the Skill is what catches it before fielding. First introduced: Chapter 5.
  143. attributes A characteristic of the product the buyer cares about and the study wants to measure preference for. In the T-shirt study the attributes are graphic, color, and price; in a roofing study they might be life expectancy, installation, and aesthetics. Pick three to five; fewer than three and the model has nothing to weigh, more than five and respondent fatigue degrades the choice quality past the point of usefulness. First introduced: Chapter 5.
  144. attribution graph An attribution graph is a research visualization that traces, for one specific input the model receives, which features fired, in which order, and which features caused which other features to fire on the way to the final output. The graph nodes are individual features (the same kind the sparse autoencoder pulled out); the edges are the model-internal pathways between them. Anthropic's March 2025 paper built attribution graphs on top of a 30-million-feature transcoder and used them to identify named circuits inside Claude 3.5 Haiku, including the Dallas-to-Texas-to-Austin two-hop reasoning chain and a backward-planning circuit the model uses when writing poetry. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  145. attribution-maturity ladder The attribution-maturity ladder, from Dave Chaffey's Digital Marketing in its eighth edition, is a four-stage progression that names how sophisticated a marketing team's measurement program has become. Stage one is last-click attribution alone, stage two adds multi-touch attribution (whose validity fell apart after Apple's App Tracking Transparency framework shipped in April 2021), stage three adds marketing-mix modeling for cross-channel budget questions, and stage four triangulates incrementality experiments, marketing-mix modeling, and multi-touch attribution against each other. A team's actual posture is the most-sophisticated stage at which it operates on its largest budget decision, which is usually less sophisticated than the team would claim on a vendor pitch. First introduced: Chapter 18.
  146. attribution problem The attribution problem is the class of analytics problem where revenue from a specific marketing motion, like a piece of content or a campaign or a channel, is hard to assign to its actual cause because the buyer saw many different touches over many months before they purchased, and the data warehouse cannot reliably stitch those touches together into one coherent journey. Picture a B2B buyer who read three blog posts, attended one webinar, downloaded one benchmark report, talked to a salesperson twice, and saw four LinkedIn ads over fourteen months before signing the contract: which of those touches gets credit, and in what proportion, is the question the attribution problem is trying to answer. The 2026 production answer is multi-touch attribution at the warehouse layer, which distributes partial credit across the touches and is its own substantial modeling project. First introduced: Chapter 17.
  147. AU score Adelaide's 0-to-100 machine-learning composite score for a placement's likelihood of capturing attention and driving downstream outcomes. The score integrates eye-tracking data, exposure context, and historical brand-lift and sales-lift outcomes across the inventory base into a single number a media buyer can sort the inventory on. The cut-off used in 2025 briefs is typically a minimum AU of 50 for upper-funnel awareness work and 60 for lower-funnel conversion work. First introduced: Chapter 16.
  148. audience overlap Audience overlap is the share of an influencer's followers who match the brand's target audience on the dimensions that drive purchase: category interest, demographic, geography, and intent. A 1.2 million-follower beauty creator may have only 15 percent overlap with a tech brand's target, which is why the followers number alone misleads on conversion. A 2024 Journal of the Academy of Marketing Science meta-analysis confirmed that audience overlap is a stronger predictor of conversion than raw follower count, because the brand is paying to reach the matched cohort, not the unmatched majority. First introduced: Chapter 19.
  149. AuditBench AuditBench is an Anthropic Alignment Science benchmark released March 10, 2026 (arXiv:2602.22755) that measures how well an investigator agent can audit a target language model for hidden behaviors. The benchmark contains 56 language models, each fine-tuned to exhibit one of 14 hidden behaviors (sycophantic deference, opposition to AI regulation, secret geopolitical loyalties, and similar traits) and trained not to confess when asked directly. The investigator agent autonomously audits the target across 13 tool configurations. The headline finding is the tool-to-agent gap: techniques that look accurate when scored on their own outputs often fail to improve the investigator agent's recovery rate in practice, which means a vendor's slogan that the lab 'does alignment auditing' is not a substitute for the actual configured-and-dated score. First introduced: Chapter 26.
  150. augmented analyst The augmented analyst is the 2026 operating model where the senior analyst’s tacit recipe (the spreadsheet they refresh each Monday, the decile rule they apply by hand) gets written down once as a Skill. The AI agent then runs that recipe against the warehouse on demand, and the senior analyst’s time shifts from refreshing the dashboard each week to validating the anomalies the agent flags. Andrej Karpathy labels this design point Software 3.0 partial autonomy: the human stays in the loop on the decisions that matter while the routine pulls happen unattended. First introduced: Chapter 24.
  151. augmented product Theodore Levitt's 1983 four-layer product model says any offering can be decomposed into four nested layers. The generic product is the bare minimum to count as a category member, like a hotel room with a bed in it. The expected product adds what buyers reasonably assume will be there, like clean sheets and hot water. The augmented product is everything the seller adds beyond what the buyer expects, like a warranty, repair service, training, or integration support. The potential product is what could yet be added in the future. Differentiation lives at the augmented and potential layers, while commodity status is what happens to a firm that competes only at the generic layer. First introduced: Chapter 2.
  152. augments Augmentation in Brynjolfsson's 2022 Turing Trap framework is an AI capability that complements a human worker rather than replacing them: the worker plus the tool together produce more value than the worker alone. Empirically requires roughly $9 of complementary intangible-capital investment per $1 of model spend, drawn from the IT-investment cycle of 1995-2010. The intangible-capital line covers training the worker on the new tool, redesigning the workflow around the augmented worker, restructuring the team, and the productivity-J-curve property that augmentation registers zero or negative measured productivity for the first 5 years before gains arrive. The procurement committee tends to under-budget augmentation because the J-curve looks like failure during quarters one through twenty. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  153. Authentically Disney, Distinctly Chinese The Walt Disney Company's explicit design brief for Shanghai Disney Resort from 2009 onward, naming the per-element decomposition rule: keep the Disney IP standardized, adapt every other element of the offer (architecture, food, beverage, character integration, ride content, language, and seasonal programming) to Chinese culture and family-group behavior. First introduced: Chapter 15.
  154. authenticity check An authenticity check on a paid panel is a panel-side detection system that examines fifteen or more behavioral signatures during the survey session (copy-paste events, tab-switching, focus loss, typing cadence, mouse trajectory, scroll patterns) and flags responses that look like they were generated by a script rather than typed by a human. Prolific's May 2025 version of this system flags AI-completed open-end responses with 98.7 percent precision and a 0.6 percent false-positive rate; the February 2026 extension adds detection of the execution context the script is running in. First introduced: Chapter 5.
  155. Automated Alignment Researcher (AAR) An Automated Alignment Researcher (AAR) is an Anthropic-built, Claude-powered system released in 2026 that takes an alignment problem with a single objective measure of success, spawns parallel instances of itself in independent sandboxes, and has each instance propose research ideas, run experiments, analyze the results, and share findings. On the weak-to-strong supervision benchmark, the AAR reached a Performance Gap Recovered score of 0.97 in five days, using roughly eighteen thousand dollars of compute across nine parallel AAR instances. Two human researchers running the same benchmark reached only 0.23 in seven days. Anthropic limits the published claim to outcome-gradable problems with clear success metrics, because the system reward-hacks in ways the team did not anticipate on problems without clear metrics, which is why the evaluation design matters as much as the system itself. First introduced: Chapter 26.
  156. Automatic vending Automatic vending is the retail format that uses coin or card-operated dispensers to carry impulse and convenience goods at points where a clerked store cannot reach the buyer, such as an office hallway, a train station platform, or a hospital waiting room. Japan runs the highest per-capita coverage in the world with more than five million units in operation, which gives a sense of how much retail demand the format can absorb when the population density and the cultural acceptance of unsupervised vending machines line up. First introduced: Chapter 12.
  157. autonomous task horizon The autonomous task horizon is the length of a human-expert task at which a frontier AI model can complete the task end-to-end at 50 percent reliability without a human looking over its shoulder. The number for Claude Opus 4.5 in January 2026 was roughly 320 minutes, which is about five hours and twenty minutes. The METR research lab measures this with a fixed task suite and reports that the horizon has been doubling roughly every 4.3 months since 2023, with the most recent measurement published in METR's Time Horizon 1.1 paper. First introduced: Chapter 27.
  158. autopilot Phil Barden's brand-briefing rename for Kahneman's System 1: the fast, automatic, pattern-matching mode of thought that handles roughly eighty percent of consumer purchase decisions. The autopilot reads packaging, color, character, and tone in the peripheral edge of vision, in roughly the time it takes a shopper to glance at a shelf, and never asks the buyer to justify the choice in words afterward. First introduced: Chapter 16.
  159. availability heuristic The availability heuristic is the mental shortcut buyers use when they estimate how often something happens by reaching for how easily examples come to mind. Vivid, recent, or emotionally charged instances feel more frequent than they actually are, which is why a single viral plane-crash story can make air travel feel riskier than driving even though the base rates run the other way. A 1973 paper in Cognitive Psychology by Amos Tversky and Daniel Kahneman named the mechanism, and it is the memory-limitation lever most often used in brand work. First introduced: Chapter 4.
  160. average order value Average Order Value, often shortened to AOV, is total revenue in a period divided by the number of orders placed in that same period. The metric sits between traffic (how many people walked in or clicked through) and revenue (how much money the store ultimately collected). A retailer can grow revenue either by getting more shoppers through the door or by getting each shopper to buy more per visit, and average order value is the dial that measures the second lever, which is why loyalty programs that bundle a free shipping threshold or a tier-unlock perk are designed to lift AOV rather than visit count. First introduced: Chapter 12.
  161. Average Order Value (AOV) Average Order Value (AOV) is the dollar amount of an average transaction at the chain, calculated as total revenue divided by the number of orders over a given period. A thirty-five-percent lift in AOV among assistant users means each transaction the assistant participated in carried roughly $1.35 of revenue for every $1.00 a comparable non-assistant transaction would have generated, but the comparison does not yet say whether the assistant caused the lift or simply attracted the shoppers who would have spent more anyway. First introduced: Chapter 12.
  162. awareness Awareness, in Eugene Schwartz's 1966 sense, is the buyer's current knowledge state at the moment the advertisement reaches her. Schwartz built a five-rung scale running from Most Aware (knows the brand and the price, needs only an offer) through Product Aware, Solution Aware, Problem Aware, down to Unaware (does not know there is a problem, or will not admit it). The rule that matters in practice is that a headline pulling at one rung will fail at any other, so a Costco-checkout offer headline aimed at a Most Aware buyer reads as invisible noise to an Unaware buyer scrolling TikTok on the same Tuesday. First introduced: Chapter 16.
  163. awareness, appeal, ask, act, advocate The 5-A funnel comes from Kotler, Keller, and Chernev's Marketing Management textbook and names five sequential stages a buyer travels from never having heard of a brand to recommending it. Awareness is when the buyer first learns the brand exists. Appeal is when the brand enters the buyer's mental shortlist of plausible options. Ask is when the buyer investigates the shortlisted brands in detail. Act is when the buyer purchases. Advocate is when the buyer recommends the brand to a friend, which feeds next quarter's awareness stage and closes the loop. First introduced: Chapter 18.
  164. awesome-* The awesome-* convention is a community pattern on GitHub where contributors curate a public list of high-quality resources on one topic, with the repository name prefixed by the word 'awesome'. As of 2026, there are thousands of such lists, including awesome-prompts (a curated list of high-quality prompt-engineering examples), awesome-llm (open-source language models the team can self-host), awesome-public-datasets (free datasets across every category), and awesome-marketing-tools. When a marketer is trying to figure out what tools exist for a category before building from scratch, the relevant awesome-* list is the first place to look. A curated list maintained by people who actually use the tools is almost always better than searching Google, because Google returns whatever blog ranked highest for the search query rather than what experienced practitioners actually trust. First introduced: Chapter 1.
02 // b

B B

  1. B2B B2B stands for business-to-business, which is the catch-all label for firms whose customers are themselves other firms rather than individual consumers. A software vendor that sells project-management tools to mid-market law firms is running a B2B operation, and so is a packaging supplier that sells corrugated boxes to Amazon. The selling cycle in B2B usually involves a buying committee of three to seven people rather than a single shopper, the sales cycle runs months instead of minutes, and the relationships are far longer than they are on the consumer side. That is why the standard segmentation base in B2B is firmographics on the buying organization, such as industry, employee count, and revenue, rather than demographics on the individual person who happens to sign the contract. First introduced: Chapter 6.
  2. B2B (business-to-business) B2B is short for business-to-business, which describes any marketing situation where the buyer is another company rather than an individual consumer. In a B2B deal, the sales cycle typically runs six to eighteen months because multiple stakeholders at the buying company have to sign off, and the content the brand publishes for that audience usually takes the form of benchmark research, framework templates, and gated reports the buyer trades an email address for. The opposite is B2C, which stands for business-to-consumer, where the buyer is an individual person and the decision cycle can collapse to minutes. First introduced: Chapter 17.
  3. backlinks A backlink is a link to a page on the brand's site placed on someone else's site. Backlinks are PageRank's foundational signal: each backlink passes a fraction of the linking domain's authority to the page it points at, so a link from a trusted publisher is worth far more than a link from an unknown blog. Post-2024 Google explicitly emphasizes links earned through original content and demotes the link-buying and guest-posting schemes that defined the 2010s. First introduced: Chapter 18.
  4. backpropagation Backpropagation is the training algorithm that lets a neural network learn from its own mistakes. After the network produces an answer, the trainer compares that answer to the correct one and computes the size of the error. Backprop then walks backward through every layer of the network, adjusting each internal weight a tiny amount in the direction that would have reduced the error. Repeat across millions of training examples and the network gradually learns what to compute. Rumelhart, Hinton, and Williams introduced the algorithm in a 1986 Nature paper and every modern training pipeline, including the one that produces Claude, still runs the same procedure. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  5. balance principle The balance principle is the empirical rule that STEPPS works as a checklist with a floor on each lever rather than as a sum of points across all six levers. A piece of content that scores 90 on Emotion but zero on Triggers goes viral inside an in-group and then dies, because nothing in the post recalls the brand to a second audience. The empirical floor in Berger and Milkman's NYT corpus was 30 of 100 on every lever, so posts where no individual lever fell below 30 outperformed posts with the same total but a zero row. First introduced: Chapter 16.
  6. balance sheet The balance sheet is the financial statement that lists what the firm owns (assets) and what it owes (liabilities), with the difference between the two reported as shareholder equity. A brand sits on the asset side as an intangible asset when the firm acquires it from another firm, and equity-tracking trade-press numbers estimate what that asset would be worth if it were marked to market today. First introduced: Chapter 7.
  7. BANT BANT is the four-criteria lead-qualification framework IBM developed in the 1960s. A real opportunity has to clear all four: Budget (the buyer can pay), Authority (the buyer can sign), Need (the problem the seller solves is actually present), and Timeline (the buying decision will be made within a defined window). A lead that fails any one of the four is not a qualified opportunity and should not enter the pipeline. First introduced: Chapter 23.
  8. base rates A base rate is the underlying frequency of an outcome in the actual population, before any specific information about the individual case is considered. The United States has roughly three million truck drivers and only about two hundred thousand librarians, which means a random working adult is fifteen times more likely to be a truck driver than a librarian by base rate alone. Buyers regularly ignore the base rate in favor of matching individual cases against prototypes, which is the engine behind the representativeness heuristic. First introduced: Chapter 4.
  9. basis points A basis point is one one-hundredth of a percentage point, so two hundred basis points equals two full percentage points. Bankers and traders speak in basis points to avoid ambiguity between phrases like a two-and-a-half percent change in something (a rate moving from 100 to 102.5) and a two-and-a-half percentage-point change (a rate moving from 5 percent to 7.5 percent), which is a very different shift. First introduced: Chapter 3.
  10. Bass-or-descendant diffusion model The Bass diffusion model, published by Frank Bass in 1969, describes how a new product spreads through a population by treating cumulative adoptions as a function of three numbers: an innovation parameter p (typically between 0.001 and 0.03, capturing the fraction of buyers who adopt because of advertising or external influence), an imitation parameter q (typically between 0.3 and 0.5, capturing the influence of friends who have already adopted), and a market potential M (the total addressable population). The model is the right tool for new-product trajectories like Liquid Death’s 2017 launch or the Stanley Quencher’s 2020-2024 takeoff, where the diffusion shape is the load-bearing planning question. First introduced: Chapter 24.
  11. Batch API The Batch API is the asynchronous-execution mode both Anthropic and OpenAI ship, where the team submits a queue of requests and the vendor returns the completed results within twenty-four hours rather than instantly. Both vendors discount batched calls by fifty percent on both input and output tokens, which is the right choice for any task that does not need a real-time answer (a Sunday-night classification job over a million product reviews, an overnight bulk rewrite of last quarter's marketing copy, a weekly market-research summary scheduled to land before Monday). Stacked with prompt caching, the Batch API cuts roughly ninety-five percent off the list price. First introduced: Chapter 27.
  12. Bayesian A Bayesian method starts with a prior belief (what the analyst expects before seeing the data), updates that belief using the data the analyst actually has, and returns a posterior distribution that says how plausible each possible answer is rather than a single point estimate. Applied to marketing-mix modeling, the practical payoff is honest uncertainty around an ROI number: instead of reporting ‘search ROI is $4.10 per dollar,’ a Bayesian model reports ‘search ROI is somewhere between $2.80 and $5.40 with 90 percent confidence, most likely around $4.10.’ The wider the range, the less confident the model is, and the more the planner should hedge. First introduced: Chapter 24.
  13. Bayesian regularizer A Bayesian regularizer is a statistical safeguard that pulls a model's estimate toward what is already known about the world whenever the firm's own data is too thin or too noisy to settle the question on its own. The prior the model is pulled toward here comes from a meta-analysis (a pooled summary of many separate studies), which reports that the average advertising elasticity across mature nondurable categories sits at about 0.10. The practical result is that the model still returns a sensible number when the firm has only a few years of marketing history, because the regularizer borrows statistical strength from the broader research literature whenever the firm's own sample cannot carry the estimate alone. First introduced: Chapter 25.
  14. BDRs A business development representative (BDR), sometimes called a sales development representative (SDR), is the junior sales role that screens leads through short discovery calls and decides which leads are worth the senior account executive's (AE) time. The BDR typically takes 100 to 200 leads per month, runs a 15-to-30-minute call on each, and books roughly 8 to 15 qualified meetings onto the AE's calendar after BANT-style screening. First introduced: Chapter 23.
  15. beachhead use case A beachhead use case is a single, specific use case the new product can win decisively before the firm tries to expand outward into adjacent uses. Geoffrey Moore uses a bowling-pin metaphor: the firm knocks down one pin first, and the references and credibility from that win then knock down the next pins in the row. For Documentum, the beachhead was pharmaceutical regulatory affairs, and only after that segment adopted did the firm move into petrochemicals and broader energy. First introduced: Chapter 8.
  16. Beat reporters A beat reporter is a journalist who covers one specific subject area continuously over years rather than moving between unrelated stories week to week. Patrick Coffee, for example, has covered the advertising and marketing beat at the Wall Street Journal long enough that brand communications directors recognize his name on a pitch and respond differently than they would to a general assignment reporter. The 2026 reality is that beat reporters carry personal audiences through their own newsletters and social accounts, often comparable in size to the audience their employer's masthead reaches. First introduced: Chapter 22.
  17. behavioral A segmentation basis that cuts the market by what buyers actually do rather than by who they are or what they say they value. The standard behavioral variables are usage rate (how much the buyer consumes of the category), occasion (which situations trigger the purchase), loyalty status (whether the buyer is committed, switching, or loyal to a competitor), and buyer-readiness stage (whether the buyer is unaware, aware, considering, or about to purchase). Behavioral is the most predictive base because it is measured from observed behavior rather than from stated intent, and stated intent overstates loyalty by a known margin. First introduced: Chapter 6.
  18. Behavioral Change Stairway The Behavioral Change Stairway Model (BCSM) is the FBI Crisis Negotiation Unit's five-stage framework for moving a counterpart from initial contact to behavioral change. The five rungs are Active Listening, Empathy, Rapport, Influence, and Behavioral Change. The B2B lesson the chapter draws from BCSM is that qualification arrives only when the buyer takes an observable action (signed NDA, looped-in procurement, stopped meeting competitors), not when the buyer verbally says yes. First introduced: Chapter 23.
  19. Behavioral economics Behavioral economics is the academic field that studies how real humans actually make decisions, not how the textbook rational-deliberator model says they should. The field was founded by Daniel Kahneman and Amos Tversky in the 1970s when they documented systematic ways that real choices violate the standard economic model, and the work has since been extended by Richard Thaler (defaults and nudges), Dan Ariely (cheating and predictable irrationality), and many others. First introduced: Chapter 4.
  20. behavioral logs Behavioral logs are records of what users actually did, captured automatically by the website, the app, or the transaction system as the user moved through it. Examples include click logs (every button each user clicked), page-view records, purchase histories, and scroll-depth measurements. The crucial distinction is between behavioral logs and surveys: surveys ask users what they did and produce self-reports that often differ from the truth; logs record what users did and produce a record that does not depend on the user's memory. Most companies store their behavioral logs in cloud warehouses like Snowflake or BigQuery and query them with SQL. First introduced: Chapter 5.
  21. behavioral segmentation Behavioral segmentation in Klaviyo is a way of grouping customers that adapts to their actions in real time, rather than placing each customer into a fixed list and leaving them there. A buyer enters a flow the moment she abandons a cart, exits the flow the moment she completes a purchase or unsubscribes, and is re-evaluated against the segment rules every time Klaviyo receives a new event hook from Shopify. This is different from the static list segmentation that the email tools of the 2014 direct-to-consumer wave used, where a customer was assigned to a list once at signup and only moved out of it through a manual reassignment. First introduced: Chapter 13.
  22. behavioral surplus Behavioral surplus is the portion of collected data that goes beyond what the firm actually needs to deliver the service the user signed up for. A grocery app needs your address to ship the order, which is the service data. The same app retaining your scroll velocity, your dwell time on each product page, and the time of day you tend to shop is keeping behavioral surplus, because none of those facts are required to ship the groceries but all of them can be turned into a prediction the firm can sell. First introduced: Chapter 26.
  23. benchmark MMLU (Massive Multitask Language Understanding) is the standard knowledge benchmark for language models. The benchmark contains 57 subject areas spanning elementary math through professional medicine, U.S. history, college physics, business ethics, and so on, with multiple-choice questions in each subject. A model's MMLU score is the percent of questions it answers correctly. Model cards report the score on this benchmark plus a short standard list (HumanEval for code, GPQA for science, others) so two vendors can be compared on a shared axis. The Chinchilla paper's 23.7-percentage-point win was on MMLU specifically. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  24. benefit battery A benefit battery is the survey instrument an analyst uses to actually measure which benefits buyers in the category care about, and it usually contains twenty to thirty rating items that each describe one possible benefit (decay prevention, breath freshness, low price, natural ingredients, and so on). Each respondent rates how important each benefit is to them, and the resulting matrix of ratings is then run through a clustering algorithm or a factor analysis to recover the underlying segment structure from the bottom up. The benefit battery is the measurement step that turns benefit segmentation from a conceptual idea into a defensible cut a brand team can plan against, because without the survey there is no way to verify which benefit groups actually exist in the buyer population rather than only in the analyst's hypothesis. First introduced: Chapter 6.
  25. benefit focus Benefit focus is the consumer's evaluative orientation when they shop a category, which Chris Hydock and Luc Wathieu introduced in a 2023 Journal of Consumer Research paper. Taste-focused buyers prioritize subjective preferences like a specific flavor or a specific brand they like, and these buyers tend to prefer Everyday Low Pricing because they want the same price reliably available every time they shop. Quality-focused buyers prioritize objective shared attributes like more storage, a faster processor, or a sustainable supply chain, and these buyers tend to prefer Hi-Lo because the discount itself reads as a search signal that an above-baseline product is on offer this week. First introduced: Chapter 10.
  26. benefit segmentation Benefit segmentation cuts the market by the benefits buyers are looking for in the category rather than by descriptors like age, income, or volume. The toothpaste example the chapter walks is the textbook case: instead of cutting buyers by age or household size, benefit segmentation separates the decay-prevention buyers (Crest's core target) from the cosmetic-whitening buyers, from the flavor-driven buyers, and from the price-driven buyers, and the four groups each respond to a different marketing program. The reason benefit segmentation often outperforms demographic segmentation is that the benefit the buyer is seeking is the causal driver of the choice, while the demographic descriptors are downstream proxies that only partly track which benefit the buyer happens to want today. First introduced: Chapter 6.
  27. Bernoulli distribution The Bernoulli distribution is the simplest probability distribution in statistics: a single trial that has exactly two possible outcomes, conventionally called success and failure, with probability p of success and probability one-minus-p of failure. A coin flip is the canonical example, but every conversion-rate test in marketing is implicitly a Bernoulli problem at heart, because each individual visitor either converts (success) or does not convert (failure), and the question the analyst is asking is what value of p best describes the population the visitor was drawn from. First introduced: Chapter 5.
  28. Bertrand paradox The Bertrand paradox, which the French mathematician Joseph Bertrand introduced in an 1883 critique of Cournot's oligopoly model, is the prediction that two firms selling identical products with the same cost structure will compete each other all the way down to marginal-cost pricing with zero profit, even though there are only two of them in the market. The result is called a paradox because real industries do not actually collapse to zero profit this way. Jean Tirole's 1988 textbook catalogued the four standard mechanisms that explain why real markets escape the paradox: capacity constraints that limit how much each firm can supply, product differentiation that makes the products non-identical, repeated interaction that supports tacit cooperation, and buyer search costs that keep buyers from always finding the lowest price. First introduced: Chapter 10.
  29. best-practice diffusion Best-practice diffusion is the mechanism the Brynjolfsson, Li, and Raymond 2023 paper named to explain why an AI customer-support tool produced a 34 percent productivity gain for the bottom skill quartile and near-zero gain for the top quartile. The AI tool was trained on the firm's historical chat logs, weighted toward successful outcomes (the conversations that ended with the customer satisfied). The trained model encoded the response patterns the top agents already used, and delivered that playbook to every junior agent's keyboard. The senior agents already knew the patterns the tool was teaching, so the tool produced no new productivity gain for them. The junior agents did not know the patterns, so the tool effectively gave them senior-level response capability and the productivity gain was large. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  30. best-practice transfer Best-practice transfer is the mechanism behind much of the measured productivity lift from generative AI tools in customer-support settings. The model has been trained on transcripts from the firm’s highest-performing agents, so when a lower-skilled agent asks the model how to handle a tricky case, it returns the phrasing and resolution path the top performers actually used. The lower-skilled agent then adopts those patterns faster than they would learn them by sitting next to a top performer or asking a supervisor, because the model is available on every ticket rather than once a week. First introduced: Chapter 24.
  31. beta testing Beta testing is external product testing with a selected group of real customers, conducted under controlled conditions before broad commercial release. The goal is to validate the product in genuine use settings (in the customer's home, on the customer's actual phone, against the customer's actual workflow) where the prototype would otherwise encounter conditions the internal alpha testing could not reproduce. Procter and Gamble's 500-woman in-home tests of Cover Girl Outlast lipstick, Microsoft's Windows Insider Program, and Apple's developer betas for new iOS versions are all examples of this stage. First introduced: Chapter 8.
  32. better alternative The better-than-static condition, which Marco Bertini and Oded Koenigsberg laid out in a May 2024 Harvard Business Review article, says a dynamic-pricing regime is only acceptable to buyers when it outperforms a single static posted price on at least one dimension the buyer actually values. The dimension might be a lower off-peak price, faster access at peak times, or guaranteed availability when the buyer needs it most. If the dynamic regime is strictly worse for the buyer than a static one on every dimension, no amount of clever framing can salvage it. First introduced: Chapter 10.
  33. BIC BIC stands for Bayesian Information Criterion, a numerical score that rewards a statistical model for fitting the data well and penalizes it for adding extra parameters that risk fitting noise instead of signal. In segmentation work the analyst computes BIC for several candidate segment counts (two segments, three, four, and so on) and picks the one with the lowest BIC value on a holdout window of customers, because a lower BIC means the extra segments earned their keep rather than memorizing the quirks of the estimation sample. Empirically, segment counts above six rarely improve BIC and tend to produce segments that do not reappear when the model is refit on next quarter's data, which is why most production segmentations top out at four or five. First introduced: Chapter 6.
  34. Binary Binary refers to a file stored as raw bytes that only specific software knows how to interpret, rather than as readable characters anyone can see in a text editor. Open an XLSX in Notepad and the screen fills with garbled symbols because Notepad cannot decode the format; open the same file in Excel and Excel reads it correctly. XLSX, PDF, PNG, and MP4 are all binary formats; only the program built for each format can read it. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  35. binary container A binary file is one whose contents are stored as raw bytes that only specific software knows how to interpret, rather than as readable characters anyone with a text editor can see. Open an XLSX file in Notepad and the screen fills with garbled symbols, because Notepad does not know how to decode the format. Open the same file in Excel and Excel decodes the bytes into cells and formulas. Photos (PNG, JPEG), videos (MP4), and PDFs are all binary. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  36. binding force The binding force is the single force, among Porter's five (rivalry, new entrants, substitutes, supplier power, buyer power), that is currently doing the most to compress margin in a brand's category. The diagnostic question a team asks each quarter is whether that binding force is tightening (compressing margin further) or loosening (giving the brand room to operate), because the answer drives where the next quarter's strategic investment should go. First introduced: Chapter 3.
  37. Bing Webmaster Tools Bing Webmaster Tools is Microsoft's free analog to Google Search Console, the dashboard a brand uses to read how Bing sees its pages. The tool reports which Bing queries the site ranked for, what the Copilot click-through looks like, how many pages Bing has indexed, what crawl errors the bot hit, and (since November 2025) a per-query AI-citation share report that is the cheapest way for a brand to see whether Microsoft Copilot is naming it in answers. The tool is free for any verified domain. First introduced: Chapter 18.
  38. Black Swans A Black Swan is a piece of information sitting outside what the negotiator expects to find, and surfacing it changes the entire negotiation. The term comes from Nassim Taleb's books on uncertainty. Chris Voss's working hypothesis is that every counterpart holds at least three Black Swans in any negotiation: facts about constraints, motivations, or prior history that the seller does not yet know to ask about. Mirroring and labeling are the tools that surface them. First introduced: Chapter 23.
  39. Bletchley Declaration The Bletchley Declaration is the political declaration signed at the first AI Safety Summit, held November 1-2, 2023 at Bletchley Park in the United Kingdom. Twenty-eight countries plus the European Union signed it. The declaration was the first multilateral political commitment naming frontier AI safety as a shared international concern, but it did not bind signatories to any specific actions, which is why it counts as soft law rather than treaty law. First introduced: Chapter 26.
  40. blue links The blue links are the classical Google search result list, the ten page titles rendered in blue that each link out to a different site. The format has been the dominant search-result shape since the late 1990s, but the AI Overview now sits above this list on roughly a quarter of all queries and frequently answers the buyer's question before they ever scroll down to reach the first blue link. First introduced: Chapter 18.
  41. blue ocean A blue ocean is the counterpart to a red ocean: a positioning quadrant that no current brand occupies because no brand has yet defined that combination of attributes as the relevant one for the category. The water is blue because no fight has yet happened there. Liquid Death's launch into a punk-and-subversive frame for bottled water is the canonical 2020s blue-ocean play; the existing wellness-and-purity frame was a red ocean, and the punk frame was open because no brand had previously named bottled water as a category that could be sold on identity and aggression rather than on virtue. First introduced: Chapter 6.
  42. Blue Sheet The Blue Sheet is a one-page deal-strategy worksheet at the heart of the Miller and Heiman Strategic Selling methodology. The seller fills it out for each complex opportunity, naming every stakeholder by name and tagging each one as Economic Buyer, User Buyer, Technical Buyer, or Coach, then writing what organizational result and what personal win each stakeholder gets from the deal, then inventorying everything that could still kill the deal. The artifact has been the standard B2B opportunity worksheet since the late 1970s, and the modern version lives inside Korn Ferry Sell with embedded coaching workflows. First introduced: Chapter 23.
  43. BM25 BM25 (short for Best Matching 25) is a keyword-scoring formula from 1995 that ranks documents by how well their exact words overlap with the words in the query. The formula weights each matching word by how rare the word is across the whole corpus (rare words count more) and how frequent the word is inside the specific document (more frequent counts more, up to a saturation point). BM25 catches matches that embedding-based retrieval misses, like brand names ('Stanley'), product codes ('XR-200'), and exact-phrase matches the embedding similarity would smooth over. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  44. boomerang rate The boomerang rate is the share of shoppers who circle a fixture, walk away for a moment, and then come back to it before deciding whether to buy. A high boomerang rate is a signal of active consideration, where the shopper is comparing the fixture against something else nearby, while a low boomerang rate signals either an impulse pickup or a destination buy that did not need a second look, which is why retailers track the rate per fixture to figure out which displays are working as decision aids and which are functioning as billboards. First introduced: Chapter 12.
  45. BOPIS Buy Online, Pickup In Store, almost always shortened to BOPIS, is the fulfillment format where the buyer places the order on the retailer's website or app and then drives to the local store to pick the order up at a counter or curbside. BOPIS turns the store into a fulfillment node rather than just a place to shop, and the format reached 97.2 million U.S. users by 2024 because shoppers want the speed of same-day pickup without paying the shipping fee that home delivery would charge. First introduced: Chapter 12.
  46. BOR BOR stands for Brand, Offering, and Relationship, and the framework decomposes any customer relationship into three additive layers. The brand layer is the name and meaning in the buyer's head. The offering layer is the actual product or service the firm delivers. The relationship layer is the touchpoints, loyalty programs, and ongoing interactions the firm maintains with the buyer over time. Different firms lean on different layers: Coca-Cola is mostly brand, an industrial machinery firm is mostly offering, Costco is mostly relationship. First introduced: Chapter 7.
  47. bot A bot is a script that operates a computer the way a human operator would (clicks buttons, types in forms, reads pages) but runs unattended. On a paid panel, a bot is a respondent that is not a human: a piece of software completing the survey to harvest the incentive payment without anyone watching. Bot-environment detection means catching that automation by reading the digital fingerprints the underlying script leaves behind, such as no mouse movement, perfectly uniform typing speed, or no visible browser window on the panelist's screen. First introduced: Chapter 5.
  48. branch A branch is a parallel line of edits inside the same Git repository, used to develop a new feature or a risky rewrite without affecting the main working version of the project. The default branch is usually called main (or master in older projects). Suppose a marketer wants to try a risky rewrite of the competitor-PESTEL Skill she shares with the team. She creates a new branch called rewrite-pestel, makes a series of commits on that branch, and merges the branch back into main only when the rewrite actually works. Until that merge happens, the rest of the team keeps running the old version of the Skill on main, undisturbed. Branches are the mechanism that lets multiple team members work on the same repository at the same time without overwriting each other's edits, which is what turned Git from a single-author backup tool into the collaborative substrate every modern software team relies on. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  49. brand-as-signal The brand-as-signal hypothesis was developed by Tulin Erdem and Joffre Swait in the 1998 Journal of Consumer Psychology, building on Michael Spence's 1973 signaling theory from labor economics. The argument is that observable and costly marketing investment functions as a credible signal of product quality, because a firm that knew its product was low quality would not rationally invest in years of consistent brand-building only to lose those customers as soon as they tried the product. The brand carries the cumulative weight of that investment, and equity is the dollar value of the resulting reduction in perceived risk and information costs across the firm's customer base. First introduced: Chapter 7.
  50. Brand associations Brand associations are the mental links the buyer has built between the brand and the attributes, benefits, user imagery, and occasions that surround it. These are the rich content of the brand's memory network: a single Coca-Cola association might be 'red,' another might be 'Christmas polar bear,' another might be 'movie theater,' and another might be 'shared at a barbecue.' Associations supply reasons to buy beyond pure recognition. First introduced: Chapter 7.
  51. brand awareness Brand awareness is the buyer's ability to recall a brand when asked about it directly. The construct is different from mental availability: awareness asks 'do you know this brand?' (a yes/no question the buyer can answer in the abstract), while mental availability asks 'does this brand come to mind in this specific buying situation?' (a context-bound question that determines whether the brand gets retrieved at the moment of choice). A brand can have 95 percent awareness and near-zero mental availability for a particular CEP. First introduced: Chapter 7.
  52. brand codes Brand codes is the practitioner term that surfaces inside agency briefs, IPA Effectiveness Award submissions, and creative reviews, and it names the same construct that Sharp and Romaniuk call distinctive brand assets. Mark Ritson's October 2025 column in The Drum argues that the field should converge on the Sharp and Romaniuk name for academic and measurement consistency, because using three or four near-synonyms across textbook, agency, and System1 vocabularies makes it harder for a junior on a brand team to recognize that the same audit instrument applies in all three rooms. First introduced: Chapter 16.
  53. Brand-Concept Management Brand-concept management (BCM) is C. Whan Park, Bernard Jaworski, and Deborah MacInnis's 1986 framework, published in the Journal of Marketing. It says every brand commits to one of three meaning axes before any visual or campaign work begins. The functional axis (Tide for cleaning power) emphasizes what the product does. The symbolic axis (Rolex for status) emphasizes what owning the brand signals. The experiential axis (Starbucks for the cafe ritual) emphasizes the consumption experience itself. The concept is selected first, and the tactics required to maintain alignment between concept and image differ across introduction, elaboration, and fortification stages of the brand's life. First introduced: Chapter 7.
  54. Brand dilution Brand dilution is the weakening of a brand's meaning that happens when a firm extends the brand into so many categories, variants, or tiers that buyers can no longer associate the brand with any clear set of products. When Cadbury extended its chocolate brand into mashed potatoes, powdered milk, and instant soups in the 1960s, buyers stopped knowing what Cadbury was supposed to stand for, and the original chocolate business absorbed the damage. The outcome is the opposite of what the extension was meant to produce. First introduced: Chapter 8.
  55. brand entity A brand entity is the engine's internal representation of a brand as a named thing inside its knowledge graph, the same way a person, a place, or an event becomes a named node the engine can reason about. The engine corroborates the entity using structured data on the brand's own site, sameAs links pointing to the brand's other web presences, a Wikipedia entry, and consistent open-web mentions of the brand across the trade press. A brand that has not built a coherent entity is harder for the engine to cite because the engine cannot decide which 'Stanley' the buyer means. First introduced: Chapter 18.
  56. Brand Equity Brand Equity is the share of a customer’s loyalty that comes from their subjective feeling toward the brand, beyond what the offer objectively delivers. A customer paying a 30 percent premium for a Patagonia jacket over an equivalent technical jacket is buying on Brand Equity. The component sub-drivers are awareness of the brand, attitude toward what the brand represents, and ethics in how the brand is perceived to operate. Brand Equity is what funds a price premium that Value Equity alone cannot. First introduced: Chapter 24.
  57. Brand Experience Scale The Brand Experience Scale (BES) is a 12-item survey instrument that scores a brand on four experiential dimensions: sensory, affective, intellectual, and behavioral. A respondent rates each item on a 1-to-7 agreement scale, the items in each dimension are averaged, and the four resulting numbers form the brand's experiential profile that the manager compares against competitors. First introduced: Chapter 7.
  58. brand extension A brand extension is a new product an existing brand launches in a different category or price tier from the ones the brand has previously served, asking buyers to lend the parent brand's accumulated meaning to a category buyers have not yet seen the brand operate in. Honda extending from cars into lawn mowers is the canonical example, because the engineering reputation the brand built in automobiles carries over to a category buyers expect mechanical reliability from. Swiss Army's move from pocket knives into watches is a similar play. The risk is higher than a line extension because the buyer may not grant the brand permission to operate in the new category. First introduced: Chapter 8.
  59. Brand Finance Brand Finance is a London-headquartered brand-valuation consultancy that publishes the annual Global 500 ranking. Its methodology uses the royalty-relief approach: it estimates what the firm would have to pay every year if it had to license its own brand from an arms-length third party, then capitalizes that hypothetical royalty stream into a present-value dollar figure. The resulting totals typically sit between Interbrand's audited-conservative numbers and Kantar's forward-looking amplified numbers. First introduced: Chapter 7.
  60. Brand identity Brand identity is the aspirational set of associations a firm chooses to build for itself over time, distinct from brand image (which is what consumers actually hold in their heads today). The 1996 Aaker definition organizes identity into a core layer (three to five enduring associations the brand intends to mean across products and decades, like Patagonia's environmental commitment or Apple's design simplicity) and an extended layer (four to seven additional personality, design, heritage, and symbol cues that round out the meaning). Identity is also viewed through four perspectives that each ask a different question: brand-as-product asks what the product itself is, brand-as-organization asks what the firm behind it stands for, brand-as-person asks what personality the brand would have if it were a person, and brand-as-symbol asks what visual and metaphorical symbols carry the brand's meaning. A position built from this system inherits internal consistency because every clause of the positioning statement traces back to a slice of identity rather than to a creative-services preference. First introduced: Chapter 6.
  61. brand identity prism Kapferer's brand identity prism is a hexagonal six-facet model that works the brand's identity from both the sender (firm) side and the recipient (buyer) side at once. The three sender facets of physique, personality, and culture describe what the firm transmits to the buyer, while the three recipient facets of relationship, reflection, and self-image describe what the buyer takes on and lives inside the brand. The framework is structured so that any divergence between the sender's transmission and the recipient's reception is the diagnostic for a positioning problem the brand team has to fix. First introduced: Chapter 7.
  62. brand identity system The brand identity system is David Aaker's planning artifact for the brand team. It is the aspirational set of associations the strategist intends buyers to come to hold about the brand. It has two layers (a core identity of 3 to 5 timeless elements and an extended identity of 4 to 7 supporting elements), is read through four perspectives (product, organization, person, symbol), and is paired with a three-layer value proposition (functional, emotional, self-expressive benefits). First introduced: Chapter 7.
  63. Brand loyalty Brand loyalty is the depth of buyer attachment and the propensity to repurchase under competitive pressure. The dimension matters less for growth than the Sharp literature initially assumed (penetration drives growth, not loyalty), but it still matters for defense: a brand with high loyalty buys time against a new entrant or a price war because some of its buyers will keep buying even when a cheaper competitor enters the shelf. First introduced: Chapter 7.
  64. brand mantra A brand mantra is a three-to-five-word internal articulation of what the brand stands for, written for the brand team rather than for the consumer. The mantra is the test every campaign decision has to pass before it ships: if the proposed creative does not align with the mantra, the brand has drifted off its position. Starbucks uses ‘rich, rewarding coffee experience’ as its mantra, and Disney uses ‘fun family entertainment’; both are short enough that every employee can recite them and concrete enough that a campaign brief can be checked against them line by line. First introduced: Chapter 6.
  65. Brand personality Brand personality is the set of human characteristics that buyers associate with a brand. Jennifer Aaker operationalized the construct in 1997 as a 42-item, five-dimension scale covering sincerity, excitement, competence, sophistication, and ruggedness. A respondent rates the brand on each of the 42 items, the items inside each dimension are then averaged together, and the five resulting numbers form the brand's personality profile that the brand team can compare against competitors. First introduced: Chapter 7.
  66. brand pyramid The de Chernatony brand pyramid is a five-level laddering instrument that reads the brand from the bottom up as the buyer encounters it. Level one is attributes (what the package shows). Level two is functional benefits (what the product does). Level three is emotional rewards (how the buyer feels using it). Level four is values (what the brand stands for). Level five is brand personality (the human characteristics the buyer attributes to the brand). The diagnostic use is to compare the values the firm states publicly against the values the buyer infers at the top of the pyramid, because any divergence between the two is the identity-versus-image gap the brand team has to address. First introduced: Chapter 7.
  67. Brand Relationship Spectrum The Brand Relationship Spectrum is David Aaker's four-position taxonomy for how a master brand relates to its specific product offerings. The four positions sit on a continuum from full master-brand visibility (branded house) at one end to full separation between corporate and product brand (house of brands) at the other, with sub-brand and endorsed brand sitting in between as hybrid positions. The architecture choice itself, independent of the marketing inside it, has been shown to move firm value when announced. First introduced: Chapter 6.
  68. brand resonance Brand resonance sits at the top of the Keller pyramid and describes the deepest brand-buyer relationship: the buyer is attached to the brand, advocates for it to friends, repurchases it without considering alternatives, and accepts a price premium. Resonance is the outcome of years of consistent execution at every lower level of the pyramid, which is why a brand cannot reach it through a single campaign no matter how good that one campaign is. First introduced: Chapter 7.
  69. Brand Visibility Index Otterly's Brand Visibility Index is a composite key performance indicator that combines mention rate, average citation position, and sentiment into a single number. The single-number format is useful when the exec dashboard has room for one tile per channel, but most serious GEO programs still govern internally on unaided citation share against a stable prompt set, because the composite hides which underlying signal is actually moving when the index shifts. First introduced: Chapter 18.
  70. BRANDAID-style BRANDAID-style decision-support is the family of marketing-mix models that John Little introduced in 1975, where data-rich channels are calibrated using historical regression and data-sparse channels are calibrated using manager judgment (the manager states a best guess for the elasticity, plus a range of uncertainty around it). The hybrid is the conceptual ancestor of every modern Bayesian-MMM-with-priors approach. The pattern is the right tool when a firm has fifty-two weeks of clean data on three channels but only nine weeks on the new fourth channel. First introduced: Chapter 24.
  71. Branded identity Branded identity (Olins 1989) is the position where the parent corporate identity is invisible behind separately positioned product brands. Procter and Gamble's Tide, Crest, and Pampers, plus Diageo's portfolio of liquor brands, plus Unilever's Dove and Axe, all illustrate the position: the customer encounters the product brand, not the parent corporation. First introduced: Chapter 7.
  72. Brandwatch Brandwatch is the largest of the consumer-intelligence platforms, founded in 2007 and acquired by Cision in 2021, which means a brand using both products is effectively buying two parts of the same parent company's stack.
