It combines earned-media monitoring with social listening, so the same dashboard shows news mentions alongside Twitter and TikTok conversation, and its client list includes roughly 60 percent of the Fortune 100. Annual licensing starts around $30,000, which makes it a stack typical for large consumer-facing brands rather than for mid-market communications teams. First introduced: Chapter 22.
  73. Brave Grounding Brave Grounding is Brave Search's wholesale grounding API that returns clean text excerpts drawn from Brave's thirty-billion-page index for $5 per thousand calls. The same underlying infrastructure powers roughly twenty-two million Brave Search answers per day, which means the brand's pages can earn citations across the entire Brave ecosystem in one investment rather than having to optimize separately for the consumer Brave product and the API. First introduced: Chapter 18.
  74. Breakage rate Breakage is the share of issued loyalty points or rewards that expire or sit permanently unredeemed by the member who earned them. Airlines deliberately design their frequent-flyer programs for 15 to 25 percent breakage and book the unredeemed points as a recurring revenue line on the income statement. Retail loyalty programs above 30 percent breakage are usually a symptom rather than a strategy: the program is training members to ignore the rewards rather than to chase them, which is a long-run cost the firm pays in disengagement rather than a profit the firm books in margin. First introduced: Chapter 11.
  75. breaks bulk Bulk-breaking is what happens when the retailer buys merchandise in pallet or truckload quantities and resells it in household-size units, taking on the labor of unpacking each pallet into the single bottles, boxes, and shirts the shopper actually wants. The markup the retailer charges over the manufacturer's price is paying for that labor plus the storage and shrink risk of holding the inventory until the household needs it. First introduced: Chapter 12.
  76. BRIC BRIC is the four-country grouping a Goldman Sachs economist invented in 2001 to name the large emerging economies (Brazil, Russia, India, China) he thought would dominate growth over the following decades. South Africa joined in 2010, which turned the acronym into BRICS, and several additional countries joined in 2024-2025 under the BRICS+ label. First introduced: Chapter 15.
  77. brief A creative brief is the document the brand team writes to instruct the agency on what a campaign should communicate. It typically names the audience, the key message, the supporting claims, and the brand-voice constraints. Briefs are where the curse of knowledge does its worst damage: the team writes a brief that reads as obvious to people who already know the strategy and produces copy that reads as generic to people who do not. First introduced: Chapter 7.
  78. Brignull 12-pattern dark-pattern catalog The Brignull 12-pattern dark-pattern catalog is the deceptive-design taxonomy that user-interface researcher Harry Brignull built between 2010 and 2024, naming the twelve specific moves by which an interface extracts consent, conceals cost, or blocks exit against the user's actual preference. The twelve patterns are trick questions, sneak into basket, roach motel, privacy zuckering, price comparison prevention, misdirection, hidden costs, bait and switch, confirmshaming, disguised ads, forced continuity, and friend spam. Each one has appeared in a regulatory enforcement case since 2018, and California's CPRA regulations at Cal. Code Regs. tit. 11 §7004 explicitly name dark patterns as grounds to invalidate consent, which means a flow built on any one of the twelve patterns fails the consent regime as a downstream consequence of failing the interface audit. First introduced: Chapter 26.
  79. BrowseComp BrowseComp is OpenAI's open-web research benchmark, which scores how well an agent can answer questions that require browsing the live web. When Anthropic ran its own multi-agent research system on BrowseComp, the team reported that the single best predictor of a run's score was the number of tokens the run spent, and that token volume alone explained roughly 80 percent of the variance in scores. Tool-call count and model selection together accounted for only the remaining 20 percent. First introduced: Chapter 27.
  80. Bubble.io Bubble.io is a no-code platform that gives the builder full visual logic, a relational database, custom workflows, and layouts that respond to the screen size, which together make it the most full-stack of the no-code tools and the closest you can come to building a real custom app without writing code. Bubble bills on a metered unit called the Workload Unit (WU), which means every database query, every workflow step, and every page render consumes a small chunk of your monthly allowance. The Starter plan runs about $32 per month on annual billing, and the mobile-app capability sits on a tier above the web-only baseline. For a builder who wants the broadest design freedom and is comfortable monitoring usage, Bubble is the no-code platform that grows with the project. First introduced: Chapter 14.
  81. build-versus-buy The procurement decision here is a straight choice between two implementation paths the sales-enablement team will have to commit to before the renewal date. The first path is a custom Codex job (a Tier 4 scheduled-agent setup) that reads the Gong application-programming interface every morning and drafts coaching emails the head of revenue then forwards. The second path is to turn on Gong's own shipped call-summary auto-coaching feature, which is a Tier 2 Skill specification the team loads into the vendor product itself. The right choice turns on a simple question: can the sales-enablement team realistically maintain its own Skill folder over the next two years, or is it better off letting Gong's product team maintain that logic for them? Teams that lack a dedicated revenue-operations engineer should pick the vendor-product path almost every time. First introduced: Chapter 23.
  82. bullwhip effect The bullwhip effect is the amplification of order variance as demand information travels upstream through a multi-tier channel. A 10 percent week-over-week jump in end-consumer sales becomes a larger jump in retailer orders, a larger jump in distributor orders, and the largest jump of all in factory orders, even when end demand was actually flat. The name comes from the wrist-to-tip mechanics of a whip: a small flick at the handle produces a crack at the tip. First introduced: Chapter 11.
  83. Bundeskartellamt The Bundeskartellamt is Germany's Federal Cartel Office, the antitrust authority that regulates competition law in the country. On September 1, 2000 it issued a prohibition order against Walmart's systematic below-cost sales of staple goods, which Germany treats as predatory pricing rather than a legitimate price weapon. The Federal Court of Justice reinstated the order on November 12, 2002 after an appeal, and the ruling stripped Walmart of the loss-leader half of its EDLP strategy. First introduced: Chapter 15.
  84. Business Potential Tim Soulo of Ahrefs uses a Business Potential rubric that scores each candidate keyword from zero to three on how naturally the brand can be mentioned as an answer to that keyword. A score of three means the brand is essentially irreplaceable on the topic (Stripe on 'how to accept credit card payments online,' Shopify on 'how to start an online store'), and a score of zero means there is no defensible mention possible and the keyword should be skipped no matter how high the search volume looks. Ahrefs publishes its own blog posts only on topics scoring two or three, which is the editorial discipline the rubric is meant to enforce. First introduced: Chapter 18.
  85. business-to-business Business-to-business (B2B) describes firms whose customers are other firms rather than individual consumers. Salesforce selling its CRM platform to companies, Costco Business Center selling pallets of supplies to small-business owners, and a paper-mill selling reams of bond paper to a printer are all B2B examples. The contrast is business-to-consumer (B2C), where the customer is an individual buying for personal use. B2B sales cycles tend to be longer, deal sizes larger, and the buying decision shared across multiple people inside the customer firm, which is why B2B marketing plans look structurally different from B2C ones. First introduced: Chapter 24.
  86. butt-brush effect The butt-brush effect is the observation, first caught on tape in Paco Underhill's video studies at a Bloomingdale's tie rack, that shoppers (especially women) abandon a display the moment another customer bumps them from behind. The operational implication is that high-engagement merchandise has to sit in wide aisles with six feet of clearance, never in narrow aisles near a doorway, because the geometry of the floor decides whether the shopper stays at the fixture long enough to put anything in the cart. First introduced: Chapter 12.
  87. Buy Online, Pick Up In Store Buy Online, Pick Up In Store (BOPIS) is an omni-channel fulfillment pattern where the buyer places an order through the retailer’s app or website and then drives to the nearest store to walk out with the goods, instead of waiting for the order to ship to her home. Target Drive Up and Walmart Pickup are the canonical 2026 implementations in the United States, and curbside handoff is the variant where a store associate brings the order to the buyer’s parked car rather than the buyer walking inside. First introduced: Chapter 11.
  88. Buying Influences Buying Influences are the four roles every complex deal contains, regardless of how the buyer's organization chart happens to be drawn. The Economic Buyer is the person who controls discretionary funds and holds veto power over the purchase. The User Buyer is the person whose daily work the product will actually touch. The Technical Buyer is the screener responsible for security, compliance, integration, and procurement. The Coach is the buyer-side stakeholder who wants the seller to win, has political access inside the account, and has already supplied actionable intelligence to the seller. The key point is that these four are roles, not job titles, which means one person can occupy multiple roles in the same deal, and any one of the four roles the seller cannot identify is what Miller and Heiman call a Red Flag. First introduced: Chapter 23.
  89. byte A byte is the smallest practical unit of digital storage, large enough to hold one English letter. The marketer encounters bytes in three scaled units: a kilobyte is roughly 1,000 bytes and holds a short email, a megabyte is roughly 1,000,000 bytes and holds a standard photo, and a gigabyte is 1,000 megabytes and holds a short HD movie. The token a language model bills against averages about three or four bytes for English text, which is why a 200-word brief at 260 tokens runs about 1,000 bytes. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  90. byte-pair encoding Byte-pair encoding (BPE) is the standard tokenizer family used by GPT, Claude, and most modern language models. The procedure starts from individual characters and repeatedly merges the most frequent adjacent pair into a single token. The merging continues until the vocabulary reaches a target size (around 100,000 tokens for a 2026 frontier model). The result is a tokenizer where common short words become a single token, while long or rare words split into recognizable subword pieces (the word 'tokenization' might split into 'token' + 'ization', and 'multimodal' into 'multi' + 'modal'). The marketer can paste any text into the chapter's Token Tokenizer figure and watch the same chop the model performs. First introduced: Chapter 1.
03 // c

C C

  1. 16 CFR Part 465 16 CFR Part 465 is the FTC's final rule on consumer reviews and testimonials, effective October 21, 2024, which made six categories of review manipulation explicitly prohibited (including AI-generated fake reviews, paid testimonials presented as organic, and reviews from undisclosed insiders). Violations carry a civil penalty of $51,744 per individual review under 15 USC §45(m)(1)(A), which means a brand that posts a thousand AI-generated five-star reviews is exposed to roughly $52 million in penalties at the per-review ceiling. First introduced: Chapter 26.
  2. 4Cs The 4Cs are Robert F. Lauterborn's 1990 buyer-side audit lens for the marketing mix, designed to stress-test the firm-side 4Ps from the buyer's seat. Consumer wants and needs replaces product, because the buyer is looking for a want satisfied rather than for an artifact on a shelf. Cost to satisfy replaces price, because the buyer pays in time, effort, and switching cost as well as in money. Convenience to buy replaces place, because what matters is whether the buyer can easily reach the offer at the moment she wants it. Communication replaces promotion, because the buyer talks back in 2026 rather than passively receiving messages. The 4Cs do not retire the 4Ps. Instead, they run alongside the older frame as a buyer-side audit of the same five decisions, so the firm can ask whether each lever it pulled actually lands as intended on the buyer's side of the transaction. First introduced: Chapter 2.
  3. 4Cs framework Andrew Faris's 4Cs framework treats a brand's growth health as the joint product of four factors that all have to work at the same time. Customer is the buyer the brand serves. Cost is the acquisition cost on each channel the brand spends against. Channel is where each buyer is actually reachable today, given how the landscape has shifted since 2014. Conversion is the fraction of channel traffic that actually completes a purchase. The 2022 lesson from the framework was that all four levels have to clear the bar simultaneously, because a 2026 channel bottleneck cannot be papered over by raising spend on a 2018-style channel that no longer works. First introduced: Chapter 13.
  4. 5C framework The 5C framework, popularized by Alexander Chernev in Kotler's Marketing Management 16th edition, names the five categories a brand has to scan as part of its working microenvironment. The five categories are Customers, Company (the brand itself), Collaborators (channel partners, agencies, suppliers), Competitors, and Context (the macroenvironment that surrounds all four). The framework is used as a customer-centered scan, in contrast to Porter's Five Forces, which is an industry-centered scan that ignores the firm's own internal capabilities. First introduced: Chapter 3.
  5. C2PA C2PA stands for the Coalition for Content Provenance and Authenticity, the open standard that Adobe, Microsoft, BBC, Google, Meta, and Sony agreed in 2021 as the cryptographic metadata layer for digital content. A C2PA manifest is a small signed file that travels with an image or video and names who created it, which device or AI model produced the pixels, what editing history followed, and the chain of signatures that prove the manifest has not been tampered with. For a brand team, the practical move is to sign every distinctive-asset master at creation, so a later claim that someone else's generative output is the brand's authentic work can be verified against the original signature rather than litigated by eye. First introduced: Chapter 16.
  6. CAC Customer acquisition cost (CAC) is the total marketing-and-sales spend over a period divided by the number of new customers acquired in that period. A firm that spent $500,000 on marketing and sales last quarter and acquired 100 new customers has a CAC of $5,000. CAC is the denominator in the lifetime-value-to-CAC (LTV/CAC) ratio, which most SaaS firms target at 3x or higher to justify the acquisition spend. First introduced: Chapter 23.
  7. Cactus Plant Flea Market The Cactus Plant Flea Market collaboration was the October 2022 limited-edition partnership that brought back the McDonaldland characters Grimace, Hamburglar, and Birdie alongside a new Cactus Buddy figurine, all packaged inside an adult Happy Meal box aimed at the millennial cohort that had grown up with the originals. The four-figurine set resold on secondary markets at up to $300,000 a set, which put the dormant character library back on the cultural surface and gave the brand's 2023 Grimace Birthday Shake the inherited recognition the TikTok trend later amplified. First introduced: Chapter 16.
  8. cadence A cadence is a sequenced multichannel touch pattern the sales-development representative (SDR) runs against each prospect. The canonical post-2015 SaaS pattern uses phone calls, voicemails, emails, LinkedIn touches, and SMS messages across roughly seven days, totaling six to ten touches per prospect. The pattern is designed to close the gap between the 1.7 to 2.1 attempts untrained reps default to (which reach only one in ten cold leads) and the six to ten touches the math actually requires to reach about half of cold leads. First introduced: Chapter 23.
  9. CAGE distance CAGE distance is Pankaj Ghemawat's 2001 framework that decomposes the gap between any two countries into four separate axes a marketer can audit one at a time: Cultural distance, Administrative distance, Geographic distance, and Economic distance. The empirical anchors are striking: two countries that share a common language trade roughly 200 percent more with each other than two that do not, a shared colonial history multiplies trade by roughly nine, and membership in the same trade bloc adds about 47 percent on top. First introduced: Chapter 15.
  10. CAGE distance audit A CAGE distance audit is the practical exercise of scoring a candidate foreign market on each of the four CAGE axes (Cultural, Administrative, Geographic, Economic) one at a time, pulling the inputs from sources like the World Bank, Euromonitor, Hofstede country scores, and current trade-policy filings. A brand manager comparing the U.K. and Mexico as the next U.S. entry uses the audit to surface which specific axis is binding before any capital is committed. First introduced: Chapter 15.
  11. CAGE framework The CAGE framework, developed by Pankaj Ghemawat in a 2001 Harvard Business Review article, scores the distance between a brand's home market and a potential target market across four dimensions. The C stands for Cultural distance, the A for Administrative and political distance, the G for Geographic distance, and the E for Economic distance. The framework's job is to make a brand price market-entry decisions against the four distances before committing capital to the entry. First introduced: Chapter 3.
  12. Calibrated questions Calibrated questions are open-ended ‘how’ and ‘what’ questions (and occasionally ‘why’) the rep uses to give the buyer the feeling of control while constraining the conversation toward the rep's actual answer. The discipline is to avoid yes/no question stems (‘can,’ ‘is,’ ‘are,’ ‘do,’ ‘does,’ ‘will’), because those stems train the buyer to say no. The good form is ‘how am I supposed to make that work?’ rather than the bad form ‘can you give us a discount?’. First introduced: Chapter 23.
  13. Calibration Calibration is the fairness criterion (Kleinberg, Mullainathan, and Raghavan, 2017) that requires the model's output score to mean the same thing across protected groups. If the model assigns a score of 0.7 to a buyer in group A and a score of 0.7 to a buyer in group B, calibration asks whether both buyers in fact go on to exhibit the predicted outcome at the same rate. The criterion is the right one when the score itself is the downstream decision input rather than just an intermediate signal: credit-card limit assignment, insurance-premium pricing, mortgage-eligibility ranking, and FICO-equivalent retail-trade-credit scoring all hinge on whether the score predicts what it says it predicts in every group. First introduced: Chapter 26.
  14. California Office of Emergency Services The California Office of Emergency Services (Cal OES) is the state agency the Transparency in Frontier Artificial Intelligence Act designated as the recipient of critical safety incident reports from frontier AI developers and from members of the public. The standard reporting window is fifteen days from the incident, shortened to twenty-four hours when an incident poses imminent danger of death or serious physical injury. Cal OES is required to publish its first annual aggregated and anonymized incident report on January 1, 2027, which will be the first state-level public dataset on frontier AI deployment incidents. First introduced: Chapter 26.
  15. California Privacy Protection Agency The California Privacy Protection Agency (CPPA) is the first dedicated state-level privacy regulator in the United States, created when the California Privacy Rights Act took effect in 2023. Unlike the FTC, which enforces privacy through unfair-or-deceptive-practices authority on a case-by-case basis, the CPPA issues regulations, conducts proactive audits, and levies its own fines; the agency's first stand-alone enforcement was the March 2025 Honda settlement at $632,500. First introduced: Chapter 26.
  16. cannibalizes Cannibalization happens when a new variant a firm launches takes most of its sales from the firm's own existing variants rather than from competitors, so the firm trades one of its own dollars for another rather than winning new revenue. Cannibalization is bad when it shifts buyers from a higher-margin item (regular Tide) to a lower-margin item (Tide Basic), because the firm loses contribution dollars on every switching buyer. It is acceptable, and sometimes desirable, when it preempts a defection the firm would otherwise lose to a competitor. Suppose a buyer is about to switch from regular Tide because she wants a pod format and Tide does not offer one. A buyer who switches from regular Tide to Tide Pods is still a Tide buyer and her dollars stay inside Procter and Gamble, while a buyer who switches to a competitor's pods is gone from Tide entirely. The trade is therefore worth making when the lost dollar would have gone to a rival and is not worth making when the lost dollar would have stayed on the regular Tide shelf anyway. First introduced: Chapter 8.
  17. capability A capability is an internal strength of the firm that customers actually reward by paying for it, choosing the brand over alternatives, or remaining loyal across cycles. A strength no customer values scores zero on the importance axis of a magnitude-importance SWOT, which means it does not drive strategy regardless of how impressive the trait looks on an internal capabilities map. First introduced: Chapter 3.
  18. cardholder data fabric A cardholder data fabric is a retailer’s identity-resolved customer file, in which every transaction is tied back to a known cardholder through the membership swipe at the register and the co-branded credit card the buyer uses to pay. The record stays persistent across the warehouse, the website, and the app, so the retailer sees one continuous purchase history rather than three fragmented ones. The relationship sitting under the file is contractually durable through the issuer agreement (at Costco, the September 2024 amendment to the Citibank-Visa-Costco contract extended that relationship through June 2029), which is what makes the file a long-lived asset rather than a quarterly operating metric. The file is also what closed-loop attribution and retail-media monetization actually run against. First introduced: Chapter 11.
  19. CARM, the Cross-platform Audience Real-time Measurement project The Cross-platform Audience Real-time Measurement project, abbreviated CARM, is a University of South Australia research program that uses webcam-based eye-tracking to count how many actual seconds of human attention each impression delivers on YouTube, TikTok, Instagram, Facebook, and broadcast TV. The headline finding from the 2022-to-2024 panels is that one TikTok impression delivers two-to-four times as many seconds of attention as one Facebook impression, even though both platforms charge the brand for the same single ‘impression’ in their reporting. That gap is why a cost-per-engaged-second calculation tells the marketer something the standard CPM number hides. First introduced: Chapter 20.
  20. cart-abandoners Cart-abandoners are buyers who added a product to their cart on the brand's site but left before completing the checkout. They are the hottest possible audience for paid ads because their purchase intent is already proven, which is why a follow-up ad paired with a 10 percent discount code typically converts cart-abandoners at five to fifteen times the rate of cold prospects who have never interacted with the brand before. First introduced: Chapter 18.
  21. category breadth Category breadth is the count of distinct category-entry points (CEPs) across the entire category that the brand covers with strong links. McDonald's at nine CEPs is leading its category, while a regional burger brand with a strong link to only 'lunch' covers a single CEP and competes for a much smaller slice of the buyer's week. Brands that cover more than 20 CEPs are typically the category leader in their market, because the breadth of coverage produces the share of category moments that drives growth. First introduced: Chapter 7.
  22. category-buyer asset recall floor The category-buyer asset recall floor names the empirical observation that distinctive assets retired for less than thirty years typically retain 20 to 40 percent recall among older category buyers (the cohort that grew up with the asset on the box) and fall below 15 percent recall among younger category buyers (the cohort that has never seen it). The asymmetry between the two cohorts is what creates a reactivation opportunity, because the older cohort still has the memory link the brand needs to refresh while the younger cohort can be introduced to the asset as if it were new. McDonald's Mayor McCheese sat near this asymmetry in 2020 at roughly 25 percent overall Fame and 85 percent Uniqueness, which placed the asset in the BUILD cell of the Romaniuk grid rather than the KILL cell. First introduced: Chapter 16.
  23. category entry point A category entry point (CEP) is a specific buying situation that triggers a category thought in the buyer's head. 'Hungry on a road trip' is a CEP for fast food, 'mid-afternoon slump at work' is a CEP for snacks and coffee, 'sober night out' is a CEP for non-alcoholic beverages. A brand wins by being the first one that comes to mind when the situation arrives, and a brand linked to many CEPs has many chances to be retrieved across the buyer's week. First introduced: Chapter 7.
  24. category-entry points A category-entry point (CEP) is a buying situation a brand is linked to in the buyer's memory, like thirsty after exercise, something edgy at a party, or a late-night road-trip stop. The more such situations the brand is linked to, the more often the brand fires into the buyer's mind at the moment of need. McDonald's is linked to roughly 36 CEPs across breakfast, lunch, snack, treat-the-kids, and meeting-up situations, while most brands cluster at 6 to 12 such links and category leaders run above 20. CEPs are tracked through panel surveys of roughly 384 to 1,000 buyers per quarter, and the count is the empirical operationalization of mental availability. First introduced: Chapter 2.
  25. category entry points (CEPs) Category Entry Points (CEPs) are the specific situations, occasions, and cues that prompt a buyer to think of the category in the first place. For beverages, CEPs include morning, post-workout, with pizza, commute, party, road trip, gift; each one is a distinct retrieval moment. A brand's mental availability is roughly the count of CEPs it is linked to multiplied by the average strength of each link, which is why a brand that owns many weak CEPs often outperforms a brand that owns a few strong ones. First introduced: Chapter 4.
  26. category killer A category killer is a retailer that picks one product category and carries a narrow but extremely deep assortment inside it, so a shopper who comes in looking for a hammer finds forty kinds of hammer instead of the three a general-merchandise store would carry. Home Depot in home-improvement, PetSmart in pet supplies, and Staples in office supplies are the U.S. archetypes, and the format wins by having the selection the specialist shopper wants in the category the specialist shopper cares about. First introduced: Chapter 12.
  27. Category-level demand Category-level demand refers to the total dollars a shopper spends across all brands in a category in a given trip, and it tends to be relatively insensitive to price because the household still wants cereal whether the shelf is on sale or not. A 10 percent price cut on every cereal brand at once does not move category-level demand by much, because the shopper still buys roughly the same number of boxes, so the only effect is that the chain compresses its own margin without gaining a corresponding lift in volume. Brand-within-category demand is the share of those category dollars that gets allocated across the individual brands inside the category. A 10 percent cut on just Cheerios while Lucky Charms holds at full price will pull substantial brand share toward Cheerios, because the shopper readily substitutes one brand for another inside the same category. The asymmetry between category-level inelasticity and brand-within-category elasticity is the empirical foundation for the EDLP-at-category-level plus HiLo-at-brand-level hybrid that anchors modern grocery pricing. First introduced: Chapter 12.
  28. causal forest A causal forest is a machine-learning method for estimating how a treatment effect varies across different kinds of customers, rather than just reporting one average effect for the whole group. The method works by adapting the random-forest algorithm so that one subsample of the data is used to choose how to split customers into similar groups, and a different, non-overlapping subsample is used to estimate the treatment effect inside each group. This trick (called honest splitting) is what lets the method produce valid confidence intervals on the per-group effects. It is implemented in the R package grf and in Microsoft's EconML library, both free and open-source. First introduced: Chapter 5.
  29. causal-structure-learning Causal-structure-learning MMM is the 2024-2026 academic frontier that fixes a known limitation of classical marketing-mix modeling: classical MMM treats every channel as independent of every other channel, but in real campaigns the channels feed each other in chains. Television advertising lifts brand search, brand search lifts retargeting, retargeting closes the sale, and a regression that ignores those chains either over-credits or under-credits each step. Causal-structure-learning MMM learns the chain itself from the data, using directed-acyclic-graph (DAG) learning combined with deep-learning temporal models. Published references include CausalMMM at KDD 2022 and DeepCausalMMM at arXiv 2510.13087 in October 2025. First introduced: Chapter 24.
  30. CausalImpact CausalImpact is Google's 2015 Bayesian state-space implementation of the synthetic-control idea, developed by Kay Brodersen and colleagues. The method decomposes the response variable into a local trend, seasonal cycles, and a regression on covariate series, then returns a posterior distribution over the counterfactual outcome. CausalImpact works best when the data has rich seasonality and a single, clean intervention date. The open-source package is available in both R and Python at google.github.io/CausalImpact. First introduced: Chapter 5.
  31. CCPA/CPRA The California Consumer Privacy Act came into force in January 2020, and the California Privacy Rights Act expanded it in January 2023. Together they form the strictest US state privacy regime in operation, and because California's market is large enough that most national brands comply rather than carve the state out, the CCPA and CPRA function as the de facto US privacy default for any brand selling to American consumers. First introduced: Chapter 26.
  32. cell-level citation Cell-level citation is a provenance feature in Claude for Excel where every assertion the model returns in the sidebar is linked back to the specific cell it was derived from. Each citation is clickable, and clicking it scrolls the spreadsheet to the source cell so the analyst can see exactly which row and column the model read to produce the claim. The analyst can therefore verify the analytical chain end-to-end inside Excel itself, without needing a separate provenance system or a screenshot-and-circle workflow. This is what turns a black-box AI summary into an auditable analytical artifact. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  33. Center for AI Standards and Innovation (CAISI) The Center for AI Standards and Innovation (CAISI) is the US federal body responsible for cross-evaluation of frontier AI models, sitting inside the Department of Commerce. The body was renamed from the US AI Safety Institute on June 3, 2025, and its operational work picked up through 2026 with cross-evaluation agreements signed with multiple frontier labs. CAISI shares the open-source Inspect evaluation framework with the UK AISI, which is how a model evaluated by one institute carries the evaluation methodology over to the other. First introduced: Chapter 26.
  34. Central Limit Theorem A foundational result in statistics that says the average of many independent samples from any population forms a bell-shaped curve centered on the true population average, with a width (the standard error) that shrinks as the square root of the sample size grows. Stated in 1810 by Pierre-Simon Laplace, formalized by Lyapunov in 1901; the reason a survey of a few hundred people can be informative about millions. First introduced: Chapter 5.
  35. central route The central route is what the buyer's mind does when motivation, ability, and opportunity are all high enough for them to actually evaluate the arguments in a message. A buyer in a car dealership comparing models on horsepower and fuel economy is running central-route processing. Attitudes formed this way are strong, last a long time, and resist counter-persuasion better than attitudes formed quickly through cues. First introduced: Chapter 4.
  36. centroids The geometric center of a cluster, computed as the average of every customer’s coordinates inside it. If a cluster contains three buyers at (10, 50), (20, 70), and (30, 60), the centroid sits at (20, 60). K-means moves each centroid step by step toward the average position of whichever customers are currently closest to it. First introduced: Chapter 5.
  37. CEO championing of pricing CEO championing of pricing, which Stephan Liozu and Andreas Hinterhuber introduced as a measured construct in their 2013 work, captures how visibly the CEO defends pricing as a strategic priority inside the firm. The construct has three operational components: the CEO articulates pricing-strategy priorities to the leadership team, defends realized prices against quarter-end discount pressure from the sales force, and links pricing performance to executive compensation. Liozu and Hinterhuber operationalized this as a seven-item survey scale, and their firm-level data showed that it strongly moderates whether the firm's pricing capabilities actually translate into higher profitability or just stay on the strategy slide. First introduced: Chapter 10.
  38. CEP A Category Entry Point (CEP) is any situation, mood, or cue that prompts a buyer to think of the category in the first place. The morning routine prompts coffee, a party prompts beer, gift-giving prompts flowers; each of those triggers is a CEP, and the brand a buyer remembers in that moment is the brand they reach for. The chapter walks the full inventory in section 4.7. First introduced: Chapter 4.
  39. CEPs Category entry points (CEPs) are the specific buying situations the buyer can recall the brand from: morning, gym bag, gift, road trip, party, post-workout. Each one is a distinct retrieval moment, and the brand a buyer thinks of at that moment is usually the brand they reach for. Jenni Romaniuk and Byron Sharp catalog the CEP inventory in How Brands Grow Part 2. First introduced: Chapter 4.
  40. Cerebro Cerebro is Helium 10's reverse-ASIN search tool. The seller pastes the ten-character Amazon Standard Identification Number of a competitor's product into the tool, and Cerebro returns every search keyword that competitor's listing currently ranks for, alongside the rank position and the monthly search volume for each keyword. This is used in two practical ways. The seller scans the list to find keyword opportunities the competitor is winning that the seller is not yet targeting, and the seller then uses the same list as the seed for optimizing her own product listing on the keywords that actually drive traffic in the category. First introduced: Chapter 13.
  41. chain of thought A chain of thought is the intermediate reasoning a model writes before its final answer. The chain steps through the problem the way a student showing work on a math test steps through it, naming intermediate quantities, checking each step against the previous one, and revising when an intermediate conclusion contradicts an earlier one. The chain is often hidden in the chat surface (the marketer only sees the final answer in the chat window) but is always billed as output tokens through the API. Standard 2026 reasoning models like OpenAI o3 and DeepSeek R1 produce chains of 5,000 to 50,000 tokens before the final answer, while chat-class models like Claude Sonnet 4.6 in its default mode produce no chain at all and reply directly. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  42. Chain-of-thought faithfulness Chain-of-thought faithfulness is the degree to which an AI model’s natural-language reasoning trace actually reflects the internal computation that produced its answer. Anthropic’s interpretability research published in March 2025 showed that a model can confidently narrate one algorithm in its trace while internally using a completely different procedure to compute the answer. The practical consequence for an analyst is that the model’s explanation is a hypothesis about its reasoning, not an audit trail. Anything load-bearing has to be checked against action-layer ground truth (the actual database query result, the verified citation, the re-run number) rather than against the trace itself. First introduced: Chapter 24.
  43. CHAMP CHAMP is a four-criterion lead-qualification framework from the 2010s that reorders BANT to open with the buyer's problem rather than her budget: Challenges, Authority, Money, and Prioritization. The change matters because leading with the budget question in an inbound or consultative motion signals that the rep cares more about whether the buyer can pay than about whether the buyer's problem is real, which kills the call before the rep has earned the right to ask about budget. First introduced: Chapter 23.
  44. channel-resilience tower The channel-resilience tower is a Coughlan and Jap framework (2016) that models a marketing channel as a stack of partner, route, and product-line blocks resembling a Jenga tower. The marketer’s job is to pull blocks out and push new blocks in as the market shifts, without ever removing a load-bearing block faster than its replacement can slide into position. Resilience, in this framing, is the capacity of the route-to-market to absorb partner failure, e-commerce disintermediation, regulatory shocks, and product-line expansion without losing the underlying service outputs the buyer is actually paying for. First introduced: Chapter 11.
  45. Chapter 11 Chapter 11 is the section of the US bankruptcy code that a struggling company files under in order to reorganize its debts while continuing to operate. Chapter 11 differs from Chapter 7, which is the liquidation filing where the company simply ceases to exist and its assets are sold off. Most large corporate bankruptcies in the United States, including Toys R Us, Sears, and Blockbuster, were filed under Chapter 11 rather than Chapter 7. First introduced: Chapter 3.
  46. character training Character training is the downstream alignment stage Anthropic published on June 8, 2024, applied after Constitutional AI in the training pipeline. The technique uses synthetic data and Reinforcement Learning from AI Feedback to instill traits that go beyond simple harm avoidance: curiosity about the user's actual question, honesty without sycophancy, calibrated confidence on factual claims, and transparency about being an AI rather than passing as a human. Amanda Askell led the work, and the published list of trained traits is a procurement-grade artifact because a brand can compare it against the behavior the brand wants its customer-facing assistant to exhibit. First introduced: Chapter 26.
  47. Charm pricing Charm pricing sets the last digit of a posted price to a 9 (either as a whole-dollar ending like $39 or as a cents-ending like $9.99 or $1.99) because that final digit moves demand more than the math alone suggests it should. The mechanism runs on leftmost-digit encoding: buyers read prices left-to-right, weight the leftmost digit disproportionately, and under-process whatever comes after. Walmart, Kohl's, and most fast-food chains run charm pricing as the category default. Apple, Patagonia, and most premium brands round to whole dollars because a 9-ending reads as a discount signal that undercuts their premium positioning. First introduced: Chapter 10.
  48. chatbot A chatbot is a program that holds a text conversation with a human user. Pre-2022 chatbots ran on hand-written rule trees ('if the user says X, reply with Y; if the user says something unrecognized, reply with a generic deflection'), which is the architecture Casper's Insomnobot ran on. Chatbots built from 2023 onward are usually wrapped around a large language model instead, which lets them respond to any input fluently but introduces the corresponding risk of the model saying something the brand did not intend. First introduced: Chapter 18.
  49. ChatGPT ChatGPT is OpenAI’s consumer assistant, the closest competing product to Anthropic’s claude.ai. The 2026 GPT-5 release added file upload, persistent project workspaces (called Custom GPTs or Projects depending on the tier), and built-in web browsing at the Plus tier. The Channel Audit prompt in this section runs identically on either platform; choose whichever the brand team already has on contract. First introduced: Chapter 11.
  50. ChatGPT Shopping ChatGPT Shopping is the cross-merchant product-discovery and checkout feature OpenAI added to ChatGPT in April 2025. A user types a shopping question into ChatGPT, such as 'find me a soy candle gift set under thirty dollars,' and the assistant searches across participating merchants, compares options, presents a shortlist with prices and reviews, and lets the user complete the purchase with one click inside the chat session itself. The buyer never visits the merchant's website. The merchant's listing reaches the buyer through OpenAI's index of participating storefronts, much the way an SEO-optimized listing reaches a buyer through Google's index, except that the gatekeeper is an AI assistant rather than a search-results page. For the merchant, this is a new discovery channel that does not require a click-through to the brand's site to convert, which means the brand's site is no longer the only surface where the brand has to be readable. First introduced: Chapter 13.
  51. Chi-square The Chi-square test is the categorical counterpart to the Kolmogorov-Smirnov test. Where Kolmogorov-Smirnov compares two numeric distributions, Chi-square compares two distributions of category counts, for example the share of conversions coming from each channel this week against the historical mix over the prior eight weeks. The test counts how many observations actually fell into each category, compares those counts against the counts the team would expect if nothing had changed, and reports a p-value showing how likely a difference that large would be under random sampling alone. Inside Evidently AI, the analyst points the test at categorical columns like channel mix, device type, or campaign tag, and the test flags any column whose distribution has drifted by more than random noise would explain. First introduced: Chapter 24.
  52. choice-based conjoint The most common conjoint variant since the late 1990s. Respondents are shown sets of three to five product concepts (each a different combination of attribute levels) and pick the one they would actually buy, repeated across roughly 8 to 15 sets per person. The model recovers utility weights per attribute level from the choice pattern. Sawtooth's CBC module is the production reference; Conjointly and Qualtrics ship competing implementations. First introduced: Chapter 5.
  53. choice-context effects Choice-context effects describe the empirical pattern that the buyer's preference between two options can change when a third option is added to the choice set, even when the new option is never selected. The pattern violates the classical economic rule called independence of irrelevant alternatives, which says the buyer's relative ranking of A and B should not depend on whether C is also available. Decades of evidence say that rule is wrong; the family of context effects includes decoy, compromise, phantom, and upscaling, each of which works through the assortment shape rather than through any change in the buyer's underlying preferences. First introduced: Chapter 4.
  54. choice overload Choice overload is the empirical pattern that adding more options to a choice set can reduce the probability the buyer picks any option at all. The original anchor is Iyengar and Lepper's 2000 jam study, where a tasting table of 24 flavors converted 3 percent of passers-by while a tasting table of 6 flavors converted 30 percent. A 2015 meta-analysis weakened the finding by showing the effect was not consistent across categories, but a 2024 Dean-Ravindran-Stoye paper restored it using a higher-powered random-utility-model test (the standard statistical workhorse for choice-set studies), which is why curated-assortment retailers like Trader Joe's and Costco are back on solid empirical footing. First introduced: Chapter 4.
  55. chunk Chunking is the standard pattern when one document is too large for one model context window: split the file into smaller pieces (one chunk per chapter, or every 10,000 tokens, or one row range per chunk), summarize each piece in its own model call, then have the model summarize the summaries. The chunks are processed sequentially or in parallel and the final answer combines what each chunk surfaced. Combined with retrieval (the contextual-retrieval recipe earlier in the chapter that cut failure rates from 5.7 percent to 1.9 percent) chunking scales to corpora millions of tokens long. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  56. churn Churn is the share of customers who leave the firm in a given period, calculated as customers lost during the period divided by customers active at the start of the period. An annual churn rate of 17.7 percent means roughly 18 of every 100 customers who started the year with the firm are gone by year-end. Churn and retention are the same idea read from opposite directions: a 17.7 percent churn rate is equivalent to an 82.3 percent retention rate. First introduced: Chapter 24.
  57. Cint Cint is the largest panel exchange in the marketing-research industry, connecting researchers to roughly 300 million respondents across 130-plus countries by routing their study to whichever sub-panel matches the demographic quota. A panel exchange does not own the respondents directly; it brokers access across many smaller panels and bills per completed response. The exchange model is why a class team can recruit a national sample in a few hours without negotiating with twenty separate vendors. First introduced: Chapter 5.
  58. circuit-tracer Circuit-tracer is an open-source Python library that Anthropic released on May 29, 2025, hosted at github.com/safety-research/circuit-tracer. The library generates attribution graphs against any open-weight transformer, which means it shows which internal features in the model fired together to produce a given output. The companion Neuronpedia frontend lets non-research teams inspect those intermediate features and run counterfactual interventions on a single forward pass, which converts what was a research-only technique into a trust-but-verify procedure a deployment team can actually run on production prompts. First introduced: Chapter 24.
  59. circuits A circuit inside a neural network is a recognizable internal pathway that performs a specific computation, in the same way an electronic circuit on a chip performs a specific function. The 2025 Anthropic paper named three concrete circuits in Claude 3.5 Haiku: a two-hop reasoning circuit (when asked about Dallas, the model activates Texas before activating Austin), a backward-planning circuit the model uses when writing poetry to a target ending rhyme, and an entity-recognition gate whose misfires produce a class of hallucinations on partially-familiar brand names. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  60. Cision Cision is the legacy enterprise vendor in the PR software market, with origins as a press-clipping bureau founded in 1867 and acquisition by the private-equity firm Platinum Equity in 2014. It is the default tool used by Fortune 500 communications departments, and it carries a database of roughly 1.6 million journalists worldwide. The per-seat license starts around $7,000 a year, which is high enough that small brands typically choose Muck Rack or Prowly instead. First introduced: Chapter 22.
  61. Cision MCP server The Cision MCP server is a small program that exposes Cision's monitoring database to any AI application that speaks the Model Context Protocol. When the brand's PR Skill calls cision.mcp.local with a query, the server runs that query against the live Cision database and returns a structured response that the model can then use in its own reasoning. The architectural advantage is that the model itself never sees the API key, which means a brand can give a model the ability to query Cision without giving it the credentials that would let it do anything else with that account. First introduced: Chapter 22.
  62. citation agent A citation agent is a post-processing subagent that rechecks every claim in the final synthesis against the source documents the lead and subagents collected, and inserts source links for human verification. The citation agent's job is to catch the hallucinated quote or the fabricated statistic that the writer subagent might have produced under deadline pressure. First introduced: Chapter 5.
  63. citation share Citation share is the fraction of times a brand is cited inside AI answers across a defined set of queries the brand cares about, and it is the primary key performance indicator for any generative-engine-optimization program. A brand probing fifty category queries each week and being cited in twelve of them has a 24 percent citation share, and the metric is tracked over time to see whether the GEO investment is moving the number up. First introduced: Chapter 18.
  64. Citation Source Index 2026 The Citation Source Index is the 2026 5WPR research synthesis that aggregated 680 million AI-engine citations across ChatGPT, Claude, Perplexity, Gemini, and Google AI Overviews between August 2024 and April 2026. The index ranks the top fifty most-cited domains the AI engines actually drew from when synthesizing answers, which makes it the canonical reference for which kinds of sites a brand has to be on if it wants to appear in those citations. First introduced: Chapter 18.
  65. Clari Clari is forecasting and deal-management software founded by Andy Byrne in 2012. The pre-merger anchor product was pipeline forecasting with confidence scoring on each open deal, used by heads of revenue every Friday afternoon to see which deals would actually close that quarter. Clari acquired Wingman in 2021 to add conversation intelligence to the forecasting stack, and then merged with Salesloft on August 5, 2025 to form the Revenue AI Powerhouse. First introduced: Chapter 23.
  66. classifier A classifier is a small program that takes one piece of input (a customer-service transcript, a survey open-end, a product image) and returns a category label (‘complaint about price’, ‘complaint about shipping’, ‘not a complaint’). Before language models, classifiers were the dominant tool for sorting text at scale; the analyst hand-labeled a few thousand examples and the classifier learned the pattern. In 2026 most classification tasks have been replaced by a one-paragraph prompt to a language model, which is the Software 3.0 substitution Karpathy is naming. First introduced: Chapter 5.
  67. Claude Code Claude Code is Anthropic's terminal-based AI agent runtime, meaning a version of Claude that runs inside the command line on a developer's computer rather than inside a chat window in a browser. Once installed, the user can save a prompt as a slash command and invoke it the same way they would call any other terminal command. Useful for repeatable workflows like running a Skill on a fresh document every Monday. First introduced: Chapter 4.
  68. Claude Cowork Anthropic's off-the-shelf productivity-suite agent surface, named Cowork. Research preview in late January 2026, full enterprise launch February 24, 2026, with native connectors that let the agent read from Google Drive, send mail through Gmail, sign documents through DocuSign, and pull data from FactSet without any custom wiring. The right pick when the client is a small marketing team with no engineering function and the team wants the agent to work across the productivity suite the same way Claude Code works across the terminal. First introduced: Chapter 27.
  69. Claude for Chrome A browser-only Anthropic extension that runs the same Computer Use agent loop against whatever Chrome tabs the user has open. Research preview August 26, 2025; beta on all paid Claude plans (Pro, Max, Team, Enterprise) as of 2026. Install takes three clicks and no Docker container is required, which makes it the right pick for browser-only marketing automation against tools like Klaviyo, Sprout, Google Analytics, and Asana. First introduced: Chapter 27.
  70. Claude for Office Claude for Office is Anthropic’s integration that puts the Claude model into a sidebar inside Microsoft Excel, PowerPoint, Word, and Outlook. It entered private beta on October 27, 2025 and reached general availability in Q1 2026. The practical attraction is that one model session carries shared context across the four apps, so an analyst who pastes a cohort retention curve into Excel can ask Claude to draft the briefing slide in PowerPoint and the executive summary in Word without re-uploading the data each time. First introduced: Chapter 24.
  71. claude-haiku-4-5 Claude Haiku 4.5 is Anthropic's fast and cheap model tier, priced at roughly one dollar per million input tokens and five dollars per million output tokens. The tier is designed for high-volume routine calls where the answer pattern is consistent and the firm just needs reliable throughput, like auto-tagging messages, classifying survey responses into categories, or running deterministic procedures step by step. It is the right tier to put behind a Skill that has to run the same monotonicity-filter step on every Van Westendorp price-sensitivity CSV the firm collects. First introduced: Chapter 10.
  72. Claude Haiku 4.5 Claude Haiku 4.5 is the smallest model in the current Claude 4-series family, priced at one dollar per million input tokens and five dollars per million output tokens as of May 2026. That is roughly one-third the price of Claude Sonnet 4.6, and Haiku is generally good enough for shallow-reasoning steps such as flagging planogram-misranking SKUs from a tabular sell-through file, while the heavier-reasoning steps (such as the format-recommendation paragraph) still call for Sonnet or Opus. First introduced: Chapter 12.
  73. Claude Marketplace Claude Marketplace is the procurement surface Anthropic launched on March 6, 2026, which lets an enterprise buy partner-built Claude-powered tools through a single Anthropic invoice rather than negotiating separate contracts with each vendor. The Marketplace opened with six launch partners and a zero-commission policy, meaning Anthropic takes no cut of the vendor's revenue, which is the signal a procurement officer reads to mean the platform is competing on developer attraction rather than on rent extraction. For marketing teams, the Marketplace is the rung-five move past the four-tier ladder: procuring someone else's scheduled agent rather than building it. First introduced: Chapter 27.
  74. claude-opus-4-7 Claude Opus 4.7 is Anthropic's deepest-reasoning model tier, priced at roughly fifteen dollars per million input tokens and seventy-five dollars per million output tokens. The tier is designed for cases where the answer requires synthesizing across competitor positioning, brand-equity context, fairness ceilings, and psychological-pricing levers all at the same time, rather than running a single repetitive procedure. It is the right tier to use when the pricing audit is supposed to change the firm's strategy, not just adjust a number on a shelf tag. First introduced: Chapter 10.
  75. claude-sonnet-4-7 Claude-sonnet-4-7 is Anthropic's mid-tier model in the Claude 4 family, designed to balance reasoning quality against per-call cost. It is priced at $3 per million input tokens and $15 per million output tokens, which means a typical pitch-drafting call runs around 4 to 8 cents. It is the default workhorse for production marketing workflows where cost-per-call matters but the reasoning still needs to be solid enough to trust on real customer-facing output. First introduced: Chapter 22.
  76. Claude Sonnet 4.6 Claude Sonnet 4.6 is Anthropic's mid-tier 2026 model, released in February of that year. The model is priced at three dollars per million input tokens and fifteen dollars per million output tokens, which is one-fifth the input cost of Opus 4.7. Sonnet 4.6 is the default workhorse for the bulk of marketing analysis work, where Opus 4.7's deeper reasoning is overkill and Haiku 4.5's faster response is not strong enough. First introduced: Chapter 3.
  77. claude.ai Claude.ai is Anthropic's hosted chat interface, where conversations are organized into reusable containers called Projects. Each Project carries its own system prompt, uploaded files, and Skill folder, which means the same configuration is applied to every conversation inside that Project rather than having to be re-typed each time. Pricing in 2026 runs free for limited use, $20 per month for the Pro tier, and $30 per seat per month for Teams. First introduced: Chapter 22.
  78. claude.ai Project A claude.ai Project is a workspace inside Anthropic’s consumer assistant where you can attach files (CSV exports, PDF documents, Google Sheets downloads) and chat against pinned context that stays with the Project across conversations. Marketing-Research students at Oklahoma State log in to claude.ai with their OSU single-sign-on credentials and create Projects for each assignment. First introduced: Chapter 11.
  79. claude.ai Projects A claude.ai Project is a workspace inside Anthropic’s consumer assistant where you can attach files, set persistent system instructions that the model sees on every turn, and chat against the pinned context across multiple conversations. Anthropic shipped the general-availability release of Projects in April 2026, with built-in web search and document upload included at the Pro tier. First introduced: Chapter 11.
  80. clean-room A clean room is a privacy-preserving environment that lets two parties (typically a brand and an ad platform, or two brands that want to compare audiences) upload encrypted user-level data, run analyses on the joined dataset, and then read out only aggregate, differentially-private results. The point of the architecture is that the row-level data never leaves the room, so neither side sees the other's underlying records, yet both sides get to ask questions like 'how much audience overlap do our customer lists have' or 'which campaign produced the most incremental purchases against our shared customer base.' The vendors that ship the architecture in 2026 are Google Ads Data Hub, Meta Advanced Analytics, AWS Clean Rooms, and Snowflake Data Clean Rooms, and each one trades a different mix of platform reach for analytical flexibility. First introduced: Chapter 18.
  81. click-through rate Click-through rate (CTR) measures, of every hundred people who saw a search result or ad, how many actually clicked it. An 8 percent CTR means that of every hundred viewers, eight clicked through and the other ninety-two read the Overview or saw the ad and left without engaging. CTR is the single most-watched intermediate metric in paid search and SEO because it sits between impressions (how many times the brand was shown) and conversions (how many of those views produced revenue). First introduced: Chapter 18.
  82. clickstream panel A clickstream panel is a population of consenting users whose every browser click is recorded by a research firm and then sold to anyone who needs to study real internet behavior. SparkToro, Datos, and SimilarWeb are the three vendors that buy and resell the de-identified click data in 2026, and the data is the single best source for measurements like 'how many clicks does the average Google search actually produce' because the panel observes real users instead of asking them what they did. First introduced: Chapter 18.
  83. client-rendered A client-rendered page is one where the server sends a near-empty HTML shell to the browser, and the browser's JavaScript then fills in the actual content (the page text, the product prices, the customer reviews) inside that shell. The pattern is the default behavior of single-page applications built with frameworks like React, Next.js, or Vue. The structural consequence is that crawlers and AI bots that do not execute JavaScript see only the shell, with none of the content the brand actually wants to be cited on. First introduced: Chapter 18.
  84. closed-loop attribution Closed-loop attribution is a measurement property of advertising platforms where the same company shows the buyer the ad and rings up the buyer’s eventual purchase, so the platform can see the full path from impression to checkout inside one database. Google and Meta cannot match this property because they show the impression but do not own the register that rings up the sale, so they have to infer the connection from device fingerprints and probabilistic stitching. Retail-media networks like Amazon Ads and Walmart Connect do match it, which is the structural reason they have been winning ad budget back from Google and Meta since roughly 2020. The mechanism is explored in deeper depth in your digital marketing course. First introduced: Chapter 11.
  85. cluster analysis Cluster analysis is a family of statistical methods that group customers with similar response patterns into segments without the analyst naming the groups in advance. K-means is the most common variant, in which the algorithm picks a number K of segments, assigns each customer to whichever segment's center is closest, recomputes the centers from the assigned customers, and repeats until the assignments stop changing. Hierarchical clustering builds a tree of nested groupings and lets the analyst pick the height at which to cut. Latent-class clustering treats segment membership as an unobserved variable that the algorithm infers from the observed purchase pattern. All three approaches produce groups directly from the data, which is what makes them post hoc rather than a priori. First introduced: Chapter 6.
  86. cluster-lifecycle events The cluster-lifecycle events are the four named ways a segment can change between two refits of the segmentation model, which is what an analyst monitors when they want to know whether the segmentation is stable enough to keep using. A birth event names a new segment that appears in the new refit with no equivalent in the prior one, which usually signals a new buyer type has emerged in the category. A death event names a prior segment that has no equivalent in the new refit. A merge event names two prior segments that have collapsed into one. A split event names one prior segment that has fanned out into two. The 2025 academic framework adds a fifth event called drift, which describes a segment that kept its identity but whose center moved more than a calibrated threshold, signaling that the buyers inside it are gradually changing their behavior. First introduced: Chapter 6.
  87. cluster pages Cluster pages are the shorter, narrowly focused articles, typically running eight hundred to two thousand words each, that go deeper on one sub-topic of a pillar page. Every cluster page links back to the pillar and the pillar links out to all of them, so the bidirectional structure looks to a search engine like a coherent body of work on the broader topic. That signal of topical authority is also what 2025-onward language-model retrievers read when they decide which sites to cite in an AI Overview answer. First introduced: Chapter 17.
  88. CLV Customer Lifetime Value (CLV) is the total margin a single customer is projected to generate for the firm across the entire time that customer remains a customer, after subtracting what the firm spent to acquire them in the first place. The standard formula adds up the customer's expected monthly margin period by period, where each period's contribution is discounted by a retention rate (the probability the customer is still around in that period) and by a discount rate (because a dollar of profit next year is worth less than a dollar today). A coffee subscription that generates $8 a month at a 95 percent monthly retention rate and a 1 percent monthly discount rate is worth roughly $145 in lifetime value, which is the number the firm should compare against its $30 acquisition cost when deciding whether to keep running the same ads. First introduced: Chapter 6.
  89. CMI CMI stands for the Content Marketing Institute, the organization Joe Pulizzi founded in 2007 to professionalize the discipline of brands publishing their own audiences rather than renting attention through advertising. CMI runs the annual B2B and B2C content marketing benchmarks reports that have served as the standard industry reference since 2010, and the institute itself was acquired by media holding company UBM in 2016 and is now part of Informa. First introduced: Chapter 17.
  90. CMO The Chief Marketing Officer (CMO) is the senior executive who owns the firm's marketing function and reports to the Chief Executive Officer. The CMO sets the annual marketing budget, approves campaign decisions, and is the executive a brand manager presents to when a campaign result reaches the all-hands. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  91. co-innovation risk Co-innovation risk is the dependence on partner innovations and partner products that the firm does not control but that have to be in place for the firm's own launch to deliver its value proposition. Ron Adner introduces the concept in The Wide Lens (Portfolio 2012). At a Fortune-500 scale the partner innovations might be a chip foundry's new process node or a payment network's tokenization standard. At Kickstarter scale, a student card-game launch's co-innovation dependencies include the buyer's home printer access for a downloadable score sheet, a six-sided die that did not ship in the box, and a Discord channel moderator running through launch week. If any of those partner products is not in place on launch day, the launch's value proposition has not actually arrived. First introduced: Chapter 8.
  92. co-opted resistance Co-opted resistance is the pattern by which corporate marketing absorbs and remarkets the visual language of the anti-corporate critique that emerged in the 1990s. Examples include Absolut's label-less premium campaigns, Starbucks Reserve outlets stripped of the corporate green-and-white branding, and the post-2020 indie-sublabel pattern run by major holding companies. The 2026 form extends to AI-generated faux-handcraft packaging visuals and AI-generated faux-personal social voice that signal authenticity while concealing the corporate parent. First introduced: Chapter 7.
  93. coarse personalization Coarse personalization is a 2025 two-step targeting procedure introduced by Zhang and Misra that picks the optimal number of distinct marketing treatments the firm should offer and assigns each customer to one of them. The first step uses causal machine-learning methods (causal forests and several related learners) to estimate, for every individual customer, how strongly that customer would respond to each of the available treatments. The second step uses an optimal-transport clustering algorithm to group customers whose response patterns are similar into a small number of segments K, where K itself is jointly chosen rather than picked in advance. On a large-scale promotions-management field experiment the procedure recovered over 99.5 percent of the expected profit that full one-to-one personalization would have earned while offering only five segments instead of one offer per customer. First introduced: Chapter 6.
  94. Cochran sample-size formula The Cochran sample-size formula comes from William G. Cochran's 1977 textbook Sampling Techniques, which has been the standard reference for survey sample-size planning since the late 1970s. The formula tells the analyst how many respondents she needs for a given combination of confidence level and margin of error. To anchor the math in a concrete number: for a fifty-fifty proportion (the worst-case scenario, which gives the most conservative sample-size estimate) at ninety-five percent confidence and a five-percentage-point margin of error, the required sample size for a single proportion in a large population works out to roughly three hundred eighty-four respondents. Smaller margins or higher confidence levels push that number up substantially, while the dependence on the size of the underlying population becomes negligible once the population exceeds about fifty thousand. First introduced: Chapter 5.
  95. Code of Practice The Code of Practice on General-Purpose AI is the voluntary instrument the EU AI Office published under Article 56 of the EU AI Act, operationalizing the Article 53 documentation duties and the Article 55 systemic-risk duties for GPAI providers. Signing the Code earns the developer two procurement-relevant benefits: a reduced administrative burden in demonstrating Article 53 compliance, and mitigating-factor weight in any Article 101 fine calculation. Non-signatories are not exempt; they must show compliance through 'other adequate means,' which is a higher procurement burden. The first wave drew twenty-three signatories in 2025 (Amazon, Anthropic, Google, IBM, Microsoft, Mistral, OpenAI among them) while Meta declined. First introduced: Chapter 26.
  96. Coded exposure Coded exposure is mode three of the New Jim Code framework: a system that combines hyper-visibility and invisibility by race in ways that produce harm. The pattern is that the system makes one group highly visible at the wrong moments (a body camera tuned to specific skin tones so darker-skinned subjects are captured at higher resolution) while making the same group invisible at the right ones, and the asymmetry compounds over time when prior contact creates the data the system uses to decide who to surveil next (PredPol-style predictive policing returns officers to neighborhoods where prior contact created the case file). The marketing analogs include surveillance-pricing systems that price-discriminate by inferred demographic, behavioral-advertising audiences that re-target Black users for predatory financial products, and retail loss-prevention facial-recognition that flagged Black customers at higher rates in the Rite Aid 2023 case. First introduced: Chapter 26.
  97. Codex Cloud Tasks Codex Cloud Tasks is OpenAI's hosted variant of the Codex coding agent. The user creates a task inside ChatGPT, supplies a repository URL plus a task description, picks a recurring time (or a GitHub pull-request trigger, or a manual fire), and the agent runs on OpenAI infrastructure at the scheduled cadence rather than on the marketer's laptop. The product was the first widely available native scheduling feature in a coding-agent runtime, and it is the OpenAI-side equivalent of running Claude Code under a GitHub Actions cron line. The output lands as a chat thread containing the agent's reasoning, file edits, and any artifacts produced. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  98. coding Coding is the systematic labeling of qualitative data so that twenty unstructured interview transcripts can be turned into something the analyst can actually act on. The discipline runs in three passes. Open coding tags every interesting phrase in the transcripts with a short descriptive label. Axial coding groups those tags into themes that connect to each other. Selective coding picks the two or three themes that explain the most variation and anchors the final analytic story around them. First introduced: Chapter 5.
  99. coercive power Coercive power is the ability to make a channel partner do something by credibly threatening to take something valuable away if the partner refuses. The thing taken away might be shelf space, a co-op marketing dollar, the next product allocation, or the contract itself. Walmart has used the lever for decades by implying that a supplier who resists a price concession will lose its slot on the next planogram review, and the threat works because losing Walmart shelf is a single-digit-percent-of-revenue event for most consumer brands. First introduced: Chapter 11.
  100. cognitive interviewing A pretesting protocol where 8-12 respondents drawn from the target population are recruited (typically through Prolific or User Interviews at 15-30 dollars per respondent) and asked to think aloud as they answer each draft question. The researcher records where the respondent hesitates, asks clarifying questions, or interprets a word differently than intended. Cost: 4-8 hours of researcher time plus the panelist incentives. Expected yield: 3-7 questions identified as comprehension or retrieval failures and fixed before the production survey spends its full budget. First introduced: Chapter 5.
  101. Cohen's kappa Cohen's kappa is a zero-to-one score that measures how often two coders looking at the same data assign the same code, after subtracting the agreement that two coders would have hit by chance even if they were just guessing. A kappa above zero-point-eight is called strong agreement; zero-point-six to zero-point-eight is acceptable; below zero-point-six is unreliable. The standard validation move on a human-AI coding pass is to re-code fifty to one hundred responses both ways (human and AI) and accept the AI codes only if the kappa between them clears the zero-point-seven threshold. First introduced: Chapter 5.
  102. cohort A cohort is a group of buyers defined by a shared characteristic, most often age or the date they first signed up with the firm. The phrase '35-44 cohort' means buyers who fall between those ages, while a 'March 2024 cohort' means buyers who signed up that month regardless of age. Cohort analysis (covered in more depth in MKTG 4423) lets the firm see whether buyers who joined in March behave the same way as buyers who joined in September, which matters because pulling them all into one average can hide drift in the customer base over time. First introduced: Chapter 2.
  103. cohort-quality-by-acquisition-source Cohort-quality-by-acquisition-source splits cohort retention not just by start month but by start month crossed with acquisition channel. A cohort that signed up in March 2024 via paid search retains very differently from a cohort that signed up in March 2024 via a referral from an existing customer, even after controlling for demographics. The pattern was forced into the mainstream by the 2020-2021 cohort reversals at Wayfair, Carvana, Peloton, and Lemonade, where pandemic-era acquisition through paid channels brought in customers whose retention parameters never matched the pre-pandemic referral cohorts. First introduced: Chapter 24.
  104. collaborative customization Collaborative customization is the quadrant where the buyer co-designs the product with the firm before it ships, which means the underlying item physically changes from order to order and the buyer is fully aware of the change. Nike By You lets a buyer pick the colorway, the material, and the printed name on a pair of sneakers before the factory cuts the leather. Indochino tailors a full suit to the buyer's own measurements, which the buyer either submits online or has taken in one of the firm's showrooms. Lego ships specialty sets the buyer has configured online from a parts library, then assembles to that buyer's exact spec. The model fits best when the buyer actively wants to design rather than just choose from a menu, and when the firm's manufacturing architecture is modular enough to support the design space without raising the marginal cost of every unit. First introduced: Chapter 8.
  105. Comet Comet is Perplexity's Chromium-based browser that first launched July 9, 2025, then opened free worldwide on October 2, 2025, and shipped on Android on November 20, 2025. The AI assistant runs in the sidebar and reads whatever page the user has open as a privileged source, summarizes content on demand, and (in the paid tier) executes multi-step tasks like booking a flight or filling a comparison spreadsheet on the user's behalf. First introduced: Chapter 18.
  106. commit A commit is a frozen snapshot of every file in the repository at a given moment, attached to a short message explaining the change and the name and email of the person who saved it. Commits form a chain over time: each new commit points back to the previous one, so the full edit history is reconstructable from the most recent commit back to the very first one. The Monday version of this discipline is that every named edit a marketer makes to a Skill or MCP config becomes one commit, which means the team can answer 'what changed last Friday and who did it' without guessing or reading every file by hand. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  107. commitments A commitment, in Anthony Iannarino's 2017 framework, is one of ten sequential buyer-side commitments the seller is trying to earn through the deal cycle. The ten rungs are time, explore, change, collaborate, build consensus, invest, review the solution, resolve concerns, decide, and execute. The seller's discipline is to know exactly which rung the deal is sitting on at any moment and to ask for the next rung up rather than asking for the close prematurely. First introduced: Chapter 23.
