  108. community-led growth Community-led growth (CLG) is a go-to-market strategy where a brand-owned or brand-adjacent community, often a Discord server, a subreddit, or a niche forum, is the primary distribution and retention channel for the product. The members of the community create the content the brand would otherwise have to commission, recruit the new members the brand would otherwise have to acquire through paid ads, and provide the product feedback the brand would otherwise have to run user-research panels to collect. Notion's Ambassadors program, Figma's Friends program, Lenny Rachitsky's Slack, and the Webflow forum are the canonical examples, and each of them produces a scale of word-of-mouth that paid acquisition simply cannot match at the same cost. The marketer's job inside a CLG company is to seed and steward the community rather than to run the typical campaign-and-funnel apparatus. First introduced: Chapter 23.
  109. compaction API The compaction API is an Anthropic feature shipped alongside the February 2026 one-million-token context window. The API automatically rewrites a long conversation history into a shorter summary once the input crosses a configurable threshold the team sets, so a multi-turn agent can run for hours without the input bill growing linearly with the conversation length. Without compaction, a 100-turn agentic loop pays for the full conversation on every turn after the first, which means the bill on turn 100 is roughly 100 times the bill on turn 1. With compaction enabled, the loop pays for a small running summary plus the latest few turns, so the per-turn bill stays roughly flat across the run. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  110. compelling desired customer action A compelling desired customer action is the 2025 Roger Martin reframing of what a strategy actually has to specify. A cascade qualifies as a strategy only when the firm can name four things together: the customer who is supposed to act, the action she is supposed to take, the circumstance under which the action is supposed to occur, and the alternative the customer was supposed to take but did not. The four-part naming is the falsifiability condition that distinguishes a real strategy from a planning artifact. If the firm cannot name all four, the cascade is a planning document, not a strategy. First introduced: Chapter 2.
  111. competence-destroying A competence-destroying discontinuity is a new technology that requires skills the incumbent firm does not have, which usually means the firm that led the prior generation fails to lead the next one. The canonical examples come from Michael Tushman and Philip Anderson's 1986 Administrative Science Quarterly paper: the minicomputer's emergence inside the mainframe era and the diesel-electric locomotive's displacement of steam, in both of which the incumbent's accumulated skills did not carry over to the new technology base. First introduced: Chapter 3.
  112. competence-enhancing A competence-enhancing discontinuity is a new technology whose mastery builds on the skills, knowledge, and capital that incumbent firms already have. The canonical examples come from Michael Tushman and Philip Anderson's 1986 Administrative Science Quarterly paper: Pilkington's float-glass process and Boeing's 707, in both of which the same firms that led the prior generation also led the next one because their existing competences transferred to the new technology. First introduced: Chapter 3.
  113. competitive displacement Competitive displacement is the B2B selling scenario in which the target buyer already has a vendor and is not currently in the market for a replacement. The seller's job is not to walk a fresh buyer through change but to convince an already-served buyer that the change itself is overdue. Anthony Iannarino's 2018 Eat Their Lunch is the first systematic playbook for this scenario, and the framework's central claim is that the displacement conversation has to start at Level 4 (strategic partnership) rather than at Level 1 (product), because if the buyer already has an incumbent then Levels 1 through 3 are by definition already provided. First introduced: Chapter 23.
  114. competitive frame The competitive frame is the set of brands a buyer considers as genuine alternatives in the moment they are deciding what to buy. The frame is defined from the buyer's point of view, not the firm's: two products that an industry analyst would group together as competitors are not in the same competitive frame if buyers do not actually consider one when they would consider the other. A Stanley Quencher and a Yeti Rambler are in the same competitive frame because the buyer comparison-shops them against each other, but a Stanley Quencher and a generic Amazon-brand tumbler may not be in the same frame even though both are insulated drinkware, because the buyer choosing on identity has already filtered the generic out of the consideration set. First introduced: Chapter 6.
  115. competitive intelligence Competitive intelligence is the discipline of legally and ethically gathering, tracking, and analyzing information about a brand's competitors so the brand can make better strategic decisions. The Cengage marketing-strategy textbook formalizes the practice as a five-stage loop covering identification, characterization, assessment, capability mapping, and response planning, walked in §3.6 of this chapter. First introduced: Chapter 3.
  116. Competitor-based pricing Competitor-based pricing (sometimes called going-rate pricing) sets the firm's price by anchoring to what rivals are charging and then adjusting up or down for differentiation. A firm might match the category leader, beat the leader by 5 percent, or charge a 5-to-10 percent premium for a better brand reputation. The common failure mode is a downward price spiral, where each rival's match becomes the next rival's anchor and the whole category loses margin in a price war that nobody wins. First introduced: Chapter 10.
  117. complements Complements are products whose presence raises the value of an industry's output to the buyer. The classic example is the relationship between consoles and games: a video game console is more valuable to a buyer when the library of available games is larger. Porter's 2008 update of his five-forces framework treated complements as a sixth quasi-force, because a strong complement ecosystem can lift an entire industry's prices, while a weak one can drag a category down regardless of the rivalry inside it. First introduced: Chapter 3.
  118. complete A complete is a single respondent who finished the survey from end to end without dropping out partway through. Panel providers bill per complete, not per attempt, which is the basic accounting unit of the industry. Half-completes (people who started but quit before the last question) do not count and are not paid for. This is why the practical recruit-frame the team budgets against is always 1.3 to 2 times the target completed count: the team has to invite enough respondents to absorb the half-completes plus the screen-outs. First introduced: Chapter 5.
  119. completion The completion is the model's reply, generated word by word from the prompt plus the model's frozen weights. In a chat surface like Claude.ai, the completion is exactly the text that shows up after the marketer presses Enter. The bill for any single call counts both prompt tokens (the input) and completion tokens (the output) at separate per-million rates, with the output rate typically three to five times the input rate. A 1,000-token prompt that produces a 500-token reply on Sonnet 4.6 ($3 input, $15 output per million tokens) costs $0.003 on input plus $0.0075 on output, so about a penny per call. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  120. completion-rate bar The completion-rate bar is the share of a video the average viewer has to watch through to the end before TikTok's algorithm will push the post past the brand's existing followers and into the wider For You audience. The bar moved from roughly 50 percent in 2024 to roughly 70 percent in 2025, and continued upward through 2026. The practical consequence is that a 30-second clip now has to hold the viewer through 21 seconds of watch time, not 15, to escape the existing-follower test pool. The platform is essentially using the brand's existing audience as a quality jury before it grants the post broader distribution. First introduced: Chapter 19.
  121. comprehension, comparative advantage, interpersonal relations, history, preference, and attachment In 2008, Donald Lehmann, Kevin Lane Keller, and John Farley factor-analyzed brand-tracking survey items across U.S. and China samples in three categories (soft drinks, toothpaste, fast food), and the analysis recovered six recurring factors. The six are comprehension (does the buyer understand what the brand offers), comparative advantage (does the buyer think this brand is better than alternatives), interpersonal relations (do friends and family endorse the brand), history (does the brand have continuity and heritage the buyer recognizes), preference, and attachment. History was the empirical surprise because it held as a distinct factor rather than as a sub-component of performance. First introduced: Chapter 7.
  122. compromise effect The compromise effect, which Itamar Simonson documented in a 1989 Journal of Consumer Research paper, is the empirical pattern where in a three-option set arrayed along a tradeoff (cheap and basic, mid-priced and mid-featured, expensive and premium), the middle option gains share simply by being the compromise position. The Goldilocks rule in choice psychology. The effect is distinct from the decoy or attraction effect because compromise does not require one option to dominate another, only that one option sits in the middle of a salient tradeoff dimension. First introduced: Chapter 10.
  123. Computer-and-mathematical occupations Computer-and-mathematical occupations is one of the U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics' major occupational groups, covering software developers, data scientists, statisticians, operations-research analysts, computer systems analysts, and roughly twenty other related job titles. The group holds about 3.4 percent of the total U.S. workforce as of 2025 but accounted for 37.2 percent of Claude.ai conversation volume in the February 2025 Economic Index report, which is the highest concentration of any major occupational group relative to its workforce size. The asymmetry is the empirical anchor for the claim that AI adoption is currently dominated by the workers who write the AI tools, which makes the early-adoption pattern look different than the eventual mass-adoption pattern will. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  124. computer use Computer use is the family of agent capabilities in which a frontier model takes a screenshot of a screen, decides what to do, and executes the action by moving the cursor, clicking, or typing. The model uses the same gestures a human operator uses on the same software, which means any application a human can drive can be driven by an agent. Anthropic launched computer use on October 22, 2024 with five primitive actions: screenshot (capture the current screen), mouse_move (move the cursor), click (click at a location), key (press a keyboard key), and type (type a string of characters). OpenAI's Operator and Google's Project Mariner shipped equivalent capabilities in 2025. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  125. computer-using agent A computer-using agent is an agent that perceives a graphical user interface (the actual screen the user would see) through screenshots, plans its next action as pixel coordinates and keystrokes the way a human would, and executes that action through a sandboxed browser harness so the agent's clicks land inside a contained environment. OpenAI named its own version Computer-Using Agent, abbreviated CUA, and Anthropic ships the same capability under the Computer Use API. First introduced: Chapter 27.
  126. computer-vision shrink monitoring Computer-vision shrink monitoring is a set of ceiling-mounted cameras paired with a model trained to recognize when a shopper at a self-checkout lane scans one item but bags two (a 'non-scan' or 'pass-through'), when a basket of items leaves through the exit without being bagged, or when a known-high-loss SKU is taken off the shelf without being scanned later. The model does not arrest anyone. It flags the transaction for a clerk's attention in real time, or it logs the loss for the next shift's shrink-prevention review. Self-checkout is the canonical deployment because the camera-to-transaction link is cleanest there and the technology pays for itself in twelve to eighteen months in most reported deployments. First introduced: Chapter 12.
  127. Concentrated targeting Concentrated targeting is the coverage strategy that picks one segment and serves it deeply rather than spreading marketing and operational effort across several segments at once. The strategy is the classical small-firm play, sometimes called niche marketing in older textbooks, and it works when the firm has limited resources and the target segment is willing to pay a premium for depth of fit. Porsche on the sports-car enthusiast is the canonical example, and Liquid Death's entry into the bottled-water category against a punk-and-subversive 'death to plastic' positioning is the 2024-2026 version of the same play. First introduced: Chapter 6.
  128. concept brief A concept brief is a one-page document that pins down what a new product idea is supposed to do before any engineering or prototype work begins. The brief names the job-to-be-done the concept addresses, identifies the target user (the segment, the occasion the buyer would use the product in, and the trigger that would make them switch), spells out the reasons a buyer would actually adopt the product, and writes down the first falsifiable acceptance test the team will run against the concept. Cooper's NewProd benchmarking found that new-product-development success rates roughly double when the team is forced to write a brief through Gate 1 before any prototype work starts, because the discipline of writing the brief surfaces gaps the team would otherwise discover later and more expensively. First introduced: Chapter 8.
  129. concept testing Concept testing is the structured presentation of a product idea to target buyers, with scoring questions that measure how each buyer responds. The concept can be a paragraph description, an image, a short video, or an interactive prototype, depending on how concrete the idea is at this stage. The point is to surface weaknesses in the concept while changes are still cheap, before the team commits to a prototype budget and tooling decisions that get expensive to reverse. The BREXI rubric used later in this section scores each concept on five dimensions: Believable, Relevant, Engaging, X-factor, and Inspiring action. First introduced: Chapter 8.
  130. concrete language Concrete language is language anchored in tangible, specific, imaginable words rather than abstract ones. The word coffee is more concrete than beverage; the phrase a green Stanley tumbler with the lid up is more concrete than hydration product. Grant Packard and Jonah Berger published a 2021 study in the Journal of Consumer Research showing that customers spent roughly 30 percent more over the following 90 days after receiving a customer-service email written in concrete language than after receiving the same content written in abstract language. First introduced: Chapter 19.
  131. concreteness Concreteness, in the Banerjee and Urminsky 2024 study of 8,977 A/B-tested headlines, is the empirical property of a headline that names the domain and the stakes without disclosing the resolution. The relationship between concreteness and click-through is an inverted U, which means that headlines too abstract to register underperform and headlines that give the punchline away also underperform, while the sweet spot sits in a calibrated middle band. A 2017 Liquid Death headline reading Murder Your Thirst names the domain (thirst) and the stakes (resolving it) in three words without disclosing that the product is canned water, which lands in the 4-to-7 concreteness band the study identified as the sweet spot. First introduced: Chapter 16.
  132. concurrent Concurrent attention is the everyday situation where a single viewer is split across multiple apps in the same minute, never landing sustained focus on any one of them. Picture a college student who is technically watching a TikTok but is also checking iMessage, switching to Instagram between posts, and glancing at a browser tab; industry panel tracking through 2025 and 2026 puts the median teen app-switch interval at 44 seconds, down from about 2.5 minutes a decade ago, which means even the platform-level attention seconds are split across competing apps inside the user's head. First introduced: Chapter 19.
  133. Conditional Average Treatment Effect Conditional Average Treatment Effect, abbreviated CATE, is the expected difference between a customer’s outcome when she receives the marketing treatment and her outcome when she does not, conditional on everything else the firm knows about her. CATE is the per-user version of the average treatment effect a campaign-level experiment would estimate. Where an average treatment effect tells the marketer whether the coupon worked across all customers on average, CATE tells the marketer whether the coupon is worth sending to this specific customer given her features. First introduced: Chapter 24.
  134. confederate A confederate is an actor working secretly for the researcher and posing as another participant in the experiment. The real subject thinks the confederate is a fellow volunteer doing the same task; in fact the confederate is following a script. The technique lets the experimenter precisely manipulate one variable (in this study, whether the buyer received an unsolicited Coke) while keeping every other social and physical detail constant. First introduced: Chapter 4.
  135. conformal prediction Conformal prediction is a statistical method that wraps any point predictor (a regression model, a classifier, a language model) and turns its single best-guess output into a prediction interval (for a continuous forecast) or a prediction set (for a category label), with a mathematical guarantee about how often that interval or set contains the actual truth. The guarantee is finite-sample valid (it holds for any sample size, not just asymptotically), distribution-free (it does not require the data to follow a normal distribution or any other specific shape), and conditional only on the calibration data and the test point being exchangeable (drawn from the same underlying distribution in any order). The method wraps the underlying model without retraining it, which is why a marketer can apply it to a Claude classifier, a GeoLift fit, or a Cochran-formula sample-size estimate without rebuilding any of the three. First introduced: Chapter 5.
  136. conformance quality Conformance quality is the degree to which every individual unit a firm produces matches the design specification the engineering team set. High conformance means every can of Coca-Cola tastes the same as every other can, every BMW M3 hits zero-to-sixty within a tight tolerance of the spec, and every Stanley Quencher seals identically on the lid threads. Low conformance means two buyers of the same brand have different experiences depending on which production batch they bought from, which kills the trust the brand promise depends on. The plain-English contrast worth carrying is that performance quality describes the ceiling the product can hit on its best day, while conformance quality describes whether every buyer who walks out of the store actually experiences that same best day rather than a worse day produced on a worse shift. First introduced: Chapter 8.
  137. conjoint analyses Conjoint analysis is a survey method that shows buyers different bundles of features and prices and asks them which one they would pick, with the bundles designed to mathematically reveal how much each individual feature is worth to the buyer in dollars. The technique is the standard way of measuring willingness-to-pay for individual product attributes, and the course MKTG 4423 walks the design and analysis in detail. First introduced: Chapter 4.
  138. conjoint analysis Conjoint analysis is a survey-based method that infers a dollar value for each individual product feature by forcing buyers to choose between complete product bundles rather than rating features in isolation. Because every choice involves trade-offs across price, capacity, brand, and other attributes, the statistical model can back out how much each attribute is worth to the buyer in dollars. First introduced: Chapter 10.
  139. conjoint study A conjoint study is a survey instrument that asks respondents to choose between hypothetical product profiles built from combinations of attributes, then estimates the dollar value the respondent puts on each attribute level (partworth utility) from the pattern of choices. Used for sample sizes of 300-1,000 respondents to recover preference heterogeneity and price elasticity simultaneously, conjoint is the workhorse instrument behind most commercial segmentation projects that need brand-level price sensitivity. First introduced: Chapter 6.
  140. Conjointly A web-based competitor to Sawtooth, free at the basic tier (panel sample sold separately, video responses billed at 1.60 dollars per minute capped at 10 dollars per respondent), with paid tiers for larger studies. Sufficient for a 92-respondent CBC on three attributes and three levels each; project results export to CSV for external analysis if the marketer wants to fit hierarchical-Bayes utilities outside the platform. First introduced: Chapter 5.
  141. consensus Build-consensus is the rung on the Iannarino commitment ladder where the seller makes an explicit ask that her primary contact open the door to the other stakeholders the buying decision will require. Without that ask, the deal is single-threaded against one person's preference, and procurement, security, IT, finance, and line-of-business objections all arrive at the signature stage rather than during discovery, which is exactly when the deal stalls into no-decision. First introduced: Chapter 23.
  142. consideration set The consideration set is the small group of brands a buyer can actually recall at the moment they are making a decision in the category. The set typically holds between two and eight brands, with the modal number closer to three. A brand outside the buyer's consideration set effectively does not exist for that buyer in that moment, regardless of how strong the product is on attributes the buyer never gets the chance to evaluate. First introduced: Chapter 4.
  143. Constitutional AI Constitutional AI is a model-training method that replaces millions of human harm labels (the expensive ingredient in standard RLHF) with a short written list of natural-language principles, called 'the constitution.' In phase one, the model is trained to critique and revise its own responses against those principles. In phase two, a second model uses the same principles to judge which of two candidate responses is better, and that AI-generated preference signal feeds the reinforcement-learning loop. Yuntao Bai and colleagues at Anthropic introduced the procedure in a December 2022 paper, and it is the documented kickstart of what is now a wider field of AI-feedback alignment methods. The 2026 version of the constitution is 23,000 words and Creative Commons CC0 licensed, so a brand legal team can read the full document in an afternoon. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  144. constitutional classifiers A constitutional classifier is a real-time filter that sits in front of (and behind) the deployed model and blocks a defined class of outputs the deployment standard prohibits. The classifier itself is a smaller model trained on synthetic jailbreak attempts plus human-curated red-team data, so it has seen both the attacks attackers try and the patterns that distinguish acceptable from unacceptable outputs. Anthropic activated constitutional classifiers on Claude Opus 4 when AI Safety Level 3 (ASL-3) protections went live on May 22, 2025. The classifier runs on every input and every output the model produces, and any block fires fast enough that the user experiences a polite refusal rather than a multi-second delay, which is how the brand can ship the safety check without making the chat noticeably slower. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  145. Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (CFPB) The Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (CFPB) is the U.S. federal regulator that supervises consumer finance and issues consent orders against banks for unfair, deceptive, or abusive practices toward customers. Created by the Dodd-Frank Act of 2010 in the wake of the financial crisis, the CFPB is the agency that levied the $185 million penalty on Wells Fargo in September 2016 for the unauthorized-account-opening scandal, and the agency's consent orders triggered the broader $9 billion settlement wave that followed. First introduced: Chapter 23.
  146. consumer packaged goods Consumer packaged goods (CPG) is the United States label for the same category that fast-moving consumer goods (FMCG) describes in markets outside the U.S. Both labels point at low-price, frequently bought items sold through retail: groceries, soft drinks, paper products, snacks, personal care, household cleaners. Procter and Gamble, Unilever, Nestle, and Coca-Cola are the canonical multinational CPG firms, and the category is where most retail-marketing science (Sharp, Ehrenberg-Bass, the IPA Databank) draws its empirical regularities from. First introduced: Chapter 24.
  147. consumer surplus Consumer surplus is the gap between the maximum price a buyer was willing to pay for a product (their WTP) and the price they actually paid at the register. If a buyer would have paid up to $100 for a jacket and the firm charges $70, the buyer captures $30 of consumer surplus. First-degree price discrimination drives that gap toward zero by charging each buyer their personal WTP, which transfers the surplus from the buyer to the firm. First introduced: Chapter 10.
  148. content farm A content farm is a vendor that publishes large volumes of cheap shallow articles aimed at ranking on long-tail keywords rather than at helping readers. Pre-2024 the typical operating model was about $30 per article, fifty to two hundred articles a month, and a long-tail capture strategy, and Google's 2024 update sequence demoted the sites running that playbook by tens of percent on average. First introduced: Chapter 18.
  149. context-editing Context editing is a server-side strategy Anthropic shipped on September 29, 2025 that automatically clears the oldest tool calls and tool results from the prompt once the input-token total crosses a configurable trigger. The strategy is configured with three numbers: trigger (the token count at which clearing starts), keep (the number of most recent tool uses to retain), and clear_at_least (the minimum number of tokens to remove per clearing event, so the prompt-cache rewrite pays for itself by avoiding small repeated rewrites). The purpose is to keep the active context window small even on long runs: stale tool results that the agent already summarized into /memories no longer need to occupy active prompt space, so they get dropped. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  150. context rot The empirical phenomenon, measured by Hong, Troynikov, and Huber at Chroma in July 2025 across 18 frontier models including GPT-4.1, Claude Opus 4, and Gemini 2.5: every model showed measurable accuracy degradation as input length grew, and the degradation was not uniform across the window. The replication notebook is open-source. First introduced: Chapter 27.
  151. context window The context window is the model's working memory, measured in tokens. Every model has a fixed ceiling on how many tokens it can hold in mind at one time, and anything past that ceiling is silently truncated before the model ever sees it. As of May 2026, Claude Sonnet 4.6 and Opus 4.7 hold one million tokens, Haiku 4.5 holds 200,000, and ChatGPT's GPT-5 caps at 400,000. The opening scene of this chapter (the 67-percent shipping-complaint number that was actually from the rows that fit) is the failure this primitive predicts. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  152. contextual native ads A contextual native ad is a brand mention that is woven directly into the body of an answer a conversational AI assistant gives the user, formatted to look like part of the assistant’s normal recommendation rather than a separate banner or sidebar. If a user asks Claude or ChatGPT for ‘a good blender for smoothies under one hundred dollars,’ a contextual native ad would surface a specific brand inside the recommended answer in a way that is visually indistinguishable from an unpaid suggestion. The 2025 Journal of Marketing paper by Hermann, Puntoni, and Schweidel that introduced the term argues this format is on track to dominate chatbot monetization the way keyword ads dominated search results in the early 2000s and feed-ranked sponsored posts dominated social-media monetization in the 2010s. First introduced: Chapter 5.
  153. Contextual placement Contextual placement is the practice of buying ads on sites whose topic is related to the brand's offer (car ads on car-magazine sites, cookware ads on recipe sites, project-management software ads on developer blogs). The targeting works because the buyer is already engaged with the category at the moment they see the ad, and the format does not depend on a third-party cookie the way behavioral targeting did, which is why contextual returned to budget prominence after the 2024 cookie reset. First introduced: Chapter 18.
  154. Contextual retrieval Contextual retrieval is a technique Anthropic published in September 2024 that improves the accuracy of retrieval-augmented generation by adding context to each chunk of text before it is searched. The procedure prepends fifty to one hundred tokens of explanatory context (describing what the chunk is about and where it came from) to each chunk, then combines semantic embeddings (which find chunks similar in meaning) with BM25 search (which finds chunks containing the same keywords), and optionally reranks the top results. The published benchmark says this setup cuts top-twenty-chunk retrieval failure from five-point-seven percent down to one-point-nine percent, which is the difference between a system that misses one in twenty questions and one that misses one in fifty. First introduced: Chapter 5.
  155. continuous-replenishment supply chain A continuous-replenishment supply chain is a configuration designed for predictable, brand-loyal demand, where the manufacturer and the retailer share forecasts and inventory data so that shelves are restocked on a steady schedule rather than waiting for stockouts to trigger orders. The textbook example is the Walmart and Procter and Gamble vendor-managed-inventory partnership that began in 1989, in which P and G monitors Walmart’s sales data in near real time and ships replacement stock before the shelf runs low. First introduced: Chapter 11.
  156. contribution margin Unit contribution is the dollar amount each sale of a product adds toward covering the firm's fixed costs and producing profit, calculated as the selling price per unit minus the variable cost of producing that unit. A Stanley Quencher selling for $45 with $18 of variable cost (materials, manufacturing, packaging, freight) generates $27 of unit contribution, which is what is left to cover the salaries, the rent, the marketing budget, and the eventual profit. The metric matters because two products with identical sales can have very different unit contributions depending on the cost structure underneath. First introduced: Chapter 8.
  157. control Control is the discipline of comparing planned to actual often enough to revise the plan inside the horizon it covers. A plan with no control mechanism is an annual ritual: the firm writes it in November, files it, and learns whether it worked the following November when it is too late to fix. A controlled plan reads the actuals quarterly or monthly and revises while there is still time. First introduced: Chapter 24.
  158. controllable The controllable variables are the decisions the retailer actually gets to make, such as where to put the store, what to stock on the shelf, what hours to open, and how many clerks to schedule on a Saturday afternoon. These are the levers the manager can pull when next quarter's numbers come in soft. First introduced: Chapter 12.
  159. controllers In the ZS consulting firm's five-class framework, controllers are the decisions about performance management and the operating cadence the salesforce runs against day by day. The class covers the service-level agreement between sales and marketing itself, the cadence at which leadership reviews the pipeline, the rules that decide when a deal advances from one stage to the next, and the criteria that disqualify a lead from the funnel. A firm that has no quarterly service-level-agreement audit is still making a controller choice, just by omission rather than by design, which is the path Wells Fargo took for years before the regulator finally arrived and forced the issue. First introduced: Chapter 23.
  160. controlling idea A controlling idea is a single declarative sentence written in IF-THEN form that names the value a brand story produces and the cause that brings the value about (for example, IF Stanley's cup survives a car fire, THEN the brand is unbreakable in a way the owner can publicly attest to). Marketers use the controlling idea as a diagnostic for whether a campaign has narrative coherence across iterations: if every piece of content the brand publishes can be traced to the same IF-THEN sentence, the campaign is coherent; if different pieces require different sentences, the brand is producing noise that the audience cannot stitch into a single brand memory. First introduced: Chapter 19.
  161. convenience store A convenience store is a small store sited in a residential neighborhood or along a commuter route, open long hours or twenty-four hours a day, and stocking a limited line of high-turnover items at prices that carry a convenience premium over the supermarket equivalent. 7-Eleven, Circle K, and Oxxo are the global examples, and the shopper is paying the premium for the proximity and the speed of the trip rather than for any difference in the merchandise itself. First introduced: Chapter 12.
  162. conversation-intelligence transcripts Conversation-intelligence transcripts are the searchable text records produced by platforms like Gong, Chorus, and Salesloft Conversations, which record every sales call, transcribe it automatically, and index the transcript against a library of buyer-signal patterns the platform has learned from prior calls. Gong, the category leader, reached $300 million of annual recurring revenue across more than 4,500 enterprise customers by January 2025. First introduced: Chapter 23.
  163. Conversation Prism The Conversation Prism is a wheel-shaped infographic Brian Solis and the design firm JESS3 first released in 2008 to map the social-media landscape by use case rather than by company, with each spoke of the wheel naming a kind of conversation (publishing, photo sharing, live streaming, customer-service triage, niche community, and so on) and each platform plotted onto the spoke whose conversation it actually hosts. Solis and JESS3 redrew the wheel through five versions ending with v5.0 in 2017, retiring spokes whose platforms had died and adding new ones as new platforms arrived, and in December 2023 the same team shipped a successor called the GenAI Prism that carried the map-the-ecosystem method into the generative-AI landscape. The point for a brand team is that no single channel map stays accurate for more than two or three years, so the audit cadence has to be rebuilt against a fresh map every cycle. First introduced: Chapter 19.
  164. conversational AI monetization Conversational AI monetization is the revenue logic by which a platform running a large language model interface (ChatGPT, Claude.ai, Perplexity, Gemini) charges third parties to be named, recommended, or surfaced inside the model's reply to a user. The unit of monetization is the conversational turn, the same way the unit of search monetization on Google is the impression. When a buyer asks the model to recommend a tumbler, the slot in the model's reply where a brand name appears will eventually be priced and auctioned the way Google search keywords are priced today. First introduced: Chapter 4.
  165. conversion A conversion is the specific action the brand wanted the visitor to take, such as a purchase, a signup, a demo booking, or an app install. Conversion rate is the count of conversions divided by the count of visitors, usually expressed as conversions per hundred ad clicks, and it is the single most-watched outcome metric in any digital-marketing program because every other intermediate metric (impressions, clicks, sessions) is only worth what it produces in conversions. First introduced: Chapter 18.
  166. conversion-rate optimization Conversion-rate optimization is the second half of App Store Optimization, where the work is turning the user who has already landed on the listing into someone who actually taps the install button. The screenshots, the preview video, the app icon, the star rating, and the user reviews are the elements that drive this decision, because they are what the buyer sees once they have arrived at the page. A top-quartile subscription app typically converts 25 to 35 percent of the people who see its listing into installs, while a poorly designed listing on the same traffic can sit closer to 7 percent. First introduced: Chapter 14.
  167. Conversions API (CAPI) The Conversions API (CAPI) is Meta's server-to-server event API that sends conversion events directly from the brand's own server to Meta's ads platform, rather than relying on a pixel that fires inside the user's browser. The architecture bypasses both Apple's iOS ATT browser-tracking limits and any ad-blockers the user has installed. Brands typically pair the new server-side path with the older browser pixel for redundancy and deduplicate the two streams using a shared event_id parameter so Meta does not count the same conversion twice. First introduced: Chapter 18.
  168. convolutional network A convolutional neural network is a network designed to look at images. Instead of treating every pixel as independent, the network slides small filters across the image looking for patterns like edges, corners, and shapes, and it learns which filters matter for the task at hand. The same filter is reused at every position, which is why the network can recognize a face whether the face is in the top-left or the center of the photo. Yann LeCun's team built the first practical convolutional network for reading handwritten digits on bank checks in 1998. Fourteen years later, the 2012 ImageNet competition winner (a network called AlexNet, built by Hinton's graduate students at the University of Toronto) was a much larger version of the same architecture, and that single result is what kicked off the 2010s deep-learning boom. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  169. cookie lifetime Cookie lifetime is the number of days between when a third-party tracking cookie is first set on a buyer's browser and when it is finally retired, either because the buyer clears their cookies, the platform expires it, or a regulator caps its allowed duration. The 2018-to-2021 European panel of 54,127 users averaged a cookie lifetime of 279 days, which means the typical tracked buyer was identifiable across more than nine months of browsing before any reset. Regulatory caps under discussion in 2024-2026 would shorten this window to 90 or 180 days and directly reduce the data-asset value a personalized-pricing engine depends on. First introduced: Chapter 10.
  170. Cookies A cookie is a small file a website saves on the visitor's browser so the site can remember things about that visitor across page loads. First-party cookies, set by the site the user is actually visiting, remember logins and shopping-cart contents. Third-party cookies, set by ad networks that operate across many sites, are what Apple's Safari, Mozilla's Firefox, and (eventually) Google's Chrome all block or restrict, which is why the cross-site tracking that defined the 2010s ad industry has been collapsing for several years now. First introduced: Chapter 18.
  171. COPPA COPPA is the Children's Online Privacy Protection Act of 1998 (15 USC §6501 et seq.), with the FTC's implementing rule at 16 CFR Part 312. The core rule is that verifiable parental consent is required before any brand may collect, use, or disclose personal information from a child under thirteen. Per-violation penalties run up to $51,744 (the 2024 inflation-adjusted figure), and the 2025 amendments tightened the consent requirements for behavioral advertising and added biometric and health information to the regulated categories. Major settlement history sets the price tag: TikTok paid $5.7 million in 2019, YouTube paid $170 million in 2019, Epic Games paid $275 million for COPPA violations plus $245 million for dark-pattern practices targeting minors in 2022, and Microsoft Xbox paid $20 million in 2023. First introduced: Chapter 26.
  172. core competence A core competence is a capability that gives the firm access to several distinct markets, contributes meaningfully to customer-perceived benefit, and resists imitation by rivals over a multi-year horizon. The term comes from Prahalad and Hamel's 1990 Harvard Business Review article and is the inside-out counterpart to Porter's outside-in industry-structure view. First introduced: Chapter 3.
  173. core identity The core identity is the 3 to 5 timeless identity elements that stay constant as the brand extends into new categories or geographies. Every campaign brief and every product decision must reinforce these elements. The point of holding the core identity small is that a brand cannot defend more than five non-negotiable elements at one time, which means anything beyond that count is texture rather than core, and texture is allowed to evolve in a way that core is not. First introduced: Chapter 7.
  174. corporate branding Corporate branding is the discipline that treats the firm itself as the brand the customer encounters, rather than the firm operating behind product brands. Patagonia, Apple, IBM, and Unilever (for its corporate purpose communications) are corporate brands. The discipline's identity is built on values, heritage, employee culture, and stakeholder relationships rather than on a single product line, and the diagnostic question is whether the firm's external promises align with its internal reality. First introduced: Chapter 7.
  175. Corporate communications Corporate communications is the umbrella term for an organization's full communications stack, which sits above the individual functions of public relations, internal communications, executive communications, government relations, and investor relations. At companies large enough to need it, the whole stack reports to a chief communications officer (CCO) who in turn reports to the CEO, and at smaller companies it rolls up directly under marketing or under the CEO. The job of the role is message coherence, which means making sure the company says compatible things to every audience even when each audience speaks a different language. First introduced: Chapter 22.
  176. cosine-similarity Cosine similarity is a geometric measure of how similar two texts are in meaning, computed against the numerical embeddings of each text. A score of 1.0 means the two texts are identical in meaning, a score of 0.0 means they are completely unrelated, and a score of 0.88 means the passage and the query are clearly about the same thing. The metric is the standard way retrieval systems decide which passages to surface in response to a query. First introduced: Chapter 18.
  177. cosmetic customization Cosmetic customization is the quadrant where the underlying product stays identical across buyers but the surface (the color, the printed name, the packaging) is personalized so each buyer feels the item is theirs. Stanley's colorway drops, the printed initials on M&Ms, and the named bottles in Coca-Cola's Share-a-Coke campaign all fit this pattern. The premium buyers pay is mostly identity-signaling rather than functional improvement, so the engagement loop has to be sustained by a steady cadence of new surface variants the buyer wants to collect or refresh. First introduced: Chapter 8.
  178. Cost-based pricing Cost-based pricing sets the selling price by adding a markup to what the unit cost the firm to produce or acquire. A $60 cost with a 50 percent markup becomes a $90 sticker. The method is simple and easy to defend to a regulator or an accountant, but it ignores both what the buyer is actually willing to pay and what the competition is charging next door. First introduced: Chapter 10.
  179. cost of delay Cost of delay is the dollar value a firm forfeits for every week a product-development project is delayed, introduced by Donald Reinertsen in his 2009 book The Principles of Product Development Flow. The calculation estimates what the eventual launch would have earned per week at full ramp, attaches that weekly number to every gate review, and forces scope-versus-schedule debates to be settled in dollars rather than in personal preferences. A project earning 200,000 dollars per week at full ramp has a 200,000-dollar cost-of-delay number that gets multiplied by every week added to the schedule. Reinertsen's full framework is 175 numbered principles organizing product-development economics around queues, batch size, work-in-process limits, and cadence and synchronization across the pipeline. First introduced: Chapter 8.
  180. cost of goods sold COGS stands for cost of goods sold, and it captures the direct costs of producing the products the firm actually shipped during the period, including raw materials, the labor that made the product, and the manufacturing overhead the factory needs to run. A direct-to-consumer brand counts packaging and per-unit fulfillment in COGS, while a software firm counts hosting and per-user infrastructure, because what counts as a direct production cost depends on what the firm sells. When you subtract COGS from revenue, what you get is gross margin, the dollars the firm can spend on everything that is not the cost of making the product itself. First introduced: Chapter 25.
  181. cost per install Cost per install (CPI) is the dollar cost of acquiring one install through a paid advertising campaign. You compute it by dividing the total ad spend by the number of installs the campaign produced. Sensor Tower's 2025 benchmarks put CPI at 2.50 to 8.00 dollars on iOS in the US for non-gaming categories, 1.50 to 5.00 dollars on Android, with subscription apps running 4 to 12 dollars per install and gaming apps as low as 0.80 dollars on Android in lower-income markets where the auction is less crowded. First introduced: Chapter 14.
  182. cost per thousand impressions, also written as CPM Cost per thousand impressions, also known as CPM (the M is the Latin word mille, meaning thousand), is the dollar amount a marketer pays for every thousand views of a sponsored post or ad. You compute CPM by taking total spend, dividing by total impressions, and multiplying by one thousand, so a 500-dollar post that reaches 25,000 viewers has a CPM of 20 dollars, because 500 divided by 25,000 is 0.02 dollars per view (which is 2 cents per view), and 0.02 times 1,000 is 20. CPM is the single number that lets a marketer compare a creator buy against a TV spot, a Google ad, and a billboard on the same dollar axis. First introduced: Chapter 20.
  183. cost-to-satisfy Cost-to-satisfy is the total cost the buyer pays to obtain the value the firm is offering, not just the price on the tag. It decomposes into five sub-costs the firm has to track together: the money price itself, the time the buyer spends shopping, the search effort she expends comparing options, the post-purchase use cost (assembly, learning, maintenance, accessories), and the switching cost when she has been using a rival product. A firm that wins on money price while quietly losing on three of the other four can still lose the buyer overall. First introduced: Chapter 2.
  184. Costco Costco is a membership-based retail wholesaler with about 890 warehouses globally as of 2025 and roughly 250 billion dollars in annual revenue, which makes it one of the largest retailers in the world. The brand also operates one of the largest closed-circulation magazines in the United States: The Costco Connection has about 14 million subscribers, second-highest US magazine circulation behind only AARP, and the publication is available only to paying members with no advertising of the magazine itself. The brand returns as a worked example in chapter 9 on Distribution. First introduced: Chapter 17.
  185. Costly-signaling theory Zahavi's handicap principle imported into advertising: a signal is reliable to the extent that it is expensive to fake. The cost of placing a Super Bowl spot signals confidence and scale in a way a cheaper preroll cannot, regardless of what the spot itself says. First introduced: Chapter 16.
  186. Coulombe's Four Tests Coulombe's Four Tests are the four criteria Joe Coulombe, the founder of Trader Joe's, established for deciding which products the chain would carry. The first test is high value per cubic inch: the product has to be worth enough relative to the shelf space it occupies that the narrow store can afford to give it that space. The second test is high rate of consumption: customers have to repurchase regularly enough that the limited assortment turns inventory rather than sitting. The third test is that the product be easily handled, meaning the supply chain can move it without specialized infrastructure. The fourth test is that the product offer either outstanding price relative to comparable alternatives or outstanding assortment uniqueness the customer cannot find elsewhere. Together the four tests substitute for the algorithmic assortment-optimization tools larger chains use, because if a product clears all four tests the chain stocks it and if it fails any one the chain does not. First introduced: Chapter 26.
  187. Council of Europe Framework Convention on AI The Council of Europe Framework Convention on Artificial Intelligence (CETS 225) is the first binding international treaty on AI, opened for signature in Vilnius on September 5, 2024. It rests on three pillars: human rights, democracy, and the rule of law. Articles 7 through 13 name ten substantive principles signatory states commit to (including human dignity, transparency, accountability, equality, privacy, reliability, and safe innovation, plus remedies and impact-assessment duties). Treaty obligations cover the full AI lifecycle when a system is operated by a public authority or by a private actor acting on a public authority's behalf, and each signatory state has five years from ratification to translate the principles into national law. First introduced: Chapter 26.
  188. counterfactual explanation A counterfactual explanation, as Wachter, Mittelstadt, and Russell defined it in the Harvard Journal of Law and Technology in 2017-2018, is the minimal input change that would have flipped the decision. Instead of explaining the model's full logic, the counterfactual tells the affected person 'you would have qualified if your purchase frequency had been one order per month higher,' which is the form of explanation a person can actually act on. The technique is now operational in Microsoft DiCE, Google What-If, and IBM AI Explainability 360, and it is the industrial answer to the gap GDPR's right-to-explanation framework leaves in Article 22(3) for the actual contestability of specific automated decisions. First introduced: Chapter 26.
  189. Country of origin Country of origin (COO) is the bundle of mental associations a buyer carries about products that come from a particular country (French perfume is stylish, German cars are engineered, Japanese electronics are reliable). The associations are often factually inaccurate, but they still move willingness to pay because buyers use them as quick mental shortcuts at the moment of purchase rather than as carefully researched claims about the producer. First introduced: Chapter 15.
  190. CPA Cost per acquisition (CPA) is the ad spend divided by the number of conversions the spend produced. A $5,000 ad budget that produced 100 conversions has a $50 CPA, meaning each new customer cost $50 in ad spend to acquire. The metric only tells the marketer whether the campaign pays back when compared against customer lifetime value: a $50 CPA on a customer worth $500 over their lifetime is excellent, while the same $50 CPA on a customer worth $40 is a money-losing campaign. First introduced: Chapter 18.
  191. CPC Cost per click (CPC) is the amount the advertiser pays each time someone clicks one of its paid ads. A $1.20 CPC on a paid-search keyword means the firm pays Google $1.20 every time a searcher clicks the ad, regardless of whether the searcher then buys. CPC is an input into the return-on-ad-spend math, and it varies by auction density (how many advertisers are bidding on the same keyword) and by quality score (how relevant Google judges the ad to be to the search query). First introduced: Chapter 24.
  192. CPG Consumer packaged goods (CPG) are low-cost, frequently-purchased products sold through retail channels: snacks, beverages, packaged food, personal care, household cleaning, and similar categories. The category is where most of the brand-building empirical record this chapter draws on was generated, because frequently-purchased products produce the panel data the research methodologies require. First introduced: Chapter 7.
  193. CPM CPM stands for cost per mille (mille is Latin for thousand), and it is the standard pricing unit for paid media. A CPM of five dollars means the brand pays five dollars to have its ad shown to 1,000 viewers, regardless of how many of those viewers click or convert. A boosted Instagram post in 2026 typically costs four to eight dollars per thousand views, while a Super Bowl 30-second spot lands closer to 54 dollars per thousand, and an earned-media campaign that produces 1.7 billion impressions on a 100,000-dollar production budget runs below six cents per thousand. Lower CPM means cheaper reach. First introduced: Chapter 19.
  194. crawler A crawler is an automated program (Googlebot for Google, Bingbot for Bing, ClaudeBot for Anthropic) that visits every page on the public web it can reach and downloads the content into the engine's local index. The mechanic matters because a page the crawler cannot reach cannot be indexed, and a page that is not in the index cannot rank or get cited in an AI answer, no matter how good the content is. First introduced: Chapter 18.
  195. Creative Consistency Score The Creative Consistency Score, abbreviated CCS, is a thirteen-feature metric that System1 and the IPA published together in their October 2024 Compound Creativity study. The thirteen features fall into three groups. Foundations covers brand positioning, central creative idea, and agency tenure, on the premise that a brand that switches agencies every two years cannot accumulate consistency at the level of strategy. Culture covers wear-in patience, cross-channel application, showmanship, and asset reuse, which together measure whether the organisation has the discipline to hold a creative platform long enough for it to compound. Execution covers logos, brand voice, slogan, celebrities, characters, and soundtrack, which are the surface features any reader can audit on a single spot. A brand gets one point per feature it holds; the sum is its CCS for that period, and the top-quintile brands across the study produced 27 percent more very-large brand effects and 28 percent more very-large business effects than the median. First introduced: Chapter 16.
  196. creative multiplier The creative multiplier is Les Binet and Peter Field's name for the lift on long-term profit that an emotional brand campaign earns over a rational activation campaign of the same spend, drawn from the IPA Effectiveness Databank. The multiplier ran at roughly twelve to one in 2008, which meant the brand campaign returned twelve times the long-term profit of the activation campaign on the same dollar. It collapsed to roughly four to one by 2018 and toward parity by 2024, and the cause was structural rather than creative: the share of campaigns evaluated on short-horizon metrics rose from 10 percent to 25 percent over the same span, which cut the run-time the multiplier needs in order to land at all. First introduced: Chapter 16.
  197. creator A creator is a person who builds an audience by publishing their own video, audio, writing, or images on a platform somebody else owns, such as TikTok, YouTube, Instagram, Substack, or Twitch. The audience follows the creator (think Emma Chamberlain's 12 million viewers who would move with her to a new platform tomorrow), not the platform brand, which makes the creator different from a celebrity whose fame came before the platform and different from an employee whose work belongs to the company that signs the paycheck. First introduced: Chapter 20.
  198. creator-affiliate channel The creator-affiliate channel is the part of TikTok Shop where a creator receives a commission for posting another brand's product in a short video, a livestream, or a shop-tab placement, with the brand's own storefront fulfilling the actual order. The channel is distinct from a first-party storefront, where the brand operates its own TikTok Shop and posts its own content, and the fact that roughly sixty percent of TikTok Shop volume flows through the affiliate channel rather than the first-party channel is the empirical version of the claim that on social commerce the creator is the new shelf. First introduced: Chapter 12.
  199. creneau Creneau is a French military term that originally named a gap in a defended battle line, the kind of opening through which an attacking force could move without facing the dug-in defenders to either side. The positioning literature borrowed the word in the 1970s for an unoccupied position in the buyer's mind that a new entrant can credibly fill. Rolex's creneau was the expensive-watch position; Orville Redenbacher's creneau was the expensive-popcorn position; Michelob's creneau was the expensive-domestic-beer position. Each one cleared a rung on the buyer's mental ladder by claiming a price band the established leaders had not bothered to occupy. First introduced: Chapter 6.
  200. Critical Capability Levels (CCLs) A Critical Capability Level (CCL) is a specific AI capability that the lab considers so dangerous that crossing the level requires the lab to apply a defined set of mitigations before the model can be deployed. Examples include capabilities like systematically influencing political beliefs at scale, providing meaningful uplift to a bioweapon researcher, or resisting shutdown commands from human operators. Each CCL is paired with an evaluation protocol the lab runs against new model checkpoints, and a mitigation protocol the lab applies when an evaluation indicates the capability is present. First introduced: Chapter 26.
  201. Critical Few The Critical Few is Avinash Kaushik's name for the half-dozen numbers a leadership team actually steers the company with, as distinct from the twenty-eight numbers a default Google Analytics 4 report wants to show. Six is not magic; it is the empirical limit on how many things humans can hold in working memory while talking through a strategy review. Anything past the seventh tile dilutes attention away from the few that matter rather than adding more information. First introduced: Chapter 18.
  202. critical incident technique The critical incident technique (CIT) is a research method that collects narrative descriptions of specific service encounters from customers and codes them into recurring categories of what went right and what went wrong. The researcher asks the buyer to recall one particularly satisfying or dissatisfying encounter and to describe it in detail, then groups the resulting stories into types. The Bitner-Booms-Tetreault 1990 study used the technique on 700 airline, hotel, and restaurant encounters and showed that encounter-level performance drives overall service-quality judgments more than aggregate-attribute scoring does. First introduced: Chapter 9.
  203. Critical Mention Critical Mention is a specialized broadcast-monitoring platform founded in 2002 that captures television and radio mentions of a brand in addition to print and online coverage. It is the standard tool for brands whose category routinely gets discussed on cable news shows or terrestrial radio, where the conversation moves faster than print can keep up. Annual licensing starts around $10,000, which fits brands that need broadcast coverage tracked but do not need Brandwatch's full consumer-intelligence stack on top. First introduced: Chapter 22.
  204. critical uncertainties Critical uncertainties are forces whose direction within the planning horizon cannot be forecasted from current evidence and that carry material strategic consequence regardless of which way they ultimately break. Pierre Wack named the category in his 1985 Harvard Business Review article on scenario planning. Critical uncertainties become the two axes of the scenario-planning 2x2, because they are the forces where building a strategy that works under both directions actually matters. First introduced: Chapter 3.
  205. CRM Customer Relationship Management (CRM) software is the firm's central database of everything it knows about each customer. The CRM holds purchase history, support tickets, email opens, sales-call notes, and any other interaction the firm has tracked. Salesforce and HubSpot are the two dominant vendors; nearly every consumer-facing company runs one of them or a smaller competitor. First introduced: Chapter 4.
  206. cron Cron is the Unix scheduler that runs a script automatically at a recurring time, the way a calendar alert fires at the same hour each day. In the four-tier AI workflow, cron is what turns Tier 3 (a Skill the marketer types into a chat by hand) into Tier 4 (a script that Claude Code or Codex runs unattended every Monday at 09:00, posting its output to a Slack channel before the team meeting starts). Cron is the production substrate that lets the analyst remove themselves from routine runs while staying in the loop for the approvals. First introduced: Chapter 24.
  207. Cron job or GitHub Action Cron is the standard tool on Mac and Linux for running a command on a recurring schedule, named after the Greek word for time. The marketer edits a file called the crontab and adds one line per scheduled task, in the format 'minute hour day-of-month month day-of-week command' (so '0 9 * * MON /path/to/script.sh' fires the script at 9 a.m. every Monday). A GitHub Action does the same job on GitHub's servers, controlled by a YAML config file inside the repository. Both let a script run on schedule with no human typing during the run. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  208. cron schedule A cron schedule is a small scheduler built into Mac and Linux that runs a command at a fixed time without anyone clicking go. The marketer writes a one-line cron entry saying ‘every Monday at 9 a.m., run this script,’ and the operating system handles the rest. Windows offers an equivalent feature called Task Scheduler. The point of using cron is that the marketer sets the schedule once and the pipeline runs automatically from then on. First introduced: Chapter 5.
  209. Cronbach’s coefficient alpha Cronbach's coefficient alpha is the standard reliability statistic for a multi-item Likert scale. It is computed from the correlations among the items in the scale and ranges from zero (the items are unrelated to each other) to one (the items are perfectly redundant). Alpha is technically a lower-bound estimate of the reliability of the composite scale under the assumption of essential tau-equivalence, but the field treats it as a working approximation of the share of the variance in the scale total that reflects the underlying construct rather than random measurement error. Lee Cronbach published the statistic in 1951 in Psychometrika, and it has been the dominant reliability diagnostic for psychological and marketing scales ever since. First introduced: Chapter 5.
  210. cross-functional growth team A growth team is a cross-functional unit, codified in Sean Ellis and Morgan Brown's 2017 book Hacking Growth chapter 1, that brings together product managers, engineers, designers, data analysts, and marketers under a single growth lead with the explicit charter to run experiments that move the firm's North Star Metric. The structure is distinct from a traditional marketing department in two ways. First, the team has engineering and analytics built in, which means it can ship product changes and instrument them rather than handing requests across to engineering. Second, its scope runs the full funnel from acquisition through referral, not just the post-acquisition product surface a traditional product team would own. First introduced: Chapter 8.
  211. cross-tab A cross-tab, short for cross-tabulation, is a two-way table that counts how many respondents answered one way on one question and another way on a second question. The rows of the table are categories from the first variable (age band, gender, region), the columns are categories from the second variable (which brand the respondent said she prefers), and each cell shows the count or percentage of respondents who fell into that intersection. Cross-tabs are the bread-and-butter survey output and almost always the first thing a marketer reads after fielding a study. First introduced: Chapter 5.
  212. CSAT CSAT stands for Customer Satisfaction score, and it is one of the three standard service-quality survey metrics. The firm asks the buyer a single question right after the touchpoint, ‘How satisfied were you with this interaction?’ on a one-to-five or one-to-ten scale, and reports the result as the percentage of buyers who gave a four or five (or a nine or ten). CSAT measures emotional satisfaction in the immediate moment; its sister metrics are NPS, which asks whether the buyer would recommend the firm, and CES, which asks how much effort the interaction required. First introduced: Chapter 9.
  213. CSMs A customer success manager (CSM) is the post-sale role that owns the customer once the deal is signed. The CSM onboards the customer onto the product, drives adoption inside the customer's organization so churn does not happen at renewal, and identifies expansion opportunities (new departments, new use cases) that grow the contract value over time. The CSM is typically paid on retention and expansion metrics, not on new-account close rates. First introduced: Chapter 23.
  214. CSR Corporate Social Responsibility (CSR) is the umbrella term for a firm's voluntary commitments to social and environmental goals beyond what the law requires. Until the SEC's 2024 climate-disclosure rule landed, climate reporting sat inside CSR as a discretionary side-quest, but the rule moved climate-related risk and emissions reporting into mandatory financial disclosure on Form 10-K, which is a different and stricter regime than voluntary CSR communication. First introduced: Chapter 3.
  215. CSV A CSV (Comma-Separated Values) file is a plaintext spreadsheet where each row sits on its own line and each column is separated from the next by a comma. The format is the simplest one a spreadsheet program can export, which is why a model reads it cleanly. A file named feedback.csv with three columns might look like 'date,customer,comment' on the first line and '2026-05-01,jane@example.com,shipping was slow' on the next. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  216. CTV Connected television. Streaming-television inventory delivered through smart TVs and over-the-top devices like Roku, Fire TV, Apple TV, and the smart-TV-native apps for Netflix, Disney+, and the others, rather than through traditional cable or broadcast networks. The 2024-2026 share of US household television viewing on connected-TV exceeded the share on cable for the first time, which is why media plans started routing premium video budget through this channel. First introduced: Chapter 16.
  217. cue-context-response loop A cue-context-response loop is a learned association the brain forms between an environmental cue (a time of day, a place, a preceding action) and a behavior, built up by repetition with reward until the cue alone activates the behavior without conscious deliberation. Walking past the coffee shop on the morning commute fires the loop; the buyer is inside the shop ordering before they have decided to. Wendy Wood walks the mechanism in detail in her 2024 work on habit research. First introduced: Chapter 4.
  218. cultural appropriation Cultural appropriation, in marketing, is the use of a culture's symbols, language, sounds, or imagery by a brand outside that culture in a way that strips meaning, monetizes the surface, and excludes the originating community from authorship and benefit. The line is movement: are members of the originating community designing, narrating, and getting paid? First introduced: Chapter 15.
  219. cultural-appropriation alarm The cultural-appropriation alarm is the buyer's fast-system check on whether a brand has the right to use a culturally significant symbol, tradition, or aesthetic. The check runs on five features the buyer reads almost simultaneously: whether the brand has signaling rights to the symbol, whether anyone on the brand team comes from the source community, whether the use harms the source community, whether the historical context is acknowledged, and whether the brand gives something back in return. Lin, Kim, Uduehi, and Keinan named and measured the five-feature check in a 2024 paper. First introduced: Chapter 4.
  220. cultural consultant A cultural consultant is a paid expert from an originating community whose role is to assess whether a brand's use of cultural elements honors authorship, avoids stereotyping, and adds rather than extracts value from the community. Networks include Sensitivity Read services, Mama Glow (Black maternal health), Latinx in Marketing, and academic-affiliated practitioners. First introduced: Chapter 15.
  221. cultural orthodoxy Cultural orthodoxy (Holt and Cameron 2010) is the dominant ideological framing that a category's incumbent brands collectively broadcast. In bottled water, the orthodoxy through the 2010s was wellness-as-restraint (Fiji, Voss, Evian: pure, premium, glass bottle, healthful). In beer, the long-standing orthodoxy was working-class male sociability. The orthodoxy becomes increasingly disconnected from emergent cultural demand as the surrounding society shifts, and that disconnection opens the door to challenger brands. First introduced: Chapter 7.
  222. cumulative response rate A cumulative response rate is the product of two stages: how many people Pew or AmeriSpeak originally invited to join the panel actually agreed to join, multiplied by how many of those who joined actually completed the specific survey being measured. It is the number that maps to inferential validity, because it answers the question ‘of every hundred randomly selected adults the panel tried to recruit, how many produced data for this study?’ The cumulative figure is typically one to two orders of magnitude lower than the within-panel completion rate the vendor quotes in its pitch deck. First introduced: Chapter 5.
  223. cURL cURL, written lowercase-c uppercase-URL, is a command-line tool that lets a developer hit any web address from a terminal window, with full control over the request headers, the request body, and the authentication credentials sent with the call. The practical use in technical documentation is that Stripe and similar developer-tool companies include exact cURL commands the developer types directly into a terminal to send real test requests against the company's API. The advantage is that the developer can verify the integration actually works against the live service before writing any application code at all, which catches authentication problems and request-shape mistakes much earlier than the alternative of writing the production code first and debugging it second. First introduced: Chapter 18.
  224. curse of knowledge The curse of knowledge is the empirical finding that experts cannot simulate not knowing what they know. A brand team steeped in its own strategy hears its own messages as obvious and assumes outsiders will hear them the same way. The Newton 1990 tapper experiment measured the gap at roughly 47 percentage points: tappers predicted 50 percent recognition, listeners actually achieved 2.5 percent. The implication for brand copy is that the team's intuition about how clearly outsiders will read a message is systematically too optimistic. First introduced: Chapter 7.
  225. Cursor Cursor is an AI-augmented code editor built as a fork of the popular Visual Studio Code editor, which means the keyboard shortcuts and extensions a developer already knows from VS Code carry over. Its defining feature is sub-second tab prediction, where the editor guesses the next change the developer is about to make and offers it as a tab-to-accept suggestion. It comes with built-in access to both Anthropic and OpenAI models and a chat panel that can read the whole project. Pricing is $20 per month for the individual Pro plan and $40 per seat for the Teams plan. Cursor is the right 2026 tool when the developer wants to stay in the loop on every line, and most production teams pair it with Claude Code for the larger multi-file work. First introduced: Chapter 14.
  226. Custom GPT A Custom GPT is OpenAI's no-code interface for building a branded ChatGPT assistant without writing any code. The builder loads a set of custom instructions (the persona and rules), an optional collection of reference files the assistant reads through retrieval-augmented generation, and optional Actions that let the assistant call external APIs the operator names. The whole loop takes between five and ten minutes from concept to a working tool. Building a Custom GPT requires a ChatGPT Plus subscription, but anyone with the link can use one once it is published. First introduced: Chapter 27.
  227. customer acquisition cost Customer Acquisition Cost (CAC) is the dollar amount a brand spends to win one new customer, computed by dividing total marketing and sales spend in a period by the number of new customers acquired in that period. The number is most useful when compared against customer lifetime value, which is the total revenue the brand will earn from that customer over the relationship, because a channel only pays back when lifetime value exceeds CAC by a reasonable margin. First introduced: Chapter 3.
  228. customer acquisition cost (CAC) Customer Acquisition Cost (CAC) is the total marketing-and-sales spend the firm pays to convert one new paying user. If the firm spends $30,000 on ads in a month and acquires 1,000 new paying users, the CAC for that month is $30 per user. When CAC ends up larger than the lifetime revenue the user produces, the firm is paying more to acquire each customer than that customer will ever return, which is the upside-down unit-economics problem every consumer-software startup is built to avoid. First introduced: Chapter 10.
  229. customer asset value Customer asset value is the goal-layer dollar number of the 2026 marketing plan, calculated as the sum of customer lifetime value (CLV) across every active customer in the base, net of fixed costs. The label replaced ‘customer equity’ in 2020, because the earlier label did not subtract fixed costs and was therefore not equity in any finance sense. A firm with 145 million cardholders each worth $40 in CLV has a $5.8-billion customer asset before fixed costs and ownership structure adjust it. First introduced: Chapter 24.
  230. Customer-based brand equity Customer-based brand equity (CBBE) is Kevin Lane Keller's framework that defines the brand's value through what buyers know and feel about it, organized as a four-level pyramid with six blocks. The four levels rise from salience (does the brand come to mind in a category context) through performance and imagery, then judgments and feelings, to resonance at the apex where buyers actively recommend the brand. First introduced: Chapter 7.
  231. customer data platform A Customer Data Platform (CDP) is a packaged software system that listens to customer activity from every place the brand owns and records it as one continuous profile per buyer. The platform reads from server-side purchase events, client-side web and app analytics, in-store point-of-sale scans, call-center transcripts, loyalty-card swipes, and partner data feeds. It writes a single persistent customer record that downstream tools (email, paid-media platforms, on-site personalization, business intelligence dashboards) all read in the same shape, so the brand sees one buyer rather than six fragments. First introduced: Chapter 11.
  232. Customer-determined pricing Customer-determined pricing flips the usual price-setting direction so that the buyer names a price and the firm decides whether to accept it. Priceline's name-your-own-price flights are the consumer-facing version, Humble Bundle's pay-what-you-want game packs are a charitable-donation variant, and B2B reverse auctions are the procurement-side equivalent where a buyer posts a job and suppliers bid down to win it. First introduced: Chapter 10.
  233. customer-effort rating The Customer Effort Score (CES) is a service-quality survey metric that asks one question: ‘How much effort did it take to handle your request?’ on a scale from one (very low effort) to seven (very high effort). The empirical record from the Dixon-Freeman-Toman Harvard Business Review work is that the effort score predicts repeat purchase more strongly than either CSAT or NPS. In plain terms, a buyer who had to call three times to get a refund and felt the process was hard will leave the brand even if they ended up satisfied with the outcome, while a buyer whose refund happened in one click stays with the brand even if they never felt particularly delighted. First introduced: Chapter 9.
  234. Customer heterogeneity Customer heterogeneity is the variation among real customers in what they want, what they will pay, and how they behave in the category. Two buyers reaching for the same shelf at the same store can want completely different things from the same product, and the formal name for that fact is customer heterogeneity.
