Heterogeneity is the empirical precondition that makes segmentation necessary in the first place: if every buyer wanted the same thing, the firm could serve them all with one offer, but because buyers differ, the firm has to choose which differences to serve. First introduced: Chapter 6.
  235. customer indecision Customer indecision is the Dixon and McKenna 2022 label for the sub-population of no-decision B2B deals where the buyer already accepts that the status quo is broken and already accepts that the seller's solution would address it, but still cannot or will not commit to making the change. The right fix for indecision is active risk removal, which means the rep offers a pilot, a rollback clause, or a named success criterion that lets the buyer back out cheaply if the deployment fails. The wrong fix is to push harder on feature comparison or pricing, because the buyer is not stuck on whether the solution works, she is stuck on the cost of being wrong. The forty-four percent counterpart to the indecisive buyer is the buyer in genuine status-quo preference, who actually does prefer the current state and is not going to change, and that buyer needs the rep to disqualify her early rather than push, because pushing only burns the rep's time on a deal that was never going to close. First introduced: Chapter 23.
  236. customer journey Lemon and Verhoef's 2016 customer-journey concept describes the cumulative response a buyer has to a brand across her cognitive, emotional, behavioral, sensorial, and social channels. The journey runs across three stages (prepurchase, purchase, and postpurchase) and across four kinds of touchpoints (brand-owned like the website, partner-owned like a retailer, customer-owned like the buyer's own use of the product, and external like a friend's recommendation). The point is that the firm only controls some of the touchpoints, so designing the journey means designing how the brand reads across all four. First introduced: Chapter 2.
  237. customer lifetime value Customer lifetime value (CLV) is the total profit, in today's dollars, the firm expects to earn from one customer across the years that customer stays a customer. A customer worth $100 of margin per year who stays for ten years and is discounted at 8 percent has a CLV of roughly $720, whereas a customer worth $50 per year who stays only five years has a CLV of roughly $200, which shows how strongly both the margin per year and the length of the relationship swing the final number. First introduced: Chapter 7.
  238. customer pricing intervention Customer pricing intervention is a class of pricing structures in which the firm deliberately cedes some of the price-setting authority to the buyer, which Marco Bertini and Luc Wathieu introduced in their 2012 research and Bertini and Eva Ascarza extended in a 2014 MIT Sloan Management Review article. The forms include pay-what-you-want (Humble Bundle, some Radiohead album releases), name-your-own-price (Priceline), sliding-scale pricing (some yoga studios and software products), community pricing (a cooperative discount tied to the size of the joined group), and value-adjustment-line invoicing (the buyer can adjust the invoice within bounds after delivery). Letting the buyer participate shifts their attention from price-as-constraint to price-as-expression of their own valuation, which lifts the buyer's fairness perception without uniformly destroying the firm's margin. First introduced: Chapter 10.
  239. customer profile The customer profile is the bridge between a strategic segment definition and a buyable media target. Where the strategic definition might say 'women 25 to 44 who view hydration as identity expression,' the customer profile translates that into demographic, geographic, behavioral, and psychographic descriptors a media planner can actually buy: TikTok users in US metros above 250,000 population, household income above $60,000, prior engagement with wellness or fitness content. The profile is what makes the strategic segment operational rather than aspirational. First introduced: Chapter 6.
  240. customer relationship management Customer Relationship Management (CRM) is a software system that records every interaction a customer has had with the brand (orders, returns, service calls, email opens, store visits, app sessions) as one continuous profile per buyer. The point of a CRM is that the next interaction picks up where the last one left off rather than starting over, and the canonical 2026 platforms a marketing team actually buys are Salesforce, HubSpot, and Microsoft Dynamics. First introduced: Chapter 11.
  241. customer service Customer service in a logistics context is the bundle of operational moves the channel performs across three time windows around a single transaction. The pre-transaction window covers the channel commitments the buyer can read before placing an order, including a written customer-service policy, accessibility, organizational structure, system flexibility, and management services. The transaction window covers everything that happens between order placement and order receipt, including the stockout level, order-information visibility, the order cycle itself, expediting, transshipment, system accuracy, and order convenience. The post-transaction window covers everything that happens after the buyer has the product in hand, including installation, warranty, parts replacement, product tracing, and the handling of complaints, claims, and returns. First introduced: Chapter 11.
  242. Customer.io Customer.io is a marketing automation platform built for the case where the trigger that should fire a message is not a typical e-commerce event (like an order or a cart abandonment) but a custom event inside the brand’s own product, such as ‘the user finished onboarding step 3’ or ‘the user completed a session but did not return for 14 days.’ The platform accepts a continuous feed of every action users take inside the brand’s app or website, which engineers call an event stream, and lets those engineers define the triggers in code rather than through a visual interface. This is the right setup for software-as-a-service companies and mobile-app brands that need to fire messages on product behavior the marketing team cannot easily configure in a no-code editor like Klaviyo or Postscript. First introduced: Chapter 21.
04 // d

D D

  1. DACH region The DACH region is the German-speaking B2B market, made up of Germany, Austria, and Switzerland. Marketing-research firms group these three countries together because they share a language, similar buying conventions, and a long history of cross-border industrial supply chains, so a single sales motion typically covers all three rather than being designed country-by-country. First introduced: Chapter 23.
  2. DALL-E 2 DALL-E 2 is OpenAI's second-generation text-to-image model, released to the public in April 2022. The user types a description in natural language (for example, 'ketchup on a Renaissance table'), and the model generates an image matching the description. The model was the first widely-available text-to-image system, and its launch is the moment generative AI became a creative production tool in marketing teams rather than a research curiosity. First introduced: Chapter 18.
  3. dark patterns Dark patterns are user-interface elements designed to trick users into taking actions they would not otherwise have taken: pre-checked consent boxes, cancellation flows that require a phone call, opt-out links rendered as grey text below the fold. Harry Brignull coined the term in August 2010 and stabilized the 12-pattern catalog by 2014. By 2024-2025 the FTC, the OECD, and the European Data Protection Board converged on the synonym 'deceptive design' as regulators shifted to an explicit-deception frame. First introduced: Chapter 26.
  4. dark store A dark store is a retail-format warehouse that fulfills online and delivery orders only, with no walk-in customers ever stepping inside. The layout looks like a grocery store, but the operation is a fulfillment center: the aisles are arranged for fast picking by trained workers rather than for browsing by shoppers. Walmart treats "dark store" as a posture rather than a separate building, converting pieces of its physical-store network into ship-from-store fulfillment nodes during the peak demand windows when online demand spikes faster than the store can serve walk-in shoppers and online orders at the same time. First introduced: Chapter 11.
  5. dark-triad The dark triad is a cluster of three sub-clinical personality traits the academic-psychology literature names: Machiavellianism (strategic manipulation), narcissism (entitlement and grandiosity), and psychopathy (low empathy and impulse control). Paulhus and Williams introduced the cluster in 2002. The traits do not measurably predict sales misconduct under a clean compensation plan, but they amplify violation rates 5 to 12 times at the high-trait tail when the structural conditions described above are present. First introduced: Chapter 23.
  6. dashboard A dashboard is a single screen of charts and tables that rolls up the company's key operating numbers in near-real-time, so an executive can see weekly sales, returns, web traffic, and conversion rate without having to ask anyone to pull them. Dashboards are built on top of the data warehouse the company already has, and they refresh automatically as new transactions land. The three most-used dashboard tools in 2026 are Looker (for SQL-driven enterprise dashboards), Tableau (for ad-hoc visual analysis), and Power BI (Microsoft's competing platform, integrated with Excel and the broader Office stack). First introduced: Chapter 5.
  7. data-asset value Data-asset value is the firm-side revenue produced by a single tracked customer over the full cookie lifetime, summed across every personalized-pricing decision the data enables. The European panel measured an average of 2.52 euros per cookie, with the top 13 percent of cookies producing roughly four times that value. The data-asset value is the economic substrate every cross-site personalized-pricing engine runs on, which is why a regulatory cap on cookie lifetime cuts that value directly. First introduced: Chapter 10.
  8. data clean room A data clean room is a privacy-preserving compute environment where two or more parties contribute their first-party data, run an agreed-upon analysis against the joined data, and only see aggregated results that sit above a minimum-cohort threshold (commonly k equals 50 or k equals 100, meaning no row is reported until at least 50 or 100 distinct customers contribute to it). The raw underlying data never leaves either contributing party. The four canonical platforms a 2026 brand actually evaluates are AWS Clean Rooms, Habu (which LiveRamp acquired in January 2024), InfoSum, and Snowflake Data Clean Room. First introduced: Chapter 11.
  9. Data Cloud Data Cloud is Salesforce's unified-customer-data lakehouse, which joins records from CRM, Marketing Cloud, Commerce Cloud, and external sources into one customer record per buyer with shared identity resolution across all the silos. Once the customer record is unified, Data Cloud exposes it back to Agentforce as the long-term memory layer the agent reads at the start of every run, which is why a Service Agent answering a customer's question can see that the same person bought a product in March, opened a support ticket in April, and clicked a remarketing email in May without the operator wiring three separate APIs by hand. First introduced: Chapter 27.
  10. Data-flow alignment Data-flow alignment is the 2024 reframe of sales-marketing alignment as a data-integration problem rather than an incentive problem. The argument is that two functions cannot share a goal until they share the data that names whether the goal is being met, so the fix is to build a shared schema (typically mediated by the Model Context Protocol) where marketing's lead score and sales's pipeline view read and write to one record before the firm runs the offsite that rewrites the handoff contract. First introduced: Chapter 23.
  11. data-trust research The Anderson, Hansen, and Simester research stream out of the Kellogg School of Management documents that CFOs systematically discount marketing-team-reported metrics relative to finance-team-reported metrics drawn from the same warehouse, simply because the marketing-team numbers carry no audit trail back to the income statement. The fix the research prescribes is publishing each marketing metric in the format the finance team already uses (cohort retention shown as a survival curve, CAC presented as a unit-economic line, LTV presented as a present-value calculation), so the very same number the marketer cites in the deck is the number the CFO can pull from the warehouse and verify on the spot. First introduced: Chapter 25.
  12. data warehouse A warehouse here means a cloud database that holds the company's own transaction logs, customer profiles, and product events at scale (often petabytes). Snowflake and BigQuery are the dominant warehouses in 2026. For any consumer brand with a digital footprint, the warehouse is usually a richer source of insight than any survey, because it records what buyers actually did (purchases, page views, app sessions) rather than what they say they did when asked on a panel. First introduced: Chapter 5.
  13. data, analysis, reporting, message, action (DARMA) DARMA, an acronym for Data, Analysis, Reporting, Message, and Action, names the five sequential steps Avinash Kaushik traces from a raw signal to a marketer actually changing what they do. The diagnostic value of the framework is that most marketing dashboards die at step three (Reporting): the team produces beautiful tiles, but nobody is held accountable for the Message step where the analyst writes what the number means, so the Action step never fires and the dashboard becomes decorative. First introduced: Chapter 18.
  14. DataForSEO MCP DataForSEO MCP is the official DataForSEO TypeScript Model Context Protocol server (hosted at github.com/dataforseo/mcp-server-typescript) that covers four research modules: live SERP data, keyword research data, on-page audits, and the DataForSEO Labs research endpoints. The pricing is pay-per-request rather than subscription, which is what makes it competitive with the LLM-monitoring vendor stack for any brand running queries on demand rather than continuously. First introduced: Chapter 18.
  15. Dawn Dawn is Shopify's flagship free theme and the default theme that ships on every new Shopify account. It is built on the Online Store 2.0 architecture, which is a modular system where each page is composed of independent sections and the theme settings live in JSON templates the merchant can edit through the admin. Dawn powers over 268,000 active stores in 2026 and works for general retail across most categories, including candles, apparel, home goods, and food and beverage. This is the reference theme, and the candle-store student should start here unless the brand voice specifically calls for one of the alternative themes. First introduced: Chapter 13.
  16. day-1, day-7, and day-30 retention Day-1, day-7, and day-30 retention are the share of installed users who are still actively opening the app one day, seven days, and thirty days after their first open. They are the single best leading indicator of long-term revenue, because a user who is still around at day thirty is far more likely to become a paying subscriber than one who churned by day seven. AppsFlyer's 2025 Performance Index reports a global day-30 retention median of about 4.3 percent across non-gaming apps and roughly 8.7 percent for top-quartile subscription apps. The shape of the curve from day one to day thirty is itself diagnostic: a steep drop in the first week points to an onboarding problem; a gradual decline through the month points to a value-delivery problem. First introduced: Chapter 14.
  17. DCF Discounted cash flow (DCF) is the standard finance method for putting a single present-value number on a multi-year project. The procedure projects the cash the project will generate (or consume) in each future year, discounts each year's number back to today's dollars using the firm's cost of capital as the interest rate (because a dollar in five years is worth less than a dollar today), and sums the discounted yearly flows into one valuation. Suppose a Stanley Quencher line extension is projected to generate 40 million dollars of cash flow per year for five years. The project is not worth 200 million dollars today, because the DCF calculation first discounts each future year's 40 million back to its present-day value (using the firm's cost of capital as the discount rate), and only then adds the five discounted figures together to produce the project's actual present value. In this chapter, the DCF appears as a Gate 3 instrument the product manager updates whenever the development cost, the schedule, the manufacturing cost, or the sales-volume forecasts change. First introduced: Chapter 8.
  18. death spiral Binet's 2025 diagnosis: a self-reinforcing feedback loop in which a brand team's ROI focus drives quarter-over-quarter budget cuts, the budget cuts drive paid reach below the brand-building thresholds at which long-term sales effects can land, the missed thresholds reduce long-term profit, the profit decline triggers more efficiency demands from finance, and the efficiency demands drive further cuts. The loop ends only when the brand has cut deep enough that long-term profit collapses entirely, or when leadership breaks the loop by re-funding brand-building at the reach floor even though the short-term ROI looks worse. First introduced: Chapter 16.
  19. Decagon Decagon is an AI customer-service agent platform built on frontier large language models, such as Claude and GPT. Klarna deployed Decagon for its inbound customer-service chats starting in 2024, and the platform's pricing is per resolved conversation rather than per seat, which is a structural break from the seat-based licensing model that defined the 2010s software-as-a-service industry. First introduced: Chapter 18.
  20. decile A decile is one tenth of a sorted list, used as a way to slice the customer base into ten equal-sized buckets ranked from most profitable to least profitable. The top decile (the most profitable 10 percent of customers) typically produces 40 to 60 percent of the firm’s total profit, and the bottom decile is often unprofitable to serve at all once cost-to-serve is netted. Reading the deciles separately is the discipline that prevents the average customer from hiding the spread underneath it. First introduced: Chapter 24.
  21. decision interface Phil Barden's term for the gating layer where everything a brand has built upstream becomes a purchase choice: the packaging on a shelf, the search-results listing on Amazon, the menu board behind the counter, the click on a banner, the homepage of a direct-to-consumer website. The decision interface is the only surface the autopilot actually reads at the moment of choice, and most marketing organizations under-invest in it relative to upstream brand-strategy work. First introduced: Chapter 16.
  22. deck Deck is office shorthand for a slide presentation, usually built in PowerPoint, Google Slides, or Keynote. The deck is the deliverable a brand manager builds for an internal review meeting, where the typical pattern is one slide carrying one claim with one supporting number. A 20-page brand-tracker report becomes a 12-slide deck in which each slide answers one question the executive committee was going to ask anyway. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  23. decoy effect The decoy effect, which Huber, Payne, and Puto documented in their 1982 Journal of Consumer Research paper under the technical name asymmetric dominance, is the empirical pattern where adding a third option to a choice set shifts buyers toward whichever of the original two options dominates that third option. The classic example is the Economist subscription set, where adding a print-only option (more expensive than the digital-only option and identical to the print-plus-digital combo) shifts buyers toward the print-plus-digital combo because it suddenly looks like the obvious deal. The effect is among the most reliable findings in behavioral pricing, replicated across product categories from rental cars to laundry detergent to MBA-program tuition. First introduced: Chapter 10.
  24. deepfake A deepfake is an AI-generated or manipulated image, audio, or video that resembles existing persons, objects, places, or events and would falsely appear authentic to a viewer who did not know it was synthetic. Article 50(4) of the EU AI Act requires the brand deploying the content to disclose that it is AI-generated, and the obligation applies even when the content itself is otherwise lawful and inoffensive. First introduced: Chapter 3.
  25. Default discrimination Default discrimination is mode two of the New Jim Code framework: a system that inherits the world's existing inequities untouched because no countervailing correction was applied during design or training. Worked examples include Nikon's 2009 'did someone blink' eye-detection feature that flagged Asian users' open eyes as closed, and HP's 2010 webcam that failed to track darker-skinned faces because the training images underrepresented them. The marketing analog is a personalization model that surfaces fewer high-margin offers to majority-Black audiences because the training engagement data reflects historical under-investment in those audiences, which the model then perpetuates as if the under-investment were a fact about the audience rather than a fact about the data. First introduced: Chapter 26.
  26. deferral budget A deferral budget is the maximum time window the AI path is allowed to produce a confident, safe answer before the request must escalate to the human reviewer queue. The 2024-2026 production norm for customer-facing text is 30-60 seconds: the model has at most that long to clear the confidence threshold and the safety classifiers; any breach reroutes the customer to a human agent without further delay. The budget exists so the AI path does not stall indefinitely on hard cases, which is the failure mode that drove most early AI-customer-service complaints. First introduced: Chapter 26.
  27. Defined Defined alignment is the second rung of the Kotler-Rackham-Krishnaswamy 2006 ladder. Both functions have written down what each does, the boundary between marketing's job and sales's job is clear, and meetings happen on a predictable cadence. But each function still reports to its own scorecard, so when marketing's lead-volume number is green and sales's win-rate number is red, neither side feels accountable for the other's miss. The paper notes that most firms reach this rung and then plateau because the political cost of merging the two scorecards is genuinely high. First introduced: Chapter 23.
  28. definers In the ZS consulting firm's five-class framework, definers are the decisions about salesforce strategy, structure, sizing, and territory, which means the firm has to settle five questions before any rep ever picks up the phone: which customer segments the firm sells to, how the reps are organized (by geography, by industry, or by account size), how many reps the firm needs to cover its named accounts, and which accounts get assigned to which rep. Costco's decision to skip an outside salesforce entirely and run on member loyalty instead is a definer choice, and Patagonia's decision to run three parallel channels (wholesale to retailers, its own retail stores, and direct-to-consumer e-commerce) is a different definer choice. Both choices are upstream of every other sales-management decision the firm will make later, which is why ZS calls them definers. First introduced: Chapter 23.
  29. definitional perspectives The four definitional perspectives are the readings of generative AI as a technology category that Wang and colleagues proposed in a 2026 Journal of Personal Selling and Sales Management conceptual article. The technical-substrate reading defines the category by the large-language-model architecture with retrieval augmentation. The functional-output reading defines it by the multi-turn natural-language artifacts the tool produces (drafted emails, drafted call summaries, drafted proposal sections). The workflow-position reading defines it by whether the tool replaces an assistant-class capability with an agent-class one. The organizational-consequence reading defines it by the role redesign the deployment forces on the sales-development representative, account executive, customer-success, and revenue-operations seats. The four readings ask different procurement questions and produce different success metrics, so the procurement team has to pick which reading the vendor is selling against. First introduced: Chapter 23.
  30. deliberate Deliberate strategy is the term Henry Mintzberg and James Waters used in their 1985 Strategic Management Journal paper to describe a strategy the firm articulated explicitly before the action and then realized as intended. Liquid Death's death-metal positioning is the worked example, because Mike Cessario wrote it into the 2017 founding pitch deck and the brand still executes that same positioning eight years later, with the same voice, the same aesthetic, and the same sustainability frame Cessario named on day one. First introduced: Chapter 3.
  31. Deliberative alignment Deliberative alignment is a post-training method OpenAI published on December 20, 2024 (Wallace, Xiao, Leike, Weng, Heidecke, and Beutel). The method teaches a reasoning model to consult a written safety specification at inference time before producing its answer, so that the model's response is grounded in the spec rather than in whatever response pattern the reward model happened to prefer. The technique partially addresses the structural bias of RLHF by inserting a written reference document between the user's prompt and the model's answer. First introduced: Chapter 26.
  32. deliverability Deliverability is the probability that an email the brand sends actually arrives in the recipient's inbox rather than getting routed to spam, dropped into the promotions tab, or quietly bounced by the mailbox provider. It is distinct from open rate, which only counts the recipients who opened a message that already arrived. Deliverability sits underneath every other email metric, because an undelivered message has zero open rate by construction. A campaign with a beautiful subject line and brilliant copy can still flatline if the deliverability layer is broken upstream. First introduced: Chapter 21.
  33. deliverability tests The deliverability tests are the second triad in the brand-equity tradition's POD filter, layered on top of the desirable, deliverable, and differentiating tests that come first. These three additional tests ask whether the firm can actually operate the claim over time rather than just demonstrate it once. Feasibility asks whether the firm can produce the attribute at a cost the firm's price point can support, not just demonstrate it once for the brochure. Communicability asks whether the firm can convey the attribute through the media it can buy in language buyers parse without coaching. Sustainability asks whether the firm can defend the attribute against competitor encroachment over the multi-year planning horizon, because a POD that works for one quarter and fails to defend against a fast-follower for the next four is a POD that does not earn the brand a position. First introduced: Chapter 6.
  34. Delivery Service Partners A Delivery Service Partner (DSP) is an independent small business that operates Amazon-branded delivery vans on contract to Amazon. The program launched in 2018, and by 2026 roughly 2,500 DSPs operate together, employing about 200,000 associates and handling roughly 60 percent of Amazon’s U.S. last-mile package volume on regular suburban and exurban delivery routes. First introduced: Chapter 11.
  35. DELTA Plus DELTA Plus names the seven organizational capabilities a firm needs to climb the analytical-maturity staircase. The five original capabilities are Data (clean, integrated, accessible records), Enterprise integration (numbers tie across functions, not just inside marketing), Leadership (the CEO names analytics as a core competence), Targets (numerical goals worth measuring), and Analysts (the people who run the math). The two additions for the AI era are Technology (the warehouse, the pipeline, the model layer) and Analytical Techniques (the modern toolkit: causal inference, Bayesian methods, agent-augmented workflows). Each capability is something a firm builds over years, not something it buys in a quarter. First introduced: Chapter 24.
  36. demand generation Demand generation is marketing's top-of-funnel activity: producing content, running advertising, optimizing for search, hosting events, and otherwise creating reasons for target-profile buyers to register interest in the firm's product. The output of demand generation is a stream of leads (form fills, content downloads, webinar registrations), which marketing hands to the business development representative (BDR) team for screening into qualified leads. First introduced: Chapter 23.
  37. demographic Demographic segmentation cuts the market by who the buyer is rather than where they live or what they do, using variables like age, gender, income, household size, life stage, ethnicity, and generation. Demographic is the most legible of the four bases because the variables are public-record or census-derived, which means a media-buying system can verify them after the fact without surveying the buyer. The catch is that demographics describe who the buyer is rather than why the buyer buys: two thirty-year-old women in the same income bracket can want completely different things from the same product, which is why demographic cuts on their own often under-predict the choice the buyer actually makes at the shelf. First introduced: Chapter 6.
  38. Demographic parity Demographic parity is the fairness criterion that requires the same percentage of each protected group to be selected by the model, regardless of base rates in the underlying population. If a retention-discount campaign sends the offer to 5 percent of group A buyers, demographic parity asks whether the model picked 5 percent of group B too. Chouldechova in 2017 and Kleinberg, Mullainathan, and Raghavan in 2017 proved this criterion incompatible with the other two operationalizations of fairness when base rates differ. Demographic parity is the strongest criterion to apply when the decision class is offer distribution and being excluded from the audience is functionally being excluded from a benefit the brand chose to extend. First introduced: Chapter 26.
  39. department store A department store is a retailer that carries several distinct product lines, such as apparel, home goods, beauty, and accessories, under one roof and organizes them into named departments the shopper navigates between. JCPenney, Macy's, and Bloomingdale's are the U.S. examples, and the format is currently in the vulnerable phase of the wheel of retailing because the cost of running a full-line store at scale has drifted above what the mid-market shopper is willing to pay against Walmart and Target on one side and Amazon on the other. First introduced: Chapter 12.
  40. deprecation date A deprecation date is the published date on which a vendor will stop serving requests against a specific model identifier. After that date, calls to the retired identifier fail entirely and any script still pinned to the retired model breaks immediately rather than degrading silently. Anthropic typically announces deprecation about six months ahead of the actual cutoff, so a team that reads release notes once per quarter catches every deprecation cliff in time. The current active cliff: Sonnet 4 and Opus 4 both retire on June 15, 2026, after which any Cron job still pinned to claude-sonnet-4-20250514 stops running at midnight UTC on that date. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  41. descriptor profile The descriptor profile is the second stage of Haley's two-stage benefit-segmentation workflow, where the analyst takes the benefit-driven clusters the survey just produced and layers demographic, behavioral, and brand-preference variables on top of each one. The point of the layer is to make the benefit segments actually addressable in real media-buying systems: a segment defined only as 'buyers who want decay prevention' cannot be reached on Facebook or in a Nielsen panel, but the same segment described as 'parents aged thirty to forty-five with children in elementary school who already buy fluoride toothpaste' can be. Without the descriptor profile, the benefit segments are conceptually clean but operationally useless, which is why the workflow runs in two stages rather than stopping at the clustering step. First introduced: Chapter 6.
  42. Design for Manufacturing Design for Manufacturing (DFM) is a five-step engineering methodology that asks the design team to redesign the product itself to cut the cost of making it, codified by Geoffrey Boothroyd and Peter Dewhurst in the 1980s and brought into the engineering canon by Ulrich and Eppinger's chapter 13. The team systematically reduces the total parts count, simplifies the assembly steps a worker has to perform, and substitutes standardized off-the-shelf parts for custom-engineered ones, with the goal of cutting unit manufacturing cost by 25 to 50 percent. The IBM ProPrinter project in 1985 is the canonical case. The team cut the parts count by 79 percent (from 152 parts down to 32), dropped the unit cost by 50 percent against the prior printer generation, and accelerated assembly by an order of magnitude. Every one of those gains came from redesigning the printer itself rather than from squeezing the supply chain or pushing suppliers on price. First introduced: Chapter 8.
  43. diagnostic An attribute is diagnostic when it actually helps the buyer tell two options apart, meaning the brands in the category genuinely differ on that attribute. 'Tells time' is salient for a watch but it is not diagnostic, because every watch tells time. The marketer's lever is to find the attribute that is both salient and diagnostic for the brand, or to make a non-diagnostic attribute diagnostic by visibly setting a higher bar that competitors do not match. First introduced: Chapter 4.
  44. difference-in-differences Difference-in-differences (often shortened to DiD) compares the before-after change in a treated group against the before-after change in an untreated comparison group over the same calendar period. The method identifies the causal effect of the treatment under the assumption that the two groups would have moved in parallel through time absent the treatment (the ‘parallel-trends assumption’). Used for state-level policy changes, market launches, and geo-experiments where the treatment is assigned to one geography and a comparable untreated geography is available. First introduced: Chapter 5.
  45. Differentiated targeting Differentiated targeting is the coverage strategy where the firm serves multiple segments at once with offerings tailored to each, accepting the operational overhead of running tailored product lines, messages, and channels in parallel because the firm has the scale to recoup the cost. The General Motors Sloan ladder of the 1920s (Chevrolet, Pontiac, Oldsmobile, Buick, and Cadillac at five rising price points) is the canonical historical case, and Procter and Gamble's laundry-detergent portfolio (Tide, Gain, Cheer, Era, and Dreft, each targeting a different buyer benefit) is the modern parallel. The strategy is the standard play for mid-to-large firms because it captures more total profit than a single product could when buyer needs are heterogeneous enough that a tailored offer outperforms a generic one in every slice. First introduced: Chapter 6.
  46. differentiation Differentiation as a structural strategy means the firm offers something the industry recognizes as unique, which lets the firm charge a price premium that funds higher costs of producing the differentiated offer. Apple is the canonical example: the hardware design, the software ecosystem, and the retail experience together create a uniqueness the customer pays a premium for. Differentiation requires the firm to keep producing the unique elements faster than competitors can imitate them. First introduced: Chapter 24.
  47. difficulty Difficulty is an estimate of how hard it is to rank for a given keyword, based on how many other apps are competing for it and how strong their listings are. AppTweak and Sensor Tower normalize difficulty to a 0-100 scale where below 30 is easy, 60 to 80 is hard, and above 80 is the territory of the category leader where breaking in costs paid acquisition spend on top of any organic ASO work. First introduced: Chapter 14.
  48. digilog Digilog is the term Rishad Tobaccowala introduced in his 2020 book Restoring the Soul of Business for a company where digital tooling and analog people are integrated as complements rather than substitutes. The unmeasured layer of the business (story, taste, intuition, interaction) is held accountable alongside the measured layer (impressions, conversion, return on investment), because the unmeasured layer is what produces the value the measured layer is later trying to optimize. First introduced: Chapter 19.
  49. Digital Marketing and Measurement Model The Digital Marketing and Measurement Model, abbreviated DMMM, is Avinash Kaushik's five-step planning sequence that fixes the order of operations every digital-marketing campaign meeting follows before any data is pulled. The steps are business objectives, goals, key performance indicators, targets, and segments, and the discipline of running them in that order keeps the team from picking a metric before it has agreed on the business outcome the metric is supposed to measure. First introduced: Chapter 18.
  50. Digital Markets Act The Digital Markets Act (DMA) is the European Union regulation that became enforceable on March 6, 2024. It names seven large digital platforms as 'gatekeepers' and imposes rules on their core services in advance of any wrongdoing, rather than waiting to litigate after the fact the way U.S. antitrust does. Apple, Google, Meta, Amazon, Microsoft, ByteDance, and Booking are the designated gatekeepers as of 2024-2026. First introduced: Chapter 15.
  51. digital poorhouse The digital poorhouse is Virginia Eubanks's 2018 name for the administrative-software stack that automates eligibility decisions, prioritization, and risk scoring across US social-service programs. She names it in deliberate continuity with the 19th-century county poorhouse: the same project of sorting poor people into deserving and undeserving categories, run at greater speed and lower regulatory visibility than the brick-and-mortar predecessor. Her three-jurisdiction ethnography (Indiana eligibility automation 2006-2008, Los Angeles homeless-services scoring 2013, Allegheny Family Screening Tool 2016) is the empirical record that established the diagnostic. First introduced: Chapter 26.
  52. digital twin A simulated respondent built by feeding the model a single human’s demographics, attitudes, and prior answers, then asking the model to answer new questions ‘as that person.’ Toubia’s preferred term for the same idea Argyle called a silicon sample. Useful for piloting a survey on a 500-person twin set before fielding to humans; not yet safe as a stand-in for the human sample on a decision-grade study. First introduced: Chapter 5.
  53. digital twins A digital twin is an AI-generated likeness of a real person, alive or deceased, that can be deployed in new contexts the person did not actually appear in. The 2026 Interactive Advertising Bureau framework treats the two cases differently: digital twins of deceased individuals always require a consumer-facing disclosure, while digital twins of living individuals require disclosure when they are used in fabricated scenarios the person never actually participated in. First introduced: Chapter 4.
  54. Direct asking Direct asking is a survey procedure that simply asks the buyer to state the maximum price they would pay for the product, with no choice context, no competitive alternatives shown, and no consequence attached to the answer. It is the cheapest of the four reservation-price procedures to run and the least reliable of the four, because the buyer has every incentive to under-report a number they suspect the firm will use to set the price they personally have to pay. First introduced: Chapter 10.
  55. direct/assertive voice The direct/assertive voice is the traditional sales-closer register: louder, faster, and pushier than the default conversational tone. The Voss research finds it counterproductive in relational B2B sales because it provokes the exact defensiveness it is trying to overcome. The buyer's amygdala registers the pressure as a threat, the buyer's prefrontal cortex disengages, and the deal moves backward rather than forward. First introduced: Chapter 23.
  56. Direct Product Profitability (DPP) Direct Product Profitability, almost always shortened to DPP, is the true per-SKU profit the retailer keeps once the handling costs of receiving, storage, paperwork, picking, checking, and loading are taken out, along with the linear-foot share of rent the SKU consumes on the shelf. A slow-moving twelve-pack of niche flavor can post a healthy gross margin and still come out negative on DPP because the linear feet it occupies and the labor it absorbs cost more than the few units a week it actually sells, which is why the assortment cull runs on DPP rather than on gross margin. First introduced: Chapter 12.
  57. Direct-to-consumer Direct-to-consumer (DTC) is a zero-tier channel strategy in which the brand sells straight to the buyer through its own website, app, or stores, without any merchant intermediary in between. The 2010s DTC wave (Warby Parker, Casper, Allbirds, Glossier) reframed the playbook around digital-first acquisition using paid social ads to reach buyers cheaply, but the underlying channel structure is just zero-tier: the brand carries the cost of acquiring every customer because there is no retailer doing the acquisition work in its place. First introduced: Chapter 11.
  58. directive conversations A directive conversation, in the language Anthropic uses for its Economic Index reports, is a chat in which the user delegates an entire task to the model rather than co-producing the answer turn by turn. A user who types ‘write me a memo summarizing this report and send the draft back when you are done’ is having a directive conversation; a user who types one question at a time and refines the answer through back-and-forth is having a co-production conversation. The directive share is the Economic Index's headline metric for how often AI is being used to automate work outright (directive) versus to augment a human's own work (co-production). First introduced: Chapter 5.
  59. discount discipline Discount discipline, named by Reed Holden and Mark Burton in their 2008 book Pricing With Confidence, is the organizational mechanism that stops end-of-quarter discounting from destroying the firm's pocket-price band. The discipline operates at four levels, all of which have to be in place at once. The first level is the sales representative's mental model, which has to be built around the value habit rather than the discount habit. The second is the representative's compensation structure, which has to tie commission to pocket margin rather than to booked revenue so the representative personally feels the cost of every discount they grant. The third is the deal-approval workflow, which gives the representative a per-account discount budget they know before walking into the meeting. The fourth is the corporate calendar, where the last two weeks of every quarter produce the worst leakage and demand the tightest review. First introduced: Chapter 10.
  60. discount rate The discount rate is the interest rate used to translate future dollars into today's dollars. A dollar one year from now is worth less than a dollar today, and the discount rate is how much less the future dollar is worth in present terms. The rate reflects what the firm could earn on a safe alternative investment, like a US Treasury bill, with the dollar it would otherwise tie up. A 10 percent discount rate means a dollar arriving a year from now is worth roughly 91 cents today, because 91 cents invested at 10 percent grows to a dollar in a year. Finance courses cover the math in more depth. First introduced: Chapter 2.
  61. Discriminant analysis Discriminant analysis is a statistical procedure that treats the brand the buyer chose as a categorical outcome variable and the buyer's attribute ratings as predictors of that outcome. It then searches for the linear combinations of attributes that maximally separate the brands from each other in the rating space, the way a botanist would search for the combination of measurements that best tells one species of iris apart from another. The first two combinations the procedure finds become the map's x and y axes, which means the resulting map is explicitly tuned to push the brands as far apart as the data allow. Factor analysis asks 'what do the ratings have in common,' while discriminant analysis asks 'how do the brands differ,' and the two procedures often produce different maps from the same survey because they are optimizing different things. First introduced: Chapter 6.
  62. disruptive innovation Disruptive innovation is the term Clayton Christensen named in his 1995 Harvard Business Review article and his 1997 book The Innovator's Dilemma. The term describes the entry of a lower-end or new-market product whose business model is structurally different enough from the incumbents that the incumbents cannot effectively compete against it on the entrant's terms. Netflix's DVD-by-mail and then streaming model versus Blockbuster's retail-storefront model is the canonical worked example of the dynamic in action. First introduced: Chapter 3.
  63. Distillation Distillation is a model-training technique where a large 'teacher' model generates training examples (a question, a worked-out reasoning trace, an answer) and a smaller 'student' model is fine-tuned to produce the same outputs on the same questions. The student inherits most of the teacher's behavior at a fraction of the parameter count and a fraction of the per-token inference cost. DeepSeek used R1 as the teacher to generate 800,000 reasoning examples, then fine-tuned smaller open-source bases (Qwen 1.5B/7B/14B/32B and Llama 8B/70B) on those examples. The resulting 7-billion-parameter Qwen distillation runs on a 16-gigabyte consumer laptop and scores 55.5% on the AIME 2024 math benchmark, surpassing the prior state-of-the-art 32-billion-parameter open model. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  64. distilled R1 checkpoints A distilled R1 checkpoint is a smaller model trained to mimic the reasoning behavior of the full DeepSeek-R1 model. The distillation process takes R1 as a teacher: R1 generates training examples (a question, a chain of thought, an answer), and a smaller open-weights model (Qwen 7B, 14B, 32B; Llama 8B, 70B) is fine-tuned to produce the same chains of thought on the same questions. The resulting smaller model copies most of R1's behavior at a fraction of the parameter count and a fraction of the per-token inference cost. The R1-Distill-Qwen-32B checkpoint runs at about $0.27 per million input tokens and $1.10 per million output tokens through third-party hosts (Together.ai, Fireworks, DeepInfra, Replicate), against $15/$60 for OpenAI o1 at comparable benchmark scores. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  65. distinctive asset A distinctive asset is anything that lets a buyer identify the brand without seeing its name. A color like Tiffany blue, a shape like the Coca-Cola contour bottle, a sound like the Intel chime, or a character like the Geico gecko all qualify. Each asset gets evaluated on two axes: fame (how many buyers link the asset to the brand) and uniqueness (how many of those buyers link the asset only to that brand and to no competitor). Trademark law protects distinctive assets so the firm can defend them, but it does not protect claimed differences in product attributes. First introduced: Chapter 2.
  66. distinctive-asset density Distinctive-asset density measures how many distinct branded references appear inside a thirty-second spot, where a reference can be a logo flash, a character on screen, the brand color held in frame, a jingle cue, or a slogan in voiceover. The System1 and Effies fusion study identified seven references as the inflection point, which is the count at which brand recall approaches saturation rather than continuing to climb with each additional cue. Seven references in thirty seconds works out to one reference every four seconds, and a continuous appearance of two seconds or more counts as one reference rather than as a hold-the-logo-on-screen workaround that would inflate the count without earning the recall. First introduced: Chapter 16.
  67. Distinctive Asset Grid The Distinctive Asset Grid is Jenni Romaniuk's 2018 measurement instrument for sorting any candidate brand asset onto a two-by-two of Fame and Uniqueness. Fame is the percentage of category buyers who can correctly name the brand when shown the asset alone, and Uniqueness is the percentage who name only this brand rather than any competitor that shares the same cue. The grid prescribes a different action in each of the four cells: the top-right cell (high Fame, high Uniqueness) earns continued investment and execution refresh; the top-left cell (low Fame, high Uniqueness) is the build candidate the brand should keep amplifying; the bottom-right cell (high Fame, low Uniqueness) is a category code the brand either differentiates from or de-prioritizes; and the bottom-left cell (low Fame, low Uniqueness) is the kill candidate the next brief should drop. First introduced: Chapter 16.
  68. distinctive-asset reactivation Distinctive-asset reactivation is the deliberate revival of a heritage brand asset that has been retired or de-emphasized, with the asset placed at the center of a new advertising claim rather than as nostalgia decoration in the background. The discipline is distinct from a rebrand, which replaces the brand's assets with new ones, and it is also distinct from heritage marketing, which signals that the brand is old without actually re-investing in the dormant asset itself. McDonald's 2025 McDonaldland Meal is the canonical worked case: Mayor McCheese had been retired since 1985 because of trademark litigation, and the reactivation lifted his name-recognition from below 25 percent to roughly 45 percent among 13-to-24-year-olds within four weeks of launch. First introduced: Chapter 16.
  69. distinctive asset tariff The distinctive asset tariff is the share of advertising spend that produces no measurable memory encoding because the ad's distinctive assets are weak or absent. It is a self-imposed tax: every dollar spent on inventory that fails the asset-on-frame-one test is a dollar producing no measurable memory trace, scaling to roughly $198 billion of US industry wastage in 2024 by the VCCP Media estimate. First introduced: Chapter 7.
  70. distinctive assets Distinctive assets are the visual, sonic, and verbal cues a brand owns uniquely, the elements that make the brand recognizable inside three seconds of exposure. The roster typically includes the logo, the color palette, the mascot, the sonic identifier (the jingle or audio sting), the silhouette of the packaging, and the signature voice or tagline. Distinctive assets crosspost across platforms in hours because they are visual and verbal facts, not platform-specific creative templates. First introduced: Chapter 19.
  71. distinctive brand assets A distinctive brand asset is a non-verbal recognition trigger that identifies the brand without arguing for difference, and Jenni Romaniuk's 2018 Building Distinctive Brand Assets is the canonical measurement instrument for the construct. The asset can be a color (Coca-Cola red, Tiffany blue, Cadbury mauve), a character (Tony the Tiger, the Geico gecko), a logo silhouette (the bitten apple, the golden arches, the Nike swoosh), or an audio signature (the Intel five-note bong). The asset is what the buyer's autopilot pattern-matches against in the half-second before her hand reaches for the shelf, and the brand's job is to refresh that trigger across years rather than to swap it out at the first creative-director transition. First introduced: Chapter 16.
  72. distinctiveness Distinctiveness in this measurement context asks whether the brand's category-entry-point link is uniquely held by this brand or shared with competitors. A unique link such as Coca-Cola owning 'sharing at a barbecue' retrieves this brand alone when the situation arrives, whereas a shared link such as every soda brand fighting over 'with pizza' produces category buying without picking out a specific brand, so the shared link does not grow the brand even when the buyer encodes it. First introduced: Chapter 7.
  73. Distinctiveness Grid The Distinctiveness Grid is a two-axis scoring tool that plots every candidate brand asset (a logo, a color, a tagline, a jingle, a packaging shape, a celebrity face) on prevalence and uniqueness. Prevalence is the share of category buyers who, when shown the asset, correctly attribute it to the brand. Uniqueness is one minus the share who would attribute the asset to a different brand. Strong assets occupy the upper-right corner where both prevalence and uniqueness are high, and the grid sorts the brand's asset portfolio so the team knows which assets to defend, which to refresh, and which to retire. First introduced: Chapter 6.
  74. distribution centers A Distribution Center (DC) is a warehouse that a manufacturer or retailer runs as a sortation node between supplier and store. The DC holds inventory just long enough to consolidate the inbound truckload and re-route the contents toward their final destinations, rather than storing the inventory for weeks or months. Walmart’s hub-and-spoke DCs averaged 124 miles to the 90 to 170 stores each one served, which is the geometry that made the cross-docking operation pay back. First introduced: Chapter 11.
  75. distribution centers (DCs) A distribution center is a warehouse a retailer uses to receive bulk shipments from manufacturers, hold inventory, build store-bound pallets, and dispatch trucks to individual stores. An automated DC replaces forklift drivers and pickers with conveyor belts, robotic arms, and an automated storage and retrieval system that stacks cases vertically and pulls them on demand. The defining capability of Walmart's new high-tech grocery DCs is store-ready pallet build: the machine retrieves cases from storage and assembles each store's pallet sorted by aisle department with fragile items toward the top, so the pallet can be cross-docked from truck to aisle with one less labor step per case. First introduced: Chapter 12.
  76. distributional collapse Distributional collapse is the Bail et al. 2025 term for the pattern they document: language models simulating survey respondents reproduce the population means almost correctly, but they compress the variance to forty to seventy percent of the human spread. The practical consequence is that confidence intervals computed from the synthetic sample are too tight by roughly that factor, and downstream A/B power calculations built on the synthetic numbers are wrong by a factor of two. First introduced: Chapter 5.
  77. divest To divest a product is to sell the product line, the brand, or the entire business unit to another firm, often a specialist that revitalizes orphan brands or a private-equity buyer that believes it can operate the product more profitably than the current owner. Procter and Gamble divested dozens of smaller brands (including Folgers coffee to Smucker's and Pringles to Kellogg's) in the 2010s to focus capital on its largest categories. The decision is the exit of last resort when the firm concludes it cannot run the product profitably. First introduced: Chapter 8.
  78. DKIM DomainKeys Identified Mail attaches a cryptographic signature to every outgoing email, generated by a private key that only the brand’s mail servers hold. The receiving server verifies that signature against a matching public key the brand has published in its DNS records. If anyone has altered the message body in transit, even by a single character, the signature stops matching and the check fails. DKIM was standardized as Internet Engineering Task Force RFC 6376 and pairs with SPF for stronger authentication; together they cover both who sent the message and whether it arrived intact. First introduced: Chapter 21.
  79. DMA The Digital Markets Act (DMA) is a 2022 European Union regulation that imposes obligations on large platform gatekeepers like Apple, Google, Meta, and Amazon. The Act's first round of non-compliance fines landed on 23 April 2025, when Apple was fined 500 million euros and Meta was fined 200 million euros for specific violations. The maximum penalty under the DMA is ten percent of the platform's worldwide turnover, which gives the regime real financial teeth. First introduced: Chapter 3.
  80. DMARC Domain-based Message Authentication, Reporting and Conformance is a policy the brand publishes in its DNS records telling receiving servers what action to take when a message fails SPF or DKIM. The three options are none (deliver anyway and just send a report), quarantine (drop into spam), or reject (refuse delivery entirely). DMARC also tells the receiving server where to send aggregated reports about all the messages that claimed to come from the brand’s domain in the last 24 hours, including any failed ones. DMARC was standardized as Internet Engineering Task Force RFC 7489. Gmail’s February 2024 bulk-sender rules now require a published DMARC record for any sender pushing more than 5,000 messages per day to Gmail addresses. First introduced: Chapter 21.
  81. DMAs A Designated Market Area, almost always shortened to DMA, is Nielsen's geographic unit for U.S. media buying. A DMA is roughly ‘the set of cities that one television station reaches with its signal,’ and there are about two hundred ten of them covering the whole country. The DMA is the standard slicing unit for any geographic experiment, because broadcast media buys, regional ad targeting, and most market-rollout decisions all happen at DMA granularity. First introduced: Chapter 5.
  82. DOI A DOI (Digital Object Identifier) is the unique permanent ID assigned to every academic article when it is published, such as 10.1126/science.abc1234. The DOI resolves to the article's permanent URL through doi.org, which means a real DOI types into the browser bar and lands on a real article. A DOI a model invented in a hallucinated citation does the opposite: it either fails to resolve at all, or it resolves to an unrelated paper the model stitched together from neighboring memories. Clicking the DOI is therefore the cheapest possible verification step a marketer can run on any cited claim, because the click takes one second and exposes the fabrication immediately. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  83. DOM The Document Object Model (DOM) is the browser's in-memory tree of every element on a page after JavaScript has finished running, holding every heading, paragraph, image, and button in a structured hierarchy the rest of the browser can read. A useful analogy is that the initial HTML the server sent is the recipe, and the DOM is the finished dish: the recipe describes what should be on the page, but the DOM is what is actually there after all the JavaScript has executed. The reader can right-click any page, choose 'Inspect,' and the developer panel that opens shows the live DOM as the browser sees it. First introduced: Chapter 18.
  84. domain authority Domain authority is a score from one to one hundred (Moz, Ahrefs, and Semrush each publish their own version) that measures how strongly a domain ranks across queries. The score is built from the backlink graph: a domain with many high-quality inbound links from authoritative sources scores higher, and shopify.com at a domain authority near ninety outranks a brand-new site at a domain authority of ten even on identical content because the ranking algorithm trusts the older, more linked-to domain by default. First introduced: Chapter 18.
  85. dose-response A dose-response relationship is a curve in which the outcome rises as the input rises, but only up to a peak, after which the outcome falls again. The same pattern shows up in pharmacology (the right dose of a medication helps, while too low a dose is ineffective and too high a dose becomes toxic) and in marketing (the right level of brand-message repetition is persuasive, while too little leaves no memory trace and too much produces wear-out and irritation). Hand-gesture intensity follows the same shape: moderate gesturing drives the most viewer engagement, while motionless hands under-engage and frenetic gesturing over-engages to the point that the gestures themselves become visual noise. First introduced: Chapter 7.
  86. double-barreled A question that bundles two constructs into one sentence, usually with ‘and’ or ‘or’. ‘How satisfied are you with the price and quality of our product?’ is double-barreled because the respondent who likes the quality but resents the price has no honest answer. The fix is to split the question into two items: one on price, one on quality. First introduced: Chapter 5.
  87. double-blind A double-blind study is one in which neither the respondent who is being tested nor the person actually handing the respondent the cup, survey, or product knows which version is the ‘test’ condition and which is the ‘control’ condition while the data is being collected. The reason this matters is that the person administering the study almost always knows something about which version is supposed to win, and that knowledge leaks through in subtle ways (tone of voice, eye contact, how a question is phrased) that the respondent then picks up on and unconsciously responds to. Hiding the assignment from both sides of the encounter removes those leakage cues, so the only thing the respondent is reacting to is the product itself rather than the administrator’s expectations about the product. The technique was borrowed from medical clinical trials in the 1950s, and it is now the standard rigorous setup for taste tests, package-design tests, and ad-effectiveness tests where the perception of the version under study is the entire thing the researcher is trying to measure. First introduced: Chapter 5.
  88. double jeopardy Double jeopardy is the empirical regularity that smaller brands suffer two penalties at once: they have fewer buyers than larger brands, and each of those buyers buys the smaller brand slightly less often than larger-brand buyers buy theirs. The two penalties move together, which means a small brand cannot escape its low purchase frequency through retention work because the frequency is what its size predicts. First introduced: Chapter 7.
  89. double-jeopardy law The double-jeopardy law is the empirical regularity that smaller-share brands have fewer buyers AND those few buyers are slightly less loyal than the buyers of larger brands. Both metrics fall together as share falls, so the small brand gets penalized twice on the same scoreboard. The strategic implication is that loyalty is mostly a consequence of penetration, not a separate lever the brand can pull on its own. A firm trying to grow by making existing buyers more loyal is fighting the wrong battle, because growth actually comes from getting more buyers into the brand, and the loyalty improvement follows from that growth rather than the other way around. First introduced: Chapter 2.
  90. double machine learning Double machine learning is a two-stage method for estimating a causal effect from observational data when the analyst needs to control for many possible confounders at once. In the first stage, the analyst uses any modern machine-learning method (a random forest, a neural network, or a regularized regression) to estimate two auxiliary models: one that predicts the outcome from the confounders, and one that predicts the treatment from the same confounders. In the second stage, the analyst combines those two auxiliary models into an estimator of the causal effect that is mathematically constructed to be robust to small errors in either auxiliary model. The result is a causal estimate that preserves the standard one-over-square-root-of-n statistical guarantees, even though the underlying confounder set is too large to handle with classical regression alone. First introduced: Chapter 5.
  91. Douglas and Wind Susan Douglas of NYU Stern and Yoram Wind of the Wharton School wrote the 1987 reply to Levitt called 'The Myth of Globalization,' arguing that convergence is real for technology-and-commodity categories but illusory for anything tied to language, social ritual, or local identity. McDonald's running 40,000 stores on a menu that adapts country by country is the kind of evidence Douglas and Wind had in mind when they pushed back. First introduced: Chapter 15.
  92. downsample Downsampling means cutting the file down to a smaller representative sample that fits inside the model's context window: every tenth row of a long CSV, the 200 most recent rows of a feedback log, or a stratified sample by date across the full file. The model now sees a slice that fits, and the marketer knows exactly what slice the model saw because they chose it. The undergrad version is the same move a junior analyst makes when a senior asks for a quick read on a quarter of survey responses: rather than reading all 8,000, they pick 100 representative ones and report what those 100 said. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  93. drip campaign A drip campaign is a pre-built sequence of messages that fires automatically when a buyer hits a defined trigger (signed up, made a first purchase, abandoned a cart, went quiet for 30 days, lapsed for 180 days). Each message in the sequence is sent on a fixed delay measured from the trigger event, not on a calendar date the brand picks in advance. The marketer writes each message once; the platform delivers it once to every buyer who hits the trigger, on the day that buyer hits it, however many weeks or months apart different buyers may be. Synonyms: automation (the platform-agnostic term), flow (Klaviyo's term), journey (Iterable's and Salesforce's term). All four words name the same mechanism. First introduced: Chapter 21.
  94. drip surcharges Drip pricing is the practice of revealing the total cost to the buyer in stages rather than all at once, with mandatory fees disclosed only late in the checkout flow after the buyer has already committed time and intent to the purchase. Hotel resort fees, Ticketmaster service fees, and airline seat-selection fees are the textbook examples; the buyer starts with a posted price of $89 a night or $40 a ticket and ends up at checkout staring at a total 30 to 50 percent higher. The FTC's 16 CFR 464 (effective May 2025) now requires lodging and ticketing firms to show the all-in total up front, more prominently than the component prices. First introduced: Chapter 10.
  95. Drugstores A drugstore is a specialty retailer that sells prescription drugs at the back-of-store pharmacy alongside health, beauty, and miscellaneous everyday items in the front-of-store aisles. CVS and Walgreens are the U.S. archetypes, and the format runs a regulated pricing regime on the prescription side, where the pharmacy benefit manager sets the prices, alongside an unregulated retail regime on the front end, where the chain can run the same EDLP or HiLo pricing decisions a supermarket would. First introduced: Chapter 12.
  96. DTC Direct-to-consumer (DTC) is when a brand sells its own product through its own website or social channels rather than through wholesalers and retailers. The allure is that the firm keeps the retail margin for itself and owns the customer relationship instead of renting it from a retailer. The cost is that the firm has to pay the marketing-and-shipping bill the retailer used to absorb, which means the brand has to be confident the relationship it owns is worth more than the distribution reach it gives up. First introduced: Chapter 2.
  97. dual-entitlement principle The dual-entitlement principle, named by Kahneman, Knetsch, and Thaler in their 1986 American Economic Review paper, says that buyers grant the firm two separate entitlements. The firm is entitled to defend its reference profit when its costs rise, so a posted price increase tied to documented input-cost inflation reads as fair. The firm is not entitled to capture extraordinary profit from a demand spike, so the same posted price increase tied to a heatwave or a hurricane reads as gouging. The mechanism explains why an identical dollar amount of price increase produces opposite fairness reactions depending entirely on which story the firm tells about why. First introduced: Chapter 10.
  98. dual-surveillance Dual surveillance, in Bruce Schneier's framing from Data and Goliath in 2015 and reaffirmed in 2025, is the observation that corporate data collection and government surveillance are not separate systems. The data a corporation collects to predict consumer behavior also flows, by statute or by breach, into government programs (FISA §702, fusion centers) and into criminal markets. The marketer who retains a behavioral profile is therefore building infrastructure that will eventually be visible to state agencies and to data thieves, regardless of the marketer's intent. First introduced: Chapter 26.
  99. DUMB test The DUMB test, also from Avinash Kaushik, asks whether a marketing objective is Doable by the team this quarter, Understandable to a non-marketer who reads it on a slide, Manageable by the team that owns the budget, and Beneficial enough to materially change the business if it is achieved. An objective that fails any of the four tests is a slogan, not an objective, and a campaign built around a slogan produces metrics nobody can act on. First introduced: Chapter 18.
  100. dunnhumby Retailer Preference Index The dunnhumby Retailer Preference Index is an annual ranking of US grocery chains that the customer-data firm dunnhumby publishes each year based on a composite of five attributes the chains are scored on: price, ease of shopping, assortment, in-store experience, and digital experience. dunnhumby was founded in 1989 in the UK and built the customer-data system underneath the original Tesco Clubcard, and the firm now publishes the index as the standard external scoreboard for grocery brand health that retailers and packaged-goods firms benchmark against. A grocery brand that sits in the top quartile of the index across all five attributes is one that has built mental availability across the full range of shopping occasions rather than only on price. First introduced: Chapter 6.
  101. dunning Dunning is the discipline of automatically retrying failed credit-card charges and emailing the customer with a new payment-method link, repeated until either the charge eventually succeeds, the customer updates her card, or the system closes the subscription as cancelled after a set number of attempts. This is a load-bearing piece of subscription economics because failed-card recovery alone accounts for 30 to 50 percent of subscription churn rescue across the industry. A subscription business without a working dunning flow is leaking revenue every month from customers who wanted to stay but whose expired card silently dropped them. First introduced: Chapter 13.
  102. Duolingo Duolingo is the language-learning app that crossed roughly 110 million monthly active users in 2025, which is the rough population of Mexico learning a language through one app every month. The brand is famous in marketing circles for two moves: the green owl Duo character whose voice carries the entire short-form social presence, and a TikTok account at @duolingo with about 14 million followers that has demonstrated how a single repeating character can build a social channel without celebrity endorsements or paid amplification. First introduced: Chapter 17.
  103. duplication of purchase The duplication-of-purchase law says that a brand's buyers also buy other brands in proportion to those other brands' market shares. If a competitor has 30 percent of the category, then roughly 30 percent of your brand's buyers also bought that competitor in the same period. The crossover follows category share, not similarity in taste, packaging, or positioning. First introduced: Chapter 7.
  104. duplication-of-purchase law The duplication-of-purchase law is an empirical generalization from the Ehrenberg-Bass Institute that says every brand within a category shares its buyers with every other brand in proportion to that other brand's size. The bigger the other brand, the more of its buyers also buy your brand, and vice versa. The practical implication is that Pepsi competes most with Coca-Cola not because of similar positioning but because Coke is the biggest source of any cola buyer in the market. The law predicts buyer-overlap percentages with high accuracy across categories from cars to soft drinks to detergent, and it tells the firm its real competitive set is whoever is biggest, not whoever sounds most similar. First introduced: Chapter 2.
  105. durable demand shock rather than a flash impression A durable demand shock is the pattern where a viral moment keeps producing measurable behavior (catalog streaming, follower growth, repeat plays, repeat purchases) for months after the original video itself has faded from feeds, instead of spiking once and vanishing the way a regular impression does. A 2025 working paper by Kim and Raj called Catching Lightning matched TikTok virality events against Spotify streaming data and found the post-viral lift persists well past the viral video, with the largest effects for artists who had small pre-virality bases and for those who shipped new material inside the viral window. First introduced: Chapter 19.
  106. dynamic capabilities Dynamic capabilities is the term David Teece, Gary Pisano, and Amy Shuen named in their 1997 Strategic Management Journal paper. The concept describes a firm's ability to integrate, build, and reconfigure internal and external competences in response to rapidly changing environments. The capability has three sub-components running in sequence: sensing (reading the environment), seizing (committing the firm to a move on the read), and transforming (reconfiguring internal structures so the move can actually be executed). The triad explains why two firms reading the same PESTEL scan can produce very different strategic outcomes. First introduced: Chapter 3.
  107. dynamic filtering Dynamic filtering is the 2026 model capability where the AI writes and runs short filtering scripts against the search results it just retrieved, keeping only the passages that are actually relevant to the query before loading any of them into the model's context window. The feature reduces token consumption (the team's API bill drops) and improves answer quality (the citations are more on-topic), which together is what makes a fifty-cornerstone-query weekly audit affordable to run. First introduced: Chapter 18.
  108. Dynamic pricing Dynamic pricing is the practice of changing the posted price in real time based on observed demand, time of day, segment, channel, or inventory level, rather than holding a single posted price until the next planning cycle. The 2024-2025 academic record produced a structural finding the chapter walks at length: independent Tabular Q-learning pricers, with no communication or instruction to coordinate, tacitly converge to supra-competitive prices, which raises Section 5 antitrust exposure regardless of whether any human at the firms intended the collusion the algorithm produced. First introduced: Chapter 10.
  109. Dynata Dynata is the largest first-party panel vendor in the marketing-research industry, holding roughly 70 million panelists across 88 markets. Unlike Cint, Dynata recruits and owns its respondents directly rather than routing through sub-panels, which lets it publish a single end-to-end data-quality whitepaper on its own field. A first-party panel is faster to audit but narrower than a panel exchange. First introduced: Chapter 5.
05 // e

E E

  1. 9-ending A 9-ending is a posted price whose final digit is a 9 ($39, $1.99, $1,099). The empirical regularity is that 9-endings lift demand relative to round-ending alternatives, even when the absolute number is higher than the rounded one. Anderson and Simester's catalog experiments document the lift at roughly 8 to 24 percent depending on category. The mechanism is that the 9-ending reads as a sale signal to buyers who do not directly comparison-shop, and the signal disappears for buyers who look up the actual number online before deciding. First introduced: Chapter 10.
  2. e-commerce Electronic commerce (e-commerce) is the practice of selling and fulfilling a product over the internet rather than through a physical store. For grocery in 2026, e-commerce includes Instacart-style same-day delivery from third-party platforms, retailer-direct delivery programs such as Walmart+ and Kroger Boost, and click-and-collect pickup where the buyer orders online and drives to the store to load the order into the trunk. First introduced: Chapter 11.
  3. E-E-A-T E-E-A-T is Google's acronym for the four qualitative properties its human quality raters score every page on: Experience (does the author have first-hand knowledge of the subject), Expertise (does the author have the credentials or skills the topic requires), Authoritativeness (is the source recognized as a leading voice on the subject), and Trust (can the reader rely on the page to be accurate and safe to act on). The framework is the standard Google uses internally to decide which pages deserve to rank near the top of search results, and the algorithm proxies each of the four letters using structured signals like author bylines, original-research markup, citation patterns, and HTTPS configuration. The full definition box later in this section walks each letter and the specific signals the algorithm relies on, but the short version is that a page without a named expert behind it now competes from behind against pages that show their work. First introduced: Chapter 18.
  4. earned media Earned media is the coverage the brand receives without paying for it: a press article in The Drum, a Reddit thread recommending the product, an organic social share, an AI engine citing the brand inside a synthesized answer.
