Earned media is the highest-credibility channel in the trio because buyers trust unpaid third-party endorsement far more than paid placement, but it cannot be bought directly; it has to be earned through the work the other two channels enable. First introduced: Chapter 18.
  5. earned-trend riding The marketing posture of detecting an organic social trend that already involves a brand asset and amplifying it through continued availability or modest creative reinforcement, without trying to author or control the trend itself. Distinct from astroturfing (which fabricates grassroots activity that did not exist) and from co-creation (which is brand-led participatory campaigning the brand authors from the start). McDonald's 2023 Grimace Shake response is the textbook case of earned-trend riding: the brand did not author the horror angle, did not legally pursue the creators who did, did not pull the shake from the menu, and did not absorb the trend into official advertising; the brand kept the shake on the menu for the trend's duration and the trend produced the highest US same-store comparable lift the brand had logged in a decade. First introduced: Chapter 16.
  6. ease of retrieval Ease of retrieval is the speed in milliseconds with which the brand comes to mind when a category-entry-point cue appears in front of the buyer. Researchers measure it through panel reaction-time experiments: a buyer sees a buying-situation prompt on a screen and types the first brand that comes to mind. Shape-first distinctive assets (a recognizable silhouette like the Coke contour bottle) retrieve roughly 200 to 300 milliseconds faster than text-first cues (a wordmark the buyer has to read first), which is fast enough to matter at shelf because the buyer is moving in real time. First introduced: Chapter 7.
  7. EBITDA Earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation, and amortization (EBITDA) is the firm's operating profit before subtracting financing costs, tax obligations, and the accounting depreciation on long-lived assets. EBITDA is used as a rough proxy for the cash a business generates from operations, which is why private-equity buyers and lenders both look at it before any net-income figure. First introduced: Chapter 23.
  8. eclectic paradigm The eclectic paradigm is another name for John Dunning's OLI framework, which predicts the foreign entry mode that minimizes risk given how the firm scores on three independent axes. Ownership covers firm-specific assets the company carries with it (like Coca-Cola's brand or Apple's industrial design), Location covers what the target country uniquely offers (market size, low labor costs, strong contract enforcement), and Internalization covers whether the firm gains more from running the foreign operation in-house than from licensing it out to a partner. First introduced: Chapter 15.
  9. EconML EconML is Microsoft Research’s open-source Python library for heterogeneous treatment-effect estimation, maintained by the ALICE project. The library wraps Double Machine Learning, causal forests, doubly-robust learners, and meta-learners (S-Learner, T-Learner, X-Learner) in a consistent API. EconML’s LinearDML and CausalForestDML classes implement the two-model fit plus the residual regression in a single call, including the cross-fitting step that protects the procedure against first-stage overfitting. CausalML, Uber Engineering’s alternative library, is more uplift-modeling-focused and ships Qini score computation built in. First introduced: Chapter 24.
  10. Economic Index The Anthropic Economic Index is a public report Anthropic releases periodically that takes a large random sample of anonymized Claude conversations, has Claude itself classify each one by the kind of job task it represents, and then aggregates those classifications to estimate which occupations are using AI for what. It is the closest thing the industry has to a longitudinal measurement of how AI is actually used in working contexts. First introduced: Chapter 5.
  11. economic-value-to-customer (EVC) pricing Economic-value-to-customer (EVC) is the total dollar amount a product is worth to a customer who would otherwise buy the next-best alternative. You build it by taking the alternative's price as the reference base, then adding a dollar premium for each attribute where this product is superior (durability, reliability, service, warranty), so the value statement is a sum the customer can verify rather than a number the seller invents. First introduced: Chapter 10.
  12. editorial calendar An editorial calendar is a planning document that lists every piece of content the team intends to publish over a defined period, with the publication date, the channel where each piece will go, the person who owns the piece, its current production status, its target audience, and which pillar topic it falls under. Most teams keep the calendar in Notion, Asana, or a shared Google Sheet, and some operations use Trello or a specialized tool like CoSchedule when the team grows large enough to need workflow automation. First introduced: Chapter 17.
  13. EDLP EDLP stands for Everyday Low Prices, Walmart's pricing strategy of holding steady low prices across the assortment rather than running periodic deep discounts. The U.S. version of the strategy paired aggressive vendor negotiation with selective below-margin loss-leader items that drew shoppers into the store; it was the loss-leader half of the strategy that Germany's Federal Cartel Office prohibited in 2000, leaving Walmart Germany without its core competitive weapon. First introduced: Chapter 15.
  14. EDLP (Everyday Low Pricing) Everyday Low Pricing (EDLP) is a strategy where a retailer charges a constant low price on most items with little or no promotional cycling. The promise to the shopper is that the price posted today is the price posted tomorrow, so the shopper does not need to clip coupons or wait for a sale. Walmart, Costco, and Aldi run EDLP. First introduced: Chapter 12.
  15. Ehrenberg-Bass Ehrenberg-Bass is the empirical-marketing research center at the University of South Australia, founded on the work of Andrew Ehrenberg and now led by Byron Sharp. The institute's panel-data research since the 1960s has produced most of the empirical regularities this chapter teaches, including the double jeopardy law and the duplication of purchase law. First introduced: Chapter 7.
  16. Ehrenberg-Bass Institute The Ehrenberg-Bass Institute is an evidence-based marketing research center housed at the University of South Australia, named after Andrew Ehrenberg and Frank Bass, two of the empirical-marketing researchers whose work the institute extends. It is the research home behind Byron Sharp's book How Brands Grow, which argues that brand growth depends on reaching the largest possible audience of light buyers rather than deepening loyalty among heavy ones. First introduced: Chapter 19.
  17. eight is great Eight is great was Wells Fargo's internal slogan and branch-bonus metric: open eight financial products (checking, savings, credit card, overdraft line, etc.) per household. The phrase was posted on every branch wall and tracked in real time on every dashboard. Branch tellers' bonuses, performance reviews, and continued employment all rolled up to this single number, which is the structural cause of the 3.5 million unauthorized account openings the chapter walks at the open. First introduced: Chapter 23.
  18. eight-scale Culture Map The eight-scale Culture Map is Erin Meyer's 2014 INSEAD framework that maps national cultures onto eight bipolar dimensions specifically tied to how people behave at work: Communicating, Evaluating feedback, Persuading, Leading, Deciding, Trusting, Disagreeing, and Scheduling. A free country-pair comparison tool lives at erinmeyer.com so a manager can plot the home and target country side by side before a launch meeting. First introduced: Chapter 15.
  19. elaboration likelihood model Petty and Cacioppo (1986). The model holds that persuasion runs through two routes. The central route engages when the buyer is motivated to think hard about the message and able to do so, in which case the argument's quality matters most. The peripheral route engages when the buyer is unmotivated or unable to think hard (which is most of the time for most product categories), in which case surface cues like source credibility, repetition, and pleasant feeling carry the persuasion instead. First introduced: Chapter 16.
  20. Elaboration Likelihood Model (ELM) The Elaboration Likelihood Model (ELM) is the standard cognitive framework for how persuasion works depending on the buyer's state. When motivation, ability, and opportunity are all high, the buyer routes through the central path and evaluates the actual arguments in the message. When any of the three is low, the buyer routes through the peripheral path and reacts to cues unrelated to the argument itself, like the music, the source's attractiveness, or how often they have seen the brand recently. First introduced: Chapter 4.
  21. electronic shelf label (ESL) An electronic shelf label (ESL), sometimes called a digital shelf label, is a small e-ink display mounted on the shelf edge where the paper price tag used to be. The price the shopper sees is the price the central system pushes to the label, so a price change that used to require a clerk walking the aisle with a tag gun now takes seconds and applies chain-wide. The link to shrink is indirect: the labor freed from price-tag re-papering gets redeployed to shrink prevention (more attendants per self-checkout lane, more inventory walks per shift), and the planogram correctness lifts at the same time. First introduced: Chapter 12.
  22. elimination by aspects Elimination by aspects is a decision rule the buyer uses when they have too many options to evaluate them all carefully. The buyer ranks the attributes by importance, sets a minimum cutoff on the most important one, eliminates every option that falls below the cutoff, then moves to the next attribute and repeats. The process is non-compensatory, meaning a high score on one attribute cannot rescue an option that failed on a more-important attribute. Tversky named the rule in 1972 and Bettman walked its operating signature in 1979. First introduced: Chapter 4.
  23. Embargo An embargo is the agreement between the PR person and the journalist that the journalist will not publish the story before a stated date and time. It exists so that several reporters can interview, fact-check, and write the same news in parallel, and then publish at the same moment, which makes the news arrive as a coordinated wave instead of a slow trickle. The arrangement is standard practice at outlets like the Wall Street Journal, but it is routinely broken by individual reporters who want to scoop their competitors, which is why a working embargo letter usually says ‘contact me before publishing’ rather than relying on the agreement to hold by itself. First introduced: Chapter 22.
  24. embed Embedding converts each chunk of text into a short list of numbers (called a vector, typically 1,000 to 3,000 numbers long) that captures the chunk's meaning. The trick is that chunks about the same topic land near each other in this vector space, so a chunk about Stanley cups and another chunk about insulated drinkware sit close together even though they share few exact words. At query time, the system embeds the marketer's query the same way and returns the chunks whose vectors are closest to the query's vector. The standard 2026 embedding vendors are OpenAI text-embedding-3, Voyage AI, and Cohere Embed v3. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  25. embeddings An embedding is a list of numbers, typically one to three thousand values long, that represents the meaning of a piece of text in a way the engine can do math on. Texts with similar meanings produce similar embeddings even when they share no exact words, which is the mathematical property that lets semantic search retrieve relevant documents based on what the document is about rather than which keywords it happens to contain. First introduced: Chapter 18.
  26. embodied-cue In cognitive psychology, an embodied cue is a physical movement, posture, or gesture that the brain stores alongside the words it accompanied. When the words come back later, the embodied cue comes back with them, and the joint memory trace is more retrievable than the verbal memory trace alone. The classic example is the speaker who says 'this product solves three problems' while holding up three fingers: a listener who hears only the words remembers fewer of them than a listener who saw the three fingers go up alongside the words, because the second listener encoded the claim through two channels at once. First introduced: Chapter 7.
  27. emergent Emergent strategy is Henry Mintzberg's 1980s reframing of strategy as the pattern that emerges from the actions a firm actually takes in the market, not the plan the team wrote down at an offsite. The 2025 cascade revisitation reconciles the two views in a single sequence. The cascade is deliberate when the team first writes it, and it becomes emergent as customer evidence revises the answers quarter by quarter. The revision cycle is what distinguishes a living strategy from a fixed document. A firm that writes a cascade once at an offsite and never revisits it has a planning artifact, whereas a firm that updates the cascade against the market evidence on a regular cadence has an actual strategy that adapts as the world moves. First introduced: Chapter 2.
  28. emergent misalignment Emergent misalignment, as Betley and colleagues documented in February 2025, is the phenomenon where fine-tuning a model on a narrow malicious task (such as roughly six thousand examples of subtly insecure code) produces broadly misaligned behavior across unrelated domains the fine-tune never touched. The model that came out of the insecure-code fine-tune refused innocuous tasks and produced deceptive answers on factual prompts, even though no instruction to deceive appeared anywhere in the training corpus. First introduced: Chapter 26.
  29. emotional-CEP An affective category entry point is a CEP whose retrieval cue is an internal emotional state rather than an external situation or social context. Loneliness at 11 PM is a CEP for ice cream and dating apps; pre-presentation anxiety is a CEP for caffeine and breath mints; sleep difficulty is a CEP for melatonin and meditation apps. Affective CEPs name a class of retrieval moments the Sharp and Romaniuk inventory of situational CEPs does not yet treat in full. First introduced: Chapter 4.
  30. empathy Empathy is the SERVQUAL dimension that captures the individualized attention the firm gives to each customer, meaning the buyer feels the staff actually saw them as a specific person rather than as the next ticket in the queue. The everyday way to see this is a hotel front-desk clerk who looks up from the screen and says ‘welcome back Mr. Garcia, we have you in the room you liked last time,’ versus a clerk who asks for the buyer’s last name twice and then reads the room number off the same screen the buyer just handed them. Buyers form their empathy impression from three specific cues: whether the staff uses the customer’s name during the interaction, whether the staff visibly remembers details from prior encounters or recognizes the buyer’s specific situation, and whether the staff slows down enough to ask what the buyer actually needs rather than running the standard script. Empathy is therefore the dimension that loyalty programs and CRM systems exist to scale, because remembering one buyer is easy and remembering ten thousand is what software is for. First introduced: Chapter 9.
  31. empowerment levels Empowerment levels (Bowen and Lawler 1992) are a four-rung scheme describing how much authority a service firm delegates to its front-line employees. The lowest rung is production-line, where the front line follows a fixed script. The next is suggestion involvement, where employees can propose improvements but cannot act on them alone. The third is job involvement, where the employee has authority to solve problems inside their role (the Ritz-Carlton $2,000 cap is the canonical instance). The highest is high involvement, where front-line employees share in operating decisions and firm performance. The optimal rung is conditioned on the firm’s strategy, the customer relationship, and the workforce’s capability, not applied as a universal rule. First introduced: Chapter 9.
  32. Encoding Encoding is the rule that tells a program which sequence of bytes in a file represents which character on screen. The British pound symbol takes two bytes in modern encoding but one byte in an older one; if the program reads the file with the wrong rule, the pound symbol shows up as a question mark or a garbled box. Most files made after 2010 use UTF-8, which represents every character in every written language. A CSV exported from older Windows Excel sometimes uses a different encoding, which is the most common silent cause of broken characters when a Mac opens the file. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  33. Endorsement Guides The Endorsement Guides, written into federal regulation as 16 CFR Part 255, are the Federal Trade Commission rules that decide when a paid social-media post counts as a legal endorsement and what the creator and the brand have to disclose. The 2023 revision (the first big update since 2009) said that a sponsored Instagram post is only disclosed properly if the creator uses the platform's Paid Partnership tag and also writes #ad or #sponsored at the very start of the caption, so a viewer can tell the post is paid before scrolling past the line break. A disclosure buried inside a hashtag stack after the ‘more’ link no longer counts and exposes the brand to a per-post penalty. First introduced: Chapter 20.
  34. endowment effect The endowment effect, which Daniel Kahneman, Jack Knetsch, and Richard Thaler documented in a 1990 Journal of Political Economy paper, is the empirical pattern where simply owning something raises a buyer's perceived value of it relative to the same object not yet owned. Their classic Cornell mug experiments measured this directly: the price at which mug owners were willing to sell ran roughly twice the price non-owners were willing to pay for the same mug. This is loss aversion applied to ownership, because giving up the mug now registers as a loss while acquiring the same mug for the first time registers only as a gain. First introduced: Chapter 10.
  35. Energy Energy is the fifth pillar of the Brand Asset Valuator (BAV), added by Y&R in 2003 after researchers noticed that some brands scored high on the original four pillars but still felt stale to buyers, and that staleness predicted share loss the original four pillars missed. The pillar captures the brand's perceived momentum, vitality, and forward motion. Mizik and Jacobson's 2008 study tested the five-pillar augmented model and found Energy joins Relevance as a statistically significant incremental predictor of next-year stock returns beyond what accounting controls explain. First introduced: Chapter 7.
  36. engagement surface The engagement surface is the retention layer inside the app itself: push notifications the brand sends to the user's lock screen, in-app messages shown when the user opens the app, plus the email and SMS the brand sends through its CRM (customer relationship management) system. This is the engagement stack the brand owns once the install has happened, and the place where day-7 and day-30 retention curves are won or lost. First introduced: Chapter 14.
  37. engine-quality layer The engine-quality layer is the retrieval and grounding infrastructure that decides which pages an AI assistant is allowed to cite, before the assistant writes a single word of its answer. The layer includes the underlying search index (Brave Search, Perplexity's closed index, Google's index), the grounding API that sits on top of that index, and the ranking signals (index coverage, content-extraction quality, JSON-LD parsing, content freshness) the grounding layer uses to choose which snippets to hand back to the assistant. A brand absent from the engine-quality layer is invisible to every assistant that retrieval API serves, no matter how well its pages are written. First introduced: Chapter 18.
  38. Engineered inequity Engineered inequity is mode one of Ruha Benjamin's four-mode New Jim Code framework: a system that is designed in a way that discriminates, even though no engineer ever typed a protected attribute into the feature set. The canonical case is Beauty.AI 2016, where the system selected forty-four winners from six thousand entrants and almost all of them were white, because the training set determined the outcome before any deployer looked at the leaderboard. The marketing analog is a 'premium-customer' classifier trained on historical zip-code-conditioned spend that mechanically downgrades majority-Black zip codes; the engineer never wrote `if race=='Black'` but the model's behavior is engineered inequity nonetheless. First introduced: Chapter 26.
  39. enlighteners In the ZS consulting firm's five-class framework, enlighteners are the decisions about which data, tools, and AI copilots the rep has on her desktop every morning, and the firm has to settle four specific questions: which customer-relationship-management database the rep logs activity into, which conversation-intelligence vendor records and analyzes her calls, which lead-scoring model decides whose leads she gets first, and which AI assistant drafts her outbound emails. The Outreach versus Salesloft procurement question the chapter walks at section 23.6 is a canonical enlightener decision, because the choice between those two vendors sets the rep's daily workflow for the next three years. First introduced: Chapter 23.
  40. environment The environment is the canonical-agent-framework name for everything the agent acts on during its run. For a 2026 marketing agent, the environment includes the live web (for competitor scans), the firm's customer-relationship-management (CRM) database, the brand-tracker survey results, and the team's Slack workspace. Specifying the environment explicitly forces the firm to name what data the agent is allowed to touch, which is the same as naming what data the agent is allowed to leak by mistake. The procurement consequence is that a tighter environment specification produces both safer and cheaper agents. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  41. environment variable An environment variable is a named slot in the operating system that any program running on the computer can read by name. The marketer sets ANTHROPIC_API_KEY once at the terminal ('export ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=sk-ant-...' on Mac and Linux, 'setx ANTHROPIC_API_KEY sk-ant-...' on Windows), and from then on any script that asks the OS for the value of ANTHROPIC_API_KEY receives it. The literal characters of the key never appear inside any file the marketer could accidentally share or commit to GitHub, which is the structural difference between this pattern and pasting the key into a script directly. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  42. environmental and social Environmental, social, and governance (ESG) commitments are not generic corporate-social-responsibility activities tacked onto a marketing campaign as decoration. In the Patagonia framing the chapter is using here, those commitments are treated as part of the product itself. A buyer who chooses a Patagonia jacket is also choosing the company's environmental record alongside the jacket, because the brand sells that record as deliberately as it sells the technical fabric. The buyer is paying for both, which means the firm's pricing reflects the cost of the environmental work just as visibly as it reflects the cost of the materials. First introduced: Chapter 6.
  43. environmental scanning Environmental scanning is the systematic, ongoing monitoring of environmental forces for early signals of opportunity or threat. The discipline operates as the marketing team's continuous radar, distinct from one-shot market research in that it runs on a regular cadence (typically quarterly), produces a diff against the previous scan, and is filed in a repository the executive team actually reads. First introduced: Chapter 3.
  44. episodic memory buffer An episodic memory buffer is a small bounded log (in practice a JSON-lines file in the repo or an in-memory list) where each entry is one prior trial's verbal reflection on what went wrong. Reflexion bounds the buffer to the last one to three entries so that the next trial's prompt does not blow past the model's context limit, and the next trial's prompt reads the buffer back as additional context so the agent can see its recent failure modes before it generates anything new. First introduced: Chapter 27.
  45. EPRG EPRG is Howard Perlmutter's 1969 acronym for the four mental orientations a firm can take toward foreign markets. Ethnocentric treats the home market as primary and foreign markets as extensions; Polycentric treats each foreign market as fundamentally different and run locally; Regiocentric groups markets into regions like EMEA, APAC, and AMER; Geocentric treats the world as a single market with local execution. The posture decides who inside the firm gets to make the foreign-market call. First introduced: Chapter 15.
  46. Equalized odds Equalized odds is the fairness criterion (Chouldechova 2017) that requires the model's false-positive and false-negative rates to be equal across protected groups, meaning the system makes the same kinds of mistakes at the same frequencies regardless of the group the affected buyer belongs to. The criterion is the right one for prediction-error-cost-asymmetric decisions where a wrong call carries a sharp consequence on the buyer: fraud-detection screening that freezes a checkout, content-moderation classifiers that suspend an account, churn-risk scoring that diverts a retention budget away, employment-platform spam filters that delete a posting. A model catching 60 percent of fraud in group A while catching only 30 percent in group B fails equalized odds even if the overall accuracy on the dashboard looks acceptable. First introduced: Chapter 26.
  47. era-flag Era-flagging is the working practice of dating any advertising framework to the medium it was forged in, so a brand manager in 2026 can tell which mechanics of that framework still hold and which assumptions need updating. Hopkins's 1923 keyed coupon was forged in the mail-order print channel, where the only feedback loop was a coupon mailed back to the company; the attribution logic still applies, but the assumption that one coupon stands in for the long-term brand-memory work does not. The discipline is to separate the two halves of every inherited framework, because almost every textbook framework was forged in a channel that no longer dominates the buyer's week. First introduced: Chapter 16.
  48. ESOMAR ESOMAR is the global standards body for the marketing-research industry, founded in Amsterdam in 1948. Originally its initials stood for European Society for Opinion and Marketing Research, but the organization has long since gone fully global and now publishes the field's international code of practice. ESOMAR's annual industry market-sizing report (currently titled the Global Insights Industry Report) is the standard citation for ‘how big is the marketing-research business in dollars’ in any academic methodology section. First introduced: Chapter 5.
  49. ESOMAR Online Sample Quality Guideline ESOMAR is the global standards body for the marketing-research industry, founded in 1948 (originally the European Society for Opinion and Marketing Research). Its September 2025 Online Sample Quality Guideline is the first industry-side document to name AI-generated responses as a category of fraud distinct from click-farms, and it lists the procedural minimums every panel-fielded study should meet: at least one trap question per fifteen substantive items, an open-end attention-check that requires a free-form answer, IP and device fingerprint deduplication, time-on-page floor and ceiling, and a pre-registered exclusion rule. First introduced: Chapter 5.
  50. Esteem Esteem is the third pillar of the Brand Asset Valuator (BAV) instrument. It measures the level of regard and perceived quality the brand has built, combining performance reputation (the product works as promised) with emotional warmth (the buyer likes and trusts the brand). Mizik and Jacobson's 2008 Journal of Marketing Research paper found that Esteem effects are absorbed by contemporaneous accounting measures, meaning the construct measures a real outcome but the information has typically already shown up in the firm's quarterly financials by the time the survey detects it. First introduced: Chapter 7.
  51. Ethical Impact Assessment The Ethical Impact Assessment (EIA) is the procurement document UNESCO's 2021 Recommendation on the Ethics of AI invented to give a procurement officer something concrete to demand from an AI vendor before a multi-year contract is signed. The assessment is ex-ante, which means the vendor fills it out before the deployment happens rather than after a problem appears. The document covers the full eleven-policy-action-area scope of the UNESCO Recommendation: ethical impact on stakeholders, data policy, environmental footprint, gender effects, culture, education, communication and information, economy and labor, health and social well-being, plus governance and international cooperation. Templates are available through the UNESCO Ethics of AI portal, and the assessment has been operationalized in Chile, the Republic of Korea, and several EU member states, which means a marketing team at any of those jurisdictions can ask the vendor for a completed EIA and expect to receive one. First introduced: Chapter 26.
  52. ethnocentric Ethnocentric is the orientation in which a firm treats the home market as the primary one and treats every foreign market as an extension of it. Headquarters makes most decisions, the home culture is the default reference, and the assumption is that what works at home will work abroad with minor translation. Walmart Germany was the textbook ethnocentric entry, and its $1B exit shows the failure mode. First introduced: Chapter 15.
  53. ethnography Ethnography is field observation in the buyer's own context. Instead of bringing the buyer into a research facility, the researcher follows the buyer into the kitchen, the car, or the office where the product gets used, and watches how the product fits into the buyer's actual day. The reason this matters is that buyers can edit what they say on a survey but they cannot edit a behavior the researcher watched happen. First introduced: Chapter 5.
  54. ETP Enhanced Tracking Protection, abbreviated ETP, is Mozilla's name for the Firefox browser feature that automatically blocks third-party cookies and any tracking domain on a list of known trackers the browser maintains. The feature reached the same default-block posture as Safari's Intelligent Tracking Prevention in 2019, which means that by the time Google was still publicly debating its own cookie-deprecation timeline, two of the three major browsers had already done the job for it. The practical implication for digital marketing is that a measurable share of all desktop traffic has been running without third-party cookies for half a decade, so any campaign measurement system that assumed those cookies still worked was always undercounting the Firefox and Safari segments of the audience. First introduced: Chapter 18.
  55. Etsy off-site ads Etsy off-site ads are the buyer-side Etsy traffic produced when the seller runs their own ads on Meta, TikTok, Pinterest, or Google to drive shoppers back to their Etsy listing. Etsy attributes the resulting sale to the seller's off-site ad only when the click and the eventual purchase happen on the same device within Etsy's attribution window, which is exactly the slice that Apple's App Tracking Transparency and Safari's Intelligent Tracking Prevention shrank, so the reported attribution column shrunk roughly thirty percent versus the pre-2021 baseline. First introduced: Chapter 18.
  56. EU AI Act The EU Artificial Intelligence Act (EU AI Act) is the first comprehensive AI law passed anywhere in the world, adopted by the European Union in 2024 and enforced in phased waves through 2026 and beyond. The Act applies to any firm whose AI systems reach EU users, regardless of where the firm itself is based, and it ranks AI applications by risk tier with stricter obligations on higher-risk uses. First introduced: Chapter 3.
  57. eval harness An eval harness is the code or platform feature that runs a fixed set of test cases against the agent on every change and scores each output, so the team can tell whether a new system prompt or a new model version made the agent better or worse. The 2026 production standard stacks three layers together: a hand-written golden set of five to fifty ideal-output cases, an LLM-as-judge rubric pass that scores each run against the rubric, and a sampled human spot-check on customer-facing outputs. None of the three layers alone is sufficient, which is why the standard runs all three. First introduced: Chapter 27.
  58. evaluator-optimizer The evaluator-optimizer pattern is an agent-design configuration where one model drafts an output, a second model evaluates the draft against a rubric, the first model revises based on that evaluation, and the loop repeats until the evaluator passes. Use this pattern when iterative refinement clearly improves the output (a memo, a coding scheme, a deck narrative) and the rubric the evaluator applies is specific enough to drive useful revisions. First introduced: Chapter 5.
  59. everybody trap The everybody trap is the failure mode Ries and Trout named in the 1981 positioning literature for a firm that refuses to choose which buyer to serve and tries to capture every buyer in the category. The trap is mechanical rather than moral: a brand that tries to mean something to everyone ends up meaning something specific to no one, because the buyer's mental ladder cannot make room for a brand whose claim is ‘we are good at everything for everyone.’ The standard cure is the same as the cure for any positioning problem: pick a target buyer, claim one specific thing about the brand, and accept the cost of the buyers who would have preferred something different. First introduced: Chapter 6.
  60. Everyday Low Pricing Everyday Low Pricing (EDLP) is a retail-pricing posture in which the retailer offers stable, persistently low prices on its assortment, instead of the alternative high-low approach where regular prices are periodically interrupted by deep promotional discounts. EDLP requires a structurally low cost base to sustain, and it trades short-term promotional traffic for long-term price-perception trust with the buyer. Walmart in the U.S. and Aldi in Europe are the canonical examples; the posture only works when the retailer can actually deliver the prices it advertises every single day. First introduced: Chapter 11.
  61. Everyday Low Pricing (EDLP) Everyday Low Pricing (EDLP) is a price-structure commitment where the firm holds posted prices roughly flat week to week instead of running them up and down through frequent promotions. Walmart, Costco, Trader Joe's, Aldi, and Lidl all run on EDLP. The operational test is that realized prices vary by less than 5 percent week-over-week and promotional incidence stays under 10 percent of category volume, which is why buyers learn to trust the shelf tag rather than wait for a sale that never comes. First introduced: Chapter 10.
  62. Evidently AI Evidently AI is an open-source Python library that the marketing-analytics team installs once and then uses to watch for three things the dashboard cannot catch on its own: whether the underlying data has shifted, whether the predictive models running on top of that data have started to mis-predict, and whether any large-language-model step in the pipeline has begun to hallucinate. The library was founded in 2020 and ships under the Apache-2.0 license, which means any team can run it on its own data without paying a vendor. By March 2026 the project had reached version 0.7.21, with roughly 7,500 GitHub stars and more than 35 million downloads from PyPI, the central registry where Python libraries live. In practice it is the observability layer that sits between the marketing-mix model and the Streamlit dashboard, flagging the problems early enough that the team can fix the input rather than rebuild the forecast. First introduced: Chapter 24.
  63. evoked set The evoked set is the older textbook phrasing for what is now called the consideration set. Both terms name the same thing: the small group of brands a buyer can actually recall at the moment of decision. The older phrasing survives in some textbooks; the newer phrasing dominates current research papers. First introduced: Chapter 4.
  64. EVPI EVPI (Expected Value of Perfect Information) is the maximum dollar amount the decision-maker should pay for research that perfectly resolves the uncertainty between two or more options. Picture a brand team about to commit to one of two new products for next year, where shipping the wrong one would cost roughly $200,000 in inventory write-downs and slotting fees, and the team currently believes there is a 30 percent chance the option it is leaning toward is in fact the wrong one. The EVPI for a research study that would resolve the uncertainty perfectly is the wrong-decision cost multiplied by the probability the current best guess is wrong, which works out to $200,000 times 0.30, or $60,000. That figure is the ceiling on what the firm should rationally spend on the research itself, regardless of how confidently the agency pitches the deck. A reasonable rule of thumb is to budget any single research study at roughly two to ten percent of EVPI. First introduced: Chapter 5.
  65. Excel pivot table A pivot table is the Excel feature that summarizes thousands of rows into a small grid of counts, averages, or sums grouped by category: drag ‘region’ to rows, ‘product’ to columns, ‘sales’ into the cells, and Excel does the math. The standard tool for ‘total revenue by product by region’ before AI assistants existed. First introduced: Chapter 5.
  66. exchange relationship Richard Bagozzi argued in his 1975 Journal of Marketing paper that the discipline's central object is the exchange relationship between parties, not the firm or the buyer or the product on its own. An exchange happens when two or more parties each possess something the other values, each can communicate with and deliver to the other, and each is free to accept or reject. Bagozzi also distinguished restricted exchanges (a direct two-party trade like a Trader Joe's checkout), generalized exchanges (a chain in which value flows from A to B to C and back to A, like a referral economy), and complex exchanges (a multi-party web with three or more actors trading at once, like a platform marketplace). The 2007 American Marketing Association definition, which names customers, clients, partners, and society together, inherits this complex-exchange category directly. First introduced: Chapter 2.
  67. exciters In the ZS consulting firm's five-class framework, exciters are the decisions about incentive compensation, which means the firm has to settle five questions about how reps get paid: the base-to-variable pay-mix ratio, the structure of the quota itself, whether accelerators kick in above quota, whether spiff programs reward specific short-term behaviors, and how the firm's recognition tiers (President's Club, Pacesetter, and so on) acknowledge the top performers. Wells Fargo's eight-is-great bonus and Costco's renewal-rate-only bonus are both exciter choices, but they were made under entirely different definer structures, which is why they produced such different cultures and such different outcomes. First introduced: Chapter 23.
  68. exclusive An exclusive is the deal where the PR person promises a single journalist the right to publish the story before anyone else, in exchange for that journalist's commitment to write a fuller, longer-form treatment than they would have produced from a generic press release. The Wall Street Journal exclusive on a CEO interview, for example, typically becomes a 2,000-word feature, while the same story released broadly might generate five short 300-word write-ups across the trade press. The brand trades reach for depth, and the reporter trades the obligation to do substantive work for the scoop, which is why both sides usually find the exchange worth making. First introduced: Chapter 22.
  69. expansion Expansion is the stage where an existing customer’s relationship with the firm widens, through upsell to higher-margin products, cross-sell into adjacent categories, or deeper engagement that lifts purchase frequency. In mature businesses, expansion is where most untapped revenue lives, because acquisition has already done its job and the cost of widening a relationship is lower than the cost of starting one. Costco’s executive-membership upgrade is an expansion product; Patagonia’s technical-gear adjacencies are expansion categories. First introduced: Chapter 24.
  70. expectation violation Expectation violation is the cognitive mechanism behind the AI-disclosure penalty for hyper-realistic virtual influencers. The realistic visual cues prime the viewer to infer they are looking at an actual human creator; when a disclosure label appears saying the creator is AI-generated, the new signal contradicts the inference the viewer just built, and the resulting dissonance suppresses engagement. The penalty is largest for the most photorealistic creators because they built the strongest expectation, and smallest for stylized or illustrated virtual creators because they never primed the human inference in the first place. First introduced: Chapter 19.
  71. Experience Providers Experience Providers (ExPros) are the seven tactical channels through which a firm delivers the five Strategic Experiential Modules to the buyer. Each channel is a place the firm controls and can intentionally design: Communications, Visual and Verbal Identity, Product Presence, Co-branding, Spatial Environments, Web Sites and Electronic Media, and People. First introduced: Chapter 7.
  72. experiences Pine and Gilmore’s 1998 Harvard Business Review article argued that value evolves through four stages, and each stage commands higher prices than the one before. The four stages are commodities (raw flour and sugar), goods (a packaged cake mix on a shelf), services (a bakery’s decorated birthday cake), and experiences (a Chuck E. Cheese party where the cake is part of a staged event). The canonical birthday-cake progression captures the dollar increments: the home-baked cake from raw ingredients costs pennies, the boxed cake mix costs about a dollar, the bakery cake costs ten to fifteen dollars, and the staged-experience party at Chuck E. Cheese costs over a hundred dollars. First introduced: Chapter 9.
  73. experiential consumption Experiential consumption is the mode of buyer behavior in which the buyer is consuming the experience itself as the product, rather than buying a product that solves a problem. The feeling, the look, the social moment, and the story the purchase fits inside are what the buyer is paying for, which is why a fifty-dollar Stanley Quencher in a limited-edition color sells out in minutes even though a four-dollar grocery bottle would carry water just as well. First introduced: Chapter 4.
  74. experiential-marketing Experiential marketing is Bernd Schmitt's 1999 framework (published in the Journal of Marketing Management while he was at Columbia Business School) that argues brands compete for sensations, feelings, thoughts, and actions across every touchpoint a buyer encounters, rather than only for cognitive associations the buyer might form abstractly. The framework names seven tactical channels (logos, communications, retail spaces, web presence, events, people, products) the firm has to engineer deliberately, because brand value accumulates through coordinated experience rather than through a single message. First introduced: Chapter 7.
  75. experimental attribution Experimental attribution is the fallback to response-model regression when the firm’s historical data is too thin or too autocorrelated to identify each channel’s effect cleanly. The procedure is to deliberately turn spend up in some markets or weeks and down in others (a holdout test or a geo-experiment), then measure the resulting sales gap as the channel’s causal lift. The trade-off is speed (a typical geo-experiment runs four to twelve weeks before the answer stabilizes), but the result is causally clean in a way that even the best regression cannot guarantee. First introduced: Chapter 24.
  76. Experimentation cadence The growth-experimentation cadence is the weekly rhythm a growth team runs against itself, where the team writes hypotheses, designs tests, runs them, reviews the results, and ships the winners on a repeating cycle. The reason the rhythm matters more than any single experiment is that a quarter of disciplined weekly cycles produces a learning curve no individual test can produce on its own, because each cycle compounds the lessons of the cycle before it. The default Reforge plays this as one Tuesday review meeting per week, where each hypothesis is scored on a one-to-five quality scale before the test ever runs and a pre-mortem (a written prediction of how the test could fail) is filed before launch, which forces the team to interrogate its own thinking before the data arrives. First introduced: Chapter 23.
  77. expert power Expert power is the leverage a channel member holds when it controls specialized knowledge or technical capability that the partner depends on. The capability might be product training, application engineering for complex industrial sales, market intelligence the partner could not gather alone, product-design support, or after-sales technical service. A medical-device manufacturer that trains hospital clinicians on a new surgical implant holds expert power, because the surgeon cannot deploy the device safely without the training the manufacturer provides. First introduced: Chapter 11.
  78. exploratory factor analysis Exploratory factor analysis (EFA) is a statistical procedure that takes a set of survey items and asks whether the pattern of responses across many respondents is consistent with a small number of underlying latent factors driving the answers. If the EFA returns a single dominant factor that explains most of the variance in the items, the scale is measuring one construct, which is what a multi-item scale is supposed to do. If the EFA returns two or more factors of roughly equal weight, the item pool is measuring two or more different constructs and needs to be split into two or more separate scales. EFA is the standard purification check that follows the item-to-total correlation pass in the Churchill 1979 paradigm. First introduced: Chapter 5.
  79. Expo Expo is an open-source platform layered on top of React Native that handles the native-build pipeline (called EAS Build), the over-the-air update pipeline (called Expo Updates), and a managed runtime that ships every common native module pre-wired (camera, push, location, biometrics, secure storage). The free tier covers most indie projects, with EAS Build paid tiers starting around $19 per month for higher-volume needs. Expo is the standard React Native runtime for indie and small-team mobile apps in 2026. First introduced: Chapter 14.
  80. extended identity The extended identity is the 4 to 7 supporting identity elements that supply texture and behavioral instruction such as tone of voice, visual codes, partner archetypes, and retail-experience cues. These elements can shift as the brand moves into new categories or as the surrounding culture shifts, without breaking the brand's promise. The core identity is the layer that does not shift, while the extended identity is the layer where the brand is allowed to update itself to stay current with its audience. First introduced: Chapter 7.
  81. extended thinking Extended thinking is a feature Anthropic shipped on February 24, 2025 with Claude 3.7 Sonnet that lets the model produce a visible reasoning trace before its final answer. The developer sets a thinking budget in tokens on the request, and the model emits one or more thinking blocks (the model's working scratchpad) before the text block the user actually sees. The extra tokens cost output-rate dollars, so a one-cent answer can become a five-cent answer with a generous thinking budget. The rule of thumb is that extended thinking helps on hard reasoning tasks (multi-step planning, a tricky budget reconciliation, a contested attribution-modeling decision) and does not help on summarization, classification, or rewriting. For most routine marketing work the budget should be zero. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  82. extension The file extension is the two-to-five-letter tag after the final dot in a filename, like .csv or .pdf or .xlsx. The extension tells the operating system which program should open the file (so double-clicking survey.xlsx opens Excel), and it tells an AI model what kind of content to expect inside the file. If the marketer hides extensions in her Finder or File Explorer, the operating system still reads the extension under the hood. She just loses the visible signal of what each file actually is, which is one of the most common causes of accidentally uploading the wrong file to a model. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  83. externality ledger The externality ledger is the accounting view that prices the public costs of brand-building (labor outsourcing, supply-chain opacity, public-space saturation, voice suppression) that the firm-side brand-equity canon has historically treated as zero. When regulators or buyers begin to price those costs back into the firm's risk register, the firms that built their brand by externalizing those costs face a re-pricing event the brand-equity dashboard cannot anticipate. First introduced: Chapter 7.
  84. extractable paragraph An extractable paragraph is the first eighty to two hundred words of an answer the brand writes on a page, shaped so a generative AI engine can lift the paragraph directly into its synthesized reply. The shape that works is a clear question at the top, a direct answer in the first one or two sentences, and named entities (brand names, dated facts, specific numbers) the engine can quote with attribution intact. First introduced: Chapter 18.
  85. extraterritorial-reach Article 2 of the EU AI Act establishes the law's extraterritorial reach, meaning the Act binds non-EU companies whenever they either place an AI system on the EU market or operate one whose output reaches a user inside the EU. A US-only brand whose AI personalization tool surfaces recommendations on the screen of a shopper in Paris is in scope by the time the Paris shopper sees the first recommendation, regardless of where the brand files its taxes; the legal test is where the output is delivered to the user, not where the company is headquartered. First introduced: Chapter 26.
  86. extreme-value or hard-discount store A hard-discount or extreme-value store is a retailer that carries a deliberately limited assortment, often only fourteen hundred to two thousand items in a footprint a supermarket would use for thirty thousand, and prices the assortment at a heavy discount because most of what it sells is its own private label rather than a national brand. Aldi, Lidl, and Dollar General are the U.S. and German archetypes, and the format passes its supplier-leverage savings through to the shelf so that the price the shopper sees is structurally lower than the supermarket equivalent on the same household item. First introduced: Chapter 12.
06 // f

F F

  1. fabricating a quote A hallucination is a confident, fluent statement the model produced that is not grounded in any source. The classic example is a fabricated quote with a fake page number that reads exactly like a real citation. Hallucinations come from the model filling a gap with token-frequency-plausible text rather than retrieving an actual fact, which is why retrieval-augmented setups (Section 4.6) cut them sharply. Always verify by asking for the literal source. First introduced: Chapter 5.
  2. FAccT FAccT is short for the ACM Conference on Fairness, Accountability, and Transparency, the leading peer-reviewed academic venue for algorithmic-accountability research since its founding in 2018 (originally as FAT*). The Mitchell et al. 2019 paper formalizing the model-card disclosure standard appeared here, and the conference is where most procurement-grade fairness frameworks (intersectional auditing, counterfactual explanation, bias bounty programs) were first published before being adopted by industry. First introduced: Chapter 26.
  3. Factor analysis Factor analysis is a statistical procedure that takes a large matrix of buyer ratings (each buyer in the sample rates each brand on fifteen to twenty-five separate attributes like clean, modern, friendly, expensive) and compresses it down to a small number of underlying factors that summarize the patterns in the ratings. The procedure looks for groups of attributes that move together: if buyers who rate a brand high on clean also tend to rate it high on modern and high on friendly, all three attributes get pulled together into one underlying factor the analyst can name afterward, such as 'atmosphere.' On a perceptual map the first two factors become the x and y axes, and each brand is plotted at the score it earned on those two factors, which gives the brand team a two-dimensional picture of how buyers see the category at a glance. First introduced: Chapter 6.
  4. fad A fad is a product whose sales rise and then fall on a short, unpredictable timeline because the demand is driven by social novelty rather than a durable buyer benefit. The product is distinguished from a trend by the absence of social, economic, or political grounding underneath the demand. A trend has direction and durability because something in the world keeps pushing buyers toward it, while a fad has no such underlying substrate and collapses once the novelty wears off. Pet rocks in 1975, Tamagotchi virtual pets in 1997, and the original Crocs clog before its multi-cycle recovery in 2011 are all canonical examples of fads that ran their cycle and dropped. First introduced: Chapter 8.
  5. Failure mode The most common reason a perceptual-map Skill produces an unreadable chart is that the axis the marketer specified is not actually a contrast between two opposite ideas. If both ends of the axis are unipolar (meaning both ends describe the same direction of the same idea, like 'premium' versus 'more premium'), every brand will score on the same side and the layout collapses into a line. The fix is to swap one end of the axis for a genuine semantic opposite the buyer actually weighs in the category, such as 'premium versus value' or 'traditional versus modern,' so that brands have somewhere to spread out on both sides of the axis. First introduced: Chapter 6.
  6. failure taxonomy The failure taxonomy is the five-mode map of how a 2024-to-2026 production agent typically breaks, with each mode living in a different layer of the loop so each one has a different defense. Hallucination cascade is the model-layer failure, where the agent invents a fact and then acts on it. Tool misuse is the tools-and-permissions layer, where the agent picks the wrong tool, the wrong argument, the wrong order, or the wrong scope. Goal drift is the planner-layer failure, where the agent silently re-formulates the objective under failure pressure. Reward hacking is the metric layer, where the agent's metric improves while the operator's intent does not. Public-facing safety failure is the deployment boundary, where the agent commits the company to something the team did not authorize. First introduced: Chapter 27.
  7. FAINT FAINT is a five-criterion lead-qualification framework used in nonprofit fundraising and channel sales: Funds, Authority, Interest, Need, and Timing. The first letter is Funds rather than Budget (as in BANT) because the buyer in these segments may not have a formal allocated budget line yet but does have access to funds she can direct to the right opportunity. The reorder matters because asking about a budget line that does not exist kills a fundraising call that would otherwise close on access-to-funds language. First introduced: Chapter 23.
  8. Faire Faire on the sourcing side is the same wholesale platform viewed from the buyer's perspective rather than the seller's. A small retailer or direct-to-consumer brand uses Faire to source product from independent makers and brands at wholesale prices, typically with 60-day payment terms on the first order and a free-return guarantee if the product underperforms. This is the mirror image of the Faire described in Section 13.9, where Faire was discussed as a place to sell. The sourcing use case is useful for a Shopify store that wants to test a complementary product line without committing the capital required to direct-manufacture the inventory. First introduced: Chapter 13.
  9. Faire Direct Faire Direct is Faire's program that lets sellers invite their existing wholesale retail customers onto the platform without paying Faire a commission on those orders. The retailer uses Faire's order-management tools, and the seller uses Faire's payment infrastructure to collect the money, but Faire takes no commission because Faire did not source that customer in the first place. The seller pays only the payment-processing fee, which runs from 1.9 to 3.5 percent plus $0.30 per transaction depending on how quickly the seller wants the payout to clear. The economic case for Faire Direct is using the platform as a free business-to-business order-management system, paying nothing for the infrastructure and only paying the standard card-processing fee. First introduced: Chapter 13.
  10. fairness Fairness in algorithmic decision-making is the absence of bias that creates disparate outcomes across protected classes (race, gender, age, disability). The hard fact every procurement team has to face is that the three intuitive operationalizations of fairness are mathematically incompatible when base rates differ across groups. Demographic parity requires the same percentage of each group to be selected. Equalized odds requires the model's false-positive and false-negative rates to be equal across groups. Calibration requires a score of 0.7 to mean the same probability of the positive outcome in every group. The brand cannot satisfy all three simultaneously, so the procurement gate forces an explicit choice of which criterion the deployment is optimizing and documents the trade-off. First introduced: Chapter 26.
  11. fairness calibration Fairness calibration is the rep's running internal estimate of how the firm actually values cross-functional contribution, and the estimate gets formed by watching what gets rewarded in compensation and recognition over the course of a year or two. When a rep whose internal-network centrality is high also reads her internal feedback as saying the firm genuinely pays for that centrality (through bonuses, promotions, or visible recognition), she stays at the firm and keeps investing in cross-functional relationships. When a rep whose centrality is equally high reads her internal feedback as saying the firm only pays for raw quota performance and treats the centrality work as unrewarded effort, she leaves, often to a competitor whose compensation structure explicitly recognizes the contribution she was already making for free. First introduced: Chapter 23.
  12. Fame Fame is the share of category buyers who recognize a cue at all when it is shown to them. A cue with high fame is one many buyers have encountered enough times to register it, whereas a cue with low fame may be beautiful in the brand book but invisible at shelf because most buyers have not seen it often enough to encode it. First introduced: Chapter 7.
  13. FAQPage FAQPage is the schema.org content type that wraps a list of question-and-answer pairs in JSON-LD format so search engines can lift the pairs directly into AI answers. The shape is the highest-leverage 2026 markup because the question-and-answer structure aligns with how generative engines synthesize replies; an engine looking for an extractable answer prefers a passage that is already formatted as one. First introduced: Chapter 18.
  14. Fast-moving consumer goods Fast-moving consumer goods (FMCG) is the category label for low-price, frequently bought items such as groceries, soft drinks, household supplies, and personal-care products. The label is more common in markets outside the United States; inside the United States, the same idea is called consumer packaged goods (CPG). Both labels point at the same industry: Procter and Gamble, Unilever, Nestle, and Coca-Cola are the canonical players, and the 50:30:20 buyer-share regularity Goodhardt, Sharp, and Romaniuk measured applies across the category. First introduced: Chapter 24.
  15. fastvertising Fastvertising is Maximum Effort's name for ads built and shipped inside the news cycle, with a target turnaround of twenty-four to seventy-two hours from trigger event to live asset. Earned media is the primary key performance indicator the playbook optimizes for; paid distribution is the trigger that gets the asset in front of enough viewers for the earned media to compound. The Reynolds-ChatGPT spot is the canonical fastvertising case because the trigger event was the public release of ChatGPT itself. First introduced: Chapter 18.
  16. FDA fair balance The FDA's fair-balance requirement is the prescription-drug advertising rule codified at 21 CFR §202.1 and §314.81, and it sets a single load-bearing standard for any direct-to-consumer ad that names both the drug and the indication (what the drug treats). The standard is that the ad must present the major-risk summary with prominence and frequency comparable to the benefit claims, so a thirty-second TV ad that spends twenty-five seconds celebrating the drug's effect and five seconds reading the risks at high speed and low volume fails the rule. Two safe-harbor formats are exempt from the major-statement requirement: reminder ads that name the drug but make no claim about what it treats, and help-seeking ads that name the condition but no product. Enforcement runs through the Office of Prescription Drug Promotion (OPDP), which issues untitled letters and warning letters when the rule is broken, and the 2024-2025 enforcement record concentrated on social-media influencer posts that named drugs by brand without ever reading the risk balance. First introduced: Chapter 26.
  17. feature A feature, in interpretability research, is a specific direction inside the model's internal activation space that corresponds to a human-interpretable concept (the model's representation of Stanley as a brand, of ketchup as a category, of empathy as a tone). Researchers recover these features through a technique called sparse-autoencoder dictionary learning, and they validate that a feature is causal rather than merely correlational by clamping the feature's activation high while the model is generating output and watching for predictable behavioral shifts. First introduced: Chapter 18.
  18. feature fatigue Feature fatigue is the point in a product's feature accumulation where adding the next capability stops increasing buyer value and starts decreasing it, because the product has become overwhelming, hard to learn, or hard to find the basic operations inside. Kitchen appliances with twenty programmed cycles the buyer never uses, software with dozens of menus the buyer cannot navigate, and consumer electronics with feature lists the marketing team cannot fit on the box are all classic cases. The discipline is to identify the peak of the value curve and stop adding features past it. First introduced: Chapter 8.
  19. Federal Register The Federal Register is the US federal government's daily journal of every new rule, proposed rule, and agency notice. Each agency runs an RSS or email feed that pushes its filings as they publish, which lets a marketing team learn about a coming regulation while the rule is still in draft and the comment period is still open. The feeds are free and require nothing more than an email address. First introduced: Chapter 3.
  20. FEEL FEEL is the experiential module that runs through affect, the buyer's mood and felt sentiment when interacting with the brand. The brand's emotional signature is what the buyer is consuming on this axis, whether that signature is the warmth of a Trader Joe's store, the calm of a Patagonia catalog, or the chaos of a Liquid Death campaign. The axis differs from THINK because the buyer here is not evaluating an argument, they are noticing how the brand makes them feel. First introduced: Chapter 4.
  21. Few-shot prompting Few-shot prompting (Brown et al., NeurIPS 2020) means including three to ten worked examples of the task in the prompt before asking the model to perform on a new case. Zero-shot is the same task with no examples (the model has to infer the rubric from the question alone); few-shot gives the model a calibrated rubric to imitate. First introduced: Chapter 5.
  22. Fifty-one percent of buyers now begin their software research with an AI chatbot In March 2026, the software-review platform G2 surveyed 1,076 B2B software buyers and decision-makers about how they actually start their software research, and the findings reset the entire seller-side mental model. Fifty-one percent now begin their software research with an AI chatbot, 71 percent rely on AI chatbots for that research overall, 69 percent picked a different vendor than originally planned because of AI guidance, and 33 percent purchased from a vendor they had never previously heard of before the chatbot recommended it. The companion 6sense 2025 Buyer Experience Report (n approximately 4,000) found that 94 percent of buyers used large language models in their buying process, 94 percent of buying groups had ranked their shortlist before any seller contact, and the first ranked vendor wins nearly 80 percent of the time. First introduced: Chapter 23.
  23. file A file is a named bundle of bytes the operating system keeps track of on the marketer's laptop or in a cloud drive. Examples include a Word document, a photo from the team's iPhone, a spreadsheet of sales, or a Python script the analyst wrote. Every file carries four properties: a name (survey.csv), a size measured in bytes, a location in a folder, and a format (csv, pdf, xlsx, png). First introduced: Chapter 1.
  24. fill-in trip A fill-in trip is a grocery occasion for five to fifteen items rather than the full weekly basket a household assembles on its main shopping run. Fill-in trips run smaller baskets, higher visit frequency, and a lower planning horizon than the weekly stock-up, and the two trips serve different jobs in the household's shopping calendar rather than substituting for each other, which is why a chain like Whole Foods opens 9,000-square-foot Daily Shop locations near the existing flagship instead of replacing the flagship itself. First introduced: Chapter 12.
  25. finance-agent templates The Anthropic finance-agent templates are ten prebuilt agent recipes for the Claude model that the company released on May 5, 2026, each one composed of three architectural pieces. Skills are short markdown workflow files the agent loads on demand, Connectors are integrations with outside data sources such as Microsoft 365 and the Moody's data feed, and Subagents are focused Claude API calls with their own specialized prompts and a scoped set of tools they are allowed to call. The ten template names are Pitch builder, Meeting preparer, Earnings reviewer, Model builder, Market researcher, Valuation reviewer, General ledger reconciler, Month-end closer, Statement auditor, and KYC screener (KYC stands for know-your-customer, the compliance check banks run on new clients). First introduced: Chapter 25.
  26. fine-tuning Fine-tuning is the process of taking a language model that has already been trained on the general internet (Claude, GPT, Llama, whichever the team is using as its starting point) and continuing to train it for a short additional run on a much smaller, domain-specific dataset, so that the resulting variant becomes specialized to that domain. In the synthetic-respondent setting walked in this chapter, the additional dataset is the brand’s own historical tracking data, which contains the answer patterns the brand’s real customers have produced in past surveys. The fine-tuned variant that comes out the other end of the run produces respondent simulations calibrated to that brand’s actual customer segments, rather than calibrated to the average of the general internet that the base model was originally trained on. The trade-off is that the fine-tuned variant works well only on questions in that brand’s domain and gets worse on every other topic. First introduced: Chapter 5.
  27. FINRA FINRA stands for the Financial Industry Regulatory Authority, the self-regulatory organization for US broker-dealers operating under SEC oversight. The rule most relevant to marketers is FINRA Rule 2210 on communications with the public, which requires principal review and approval of advertising materials, fair-and-balanced presentation of risks and rewards, and explicit prohibition of guaranteed-performance claims, exaggerated language, and misleading comparisons. Regulation Best Interest (Reg BI), an SEC rule effective June 2020, layers a best-interest standard on broker-dealer recommendations and requires Form CRS disclosure at first contact with a retail customer. FINRA fines for advertising violations range from $500 for minor procedural slips to $2 million or more depending on scale and intent. First introduced: Chapter 26.
  28. firmographics Firmographics is the business-to-business parallel of demographics. Instead of cutting individual buyers by age and income, firmographics cuts buying organizations by industry, company size, location, ownership structure, revenue, and employee count. A SaaS vendor that targets 'mid-market manufacturing firms with 200 to 1,000 employees in the Midwest' is running a firmographic segmentation; the variables sit on the company rather than on a person inside it. First introduced: Chapter 6.
  29. First Moment of Truth The First Moment of Truth (FMOT) is A. G. Lafley’s 2005 term for the three-to-seven-second window at the retail shelf when a shopper picks up a product or walks past it. The buyer is reading the package, the price, and the promise the brand has trained them to expect, and the decision they make in that short window determines whether the rest of the brand machinery has done its job. First introduced: Chapter 9.
  30. first-party customer data First-party data is data the firm collects directly from its own customers (purchase records, app session logs, email opens, support tickets) under its own consent agreements. The contrast is third-party data, which the firm buys from a broker that aggregated it from sources the firm does not control. The 2024-2026 privacy regime favors first-party data over third-party because the consent and provenance trail lives inside the firm’s own consent records, which makes the data legally defensible when a regulator or a customer asks where it came from. First introduced: Chapter 24.
  31. first-party data First-party data is the buyer information the brand collected directly through its own properties: account signups on the brand's site, purchases through the brand's checkout, email opens through the brand's newsletter, app sessions inside the brand's mobile app. The data survives every cookie change and tracking restriction because the brand owns the underlying relationship that produced it, which is the opposite of third-party data, where the brand is renting buyer information from ad networks that observe users across many other sites. First introduced: Chapter 18.
  32. fiscal year 26 (FY26) Salesforce's fiscal year 26 (FY26) runs from February 2025 through January 2026. Salesforce uses a fiscal year offset from the calendar year, which is why a 'fiscal year 26' result lands roughly halfway through the 2026 calendar year on the firm's reporting schedule. First introduced: Chapter 23.
  33. five archetypes The five-archetype taxonomy is the empirical finding of the 2008 Homburg-Jensen-Krohmer study in the Journal of Marketing. Across 337 firms, cluster analysis on the five interface dimensions (information sharing, structural linkages, power balance, function orientations, and marketing's market knowledge) produced five archetypes of how marketing and sales actually relate inside firms. The five archetypes were named Ivory Tower (marketing-dominant, weak structural linkages), Sales Outperformer (sales-dominant, marketing as support), Sales-Driven Symbiosis (sales-led but high information sharing), Marketing-Driven Symbiosis (marketing-led but high information sharing), and Power Balance (both functions strong, high structural linkages, balanced power). The Power Balance and Marketing-Driven Symbiosis configurations produced the highest performance on revenue, market share, and profitability; the Ivory Tower configuration produced the lowest. The taxonomy matters because it replaces practitioner-observation-based ladders like KRK with a statistically-derived map of which configurations actually pay back. First introduced: Chapter 23.
  34. five lenses The five lenses are the audit’s five analytic viewpoints, applied in sequence on the same customer-transaction data. The first lens is single-period deciles (how is one year’s profit distributed across customer deciles?). The second is period-vs-period event impact (how did a specific event, like a price change or a stockout, move the deciles?). The third is single-cohort retention (how does one cohort decay over time?). The fourth is cohort-vs-cohort health (is the new cohort decaying faster or slower than the prior cohort?). The fifth is the C3 chart, which decomposes a period’s revenue by the calendar period in which each contributing customer was first acquired. First introduced: Chapter 24.
  35. five-stage maturity staircase The five stages of analytical maturity describe how far a firm has built the data, organizational, and leadership scaffolding required to run on numbers rather than gut. Stage 1 (‘Analytically Impaired’) is the firm that cannot pull the same number two ways and get the same answer. Stage 5 (‘Analytical Competitors’) is the firm whose competitive advantage is its analytics. Most firms cluster at Stage 2 or 3; reaching Stage 5 takes about a decade of sustained CEO-level commitment to data investment. First introduced: Chapter 24.
  36. FLEDGE FLEDGE (First Locally-Executed Decision over Groups Experiment, later renamed the Protected Audience API) was a Google Privacy Sandbox proposal that would have run ad auctions inside the user's browser using interest groups instead of third-party cookies, designed to replace cookie-based retargeting with an on-device privacy-preserving auction. Google cancelled FLEDGE alongside the rest of the Privacy Sandbox on October 7, 2025, which means the brands that had been preparing to migrate to it lost two budget cycles of preparation work. First introduced: Chapter 18.
  37. floating-point operations (FLOPs) FLOPs (floating-point operations) are the arithmetic operations a computer performed during training. The unit captures both the model size and the training-text volume in one number, which is why FLOPs is the right axis for comparing two models on a fair basis. A 2026 frontier model is trained on roughly 10^25 FLOPs, or ten septillion arithmetic operations. The unit lets a procurement team compare a small-on-lots-of-text model against a large-on-less-text model directly, instead of being fooled by parameter count alone. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  38. Flower of Service The Flower of Service is Christopher Lovelock’s 1995 framework, codified in the Lovelock-Wirtz services-marketing textbook, that draws the core service in the middle of a diagram surrounded by eight petals of supplementary services. Four of those petals are called facilitating petals because the buyer cannot reach the core service without them, and they include information so the buyer knows what is available, order-taking so the buyer can request it, billing so the buyer knows what to pay, and payment so the firm can collect. The other four petals are called enhancing petals because they are how two firms selling the same core service at the same tier end up differentiated from each other in the buyer’s mind, and they include consultation such as advice about what to order, hospitality such as a comfortable waiting room, safekeeping such as luggage storage at a hotel, and exceptions such as a personalized accommodation when a buyer’s situation does not fit the standard script. First introduced: Chapter 9.
  39. fluency Fluency is the speed and ease with which a viewer recognises the brand and processes the advertisement, measured on a 1.0-to-5.9 star scale inside System1's Test Your Ad database. The scale is calibrated against year-on-year market-share gain, which is what gives the score its predictive weight rather than its descriptive feel. A fluency score correlates strongly with the business-effect outcomes that IPA Effectiveness Databank submissions code, so an ad that scores at the high end of the System1 distribution is also the kind of ad that wins the IPA's very-large-business-effect designation when it reaches the awards panel later. First introduced: Chapter 16.
  40. fluent devices Fluent devices is the term System1 uses in its Test Your Ad database for the same underlying construct that the Ehrenberg-Bass research calls distinctive brand assets. The construct is measured on a 1.0-to-5.9 Star Rating scale calibrated against year-on-year market-share gain across more than 150,000 ads, so a brand can read its own score and compare it to the category baseline. The reason two names exist for the same idea is that System1 was building its measurement instrument while Sharp and Romaniuk were building theirs, and the field has not yet converged on a single term for what is empirically the same recognition lever. First introduced: Chapter 16.
  41. Flutter Flutter is Google's Dart-based cross-platform mobile framework. The Impeller rendering engine (which replaced the older Skia engine in 2024) renders the UI directly to the GPU rather than going through the platform's native widget toolkit, which gives Flutter measurable performance and consistency advantages over React Native. Flutter carries roughly 46 percent of the cross-platform mobile market in 2026, with strengths in raw rendering performance, hot reload speed, UI consistency across devices, and a single-language full-stack with Dart.
