The weaknesses are a smaller ecosystem than React Native, fewer developers in the labor market, and a transition cost for JavaScript-heavy teams. First introduced: Chapter 14.
  42. FlutterFlow FlutterFlow is a no-code platform built by ex-Google engineers that quietly generates real Flutter source code (written in the Dart programming language) behind the visual editor, which means the builder gets a working app on the Basic tier at $39 per month or $29.25 per month on annual billing, with native iOS and Android publishing included. The unusual feature is what the platform calls 'code eject': when the no-code editor runs out of expressiveness, the student can download the underlying Dart source and continue building inside a normal code editor without rewriting the app from scratch. FlutterFlow also integrates tightly with Google's Firebase backend, which makes it a natural fit for apps that need authentication, database, and notifications wired together quickly. First introduced: Chapter 14.
  43. FMCG Fast-Moving Consumer Goods (FMCG) is the British and global term for the same category Americans call Consumer Packaged Goods. The naming reflects how the category actually works: per-unit margins are tiny, but the shelves turn over fast enough that volume rather than margin is the load-bearing driver of profit. First introduced: Chapter 10.
  44. fMRI Functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) is a brain-imaging method that measures changes in blood flow inside the brain in real time as a subject performs a task. Because the most active neural circuits draw more blood, the scanner can identify which parts of the brain light up during which behaviors, which is how researchers in the 2012 Tamir-and-Mitchell study could see that talking about oneself activated the same reward circuits that food and money activate. First introduced: Chapter 19.
  45. focus group A focus group is a moderated discussion among six to ten buyers in a room, lasting about ninety minutes. The point of doing it in a group rather than one-on-one is the group dynamic itself: buyers will admit things in front of other buyers (and push back on each other) that they would not say alone to a researcher. Standard practice is to run three to four groups per segment until the next group produces no new themes. First introduced: Chapter 5.
  46. folder A folder (sometimes called a directory) is a named container the operating system uses to group related files. Folders can nest inside other folders, so the marketer's Documents folder might contain a research folder, which in turn contains a survey.csv file. The chain of folder names from the root of the disk down to the file is called the file's path, written like ~/Documents/research/survey.csv where the tilde stands for the marketer's home folder. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  47. food noise Food noise is the constant background cognitive pull toward food that a buyer feels even when they are not physiologically hungry. The pre-medication buyer drives past a Dunkin' sign and a quiet pull arrives unbidden; the GLP-1 user reports that the same pull simply does not arrive anymore. The phenomenon is the dominant subjective experience users report of the medication, and it is the interoceptive cue layer underneath most cue-driven food purchasing. Numerator and Cornell Chronicle both documented the experience at scale in 2025. First introduced: Chapter 4.
  48. Forecast AI Gong Forecast AI is the May 2025 release that adds three classification layers on top of the conversation-intelligence backbone: JOLT-aware deal classification (tagging each deal as customer-indecision, status-quo preference, or competitive risk), Iannarino-aware stage classification (placing each deal on the ten-commitment ladder), and Adamson single-threading risk scoring (flagging deals where no Mobilizer has been identified). The release moved Gong from a coaching tool into a forecasting backbone. First introduced: Chapter 23.
  49. fork A personal remote copy of someone else's repository on the user's own GitHub account. The fork inherits all the original's files and history but is independently editable; commits made to the fork do not appear in the original until the user files a pull request. The standard 2026 way to take a public Skill, customize it for a specific brand, and ship the personalized version as a portfolio piece. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  50. form utility Form utility is the value a channel creates by transforming the raw production output into the configuration the buyer can actually use. The bulk sack of green coffee beans the factory produces is not what the buyer wants; the buyer wants a roasted, ground, brewed cup in a takeaway sleeve, and the steps that convert one into the other are where form utility is created. First introduced: Chapter 11.
  51. Fortune 500 The Fortune 500 is the annual ranking of the 500 largest US public companies by revenue, published by Fortune magazine since 1955. The list functions as a working census of US corporate scale, which is what makes its turnover rate the canonical measure of how much the competitive landscape churns over time, because a brand falling off the list usually means the brand was acquired, taken private, or went bankrupt rather than simply slipping a few positions. First introduced: Chapter 3.
  52. four A's Jagdish Sheth and Rajendra Sisodia introduced the four A's in their 2012 Routledge book as a customer-outcome reframing of McCarthy's four Ps. Each A names a gate the buyer has to clear in sequence for the exchange to happen. Acceptability asks whether the offer actually works for the buyer, both functionally and psychologically. Affordability asks whether the buyer can pay for it and will choose to. Accessibility asks whether she can find and reach the offer. Awareness asks whether she even knows the offer exists. Each gate maps to a role the buyer plays during the exchange (user, payer, seeker, selector), and a brand that fails on any one A loses the buyer at that gate regardless of how well it performs on the other three. First introduced: Chapter 2.
  53. four-component agent The four-component agent is the framework Lilian Weng (then Head of Safety Systems at OpenAI) named in her 2023 blog post called LLM Powered Autonomous Agents. The framework says an agent is the large language model plus four primitives: Planning covers task decomposition and self-reflection, Memory spans sensory, short-term, and long-term retrieval, Tool Use exposes external APIs the model can call, and Action is the execution surface where the model's choices reach the world. Every 2026 agent framework uses this same vocabulary because Weng's post is what gave the field the shared four-part chassis to build on. First introduced: Chapter 27.
  54. four-condition manipulation test The four-condition manipulation test, published by Iason Gabriel and colleagues at DeepMind in April 2024, names the four conditions that distinguish legitimate persuasion from manipulation. A campaign that fails on any one of the four is manipulative regardless of the marketer's intent. The four conditions are: transparency about the fact that influence is happening, respect for the user's rational agency, alignment of the campaign goal with the user's own reflective interest, and reversibility of the effect on the user. First introduced: Chapter 26.
  55. four faces of mass customization Pine and Gilmore's customization 2-by-2 is a framework from their 1997 Harvard Business Review article that sorts every form of personalization onto two axes: whether the underlying product physically changes from buyer to buyer (yes or no) and whether the buyer perceives the change (yes or no). The four resulting quadrants are collaborative, adaptive, cosmetic, and transparent customization. The point of the framework is to pick the right kind of customization for the customer-job at hand before the firm commits to the manufacturing or service architecture, since the four quadrants demand very different investments. First introduced: Chapter 8.
  56. four kinds The four-competitor taxonomy comes from the Cengage marketing-strategy textbook and names four kinds of rivals a brand has to track on different time horizons. Brand competitors sell near-identical products at similar prices. Product competitors offer the same category with different features or price points. Generic competitors solve the same buyer need with an entirely different product. Total-budget competitors compete for the same discretionary dollars regardless of the category they sit in. First introduced: Chapter 3.
  57. Four Levels of Value The Four Levels of Value is Anthony Iannarino's 2018 framework for what a B2B seller is actually offering the buyer at any given moment in the relationship. Level 1 is the product itself: does the seller have the right item at the right price. Level 2 is the service and experience that wraps the product: does the seller answer the phone, does the order ship on time, does implementation arrive when promised. Level 3 is the business outcome the buyer can attribute to the relationship: does cost go down, does revenue go up, does the buyer's profit and loss statement look better at the end of the year. Level 4 is the strategic partnership: does the seller make the buyer herself more capable, the person the buyer's chief executive officer calls before a board meeting and whose advice can be trusted when the buyer is most exposed. First introduced: Chapter 23.
  58. four Ps The four Ps are the canonical 1960 compression of the marketing mix into four levers: Product, Price, Place, and Promotion. E. Jerome McCarthy introduced the framing in 1960, and Philip Kotler's textbooks made it the dominant frame in marketing education for sixty years. The four Ps are useful as a memorable acronym, but they are weak as a contemporary operating frame because they fold the brand into the product and they bundle short-term incentives together with long-term communication, both of which the firm now manages as separate decisions. First introduced: Chapter 2.
  59. four-risks audit The four product risks, named in Marty Cagan's Inspired (2017) chapter 9, are the four classes of risk the product trio has to clear before any engineering capacity commits to building the product. The first is value: will buyers actually buy this, owned by the product manager. The second is usability: can a real user figure out how to operate the product, owned by the designer. The third is feasibility: can engineering actually build this on time and on budget, owned by the tech lead. The fourth is viability: does the product work across the firm's legal, finance, brand, channel, and regulatory constraints, owned by the product manager. All four risks have to be cleared during discovery, not after engineering has built the wrong thing. First introduced: Chapter 8.
  60. four-second hook The four-second hook is the published rule that the opening four seconds of a video must pose a question, a threat, a stakes-statement, or a visual jolt that creates information asymmetry the viewer cannot resolve without watching. The 2024 MrBeast handbook iterates the opening up to fifty times against a 65-percent retention floor measured at second five of the video, on a first-cohort viewer pool drawn from the click-through audience. The rule exists because the platform-side active-attention floor (about two seconds on Facebook, about three and a half seconds on TikTok, about ten seconds on YouTube) gives the producer roughly one breath to convince the viewer not to swipe. First introduced: Chapter 19.
  61. four-stage maturity ladder The revenue-operations maturity ladder is the four-stage framework Forrester uses to grade how seriously a firm treats its revenue-operations function. The lowest rung is Reactive, where the team works as a help desk fielding ad-hoc requests from sales reps and marketers. One rung up is Functional, where the team owns the customer-relationship-management workflow and the lead-routing rules that govern how marketing's leads reach sales. The third rung is Strategic, where the team owns the go-to-market operating model, including territory design and the quota-setting process. The top rung is Transformational, where the team runs the cross-functional revenue process end-to-end. Forrester's benchmarking finds that firms whose teams reach the Strategic and Transformational rungs deliver roughly twice the internal productivity and meaningfully higher win rates compared to firms whose teams are still stuck at Reactive or Functional. First introduced: Chapter 23.
  62. fractional-factorial design A choice-card design that shows each respondent only a carefully selected subset of all possible attribute-level combinations, because asking each respondent to evaluate all combinations would take hours and exhaust their patience long before useful data could be recovered. The fractional design picks a subset balanced enough that the model can still estimate every level's effect without confounding it with any other; the orthogonal version is the special case where every level pair appears with equal frequency. Sawtooth's design generator and Conjointly's study builder produce this automatically once the attributes and levels are entered. First introduced: Chapter 5.
  63. fragmentation, unification, and segmentation Fragmentation, unification, and segmentation are the three historical phases that organize how US mass marketing developed between roughly 1850 and 1925. The fragmentation phase before the 1880s describes a country of regional markets where local producers sold low volumes at high margins because no national distribution infrastructure existed. The unification phase from the 1880s through the 1920s describes the period when railroads and the rotary press let a few national producers reach the whole country at once, which collapsed prices and built the first true mass market on low margins and very high volumes. The segmentation phase after 1923 describes the reverse move, where General Motors deliberately broke the unified mass apart again into targeted slices with the Sloan five-brand ladder against Ford's single-product Model T, and the segmentation strategy that followed has been the dominant US marketing pattern ever since. First introduced: Chapter 6.
  64. framing Framing is the empirical finding that the same outcome described differently produces different choices, even when the underlying numbers are identical. Tversky and Kahneman's 1981 Asian Disease study described a public-health choice in two equivalent ways, one framed as how many lives would be saved and one framed as how many lives would be lost, and the framing alone flipped roughly fifty percentage points of preference between the two phrasings. Same facts, two phrasings, two different choices. First introduced: Chapter 4.
  65. frazzled customer The frazzled customer is the default cognitive state of the modern B2B buyer in the 2020s: time-starved, attention-fragmented, and triaging messages from forty different vendors competing for the same fifteen minutes of attention each day. The frame matters because outreach designed for a calm and curious reader gets cut in the first four seconds when the actual reader is frazzled. First introduced: Chapter 23.
  66. FRED FRED stands for Federal Reserve Economic Data, the St. Louis Fed's free public database of US economic time series including interest rates, inflation, employment, gross domestic product, and retail sales. The database is free to use, requires no API key for browsing or downloading basic series, and typically updates within a few days of the underlying release from the Bureau of Labor Statistics or the Federal Reserve itself. First introduced: Chapter 3.
  67. free-choice matching Free-choice matching is a category-recognition method that asks respondents to match brands to purpose statements with no constraint on how many statements they pick or skip. The crucial design move is that the list of purpose statements quietly includes fabricated dummy statements that no real brand actually claims (for example, a brand committed to neuroscience-led literacy). Respondents who match a real brand to one of those dummy statements are guessing, and the share of dummy matches becomes the chance-attribution floor the researcher subtracts from the surface awareness number to recover the figure that reflects real memory rather than random guessing. First introduced: Chapter 2.
  68. freemium Freemium is a two-tier pricing structure where the firm offers a free version of the product with limited features alongside one or more paid tiers with the full feature set. The free tier acquires users at near-zero marginal cost; the paid tiers generate the revenue that funds the whole operation. Spotify, Duolingo, Notion, and Slack all run freemium structures, with the conversion rate from free to paid (typically 2 to 8 percent for consumer products, higher for team-based products) deciding whether the model is profitable. First introduced: Chapter 10.
  69. frequency cap A frequency cap is a hard ceiling the marketing team sets on how many messages any single buyer can receive in a defined window, regardless of how many automated drips that buyer happens to qualify for at the same time. The industry-standard caps are no more than four marketing emails per buyer per week, no more than two SMS messages per buyer per week, and no more than one push notification per buyer per day. The reason the cap matters is straightforward: a buyer who has qualified for six different drips at once (say, an abandoned-cart flow, a 30-day-silent flow, a re-engagement flow, and three product-category welcome flows) will receive all six messages in the same day without a cap, get overwhelmed by the volume, and unsubscribe from every list rather than just the one that pushed her over the edge. The cap suppresses the surplus messages so the buyer only sees the most-qualifying few, which preserves the relationship instead of burning it out in a single bad week. First introduced: Chapter 21.
  70. freshness Freshness is how recently each of the brand's category-entry-point links has been refreshed in the buyer's memory through advertising, packaging, or another touchpoint. A link that has not been refreshed in two years decays even when it was strong at peak, while a link that gets refreshed every quarter holds because the repeated exposure keeps the association active. The metric tells the brand team which links need media support next quarter. First introduced: Chapter 7.
  71. front-door AI agent A front-door AI agent is Levelpath's 2025 framing for the autonomous procurement agent that intercepts a buyer's procurement request before any seller is ever engaged in the conversation. The agent classifies the request against the buyer's spending taxonomy, checks it against existing contracts the firm already has in place, identifies suppliers from the buyer's preferred-vendor list, scores those suppliers against the firm's weighted criteria, and produces either a shortlist of three to five candidates or a single recommendation for human approval. The category is genuinely distinct from older review-mode AI, which only summarized supplier documents for a human procurement officer to read. The front-door agent actually makes the shortlist decision itself and only routes the final approval back to a human for sign-off, which is what changes the seller's go-to-market job at the source. First introduced: Chapter 23.
  72. Frontier AI Framework A Frontier AI Framework, under the California Transparency in Frontier Artificial Intelligence Act, is the public document a TFAIA-covered large frontier developer must post on the company website. The statute names ten required topics the document has to cover: the developer's governance structures, the catastrophic-risk thresholds beyond which the lab will not deploy, the mitigations applied below those thresholds, the pre-deployment assessments performed on each model, the use of third-party evaluators, the cadence at which the framework itself is updated, the cybersecurity practices protecting unreleased model weights, the process for identifying critical safety incidents, and the internal-use risk management program. Permitted redactions cover trade secrets and national-security information; redacting every substantive answer does not satisfy the statute, which is the procurement-grade point. First introduced: Chapter 26.
  73. Frontier AI Safety Commitments The Frontier AI Safety Commitments were signed at the Seoul AI Safety Summit (May 21-22, 2024) by sixteen AI developers, with the count rising to twenty by February 2025. Each signatory committed to three duties: publish the severe-risk thresholds beyond which the lab will not deploy a model, name the people inside the lab who are accountable for those thresholds, and disclose the lab's responsible-scaling-policy-equivalent process to the public. The commitments generalize the Responsible Scaling Policy framework into a soft-law instrument that applies across labs, which is the move that makes RSP comparability a procurement question. First introduced: Chapter 26.
  74. frontier lab The frontier labs are the handful of companies training the largest, most capable language models in 2026: Anthropic, OpenAI, Google DeepMind, Meta, xAI, and a few others. 'Frontier' refers to models at the leading edge of scale and capability, where the cost to train one new model runs into hundreds of millions of dollars. Most marketers will encounter only the four largest in procurement conversations: Anthropic (Claude), OpenAI (GPT), Google (Gemini), and Meta (Llama). First introduced: Chapter 1.
  75. Frontier Safety Framework (FSF) The Frontier Safety Framework (FSF) is DeepMind's public document describing what kinds of AI capabilities the lab considers too dangerous to deploy without specific mitigations, and what evaluations the lab runs to detect those capabilities before release. The document is version-controlled, which means each release is dated and the changes between versions are public. A procurement officer can therefore read the framework, compare versions, and check whether the lab is keeping pace with the failure modes the alignment research record has been documenting. First introduced: Chapter 26.
  76. Frontmatter A frontmatter is the small block at the very top of a file that gives the program reading the file a few pieces of metadata before the body starts. The block sits between two lines of three dashes and uses a format called YAML, which is just key-value pairs that read like a short list of labels and values. A Skill's frontmatter typically names the Skill on one line and gives a one-sentence description on the next, and that two-line block is the entire frontmatter. The undergrad version is the same idea as the title and description fields a TikTok creator fills in before uploading the video. The body of the video is everything the post is about, and the title and description above it are how every other system on the platform knows what the post contains without having to watch it first. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  77. FTC The Federal Trade Commission (FTC) is the US agency that polices unfair and deceptive practices in advertising and commerce, including false advertising claims, fake reviews, undisclosed sponsorships, and antitrust violations. The FTC's 2023 amended endorsement guides expanded what a brand must disclose when it pays an influencer or runs a review program. First introduced: Chapter 3.
  78. fully flexible supply chain A fully flexible supply chain is a configuration designed for infrequent, project-style purchases of large lots, where the buyer is willing to wait and the seller charges enough to cover dedicated capacity that is not utilized between projects. Defense procurement and the Pfizer BNT162b2 vaccine ramp of 2020 and 2021 are textbook examples, where the supply chain is configured around custom configuration, high unit cost, and a willingness to stand up dedicated lines that may sit idle once the project closes. First introduced: Chapter 11.
  79. function calling Function calling is OpenAI's name for the same agent-loop mechanism Anthropic calls tool use. A function-calling-enabled model accepts a list of function descriptions in its prompt (the name of each function, what each parameter means, what type each parameter has) and emits structured JSON specifying which function to call and which arguments to fill in. The model never runs the function itself. The runtime that wraps the model receives the JSON, executes the function, and feeds the result back into the next model turn. OpenAI shipped function calling first in June 2023, and Anthropic shipped equivalent tool use shortly after. The 2026 industry now treats the two names as interchangeable in both conversation and documentation, so a marketer who reads either term in vendor docs is reading about the same thing. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  80. fungible Fungibility is the economic property of being fully interchangeable, where one unit of a thing is identical to any other unit of the same thing. Money is supposed to be fungible because a dollar from a paycheck spends the same as a dollar found on the sidewalk. Mental accounting violates fungibility because the buyer treats the two dollars differently: the found twenty gets spent as 'fun money' and the paycheck twenty as 'serious money,' even though they buy exactly the same things. First introduced: Chapter 4.
  81. funnel The funnel is the shape marketers draw to track how a wide pool of potential buyers narrows step by step toward the much smaller group who actually purchase. Top of funnel describes strangers who first saw the brand. Bottom of funnel describes the buyers who paid. Each of the seven decision stages the chapter just walked is one slice of that funnel, with the count of buyers shrinking at every slice as some abandon the process and others convert. First introduced: Chapter 4.
  82. FY24 FY24 means fiscal year 2024, the twelve-month accounting period a firm uses to report its annual results. Apple's fiscal year runs October to September rather than January to December, so Apple FY24 ended in September 2024 and Apple FY25 ended in September 2025, which is why Apple's own annual numbers sit about three months ahead of a U.S. retailer's calendar-year results. First introduced: Chapter 15.
07 // g

G G

  1. G-STIC G-STIC stands for Goal, Strategy, Tactics, Implementation, and Control, the five-stage planning loop in Kotler's Marketing Management 16th edition. The Goal stage sets what the brand wants to achieve in dollars or share points. The Strategy stage picks the route to that goal. The Tactics stage names the specific moves inside the strategy. The Implementation stage runs those moves in the world. And the Control stage monitors the environment for changes and feeds the diff back into the next Goal cycle, which is why environmental scanning lives inside Control rather than as a separate function. First introduced: Chapter 3.
  2. GA4 Google Analytics 4 (GA4) is the free analytics platform that most consumer-facing websites use to track who visits the site, what they do once they arrive, and which marketing channels brought them in. GA4 replaced an older Google product called Universal Analytics in July 2023; the older version stopped collecting data and every site that wanted to keep its analytics had to migrate to GA4. First introduced: Chapter 4.
  3. GAIA GAIA (which stands for General AI Assistants) is a benchmark published by Grégoire Mialon and co-authors in 2023 for testing how well an AI agent can answer open-web research questions a real assistant would be asked. It contains 450 questions arranged in three difficulty levels, every answer is unambiguous so the scoring is mechanical, and the human baseline on the same questions is roughly 92 percent. The benchmark is the public reference point a capstone team uses when measuring how close its open-web research agent is to a human researcher. First introduced: Chapter 27.
  4. Garden of the Twelve Friends The Garden of the Twelve Friends is a walk-through attraction at Shanghai Disneyland in which twelve Disney characters are reimagined as the twelve animals of the Chinese zodiac, so Pluto stands in for the Year of the Dog and Tigger for the Year of the Tiger. No equivalent exists at any other Disney park in the world, which is exactly why it shows up in this chapter as evidence of how deeply Shanghai Disney localized below the brand layer. First introduced: Chapter 15.
  5. GDPR General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) is the European Union's 2018 data-protection law. It requires opt-in consent for marketing emails, gives data subjects the right to access and delete their data on request, and authorizes fines up to 4 percent of the brand's global annual revenue per violation. It applies to any brand that processes the personal data of EU residents, regardless of where the brand is headquartered, which is why a US brand selling to one French customer through a Shopify storefront is still subject to the entire framework. First introduced: Chapter 21.
  6. Geert Hofstede Geert Hofstede was a Dutch organizational psychologist who built the most influential framework in cross-cultural business by analyzing IBM employee surveys from the late 1960s. His 1980 study covered 53 countries and produced four numbered dimensions that let a marketer compare any two cultures the way a chemist compares two compounds. First introduced: Chapter 15.
  7. general availability (GA) General availability (GA) is the vendor-software lifecycle stage at which a product finally moves out of beta-testing and becomes a release that any paying customer can buy off the shelf without having to know somebody at the vendor. Before a product reaches GA, vendors typically restrict access to a handful of design partners and early-access customers who agree to provide feedback and absorb bugs in exchange for early use. Once the product hits GA, it carries a standard service-level agreement and standard support contracts, which is the moment the firm's procurement team will normally agree to sign the purchase order rather than waiting another quarter. First introduced: Chapter 23.
  8. General-Purpose AI A General-Purpose AI (GPAI) model is a large foundation model trained on broad data and capable of performing a wide range of tasks, including those it was not specifically trained for. Examples include GPT-4, Claude, and Gemini. The EU AI Act imposes separate transparency and safety obligations on the firms that train these models, distinct from the obligations on firms that deploy them. First introduced: Chapter 3.
  9. general-purpose AI (GPAI) General-Purpose AI (GPAI) is the EU AI Act's category for foundation models, defined in Article 3(63) as a model trained on a broad data distribution and capable of performing a wide range of distinct tasks. The Article 51 systemic-risk threshold is 10^25 cumulative floating-point operations of training compute, which is the technical line that separates GPAI from smaller models. The label covers GPT, Claude, Gemini, Llama, and Mistral foundation models in their main released versions. GPAI providers carry Article 53 documentation duties directly (training-data summary, copyright-policy, technical-specification disclosure), plus Article 55 obligations when the model crosses the systemic-risk threshold. Deployers (brands using GPAI in their own products) inherit the disclosure chain through Article 26, which means a brand's transparency story is only as complete as the upstream provider's documentation. First introduced: Chapter 26.
  10. general-purpose scientific tool A general-purpose scientific tool is the phrase Demis Hassabis used in his 2024 Nobel Lecture in Chemistry for a piece of technology that generalizes across an entire field of research rather than serving a single narrow purpose. The microscope did this for biology, the telescope did it for astronomy, and the digital computer did it for mathematics. Hassabis claims that the AlphaFold-line of agent-on-top-of-language-model systems now plays the same role for any field whose central problem is a combinatorial search across a very large state space, with drug discovery and brand strategy as parallel examples. First introduced: Chapter 27.
  11. generative-engine optimization Generative-engine optimization (GEO) is the discipline of structuring a firm's information so generative AI engines can synthesize and cite it accurately when a buyer asks the engine for a category recommendation. It replaces classical search-engine optimization (SEO) as the dominant visibility lever in categories where buyers default to a generative engine like ChatGPT or Claude for product discovery instead of typing a query into Google. Chapter 18 covers the full discipline, but the short version is that becoming the citable source on your category is now what ranking on Google's first page used to be. First introduced: Chapter 2.
  12. Generative engine optimization (GEO) Generative engine optimization (GEO) is the practice of making a brand the citable source on its category inside generative AI engines like ChatGPT, Claude, and Perplexity. GEO is the analog to classical search engine optimization for the AI half of the dual funnel: where SEO wins the click on the ten-blue-links page, GEO wins the citation inside the synthesized answer the AI engine writes in response to the buyer's question. First introduced: Chapter 18.
  13. GEO Generative-engine optimization (GEO) is the discipline of becoming the cited source a generative AI engine pulls from when a buyer asks a question, the way classical search-engine optimization (SEO) became the discipline of ranking on Google's first page. The work runs across schema markup, citable structured data, and authoritative third-party coverage the model is likely to ingest and surface. Chapter 18 covers the operational discipline in depth, so the version that matters here is the short one. Being the brand the chatbot mentions in its synthesized answer is now the visibility lever the firm has to invest in, in the same way ranking on the first page of Google used to be. First introduced: Chapter 2.
  14. geo-experiments A geo-experiment is a randomized test in which some markets receive a campaign and matched markets are deliberately held back from it, with the difference in sales movement between the two groups serving as an estimate of the causal lift the campaign actually produced. Because the design works at the market level rather than the individual level, it does not require user tracking, which makes geo-experiments the cleanest 2026 measurement method as third-party cookies have disappeared. The trade-off is speed: a typical geo-experiment runs four to twelve weeks before the lift estimate stabilizes. First introduced: Chapter 24.
  15. geo-incrementality experiments A geo-incrementality experiment is a measurement design where the marketer holds one geographic region out from a campaign (no ads run in Cleveland for two weeks, for example) and then compares the revenue produced in that holdout region to the revenue produced in the regions that did receive the ads. The design is marketing's closest practical analog to a randomized controlled trial, because random geography approximates random assignment when neither the brand nor the buyer chose which region they were in. First introduced: Chapter 18.
  16. geocentric Geocentric is the orientation in which a firm treats the world as a single market and lets talent, capital, and decisions flow across borders without preference for the home country. Local execution happens market by market, but no one country is the default reference for the others. Apple, IKEA, and Toyota approximate it on different dimensions; pure geocentric is more of an aspiration than a destination. First introduced: Chapter 15.
  17. geofences A geofence is an invisible boundary the marketer draws on a map, usually around a retail store, a stadium, or a competitor's location, that triggers a mobile ad on every phone that crosses it. A coffee chain can geofence the half-block around a rival's storefront so that any customer walking toward the rival's door at 8 a.m. gets a coupon for the chain's own coffee on the screen of their phone, which operationalizes geographic segmentation at the scale of individual city blocks rather than at the much coarser ZIP-code level the old direct-mail tools were built for. First introduced: Chapter 6.
  18. geofencing Geofencing is the practice of targeting a buyer's mobile device with a promotion the moment the device crosses a defined radius around a store, a competitor's store, or a particular destination such as a stadium or an airport. The Walmart app's parking-lot trigger that switches the home screen into in-store mode when the phone crosses into a Walmart lot is the canonical 2026 example of how the format gets used to shape behavior inside a few hundred feet of geography. First introduced: Chapter 12.
  19. Geographic Geographic segmentation cuts the market by where buyers live, work, or buy. The granularity runs from country down to region, then to ZIP code, then to individual census block group, and the 2026 high-resolution version uses mobile-phone location signals to draw boundaries finer than a city block. The classic use case is a national restaurant chain pricing breakfast differently in the Northeast than in the Southeast, because what counts as a normal price for an egg sandwich travels with the local cost of living rather than with the chain's headquarters. First introduced: Chapter 6.
  20. GeoLift GeoLift is an open-source R package released by Meta in 2022 that runs synthetic-control and synthetic-DiD methods on geo-experiments without the analyst needing to hand-code the optimization. The marketer hands GeoLift a panel of weekly outcomes by DMA plus the list of treated DMAs, and the package returns the lift estimate, the confidence interval, and a placebo distribution the analyst can read for significance. The license is Apache 2.0, which means it can be used in commercial work without restriction. GeoLift is the standard tool for measuring city-level marketing campaigns in 2026 when randomization at the user level is not feasible. First introduced: Chapter 5.
  21. GeoLift by Recast GeoLift by Recast is the standalone synthetic-control geographic-experiment product that Recast unbundled from its MMM platform on September 30, 2025. The product compares test and control geographies to measure causal incrementality on television, paid social, or paid search, and it is priced at $100 per month after a six-month free trial. When a brand later graduates to the full Recast MMM platform, the GeoLift results calibrate the model’s priors so the channel-ROI estimates tighten around the experimentally measured numbers rather than around the regression’s assumptions. First introduced: Chapter 24.
  22. gesture intensity and verbal-stress-beat alignment A hand-gesture-intensity audit is the application of machine-vision body-pose-estimation tools (Google MediaPipe Holistic, OpenPose, or commercial wrappers like Affectiva and Realeyes) to a brand's existing spokesperson-video inventory to score each video for gesture-intensity level and verbal-stress-beat alignment. The output is a video-by-video report that flags spokespersons whose hands are too still (under-engaging the viewer) and spokespersons whose hands move too much (creating visual noise that competes with the verbal content), with the moderate-intensity peak as the optimization target the brief should specify. First introduced: Chapter 7.
  23. GET to read, POST to send data HTTP methods are the verbs an HTTP request uses to tell the server what kind of operation the caller wants. GET asks the server for data without changing anything on the server side; loading a webpage in a browser sends a GET request to the page's URL. POST sends data to the server and asks the server to do something with it; sending a chat message to Claude or placing an order on Amazon sends a POST request with the message or order in the body. GET and POST are two of the four basic verbs of the web, alongside PUT (update existing data) and DELETE (remove data). First introduced: Chapter 1.
  24. Git Git is a version-control system Linus Torvalds wrote in 2005 to coordinate development of the Linux kernel after a licensing dispute ended the team's previous tool. Git tracks every change to every file in a folder, lets multiple people work on the same files at the same time without overwriting each other's edits, and makes it cheap to roll back to yesterday's working version when today's edit breaks. By 2026, Git sits underneath nearly every collaborative software project on earth, marketing-team Skill folders included. The marketer interacts with Git by typing commands like 'git commit', 'git push', and 'git clone' at the terminal. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  25. GitHub GitHub is the largest online platform for hosting source code, founded in 2008 and acquired by Microsoft in 2018 for $7.5 billion. Most software companies and most open-source projects store their code on GitHub. Public repositories on the platform are visible to anyone on the internet without an account, which is why accidentally pasting an API key or password into a public repository leaks the secret to automated scrapers within minutes. The 2026 marketer treats every commit to a public repository as if it were a screenshot posted to a public Twitter account. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  26. GitHub Actions GitHub Actions is GitHub's built-in scheduler and event-driven workflow runner. A small YAML configuration file placed in a repository's .github/workflows/ folder tells GitHub to run a script every Monday at 9 a.m., or whenever someone files an issue, or whenever a pull request is opened. The runner provisions a fresh virtual machine for each run, executes the script, and posts results back. Free for public repositories and at modest cost for private repositories ($0.008 per minute of Linux compute on the standard tier). The standard 2026 way to schedule a marketing-research agent without renting a separate server. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  27. Glide Apps Glide Apps is a no-code platform that turns a Google Sheet, an Airtable base, or its own Glide Tables backend into a working iOS, Android, and Progressive Web App (PWA) using a drag-and-drop editor. The free tier covers Glide Tables only and lets you draft apps without publishing them, while the Maker plan at $149 per month on annual billing actually publishes up to 3 apps that read live data from Google Sheets or Excel. If a student already keeps the data in a spreadsheet, Glide is the fastest path on the market from that spreadsheet to a working app on a phone. First introduced: Chapter 14.
  28. globalization drivers The globalization drivers are George Yip's 1989 four structural forces that together determine how globalizable a given industry is at a given moment. Market drivers cover common buyer needs and the existence of global distribution channels; cost drivers cover scale economies, sourcing arbitrage, and factor-cost differences; government drivers cover trade policy, technical standards, and regulation; competitive drivers cover cross-border trade and the presence of rivals operating in multiple regions. Each force scores independently, and the combined profile tells the firm whether to standardize or to localize. First introduced: Chapter 15.
  29. GLOBE GLOBE stands for Global Leadership and Organizational Behavior Effectiveness. It is a 2004 cross-cultural study led by Robert House that surveyed 17,000 managers across 62 societies and produced nine cultural dimensions, each scored twice: how the society actually behaves now (As-Is) and how it would like to behave (Should-Be). GLOBE extends Hofstede with more dimensions and more recent data. First introduced: Chapter 15.
  30. glocalization Glocalization is the synthesis position in the standardize-versus-adapt debate: the firm keeps the brand promise and operating disciplines the same across countries while letting language, menu, channel, and price be rebuilt market by market. Roland Robertson coined the term in the 1990s, and HSBC's old 'The World's Local Bank' tagline is the textbook execution of the idea. First introduced: Chapter 15.
  31. GLP-1 drugs GLP-1 drugs are a new class of weight-loss and diabetes medications that work by mimicking the gut hormone glucagon-like peptide-1 (GLP-1), which slows stomach emptying and reduces the appetite signals the gut sends to the brain. The two best-known examples are semaglutide (sold as Ozempic and Wegovy) and tirzepatide (sold as Mounjaro and Zepbound), both taken as once-weekly injections. Their market relevance to consumer behavior is the measurable damping of food-cue purchasing they produce in users. First introduced: Chapter 4.
  32. GLP-1 receptor agonists Glucagon-like peptide-1 receptor agonists (GLP-1 agonists) are a class of injectable drugs originally developed for type-2 diabetes, including Ozempic and Mounjaro, and approved separately for weight management as Wegovy and Zepbound. The drugs work by slowing gastric emptying and blunting the hunger signal. The measured effect on consumer behavior is that households with a regular user purchase roughly 5.5 percent fewer calories within six months of adoption, with the calorie cut concentrated in salty snacks, sweet snacks, and sweetened beverages. First introduced: Chapter 3.
  33. goal A goal in a marketing plan is a measurable, time-bound statement of what the plan is trying to achieve. A working goal looks like ‘customer-asset value of $5.0 billion by fiscal year-end’ or ‘145 million active cardholders by Q4.’ A non-goal looks like ‘grow the brand,’ which has no number attached and no date attached, so the plan cannot tell at year-end whether it succeeded or failed. First introduced: Chapter 24.
  34. Goggles Goggles are a JSON-format ruleset Brave Search lets the user write to boost, demote, restrict, or expand retrieval against the Brave index for any given query. The team encodes its preferred trusted domains, the content classes it wants excluded from results, and its source-priority preferences as a versioned configuration file the team can ship alongside its application code, which is how the brand keeps the editorial preferences governing its retrieval audits under source-control rather than scattered across one-off tweaks. First introduced: Chapter 18.
  35. golden set A golden set is a small collection of test cases (typically five to fifty) where the team has written down by hand what the ideal output looks like for each input. The team runs the agent on every case before any change ships, and any case where the agent's output drifts from the hand-written ideal is the early warning that the change broke something the team had been counting on. First introduced: Chapter 27.
  36. Gong Engage Gong Engage is Gong's August 2024 sales-engagement product. It bundles sequence automation, AI-drafted email outreach, and inline coaching prompts into the rep's daily workflow, competing directly with Outreach and Salesloft for the same rep-desktop attention. The strategic move was to extend Gong from a record-and-analyze tool into a take-action tool, so the same data backplane that scores the call also drives the next email. First introduced: Chapter 23.
  37. GoodRx GoodRx is the prescription-discount platform that lets patients find lower prices on prescription drugs across U.S. pharmacies, and it became the canonical healthcare-marketing precedent because of the February 2023 settlement the FTC and HHS reached against it. The agency found that GoodRx had been sharing prescription data with Meta, Google, and Criteo for advertising-platform retargeting (the data on the wire included medication names, refill schedules, and the conditions those prescriptions implied), that the data flowed through standard marketing pixels (Meta Pixel, Google Analytics) the way any e-commerce brand routes retargeting data, and that the brand never disclosed the flow to users or sought authorization. The $1.5 million civil penalty was the first enforcement action under the FTC's Health Breach Notification Rule, and the order banned GoodRx from sharing health information for advertising purposes going forward, which makes the case the precedent every healthcare-adjacent marketing pipeline now reads in the dependency chain. First introduced: Chapter 26.
  38. Google AI Mode Google AI Mode is a full-page conversational search interface inside Google, structurally different from the AI Overview paragraph that appears above the blue links. Where the AI Overview adds a short synthesized paragraph on top of the classical results, AI Mode replaces the whole search results page with a multi-paragraph answer that cites eight to twelve sources, designed for a buyer who wants to read a complete answer rather than scan a list of links. First introduced: Chapter 18.
  39. Google Analytics 4 Experiments GA4 Experiments is a free A/B testing layer Google Analytics 4 ships with every account. It is sufficient for the landing-page tests at the 100-to-1,000-orders-per-month volume most undergraduate side businesses operate at, because the binding constraint on running an experiment is rarely the platform license. The binding constraint is whether the store has enough monthly traffic to detect a small lift with statistical confidence. First introduced: Chapter 13.
  40. Google Analytics 4 (GA4) Google Analytics 4 (GA4) is Google's free web and app analytics platform, the successor to Universal Analytics, which Google retired in July 2023. The platform uses an event-based data model where every meaningful interaction (a pageview, a button click, a form submit, an add-to-cart) is logged as a named event the team can later analyze. The default reports cover users, sessions, engagement rate, and conversions, and the free tier is sufficient for nearly every brand under enterprise scale. First introduced: Chapter 18.
  41. google-analytics.mcp The Google Analytics 4 MCP server is a Model Context Protocol server that gives an AI client a federated read across the cross-channel attribution data Google Analytics tracks, including paid search, organic search, and referral traffic. It is the third leg of the channel-fit triangulation in a typical 2026 brand stack where direct-to-consumer and Amazon are the two biggest channels and Google Analytics holds the cross-channel attribution that ties them together. First introduced: Chapter 11.
  42. Google Customer Match Customer Match is Google Ads' first-party audience-targeting feature, the durable post-cookie way for a brand to reach its existing customers and their lookalikes. The brand uploads a hashed-email list to Google Ads, Google matches those hashes against signed-in Google accounts (without ever seeing the raw email addresses), and the brand can then target paid-search and display ads against the resulting audience or use it as the seed for a lookalike audience that resembles the original list. First introduced: Chapter 18.
  43. Google Search Console Google Search Console is Google's free dashboard for site owners that reports which search queries the brand's pages ranked for, how often visitors clicked the resulting links, what the average rank position was, how many pages Google has indexed, and which technical errors the crawler hit. Every brand running a classical-SEO program verifies its domain inside Search Console as a first step, because the queries-and-clicks report is the cheapest way to see what Google thinks the site is actually about. First introduced: Chapter 18.
  44. Gorgias Gorgias is the leading customer-support helpdesk for Shopify direct-to-consumer brands. It pulls Shopify order data directly into each support ticket so that the agent answering the email sees the customer's full order history, return status, and shipping information without having to look anything up. The platform automates roughly 30 to 40 percent of routine tickets (the ones that ask where the order is, what the return policy is, or how the sizing works) using a combination of order data and AI. Gorgias bills per ticket resolved, with monthly invoices that run from $50 to $900 depending on the brand's support volume. First introduced: Chapter 13.
  45. GOVERN-MAP-MEASURE-MANAGE GOVERN-MAP-MEASURE-MANAGE names the four core functions of the NIST AI Risk Management Framework, run as a continuous loop rather than as a one-time checklist. GOVERN sets the organization's AI-risk policy and accountability structure. MAP establishes the deployment context, naming what the system is supposed to do and who it affects. MEASURE assesses the system's trustworthiness against the seven RMF characteristics (validity, safety, fairness, accountability, transparency, security, privacy). MANAGE allocates the organization's resources to the risks the MAP and MEASURE steps surfaced, then the loop cycles back to GOVERN when the policy needs updating. First introduced: Chapter 26.
  46. GPT-4 with personalization had 81.2 percent higher odds of post-debate agreement A 2025 randomized controlled trial published in Nature Human Behaviour measured how persuasive a personalized large language model is compared to a human persuader. Salvi and colleagues ran 820 participants through debates either against GPT-4 or against another human across 30 sociopolitical topics, and half the conditions gave the persuader access to the counterparty's basic demographic information. When personalization was on, GPT-4 was 81.2 percent more likely than the human debaters to win post-debate agreement. When the AI did not have demographic personalization, its advantage shrank to a smaller but still directionally positive effect, which suggests the AI's edge comes from disciplined tailoring rather than from raw rhetorical skill. First introduced: Chapter 23.
  47. GRAVITY GRAVITY is David Bell's seven-letter framework for the levers that govern how online and offline retail demand splits for any given household. The seven letters stand for Geography (density and distance to fulfillment), Resistance (frictions to purchase, both online and offline), Adjacency (the neighbor-of-an-existing-customer effect, where one online buyer in a zip code raises the probability the next-door neighbor becomes one too), Vicinity (proximity to physical infrastructure such as warehouses and stores), Isolation (the gap between what a household prefers and what local offline retailers stock), Topography (local landscape and travel costs), and You (idiosyncratic personal factors). Bell developed the framework from the Diapers.com, Wayfair, Bonobos, and Warby Parker portfolio he advised, and the seven letters are the empirical regularities that recurred across all four cases rather than a theoretical taxonomy invented in advance. First introduced: Chapter 12.
  48. gravity-of-decision rule The gravity-of-decision corrective is a 2006 Yankelovich and Meer rule (HBR) that says a category's segmentation basis must match how heavily the buyer thinks about a purchase. High-gravity categories like cars, health insurance, and school choice involve large dollars or large life consequences, and buyers in those categories use deep criteria (values, life-stage anxieties, identity signals) to choose. Low-gravity categories like snacks, deodorant, or paper towels involve small dollars and routine choice, and buyers in those categories sort on simple cues like brand familiarity, shelf placement, and price. Running deep psychographic segmentation on a low-gravity category produces over-engineered segments that buyers do not actually use to differentiate themselves at the shelf. First introduced: Chapter 6.
  49. greedflation Greedflation is the contested 2022-to-2024 claim that margin expansion by firms, rather than the pass-through of higher input costs, drove a substantial share of the price increases consumers saw after the pandemic. Category-level price-record evidence is more consistent with anticipatory pricing in response to documented cost shocks than with the pure margin-grab narrative, but the framing stuck culturally and became a flashpoint in the post-2022 fairness debate. The label matters for pricing managers because it shifts the legitimacy of the firm's stated reason: a price increase reading as a defensive cost pass-through earns the dual-entitlement protection, while one reading as a margin-grab loses it. First introduced: Chapter 10.
  50. gross margin Gross margin is the percentage of revenue left after subtracting the cost of the goods the firm sold. A 14 percent gross margin means Costco keeps 14 cents per dollar of sales to cover everything else: rent, payroll, utilities, and what remains is the operating profit. Walmart and Kroger run gross margins in the 25 to 30 percent range, while Costco intentionally runs lower because the membership fee covers what most other retailers have to pull out of the gross margin. The thin gross margin is the structural choice that makes the Costco offer feel cheap to a member without making the firm unprofitable. First introduced: Chapter 2.
  51. gross merchandise volume Gross merchandise volume, often shortened to GMV, is the total dollar value of all orders placed through a platform in a given period, before refunds, returns, and platform fees are netted out. It is the headline number Shopify and Amazon report each quarter because it sizes the platform in a way investors can compare year over year. The brand operating on top of the platform earns its own net revenue, which is a smaller and meaningfully different number, because gross merchandise volume includes the platform's take-rate, the fulfillment fees, the return reversals, and everything else that comes out before the brand sees the deposit. First introduced: Chapter 13.
  52. grounded rewards A grounded reward is the term David Silver and Richard Sutton introduced in their 2025 essay for a reward signal that arrives directly from the environment as a measurable consequence of the agent's action, rather than from a human rater scoring the output after the fact. For a marketing agent, a grounded reward is a downstream conversion the payment processor records or a return the return-merchandise-authorization system books against the order. A click is not a grounded reward, because the long-horizon cost of a click that damages brand trust is invisible to the click signal itself. First introduced: Chapter 27.
  53. Grounding API The Grounding API is Google's interface for grounded generation, the version of the Gemini model that returns both the model's response and the list of related sub-queries the model generated internally to build that response. The gemini-2.5-flash variant of the model is the one that exposes the sub-query list cleanly in third-party integrations, while the Pro variants are documented as flaky on the same endpoint as of late 2025, which is why every working agency script hard-codes gemini-2.5-flash specifically. First introduced: Chapter 18.
  54. GSTIC GSTIC is a five-step planning frame the head of revenue uses to turn an audit finding into a Monday-morning action plan. The five steps are: state the Goal (what number changes), the Strategy (which lever you pull), the Tactics (the specific moves under that lever), the Implementation steps (who does what by when), and the Control measure (the number you check on Friday to see whether the plan worked). First introduced: Chapter 23.
  55. GTM-Operator role The go-to-market (GTM) operator is the role that sits right at the seam between revenue operations and the inside-sales floor, and the role combines the operational responsibility a sales-operations analyst used to carry (CRM hygiene, lead routing, pipeline reports) with the cross-functional coordination a senior sales-development representative used to provide (handing leads off, tuning the cadence, escalating problems to marketing). The role emerged through 2025 and 2026 as the headcount that used to be allocated to junior sales-development representatives and business-development representatives was cut, and the work the rookie reps used to do moved into AI-augmented workflows that the operator now administers rather than the rookie rep executes by hand. First introduced: Chapter 23.
  56. guardrail classifier A guardrail classifier is a sampling-time content classifier that runs in front of the generative model, tagging incoming prompts and outgoing responses against the brand's policy categories (such as off-topic, abusive, regulated-advice-attempt, or personal-data-leakage) before the user ever sees the response. The 2024-2026 production options include NVIDIA NeMo Guardrails, OpenAI Moderation, Lakera Guard, AWS Bedrock Guardrails, and Azure AI Content Safety, and a competitive deployment runs the classifier under 200 milliseconds at the 95th percentile so the user does not perceive added latency. The classifier is the operational mechanism by which the deferral budget at §26.5 actually decides whether the AI path or the human path takes the next message. First introduced: Chapter 26.
  57. Guideline 2.5.2 Apple App Store Review Guideline 2.5.2: requires apps to install or launch executable code only from the App Store binary or in-app purchase mechanism, with restrictions on dynamically loading code at runtime. Apple began applying this guideline to AI-coded prompt-to-app builders in March 2026, removing tools like Replit-on-iOS and Vibecode from the store. Pre-AI no-code tools with native compilation pipelines (FlutterFlow, Adalo) remain compliant. First introduced: Chapter 14.
  58. GWI GlobalWebIndex (GWI) is a subscription consumer-research platform that surveys internet users in roughly 50 markets every quarter on values, attitudes, lifestyles, and brand affinity. The same panel is re-surveyed quarter after quarter, which is what lets a brand track the direction of a sociocultural shift rather than just its current level. First introduced: Chapter 3.
08 // h

H H

  1. habit discontinuity window A habit discontinuity window is the narrow period (typically four to twelve weeks) opened when a change in the buyer's context disrupts an established habit. A move to a new city, a new job, a baby, a graduation, a divorce: each disrupts the cue layer the old habit ran on, and during the window that follows the buyer re-deliberates by default rather than running on autopilot. Targeted promotions delivered during the window produce structurally higher lift-per-dollar than the same promotions delivered to stable-context buyers. Verplanken and Wood documented the window in 2006. First introduced: Chapter 4.
  2. hallucination A hallucination is when a model produces text that sounds fluent, plausible, and confident but is factually wrong. The model is not lying in any human sense. It is generating the sequence of words that looks most like a real answer given the prompt, and the wrong-but-fluent words happen to come out. Hallucinations are most common with specific numbers, dates, academic citations, and named entities (a CEO's name, a product launch date, a survey statistic). The opening scene of this chapter, where the brand manager's '67 percent shipping complaint' number turned out to come from a slice the model never even read, is the same failure mode. The fix is to ground the model in a real source via web search or a tool call, then verify the source by clicking the link the model returned. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  3. halo A halo is the positive spillover one product produces onto other products in the same brand or line, where the strength of a hit item lifts the sales or perceived quality of its neighbors. A breakout lipstick shade in a cosmetics brand often raises sales across the whole eye-makeup and skincare line for that brand, because the new buyer attracted by the lipstick keeps browsing the rest of the rack. The reverse can also happen, called a negative halo, where a failed launch drags neighboring products down. First introduced: Chapter 8.
  4. halo effect The halo effect is the bias where one positive trait (good looks, fame, expertise) makes observers infer that other positive traits are also present, without any evidence to back it up. The same product gets rated as functioning better when it is also rated as attractive, even when the two ratings should be independent. A handsome car salesperson is read as also more trustworthy and more competent, even when those traits were not on display. First introduced: Chapter 4.
  5. harvest To harvest a product is to deliberately reduce the firm's investment in it (research, manufacturing capacity, sales-force coverage, advertising support) while keeping the product on the shelf so the firm can extract whatever residual revenue the remaining loyal buyers still pay. The move makes sense when the buyer base is loyal enough and the decline is slow enough that the lower investment level remains profitable. Kraft has been harvesting Velveeta for years, keeping the product available without renewing it, because the remaining buyers still pay enough to cover the trim production cost. First introduced: Chapter 8.
  6. hash To hash a piece of data (an email address, a phone number) is to run it through a one-way mathematical function that replaces the original with a fixed-length scrambled string the platform cannot reverse back into the email. Matching hashes mean the same email is on both lists without either side ever seeing the raw value, which is how Meta Customer Lists and Google Customer Match keep first-party targeting working under modern privacy rules. First introduced: Chapter 18.
  7. HBS Harvard Business School (HBS) is the Boston-based graduate business school whose faculty wrote much of the canonical channel-conflict scholarship from the 1980s onward. The case-method research tradition that came out of HBS, together with the Journal of Marketing papers that followed it, supplies the conflict-management vocabulary every retailer and manufacturer still uses today (strategic justification, dual compensation, superordinate goals, and minimum-advertised-price policies). First introduced: Chapter 11.
  8. headers Headers are small labels at the top of an HTTP request that carry information about the request itself, separate from the message being sent. Each header has a name and a value, like 'Content-Type: application/json' (which tells the server that the body is in JSON format) or 'x-api-key: sk-ant-abc123' (which proves the caller has paid for the service). The right mental picture is that headers wrap around the message the way an envelope wraps around a letter. The headers carry the addressing and the metadata on the outside, and the actual message the caller wants to deliver sits inside the body underneath. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  9. headless-browser A headless browser is a web browser running with no visible window: it loads the page, clicks buttons, and reads the result, but no human ever sees a screen. Useful for legitimate automation (testing a website, scraping public data with permission), and also a tell-tale fingerprint when a bot is filling out a survey on behalf of a panelist who is not actually at her keyboard. First introduced: Chapter 5.
  10. headless-Chrome A headless browser is a copy of Chrome (or Firefox) that runs with no visible window and is controlled by a script rather than by a human clicking. The browser still parses the HTML, runs the JavaScript, and builds the DOM the way it would for a real user, but pixels are never actually drawn to a screen because no human is watching. Googlebot uses a headless Chrome to render JavaScript-heavy pages, Playwright uses one for automated testing, and any AI bot that needs to execute page JavaScript before reading the content uses one too. First introduced: Chapter 18.
  11. hedonic value Hedonic value is the value a buyer gets from the experience of the shopping trip itself, independent of whether they end up buying anything. The shopper asks whether the browsing was fun, whether the act of shopping carried its own reward, and whether the trip produced its own small charge of excitement. A Saturday afternoon at the Fayetteville farmers' market is the canonical example, and the Babin-Darden-Griffin 1994 shopping-value scale captures it as a survey factor that is statistically distinct from utilitarian value. First introduced: Chapter 4.
  12. held-out hit rate The held-out hit rate is the proportion of respondents in the validation sample whose actual choice the segmentation model correctly predicts, measured against a sample of buyers the model was never trained on. The analyst fits the segmentation on the estimation half of the data, then uses the fitted model to predict each held-out respondent's choice, then counts how often the prediction matches the choice the respondent actually made. A higher held-out hit rate means the segmentation found real heterogeneity in the population rather than fitting noise that happens to appear in the estimation sample. Typical conjoint benchmarks land between 50 and 70 percent hit rate, against a random-choice baseline that would be 20 to 25 percent for a five-option choice set, which is the kind of margin that tells the brand team the segments are worth defending in front of a methodology review. First introduced: Chapter 6.
  13. Helen Nissenbaum's contextual integrity Contextual integrity is Helen Nissenbaum's framework for analyzing whether a given information flow respects privacy, developed during her tenure as NYU professor of information science. She first published the framework in 'Privacy as Contextual Integrity' (Washington Law Review 79 Wash. L. Rev. 119, 2004) and expanded it into Privacy in Context (Stanford University Press, 2009). The core argument is that privacy is preserved when information flows respect the norms of the originating context (a medical conversation stays inside the medical relationship, a classroom conversation stays inside the educational relationship) and violated when any parameter of the flow shifts across boundaries the original disclosure did not contemplate. The framework names five parameters that describe each information flow (subject, sender, recipient, attribute, transmission principle) and nine transmission principles that govern when a handoff is acceptable. First introduced: Chapter 26.
  14. Helium 10 Helium 10 is the larger of the two dominant Amazon-research suites, covering both product research and the auction-based ads sellers run inside Amazon (which the platform calls pay-per-click, or PPC, advertising). After the April 2026 price increase, the plans run as follows. Free is the entry tier with limited tool access. Platinum at $129 per month, or $99 per month if billed annually, fits sellers in the $0-to-$100K revenue range. Diamond at $359 per month, or $279 per month annual, fits sellers in the $100K-to-$10M range. Enterprise at $1,499 per month annual fits brands above $10M. The signature tools that show up in every Helium 10 workflow are Black Box (a product database the seller filters by BSR, reviews, sales, or dimensions), X-Ray (a Chrome extension that overlays per-product data on any Amazon search-results page), Magnet (a keyword-research tool), and Cerebro (a reverse-ASIN search that returns every keyword a competitor's product ranks for). First introduced: Chapter 13.
  15. Helpful Content System The Helpful Content System is a set of Google ranking signals introduced in 2022-2023 and folded into the core search algorithm in March 2024 that demotes pages written to game search engines rather than to help readers. Pages without first-hand experience, original information, or a named expert author lose visibility, and pages by named experts with original research gain it; Felipe's chapter cites Saffron Edge's measurement of a roughly 45 percent reduction in low-quality unoriginal-content visibility across the indexed web as the published evidence the system is working as intended. First introduced: Chapter 18.
  16. heterogeneous-agent architecture The heterogeneous-agent architecture is the pattern Peter Belcak and his NVIDIA co-authors named in their 2025 paper, and it generalizes the chapter's escalation rule to the model-tier choice inside each worker. The orchestrator (the planner at the top of the agent loop) is a frontier large-language-model call, but each specialist worker runs on a small language model unless the team's eval harness shows that the small model measurably underperforms on the worker's specific task. The cost saving is large because frontier models cost roughly ten to thirty times more per token than small models at parity reasoning on bounded tasks. First introduced: Chapter 27.
  17. Hi-Lo Hi-Lo pricing is the opposite price-structure commitment to EDLP, where the firm posts a higher regular price and then breaks it with frequent deep promotional troughs. Kroger, Albertsons, Macy's, and most regional U.S. supermarkets run Hi-Lo. The promotional trough is a separate strategic lever from the regular shelf price, because it shapes how buyers perceive the firm's price image and pulls cherry-picker traffic into the store on the weeks the discount runs. First introduced: Chapter 10.
  18. hidden Markov models A hidden Markov model treats each customer as being in one of a small number of unobserved internal states at any given moment, such as 'cold lead,' 'considering,' or 'loyal,' and assumes the customer moves between those states with fixed transition probabilities the algorithm has to learn from the data. The marketer cannot directly see which state a customer is in, but they can see signals (a website visit, an email open, a purchase) whose probability of appearing depends on the underlying hidden state, and the algorithm reasons backwards from the observed signals to infer the most likely state path. Hidden Markov models were the standard customer-journey-modeling baseline in marketing analytics for roughly fifteen years before transformer-based sequence models displaced them around 2023. First introduced: Chapter 6.
  19. Hide My Email Hide My Email is an Apple iCloud+ feature that lets the user create a random throwaway email address whenever a brand asks for an email at signup. The throwaway address looks like a string of random characters followed by @icloud.com. When the brand sends a message to that throwaway address, Apple routes the message through its own servers and forwards it to the user's real iCloud address without ever exposing the real address to the brand. The user can disable the throwaway address at any time, instantly cutting off all future messages from that brand without having to click an unsubscribe link. First introduced: Chapter 21.
  20. Hierarchical Gaussian Process Hierarchical Gaussian Processes are a flexible Bayesian model class that fits how channel effects change over time without requiring the analyst to assume the effect is constant or follows a fixed shape (the technical term is ‘non-stationary’). In an MMM context, the Gaussian Process learns how each channel’s effectiveness drifts week by week. The class is usually paired with adstock (carryover across weeks) and saturation curves (diminishing returns within a week) to give the model both the time-shape and the spend-shape it needs. First introduced: Chapter 24.
  21. hierarchy of effects The hierarchy of effects is a classical six-rung ladder describing the stages a buyer climbs from first awareness of a brand through final purchase. The rungs are awareness, knowledge, liking, preference, conviction, and purchase. The ladder is grouped into three stages: cognitive (awareness and knowledge, the head-level rungs), affective (liking and preference, the heart-level rungs), and conative (conviction and purchase, the action-level rungs). The ladder is planning vocabulary rather than a literal sequence: real buyers skip rungs and revisit rungs constantly. The framework is still useful for matching channels to the rung they actually move. First introduced: Chapter 24.
  22. higher purpose, stakeholder orientation, conscious leadership, and conscious culture The four pillars of conscious capitalism come from John Mackey and Rajendra Sisodia's 2013 book and extend the customer-mix logic into a firm-architecture frame. The first pillar is higher purpose, meaning a transcendent reason for the firm's existence beyond profit maximization. The second is stakeholder orientation, meaning value is created for and by an interconnected system of customers, employees, suppliers, investors, communities, and the natural environment rather than for shareholders alone. The third is conscious leadership, meaning leaders are oriented toward service of the purpose rather than toward personal gain. The fourth is conscious culture, meaning the day-to-day fabric of trust, accountability, and care inside the organization actually delivers what the purpose claims. First introduced: Chapter 2.
  23. Highspot Highspot is a sales-enablement platform built around a content-and-coaching library and the firm's Nexus AI engine, which produces analytics and content recommendations the rep sees before each call. The pre-merger product's anchor capability was an enablement workflow tied directly to in-call coaching prompts the rep received in real time. Highspot signed a definitive merger agreement with Seismic in February 2026 at a combined valuation of roughly $6 billion. First introduced: Chapter 23.
  24. HiLo (High-Low pricing) High-Low pricing (HiLo) is a strategy where a retailer charges higher prices on most items day-to-day but runs frequent promotions that temporarily drop selected items to or below the EDLP equivalent. The promise to the shopper is that the careful shopper will find a deal this week if they wait for it. Most U.S. supermarkets, department stores, and specialty stores run HiLo. First introduced: Chapter 12.
  25. HIPAA HIPAA is the Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act of 1996, the US federal statute that governs how identifiable health data may be used and disclosed. The Privacy Rule at 45 CFR Part 164 sets the rules on use and disclosure, and the Security Rule mandates administrative, physical, and technical safeguards on the systems that store the data. Enforcement runs through the US Department of Health and Human Services Office for Civil Rights, with per-violation penalties up to $2.13 million per category in 2025 inflation-adjusted dollars. The load-bearing marketing rule sits at 45 CFR §164.508(a)(3): written, signed patient authorization is required before any PHI is used in a marketing communication that benefits a third party. First introduced: Chapter 26.
  26. historical-power-imbalance lens Lin, Kim, Uduehi, and Keinan showed in their 2024 Journal of Consumer Research paper that consumers judge cultural borrowing in a brand offer through what they call a historical-power-imbalance lens. The lens has three independent and additive determinants. The first asks how the firm obtained the cultural element it is using, whether through consent, compensation, or genuine partnership. The second asks how the firm represents the source culture in the final offer, whether faithfully and in context or stripped down to a stereotype.
