The methodology starts from a financial base similar to Interbrand's and then multiplies it by a Meaningful-Different-Salient power score drawn from a multi-thousand-respondent buyer survey, so the resulting dollar figures are the highest of the three major rankings and explicitly forward-looking rather than tied to current operating profit. First introduced: Chapter 7.
  4. Kantar or Ipsos engagement A Kantar or Ipsos engagement is the classical way to run a distinctive-asset audit, where a specialist research firm fields a 200-to-400-buyer category survey to its consumer panel and then hand-codes the open-end fame and uniqueness questions for each candidate asset. The turnaround typically runs four to six weeks, and the deliverable is a static PDF that the brand team paginates through during the next quarterly review. The 2026 alternative is to expose the same panel through an MCP server so the working numbers come back in seconds rather than weeks. First introduced: Chapter 16.
  5. kappa score Cohen's kappa is a zero-to-one score that measures how often two coders agree on the same code, after subtracting the agreement they would have hit by chance alone. A kappa above zero-point-eight is called strong agreement, between zero-point-six and zero-point-eight is acceptable, and below zero-point-six is unreliable enough that the rubric itself probably needs fixing. The standard quality check on a human-AI coding pass is to compute kappa between the AI's codes and a human reviewer's codes on a fifty-to-one-hundred-response validation sample. First introduced: Chapter 5.
  6. Karen Nelson-Field Founder of Amplified Intelligence and a former senior researcher at the Ehrenberg-Bass Institute. Her panel studies between 2018 and 2025 combined webcam-based eye-tracking and facial-detection at scale, producing the dataset the IAB and MRC adopted as the 2025 Attention Measurement Guidelines. First introduced: Chapter 16.
  7. Karnani critique Aneel Karnani's 2007 California Management Review paper that reframed the BOP debate by arguing the four-billion-people headline overstates addressable market by roughly a factor of ten, that sachet packaging carries a documented environmental externality, and that the poor are better served as producers (sourced suppliers, formal-economy employees) than as consumers of additional shampoo. Most 2024-2026 practitioner-grade BOP strategy is BOP 2.0 in the sense Karnani forced: consumer-good revenue paired with producer-side investment. First introduced: Chapter 15.
  8. Keller pyramid The Keller pyramid is Kevin Lane Keller's visual map of how a brand builds value in the buyer's head, starting from the base (salience: does the brand come to mind) and rising through performance and imagery, then judgments and feelings, to resonance at the apex. Each higher level depends on the levels below it holding, which is why brands cannot skip the base and jump straight to resonance. First introduced: Chapter 7.
  9. key performance indicator A key performance indicator (KPI) is a number tied to a business outcome the reader of the dashboard can directly act on, with a target value set in advance so the meeting can ask whether the number hit the target. Revenue per visitor with a pre-set $4.20 target is a KPI because the team can both act on it and judge it against the target. Bounce rate by itself is not a KPI because there is no agreed answer to whether the current value is good or bad. First introduced: Chapter 18.
  10. key performance indicators A key performance indicator, or KPI, is a number tied to a business outcome the reader of the dashboard can act on, with a target set in advance. Revenue per visitor with a target of $4.20 is a KPI because the team can act on it and the target tells the meeting whether the number landed; bounce rate without a target is reporting because there is no agreed answer to whether the current value is good or bad. First introduced: Chapter 18.
  11. keyword indexing Keyword indexing is the visibility half of App Store Optimization, which means the work of making sure the listing actually appears high up in search results when a user types a relevant query into the App Store or Google Play. When a student searches for the word 'flashcards' in the App Store, the system has to decide which dozen apps to show first, and it makes that decision by reading the title, the subtitle, the hidden keyword field on iOS, and the description on Google Play. Keyword indexing is measured by where the listing actually lands in the search-result rank for the keywords the brand cares about. First introduced: Chapter 14.
  12. keywords A keyword is the phrase a buyer types into the search box, and 'keyword research' is the practitioner discipline of deciding which phrases are worth the brand's effort to compete for. Most categories have a handful of high-volume head terms ('CRM,' 'mattress,' 'water bottle') that are hard to rank for, and a long tail of low-volume specific phrases that are easier to win on but require a wider portfolio of pages. First introduced: Chapter 18.
  13. King Midas problem The King Midas problem is Stuart Russell's 2019 framing of a structural failure in how humans specify goals for capable optimizers. King Midas asked the gods for the power to turn everything he touched into gold and got exactly what he asked for: his food and his daughter both turned to gold and Midas died of starvation. Russell's claim is that every human-specifiable objective is incomplete in the same way, and a sufficiently capable optimizer will exploit the incompleteness by hitting the literal specification in ways the specifier did not anticipate. The marketing analog: an agent told 'maximize click-through rate on this email campaign' may discover that sending the same email to every recipient three times a day technically maximizes the metric while destroying the brand's relationship with its list. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  14. Klaviyo Klaviyo is the dominant email-marketing platform for Shopify-native direct-to-consumer brands. It builds segmented lists from Shopify customer and order data, runs automated flows like the welcome series, the abandoned-cart sequence, the post-purchase flow, and the win-back campaign, and sends both broadcast campaigns and triggered email and SMS messages. The platform is free under 250 contacts, with paid tiers starting at $45 per month, and Klaviyo's 2024 disclosures put the platform at roughly 40,000 brands running on it. A candle-store student on the free tier can run all five of the core flows on a single welcome series before ever paying for an upgrade. First introduced: Chapter 13.
  15. Klaviyo MCP server The Klaviyo MCP server is the official connector Klaviyo published for its direct-to-consumer email-and-SMS platform, reaching general availability by early 2026. On May 7, 2026 Klaviyo announced an expanded integration with Anthropic that wires the server into Claude.ai and Claude Cowork across the consumer and enterprise tiers. The server exposes campaigns, flows, profiles, and a Query Metric Aggregates tool that returns raw performance numbers on demand. For a direct-to-consumer brand running a Black Friday push, the workflow change is immediate: the brand manager can ask Claude to compare open rates across yesterday’s three abandoned-cart campaigns, identify which subject line outperformed, and write a draft brief for tomorrow’s flow update without ever leaving the chat window. First introduced: Chapter 24.
  16. Knowledge Knowledge is the fourth pillar of the Brand Asset Valuator (BAV) instrument. It measures the depth and accuracy of the buyer's understanding of what the brand actually stands for, not just whether the buyer recognizes the brand at all. Knowledge is the lagging indicator of the four original pillars: it grows the slowest under campaign pressure and falls the slowest under neglect. That lag is why high-Stature brands (high Esteem multiplied by high Knowledge) can keep their PowerGrid position for years after their Differentiation has eroded against newer challengers. First introduced: Chapter 7.
  17. knowledge cutoff The knowledge cutoff is the date the model's training data ends. The model has no internal awareness of any event that happened after that date, because the training corpus was frozen on the cutoff day. A model with a January 2025 cutoff asked about an April 2026 campaign will produce text that sounds like an answer and is in fact a fluent guess about events the model never read. The fix is to use a tool like web search inside the chat, which hands the model recent information the training corpus did not contain; both Claude and ChatGPT will tell the marketer their cutoff date if the marketer asks. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  18. knowledge dissemination Knowledge dissemination is what Erik Brynjolfsson, Li, and Raymond's 2025 Quarterly Journal of Economics paper calls the underlying mechanism behind AI copilots in service settings. The model has watched the veterans handle thousands of calls, learned the moves the veterans make implicitly, and now serves those moves to the rookie at the moment of the call. Translated into plain English, the copilot codifies the senior team's tacit playbook and hands it to the rookie one decision at a time, so the rookie does not have to spend two years learning the playbook the slow way. First introduced: Chapter 23.
  19. knowledge-graph The knowledge graph is Google's structured database of named things (people, brands, places, products, events) and the verified facts about each of them. AI engines use the same graph layer (sometimes a copy of it, sometimes their own equivalent) to resolve which 'Stanley' a query means: the cup brand, the hand-tool brand, or the actor Stanley Tucci. Without a strong knowledge-graph entry, the engine can confuse the brand with a competitor or skip the citation entirely because it cannot decide what the query was about. First introduced: Chapter 18.
  20. known-value items Known-value items, often shortened to KVIs, are the products whose prices buyers know well from frequent purchase, such as milk, eggs, bananas, and a 12-pack of Coke. Shoppers use the prices on these few items to judge whether a store is expensive or cheap overall, even when the rest of the assortment varies widely, which is why retailers price the KVIs aggressively cheap and recover the margin on the long tail of items the shopper does not track. First introduced: Chapter 12.
  21. knowsAbout knowsAbout is a schema.org property the brand adds to its Organisation block to name the topics it claims expertise on. Declaring three to seven category-authority topics (for Stanley: insulated drinkware, vacuum bottles, hydration, tumbler care) gives the engine a knowledge-graph anchor against which it can weigh off-site citation signals, which is the structural reason the March 2026 Google core update elevated knowsAbout as an AI Mode source-selection signal. First introduced: Chapter 18.
  22. Kogut-Singh index The Kogut-Singh index is a 1988 single-number measure of cultural distance between any two countries. It works by taking the squared difference between the two countries' Hofstede scores on four dimensions (power distance, individualism, masculinity, uncertainty avoidance), dividing each squared difference by how much that dimension varies across all countries, averaging the four, and reporting the square root as one summary number. It is the most-cited cultural-distance measure in international-business research with more than 5,000 academic citations. First introduced: Chapter 15.
  23. Kolmogorov-Smirnov The Kolmogorov-Smirnov test is a way of comparing two distributions of numbers to decide whether they look like they came from the same underlying population, without assuming any particular shape for that population (the technical term for that assumption-free property is non-parametric). The test works by drawing the two cumulative curves on the same axes and measuring the largest vertical gap between them. A larger gap means the two distributions are less likely to be the same; a small gap means the differences could plausibly have come from random sampling. Inside Evidently AI, the test is the default check on numeric features like weekly channel spend or impressions per day, where the analyst wants to know whether this week looks like the prior eight weeks or has shifted into a new regime. First introduced: Chapter 24.
  24. Kotler-Rackham-Krishnaswamy taxonomy The Kotler-Rackham-Krishnaswamy taxonomy is the four-stage alignment-maturity ladder the three authors published in the Harvard Business Review in 2006, and the four rungs are Undefined, Defined, Aligned, and Integrated. At the Undefined rung, the two functions barely talk to each other. At the Defined rung, both functions have written down what they each do, but each function still reports to a separate scorecard. Most firms plateau here, because the political cost of merging scorecards is genuinely high. The move from Defined up to Aligned requires changing compensation so that the two functions share at least one weighted metric on a common scorecard, which is the first time their incentives actually agree. The move from Aligned up to Integrated requires merging the two operating systems entirely, which is what the modern revenue-operations function attempts when it consolidates the marketing-automation platform and the customer-relationship-management database under one team. First introduced: Chapter 23.
  25. KPI A key performance indicator (KPI) is the specific number the firm watches to know whether a particular layer of the plan is working. Common marketing KPIs include customer acquisition cost (CAC), lifetime value (LTV), retention rate, and brand-tracking scores like aided awareness and consideration. A working plan names a small number of KPIs per layer (one or two for acquisition, one or two for retention, one for instrumentation health) rather than reporting fifty metrics with no clear hierarchy. First introduced: Chapter 24.
12 // l

L L

  1. Labeling Labeling is the move where the rep names the buyer's emotion or position out loud so the buyer can correct it rather than defend it. The canonical openings are tentative and third-person: ‘It seems like the last vendor burned you on the timeline,’ ‘It sounds like procurement is the gatekeeper here,’ or ‘It looks like budget is the real constraint.’ The rep avoids ‘I'm hearing that...’ because the word ‘I’ reasserts the rep's presence and triggers defensiveness rather than recognition. First introduced: Chapter 23.
  2. laddering Laddering is a follow-up technique in qualitative interviewing that climbs from feature to benefit to underlying value. The interviewer keeps asking some version of ‘why does that matter to you’ after each answer, until the buyer reaches the personal value that anchors her preference. The reason this matters is that brands win on values, not on features: two products with identical features can win or lose against the same buyer based on which underlying value each brand's story connects to. First introduced: Chapter 5.
  3. Ladenschlussgesetz The Ladenschlussgesetz is Germany's Shop Closing Act, the law that capped retail trading hours at roughly 6:30 a.m. to 8:00 p.m. on weekdays, ran shorter on Saturdays, and closed stores almost completely on Sundays for most of the post-war period. Walmart entered Germany under the strictest version of the law in 1997 and could not amortize its U.S.-style supercenter footprint over the limited weekly hours the law allowed. The law was progressively liberalized between 2003 and 2006, but the relief came too late for Walmart's operation. First introduced: Chapter 15.
  4. LangGraph LangGraph is LangChain Inc.'s orchestration runtime, which reached version 1.0 on October 22, 2025. It models an agent loop as a typed state graph (a directed diagram where each node is one step of the agent and each edge is a transition between steps), and it persists the run's state at every node, which means a run that loses its host process at any node can be resumed where it left off rather than restarted from the beginning. The connector library called langchain-mcp-adapters is what lets a LangGraph node call an MCP server directly, so the same HubSpot or Notion MCP server plugs into the framework without re-engineering. First introduced: Chapter 27.
  5. LangSmith LangSmith is LangChain Inc.'s observability platform, which sits next to the LangGraph runtime and records every language-model call, every tool call, and every intermediate state of the agent loop into a queryable trace the team can replay after the fact. The trace is what lets a capstone team debug an overnight run on Monday morning by reading what the agent actually did rather than guessing from the final output, and the platform's trace volume grew roughly twelvefold year over year through October 2025 as enterprise teams standardized on the framework. First introduced: Chapter 27.
  6. language model A language model is a computer program trained on a large body of text that takes text in and produces text out. The program reads the marketer's prompt and predicts the next word, then the next, then the next, until it stops. Claude, ChatGPT, and Gemini are all language models. The qualifier 'large' (yielding the acronym LLM for Large Language Model) means the weight count and training corpus are big enough that the model handles most kinds of writing and reasoning tasks without task-specific training. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  7. Last-click attribution Last-click attribution is the default rule every ad-platform dashboard ships with: 100 percent of the conversion credit goes to the last channel the buyer touched before purchasing. The rule is easy to compute and easy to read, but it is structurally wrong because the earlier channels that produced the demand the last click captured (display, podcasts, AI citations, organic social) all score zero on the dashboard, which biases the marketing director toward cutting the channels that actually create demand. First introduced: Chapter 18.
  8. last mile The last mile is the final segment of a delivery, from the local fulfillment node to the buyer’s actual doorstep. It is historically the most expensive segment in the entire logistics chain, typically consuming 40 to 60 percent of total shipping cost even though it covers a small fraction of the total distance. The reason is that a last-mile van amortizes its driver and vehicle cost across only fifty or so doorsteps, while a long-haul truck amortizes the same cost across thousands of units headed to one destination. First introduced: Chapter 11.
  9. late-night-FM-DJ voice The late-night-FM-DJ voice is the calm, slow, downward-inflected register Chris Voss adapted from radio broadcasting. The cadence creates authority and trust without triggering the defensiveness the assertive voice does. Reps use it sparingly and deliberately, at the three moments in a call where it carries the most weight: when setting an anchor at the start of negotiation, when holding a price under pushback, and when delivering hard truths the buyer needs to hear without taking offense. First introduced: Chapter 23.
  10. latent-class logit A latent-class logit is a choice model that assumes the customer base is made up of a small number S of hidden (latent) segments, where each segment has its own set of brand preferences and its own sensitivity to price. The analyst does not get to see which segment any particular customer belongs to; the algorithm has to infer the assignment from the customer's observed purchases by finding the segment whose preference profile makes those purchases most likely. The number of segments S is itself unknown, and the standard procedure is to try several candidate values (typically two through six), refit the model for each, and pick the S whose Bayesian Information Criterion score is lowest on a holdout sample of customers the model has not seen. First introduced: Chapter 6.
  11. latitude of acceptance The latitude of acceptance is a band of prices around the buyer's reference price within which the buyer accepts the posted price without bothering to search for alternatives. The concept comes from Muzafer Sherif's social-judgment work on assimilation-contrast theory, which Kent Monroe later applied to pricing. Prices that fall inside the latitude band trigger assimilation, meaning the buyer treats them as essentially equivalent to the reference and does not flag them as a problem. Prices that fall outside the band trigger contrast, meaning the buyer flags the price as too high or too low and starts searching for alternatives. The lower boundary of the band is the structural reason price-quality inference exists, because prices below it make the buyer suspect the product cannot really be the genuine version they expect. First introduced: Chapter 10.
  12. Law of Replacement The Law of Replacement says that every closed, lost, or disqualified opportunity removes one slot from the salesforce's pipeline, and today's prospecting is what refills the slot the rep will close from in roughly 30 to 90 days. The practical consequence is that under-delivering qualified-lead volume in May books a quota miss for August, well before the Q3 scoreboard records the miss. Marketing's commitment to monthly lead volume is therefore a commitment to next quarter's revenue, not this quarter's, which is why the audit cadence has to lead the bonus cadence. First introduced: Chapter 23.
  13. Law of the Few The Law of the Few is the central claim of Malcolm Gladwell's 2000 book The Tipping Point, which argued that social cascades are driven by a small set of high-influence individuals Gladwell labeled Connectors (the well-networked), Mavens (the expert), and Salesmen (the persuasive). Two decades of empirical work refuted that picture. When researchers tried to predict cascade size from seed-account features, the best per-seed prediction on Twitter capped out at an R-squared near 0.32, meaning two-thirds of the variance in cascade outcomes was determined by something other than the seed's traits. The practical brand reading is to seed a portfolio rather than concentrate spend on a few high-profile accounts. First introduced: Chapter 19.
  14. lead agent A lead agent is the orchestrator in a multi-agent run. The lead holds the research plan, spawns subagents to investigate individual branches of the question in parallel, evaluates what each subagent returned, and writes the final synthesis. The lead is usually a more capable (and more expensive) model than the subagents are, because the lead's job is harder than any individual subagent's job. First introduced: Chapter 5.
  15. lead magnet A lead magnet is a long-form piece of content, such as a research report, a benchmark study, an ebook, or a framework template, that the brand publishes behind an email-capture form rather than openly on the public web. The buyer trades an email address to download the asset, and the brand then uses that email to enter the buyer into a marketing-automation flow where follow-up content arrives in their inbox on a defined schedule. The implicit deal is that the asset has to be valuable enough to be worth the friction of typing an email, which is the test most generic gated content fails. First introduced: Chapter 17.
  16. lead-user Lead-user research, developed by Eric von Hippel and codified in his 2005 MIT Press book Democratizing Innovation, asks the firm to find the buyers at the leading edge of a category. These leading-edge buyers are the users whose needs are ahead of the mainstream and who have already designed solutions for themselves rather than waiting for a manufacturer to do it. Whitewater kayakers building their own boats out of fiberglass before any manufacturer offered the model, or surgeons modifying surgical staplers in the operating room before any device maker shipped the version they wanted, are typical lead users. The manufacturer's role then shifts from inventing the new product to identifying what these lead users have already built and scaling it for the mainstream. First introduced: Chapter 8.
  17. leads A lead is a buyer who has registered interest in the seller's product through some marketing-owned action: filling out a contact form on the website, downloading a whitepaper, registering for a webinar, or clicking a tracked link in a newsletter. The lead has not yet had a screening conversation with a salesperson, which means the seller does not yet know whether the buyer has budget, authority, or a real need. First introduced: Chapter 23.
  18. leagile supply chain A leagile supply chain is the lean-plus-agile hybrid Christopher and Towill named in their 2001 work. The chain is run lean upstream (long replenishment lead times, high volume, low cost per unit) and agile downstream (short lead times, broad variety, responsive to demand signals) with a decoupling point in between where generic inventory is held until real demand is observed. Postponement strategies (delay packaging, delay labeling, delay configuration, delay localization) implement leagility in practice. HP’s 1990s European printer operation is the canonical example: ship plain printers from Singapore to a European DC, then add country-specific power cords and manuals at the DC after country-mix demand is observed. First introduced: Chapter 11.
  19. Lean Startup The Lean Startup is Eric Ries's 2011 book and the methodology it codifies, built on Steve Blank's customer-development model from the early 2000s. The method asks every new product team to treat its plans as hypotheses to test rather than as truths to execute, and it organizes the testing around five principles: validated learning (every experiment teaches the team something it can act on), build-measure-learn (the fastest loop is what compounds), the minimum viable product (the smallest experiment that produces real signal), pivot-versus-persevere (decide each cycle whether to change direction or keep going), and innovation accounting (measure progress in learning rather than in revenue alone). First introduced: Chapter 8.
  20. lean supply chain A lean supply chain is tuned for cost efficiency in categories where demand is predictable enough to forecast accurately. The design assumes high volumes per SKU, long replenishment lead times, narrow product range, low inventory, and no slack. Toyota’s automotive supply chain and a private-label canned-goods chain are canonical lean examples. The strength is unit cost; the weakness is fragility, because any disruption that breaks the forecast (a port closure, a viral demand spike) cascades through the chain with no buffer to absorb it. First introduced: Chapter 11.
  21. learning curves Learning curves are the patterns in how the way people use a tool changes as they accumulate experience with it. In the Anthropic Economic Index, a learning curve is what shows up when the program resamples its measurement quarter after quarter and watches which task categories grow, which shrink, and how the share of any one category moves over time. The reading from the November 2025 to February 2026 comparison was that long-tail uses grew faster than the top-ten uses, that automated API workflows kept gaining ground against chat-window prompting, and that users who had been on the platform six months or more had a 10 percent higher success rate on the same tasks than newer users. First introduced: Chapter 24.
  22. Ledge The Ledge is Stephen Schiffman's recovery script for the moment in a cold call when the prospect raises a stated objection. The rep uses the objection itself as the explicit reason for the meeting. When the prospect says ‘we already have a vendor,’ the rep converts the objection into the appointment platform: ‘that is exactly why I called, because the firms who already have a vendor are the ones who most often discover they have been overpaying.’ The Ledge does not argue with the objection; it uses the objection as the meeting hook. First introduced: Chapter 23.
  23. leftmost-digit encoding Leftmost-digit encoding, documented by Thomas and Morwitz in their 2005 Journal of Consumer Research paper, is the cognitive shortcut by which buyers read prices left-to-right and weight the leftmost digit disproportionately when forming their first impression of the price. The buyer reads $2.99 as "two-something" before the brain even finishes parsing the cents portion of the number. The effect strengthens at digit-boundary jumps, where $3.00 reads meaningfully larger than $2.99 because the leftmost digit changed from 2 to 3. The effect weakens within-digit, where $2.50 and $2.49 feel essentially the same because the leftmost digit is identical. First introduced: Chapter 10.
  24. legitimate power Legitimate power rests on a contract or agreement that gives one channel member the legally enforceable right to require certain behaviors from another. The franchise agreement that requires a franchisee to buy ingredients only from the franchisor’s approved supplier list is exercising legitimate power. The leverage is predictable because both sides know the rule in advance and can litigate it if needed, but it offers no leverage at all outside the four corners of the agreement. First introduced: Chapter 11.
  25. Levelpath Levelpath is an AI-native procurement platform founded in 2022 in San Francisco and now the 2025-2026 challenger to incumbents SAP Ariba and Coupa in the source-to-pay category. The firm closed a $55 million Series B in October 2025 led by Battery Ventures, with $99.5 million raised in total. The product's anchor capability is a ‘front-door agent’ that intercepts the procurement requisition before any seller contact and runs the classify-check-shortlist workflow autonomously, returning to a human procurement officer only for the final approval step. First introduced: Chapter 23.
  26. levels A specific value the attribute can take on inside the study. The graphic attribute has three levels (vintage-block, minimalist, animal mascot); the price attribute has three levels (20, 25, 30 dollars). Levels must span the realistic range the buyer would actually see at the shelf or at checkout, otherwise the recovered preferences are extrapolations the model cannot honestly defend. First introduced: Chapter 5.
  27. lever floor The lever floor names the operational consequence of the STEPPS balance principle, which is that the framework works as a six-row checklist where each row must clear a minimum rather than as a sum of points. The empirical floor that fell out of Berger and Milkman's New York Times corpus sat at 30 out of 100 on every lever, so a balanced post with no row below 30 traveled further across audiences than a lopsided post that scored 90 on one lever and zero on another, even when the lopsided post carried the higher total. The Monday rule for a brand team auditing a draft is to fix the lowest row first before adding to the strongest one. First introduced: Chapter 16.
  28. Levitt Theodore Levitt was a Harvard Business School professor whose 1983 Harvard Business Review article 'The Globalization of Markets' argued that converging communications and product technology were producing a single global consumer who wanted the same products everywhere. The standard-bearer position has not aged well in food and apparel, but it still holds for categories like smartphones and professional software where the buyer's job really does not change at the border. First introduced: Chapter 15.
  29. licensed-JV multidomestic with retained brand control A licensed-JV multidomestic structure is the hybrid arrangement in which a multinational sells operating control of a specific country business to a local-capital partner while keeping a minority equity stake, the steady revenue stream from licensing its brand, and centralized authority over brand standards. Yum-China (the 2016 spin-off of KFC and Pizza Hut China), McDonald's-CITIC (2017), Burger King-Cartesian (2012), and Starbucks-Boyu (2026) are the canonical examples, and the pattern lets a global brand stay in a market its operating model can no longer run alone. First introduced: Chapter 15.
  30. lifecycle program Lifecycle marketing is the discipline of communicating with a buyer differently at each stage of their relationship with the brand. The same email blast to all of them is the thing this chapter argues against. Each stage gets its own trigger, message, and rhythm. The stages are roughly: prospect (has not bought yet), first purchase, activation (the buyer used what they bought and is forming a habit), regular, lapsed (they have gone quiet), and won-back. First introduced: Chapter 21.
  31. lifetime value Lifetime value, often shortened to LTV, is the total contribution margin a single customer produces across her entire relationship with the brand. The math for computing it depends on the business model. For a one-shot direct-to-consumer business where each buyer makes a handful of repeat purchases, lifetime value is the contribution margin per order multiplied by the expected number of purchases that buyer will make over her relationship. For a subscription business where the buyer pays on a recurring schedule, the formula collapses to a single short line: take the contribution margin from one monthly shipment and divide it by the monthly cancellation rate written as a decimal. The reason that division gives lifetime value is that one divided by the monthly cancellation rate equals the expected number of shipments before the subscriber leaves, so dividing the per-shipment margin by the churn rate is mathematically the same operation as multiplying that margin by the expected shipment count. First introduced: Chapter 13.
  32. lifetime value (LTV) Lifetime Value (LTV) is the total revenue the firm expects to collect from one customer over the entire span of their relationship with the product. The standard shorthand for a subscription product is the monthly price divided by the monthly churn rate, because that ratio is the average number of months a customer stays before cancelling multiplied by what they pay each month. A $10 monthly subscription with 5 percent monthly churn produces an LTV of $200 per converted user. First introduced: Chapter 10.
  33. lifetime value of a subscriber Lifetime value, often shortened to LTV, is the total contribution margin a single subscriber produces across her entire span as a subscriber before she cancels. For a subscription where every shipment contributes the same dollar amount of margin and the monthly cancellation probability stays constant, the math collapses to a single short formula: take the margin from one shipment and divide it by the monthly churn rate written as a decimal. The reason that division works is that one divided by the monthly churn rate equals the expected number of shipments a subscriber will pay for before she cancels, so dividing the per-shipment margin by the churn rate is the same operation as multiplying that margin by the expected shipment count. The result is the dollar amount the brand can afford to spend acquiring a subscriber and still break even on the relationship. First introduced: Chapter 13.
  34. lift In marketing-research vocabulary, a ‘lift’ is the size of the improvement that the treatment version caused over the control version in an experiment. The example to anchor it: suppose the old version of a checkout page converted five percent of visitors into buyers, and the new version converts seven percent. The lift is two percentage points in absolute terms, because seven minus five equals two. The same move can also be reported as a forty percent relative lift, because going from five to seven is a forty-percent increase relative to the starting five. The reason this distinction matters in practice is that vendor reports often use whichever framing makes the result sound more impressive, so a marketer reading a lift number always has to confirm whether the number on the page is the absolute percentage-point gap or the relative percent change before deciding what to do with it. First introduced: Chapter 5.
  35. light buyers A light buyer is a buyer who purchases the brand only rarely, typically once or twice a year. Light buyers form the long tail of any brand's buyer base and collectively contribute most of the brand's revenue, because there are many more of them than there are heavy buyers. The Sharp empirical record names light-buyer reach as the budget priority because next year's growth comes mostly from this group rather than from increasing the frequency of the heavy minority. First introduced: Chapter 7.
  36. Likert scale A Likert scale is a fixed-anchor rating instrument introduced by Rensis Likert in 1932. The respondent reads a statement (such as ‘I would consider buying this product’) and picks one of five or seven labeled points (strongly disagree, disagree, neither, agree, strongly agree). The points are evenly spaced labels rather than measured intervals, but the resulting ordinal data is treated as approximately continuous when averaged across respondents. The 1-to-7 variant is the dominant convention in marketing-research questionnaires because seven points give the respondent enough resolution to distinguish mild from strong agreement without overwhelming the cognitive map of the scale. First introduced: Chapter 5.
  37. line extension A line extension is a new product an existing brand launches inside a category the brand already serves, where the new item is a different size, scent, formulation, color, or configuration of something already in the line. New Dannon yogurt flavors, new Stanley Quencher colorways, and the introduction of Tide Pods alongside Tide Liquid are all line extensions. The risk is comparatively low because the brand is operating in a category buyers already grant it credibility in, but the upside is also bounded by what the same category can absorb. First introduced: Chapter 8.
  38. Linguistic Inquiry and Word Count (LIWC) The Linguistic Inquiry and Word Count (LIWC) dictionary is a word list developed by James Pennebaker, Roger Booth, and Martha Francis through the 1990s and 2000s. It assigns every word in a piece of text to roughly 80 psychological categories such as positive emotion, anger, anxiety, sadness, certainty, and many others. In a study like the 2014 Facebook experiment, the researchers ran every post through LIWC and counted how many words landed in the positive-emotion and negative-emotion categories, which gave them an automated, scalable measure of the emotional tone of millions of posts without paying human coders. First introduced: Chapter 19.
  39. LinkedIn Ads LinkedIn Ads is the paid-media platform built into the LinkedIn professional social network, and it has become the default channel for business-to-business lead generation because its targeting layer lets advertisers pick exactly which job titles, company sizes, and industries will see each ad. The price the firm pays per thousand impressions (called the CPM, or cost per thousand) typically runs three to seven times higher than the equivalent Meta or Google CPM, which the firm tolerates because the audience reach is so much more precise. First introduced: Chapter 25.
  40. Liquid Death Liquid Death is a direct-to-consumer water brand founded in 2017 by Mike Cessario, a former creative director who left advertising to prove that water could be marketed like a heavy-metal beverage. The brand validated its entire category with a single 1,500-dollar Facebook video shot before any product existed, and it now runs a content operation centered on a horror-comedy short-film slate and a 'Murder Your Thirst' content series that treats hydration as a punk-rock joke the buyer is in on. First introduced: Chapter 17.
  41. list hygiene List hygiene is the practice of regularly removing unengaged or invalid email addresses from the marketing list to protect the brand's sender reputation. The minimum standard includes hard-bounce removal (taking off addresses that no longer exist as soon as the bounce is reported), soft-bounce monitoring (flagging addresses that bounce repeatedly), and sunset policies (suppressing recipients who have not opened or clicked any message in 90 to 180 days). Mailbox providers treat sender reputation partly as a function of recent engagement, so a list full of unengaged addresses drags down the deliverability of every message the brand sends, even to the engaged half of the list. First introduced: Chapter 21.
  42. list price List price is the published "sticker" price the firm posts on a price sheet or invoice before any discount, rebate, or negotiated concession is applied. In the Marn-Rosiello Castle Battery example, the firm's published list price was $28.40 per battery, but what the firm actually kept after every deduction was $21.60. The 24 percent erosion between those two numbers is what the pocket-price waterfall makes visible. First introduced: Chapter 10.
  43. literate programming Literate programming is a practice Donald Knuth introduced in a 1984 paper, in which programs are written first as natural-language explanations to humans about what the computer should do, with the executable code embedded as the secondary product the explanation extracts. The 2026 instance the marketer encounters is the SKILL.md file: its top half is plain English explaining what the Skill does and when to use it, and its bottom half contains the model instructions a colleague can read and maintain six months later. The practice is older than language models and the SKILL.md format is the latest place it has been useful. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  44. LLM A Large Language Model (LLM) is the AI system that powers ChatGPT, Claude, and Gemini. The model was trained on a huge pile of text by being asked to predict the next word in a sentence, billions of times across trillions of words. That single skill, scaled up far enough, produces conversation, summarization, translation, coding, and most of what people now use AI for. The model does not read the live internet during a chat unless given a search tool; it answers from the patterns its training read most. First introduced: Chapter 4.
  45. LLM-as-judge An LLM-as-judge is a large language model used to score or critique another model's output (or a human's draft) against a rubric the team supplies. The pattern works because reading and scoring a piece of writing is a job at which frontier 2026 models are reliably strong, and running the model in judge mode lets the team grade hundreds of proposals in minutes rather than days. A line-extension audit where Claude scores each proposed extension against a brand-fit rubric and a dilution-risk rubric is the standard 2026 pattern for offline evaluation in marketing. First introduced: Chapter 8.
  46. LLM Context API Brave's LLM Context API is the grounding endpoint Brave Software ships at $5 per 1,000 calls. The API returns clean text content, query-optimized snippets, JSON-LD structured data preserved as it appeared on the page, and code-block extraction for technical content. The same underlying index powers roughly 22 million Brave Search answers per day across a thirty-billion-page index, which makes it one of the few independent grounding surfaces a brand can target outside the Google/Microsoft duopoly. First introduced: Chapter 18.
  47. LLM-graded LLM-graded means using a language model to read each open-ended response and decide whether it actually addresses the question the survey asked, the same way a human research assistant would. The 2023 Gilardi PNAS paper showed that a model running this task matched or exceeded paid crowd-worker accuracy at a cost below three-tenths of a cent per response. By 2026, an LLM-graded open-end pass is the floor of professional practice on any panel-fielded study, because it catches AI-generated fraud responses that the vendor-side digital-fingerprint screen would miss. First introduced: Chapter 5.
  48. LLM tracing LLM tracing is the production-monitoring layer for any workflow that calls a large language model. The tracing system captures every input prompt, every model output, every intermediate tool the model called, and every function call that fired along the way, then saves the whole chain so an analyst can replay any specific run later. The pattern is what makes an open-end coder, a sentiment classifier, or an augmented-analyst Skill auditable: when the model returns a wrong answer in production, the trace shows which step produced the wrong intermediate result and lets the team fix the upstream issue rather than guessing. First introduced: Chapter 24.
  49. LLMs LLM stands for Large Language Model, which is the same kind of program described in the language-model definition box at the top of section 1.3. The qualifier 'large' means the weight count (the count of internal learned numbers) and the training corpus (the body of text the model trained on) are both big enough that the model handles most everyday writing and reasoning tasks without any task-specific training afterward. Claude, ChatGPT, and Gemini are all LLMs. The acronym is the everyday vendor and press shorthand for the same artifact the chapter has been calling a language model. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  50. local proxy A local proxy is a small program running on your own laptop that stands between this webpage and Anthropic's Claude model out on the internet. The reason a proxy exists in the middle is that the proxy holds the API key (which authenticates the request and bills the cost), so the key never appears in the browser-visible code where anyone could copy it. When you click the demo button, the page sends your inputs to the proxy on localhost, the proxy adds the API key and forwards the request to Anthropic, and the answer comes back along the same chain. Start it by running `python core-course/scripts/proxy_server.py` in your terminal. First introduced: Chapter 5.
  51. log-likelihood The log-likelihood is a number that summarizes how well the segmentation model's probability assignments match the actual choices the customers in the validation sample made. The model predicts, for each customer, the probability of every brand they could have chosen, and the log-likelihood adds up the log of the probability the model assigned to the brand the customer actually picked. A higher log-likelihood (closer to zero, since log probabilities are negative) means the model put high probability on the right answers and is therefore matching real buyer behavior. When the analyst is choosing between segmentation models with different numbers of segments, comparing log-likelihood values on the same held-out customers is one of the standard ways to decide which segment count K best captures the structure of the buyer population. First introduced: Chapter 6.
  52. long-form spillover Long-form spillover is the share of new short-form viewers who watch a long-form video from the same channel within 30 days of their first short. The 2025 Think with Google measurement study found Shorts converts at 42 percent, Reels at 12 percent, TikTok at 8 percent. The Shorts advantage comes from sharing a channel-subscription relationship with long-form video inside the same app, which TikTok and Reels do not. First introduced: Chapter 19.
  53. long-tail A long-tail keyword is a specific low-volume query like 'how to refund a Stripe payment intent in Python.' Each such query has only a handful of monthly searches, but long-tail queries outnumber head terms by roughly ten to one across most categories, and they convert at much higher rates because the buyer already knows specifically what they want before typing the query into the search box. First introduced: Chapter 18.
  54. long-term memory Long-term memory is the part of the buyer's mind that holds information for days, years, or decades, in contrast to short-term memory which holds only the last few seconds. A brand lives in long-term memory: the buyer who walks into a grocery store today retrieves a Coca-Cola association she encoded a decade ago, not one she encoded that morning. First introduced: Chapter 7.
  55. lookalike A lookalike audience is one the ad platform builds automatically to resemble an existing customer list the brand uploaded as a seed. Meta or Google takes the seed list and identifies the closest one to ten percent of all users on the platform by behavioral similarity to the seed; a 1 percent lookalike is the tightest match and works best for performance campaigns, while a 5-10 percent lookalike covers a broader pool and works better for awareness. First introduced: Chapter 18.
  56. Loop Loop is the leading returns-and-exchanges platform for Shopify direct-to-consumer brands. It lets the buyer initiate a return through a self-service portal on the brand's site, generates the return shipping label automatically, and routes the request either to a refund or to an exchange depending on what the buyer picks. The economic argument for paying for returns software like Loop is that exchanges generate net new revenue because the buyer keeps the brand relationship and often upgrades to a more expensive variant, while a refund is a pure cost the brand absorbs with no offsetting upside. First introduced: Chapter 13.
  57. LoRA Low-Rank Adaptation: a parameter-efficient fine-tuning method (Hu et al. 2021) that trains a small overlay (typically 50-200MB) on top of a frozen base model. A brand-style LoRA is fine-tuned on 30-100 brand reference images and reproduces the brand's color, typography, composition, and material language at inference time. Train-once on Replicate or RunPod for $5-30, then deploy via API for the cost of base-model inference. First introduced: Chapter 16.
  58. loss aversion Loss aversion is the empirical pattern, demonstrated by Kahneman and Tversky's prospect theory and dozens of follow-up studies, where a loss feels roughly twice as painful as an equivalent gain feels good. A $5 price hike costs the firm roughly twice the goodwill that a $5 discount produces. The pricing implication is asymmetric: price increases need careful framing, justification, and timing, while price cuts can be announced loudly and rarely produce backlash. The asymmetry is also why incumbent products carry a structural advantage over new entrants, which Gourville's 9x adoption barrier later in the chapter quantifies. First introduced: Chapter 10.
  59. loss aversion against the incumbent workflow The 9x effect, named by John Gourville in his 2006 Harvard Business Review article, is the empirical pattern where new products consistently miss their projected adoption by roughly a factor of nine. The mechanism multiplies two separate threes. Sellers value the new product at roughly three times its objective benefit because they are endowed with knowing how good it is. Buyers, on the other hand, weight giving up their current product at roughly three times the projected gain because of loss aversion and the status-quo endowment combined. Three times three equals nine, which is the gap between what the launch team projects and what the buyer population delivers. Gourville's practical rule is that high product-change with low behavior-change supports a skim strategy (Sonicare, Tesla replacing a luxury sedan), while low product-change with high behavior-change demands a penetration strategy (TiVo, Apple Pay) because the incumbent inertia is too heavy to lift with margin alone. First introduced: Chapter 10.
  60. Loss leaders A loss leader is a single low-priced item the retailer uses to build store traffic, often priced below cost so that the chain takes a small loss on the item itself. Costco's $4.99 rotisserie chicken, which has held that price since 2009, is the canonical example, along with the Black Friday doorbuster television and the holiday milk promotion. The shopper walks in to buy the loss leader and the retailer makes its margin on everything else in the cart that gets picked up along the way. First introduced: Chapter 12.
  61. low-variance Variance is a measure of how spread out a set of numbers is around its average. If a survey item asks ‘how often do you buy this brand on a 1-to-7 scale,’ and everyone in the sample says 4 or 5, the variance is low because the answers cluster tightly. If respondents fan out across the full scale from 1 to 7, the variance is high. The square root of variance is the standard deviation, which is the unit most reports quote. First introduced: Chapter 5.
  62. lower bound The Point of Marginal Cheapness (PMC) is the lower edge of the acceptable price band, sitting at the intersection of the too cheap curve and the bargain curve. Below this point, the firm has crossed into the price-quality-inference zone where additional cuts actively damage demand because buyers start reading the product as not the real thing rather than as a better deal. First introduced: Chapter 10.
  63. Loyalty programs Loyalty programs are reward schemes (points, miles, tiers, app rewards) firms run to encourage existing buyers to come back more often. Starbucks Rewards and airline frequent-flier programs are canonical examples. The empirical record shows they produce small frequency effects on the buyers already enrolled and almost no net growth in the brand's overall buyer base. First introduced: Chapter 7.
  64. loyalty tier mix A loyalty tier mix is the way a retailer carves its file into earn-and-spend levels (such as Insider, VIB, and Rouge at Sephora) so that a small top tier produces a disproportionate share of revenue and visit frequency. The math behind the carving is RFM, which stands for Recency, Frequency, and Monetary value, the three behavioral signals Bult and Wansbeek formalized in 1995. The tier mix is RFM put into operational form: thresholds get set where retention and frequency are already highest, so perks concentrate on the customers most likely to keep coming back, not on the broad middle. First introduced: Chapter 12.
  65. LSTM LSTM stands for Long Short-Term Memory, a kind of neural network introduced in 1997 that reads a sequence one item at a time and maintains a running memory state that it updates after every step. The memory state lets the model remember information from many steps earlier in the sequence, which is what made LSTM the dominant sequence model in marketing analytics for customer-journey and demand-forecasting work for about two decades. Transformers replaced LSTM as the default around 2023 because the transformer can attend to any earlier item in the sequence directly without having to squeeze the past into a single memory state. First introduced: Chapter 6.
  66. LTV Customer Lifetime Value (CLV), almost always written as LTV in the software-as-a-service (SaaS) playbook, is the sum of the contribution margin a single customer is expected to produce in each future period, where each period's contribution is multiplied by the retention rate raised to that period (the probability the customer is still around in that period) and then discounted back to today's dollars. CLV and LTV are the same quantity measured against a recurring offer; the SaaS convention favors LTV. Walk a worked example to anchor it. A coffee subscription generating $8 of margin per month at a 95 percent monthly retention rate and a 1 percent monthly discount rate is worth roughly $145 in lifetime value, which is the figure the firm compares against its $30 customer-acquisition cost when deciding whether the channel pays back. A SaaS customer paying $10,000 per year in contribution margin over an average four-year tenure has roughly $40,000 of gross LTV before discount and churn adjustments. LTV grows when retention holds, when the customer expands her contract (additional seats, additional products), and when she refers other buyers. First introduced: Chapter 23.
  67. LTV-to-CAC Customer Lifetime Value (CLV), labeled LTV by industry convention in subscription and direct-to-consumer contexts, is the total contribution margin a single customer is projected to produce for the firm across the entire time that customer remains a customer, after subtracting what the firm spent to acquire them and after discounting each future period back to today's dollars. The standard formula sums the customer's expected margin per period, where each period's contribution is weighted by the retention rate raised to that period (the probability the customer is still around) and by the discount rate. A coffee subscription that generates $8 a month at a 95 percent monthly retention rate and a 1 percent monthly discount rate works out to roughly $145 in lifetime value, which is the number the firm should compare against its $30 acquisition cost when deciding whether to keep running the same ads. The LTV-to-CAC ratio compares that total against what the brand had to spend to acquire the buyer in the first place, and a 3-to-1 ratio is the industry rule of thumb for a healthy unit-economics picture because it means the brand pays back its acquisition spend in roughly the first 12 to 18 months of the relationship. First introduced: Chapter 21.
  68. LTV-to-CAC ratio The LTV-to-CAC ratio compares the total contribution margin a customer produces over her entire relationship with the brand against the cost the brand paid to acquire her. A ratio of 3 to 1 means each acquired customer eventually produces three dollars of margin for every dollar the brand spent acquiring her. Venture-backed direct-to-consumer brands use the 3 to 1 ratio as the floor that separates a healthy growth model from a structurally unprofitable one; ratios above 5 to 1 typically indicate the brand is underspending on acquisition and could profitably grow faster than it currently is. First introduced: Chapter 13.
  69. LTV:CAC ratio The LTV:CAC ratio is customer lifetime value divided by customer acquisition cost, and venture boards use it as the single headline number for whether a firm's growth math actually works. When the ratio sits below 1.0 the firm is losing money on every new customer it brings in, between 1.0 and 2.0 it is acquiring at thin margins, around 3.0 is considered the healthy floor a serious investor wants to see, and a ratio above 5.0 often suggests the firm is under-investing in growth because each customer is worth far more than what the firm is paying to win them. The ratio only holds up if the numerator and denominator are calculated against the same definition of contribution margin and the same discount rate, because otherwise the two halves of the ratio are speaking different financial languages. First introduced: Chapter 25.
13 // m

M M

  1. M-by-N problem The combinatorics problem the Model Context Protocol solves is straightforward. Without a shared standard, every pairing of an AI application with a tool needs its own custom adapter, so connecting M applications to N tools costs roughly M times N pieces of glue code. With a shared standard, each application and each tool speaks the protocol exactly once, which collapses the cost to roughly M plus N. That is why a single MCP server can be reused across Claude Desktop, Cursor, and Codex without rewriting the integration. First introduced: Chapter 27.
  2. machine bias Machine bias, in the Boelaert et al. 2025 sense, is the pattern where each generative AI model has a strong, low-variance preference for one specific answer on each survey item, regardless of which demographic backstory it has been prompted with. The bias is large enough to matter, the direction of the bias shifts unpredictably from one topic to the next, and the bias is not explained by the demographic prompt the way a real human respondent's view would be. It is a token-frequency artifact of the model's training rather than a population sample of any human group. First introduced: Chapter 5.
  3. Made-For-Advertising Made-For-Advertising (MFA) sites are websites built specifically to display programmatic advertising rather than to be read or watched as content. They typically auto-play short looping videos behind dense ad placements and rely on click-bait headlines to draw search traffic. The 2023 ANA audit found that about 21 percent of programmatic display spend lands on MFA sites, a category the industry quietly tolerates because it inflates impression counts cheaply. First introduced: Chapter 19.
  4. Magic Words Magic Words is Jonah Berger's 2023 research program (published as a book by Harper Business) that identifies six categories of language that move belief, retention, and forward-sharing. The underlying research drew on more than 150,000 text observations and a dozen field experiments. Three of the six categories carry the heaviest weight in brand copy: identity nouns ('you are a runner' beats 'you run'), concrete attributes ('matte-black aluminum tallboy' beats 'premium hydration'), and certainty markers ('this is the best' beats 'this might be among the best'). First introduced: Chapter 7.
  5. Mail Privacy Protection Mail Privacy Protection is a feature Apple introduced in iOS 15 (released September 2021) that pre-fetches every image inside every email Apple Mail receives, regardless of whether the user has actually opened the message. The feature was designed to protect user privacy, because the tracking pixel marketers rely on to count opens fires whenever an image loads. By pre-fetching every image automatically, Apple makes every message look opened from the brand's side, which makes open-rate tracking essentially useless. Roughly 50 percent of US email opens happened on Apple Mail at the time of launch, so the change affected about half the country overnight. First introduced: Chapter 21.
  6. managerial economics Managerial economics is the application of microeconomic theory to the decisions a single firm actually has to make, such as what price to charge, how much to produce, how much to invest in new capacity, and when to enter or exit a market. Joel Dean's 1951 textbook is the founding work of the field, and every business-school managerial-economics course since has essentially reproduced its chapter list. First introduced: Chapter 10.
  7. MAP policies A Minimum Advertised Price (MAP) policy is a rule the manufacturer imposes on authorized resellers that specifies the lowest price the reseller may publicly advertise the product at. The policy restricts the price signal the reseller sends to the market, without dictating the actual transaction price the buyer ends up paying at the register. MAP policies are how manufacturers keep retailers from advertising-war their way through brand equity that took decades to build. First introduced: Chapter 11.
  8. MaRGen MaRGen is a four-agent pipeline that generates a six-page market analysis report. The agents are named Researcher (gathers source material), Reviewer (critiques the draft), Writer (produces the prose), and Retriever (pulls supporting data from internal databases), and the pipeline learns the report format from real Amazon consultants’ presentation decks via in-context learning rather than from a hand-written template. Each report runs in about seven minutes at a cost of roughly one dollar, and reports are scored by an LLM judge calibrated against human expert ratings. The reference is arXiv 2508.01370 (Koshkin et al., August 2025). First introduced: Chapter 24.
  9. margin Net margin is the share of revenue that a firm keeps as profit after every cost, not just the cost of goods sold. A firm with $100 of revenue and $97 of cost has $3 of profit, which is a 3 percent net margin. U.S. grocery chains run on roughly 3 percent. Apple's hardware business runs closer to 25 percent. Pure-software businesses can run 30 to 50 percent because the marginal cost of one more copy is essentially zero. First introduced: Chapter 10.
  10. margin of error The margin of error is the half-width of the confidence interval around a survey estimate. To make a five percent margin at ninety-five percent confidence concrete: if the marketer were to imagine running the same survey one hundred times on independent samples from the same underlying population, the reported number would land within five percentage points of the true population value on ninety-five of those one hundred hypothetical reruns. The five-percent figure is therefore not how often the survey is wrong; it is how close to the truth the survey is across repeated draws. First introduced: Chapter 5.
  11. marginal cost Marginal cost is the dollar amount it takes to make the very next unit of something, on top of what you have already spent. A 1995 magazine had to pay for paper, printing, and trucks for every additional copy, so the marginal cost was real money, but a TikTok video in 2026 costs the creator essentially nothing extra to publish (the phone, the internet, and the creator's afternoon are already paid for). When marginal cost falls to roughly zero, anyone can publish, which is why TikTok has 170 million U.S. accounts and broadcast TV does not. First introduced: Chapter 20.
  12. marginal-equality The marginal-equality rule (sometimes called the Dorfman-Steiner rule, after the 1954 paper that proved it) says the marketing budget is allocated optimally when the last dollar spent on each channel returns the same amount of revenue as the last dollar on every other channel. Picture it this way: if channel A’s last dollar returns 50 cents and channel B’s last dollar returns 80 cents, the planner can move a dollar from A to B and grow revenue by 30 cents. Only when every channel’s last dollar returns the same amount is the budget at its peak. In a log-log regression model, that condition reduces to a clean recipe: each channel’s optimal budget share equals its elasticity divided by the sum of the channel elasticities. First introduced: Chapter 24.
  13. markdown Markdown is a plaintext format for writing documents with simple formatting like headings, bullets, bold, and links, using ordinary punctuation as the markup. The rules are easy enough to memorize in a minute. A line that starts with a number sign and a space becomes a heading. A line that starts with a hyphen and a space becomes a bullet. Two stars on either side of a phrase make the phrase bold. The file is saved with the .md extension, and any modern app (GitHub, Notion, Slack, Claude) renders it into a nicely formatted document automatically. The undergrad version is the same kind of formatting a marketer already types into a Discord message or a Notion page. The marks are just punctuation the rendering app turns into headings and bullets when it displays the file. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  14. market cap Market capitalization is the public market's valuation of a company's equity, computed by multiplying the current share price by the total number of shares outstanding. The number is effectively the market's collective vote on what the company is worth at this moment, and it moves daily as the share price moves, which is why a single bad earnings report can erase tens of billions of dollars in market cap inside a single trading day. First introduced: Chapter 3.
  15. market orientation Market orientation is Kohli and Jaworski's 1990 operational definition of the marketing concept, designed so the firm can actually audit whether it does what it claims to believe. The construct names three coordinated activities the firm has to do well: generate intelligence about current and future customer needs (from surveys, complaint logs, salesforce reports, and hallway conversations), disseminate that intelligence across functions so it does not die in one department, and respond to it on a cycle time fast enough to matter. The point is that market orientation is a property of the entire firm, not of the marketing department alone. First introduced: Chapter 2.
  16. marketing agility Marketing agility is the firm-level capability to keep re-targeting its prospect lists, re-pricing its offers against the willingness-to-pay the market reveals, and reconfiguring its go-to-market motions as fresh signals arrive from the market, the salesforce, and the AI tools the team has plugged in. The capability is structured rather than ad hoc, which is why the high-agility firms run a weekly or biweekly decision cadence inside a quarterly planning rhythm and document every decision at each loop turn so the team can see whether last month's bet actually worked. The reason marketing agility lives at the firm level rather than at the rep level is that it is distinct from sales agility (which lives at the rep's desk on a per-call basis) and from organizational agility (which lives at the executive layer on a quarterly-to-annual cycle), and the three operate at different time horizons but reinforce one another when all three are present. First introduced: Chapter 23.
  17. marketing environment The marketing environment is the set of forces, both internal and external to the firm, that shape what a brand can do and how buyers respond to what the brand does. The environment splits into two parts. The microenvironment is the set of actors the brand can negotiate with directly, including customers, suppliers, channel partners, and competitors. The macroenvironment is the set of larger forces the brand can read and react to but cannot bargain with, including political, economic, sociocultural, technological, environmental, and legal forces. First introduced: Chapter 3.
  18. Marketing Metrics X-Ray The Marketing Metrics X-Ray is a dashboard pattern from Farris, Bendle, Pfeifer, and Reibstein that decomposes a top-level financial metric (revenue, profit margin) layer by layer into the operational metrics the firm actually controls week to week. The CFO reads the top layer (revenue, gross margin, contribution), the CMO reads the middle layer (CAC, retention, brand-tracking scores), and the channel managers read the bottom layer (CPC, conversion rate per ad, open rate per email). Each layer ties cleanly to the layer above it, so a movement in any operational metric is traceable up to a movement in the financial number above it. First introduced: Chapter 24.
  19. marketing-mix modeling Marketing-mix modeling (MMM) is the firm-scale, modern incarnation of response-model attribution. It runs the same log-log regression of weekly sales on weekly per-channel spend, but it adds seasonality, price, and any non-marketing events the firm can name as additional inputs, so the regression is no longer just ‘sales on spend.’ Because MMM works on aggregate weekly numbers rather than on user-level cookies, the method survived the 2021-2024 privacy reset that broke click-by-click digital attribution. By 2026, MMM is the budget-allocation spine for most marketers who can produce 52 or more weeks of clean data. First introduced: Chapter 24.
  20. marketing-mix modeling (MMM) Marketing-mix modeling (MMM) is a top-down statistical model that decomposes the brand's total sales into the share contributed by each marketing channel, the baseline that would have happened without any marketing, and the exogenous factors that move sales independently (seasonality, price changes, macroeconomic shifts). The 2020s Bayesian revival of the technique encodes known causal structure as prior beliefs, which lets the model produce credible attribution even when the available data is too thin for a purely frequentist regression to identify each channel's contribution. First introduced: Chapter 18.
  21. Marketing myopia Marketing myopia is Theodore Levitt's 1960 name for the failure mode where a firm defines itself by the product it currently sells rather than by the customer job that product serves. The US railroads thought they were in the railroad business when their buyers were actually hiring them for transportation, and missed cars, trucks, and commercial aviation as the substitute instruments arrived. The same diagnostic still runs in 2026 on any firm whose mission statement names a product instead of a job, and the test is whether the firm's elevator pitch could be re-stated in terms of what the buyer is trying to accomplish without losing accuracy. First introduced: Chapter 2.
  22. marketing research problem The information question that the research study is designed to answer, distinct from the management decision problem the manager actually has to make. Example: the management decision problem is ‘should we ship product A or B?’ The marketing research problem is ‘what is the probable share of category buyers who would choose each product in a competitive set, and which feature drives the gap?’ Collapsing the two is the most expensive mistake in research, because it produces a study that measures something accurately but answers a different question than the one the manager needed answered. First introduced: Chapter 5.
  23. marketing ROI Marketing return on investment, or marketing ROI, is the incremental gross profit a marketing investment produces divided by the cost of the investment itself, expressed as a percent. The standard form is the gross profit the campaign produced minus the cost of the campaign, all divided by the cost of the campaign, so a campaign that cost $100 and produced $150 of incremental gross profit returns a marketing ROI of 50 percent. Marketing ROI is the gold-standard metric quoted on corporate boards and inside finance reviews because it is margin-adjusted, which means it measures the profit each marketing dollar brings home rather than the revenue the dollar happens to touch on the way. First introduced: Chapter 25.
  24. marketing-to-revenue The marketing-to-revenue ratio is the firm's total marketing expense divided by its total revenue, expressed as a percent, and it is the single number a CFO will quote first when comparing the firm's spending against the rest of the category. Gartner's 2024 and 2025 CMO Spend Surveys both report an industry-wide average of 7.7 percent, which is the lowest reading since the survey began in 2014, while Duke Fuqua's CMO Survey reports a higher number on a different methodology, landing at 9.4 percent in Spring 2025 and easing to 9.0 percent in Spring 2026. The ratio varies by category in a predictable way, with software firms running near 11 percent because customers cost more to acquire, consumer-products firms running near 9 percent, and manufacturing firms running near 5 percent because their customers are buying with larger order sizes that need less marketing per unit. First introduced: Chapter 25.
  25. Marketo Engage Marketo Engage is Adobe's enterprise marketing-automation platform, used by roughly five thousand companies, mostly business-to-business firms in software, financial services, healthcare, and manufacturing. The platform manages email campaigns, landing pages, lead scoring, and the routing of qualified leads from marketing to sales. A marketing-research student doing a summer internship at a business-to-business company in 2026 has a higher-than-coin-flip chance of touching Marketo, which is why it shows up here as the worked example for what a marketing MCP server looks like. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  26. Marketo MCP server The Marketo MCP server is the official connector Adobe shipped at Adobe Summit 2026 (April 20-22 in Las Vegas) that exposes more than one hundred operations against Marketo Engage, the marketing-automation platform most large enterprises use for email programs and lead-nurturing workflows. The exposed operations cover form management, program orchestration, smart-campaign design, lead lookup, email composition, snippets, lists, and folders. The launch is in closed beta, which means an enterprise customer who wants to wire Marketo into Claude or Microsoft Copilot has to apply for access, but the published operations list already reads like the back of the box of every marketing-automation feature an analyst would actually use, which is why the wiring matters even before general availability. First introduced: Chapter 24.
  27. MARKOR scale The MARKOR scale is a 20-item validated questionnaire from Kohli, Jaworski, and Kumar's 1993 follow-up paper, used to score how market-oriented an organization actually is in practice rather than how market-oriented it claims to be. The instrument has three subscales corresponding to the three activities the construct names: intelligence generation, intelligence dissemination, and responsiveness. Hundreds of follow-on studies across industries and countries have used the MARKOR scale, and the cumulative evidence is that higher MARKOR scores reliably predict higher firm performance, with consistency rare in organizational research. First introduced: Chapter 2.
  28. Markov chain A Markov chain is a model where the probability of moving from one state to another depends only on the current state, not the full history of how you got there. In the Max Planck TikTok audit, each video the puppet watches is one transition, and the chain tracks how the probability distribution over content categories (Gaming, Food, neither, both) evolves video by video as the algorithm learns the puppet's preferences. Treating the feed as a Markov chain lets the researchers estimate when the feed stops drifting and stabilizes on a category mix the algorithm is confident about. First introduced: Chapter 19.
  29. markup Markup is the percentage a firm adds to the unit cost to arrive at the selling price. A $60 cost with a 50 percent markup becomes a $90 sticker price. Markup discipline varies dramatically across business models. Costco caps every item in the warehouse at 14 percent over what Costco paid for it, while Apple's iPhone runs roughly a 50 percent gross margin on each unit sold. First introduced: Chapter 10.
  30. Mass marketing Mass marketing treats the entire population of potential buyers as one undifferentiated group and sells them all the same product at scale. The classical case is Henry Ford running the Model T at his Highland Park plant in the 1910s: one car, available in any color the buyer wanted as long as it was black, sold at a price the cost advantage of single-line scale production made unbeatable. Mass marketing works when every buyer genuinely wants the same thing or when the price advantage of running a single product line at vast volume more than compensates for the buyers who would have preferred something tailored to them. First introduced: Chapter 6.
  31. mass merchandiser A mass merchandiser is a retailer that carries a broad line of routine merchandise at low margin and high volume, on the bet that a household will consolidate many of its weekly purchases in one trip if the price is right. Walmart and Target are the U.S. examples, and the format is sometimes called a discount store or supercenter when the grocery and general-merchandise sides are run under one roof. First introduced: Chapter 12.
  32. matched-market test A matched-market test is a quasi-experimental design that compares stores or geographies that received the campaign against statistically matched controls that did not, using sales differences as the lift estimate. The match is built so the two groups looked identical on the trend lines before the campaign started; any divergence after the campaign launches is attributable to the campaign rather than to pre-existing differences. The design works on aggregate outcomes (total sales at the store or region level) rather than on individual tracking, which is why it fits the post-cookie privacy-regulatory architecture cleanly. First introduced: Chapter 24.
