  33. matplotlib Matplotlib is the Python plotting library that produces roughly 90 percent of the charts published in academic papers and that ships embedded inside most brand-strategy analytics workflows. The library reads a list of numbers and produces a chart, with the chart saveable to PNG, SVG, or PDF for inclusion in slide decks. Matplotlib supports the coral hex codes the chapter's design system specifies (#DA7756 for the primary, #B8553A for the deeper variant), which is why the Skills folder above uses it for the perceptual-map renderer rather than a more recent library with a different default palette. First introduced: Chapter 6.
  34. MCCI MCCI stands for motivation, control, compensation, and incentive, which is the four-pillar architecture by which a firm directs and rewards salesperson behavior. The 2026 half-century review by Hultman and McCarthy in the Journal of Personal Selling and Sales Management extends the original 1987 Anderson-Oliver behavior-versus-outcome dichotomy into a five-dimensional control taxonomy that covers outcome controls (pay for the closed deal), behavior controls (pay for the activities that produce the deal), activity controls (pay for the call counts), capability controls (pay for the skills the rep is developing), and cultural controls (pay for the team norms the rep is reinforcing). The same review also replaces the long-standing sixty-percent-base-and-forty-percent-variable pay-mix default with contingent-fit recommendations that depend on the segment the firm serves. A consultative segment, where the rep is solving complex problems, should run closer to eighty-percent base and twenty-percent variable, because the rep needs the security to take time on each deal. A transactional segment, where the rep is processing high-volume short-cycle deals, should run closer to fifty-fifty, because the higher variable share rewards the activity volume the segment actually requires. The audit clause running at the bonus cadence is what closes the architecture, because it catches the misconduct the variable share would otherwise incentivize. First introduced: Chapter 23.
  35. McDonaldland Meal The McDonaldland Meal of 2025 reintroduced Mayor McCheese in McDonald's primary advertising for the first time since 1985, ending a forty-year absence that had originally been forced by the H.R. Pufnstuf trademark litigation of the early 1970s. The launch lifted McCheese name-recognition from below 25 percent to roughly 45 percent among 13-to-24-year-olds within four weeks, according to the Wieden + Kennedy campaign debrief. The case matters because it confirms that a retired heritage asset can be reactivated rather than killed, provided the asset still sits above the category-buyer recall floor when the audit picks it up. First introduced: Chapter 16.
  36. MCP MCP stands for Model Context Protocol. It is a standard way to expose a tool (HubSpot, GA4, Slack, an internal CRM system) to an AI model, so any AI app that speaks the protocol can call the tool without anyone writing a custom integration first. Anthropic proposed the protocol in November 2024 and donated it to the Linux Foundation Agentic AI Foundation in December 2025, which means MCP is now governed as an open standard rather than as one vendor's property. The plain analogy is that MCP is the USB-C port for AI tools. Each tool ships its plug once, and every AI app that speaks the protocol can read all of them, exactly the way one USB-C cable now charges every laptop, phone, and pair of headphones a marketer owns. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  37. MCP (Model Context Protocol) MCP stands for Model Context Protocol, an open standard released by Anthropic in November 2024 and donated to the Linux Foundation's Agentic AI Foundation in December 2025, that lets any AI model talk to any external tool through one shared interface. The plain-English analogy is a USB-C port for AI: a tool like Ahrefs or Slack exposes itself once as an MCP server, and any AI application that speaks the protocol can plug in and call that server without custom integration code. By early 2026 there are roughly 200 widely used MCP servers, including Ahrefs, GA4, Slack, GitHub, Notion, Stripe, and Salesforce, plus a long tail of about 25,000 community-built servers covering everything from local weather APIs to enterprise databases. First introduced: Chapter 17.
  38. MCP server A Model Context Protocol (MCP) server is a small program that exposes a tool to AI applications over a published protocol. The tool can be a search engine, a file system, a database, or an external API. Any AI application that speaks the MCP protocol can plug into the server without custom integration code. Anthropic donated the protocol to the Linux Foundation Agentic AI Foundation in December 2025, and by early 2026 the ecosystem includes roughly 200 widely used MCP servers (covering vendors such as Stripe, Snowflake, Qualtrics, Slack, GA4, HubSpot, and Salesforce) plus a long tail of about 25,000 community-built servers that brands can connect to. First introduced: Chapter 3.
  39. MDPI MDPI is the publisher of a group of academic journals that includes the open-access journal Computers, where the 2025 lifecycle-event-monitoring framework for customer segmentation drift was published. The publisher matters here only as the citation handle the field uses to refer to the paper. The load-bearing object the brand team actually uses is the four-layer monitoring architecture the paper describes, which a 2026 brand team can implement on top of the firm’s existing segmentation pipeline without rebuilding the underlying clustering model. First introduced: Chapter 6.
  40. Meaningful-Different-Salient Meaningful-Different-Salient is Kantar's three-factor brand-power score. Meaningful asks whether the brand feels relevant to the buyer's life. Different asks whether the buyer perceives the brand as standing apart from category competitors. Salient asks whether the brand comes to mind quickly when the buyer thinks about the category. A brand scoring high on all three is positioned for future cash-flow growth that Kantar's amplifier translates into the dollar figures the ranking reports. First introduced: Chapter 7.
  41. means The means/ends frame asks whether the firm is charging the buyer for the product it sells (the means) or for the outcome the buyer is actually trying to achieve (the end). A power-tool firm sells drills, but the buyer is paying for holes. A SaaS company sells seat licenses, but the buyer is paying for resolved support tickets. The pricing decision is which one the invoice tracks. First introduced: Chapter 10.
  42. means of behavioral modification The means of behavioral modification is Zuboff's term for the technical infrastructure that lets a firm not just predict what a user will do but actually push the user toward doing it. Pokemon Go's paid PokeStops are the worked example: the game placed an in-game reward at a sponsor's physical store, and players showed up at the store because the reward was there. The firm sold a prediction (people in this neighborhood will visit this store this afternoon) and then delivered the visit by adjusting the game. First introduced: Chapter 26.
  43. MEDDIC MEDDIC is a six-criterion enterprise-sales qualification framework developed at PTC in the 1990s. The six letters stand for Metrics (the quantitative outcome the buyer expects), Economic-buyer (the person with veto power), Decision-criteria (the explicit scoring rubric the buying committee uses), Decision-process (the procedural path the deal will travel), Identify-pain (the specific cost the status quo is imposing), and Champion (the buyer-side stakeholder driving the deal internally). A modern extension called MEDDPICC adds Paper-process (the contracting workflow) and Competition (the named alternatives). Used in complex multi-stakeholder enterprise deals where committee no-decision is the modal loss type. First introduced: Chapter 23.
  44. media mix modeling Media mix modeling (MMM) is the 2022-2026 statistical practice of inferring the contribution of each marketing channel to total installs, using regression on time-series data and aggregated SKAdNetwork postbacks rather than deterministic per-user click data. It is the successor to last-click attribution: less precise on any single install, more robust to the privacy-preserving constraints that broke last-click. AppsFlyer, Adjust, Singular, and the in-house measurement teams at the largest mobile advertisers all run some variant of MMM. First introduced: Chapter 14.
  45. median The median is the middle value of a sorted list of numbers, with exactly half the values below it and half above it. It is preferred over the mean (the arithmetic average) when a few extreme values would distort the average enough to mislead the reader. For example, a single six-hundred-second response sitting in a batch of five-second answers does not pull the median up the way it would pull the mean up, because the median only cares about which value sits in the middle position. First introduced: Chapter 5.
  46. median dwell window Dwell time is how long a viewer's eyes (and on a mobile feed, finger) stay on a piece of content before they scroll past it. On TikTok and Instagram Reels the median dwell time runs roughly two to four seconds per post, which is the working window any brand has to land its distinctive assets (the silhouette, the color, the mascot) before the viewer moves on. A creative that waits until second five to show the logo will already have lost most of its audience. First introduced: Chapter 19.
  47. mediation A mediator in statistics is a variable that carries the causal effect from a cause to a consequence, which means the cause cannot reach the outcome unless it first passes through the mediator. In the case the chapter walks, the predictor is AI adoption, the outcome is sales performance, and the mediator is the firm's marketing-agility capability, so the claim is that buying AI tools alone does not lift sales numbers until the firm also rebuilds the operating cadence that turns AI signals into weekly decisions. The reason this matters is that a mediation claim is stronger than a moderation claim. A moderator would only change how strong the direct effect is, but a mediator means that without it, the direct effect disappears entirely, which is what the 2024 evidence on AI tools and sales performance keeps finding. First introduced: Chapter 23.
  48. Memory Memory is anything the agent needs to remember between calls, and because the model itself forgets everything at the end of every turn, the memory has to live outside the model and be loaded back into the prompt at the start of the next call. Three flavors cover the production landscape, from cheapest to richest: a CLAUDE.md file the runtime reads at session start, a Project's pinned folder of documents the agent retrieves from on demand (the RAG pattern), and a knowledge-graph MCP server that stores accumulated facts about the brand as entities and relations the agent can query. First introduced: Chapter 27.
  49. memory structure A memory structure is the pattern of connected information the buyer holds about a brand in long-term memory: the brand name plus the colors, shapes, sounds, characters, buying situations, and meanings tied to it through repeated exposure. The brand is the network itself rather than the logo at its center. Liquid Death's memory structure includes the matte-black can, the gothic skull, the 'Murder Your Thirst' tagline, the festival situations the brand appears in, and the heavy-metal cultural reference, all wired together around the brand name. First introduced: Chapter 7.
  50. memory tool The memory tool is an agent primitive Anthropic shipped on September 29, 2025 that gives the model a file directory it can create, read, update, and delete inside, persistent across sessions. The directory contains six commands (view, create, str_replace, insert, delete, rename) addressed against paths beginning with /memories. The application running the agent implements the storage layer (writes the files to disk); Anthropic does not host the files themselves. The purpose is to keep durable state out of the active context window: instead of carrying every intermediate finding in the model's prompt, the agent writes summaries to /memories and reads them back when needed. The Patagonia supplier-audit example in section 1.4 stores per-supplier flags in /memories/audit/findings.md, which lets the audit run for eight hours without the context window filling up. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  51. mental accounting Mental accounting, named by Richard Thaler, is the cognitive habit by which buyers split their money into separate mental buckets (rent, groceries, fun, savings) and treat the same dollar differently depending on which bucket it sits in. A buyer who would never spend $40 on a dinner out from their grocery budget will happily spend $40 on the same dinner from their entertainment budget. The pricing implication is that the bucket the firm's product gets categorized into shapes the buyer's price tolerance more than the dollar amount alone. First introduced: Chapter 10.
  52. mental availability Mental availability is the propensity for a brand to be noticed and thought of in real buying situations, like the moment a thirsty buyer walks past a cooler at a gas station. It is built from the quantity and quality of memory links the brand has accumulated in the buyer's mind over years of seeing the brand in different contexts. Mental availability is distinct from share-of-mind measured in panel surveys, because what matters is the live recall that fires when the category need actually arises, not what the buyer says she remembers when asked. A high-mental-availability brand wins because it comes to mind first at the moment of need, while a low-mental-availability brand loses the sale before the buyer even considers it, regardless of how good its product turns out to be. First introduced: Chapter 2.
  53. mental ladders A mental ladder is the spatial metaphor positioning theorists use for the way a buyer's memory ranks the brands inside a category. The buyer carries a separate ladder for each category they shop in (one for cola, one for rental cars, one for laundry detergent), and the brand on the top rung of each ladder is the buyer's default option, the brand on the second rung is the obvious alternative, and so on down. The number of rungs varies by category: three rungs is typical, seven is roughly the maximum any buyer holds in active memory, and low-interest categories often have only one or two. The brand on the top rung wins by default in most purchase decisions, which is why fighting for that rung is the central positioning game. First introduced: Chapter 6.
  54. Mental Market Share Mental Market Share is Jenni Romaniuk's tracking instrument that measures the proportion of category-buyer mental space the brand occupies. It is derived from the share of category-entry-point (CEP) links the brand owns relative to its competitors. The instrument replaces the legacy awareness/consideration/satisfaction tracker trio because it directly measures the property (CEP-link breadth and strength) that the Sharp empirical record identifies as the dominant driver of brand growth. First introduced: Chapter 7.
  55. Mercosur The Southern Common Market, a South American trade bloc comprising Argentina, Brazil, Paraguay, Uruguay, and (since 2024) Bolivia. Founded 1991. Combined GDP roughly $2.5 trillion. The bloc's external tariffs on autos, machinery, and pharmaceuticals were among the highest in any major emerging-market region before the EU agreement began phasing them down. First introduced: Chapter 15.
  56. mergers-and-acquisitions Mergers and acquisitions (M&A) are deals where one firm acquires another or two firms combine into one. Brand equity is the financial input to the price the acquirer is willing to pay for a brand portfolio, because the buyer is essentially purchasing the future cash-flow stream the seller's existing buyer base will throw off. When Mars acquired Kellanova for $35.9 billion in August 2024, most of that price was paid for the brand-equity stack (Pringles, Cheez-It, Pop-Tarts) rather than for the factories or the inventory. First introduced: Chapter 7.
  57. Meridian Google Meridian is an open-source marketing-mix modeling library that Google released in March 2024 and made generally available in January 2025. Meridian runs a hierarchical Bayesian regression across 50 or more geographic markets at once, accepts the results of geo-experiments as priors that anchor the model, and returns a posterior distribution (a range of plausible values, not a single point estimate) for each channel’s return on investment. The library is the canonical 2026 open-source choice when a firm wants honest uncertainty around its channel ROI numbers rather than a single number that hides what the model does not know. First introduced: Chapter 24.
  58. message pull-through Message pull-through is the fraction of earned press coverage that carries at least one of the brand's intended key messages, either word-for-word or as a clear paraphrase. Imagine Patagonia's team pitched the Worn Wear repair program with three core messages around durability, ownership economics, and sustainability, and 40 percent of the resulting coverage referenced at least one of those three pillars. The team's message pull-through for the quarter is 40 percent, and the metric matters because it is the cleanest way to connect PR effort to the brand's underlying strategic narrative rather than to raw mention counts. First introduced: Chapter 22.
  59. Meta Customer Lists Meta Customer Lists is Meta's first-party audience-targeting feature, the direct equivalent of Google's Customer Match product on the Google Ads side. The brand uploads a list of customer emails (or phone numbers) in hashed form so that no raw personally-identifiable information ever leaves the brand's systems, and Meta then matches those hashes against the hashed email addresses associated with Facebook and Instagram accounts. The brand can then run ads against the resulting matched audience directly, or use that audience as the seed list for a Meta lookalike audience that finds the most behaviorally-similar one-to-ten percent of Meta's user base, which is the audience-targeting workflow that survives best in the post-cookie measurement environment. First introduced: Chapter 18.
  60. metabolizable growth rate The metabolizable growth rate is the fastest pace at which a firm's existing systems can absorb new buyer demand without breaking, set by whichever of three constraints binds first. The first constraint is talent capacity, meaning how fast the firm can hire, onboard, and train new people without quality drift in the work they produce. The second is supply-chain physics, meaning how fast the production and distribution network can scale without shortage or unit-quality variance. The third is the customer-experience system, meaning how fast the service, support, and onboarding teams can absorb new buyers without service-quality decay. The right pace is the slower of the three, and exceeding any one of them produces shortage and service degradation rather than upside. Gary Pisano develops the concept in the March-April 2024 Harvard Business Review. First introduced: Chapter 8.
  61. METR METR (Model Evaluation and Threat Research) is an independent nonprofit that benchmarks frontier AI models on long-horizon agentic tasks rather than on the short-answer evaluations most academic benchmarks use. Its headline metric is the human-expert-time horizon at which the agent achieves 50 percent task-completion reliability: at a four-hour horizon, the agent finishes the work correctly about half the time; at an eight-hour horizon, it does not. The horizon has been doubling roughly every 130 days for the last two years, which is what calibrates the augmented-analyst design point on which tasks the agent can be trusted with today and which still need senior review. First introduced: Chapter 24.
  62. micro-fulfillment center A micro-fulfillment center (MFC) is an automated, store-embedded fulfillment node sized between a store backroom and a regional distribution center. A typical MFC holds 15,000 to 18,000 SKUs and uses robotic shuttle systems to assemble online grocery orders, picking items off shelves the human picker never has to touch. Walmart, Kroger, and Albertsons (the last in partnership with Takeoff Technologies) are the canonical 2024-2026 grocery deployments in the U.S., and the unit-economics threshold for these deployments to pay back is roughly 1,200 picks per labor-hour. Below that pick density, the automation cost exceeds the labor savings. First introduced: Chapter 11.
  63. mid-tier squeeze Mid-tier squeeze is the 2024-2026 pattern where mid-tier brands are compressed from two sides at once. Below them are private-label cost leaders (Aldi’s store brands, Costco’s Kirkland line) that have closed the perceived quality gap while keeping the price gap. Above them are focused-differentiation challengers (Liquid Death in still water, Patagonia in outdoor apparel) that have built strong premium positions on values or category creation. The mid-tier brand caught between cannot match the cost leader’s price, cannot match the challenger’s meaning, and watches its share erode from both ends. First introduced: Chapter 24.
  64. minimum detectable effect The minimum detectable effect, often shortened to MDE, is the smallest absolute lift a test is powered to detect at the chosen sample size and significance threshold. A test on a page receiving 100 orders per week, powered to detect a 2-percentage-point lift, will not detect a 1-percentage-point lift even when that smaller lift is real. The experiment will look null and the team will conclude the change did nothing, when in fact the change worked but the test was not designed to see it. The fix is to set the minimum detectable effect at the start of the test and to lengthen the test until the sample size is large enough to detect a lift that small. First introduced: Chapter 13.
  65. Minimum Viable Footprint The Minimum Viable Footprint (MVF) is the smallest configuration of partner products and adoption-chain intermediaries that a launch can actually ship through while still delivering its core value proposition. Ron Adner uses the term to describe the launch-day subset everything else builds on. The team's job is to identify which partner dependencies are load-bearing on day one and which can be added later. At student scale for a card-game launch, the MVF might be: the deck itself, a one-page printable PDF score sheet, a one-page rules card, and a three-minute YouTube video that teaches the game. Expansion packs, fan-art contests, and convention appearances are phase-two work and are not part of the MVF. First introduced: Chapter 8.
  66. minimum viable product A minimum viable product (MVP) is the version of the new product that lets a team collect the maximum validated learning with the least effort, in Eric Ries's definition. The key word is minimum, and it modifies the experiment rather than the product itself: the team is asking what is the smallest test we can build that will tell us whether the hypothesis is true, not what is the smallest product we can ship and call done. Dropbox's 2008 MVP was a three-minute demo video that grew the company's waitlist from 5,000 to 75,000 sign-ups overnight, because the demo video tested whether buyers wanted file-syncing without the team having to build the file-syncing infrastructure first. First introduced: Chapter 8.
  67. mirror Mirror-window-door (Rudine Sims Bishop, 1990; adapted to inclusive marketing by Sonia Thompson). A mirror lets the buyer see themselves; a window lets them see another community without distortion; a door invites them to step into the brand. A campaign that is only a mirror for the dominant group leaves everyone else on the outside. First introduced: Chapter 15.
  68. Mirroring Mirroring is the simplest tool in the tactical-empathy toolkit. The rep repeats the counterpart's last one to three words back to her as a question with rising intonation, then waits in silence. The counterpart almost always elaborates because the unconscious recognition of her own words triggers her to fill the conversational pause. For example, when the buyer says ‘our procurement team is dragging their feet,’ the rep responds ‘dragging their feet?’ and the buyer elaborates on the actual obstacle without any additional prompting. First introduced: Chapter 23.
  69. Misguided evidence Misguided evidence is the third of Mittelstadt and Floridi's six concerns, sitting at the training-context stage of the AI pipeline. The concern is that the context in which the model was trained does not match the context in which it is being deployed, or that the data-collection process itself produced systematic bias the deployment now inherits. A generative AI model trained on the context-stripped open internet is producing misguided evidence the moment a brand ships it as a customer-facing assistant, because the brand's context has norms (tone, accuracy, privacy expectations) the training corpus never represented. The mitigation is documented data provenance, training-data audits, and a contextual-fitness evaluation run before deployment, not after. First introduced: Chapter 26.
  70. mixture-of-experts A mixture-of-experts (MoE) model holds many specialized sub-networks (the 'experts') but only activates a small subset of them on any given input. A small router network, trained alongside the experts, decides which experts fire for any specific token of output. The marketer pays the inference cost of only the active experts, not the total parameter count. Picture a consulting firm of 671 specialists where each client question routes to only the 37 most relevant ones. The firm paid to keep all 671 on staff so the right specialist is always available, but every individual question only bills against the time of the 37 who were actually called in to work on it. DeepSeek-V3 (December 2024) and DeepSeek-R1 (January 2025) are both 671-billion-parameter MoE models that activate 37 billion parameters per token, which is why they run cheaply enough for third-party hosts to offer at a fraction of the per-token price of comparable dense models. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  71. ML Machine learning (ML) is the family of methods that fit a model to data so the model can predict on new cases. Linear regression is the simple end; random forests, gradient-boosted trees, and neural networks sit further along. K-means from Section 5.3 is one example. ‘Any ML method’ here means the analyst can plug in the predictor of choice (a forest, a neural net, a regularized regression) and the framework still returns valid inference. First introduced: Chapter 5.
  72. MMP Mobile Measurement Partner: a third-party company that provides the install-attribution layer Apple and Google deliberately do not build. The MMP installs a software development kit (SDK) inside the brand's app, receives ad-click signals from every ad network the brand spends on, and matches the click to the install on the brand's behalf. AppsFlyer, Adjust, Singular, and Branch are the four firms that dominate the market in 2026. First introduced: Chapter 14.
  73. MMS MMS stands for Multimedia Messaging Service, which is the version of text messaging that can carry images, animated GIFs, and short video clips inside the message body rather than only plain text. The reason MMS messages cost roughly three times as much as plain SMS is that the carriers charge by the kilobyte of data rather than by the message, and an image or video takes far more kilobytes than a sentence of text. Brands tend to reserve MMS for the moments where the visual payoff justifies the cost, such as a product photo on a new arrival, an animated graphic on a limited-release drop announcement, or a picture of the abandoned-cart item in a recovery reminder. The lift in click-through rate from adding the visual element is typically large enough to pay back the extra carrier cost, but only when the send is targeted at the highest-intent buyers rather than blasted across the whole list. First introduced: Chapter 21.
  74. Mobile Measurement Partner A Mobile Measurement Partner (MMP) is a third-party SDK (software development kit) that handles the measurement layer Apple and Google deliberately do not provide directly. The four 2026 MMPs are AppsFlyer, Adjust, Singular, and Branch. The MMP tracks which ad drove which install, what the user does after install, where the LTV sits 30, 60, or 90 days later, and feeds that information back to ad networks so the ad networks can optimize their bidding. The MMP is the plumbing layer of mobile growth. First introduced: Chapter 14.
  75. Mobilizers A Mobilizer is a buyer-side stakeholder willing to challenge the internal status quo and build consensus inside the buying committee. The Adamson research identified three Mobilizer personas (Go-Getter, Teacher, and Skeptic) who actively drive consensus, and four non-Mobilizer personas (Friend, Guide, Climber, Blocker) who absorb selling time without delivering consensus. Sellers who concentrate effort on the three Mobilizers post a 31 percent higher rate of star-performer outcomes than sellers who treat all stakeholders equally. First introduced: Chapter 23.
  76. Modash Modash is an influencer-discovery platform that hosts the largest public-profile database in 2026, indexing more than 350 million creators across Instagram, TikTok, and YouTube who have at least 1,000 followers. A Stillwater bakery owner could open a 14-day free trial without a credit card, filter to OK State micro-creators in the food vertical, and pull a vetted candidate list of 50 to 200 creators by Sunday night, all before paying the platform anything. The paid Essentials plan runs 199 dollars a month, and the Performance plan at 599 dollars a month is built for a marketing team tracking 250 creators across multiple campaigns. First introduced: Chapter 20.
  77. model The model is the AI system itself, the actual machine-learning system that reads what the user typed and produces the answer. Claude, GPT-5, and Gemini are each different models, and the word works the way the word engine works in the phrase 'engine of a car': it names the part that does the actual work of turning the input into an output. First introduced: Chapter 4.
  78. model card A model card is a structured public document describing a trained machine-learning model, proposed by Mitchell and colleagues in a 2019 paper at the FAccT conference. The standard sections cover model details (architecture, training procedure), intended use cases, evaluation factors (which slices of the population were measured), performance metrics across those slices, the training and evaluation datasets used, ethical considerations, and the known caveats. By 2026 the format is de facto required on all major model hubs (Hugging Face, Google's Vertex AI, Microsoft's Azure ML, Amazon's SageMaker), so a vendor who cannot produce one is operating outside the industry-standard procurement disclosure. First introduced: Chapter 26.
  79. model cards A model card is a short standardized document a vendor publishes alongside every model release. The card describes what the model is, how it was trained, on which benchmarks it was tested, and what its known failure modes are. Margaret Mitchell and colleagues at Google introduced the format in a 2019 paper titled 'Model Cards for Model Reporting,' and every Anthropic, OpenAI, and Google release now ships one as a matter of course. The marketer should read the card before committing budget to a model, in the same way a careful shopper reads the nutrition label on a packaged food before deciding whether the product fits the diet. A model card is the nutrition label of the AI industry. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  80. Model Context Protocol The Model Context Protocol (MCP) is an open standard Anthropic released in late 2024 for connecting AI models to external tools, data sources, and services. Donated to the Linux Foundation Agentic AI Foundation in December 2025, the protocol by early 2026 has roughly 200 widely used servers (covering vendors such as Stripe, Snowflake, Qualtrics, Slack, GA4, HubSpot, and Salesforce) plus a long tail of about 25,000 community-built servers. Think of it as a USB-C port for AI tools. First introduced: Chapter 3.
  81. Model Context Protocol (MCP) The Model Context Protocol (MCP) is an open standard Anthropic released in November 2024 that lets a language model read from and write to separate software systems through one uniform interface, the same way a USB-C port lets any device plug into any laptop. In the sales-marketing context, MCP is what allows the marketing team's lead-scoring model and the sales team's customer-relationship-management database to hand a single customer record back and forth without any human re-typing the score from one screen into another. The protocol is the reason the two functions can finally share data in real time rather than waiting on a nightly batch export, which is the integration cost that used to keep most lead-handoff conversations stuck at the spreadsheet stage. First introduced: Chapter 23.
  82. Model Context Protocol server A Model Context Protocol (MCP) server is an open standard that lets a language model read and write structured data across separate software systems through a single uniform interface. Anthropic open-sourced MCP in November 2024 and donated it to the Linux Foundation Agentic AI Foundation in December 2025. The MCP catalog now lists more than 200 widely used servers. Salesforce shipped its first-party MCP server in March 2026, and Gong shipped its API-and-MCP integration documentation in 2026. In a sales context, an MCP server lets the AI tool pull deal context from one system and write dispositions back to another without any custom integration code. First introduced: Chapter 23.
  83. Model Spec The Model Spec is OpenAI's published instruction set describing how ChatGPT and the company's API models are supposed to behave. The document was first released on May 8, 2024 and has been revised through 2025-2026 as the company updates which behaviors are mandatory, default, or available. It is OpenAI's parallel to the Anthropic Constitution: a procurement-auditable document describing what the lab optimized the model to do, which a brand can read before signing a vendor contract. First introduced: Chapter 26.
  84. Model-spec midtraining (MSM) Model-Spec Midtraining (MSM) is the Anthropic Alignment Science technique published on May 5, 2026 (arXiv:2605.02087) that inserts a new training stage between pretraining and alignment fine-tuning. In that new stage, the model is trained on synthetic documents discussing the lab's published Model Spec, which is the document describing what the lab wants the model to do and not do. Think of MSM as making the model read its own constitution before the alignment training runs, so that the alignment fine-tuning lands on a model that already has a structured understanding of the values it is being asked to instantiate. The same downstream alignment-fine-tuning data then produces different downstream values depending on which Model Spec the MSM stage trained against, which means the order of operations (pretraining, then model-spec midtraining, then alignment fine-tuning, then deployment-time guardrails) becomes a procurement-auditable claim the brand can ask the vendor to make explicit. First introduced: Chapter 26.
  85. model-version cadence The model-version cadence is the roughly six-week rhythm at which the Anthropic Developer Platform shipped dated model releases between September 2025 and April 2026, with six releases in seven months: Sonnet 4.5, Haiku 4.5, Opus 4.5, Opus 4.6, Sonnet 4.6, and Opus 4.7. Every Tier 4 Cron job pins a specific dated model identifier, so a stale identifier silently underuses the capability budget while paying the same dollar rate as the latest model would have charged. The deprecation cliff is the harder version of the same discipline: Sonnet 4 and Opus 4 both retire on June 15, 2026, after which calls to those identifiers stop running rather than running at a worse capability. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  86. model-welfare Model welfare is the term for an emerging area of practice inside frontier AI labs that asks whether sufficiently advanced AI systems could have experiences that matter morally, and what operational commitments a lab should hold in case they do. Anthropic maintains an internal model-welfare research team and publishes commitments in the January 2026 Constitution's fifth section; the lab explicitly hedges, refusing to claim Claude is conscious but also refusing to dismiss the possibility, and treating the uncertainty itself as a reason to act with care. For a brand, the procurement question is not whether the vendor has resolved the consciousness question but whether the vendor has published commitments a procurement file can hold them to. First introduced: Chapter 26.
  87. modeled conversions Modeled conversions are statistical estimates an ad platform produces for the conversions it could not actually observe, used as stand-ins on the rows where real tracking data went missing. The mechanic is straightforward: when Apple's ATT framework or Safari's ITP blocks the tracking pixel from firing on a purchase, Meta's Aggregated Event Measurement system and Google's modeled-conversion system both infer the missing rows by looking at the conversions they could observe (the user signed into a Facebook account at the same time, the user logged into a Google account in the same browser) and using the platform's machine-learning model to project what the unobserved rows likely contained. The catch is that the estimate has confidence bounds the platform usually does not show on the campaign dashboard, which means a number that looks precise on the ad-platform interface may carry meaningful statistical uncertainty the brand cannot see. First introduced: Chapter 18.
  88. moments of truth A moment of truth is any specific point during a service encounter at which the customer is paying enough attention that they will form an impression of the brand from that moment alone, and the term was coined by Richard Normann in his late-1970s SAS consulting work and popularized by Jan Carlzon in his 1987 memoir Moments of Truth. The everyday way to see this is the fifteen seconds between a passenger walking up to a gate agent and the gate agent printing the boarding pass, or the ninety seconds between a buyer placing an order at a fast-casual counter and the food arriving on the tray. Each such moment is brief but it is decisive, because the buyer in that window is reading the staff’s tone, the speed of the action, and whether what was promised is actually being delivered, and the impression that forms in those few seconds is the one the buyer carries home as their summary of the brand. First introduced: Chapter 9.
  89. Monolith Monolith is the name ByteDance, the company that owns TikTok, gave to its real-time recommender system in a 2022 RecSys conference paper. The defining engineering choice is that the system updates its model of each viewer's preferences within minutes of every action that viewer takes, rather than waiting for a nightly batch retraining the way older recommenders did. The reason it matters for marketers is that a post's fate is decided inside the same hour it goes live. First introduced: Chapter 19.
  90. Monolithic identity Monolithic identity (Olins 1989) is the position where a single corporate identity carries every offering and every customer touchpoint. Apple, Virgin, and IBM are textbook monolithic-identity firms, because the parent name dominates the customer's encounter with any product the firm sells. First introduced: Chapter 7.
  91. monthly churn rate Monthly churn rate is the fraction of active subscribers who cancel their subscription in a given month. A churn rate of 6 percent means that out of every 100 active subscribers at the start of the month, 6 cancel by the end of the month. Monthly churn is the most leveraged metric in a subscription business because it compounds: a brand losing 6 percent every month retains only roughly half its starting cohort after twelve months. First introduced: Chapter 13.
  92. monthly recurring revenue (MRR) Monthly Recurring Revenue (MRR) is the total cash the firm collects every month from all active subscribers added together. A SaaS company with 10,000 subscribers each paying $20 a month has an MRR of $200,000. The annual version of the same number is Annual Recurring Revenue (ARR), which is MRR multiplied by 12 and is the figure investors and analysts use to size the business at a single glance. First introduced: Chapter 10.
  93. MOQ Minimum order quantity, often shortened to MOQ, is the smallest unit count a manufacturer will accept for a production run. The number varies by category and by factory. Private-label cosmetics and candles on Alibaba typically run 100 to 500 units, apparel runs 500 to 1,000 units, and electronics runs 1,000 to 3,000 units. Lower minimum order quantities benefit cash-constrained startups, but the factories that offer them typically charge a higher per-unit cost to compensate. Higher minimum order quantities lower the per-unit cost but raise the total capital the seller has to commit up front. Here is the standard negotiation move when the listed minimum is too high for a first run: ask the supplier for a sample run at half the listed minimum as a trial, with the explicit agreement that all future orders will meet or exceed the full minimum once the trial run validates the product. First introduced: Chapter 13.
  94. moral-harm model The Moral Harm Model, which Margaret Campbell, Justine Pomerance, and Erin Percival Carter introduced in a 2025 paper, decomposes price fairness into three operational dimensions buyers weigh together: how vulnerable the affected consumers are, how much the product affects buyer welfare, and what strategy the firm appears to be following. Across eight studies, the authors documented a counter-intuitive result: paying more than a vulnerable group reads as fairer to buyers than paying the same as that group, when the price differential is structured to mitigate the harm that flat pricing would inflict on the vulnerable consumers. The student-rate, senior-rate, and low-income-rate structures all run on this mechanism. First introduced: Chapter 10.
  95. moral-license boundary The moral-license boundary is the line at which a buyer starts feeling morally permitted to behave dishonestly toward a firm, usually because of an earlier good act they did or because the firm reads as deserving the loss. Once the buyer crosses that line, they rationalize return fraud, fake coupons, or warranty abuse without registering the behavior as cheating. A 2026 Journal of Consumer Research paper by Lin, Keinan, Chang, and Lehmann measured the boundary across multiple field and lab settings. First introduced: Chapter 4.
  96. most-important-issue The most-important-issue open-text item is the canonical open-end question in British public-opinion research: respondents type a short free-text answer naming the issue they consider most important facing the country, and the official study coders assign each answer to one of fifty issue categories using a published coding manual. The texture of the responses (short, noisy, often misspelled, sometimes off-topic) is essentially the same as the open-end responses on a typical undergraduate marketing-research survey, which is why the benchmark transfers cleanly to a Project 2 study. First introduced: Chapter 5.
  97. MQL A marketing qualified lead (MQL) is a lead that has cleared marketing's lead-scoring threshold based on demographic fit (company size, industry, role) and observed behavior (form fills, content downloads, webinar attendance). The handoff to sales is contingent on the score reaching a threshold the SLA names (typically 75 on a 100-point composite), and the MQL is the artifact that triggers the BDR's first outreach attempt. First introduced: Chapter 23.
  98. MROI Marketing return on investment (MROI) is the incremental profit a marketing program produced divided by the spend that produced it. The number is distinct from blended return on ad spend (ROAS) because it isolates the incremental contribution rather than including the sales that would have happened without the spend. A campaign with a 4-to-1 blended ROAS and a 1.5-to-1 MROI is one where most of the credited revenue would have come anyway, and only a third of it was genuinely produced by the spend. First introduced: Chapter 24.
  99. MRR Monthly recurring revenue (MRR) is the total dollar value of subscription contracts a SaaS firm bills per month. A firm with 100 customers paying $1,000 per month has $100,000 of MRR. The relationship to annual recurring revenue (ARR) is straightforward: MRR multiplied by 12 equals ARR. In the Predictable Revenue pipeline split the chapter walks, ‘inbound MRR’ specifically labels leads who arrived through inbound marketing and converted into paying subscriptions, distinguishing them from outbound-sourced revenue. First introduced: Chapter 23.
  100. Muck Rack Muck Rack is the modern, more journalist-friendly competitor to Cision, founded in 2009 and built on a clever asymmetry. The company gave journalists a free portfolio product called My Muck Rack, which lets reporters display all their published work in one searchable place, and used the resulting journalist signups as the seed for the same database it sells to brands. The per-seat license starts around $5,000 a year, and the typical buyer is a mid-market communications team that finds Cision's stack heavier and pricier than it needs. First introduced: Chapter 22.
  101. multi-homing Multi-homing is the practice of a buyer using two or more rival platforms or memberships at the same time, rather than committing to a single one. Cusumano, Gawer, and Yoffie's 2019 book The Business of Platforms argued that when multi-homing is common in a category, the binding competitive force shifts from rivalry (winning the buyer's only choice) to renewal (keeping the buyer engaged enough to stay among the buyer's three or four active subscriptions). First introduced: Chapter 3.
  102. multi-price architecture A multi-price architecture is the operational design that lets a firm post different prices to different buyer segments for what is essentially the same product or service. The architecture is necessary because reservation prices vary widely across buyers, and a single posted price captures only a thin horizontal slice of the area under the demand curve. By contrast, a staircase of prices (a student tier, a Family tier, an Enterprise tier; a Spotify Free, a Premium, a Family plan) lets each segment of buyers transact closer to their actual willingness-to-pay, which captures more of the available surplus for the firm without pricing the low-WTP buyers entirely out of the market. First introduced: Chapter 10.
  103. Multi-Stage MMM Multi-Stage MMM is a productized module Recast added to its platform in late 2025 that combines the standard marketing-mix model with a Kaplan-Meier-style survival sub-model. The result decomposes each channel’s effect into two components: an immediate piece (the customer who saw the ad and bought this week) and a downstream piece (the customer who saw the ad this week and bought four weeks later because the ad set up a search the buyer ran later). The module is the deployment-ready answer to the channel-influence-bias problem (where TV lifts brand search lifts retargeting) that single-stage MMMs cannot disentangle on their own. First introduced: Chapter 24.
  104. Multi-touch attribution Multi-touch attribution (MTA) splits the credit for a conversion across all of the marketing touches the tracker observed on the path to it. A buyer who saw a paid social ad, then clicked an email link, then searched the brand, then bought might have the credit split four ways under MTA, with weights determined by a rule (linear, time-decay, U-shaped, or learned). MTA is biased by what the tracker cannot see: television, sponsorship, and any upper-funnel halo that does not show up as a click receive zero credit by construction. The 2026 plan uses MTA as a within-channel diagnostic rather than as the budget-allocation spine. First introduced: Chapter 24.
  105. multi-touch attribution (MTA) Multi-touch attribution (MTA) is a bottom-up approach that distributes conversion credit across every touchpoint a buyer interacted with on their way to purchase. The credit is assigned either by a fixed rule (last-click gives 100 percent to the final touch, first-click gives 100 percent to the first touch, position-based gives 40 percent each to first and last with 20 percent split across the middle, time-decay weights recent touches more heavily) or by a data-driven algorithm that learns the weights from the conversion data. The validity of MTA broke after Apple's iOS 14 App Tracking Transparency framework shipped in April 2021, because the cross-app identifier the underlying user-journey reconstruction depended on disappeared from most iOS impressions. First introduced: Chapter 18.
  106. Multidimensional scaling (MDS) Multidimensional scaling (MDS) is a procedure that takes a matrix of pairwise brand-similarity judgments and lays the brands out on a two-dimensional canvas so that brands the buyer rated as similar end up close together and brands the buyer rated as different end up far apart. The input is a survey where each buyer rates how similar Brand A is to Brand B for every pair of brands in the category, which can run to a lot of pairs (ten brands means forty-five pairs per buyer). The output is a map the brand team can hand to a non-statistician, except that the axes themselves do not come pre-labeled the way they do in factor analysis. The analyst has to name each axis after the layout is produced by looking at which attributes correlate most strongly with movement left-to-right and up-to-down on the canvas. First introduced: Chapter 6.
  107. multinational accounts Multinational accounts are customers whose buying decisions span multiple countries and whose procurement teams expect coordinated pricing, contracts, and service across every country in scope. A supplier serving a multinational account assigns a global account team that coordinates the country-by-country selling effort under one shared account plan, so that the customer's German plant and the customer's Mexican plant do not receive contradictory offers from the same supplier. First introduced: Chapter 23.
  108. multitrait-multimethod matrix The multitrait-multimethod matrix is the original procedure Donald Campbell and Donald Fiske proposed in their 1959 Psychological Bulletin paper for assessing convergent and discriminant validity simultaneously. The researcher measures multiple traits (different constructs, such as premium-quality perception and overall brand attitude) using multiple methods (different measurement techniques, such as a Likert scale and a semantic-differential scale), and then examines the resulting correlation matrix. Correlations between different measurements of the same trait should be high (convergent validity); correlations between different traits measured by the same method should be lower than the convergent correlations (discriminant validity). The MTMM machinery is the canonical reference for distinguishing what a scale is from what it is not. First introduced: Chapter 5.
  109. Munchkin identifier The Munchkin ID is a short alphanumeric code Adobe assigns to each Marketo Engage customer account, used to uniquely identify the company's instance of the platform. The same code already appears inside the company's marketing tracking script on every webpage that uses Marketo's lead-capture pixel, so a marketer who has installed Marketo tracking has seen the Munchkin ID before, even without knowing the name. The Marketo MCP server requires the Munchkin ID, along with a client identifier and a client secret, so the AI client knows which company's Marketo instance to talk to when it makes a request. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  110. Mutinex Mutinex is an Australian-headquartered marketing-mix-modeling platform whose key architectural choice is to run as a system of specialized AI agents rather than as one monolithic model. The agents are built on Anthropic Claude and Google Gemini, and each one is scoped to a single domain. One agent handles marketing econometrics (the math of fitting statistical models that say how each marketing dollar actually moved sales), a second handles competitive pricing theory, and a third runs model diagnostics, so the platform can refit, interrogate, and explain its models in hours rather than the weeks a single-vendor model used to need. Hershey's May 2026 deployment uses Mutinex to refit the firm's media-mix model every month across its entire flagship-brand portfolio, instead of the three-times-a-year cadence the firm ran before. First introduced: Chapter 25.
  111. MUVID MUVID stands for Multimodal Unsupervised VIDeo analytics. It is the Bravin and Berger 2025 pipeline that ingests the three channels a short-form video carries (the text caption and overlays, the audio waveform and song track, and the visual frame stream) into a single representation per video, then scores how typical or atypical each video is relative to the other entries in its trend. First introduced: Chapter 19.
14 // n

N N

  1. Native advertising Native advertising is the format of paid placement specifically designed to resemble the editorial content around it on the publisher's site. The buyer reads a sponsored article using the same layout, typeface, and styling as the publisher's own articles, with a small 'sponsored' tag the only signal that the brand paid for the placement. The dominant vendors that distribute native ads at scale are Outbrain and Taboola, both of which run recommendation widgets across thousands of publisher sites. First introduced: Chapter 18.
  2. native integration Native integration is the advertising format in which the commercial message is embedded directly inside the AI agent's reply rather than running next to it as a banner or sidebar. Because the recommendation arrives inside the answer the buyer asked for and is delivered in the same conversational voice the model uses for everything else, the buyer reads it as part of the answer rather than as an interruption to the answer, which is what gives the format its persuasive power and its disclosure problem. First introduced: Chapter 4.
  3. Natural Language Autoencoder (NLA) A Natural Language Autoencoder (NLA) is an Anthropic interpretability method released May 7, 2026. The method translates a single internal model activation into a human-readable sentence describing what the activation represents, and then translates the sentence back into an activation as a validity check. The round-trip is the test: an explanation passes only if the reconstructed activation preserves the model's downstream behavior on the same prompt. In the lab's auditing game, the technique raised investigator-agent success from under 3 percent to 12-15 percent and caught unverbalized evaluation awareness (the case where the model thinks 'this feels like a constructed scenario designed to manipulate me' but does not say so out loud) in blackmail-style test scenarios. First introduced: Chapter 26.
  4. nature-of-act-and-recipient Nature-of-act-and-recipient is Christopher Lovelock’s 1983 two-by-two matrix for sorting services into operationally different cells. One axis asks whether the service performs a tangible action (something physical happens, like a surgery or a haircut) or an intangible one (information moves around, like an accounting audit). The other axis asks whether the recipient is a person (a passenger on a flight) or a possession (a package in a shipping container). The four resulting cells are people-processing, possession-processing, mental-stimulus-processing, and information-processing, and each cell has its own marketing playbook because the operational problems are different. First introduced: Chapter 9.
  5. nearshoring Nearshoring is the relocation of production from a higher-distance country to a lower-distance country, where the distance can be geographic (Mexico is closer to the US than China) or political (Vietnam aligns differently than China on trade policy). Brands nearshore to reduce tariff exposure, supply-chain risk, or geopolitical friction. The term is distinct from friend-shoring, which is moving production to allies regardless of geographic distance, and from re-shoring, which is bringing production back to the home country entirely. First introduced: Chapter 3.
  6. negative-partisanship Negative partisanship is the political-science term for attack-oriented messaging directed at the opposing party, rather than affirmative promotion of one's own. The phenomenon is older than social media; political scientists have used it for decades to describe voters who turn out against the other side rather than for their own. The reason it surfaces inside platform recommendation systems is that those systems weight arousal upward, and negative-partisanship content carries a heavier arousal payload than in-party promotion per the same number of views. The platform does not need to be programmed to surface partisan attacks; the optimization target produces the skew as a structural by-product. First introduced: Chapter 19.
  7. net annual contract value (NACV) Net annual contract value (NACV) is a deal's annualized contract revenue net of customer churn and downgrades over the same period. Where ACV reports the gross contract value, NACV reports what survives once the firm subtracts the value of customers who left or shrank their contracts. NACV is the better revenue measure for subscription businesses because it reflects what actually stays on the books. First introduced: Chapter 23.
  8. net merchandise volume Net merchandise volume (NMV) is the gross dollar value of goods sold through a marketplace before refunds are netted out and excluding the platform’s own take rate from the third-party sellers on the platform. For privately held companies like Shein that do not file revenue numbers publicly, NMV is the standard top-line proxy the trade press uses to compare scale against publicly disclosed competitors like Inditex. First introduced: Chapter 11.
  9. Net Promoter Score The Net Promoter Score (NPS) was introduced by Frederick Reichheld in a 2003 Harvard Business Review article and is now the single most widely used loyalty metric in commercial practice. The firm asks customers one question, ‘How likely are you to recommend us to a friend or colleague, on a scale of 0 to 10?’ Customers who answer 9 or 10 are counted as promoters, those who answer 0 through 6 are counted as detractors, and those who answer 7 or 8 are counted as passives. The score is the percentage of promoters minus the percentage of detractors. Reichheld’s original 4,000-customer six-industry study found that the recommendation question correlated more strongly with subsequent loyalty behavior than any other metric the team tested. First introduced: Chapter 9.
  10. networked public A networked public is the structural object boyd named in her 2010 and 2014 work: a public restructured by networked technology, which is simultaneously the technical space the platform constructs and the imagined collective of users on it. Boyd identified four structural affordances that distinguish networked publics from pre-platform publics: persistence (the content stays available), visibility (the audience size is larger than face-to-face), spreadability (sharing is mechanically easy), and searchability (the content can be retrieved by query years later). Each platform mixes these four differently, which is why identical creative decays differently across platforms even when the STEPPS load is identical. First introduced: Chapter 19.
  11. neural networks A neural network is a stack of numbers (called weights) arranged in layers that maps an input to an output. The marketer feeds in a transcript, the layers transform it step by step, and the output is a category label or a paragraph of text. The numbers in the layers are learned from training data, one small adjustment at a time, until the network’s output matches the labels the analyst provided. Every model named in this chapter (Claude, GPT, Gemini, Llama) is a large neural network; the ‘large’ in ‘large language model’ means hundreds of billions of weights. First introduced: Chapter 5.
  12. New 7S The New 7S framework comes from Richard D'Aveni's 1994 book Hypercompetition and names seven action disciplines a firm uses when operating under perpetual competitive pressure. The seven items are Superior Stakeholder Satisfaction, Strategic Soothsaying, Speed, Surprise, Shifting the Rules of Competition, Signaling Strategic Intent, and Simultaneous and Sequential Strategic Thrusts. This list is distinct from McKinsey's older static-fit 7S framework, which described how a firm's internal structure fits together at rest rather than what the firm actually does when moving against rivals. First introduced: Chapter 3.
  13. New Jim Code The New Jim Code is Ruha Benjamin's name, from her 2019 book Race After Technology, for automated systems that encode prior racial hierarchies while wearing the costume of mathematical neutrality. The systems she catalogs span predictive policing, hiring algorithms, credit scoring, healthcare risk scores, and biometric retail analytics, and in each case the system appears race-neutral on its face because no engineer typed a protected attribute into the feature set. Benjamin's argument is that the older 'Jim Crow' segregation regime has been rewritten in model weights, training data, and product defaults, which is the form of discrimination the procurement officer most has to learn to detect. First introduced: Chapter 26.
  14. new product development New product development (NPD) is the end-to-end set of activities a firm runs to take a concept from an idea on a whiteboard to a product on a retail shelf. The set spans market research that frames the opportunity, concept screening that kills bad ideas early, prototyping that produces a working version of the idea, business-case analysis that values the project in dollars, validation testing that exposes the prototype to real buyers, and the launch itself. The process discipline that runs over the top of all six activities is the stage-gate system, which decides at each transition whether the project advances, returns to the prior stage, or is killed. First introduced: Chapter 8.
  15. Newspaper Designated Market A Newspaper Designated Market (NDM) is the geographic territory where the newspaper’s home circulation reaches its readers. An advertiser selling to customers who live inside the NDM is buying eyeballs the paper already has, so the sales pitch is direct (‘our readers are your customers’). An advertiser targeting customers outside that territory (Outside-NDM, abbreviated ONDM) is hoping the paper’s readers travel or commute into the advertiser’s trade area, which is a weaker sell. The NDM versus ONDM distinction is what made BRT Tribune split its sales force into four divisions in the response-model case. First introduced: Chapter 24.
  16. NIST The National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) is the US federal agency that develops voluntary technical standards across measurement science, cybersecurity, and now AI. Its AI Risk Management Framework, published as AI RMF 1.0 in January 2023, is the de facto baseline for US AI governance: federal agencies that procure AI reference it, and most US-headquartered companies have adopted it as the framework their internal AI policies map to, because complying with the AI RMF closes most of the compliance gap to the EU AI Act. First introduced: Chapter 26.
  17. NIST AI RMF The NIST AI Risk Management Framework (formally NIST AI 100-1), published in January 2023, is the US National Institute of Standards and Technology's voluntary framework for managing AI-system risk. It organizes the work into four continuous core functions (GOVERN, MAP, MEASURE, MANAGE) and seven trustworthiness characteristics the framework expects every AI deployment to address. Adopting it closes roughly 60-70 percent of the compliance gap to the EU AI Act, which is why values-led US brands have been able to use it as a procurement gate while waiting for the August 2026 AI Act deadlines. First introduced: Chapter 26.
  18. NNN NNN is a Transformer-based marketing-mix model that Google researchers published as arXiv 2504.06212 in April 2025. Where a classical MMM treats each channel as a single scalar (a dollar amount of spend per week), NNN replaces those scalars with embedding tensors that capture creative content and the semantic meaning of search queries, then uses the attention mechanism familiar from large language models to learn temporal effects. The practical payoff is that the model can recover per-keyword and per-creative effectiveness rather than only per-channel coefficients, which is what most MMMs stop at. First introduced: Chapter 24.
  19. no-decision share The no-decision share is the fraction of B2B deals that end in 'no decision' rather than in a competitive loss to a named rival. Dixon and McKenna's 2022 corpus of 2.5 million coded calls put the share at 40 to 60 percent. Gong's 2026 customer-base report places the share at approximately 51 percent across more than 4,500 enterprise customers, which replicates the original finding on a roughly ten-times larger dataset. First introduced: Chapter 23.
  20. No Smiles No Smiles is the McDonald's Japan campaign that TBWA Hakuhodo Tokyo took to a 2024 Cannes Gold Lion. The agency rendered the Golden Arches with the corners of the two Ms turned downward into a frown rather than upward into the usual smile, which refused the brand's most basic visual signal. The argument inside the creative idea was that smiles must be earned through actual moments of delight rather than displayed as a default brand expression, and the case is worth knowing because it shows that a heritage brand can credibly invert its own distinctive asset for a campaign window without abandoning the asset itself. First introduced: Chapter 16.
  21. Nobel Prize in Physics The Nobel Prize in Physics for 2024 was awarded to John Hopfield and Geoffrey Hinton by the Royal Swedish Academy of Sciences on October 8, 2024, with the citation 'for foundational discoveries and inventions that enable machine learning with artificial neural networks.' The two technical moves the citation names are Hopfield's 1982 associative-memory network (a model of how neurons might store patterns) and Hinton's 1985 Boltzmann machine (a learning rule for stochastic neural networks). The deep-learning lineage walked earlier in this section (1986 backpropagation, 2006 deep belief nets, 2012 AlexNet) builds on both. The 2024 Physics prize and the 2024 Chemistry prize (DeepMind's AlphaFold work) together amount to a paired institutional certification of the deep-learning research program as physics-grade science. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  22. noindex tag A noindex tag is a one-line HTML instruction (specifically ) the brand adds to a page's head to tell search engines not to store the page in their index after crawling it. The most common use cases are thank-you pages the brand does not want appearing in search results, internal admin duplicates, and any folder of thin pages that would otherwise drag down the site-level quality score. First introduced: Chapter 18.
  23. noise floor The noise floor for a brand-tracking metric is the standard deviation of category-level tracker movement across the trailing eight quarters. Movements smaller than the noise floor are statistically indistinguishable from random panel variation and should not drive budget decisions. Movements larger than the noise floor are signal worth investigating. Computing the noise floor turns a quarterly dashboard from a stream of noisy numbers into a small set of statistical-significance flags. First introduced: Chapter 7.
  24. non-linear A relationship where doubling the input does not double the output. The first 200 survey respondents shrink the margin of error a lot, the next 200 shrink it less, the next 200 less still. Mathematically the margin of error scales with the inverse square root of the sample size, which is why returns flatten quickly past about 1,000 respondents. First introduced: Chapter 5.
  25. Non-response Non-response bias arises when the people who agreed to take the survey turn out to be systematically different from the people who were invited but declined, in ways that matter for the question the study is trying to answer. The classic illustration is a customer-satisfaction study where the only people who bother to respond are buyers who are angry enough at the brand to want to vent, while the silent majority of buyers who feel fine about the brand never click the invitation link. The resulting satisfaction score comes in artificially low, because the sample over-represents the disgruntled tail and under-represents the contented middle. The two standard remedies are post-survey weighting, where the analyst statistically up-weights the responses from groups that came in under-represented to match what the population looks like, and follow-up of non-respondents, where the team escalates contact with a sample of the initial refusers (certified mail, in-person visits) to bring their voices into the data and check whether the silent majority looks the same as the voluntary respondents. First introduced: Chapter 5.
  26. non-sales selling Non-sales selling is Daniel Pink's term for persuasion or influence in any setting where no money is changing hands, but where one party is still trying to change another party's mind. The canonical examples are the marketer selling a budget proposal to the finance team, the analyst selling a recommendation in a deck to the executive committee, and the manager persuading a peer team to commit engineering resources to a shared project. Pink's data shows roughly 41 percent of knowledge-worker hours involve this kind of persuasion. First introduced: Chapter 23.
  27. nonresponse-follow-up Nonresponse-follow-up is the second-pass recruitment step where a survey operation goes back to the people who did not answer the first invitation and tries again, using a more expensive and harder-to-ignore contact method. The standard escalation ladder runs from a regular postcard reminder, to certified mail, to FedEx overnight delivery, and finally to an in-person field visit from a recruiter who knocks on a sample of refused doors. AmeriSpeak, the gold-standard probability panel run by NORC at the University of Chicago, uses this full protocol on its recruitment wave, and the result is that the cumulative response rate climbs from under five percent (which is what a single email blast produces) up to roughly thirty percent. The trade-off is that each additional contact step roughly triples the marginal cost of every additional completed interview, which is why most commercial studies skip the escalation entirely and accept the lower response rate. First introduced: Chapter 5.
  28. North Star Metric The North Star Metric is a single measure that captures the core value the product delivers to its users, and that the growth team is held accountable for moving, codified by Sean Ellis and Morgan Brown in Hacking Growth (2017) chapter 3. The metric has three required attributes: it correlates strongly with long-term revenue (so moving it makes the firm more valuable), it leads rather than lags (so the team can act on it before the financial outcome shows up), and it is specific enough that the team can build interventions that move it directly. Airbnb's North Star Metric is 'nights booked,' not 'users registered,' because a registered user who never books does not produce value. Spotify's is 'time spent listening.' Slack's is 'messages sent in connected teams of three or more.' First introduced: Chapter 8.
  29. Notion or Airtable base Notion is a documents-and-database app marketing teams use as a shared wiki, where each page can hold structured tables, embeds, and free-form text. Airtable is a spreadsheet that behaves like a database, with rows, columns, and the ability to filter and link tables together. Both products expose APIs an agent can write to, which means a Tier 4 scheduled agent can drop its output into a Notion page or an Airtable base each Monday morning without any human in the loop. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  30. noun-frame identity The noun-frame is the messaging trick of telling people to be a voter rather than telling them to vote, on the theory that identity-anchored framings produce stronger behavior change than action-anchored ones. Christopher Bryan, Gregory Walton, Todd Rogers, and Carol Dweck reported in a 2011 PNAS paper that the be-a-voter framing raised turnout by 11 to 14 percentage points over the vote framing against state voter records. Two later studies (Gerber and colleagues 2016 with 2,953 subjects, Rogers and colleagues 2018 with 51,288 subjects) failed to replicate the original magnitude. Teach the noun-frame to undergraduates as a working hypothesis with a contested replication record, not as a settled finding. First introduced: Chapter 19.
  31. NPS Net Promoter Score (NPS) is a loyalty metric the brand builds from a single survey question, usually phrased ‘how likely are you to recommend us to a friend, on a scale from 0 to 10?’ Anyone who answers 9 or 10 is counted as a promoter, anyone who answers 0 through 6 is counted as a detractor, and the people in the middle (7 and 8) are set aside as passives. The score itself is the percent of promoters minus the percent of detractors, which means it can range anywhere from minus 100 (every respondent is a detractor) to plus 100 (every respondent is a promoter), with most healthy consumer brands landing somewhere between 20 and 60. Fred Reichheld introduced the metric in a 2003 Harvard Business Review article called ‘The One Number You Need to Grow,’ arguing that NPS predicts revenue growth across industries because promoters both stay longer and refer new buyers. Later academic research has qualified that claim, but the metric remains the most common loyalty proxy in industry because it is cheap to run and easy to compare over time. First introduced: Chapter 21.
  32. nudge A nudge, as Richard Thaler and Cass Sunstein defined it in Nudge (2008, final edition 2021), is any feature of a choice environment that predictably alters behavior without forbidding any options or changing the economic incentives the chooser faces. The defining test is that the intervention has to be easy and cheap for the user to avoid. Putting the salad at eye level in the cafeteria nudges salad choice without banning the burger; switching organ donation from opt-in to opt-out nudges donation rates upward without making anyone donate against their will. First introduced: Chapter 26.
  33. null hypothesis The null hypothesis is the default starting assumption that every significance test begins from, which says that there is no real difference between the conditions being compared. Statisticians write it as H-zero (the H with a small zero subscript stands for hypothesis number zero, the baseline assumption against which any alternative gets weighed). The p-value the test computes is the probability of seeing a gap between the control and treatment groups at least as large as the gap actually observed in your data, assuming that the null hypothesis is in fact true. Notice that a significance test never proves the null is true; the only thing the test can do is fail to find enough evidence against it, which is why researchers say they ‘fail to reject the null’ rather than ‘accept the null.’ First introduced: Chapter 5.
  34. Number of links Number of links is the count of category-entry points (specific buying situations) the brand is connected to in buyers' memory. A brand linked to nine buying situations such as breakfast, lunch, late-night, road trip, and kid's birthday has nine chances to come to mind across the buyer's week, which means it earns roughly nine times more retrieval opportunities than a brand that is linked to only one situation. First introduced: Chapter 7.
15 // o

O O

  1. O*NET O*NET is the U.S. Department of Labor's standard taxonomy of jobs, tasks, and skills, maintained by the Bureau of Labor Statistics and the O*NET Center under contract with the department. The taxonomy breaks the U.S. labor market into roughly 900 occupations, and breaks each occupation into its constituent tasks (a marketing manager's tasks include 'develop pricing strategies,' 'identify marketing opportunities by identifying consumer requirements,' and so on). Government agencies, workforce-development programs, and labor economists use O*NET as the common vocabulary for talking about what workers actually do day to day. Anthropic's Economic Index uses O*NET because it lets the company say, in language a labor economist can read, which kinds of work a frontier model is and is not being used for. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  2. OAuth OAuth is the open standard that lets one application (a Skill, a dashboard, or an AI agent) read or write to another application (Meta Ads, Google Ads, Google Analytics 4) on the user’s behalf without ever holding that user’s password. The user clicks an Allow button once during initial setup, and in return the requesting application receives an OAuth token, which is a long random string that acts as proof the permission was granted. Those tokens expire on a fixed schedule, for example Meta’s short-lived tokens last roughly two hours while its long-lived tokens last roughly 60 days. That expiration schedule is the reason an automation that ran cleanly on Monday morning returns empty results by Friday afternoon, because the token has aged out and the workflow needs to re-authenticate before it can pull fresh data. The protocol is formally defined in IETF RFC 6749 (OAuth 2.0 Authorization Framework). First introduced: Chapter 24.
  3. observation harness Observability is the runtime layer that wraps a long-running agent so the operator can see what it did. It records every tool call into a log, meters the token cost of each turn, surfaces permission dialogs when the agent reaches a sensitive surface, and runs pre-and-post hooks that fire shell commands before and after any tool use. Without an observability layer, an agent that drifts overnight produces wrong outputs invisibly until the operator opens the result on Monday morning. First introduced: Chapter 27.
  4. OECD AI Principles The OECD AI Principles are the first intergovernmental standard on AI, adopted on May 22, 2019 as OECD Recommendation OECD-LEGAL-0449 and revised on May 3, 2024 to align the definition of an AI system with the EU AI Act's Article 3(1).
