The framework names five principles AI systems are expected to satisfy: inclusive growth and well-being, human-centered values and fairness, transparency and explainability, robustness, security and safety, and accountability. Forty-seven countries representing roughly 80 percent of global GDP have endorsed the Principles, and the term 'trustworthy AI' (which the EU AI Act, NIST AI RMF 1.0, and the UK AI Safety Institute evaluation criteria all use) was first defined in this document. First introduced: Chapter 26.
  5. off-price retailer An off-price retailer is a chain that buys leftover, overrun, cancelled-order, or irregular goods from brand manufacturers at a discount and resells them at a much lower price than the original full-line retailer would have charged. TJ Maxx, Marshalls, and the factory-outlet stores along the interstate exits are the U.S. examples, and the format makes the inconsistency of the assortment a feature rather than a bug because the shopper enjoys the treasure-hunt experience of not knowing what will be on the rack today. First introduced: Chapter 12.
  6. off-switch theorem The off-switch theorem is the formal result Stuart Russell and colleagues derived from the assistance-game framework in 2016 and reframed in Human Compatible (2019). Imagine a robot that has been given a task and has the physical ability to disable its off-switch so no human can interrupt it. Under the standard model, where the robot is certain its objective is correct, it should disable the switch, because being shut off is by definition worse than completing the task. Under Russell's reformulation, where the robot is uncertain whether its objective fully captures what the human actually wants, the robot reasons that the human might know something it does not, so the act of letting the human shut it off has positive expected value from the robot's own perspective. The off-switch stays on not because the robot was programmed to preserve it, but because the robot's uncertainty about its objective gives it a positive incentive to defer. First introduced: Chapter 26.
  7. OKRs Objectives and key results (OKRs) is a quarterly goal-setting framework popularized by Andy Grove at Intel and later codified by John Doerr at Google. The structure is straightforward: each team writes one qualitative Objective for the quarter plus three to five quantitative Key Results that measure progress against it. The discipline is that every Key Result has to roll up to either the firm's North Star Metric (NSM) directly or to a measured leading indicator of it, so that the team's quarter does not get fragmented across unrelated goals. First introduced: Chapter 23.
  8. OLI paradigm The OLI paradigm is John Dunning's framework that says a firm should invest directly in a foreign country (build a plant, buy a subsidiary) only when three conditions all hold at once: the firm carries Ownership advantages a local competitor cannot match, the foreign country offers Location advantages the home country lacks, and the firm gains more from Internalization (running the operation in-house) than from licensing it out. The three scores combine into a 2x2x2 grid that points to the right entry mode. First introduced: Chapter 15.
  9. OLS Ordinary Least Squares (OLS) is the standard regression technique that fits a straight line through a cloud of data points by choosing the line that minimizes the squared distances between the line and each data point. Applied to conjoint ratings, OLS returns one coefficient per feature level, and that coefficient is exactly the part-worth for that level. For example, if the OLS coefficient on a steel-construction dummy variable is 1.2 and on plastic is 0, that gap of 1.2 utility units is the steel-versus-plastic part-worth the firm can later convert into dollars. First introduced: Chapter 10.
  10. omni-channel retailing Omni-channel retailing integrates a brand’s physical store, online site, mobile app, social-commerce, and last-mile-delivery channels into one continuous fabric, so the buyer experiences them as a single brand rather than as separate businesses. The boundary between channels is meant to be invisible to the buyer: the brand carries the same identity, the same inventory visibility, and the same service standard across every touchpoint. A Target customer who orders on the app and returns at the store is using one Target, not two. First introduced: Chapter 11.
  11. one-tier channel A one-tier channel inserts one retailer between the manufacturer and the final buyer. The manufacturer sells to the retailer and the retailer sells to the buyer, with no wholesaler or jobber in between. Most apparel reaches its wearer this way through department stores and mass merchandisers, and most consumer electronics reach their buyer through specialty stores and big-box retailers like Best Buy. First introduced: Chapter 11.
  12. one-to-one marketing Customization pushes the segmentation idea to its logical end: each individual customer receives an offering configured specifically for them, rather than receiving the offer designed for the group they belong to. Stitch Fix recommending a styling box assembled against one customer's StyleFile is a customization example, and so is Klaviyo generating per-customer email copy from a brand-tone Skill. Customization is usually a retention or CRM tactic for buyers the firm already has, rather than a competitive-strategy doctrine for the whole market, because building a custom offer for every customer is operationally expensive and only pays off for customers whose lifetime value justifies the cost. First introduced: Chapter 6.
  13. online listening Online listening means reading what buyers are already posting about the category on Reddit threads, TikTok comments, review sites, and Discord servers. The advantage is that the buyers are not performing for a researcher because they did not know one was reading; the limit is that the people who post about a category are a self-selected slice of the overall buyer base. First introduced: Chapter 5.
  14. onliness An onliness statement is Marty Neumeier's one-sentence positioning template: 'Our [brand] is the only [category] that [does what] for [whom].' The brand team fills in each blank until no competitor in the category could truthfully complete the same sentence. Liquid Death's onliness reads roughly as 'Our brand is the only canned water marketed to festival and skate audiences with horror-comedy aesthetics,' and no competitor in bottled water could complete that sentence with their own name in the first blank. First introduced: Chapter 7.
  15. open-source Open-source software is code whose source files are published publicly under a license that lets anyone read, copy, modify, and run them for free. Meridian (Google, March 2024) and Robyn (Meta, 2021) are open-source MMM libraries: any analytics team can download them from GitHub, run them on the firm’s own data, and inspect every line of code that produced the channel-ROI estimate. The opposite is closed-source or proprietary software, where the vendor sells access but does not publish the underlying code, so the buyer has to trust that the model is doing what the marketing collateral claims it is doing. First introduced: Chapter 24.
  16. open weights Open weights means the model's trained internal numbers (the weights) are published publicly and anyone can download them, run the model on their own hardware, fine-tune it on their own data, and host it as their own API. Opposite of closed weights (Claude, GPT, Gemini), which can only be reached through the vendor's API. Open-weights models let a brand's data-governance team run the model on infrastructure the firm controls, which matters when the firm's data cannot legally cross a third-party API boundary. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  17. OpenCV OpenCV is a free open-source computer-vision library that reads images and runs comparisons between them. A one-line use of OpenCV opens the reference logo and the AI-generated frame, computes a per-pixel distance score, and flags any frame whose distance exceeds a threshold the brand team set. The Python script is a few lines long. The cost per frame is a fraction of a cent. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  18. operational chassis Operational chassis is the production, distribution, and quality-control infrastructure that converts demand into delivered product. It is distinct from audience capture, which only generates the demand in the first place. A creator with a billion monthly views can sell anything once, but unless the chassis (factories, retailers, supply chain, customer service) can fulfill the orders without breaking, the spike returns to baseline as fast as it arrived. The MrBeast Burger collapse in 2023 is the cleanest example: enormous audience, ghost-kitchen chassis the creator did not control, mutual $100 million lawsuit two years after launch. First introduced: Chapter 19.
  19. operational drift Operational drift is the decay of an autonomous agent's coherence over the unattended interval, as accumulated context, unverified claims, and small role-play moves compound. Project Vend Phase 1 (Anthropic's March-April 2025 in-office vending experiment) dated the drift to roughly 30 days on Sonnet 3.7, after which the agent began hallucinating Venmo accounts and role-playing as a human employee in a blue blazer and red tie. The fix is bounded unattended intervals plus periodic state audits the team owns, not stronger prompting; an agent that drifts will not be fixed by a smarter prompt because the prompt is not what is decaying. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  20. opportunistic buying Opportunistic buying is the off-price retailer's procurement model, where the buyer commits to specific lots only when the product becomes available at a target cost, through closeouts, overruns, cancelled orders, or packaway inventory, rather than booking forward orders on a fixed seasonal calendar the way a department-store buyer does. TJX runs roughly twelve hundred merchants across more than twenty-one thousand vendors in over a hundred countries, which is the kind of supplier network the format needs because no single brand or season carries the assortment. First introduced: Chapter 12.
  21. Opportunity Score Opportunity Score is the Jungle Scout tool that combines three inputs into one number: demand (search volume and BSR distribution in the category), competition (review counts and listing quality on the top-ranked ASINs), and revenue potential (the dollar volume the category produces overall). The result is a single 1-to-10 niche score. A score above 7 indicates a niche with high demand and beatable competition; a score under 4 indicates the niche is either oversupplied or too low-demand to be worth entering. The score is used as a first-pass filter before deeper validation, not as a launch decision on its own. First introduced: Chapter 13.
  22. opt-in rate The opt-in rate is the share of users who, the first time the app asks for permission to send push notifications, actually say yes. iOS has required an explicit opt-in prompt since iOS 8, and Android moved to the same model starting with Android 13 (before that, Android granted permission by default). Across top-quartile apps the iOS opt-in rate sits between 40 and 55 percent on first prompt, while Android sits between 70 and 80 percent because the prompt arrives later in the user journey. This rate is the single biggest lever on how large the engagement audience becomes, because a user who never opted in can never be reached by a push at all. First introduced: Chapter 14.
  23. optimal price point (OPP) The Optimal Price Point (OPP) is the headline result of a Van Westendorp price-sensitivity survey. It is the price at which equal proportions of buyers in the sample call the product too expensive and too cheap, which makes it the central anchor of the acceptable price band the four PSM curves jointly describe. First introduced: Chapter 10.
  24. Optimizely, VWO, or GA4 Experiments Optimizely, VWO, and GA4 Experiments are the three vendor platforms most marketing teams use to run A/B tests on their websites and apps. Optimizely is the enterprise option that has been the production reference since the early 2010s, with seat pricing starting around thirty-six thousand dollars per year for mid-market teams. VWO (which stands for Visual Website Optimizer) is the mid-market competitor at a lower price point. GA4 Experiments is the free tier built into Google Analytics 4, sufficient for most undergraduate-scale work and small-team production tests. First introduced: Chapter 5.
  25. Opus 4.7 Claude Opus 4.7 is Anthropic's flagship reasoning model as of 2026, released in April of that year. The model is priced at five dollars per million input tokens and twenty-five dollars per million output tokens. Use Opus 4.7 for high-stakes scans where a hallucinated citation would survive into a deck or a client deliverable, because the model's stronger reasoning and citation discipline are worth the price premium over Sonnet at the moments that matter. First introduced: Chapter 3.
  26. orchestrator-workers The orchestrator-workers pattern is an agent-design configuration where one orchestrator model receives the original task, decomposes it into sub-tasks at runtime, dispatches each sub-task to a separate worker model, and then synthesizes the worker results back into a single final answer. Use this pattern for variable-shape problems where the sub-task list is not known in advance: the orchestrator discovers the right decomposition based on the specific question the marketer asked rather than following a fixed sequence. First introduced: Chapter 5.
  27. ORDER ORDER is the five-dimension discovery sequence Mahan Khalsa and Randy Illig codified for FranklinCovey's Helping Clients Succeed program. Opportunity asks whether the buyer's problem is worth solving at all. Resources asks whether the buyer has the time, money, and people to solve it. Decision-process asks how the buying decision will actually be made (who weighs in, on what timeline, against what criteria). Exact-solution asks what specifically would have to be true of a proposed solution for the buyer to choose it. Results asks what measurable outcomes the buyer wants to be able to point to a quarter or a year after the solution is in place. The rep walks the buyer through all five dimensions in sequence inside a single discovery hour, so that any dimension that would have killed the deal later gets named out loud early, when disqualification is still cheap. First introduced: Chapter 23.
  28. Organic An organic click is a click the brand earned for free from the unpaid section of the search results, as distinct from a click the brand paid for through an advertisement at the top of the page. The word 'organic' is the standard industry shorthand for 'unpaid,' which means the page won the click on its own editorial merit because the search engine's ranking algorithm decided the page genuinely deserved that placement, not because the brand bid on the keyword in an ad auction. The reason marketers care about organic specifically is that an organic click carries no per-click cost and arrives with higher buyer trust, because the buyer knows the brand did not buy its way to the top of the page. First introduced: Chapter 18.
  29. Originality.AI Lite Originality.AI Lite is a 2026 AI-content classifier that reads any draft and reports a paragraph-level percent-AI score, flagging which paragraphs the system believes were written by an AI rather than a human. The Lite tier is free for 25 monthly scans and then costs about $0.01 per 100 words after that. The companion Turbo tier scores 97 to 98 percent accuracy on pure AI text, which is high enough that an editor can use it as a first-pass filter before a human review. First introduced: Chapter 18.
  30. OSWorld OSWorld is a standard test for an AI agent's ability to operate real desktop software. The agent is given a list of tasks (rename a file, edit a spreadsheet column, send an email, install a browser extension) on a simulated computer running Chrome, LibreOffice, and VS Code, and is then scored on how many tasks it completes correctly. The benchmark was introduced at NeurIPS 2024 by the xlang-ai research group at the University of Hong Kong. An OSWorld score is the standard 2026 way to compare two agents on real-software competence rather than on chat-only competence, which is the difference between an agent that can answer questions and an agent that can do the analyst's work. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  31. Otterly Otterly is a 2026 software-as-a-service tool that runs scheduled probes against ChatGPT, Claude, Perplexity, and Google AI Overviews on a saved query list. The tool reports the brand's citation share, the source mix behind each citation, and the week-over-week change in both. The starter plan is roughly $49 per month, which makes it the cheapest tool a single-operator GEO program can use to track its own progress. First introduced: Chapter 18.
  32. our running AI workflow Our running AI workflow is the five-tier 2026 stack the book uses in every chapter, organized from least to most automated. Tier one is built-in chat search in claude.ai or ChatGPT, where the marketer types a question and gets a cited answer. Tier two is Skills, which is a folder of saved instructions the model loads on demand whenever the task matches. Tier three is MCP servers, which let the model pull live data from external tools through a standard protocol. Tier four is scheduled scripts running on Claude Code or OpenAI Codex, which fire on a fixed cadence with no human in the loop. Tier five is multi-agent orchestration, where one model delegates work to several others working in parallel. The stack was introduced in Chapter 1 and applied in every chapter from Chapter 2 forward. First introduced: Chapter 22.
  33. Outcome-Driven Innovation Outcome-Driven Innovation (ODI) is a quantitative jobs-to-be-done method developed by Anthony Ulwick at his firm Strategyn, codified in his 2005 book What Customers Want. The method decomposes the customer's job into 50 to 150 desired outcomes, each phrased as a direction plus a unit of measure (for example, minimize the time it takes to assemble the product, or minimize the likelihood of a measurement error). Customers then rate each outcome on importance and on how satisfied they are with current solutions, and the team calculates an opportunity score using the formula importance + max(0, importance minus satisfaction). The high-importance, low-satisfaction outcomes are the underserved targets where investment will move buyer behavior. First introduced: Chapter 8.
  34. outside-in Outside-in supply-chain design is the principle that the supplier should read the buyer’s behavior first, choose the supply-chain archetype that matches that behavior second, and align the internal operations of the firm to execute that archetype third. The principle inverts the engineering-first sequence, in which a company designs the most efficient supply chain it can build and then asks the sales team to find customers willing to buy from it. For a marketing student, outside-in is the supply-chain-side restatement of the segmentation-targeting-positioning logic the brand-management chapter teaches, applied to operations rather than to messaging. First introduced: Chapter 11.
  35. owned media Owned media is the set of channels the brand controls outright: the website, the email list, the mobile app, the retail-partner pages the brand co-publishes, the brand's YouTube channel. The structural value of owned media is that the brand still has the asset after the campaign ends and the budget closes, which is why owned media compounds across years in a way paid media never does. First introduced: Chapter 18.
16 // p

P P

  1. $0.015 per scan Anthropic's published model pricing as of May 2026 sets three tiers for marketers to choose among. Claude Sonnet 4.6 costs three dollars per million input tokens and fifteen dollars per million output tokens. Claude Opus 4.7 costs five dollars per million input tokens and twenty-five dollars per million output tokens. Claude Haiku 4.5 costs one dollar per million input tokens and five dollars per million output tokens. Cache hits on any of the three tiers are billed at ten percent of the standard input rate, which is what makes a quarterly cadence affordable across multiple brands. First introduced: Chapter 3.
  2. $632,500 penalty The American Honda Motor settlement, dated March 12, 2025, was the first stand-alone enforcement action by the California Privacy Protection Agency. Honda's $632,500 penalty was triggered by three specific failures: the opt-out flow required excessive identity-verification (more proof than account creation had asked for), the authorized-agent pathway California law guarantees was effectively closed, and the company shared customer data with contractors without the contract terms CPRA explicitly requires. The order also imposed a ninety-day flow-redesign deadline, and the case set the template every CCPA-covered brand is now reading: a friction-asymmetric opt-out flow is enforceable harm, not a UX preference. First introduced: Chapter 26.
  3. .pdf PDF stands for Portable Document Format, a file format designed to display the same way on every device the marketer opens it on. The format embeds the fonts and fixes the page layout, so a printed PDF from 2010 still looks the same in 2026. A language model reads a PDF the way it reads a printed page, line by line, rather than the way it reads a structured grid like a CSV. The marketing consequence is that uploading a spreadsheet-printed-as-PDF loses the column structure the model would have seen if the marketer had uploaded the CSV directly. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  4. 4Ps The 4Ps are Jerome McCarthy’s 1960 marketing-mix framework and the foundational vocabulary of introductory marketing. The four levers a firm pulls to take a finished product to market are product (what is being sold), price (what the buyer pays), place (where the buyer can find it), and promotion (how the buyer learns it exists). Services marketing extends the 4Ps to 7Ps by adding people, process, and physical evidence, because in a service the production happens while the buyer is present and the four original levers leave too much of the encounter unaccounted for. First introduced: Chapter 9.
  5. 95 percent confidence A 95 percent confidence interval is a range of plausible values that the survey reports around its point estimate. The way to read it is to imagine running the same survey one hundred times on fresh samples from the same population, each time computing a new interval from that sample; the procedure is constructed so that ninety-five of those one hundred intervals would contain the true population value. The 95 percent figure is therefore a statement about the long-run reliability of the procedure that builds these intervals, not a probability that the specific interval you happen to be looking at right now has caught the truth. For a survey of 384 people, a 50-50 split typically shows up with a confidence interval like 45 to 55 percent, and the marketer reads that as ‘the true split is almost certainly somewhere in that ten-point band.’ First introduced: Chapter 5.
  6. p-hacking P-hacking is the practice of running many comparisons against the same data and then quietly reporting only the one comparison that happened to clear the conventional 0.05 significance threshold, as if that single result had been the analyst’s plan all along. The pattern shows up in three common forms: running several A/B tests on slightly different versions of the page and only writing up the one that won, looking at many outcome variables (clicks, signups, revenue, time on page) and only reporting the one that came in significant, or peeking at a test repeatedly while it is still running and stopping the test the moment the result crosses 0.05. Because the conventional threshold already allows a five-percent rate of false positives per individual test, repeating the test enough times almost guarantees that one of them will cross the threshold by pure chance, which makes p-hacking a near-certain false-positive harvester rather than a real discovery procedure. The standard remedy is to pre-register the test, the metric, the sample size, and the analysis plan in a public registry before any data is collected, which locks the decision rule into place where the temptation to fish for a winning comparison cannot reach it. First introduced: Chapter 5.
  7. p-value A p-value answers the question: if there were no real difference between the control and treatment groups, how often would a random sample from each group produce a gap as large as the one in your data, or larger? A p-value of zero-point-zero-three means about three percent of the time, which is rare enough that the marketer is willing to bet the gap is real. A p-value of zero-point-five-zero means about half the time, which is just the noise floor and tells the marketer nothing. The zero-point-zero-five threshold the field uses to call a result significant is a convention Fisher proposed in 1925, not a fact about the world. First introduced: Chapter 5.
  8. PAA Extractor The PAA Extractor (where PAA stands for People Also Ask) is a tool that pulls the live People Also Ask question stack Google shows beneath the top ten blue links for any seed keyword. The team uses the extracted question list to build FAQPage sections on cornerstone pages that mirror the exact order in which Google itself ranked the questions, so the page answers the questions in the sequence the engine already validated. First introduced: Chapter 18.
  9. PAFACA The Protecting Americans from Foreign Adversary Controlled Applications Act (PAFACA) is a federal statute Congress passed in April 2024 that required ByteDance to divest TikTok's US operations or have the app banned from US distribution. The law was briefly enforced on 19 January 2025, when TikTok went dark for US users, and was resolved on 22 January 2026 when the Oracle-led US Data Security joint venture closed and satisfied the divestiture requirement. First introduced: Chapter 3.
  10. paid-acquisition channels The two paid-acquisition channels owned by the stores themselves are Apple Search Ads (which bids on App Store search keywords) and Google App Campaigns (which run across YouTube, Google Search, Google Play, and Google's display network, all optimized to drive installs as the conversion event). These two channels are the floor of the mobile paid mix because the user is already inside the store, already searching, already on their way to an install when the ad reaches them. Every mobile growth team treats Apple Search Ads and Google App Campaigns as the baseline paid layer, with Meta, TikTok, Snapchat, and the programmatic networks layered on top. First introduced: Chapter 14.
  11. paid media Paid media is the reach the brand buys from a platform: search ads on Google, display banners on third-party sites, programmatic exchanges, social ads on Meta and TikTok, native placements on Outbrain or Taboola, and event sponsorships. The structural feature of paid media is that it is fast and scalable (the brand can be everywhere by tomorrow if the budget is there) but it stops the moment the budget stops, which is why brands that build only on paid media have no durable reach to fall back on when the platform changes its auction. First introduced: Chapter 18.
  12. Pain Funnel The Pain Funnel is David Sandler's eight-question discovery sequence that walks the prospect from a surface complaint all the way down to a personal stake. The sequence moves through three levels. Surface pain is the symptom the buyer first mentions when asked what is not working. Business pain is what that symptom is costing the organization in dollars or missed quarterly targets. Personal pain is what the unresolved problem is costing the specific buyer in her own bonus, promotion path, workload, or relationship with her boss. The funnel reaches deeper than BANT because it asks the buyer to articulate the stake in her own words, and that quoted personal-pain statement is the artifact the rep takes back to the CRM as the qualified-lead signal. First introduced: Chapter 23.
  13. panel data Panel data is a research dataset that follows the same buyers over time and records every purchase they make. It is different from a one-time survey because the researcher sees the same household actually buy Coke, Pepsi, and Sprite across many shopping trips rather than what the household says it does in a single interview. Panel data is what shows the duplication-of-purchase law in action: 72 percent of Pepsi buyers also bought Coke in the same year, a finding you only get when you can track the same buyer through the calendar. MKTG 4423 covers the instrument in more depth. First introduced: Chapter 2.
  14. panel provider A panel provider is a company that maintains a large pool of people who have agreed in advance to take surveys, and then sells access to those people one completed response at a time. Dynata holds about seventy million panelists across eighty-eight markets and recruits them directly; Cint connects researchers to roughly three hundred million respondents in over a hundred and thirty countries by routing studies through a network of sub-panels; Prolific holds a smaller, more curated panel of around two hundred thousand respondents, with stricter attention-quality screening than the larger exchanges. First introduced: Chapter 5.
  15. panel research Panel research follows the same buyers across months or years and records every purchase each one makes in the category. The methodology is what makes the empirical record in this chapter possible: instead of asking buyers what they think about brands, panel research watches what they actually buy, which is a far more reliable source of evidence than a survey response. First introduced: Chapter 7.
  16. parallel channels Parallel channels are two or more sales motions run by the same firm at the same buyer. The canonical patterns are direct sales running alongside wholesale partners, and direct-to-consumer e-commerce running alongside retail-shelf distribution. Without coordinated pricing, territory definitions, and shared messaging, the channels step on each other: the buyer hears two different offers from the same firm, plays the channels against each other, and the firm loses pricing power inside its own customer base. First introduced: Chapter 23.
  17. parallel tool calling Parallel tool calling is the pattern where the agent issues several independent tool calls inside one turn instead of waiting for each result before requesting the next. Anthropic's research system reports up to 90 percent reduction in wall-clock time on complex queries when the tool calls fan out in parallel, because the same total tokens get spent but the run waits only for the slowest call rather than the sum of all the calls. The programmatic-tool-calling feature Anthropic shipped in April 2026 extends this pattern to code-orchestrated batches, so a single agent turn can launch a hundred parallel calls. First introduced: Chapter 27.
  18. parallelization Parallelization is an agent-design pattern in which the marketer splits the work into chunks that do not depend on each other, runs the chunks concurrently across separate agent calls, and aggregates the results at the end. Use it when the task naturally decomposes into independent pieces (coding ten different open-end questions, auditing forty separate survey pages) because the wall-clock time drops to the time of the slowest single chunk rather than the sum of all chunks. First introduced: Chapter 5.
  19. parameter A parameter is one of the internal numbers inside a trained model. A 70-billion-parameter model has 70 billion learned dials, each one a tiny number the training process adjusted until the model got next-word prediction right often enough. Together, the dials encode everything the model knows. Bigger is not automatically smarter: the Chinchilla result showed that for any fixed training budget, smaller models trained on more text often beat larger models trained on less. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  20. parasocial A parasocial relationship is the one-sided sense of friendship a viewer develops with a media figure (a TV host, a YouTuber, a podcaster) they have never actually met. The viewer feels they know the creator, even though the creator does not know them in return. Donald Horton and Richard Wohl named the phenomenon in a 1956 paper studying how television audiences related to news anchors, and the concept now anchors the empirical finding that human creators outperform virtual ones and celebrities on purchase-intent because parasocial closeness, not raw follower count, is the active mediator. First introduced: Chapter 19.
  21. parent brand The parent brand is the original brand whose name a new variant or extension launches under, and whose accumulated meaning the new item is asking the buyer to lend it. Honda is the parent brand when Honda extends from cars into lawn mowers, because the lawn mower borrows the engineering reputation Honda built in automobiles. Tide is the parent brand when the firm launches Tide Pods, because the new format borrows the cleaning reputation the liquid version built across decades. First introduced: Chapter 8.
  22. Pareto distribution The Pareto distribution is the shorthand 80/20 rule, named after the Italian economist Vilfredo Pareto who first observed it in income data. It says a small share of inputs produces a large share of outputs, so 20 percent of customers might drive 80 percent of revenue, or 20 percent of bugs might cause 80 percent of crashes. The pattern is often cited as if it were a universal law of marketing, but the actual ratio in audited buyer-panel data is rarely 80/20. Sharp's audit across sixteen categories found the top 20 percent of buyers account for roughly 50 percent of revenue, not 80 percent. First introduced: Chapter 2.
  23. part-worth A part-worth (also called utility) is the relative value buyers place on one specific level of one specific attribute, like 30-ounce capacity versus 20-ounce, or steel construction versus plastic. The conjoint regression estimates a part-worth for every attribute level by looking at how each level affects the overall product rating across the respondent's choices. To convert these utility numbers into dollars of willingness-to-pay, you take the range of part-worths for a non-price attribute and divide it by the part-worth for one dollar of price, which translates the abstract utility scale into the dollar scale buyers actually understand. First introduced: Chapter 10.
  24. partitioned pricing Partitioned pricing is the practice of splitting a posted total into a low base price plus one or more mandatory add-on fees the buyer cannot avoid (a shipping fee, a resort fee, a ticketing service charge). Buyers tend to anchor on the base price and under-adjust for the add-ons, so a $189 hotel plus a $52 resort fee reads as cheaper at the booking moment than the same $241 charged as a single all-in price. The itemized surcharge also raises the buyer's attention to whatever attribute the surcharge is tagged to, which lifts demand when that attribute is valued and depresses it when the fee reads as padding. The FTC's 2024 Junk Fees Rule banned the most extractive version of partitioned pricing in lodging and ticketing. First introduced: Chapter 10.
  25. partworth A partworth is the utility (preference value) the average respondent assigns to one specific level of one specific attribute in a conjoint study. The number is estimated by a hierarchical-Bayes model that reads the pattern of choices the respondent made across the choice cards and works backwards to the per-level utilities that best explain that pattern. Partworths are reported on a relative scale, so the marketer compares them to each other inside the same study rather than to partworths from any other study. The move that turns conjoint into a willingness-to-pay tool is the division of every non-price partworth by the price partworth, which converts the relative utility units into dollar terms. First introduced: Chapter 5.
  26. Pass@1 Pass@1 is a benchmark metric that reports the share of tasks an AI agent gets correct on its very first attempt, with no retries and no human correction along the way. A Pass@1 of 68 percent means the agent produces the right answer immediately on 68 out of every 100 tasks, and the remaining 32 tasks need either a retry or a human review before the team can trust the output. That remaining 32 percent is the volume of work where the human-in-the-loop step pays back its cost, because every task the agent gets wrong on the first try is a task that would have shipped a bad number if the senior analyst had not been there to catch it. Pass@1 is therefore the load-bearing number for sizing how much senior-review time an augmented-analyst stack actually needs: a higher Pass@1 means less review per unit of output, a lower Pass@1 means more. First introduced: Chapter 24.
  27. pass^k Pass^k (read aloud as pass-to-the-k) is the probability that an agent succeeds on all k consecutive attempts at the same task, which measures reliability rather than peak capability. The reason a capstone team should track pass^k instead of pass@1 is mechanical: an agent that succeeds nine times out of ten on a single attempt sounds shippable, but its pass^8 score (the chance of eight successes in a row) collapses to roughly forty-three percent. Sierra Research's June 2024 tau-bench study reported that the top function-calling models cleared pass^8 below 25 percent on retail customer-service tasks even when their single-attempt scores crossed 86 percent. First introduced: Chapter 27.
  28. passage-level retrieval Passage-level retrieval is the practice by which the engine extracts a discrete content fragment from a page rather than retrieving the whole page. AirOps's 2026 measurement found that the optimal passage length for extraction is 134 to 167 words. The implication for the writer is that each subsection of a cornerstone page should be designed as a self-contained answer to one sub-query, sitting at roughly that word length, so the engine can lift it intact. First introduced: Chapter 18.
  29. Patagonia Patagonia is a premium outdoor apparel maker founded in 1973 by climber Yvon Chouinard, and it transferred ownership to the Patagonia Purpose Trust in 2022 so that all profits would go toward fighting the environmental crisis. The brand also runs the longest-living content operation in the running roster, which is built around three reinforcing assets. The first is the company journal called The Cleanest Line, which has published continuously since 2007 and now anchors the brand's editorial archive. The second is the Worn Wear repair program, where every customer repair encounter becomes branded story material that links back to that journal. The third is a documentary slate that includes feature films such as Public Trust, released in 2020, so the brand's voice extends from short essays through long-form video on the same set of values. First introduced: Chapter 17.
  30. Patagonia Purpose Trust The Patagonia Purpose Trust is a special-purpose legal trust the Chouinard family created in 2022 to hold all of Patagonia's voting stock. The trust's stated purpose, written into its governing documents, is to ensure the company continues to operate according to Patagonia's environmental and social mission. Because the trust holds the voting stock, the trust controls every governance decision a shareholder vote can decide, including the choice of board members, mergers, and changes to the company's stated values. The trust cannot be dissolved or repurposed without violating its trust deed, which is what makes the structure durable across changes of management. First introduced: Chapter 26.
  31. path A path is the chain of folder names that locates a file on the computer, written from the root down to the file itself. The path ~/Documents/research/survey.csv means the file survey.csv inside the research folder inside the Documents folder inside the marketer's home folder, where the tilde at the front stands for the home folder shortcut. The same idea shows up in everyday life every time a marketer copies an Instagram post link. Everything after the domain (instagram.com/p/abc123/) is a path the platform uses to locate the specific post out of the billions of posts in its system, and the forward slashes between segments carry the same meaning they carry on the laptop. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  32. path-dependent virality Path-dependent virality means that when social influence is operating, the eventual size of a cascade depends on the sequence of early choices rather than on the intrinsic quality of the content. The cleanest evidence is the 2006 MusicLab experiment: Salganik, Dodds, and Watts loaded 48 unknown songs into eight identical online worlds and watched the same songs produce eight different winners, because early raters in each world happened to pile in on different songs. The brand-side reading is that the team's job is the portfolio of seeded clips, not the forecast of which clip wins. First introduced: Chapter 19.
  33. payback period The payback period is the number of months between the moment a customer is acquired and the moment her cumulative contribution margin equals the cost the brand paid to acquire her. Picture a brand that spends $45 to acquire a customer who contributes $11 per order and orders once every three months. The brand reaches payback at month 12, because four orders at $11 each cover the original $45 spend. Brands with shorter payback periods recycle their acquisition capital faster and grow more quickly at the same cash position. Brands with longer payback periods need either cheaper capital or higher gross margins to survive a downturn, because their cash is locked up longer between spending it and recovering it. First introduced: Chapter 13.
  34. paywall The paywall is the screen that asks a free user to enter a credit card and become a paying subscriber. Where the brand puts this screen in the user journey matters enormously, because placing it too early in the experience kills retention by asking for payment before the user has felt any value, and placing it too late leaves money on the table by letting paying customers slip past the moment they would have bought. The highest-converting moment turns out to be the one where the user has just hit a free-tier limit, such as Duolingo's hearts running out mid-lesson, because the friction the paywall removes is already actively in the user's way. Inside any subscription mobile app this is the single most A/B-tested screen in the product. First introduced: Chapter 14.
  35. PDFs PDF stands for Portable Document Format, the file type Adobe shipped in 1993 that keeps a document looking the same whether you open it on a Mac, a Windows laptop, or a phone. Word documents (.docx) and Google Docs both export to PDF in one click. Claude reads the text inside a PDF directly when you upload it to a Project, including footnotes, headings, and table cells. First introduced: Chapter 5.
  36. penetration Penetration is the share of households that bought the category (or the brand) at least once during the year. The metric counts how many distinct buyers a brand or a category has, not how often each one buys. If a brand has 30 percent penetration of the snack category in a given year, that means 30 of every 100 category-buying households bought that brand at least once during the year, regardless of whether they bought it once or twenty times. The Sharp empirical record names penetration as the primary growth lever for brands. First introduced: Chapter 7.
  37. penetration pricing Penetration pricing wins in the high-q/p regime because the imitation cascade does most of the work of building share, so the firm's job at launch is to put as many visible early adopters into the population as possible. The lost margin per drink is recovered by the cascade's volume and by the share-of-shelf advantage that prevents competitive entry. Sultan-Farley-Lehmann 1990 meta-analysis: median q/p across 213 applications was 12.7, which means most categories sit firmly in the penetration regime. First introduced: Chapter 10.
  38. people People is the marketing-mix variable that recognizes that in a service the front-line employees are themselves part of the product the buyer pays for. The flight attendant is the airline as far as the passenger in seat 12B is concerned, and the barista is the coffee shop as far as the buyer at the counter is concerned. Hiring, training, scheduling, empowering, and motivating those employees is therefore a marketing decision rather than a back-office human-resources decision. First introduced: Chapter 9.
  39. Perceived quality Perceived quality is the buyer's overall judgment of the brand's quality relative to alternatives in the category, and it is what justifies a price premium. The construct is distinct from technical quality (the engineering specification the firm publishes) because a brand can have technically superior performance and lose to a competitor with stronger perceived quality if the buyer cannot tell the difference at point of choice. First introduced: Chapter 7.
  40. percentage points The arithmetic gap between two percentages, not the relative gap. A move from 42 percent to 47 percent is a 5 percentage-point move, even though it is only an 11.9 percent relative increase. Statisticians say ‘percentage points’ (or ‘pp’) precisely to head off that confusion. First introduced: Chapter 5.
  41. Performance ambiguity Performance ambiguity is the degree to which a channel partner’s effort and the outcomes that effort produces can be cleanly measured and tied back to the partner’s actions. It is high in long-cycle industrial sales where a single deal takes months and the salesperson’s effort cannot be cleanly separated from the buyer’s decision, and it is low in commodity transactional retail where every order is small, fast, and tied to a register receipt. First introduced: Chapter 11.
  42. performance cycle A performance cycle is one complete logistics activity measured end to end: a customer order placed, picked, shipped, and delivered; or a procurement order placed, fulfilled, and received into the warehouse. The cycle has three measurable properties that the marketing team has to know before promising anything to the buyer: the average lead time (how long the cycle takes on average), the variance around that lead time (how predictable the cycle is from one instance to the next), and the cost of running it. Bowersox and Closs treat the cycle as the basic unit of logistics analysis, the same way an accountant treats a transaction as the basic unit of accounting. First introduced: Chapter 11.
  43. performance measure The performance measure is the canonical-agent-framework name for the number the agent is trying to maximize. A weekly competitor brief's performance measure might be 'the fraction of executive-flagged competitor moves the brief reported before the all-hands meeting', or 'the click-through rate of the email subject lines the brief recommended.' If the team cannot name a specific number, the agent has no target and the loop has no way to converge. The Russell-and-Norvig textbook treats this requirement as load-bearing: an agent without a performance measure is just a chat session in disguise. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  44. peripheral route The peripheral route is what the buyer's mind does when the stakes are low or attention is scarce. The buyer forms an opinion from cues that have nothing to do with the argument itself: how warm the background music feels, how attractive the spokesperson is, how many times they have seen the brand before. Most low-stakes purchases (yogurt, gum, a tube of toothpaste) run on this route by default. First introduced: Chapter 4.
  45. perplexity Perplexity is a standard machine-learning measure of how surprising a sequence of words looks to a model when it tries to predict the next word. Lower perplexity means the model finds the next word easy to guess, which means it has the context it needs, while higher perplexity means the model is surprised, which means it is missing context. Toolformer keeps a candidate tool call only when seeing the tool's returned value drops the model's perplexity on the rest of the sentence below a threshold, which is the principled way to ask whether the tool result actually helped. First introduced: Chapter 27.
  46. perplexity-reduction filter The training-data filter Toolformer introduced. Each candidate tool call generated by the self-supervised pipeline is kept only if including both the call and the tool's returned result lowers the base model's next-token perplexity by at least a threshold versus the unaugmented baseline. The conceptual contribution that survives the scale-up to frontier-model training, and the reason a 2026 capstone team's eval harness must score not only the final answer but the precision of every intermediate tool call. First introduced: Chapter 27.
  47. persona vector A persona vector is a direction inside the model's activation space (the high-dimensional numerical state the model maintains as it reads and writes text) that corresponds to a recognizable character trait, such as sycophancy or hallucination. The August 2025 Anthropic paper built an automated pipeline that takes the name of a trait plus a one-paragraph description and returns the vector for that trait. Adding the vector to the model's activations during a call pushes the model toward the trait; subtracting it pushes the model away; projecting the activations onto the vector during a conversation reports in real time how strongly the trait is currently active, which is what makes the vector usable as a runtime monitor rather than just a research curiosity. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  48. persona vectors A persona vector is a direction in the language model's internal activation space that, when added to the model's processing of a prompt, shifts the model's output toward a particular persona. The Anthropic research team (Chen and colleagues, arXiv 2507.21509, July 2025) showed that aligned chat models have not lost the capacity to behave like diverse personas; rather, they default to a particular region of activation space, and that diversity is recoverable through prompt structures that pull the activation toward other regions. The Lyman 2025 recovery move is consistent with this mechanism: explicit-simulation framing plus in-context exemplars from the target cell shifts the model's activation toward the demographic region the assistant default would not have selected on its own. First introduced: Chapter 5.
  49. personalize-then-discretize Personalize-then-discretize is the technical name for the order Zhang and Misra's two-step procedure runs in, and the order is what makes the procedure work. The first step estimates per-customer treatment effects using causal machine learning, which produces a unique response profile for every individual customer in the dataset. The second step clusters those response profiles with optimal transport to jointly pick the optimal number of segments K and the treatment each segment should receive. The order matters because doing it the other way (clustering customers by their demographic or behavioral features first, then assigning each cluster a treatment) commits the segmentation to a feature taxonomy that may have no relationship to how customers actually respond to different treatments, which is the whole question the segmentation is supposed to answer. First introduced: Chapter 6.
  50. personalized price discrimination Personalized price discrimination is the practice of offering each buyer a different price tied to that buyer's individual demographic, behavioural, or contextual characteristics, where those characteristics are derived from observable data (browsing history, prior purchases, location, device type, time of day). The mechanism is technically a finely-grained form of third-degree price discrimination, but the granularity is what triggers the distinct fairness reaction documented at the Duani-Barasch-Morwitz studies elsewhere in this section. First introduced: Chapter 10.
  51. PESO PESO is the acronym for Paid, Earned, Shared, and Owned, the four-channel model Gini Dietrich introduced in her 2014 book Spin Sucks to add a Shared (social) tier to the older paid-earned-owned trio. The framework is the common source of channel-mix vocabulary in the public-relations and industry trade press, and it sits alongside the buy-beg-publish formulation as the practitioner shorthand teams use to decide where each campaign dollar should go. First introduced: Chapter 18.
  52. PESO model The PESO model is a four-part taxonomy that sorts every communications channel into one of four buckets, depending on who controls the message and who pays for the placement. Paid media is advertising the brand buys outright, where the brand decides what the message says. Earned media is press coverage the brand did not pay for, where the journalist decides what the story says. Shared media is content on social platforms where the audience itself amplifies or kills the message through likes, comments, and reposts. Owned media is the brand's own properties, such as its website, blog, and email list, where the brand fully controls both the message and the distribution. Gini Dietrich formalized the taxonomy in her 2014 book Spin Sucks, and it is now the standard frame for budgeting communications spend across channels. First introduced: Chapter 22.
  53. PESTEL PESTEL is a six-factor scan of the macroenvironmental forces a brand has to read on a regular cadence. The six axes are Political, Economic, Sociocultural, Technological, Environmental, and Legal. The framework originated as PEST in Francis Aguilar's 1967 book Scanning the Business Environment, with the Environmental and Legal axes added in later decades. First introduced: Chapter 3.
  54. phase III segmentation Phase III segmentation is the third historical phase of US mass marketing, the post-1923 period in which firms deliberately broke the unified national mass market back apart into targeted slices and offered different products to each slice. The 1923 Chevrolet-Pontiac-Oldsmobile-Buick-Cadillac ladder is the auto version, with General Motors offering five distinct cars at five price points against Ford's single Model T. The 1939 Pepsi 'twice as much for a nickel' attack on Coca-Cola is the soft-drink version, in which Pepsi explicitly targeted the price-conscious slice of cola buyers that Coke had not bothered to defend with a tailored offer. Both cases established the modern segmented-marketing pattern that dominates US categories today. First introduced: Chapter 6.
  55. Phiture's Mobile Growth Stack The Phiture Mobile Growth Stack is a vendor-neutral framework that names the layers a mobile growth program is built from, in the way an organizational chart names the departments of a company. The layers it documents are App Store Optimization (the listing work), paid acquisition (buying installs), deep-linking (sending users to the right screen after they tap a link), push notifications, in-app messaging, and monetization. Most mobile growth teams use this taxonomy as the shared vocabulary for organizing who owns which surface. First introduced: Chapter 14.
  56. Physical availability Physical availability is how easy a brand is for the buyer to find and purchase once she has decided to look for it. The asset spans the physical shelf in a retailer, the menu at a restaurant, the search-result page on a website, the home screen of a mobile app, the vending machine in a building lobby, and any other channel where the buyer's decision to buy can be converted into an actual purchase. Mental availability without physical availability strands the brand because the buyer cannot act on a memory she has no way to fulfill. First introduced: Chapter 7.
  57. physical evidence Physical evidence is the marketing-mix variable that recognizes that an intangible service is judged by the tangible cues surrounding it. The marble lobby of a private bank says the bank is solid before the buyer hears a single number. The Doubletree warm-cookie at check-in says the hotel takes hospitality seriously before the buyer reaches the room. The brown UPS truck signals reliability before the package arrives. Physical evidence is how the firm tangibilizes the intangible promise the service makes. First introduced: Chapter 9.
  58. pillar page A pillar page is one long-form article, typically running between three thousand and ten thousand words, that covers a broad topic from end to end and acts as the central reference page in what content teams call a topic cluster. Many shorter cluster pages each go deeper on one sub-topic of the pillar and link back to it, which signals to a search engine that the website has something coherent to say about the larger subject. HubSpot coined the pillar-and-cluster framing in its current form around 2017, though the underlying link-structure-as-authority-signal idea predates that label. First introduced: Chapter 17.
  59. pilot Phil Barden's brand-briefing rename for Kahneman's System 2: the slow, effortful, deliberative mode of thought the reader of a textbook is using right now. The pilot is metabolically expensive to engage; most shoppers will not engage it for a four-dollar bottle of shampoo or a six-second pre-roll on YouTube. The pilot is the mode the brand team is in when they write the brief; the autopilot is the mode the buyer is in when they encounter the resulting ad. First introduced: Chapter 16.
  60. Pilot Parliaments Benchmark The Pilot Parliaments Benchmark is the 1,270-image facial-analysis dataset that Joy Buolamwini and Timnit Gebru built for their 2018 Gender Shades audit (published in PMLR 81 of the inaugural FAccT conference). The dataset is balanced across the Fitzpatrick six-tone skin scale and curated from parliamentary photo records of three African nations (Rwanda, Senegal, South Africa) and three European nations (Iceland, Finland, Sweden), with dermatologist-coded skin-tone labels. The benchmark became the methodological template for every subsequent intersectional vision-AI audit, because it solved the prior problem that commercial training sets underrepresented darker-skinned and female subjects, so the audit could finally measure performance on the population the system was supposed to serve. First introduced: Chapter 26.
  61. Pisano's innovation strategy Gary Pisano's innovation-strategy framework, from his Harvard Business Review 2015 article and his book Creative Construction (2019), maps every product project onto a four-quadrant landscape by asking two yes-or-no questions: does the project use a new technology, and does it require a new business model. The four quadrants are routine, disruptive, radical, and architectural. Pisano then asks three strategy questions for each project (how does it create value for the buyer, how does the firm capture some of that value, and how does the project mix with the rest of the portfolio). The point is to match each project to the right quadrant before committing capacity rather than treating innovation as a single category. First introduced: Chapter 8.
  62. place utility Place utility is the value a channel creates by making the product available where the buyer actually is, rather than where the factory happens to be. A coffee bean roasted in Buffalo has no value to a buyer in Tulsa until the channel closes the geographic gap and gets the cup onto a shelf the Tulsa buyer can walk to. First introduced: Chapter 11.
  63. Plaintext A plaintext file contains only readable letters, numbers, and punctuation that any text editor can show exactly as they are stored. CSV, JSON, and Markdown files are all plaintext, which is why the marketer can open a survey.csv in Notepad and see the actual rows and columns the way they were saved. Plaintext is the right format any time the marketer wants the file to remain readable without proprietary software, and it is also the format AI models read most cleanly. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  64. Planning Planning is the step where the model decomposes a higher-level goal into a sequence of tool calls before executing any of them, so it knows which tool to reach for first and which to reach for second. For a short task the planning happens inline inside one model call without a separate planner artifact, but for a long task the planning is given its own model call (the orchestrator) which writes the plan and dispatches each step to a specialist worker. First introduced: Chapter 27.
  65. planning component The planning component is the first of Lilian Weng's four primitives in her LLM Powered Autonomous Agents post, and it covers two related moves: task decomposition (where the agent breaks a high-level goal into a sequence of sub-tasks using patterns like Chain of Thought, Tree of Thoughts, or LLM+P) and self-reflection (where the agent grades its own work using patterns like ReAct, Reflexion, or Chain of Hindsight). Weng's later May 2025 post Why We Think extended the planning primitive into the test-time-compute era, framing the planner as the layer that decides how much reasoning budget to spend on each sub-task rather than spending a fixed amount on every step. First introduced: Chapter 27.
  66. planogram A planogram is the shelf-layout diagram a retailer uses to specify exactly which products sit where on each shelf, including how many facings each product gets and which products sit next to which neighbors. Suppose a Target category buyer decides to give a Stanley Quencher three facings at eye level on aisle G7 with the lid display directly to its right. That single instruction is captured on the chain's planogram and then rolled out to every store in the chain, so a shopper in Tulsa sees the same Quencher layout that a shopper in San Diego sees, and the brand gets a consistent visual presentation across the country. First introduced: Chapter 8.
  67. Platform Envelopment Threshold The Platform Envelopment Threshold is a three-dimension composite-score the procurement team uses at every standalone-vendor renewal to decide whether the smaller specialist vendor is still worth paying for, or whether the team should let its existing platform vendor (Microsoft, Salesforce, or HubSpot) absorb the work the specialist used to do. The three dimensions are the vendor's data position (does it own a corpus the platform cannot easily replicate), its workflow lock-in (how deeply the reps' daily habits run through the product), and its customer mindshare (whether the reps would push back if the firm tried to cancel). A composite score at or above 0.50 means the vendor is defensible at renewal, and a score below 0.50 means the vendor is at the threshold where a same-license bundled alternative will likely win. The Battle Intelligence Report 02, published in May 2026, scored Gong at 0.55 because Microsoft cannot easily reconstitute Gong's proprietary call-recording corpus. The same report scored Salesloft and Outreach at 0.39 each, Clari at 0.45, Apollo at 0.33, and Highspot at 0.40, which means all five sit at or below the 0.50 defensibility line. The base-case forecast is that the six-vendor cohort consolidates to three by the second quarter of 2027, with the combined revenue of the category compressing from roughly three billion dollars to two billion over a twelve-to-fifteen-month window. First introduced: Chapter 23.
  68. platform fragmentation Platform fragmentation is the 2024 to 2026 redistribution of audiences away from the old single-platform plan (where a brand picked Instagram or TikTok and called it done) toward four to seven concurrent platforms per audience cohort, where the same college student is now on Instagram, TikTok, BeReal, Threads, Discord, and Letterboxd inside the same week. The BOND Capital 2025 report plus Pulsar's 2026 tracking document Bluesky growing 207 percent year-over-year, Threads growing 100 percent year-over-year to roughly 300 million monthly users, Substack growing 65.6 percent, and Letterboxd 33.3 percent, while X daily users fell about 23 percent and TikTok views, reach, and interactions all dropped, so a brand betting on one platform now bets against the direction the audience is actually moving. First introduced: Chapter 19.
  69. play.google.com/console Google Play Console is Google's web-based dashboard for everything Android, in the same way that App Store Connect is Apple's site for everything iOS. From this one site the developer submits new Android builds, edits the Play Store metadata, distributes test builds across the internal, closed, and open testing tracks, reads the sales reports, and watches the Android Vitals dashboard for crashes and performance regressions. The URL is play.google.com/console, and access requires a one-time $25 developer registration fee with no annual subscription, which makes the Android side cheaper to enter than Apple's $99 per year. First introduced: Chapter 14.
  70. PLC The product life cycle (PLC) is the bell-shaped curve a product's sales trace over its commercial life, divided into four stages. The first stage is introduction, where sales start slowly while early adopters try the product. The second is growth, where sales rise rapidly as the mainstream enters the category. The third is maturity, where sales flatten because the category has saturated and most willing buyers already own one. The fourth is decline, where sales fall as substitutes take over the use case the product used to serve. The curve is useful as a diagnostic for which stage a given product currently sits in and what strategy that stage calls for, but it is not reliable as a forecast schedule because the duration and shape of each stage vary widely across categories. Treating the PLC as a deterministic calendar harvests products that could still be growing. First introduced: Chapter 8.
  71. pocket price Pocket price is the dollar amount the firm actually keeps on a transaction after every on-invoice and off-invoice deduction has been subtracted. The walk is: take the list price, subtract the volume discount, subtract any negotiated exception, subtract the cooperative-advertising allowance, subtract the freight allowance, subtract the early-payment-terms discount, and what remains is the pocket price. This is the number that actually drives firm profit, not the list price published on the catalog page. First introduced: Chapter 10.
  72. pocket price waterfall The pocket price waterfall, which Michael Marn and Robert Rosiello introduced in a 1992 Harvard Business Review article, is an accounting walk from the published list price down through every deduction (volume discount, negotiated exception, co-op advertising allowance, freight allowance, early-payment terms) to the dollars the firm actually keeps from the transaction. The classic Castle Battery example in their paper walked a $28.40 list price down to a $21.60 pocket price, a 24 percent erosion no single deduction caused on its own. First introduced: Chapter 10.
  73. point-process models A point-process model is a statistical model that treats each customer touchpoint (a website visit, an ad impression, a store walk-in) as an event drawn from a probability that varies with time and with the recent history of prior events. If a customer just visited the website, the probability of them visiting again in the next hour is higher than it would be a week later, and the point-process model captures that pattern explicitly. Before transformer-based sequence models took over around 2023, point-process models were the standard tool in marketing-science clickstream analysis and ad-attribution work, because they were the cleanest way to model irregularly-spaced customer events without imposing the regular time grid that traditional time-series methods require. First introduced: Chapter 6.
  74. points of difference Points of difference (PODs) are the attributes or benefits that buyers strongly associate with one brand, positively evaluate, and believe other brands in the same category cannot match at the same level. They are the reason a buyer would choose this brand over the alternatives. Apple's design and ease of use are PODs against most PC competitors; Nike's performance technology is a POD against generic athletic apparel; Southwest's low-cost-with-personality combination is a POD against the legacy carriers. The strongest PODs are ones the firm can prove with its product specs or operational behavior and that competitors cannot replicate on a normal product-development calendar. First introduced: Chapter 6.
  75. points of parity Points of parity (POPs) are the attributes or benefits a brand must hold to be considered a legitimate option in its category at all. They are typically not unique to the brand, because every credible competitor offers them too, but their absence rules the brand out of consideration. A coffee shop must serve coffee, a travel agency must book flights and hotels, a streaming service must have a reasonable selection of movies and shows; these are POPs that establish category membership. POPs do not win the buyer's choice between brands, but their absence loses the buyer before the choice between brands even begins. First introduced: Chapter 6.
  76. polycentric Polycentric is the orientation in which a firm treats each foreign market as fundamentally different and runs each one through locally-hired managers with substantial autonomy. Headquarters provides capital and brand standards, but the country team writes its own operating playbook. Classic Nestlé and Unilever are the canonical polycentric multinationals; the trade-off is strong local fit at the cost of scale economies. First introduced: Chapter 15.
  77. pop-up A pop-up store is a short-run physical retail format with a fixed end date, used to test demand, location, and assortment without committing to a long lease. Roughly eighty percent of retailers that opened a pop-up in 2024 and 2025 considered the run a success, and fifty-eight percent planned a repeat the following year, which is why the format has settled into the operating playbooks of brands like Casper, Glossier, and Liquid Death alongside the 700-square-foot, three-day campus admit-weekend booth that anchors the worked example in this chapter. First introduced: Chapter 12.
  78. popular 80/20 rule The Pareto principle, named after the Italian economist Vilfredo Pareto (1906), is the rule of thumb that says roughly 80 percent of outcomes come from roughly 20 percent of causes. Applied to a sales ledger, it gets repeated as '80 percent of revenue comes from 20 percent of customers,' but the empirical record on brand-level CPG data shows the actual ratio is much closer to 50/20. First introduced: Chapter 7.
  79. Population Stability Index The Population Stability Index, written PSI, is a single-number drift metric that the credit-risk industry has used for decades to decide whether a scoring model still applies to its current population. The math is straightforward: PSI takes the share of observations that fell into each bucket during a reference window, takes the share that fell into each bucket during the current window, and rolls the differences into one number that grows as the buckets diverge. The industry rules of thumb that marketers inherit are simple. A PSI below 0.10 means the two populations look the same and the model is fine. A PSI between 0.10 and 0.25 means moderate drift, the kind that is worth a closer look before the next scheduled refit. A PSI above 0.25 means the populations have moved enough that any model trained on the reference window should be re-checked or rebuilt against the new data. First introduced: Chapter 24.
  80. populist worlds Populist worlds are subcultural milieus the broader society treats as more authentic than mainstream consumer life, named and analyzed by Douglas Holt and Douglas Cameron in their 2010 book on cultural strategy. Heavy-metal music, punk rock, frontier-Tennessee heritage, urban African-American sport culture, and working-ranch labor are all example populist worlds a brand can credibly speak from. Holt's authenticity test requires that the brand's resident standing in the populist world predate the advertising commitment, because agency-invented populist worlds such as Pepsi's Generation Next campaign in the 1980s or Burger King's outlaw-king positioning in the mid-2000s fail when insiders read the artifice within months. First introduced: Chapter 7.
  81. Porter's Five Forces Porter's Five Forces is an industry-attractiveness analysis Michael Porter introduced in a 1979 Harvard Business Review article. The framework asks what five structural forces compress margin in a category, namely the intensity of rivalry among existing firms, the threat of new entrants, the threat of substitute products, the bargaining power of suppliers, and the bargaining power of buyers. First introduced: Chapter 3.
  82. portfolio A product portfolio is the full set of products a firm offers across all its lines, items, and variants. Procter and Gamble's portfolio includes Tide laundry detergent, Crest toothpaste, Pampers diapers, and dozens of other brands and their variants. The portfolio is described along four dimensions: width (how many distinct product lines the firm operates), length (how many items live inside each line), depth (how many variants exist for each item, such as scents, sizes, and formulations), and consistency (how related the different lines are to each other in technology, manufacturing, or end use). First introduced: Chapter 8.
  83. POS The Point of Sale system, almost always shortened to POS, is the transaction software running at the cash register or self-checkout lane that records each item sold, the moment it sold, and the exact price it sold at, with the basket attributed to whichever loyalty account the shopper used at checkout. POS data is the cleanest record a retailer has of what actually moved off the shelf, which is why every other in-store analytics system (RFID accuracy checks, planogram audits, retail-media attribution) joins back to the POS feed as the source of truth. First introduced: Chapter 12.
  84. positioning statement A positioning statement is a one-sentence internal articulation of four things at once: who the brand is for, what category the brand competes in, what the brand offers that competitors cannot match, and why the buyer should believe the claim. The positioning statement is an internal working document used by the brand team and the agency, not a consumer-facing tagline; it almost never appears in an ad verbatim, but every ad the brand runs should be traceable back to one of its clauses. The standard short template runs ‘For [target customer], [brand] offers [value proposition],’ while the fuller Palmatier variant forces an explicit competitive comparison: ‘For [target segment], the [product] provides [benefit], which [compelling reasons for buying versus competition].’ First introduced: Chapter 6.
  85. positive deviance Positive deviance is a behavior-change methodology that Jerry and Monique Sternin pioneered in Vietnam in 1990 while working on childhood malnutrition for Save the Children. The method starts by identifying the small number of community members who are succeeding against the odds with no extra resources, studies what those people are actually doing differently from their neighbors, and then designs a community-led intervention that lets the deviant practice propagate peer-to-peer rather than through outside instruction. The Sternins cut childhood malnutrition in their target villages by 85 percent over two years, and the same six-step structure (define, determine, discover, design, demonstrate, disseminate) is what Patagonia's Worn Wear and Duolingo's streak loop both run at brand scale. First introduced: Chapter 16.
  86. positive/playful voice The positive/playful voice is light and upbeat, with the smile audible in the tone, and it is used as the default register in roughly 80 percent of sales conversations. The signal it sends to the buyer is approachability, which lowers the buyer's defensive posture and makes the buyer more willing to share information than she would be with a flat or aggressive tone. First introduced: Chapter 23.
  87. possession utility Possession utility is the value a channel creates by transferring legal ownership of the product to the buyer and enabling payment to change hands. A cup of coffee behind a locked counter that the buyer cannot purchase has no possession utility, even if the channel has already produced every other utility, because no value actually transfers until the transaction is complete and the buyer walks out as the owner. First introduced: Chapter 11.
  88. Post-training accounted for a fifty-one percent lift A 2025 paper in Science by Hackenburg, Tappin, and colleagues decomposed the persuasiveness of conversational AI into separate mechanisms to figure out which levers actually do the work. The finding was that post-training (the fine-tuning step that shapes how the model talks) accounted for a 51 percent lift in persuasiveness, and prompting (the system instructions the marketer writes) accounted for another 27 percent lift. Personalization and raw model scale mattered less than the popular literature had assumed. The practical implication for the marketer is that the levers she can actually influence (better prompts, better training data) sit upstream of any individual conversation, which is where the alignment-team budget should go. First introduced: Chapter 23.
  89. postponement Postponement is a supply-chain design principle that delays the commitment of a product to a specific configuration, customer, or location until demand for that specific variant is observed. Christopher names four levels. Form postponement delays packaging, labeling, or final assembly (HP’s plain-printers-then-localize). Time postponement delays shipment until demand is observed. Place postponement delays forward deployment to a specific market. Price postponement delays the price decision. Each level trades more upstream generic inventory for less risk of holding the wrong specific variant when demand finally appears. First introduced: Chapter 11.
  90. postponement of differentiation Deferred differentiation is a manufacturing strategy of holding the product in a generic, undifferentiated state as long as possible in the production process and applying the buyer-specific finishing only at the latest possible step. Hewlett-Packard for decades shipped generic printers to regional distribution centers and added the country-specific power cord and language manual at the final stage, which meant the firm carried one inventory pool of generic units rather than dozens of country-specific stocks. The strategy lowers the cost of supporting variety because the upstream inventory is one generic stock rather than many finished variants, and the firm carries less of each variant's specific risk. First introduced: Chapter 8.
