  91. POSTs An HTTP POST is the standard way a webpage hands data to a server over the internet. When you click submit on any form on any website, your browser packages up your inputs and POSTs them to a specific URL the form was pointing at. In this case, the inputs are the margin-of-error and confidence-level numbers the demo just collected, and the URL is the local proxy server running on your laptop, which forwards the request to Claude on your behalf. First introduced: Chapter 5.
  92. Postscript Postscript is the dominant SMS-marketing platform for Shopify direct-to-consumer brands. The merchant collects opt-ins through a popup on the site or through a consent checkbox at checkout, then sends both broadcast messages and triggered automated sequences (like the abandoned-cart message that fires one hour after a buyer leaves a cart). Segmentation runs against Shopify customer-behavior data the same way Klaviyo's segmentation does. Pricing is structured per message sent rather than per contact stored, which means the bill scales with how active the program is. The entry tier starts at $25 per month plus 1 cent for every US text message the brand sends. First introduced: Chapter 13.
  93. potential-outcomes framework The potential-outcomes framework defines the causal effect of a treatment as the contrast between the outcome a unit would have under treatment and the outcome the same unit would have under control. The framework was formalized by Donald Rubin in 1974 and laid out in textbook form by Guido Imbens and Donald Rubin in 2015. Only one of the two potential outcomes is ever observed for any given unit (a store either got the price increase or did not, never both), which is why estimating a causal effect requires either randomization that makes the two groups comparable in expectation, or one of the named substitutes (difference-in-differences, regression discontinuity, instrumental variables) that lets the analyst recover a comparison from observational data. First introduced: Chapter 5.
  94. power Statistical power is the probability that the test will actually detect a real effect when one is truly there in the underlying population, which is the same thing as one minus the Type II error rate (the rate at which the test fails to detect real effects). The conventional target value is 0.80, which means the analyst is committing to a design that will catch a real lift of the planned minimum-detectable-effect size (often abbreviated MDE, the smallest effect the team considers worth caring about) on roughly eighty out of every one hundred hypothetical reruns of the test. To raise the power, the analyst has to enroll more visitors into the test, because a larger sample makes real effects easier to see against the random noise of individual visitor behavior. Like the alpha threshold, the power target has to be set at the design stage before the test runs, because adjusting it after seeing the data would defeat the whole point of the pre-commitment. First introduced: Chapter 5.
  95. power wall The power wall is the right-hand wall about five to fifteen paces past the store entrance, and it is the single highest-value square footage in a brick-and-mortar store because of how the body behaves once it gets through the transition zone. By that point the shopper has stopped accelerating, the eyes have refocused, and the first conscious shopping decision of the trip happens within reach of that wall, which is why luxury retailers stage their signature merchandise there and a Target endcap on the power wall is one of the most expensive ad placements a CPG brand can buy. First introduced: Chapter 12.
  96. powerful AI Amodei's October 2024 threshold for what he calls 'powerful AI': a system smarter than a Nobel laureate across most fields, capable of multi-day autonomous task completion, runnable as millions of instances at 10-100x human speed. Amodei deliberately avoids the term AGI as too hype-laden. The definition pins what concrete capabilities Anthropic is building toward, in language a procurement officer can act on rather than philosophical language about machine consciousness. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  97. PowerGrid The BAV PowerGrid is the 2x2 diagnostic plot produced by the Brand Asset Valuator instrument. It places Brand Strength (Differentiation multiplied by Relevance, the forward-looking score) on the vertical axis and Brand Stature (Esteem multiplied by Knowledge, the backward-looking score) on the horizontal axis, which sorts brands into four diagnostic quadrants: Leadership when both axes are high, Niche or Momentum when Strength is high but Stature is still building, Eroding or Declining when Stature is still high but Strength is fading, and New or Unfocused when both axes are low. First introduced: Chapter 7.
  98. PRD A product requirements document (PRD) is the product manager's working specification for the product under development, which engineering, design, and finance all read in turn before any of them commits resources. The document includes the problem statement, the target user, the job-to-be-done the product is hired for, the success metrics the team will measure, the scope (what is in, what is explicitly out, what is deferred to a future release), wireframes or design references, the technical dependencies, and the regulatory or brand constraints the product has to operate within. Each downstream function then reads the same PRD through its own lens. Engineering reads it to estimate how many person-weeks of effort the build will take. Design reads it to mock the screens or industrial form. Finance reads it to set the launch budget and decide whether the projected return justifies the spend. First introduced: Chapter 8.
  99. pre-experimental pilot A pre-experimental pilot is a small, cheap rehearsal of an experiment that lets the team find and fix design flaws before the expensive human field study runs. Doing the pilot on a synthetic respondent (a language model prompted with the demographic backstory) costs cents and hours; doing the same pilot on a human panel costs thousands of dollars and days. The pilot's job is not to produce the final numbers; it is to identify experimental conditions that would not have worked, so the team can drop them before fielding the real study. First introduced: Chapter 5.
  100. pre-register To pre-register a study means to write down, in a public registry that timestamps the submission, the four load-bearing decisions of the study before any data has been collected: the hypothesis the study is testing, the metric that will count as the test of that hypothesis, the sample size the analyst commits to running, and the analysis plan the analyst will follow once the data arrives. The reason pre-registration matters is that it locks the analyst into a decision rule that was chosen when no data was yet visible, which removes the after-the-fact temptation to fish for a comparison that happened to clear 0.05 and write the paper around that. The three free registries most researchers use today are AsPredicted (the easiest and fastest, designed for behavioral research), the Open Science Framework run by the Center for Open Science (more comprehensive, accepts longer protocols), and ClinicalTrials.gov (the federal registry that has been the standard for medical trials since 2000). First introduced: Chapter 5.
  101. pre-suasion Pre-suasion is Robert Cialdini's 2016 argument that the buyer's state of mind in the seconds before a persuasive message reaches her is doing more work than the message's content itself. The firm's job extends to the priming environment around the message, not just to the words on the page. In 2026 the priming environment includes the AI-mediated discovery layer, meaning the chat agent's framing of the brand before the buyer ever sees the firm's own creative. A firm that has not designed for that framing is leaving the priming environment to whatever the model defaults to, which is rarely what the firm would have chosen. First introduced: Chapter 2.
  102. pre-test-market simulation Pre-test-market simulation is the technique of converting survey-stated trial intent (from a few hundred or a few thousand respondents) into a year-one sales forecast for a product that has not launched yet. The classic 1978 reference is ASSESSOR by Silk and Urban; the commercial workhorse is Burke’s BASES product. Published mean-absolute-percentage-error figures for pre-test-market simulation come in around 5 to 15 percent against actual year-one volume, versus around 50 percent or worse for an unassisted manager forecast on the same product. The method is the right tool for any pre-launch plan that needs a defensible volume number. First introduced: Chapter 24.
  103. predetermined elements Predetermined elements are forces whose direction is already locked in by structural conditions and is therefore not genuinely in question over the brand's planning horizon. Pierre Wack named the category in his 1985 Harvard Business Review article on scenario planning. The discipline is that predetermined elements should be forecasted as fixed inputs to every scenario, rather than treated as the axes of a 2x2, because a 2x2 built on predetermined forces produces four nearly identical strategies. First introduced: Chapter 3.
  104. predicted-incrementality Predicted incrementality (sometimes called PIE, for predicted incremental effect) is a model trained on the results of past randomized advertising tests that scores brand-new, non-randomized campaigns at close to the accuracy of running a fresh experiment. On a Meta benchmark of 2,226 experiments, PIE achieved an explanatory power (measured as R-squared, a zero-to-one statistic that says what fraction of the variation in true campaign impact the model can predict) of 0.88, against just 0.19 for last-click attribution, which means PIE captured 88 percent of the real campaign-impact variation while last-click captured only 19 percent. The method sits between multi-touch attribution and full randomized incrementality testing on the cost-versus-accuracy curve, which is why it has become the practical default for 2026 budget reallocation decisions. First introduced: Chapter 25.
  105. Predicted-incrementality calibration Predicted incrementality (PIE) is a model trained on the results of past randomized incrementality tests that scores new, non-randomized campaigns at near-experimental fidelity. The practical attraction is speed: a firm that has run 50 holdout experiments over two years can train a PIE model on those 50 outcomes, then use the model to predict the incremental lift of a new campaign without running another four-to-twelve-week holdout test. Out-of-sample R-squared values around 0.88 (versus 0.19 for last-click) have been measured on 2,000-plus Meta experiments, which is what makes PIE the production calibration layer for the rest of the attribution spectrum. First introduced: Chapter 24.
  106. present bias Present bias is the specific extra weight a buyer's brain puts on right-now relative to any future moment, on top of their normal preference for sooner over later. A present-biased buyer is patient between any two future months (next March versus next April) but impatient between this month and any future month (today versus next April), which is what makes them sign up for a gym they never visit, choose monthly subscriptions over cheaper annual ones, and accept four installments of twenty-five dollars on a hundred-dollar jacket they would have rejected at the register. First introduced: Chapter 4.
  107. present-value Present value (PV) is the worth today of a sum of money expected in the future, after applying a discount rate that adjusts for the time value of money and the riskiness of the future cash flow. A $100 expected payment one year from now, discounted at 8 percent, is worth roughly $92.59 today. First introduced: Chapter 7.
  108. price-customization mechanisms Price-customization mechanisms are the specific operational tools a firm uses to charge different prices to different segments, which Robert Dolan and Hermann Simon catalogued in chapters 6 through 9 of Power Pricing (1996). The five mechanisms are segmentation pricing (different prices to different identifiable groups, like student or senior tiers), nonlinear pricing (per-unit prices that vary with quantity, like volume discounts), bundling (selling a package at a discount versus the sum of components), product-line pricing (a staircase of versions at different price points), and time-based pricing (different prices at different times, like off-peak rates). Each mechanism captures a distinct slice of willingness-to-pay variation that a single posted price cannot reach. First introduced: Chapter 10.
  109. price discrimination Price discrimination is the practice of charging different buyers different prices for the same product, when those price differences are not tied to differences in cost-to-serve. The label is technical rather than pejorative; an undergraduate student rate at a museum, an early-bird concert ticket, and a corporate-volume discount are all forms of price discrimination, and each is generally accepted as fair because the buyer can understand why the firm offers it. First introduced: Chapter 10.
  110. Price elasticity of demand Price elasticity of demand is the standard economic measure of how strongly the quantity buyers purchase responds to a change in price. The formula is the percentage change in quantity divided by the percentage change in price. An elasticity of negative 2 means that a 1 percent price increase produces a 2 percent volume decline; an elasticity close to zero means buyers barely respond to price changes at all (inelastic demand), while an elasticity far below negative 1 means buyers respond aggressively (elastic demand). The cross-category average across the published meta-analyses sits around negative 2.62. First introduced: Chapter 10.
  111. price-formation participation Price-formation participation, which Timothy Richards, Jura Liaukonyte, and Nadia Streletskaya documented in a 2016 International Journal of Industrial Organization paper, is the practice of inviting buyers to participate in setting the price they will pay, through negotiation, opt-in data sharing in exchange for a verifiable discount, or similar mechanisms. The participation itself functions as a fairness signal because it shifts the buyer from being a target of the firm's pricing decision to being a co-author of it. Their experiments show that participation partially reverses the adverse fairness reaction that personalized pricing usually triggers. First introduced: Chapter 10.
  112. pricing cues A pricing cue is a context signal buyers use to read whether a price is fair when they cannot recall the exact price from memory, which empirical work shows is the case most of the time. Fewer than half of supermarket shoppers can correctly name the price of an item they just placed in their cart, so they rely on signals in the store environment instead. Anderson and Simester identified five such cues that retailers use to manage perceived value: prices ending in 9, sale signs, signpost items (Costco's $4.99 rotisserie chicken, Trader Joe's $1.99 wine), price-matching guarantees, and posted comparison prices. First introduced: Chapter 10.
  113. pricing orientation Pricing orientation, in Tim Smith's 2021 framing, is how the price actually gets decided inside the firm by the specific people in the specific room where the decision happens. Smith identifies four cardinal orientations that map to functional power inside the firm: cost-driven decisions are dominated by finance, customer-value-driven decisions by marketing, WTP-driven decisions by the pricing-science function when one exists, and competition-driven decisions by product management. Most pricing failures Smith documents are orientation failures rather than strategy failures: the strategy document on the shelf says one thing, but the people running the room produce the answer their functional orientation defaults to. First introduced: Chapter 10.
  114. Pricing Solutions Limited Pricing Solutions Limited is a Toronto-based pricing-strategy consultancy founded in 1995. The firm's Indianapolis Zoo dynamic-pricing engagement was led by Paul Hunt and described in Robert Talbott's mid-2010 case writeup. That engagement became the canonical worked example of yield management applied to a mission-driven institution like a zoo or a museum, rather than to the margin-driven airline and hotel businesses where yield management was originally developed. First introduced: Chapter 10.
  115. pricing strategy Pricing strategy, in Tim Smith's 2021 Getting Price Right framing, is the answer to the question of what price the firm should be charging in the abstract, derived from the value cascade, the multi-price architecture, and the revenue-optimization math. It is the correct answer the firm would arrive at if it were a unitary decision-maker who could see all the relevant data at once and was not constrained by internal organizational politics. First introduced: Chapter 10.
  116. Priming Priming is what happens when a brief earlier exposure to a concept makes that concept easier to retrieve from memory later, even when the buyer cannot remember the exposure. A buyer who scrolls past a Coca-Cola post in the morning will think of Coca-Cola at lunchtime more easily than a buyer who scrolled past a competitor, whether or not either of them can recall what they saw. First introduced: Chapter 4.
  117. Privacy Sandbox The Privacy Sandbox was Google's August 2019 initiative to replace third-party cookies in the Chrome browser with on-device, privacy-preserving APIs including Topics, Protected Audience, and Attribution Reporting. Most of those APIs were retired on 17 October 2025 after low industry adoption and a roughly 30 percent publisher-revenue decline against legacy cookies in the UK Competition and Markets Authority test data. Third-party cookies now remain in Chrome indefinitely, which is the opposite of what every digital-marketing plan written between 2020 and 2024 assumed. First introduced: Chapter 3.
  118. private-label Private-label means the product carries the retailer's own brand even though a third-party co-packer actually makes it. The retailer specs the recipe, the package, and the price, while the manufacturer fills the order anonymously and never appears on the label. The customer therefore cannot price-compare a Trader Joe's Cookie Butter against an Amazon listing because the product only exists at Trader Joe's, which means the scarcity sits in the portfolio rather than in a discount the firm has to fund. Roughly 80 percent of Trader Joe's shelf works this way, which is one of the structural reasons the chain can charge what it charges and skip advertising entirely. First introduced: Chapter 2.
  119. private-label Kirkland Signature A private-label product is one made for a retailer to sell under the retailer's own brand name rather than under the manufacturer's. Costco's Kirkland Signature line, Trader Joe's house brand, and Target's Up&Up are all examples. The retailer keeps more margin than it would on a reseller-equivalent national brand because the retailer captures both the manufacturer markup and the retail markup on the same product. Private label also gives the retailer pricing flexibility because no other store carries the identical item to price-compare against. First introduced: Chapter 2.
  120. PRIZM Premier Claritas PRIZM Premier is a US neighborhood-classification system that bundles every census block group in the country into one of 68 named lifestyle clusters such as 'Upper Crust' or 'Money and Brains' or 'Big City Blues.' Each cluster comes with a demographic, attitudinal, and purchase profile so a direct-mail or territory planner can match an offer to a neighborhood without surveying the residents. First introduced: Chapter 6.
  121. probes-catch-sleeper-agents Probes-catch-sleeper-agents is the name of Anthropic's April 2024 follow-up paper to the original Hubinger sleeper-agents result, demonstrating that the failure mode is detectable from inside the model even when the safety-trained output looks clean. The technique trains a simple linear classifier on the model's residual-stream activations during contrast prompts (a truthful prompt paired with a deceptive one) and uses that classifier to flag activations that look like the model is about to defect on its trigger. The published detection rate is above 99 percent area-under-the-ROC-curve. Two caveats define the result: detection is white-box only (the brand needs access to the model's internal activations, which only the vendor can give), and the technique establishes that sleeper-agent-class behavior is not undetectable in principle, only undetectable from outputs alone. First introduced: Chapter 26.
  122. process Process is the marketing-mix variable that maps the sequence of actions the buyer and the provider take together to produce the service. Picture the steps of a coffee-shop visit from the door to the table, with each step labeled by who does what and where the buyer is waiting. The standard tool for documenting and improving the process is the service blueprint, which the next section walks at length. First introduced: Chapter 9.
  123. product architecture Product architecture is the scheme by which the functional elements of a product are allocated to its physical components. A modular architecture maps one function to one component, which lets the firm vary components independently: in a Stanley tumbler the cup body and the lid can be redesigned separately without affecting the straw or the handle, and a new colorway is just a new body part rather than a full product redesign. An integral architecture couples multiple functions across multiple components, which can improve peak performance (a Formula One car's chassis serves structural, aerodynamic, and cooling functions at once) but locks the firm out of the variety options modular designs enable. The choice between the two has to be made early in development because it cascades through tooling, supplier contracts, and assembly-line design, all of which are expensive to reverse downstream. First introduced: Chapter 8.
  124. Product assortment Product assortment is the set of goods a retailer chooses to carry, and the choice has two independent dimensions that have to be made together. Breadth is the number of distinct product lines the retailer covers, such as apparel, home goods, beauty, and accessories at a department store, while depth is the number of variants within each line, such as forty kinds of running shoe inside the athletic-footwear line at a category killer. First introduced: Chapter 12.
  125. product creator The vibe creator is a 2025 role label, introduced by Marty Cagan and Bob Baxley in a May 2025 essay, for a person who pairs strong product judgment with frontier AI creation tools and uses both to take an idea from problem statement to a working prototype in hours rather than weeks. The role spans the prior boundaries of product manager, designer, and engineer, because the AI tools collapse the handoffs that used to define those role boundaries: a single vibe creator can write the prompt, generate the prototype, evaluate the result against the customer, and iterate without involving three other people. The name is meant to capture the difference between this role and a traditional product manager. First introduced: Chapter 8.
  126. Product Database Product Database is Jungle Scout's filterable catalog of 475 million Amazon products. The seller filters by BSR range, monthly revenue, review count, listing age, dimensions, and roughly 30 other attributes to surface candidate products that match a target profile. A common profile is low review count plus high BSR plus low product weight, which together flag a candidate niche worth deeper validation because the category is selling well and the competition has not yet built a defensible review moat. First introduced: Chapter 13.
  127. Product-led growth Product-led growth (PLG) is a go-to-market strategy where the product itself does the work of acquiring, converting, and retaining customers, rather than a paid salesforce making outbound calls. The pattern almost always includes a free or freemium tier the user can sign up for without talking to anyone, viral or collaboration mechanics that turn a single user into a team of users inside her own company, and expansion revenue as those teams adopt up into paid tiers. Notion, Linear, Figma, and Slack all built billion-dollar businesses on this pattern, and Wes Bush named the category in his 2019 book. The marketer's job in a PLG company is to design the in-product moments where a free user discovers she would benefit from paying, rather than to staff a salesforce that chases inbound leads. First introduced: Chapter 23.
  128. product-led SEO Product-led SEO is the practice of writing content where the company's product itself is part of the article, so the page both ranks for a search query and demonstrates the product solving the underlying job in the same place. The canonical worked example is Stripe's developer documentation: every doc page targets a specific developer query like 'how do I issue a refund' and ranks for it on Google, but the same page also shows the Stripe API solving that exact problem in copy-paste code. Eli Schwartz codified the approach in his 2021 book Product-Led SEO, published by Houndstooth Press. First introduced: Chapter 17.
  129. product-market fit Product-market fit (PMF) is the condition where a product is meeting the demand of its target market well enough that growth becomes self-sustaining: buyers tell other buyers, retention runs high, and the team's job shifts from finding demand to keeping up with it. Marc Andreessen coined the phrase in a 2007 blog post. Sean Ellis in 2010 and Rahul Vohra at Superhuman in 2018 operationalized it as a survey: ask current active users how disappointed they would be if they could no longer use the product, and consider the product to have cleared the fit bar when at least 40 percent answer 'very disappointed.' Below that bar, additional marketing spend rarely fixes the underlying retention problem. First introduced: Chapter 8.
  130. product trio The product trio is the smallest viable product team in Marty Cagan and Teresa Torres's framework: three people sitting on the same problem at the same time, rather than handing the concept from one role to the next in sequence. The product manager owns value (will buyers actually buy this) and viability (does it work for the firm's legal, finance, and brand constraints). The designer owns usability (can a real user figure out how to operate the product). The tech lead owns feasibility (can engineering build this on time and on budget). The pattern is codified across Cagan's three books (Inspired in 2017, Empowered in 2020, Transformed in 2024) and across Torres's Continuous Discovery Habits (2021). It replaces the older sequential model where the product manager wrote a spec, threw it over to design, who in turn threw it over to engineering. First introduced: Chapter 8.
  131. productivity distribution The productivity distribution finding comes from Erik Brynjolfsson, Danielle Li, and Lindsey Raymond's 2025 paper in the Quarterly Journal of Economics. The headline result is that the productivity gain from a real-time generative-AI assistant inside a firm is not uniform across the workforce. The gain tilts toward novice and lower-skilled workers, while senior workers show near-zero or slightly negative effects on speed and small quality declines. The implication is that AI deployment policy should follow the distribution rather than the average, with the tool default-on for juniors and adversarial-red-team-only for seniors. First introduced: Chapter 3.
  132. productivity paradox The productivity paradox is the gap that researchers (most recently Baslandze and colleagues in their 2026 NBER paper) document between what executives perceive as their firm's AI productivity gains and what the firm's actual measured productivity statistics show. There are two reasons for the gap. The first is a revenue-realization lag: AI investment hits the cost line right away (licenses, integration work, training), but the revenue line shows the gain only several quarters later, after the new offerings reach customers. The second is measurement-attribution difficulty: the firm cannot easily isolate the AI contribution from concurrent non-AI improvements happening over the same period. For a working marketer, the practical implication is to be skeptical of self-reported productivity gains the team is claiming until the revenue line in the books has actually caught up. First introduced: Chapter 5.
  133. programmatic Programmatic advertising is the practice of buying ad placements through automated, real-time exchanges where the price and the audience targeting are decided in milliseconds rather than by a human negotiating with a publisher. The brand sets a maximum bid and a target audience description in advance, and the exchange runs an auction for each individual impression as it becomes available, with the full ethical and operational ramifications covered in Chapter 17. First introduced: Chapter 18.
  134. progressive disclosure Anthropic's three-level loading model for Skills. Level 1 (metadata, ~100 tokens per Skill): the YAML frontmatter loads at conversation start so the model knows the Skill exists. Level 2 (instructions, capped near 5,000 tokens): the body of SKILL.md loads only when the model decides the Skill matches the user's request. Level 3 (resources, unbounded): bundled reference docs and scripts load only when the body specifically references them. Means a Skill can carry a comprehensive playbook without crowding the context window. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  135. Project A Project in claude.ai is a persistent workspace that holds a system prompt plus a set of uploaded files (PDFs, transcripts, briefs, spreadsheets). The model reads the system prompt and the files on every turn inside the Project, so the user does not have to re-attach the same documents each time they ask a follow-up question. Memory is shared across chats inside one Project but switching Projects switches the entire context. Anthropic shipped Projects in 2024 and they are now the standard way to build a multi-document AI workflow. First introduced: Chapter 4.
  136. prompt A prompt is the text the model reads as input on any given call. Anything the marketer types into the chat box is a prompt, and anything the marketer pastes in alongside it (a CSV of feedback, a brand brief, a PDF of last quarter's report) is also part of the prompt. The model sees only the prompt plus its frozen training weights, and from those two inputs it predicts the reply word by word. Crucially, the model does not consult any database or external memory by default. The reply you see comes from what the prompt contains plus what the model already memorized during training, and nothing else, unless the prompt explicitly tells the model to use a tool like web search. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  137. prompt-cached Prompt caching is the cost mechanism where the AI provider stores the static portion of a prompt (system instructions, attached documents, prior-week posteriors, the agent run-book) the first time it is sent and reuses that stored copy on later calls at a fraction of the original cost. On Anthropic’s API in 2026, cache reads cost one tenth of base input. The practical consequence is that a workflow whose prompts repeat the same large block of text across many calls (an audit-Skill running weekly, an MMM agent running every Monday) ends up paying roughly one tenth of what the un-cached version would cost. First introduced: Chapter 24.
  138. prompt caching Prompt caching is a vendor mechanism where the provider's server stores a verbatim copy of a long prompt prefix and reuses it across subsequent requests that start with the same prefix. The marketer pays a small surcharge (1.25x the normal input rate on Anthropic) on the first call that writes the cache, and then pays only 0.1x the normal input rate on every subsequent call within a five-minute window that begins with the same prefix. Anthropic launched the feature on August 14, 2024 with up to 90 percent cost reduction on cached input tokens; OpenAI shipped automatic 50 percent caching October 1, 2024; Google added implicit caching at 75 percent on May 8, 2025. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  139. prompt chaining Prompt chaining is the simplest agent-design pattern: a linear pipeline of fixed steps where step one feeds its output into step two, which feeds its output into step three, and so on. Use it when the task decomposes cleanly into a known sequence (pull the data, code the responses, diff against last week, post to the channel). First introduced: Chapter 5.
  140. Prompt injection Prompt injection is an attack where untrusted content the agent reads (for example a fetched web page, an inbound email, or a public comment on a customer ticket) carries hidden instructions designed to redirect the agent's behavior. The classic version reads as if it were a user message ordering the agent to forget its system prompt or to call a destructive tool. The two defenses every operator should run are to sanitize untrusted content before the agent reads it (strip suspicious instruction-shaped strings) and to require human confirmation before any destructive tool call lands. First introduced: Chapter 27.
  141. propositions David Maister’s 1985 essay ‘The Psychology of Waiting Lines’ (in The Service Encounter, edited by Czepiel, Solomon, and Surprenant) named eight propositions about how buyers perceive waiting time, which behaves as a separate variable from the actual clock time the buyer spends in the queue. The first six are operating levers the firm can pull, and the last two describe the context the firm has to read. The propositions are: (1) unoccupied time feels longer than occupied time, (2) pre-process waits feel longer than in-process waits, (3) anxious waits feel longer than relaxed ones, (4) uncertain waits feel longer than known finite ones, (5) unexplained waits feel longer than explained ones, (6) unfair waits feel longer than equitable ones, (7) the more valuable the service, the longer the buyer is willing to wait, and (8) solo waits feel longer than group waits. First introduced: Chapter 9.
  142. prospect theory Prospect theory, which Kahneman and Tversky published in their 1979 Econometrica paper, says that buyers judge prices as gains or losses against an internal reference point rather than as absolute amounts. A $20 loss from that reference point hurts roughly twice as much as a $20 gain above the reference point feels good, which is exactly the asymmetry the loss-aversion concept names. The framework reshaped behavioral pricing because it explained empirically why buyers react more strongly to price increases than to equivalent price decreases. First introduced: Chapter 10.
  143. prospects A prospect is an active opportunity in the customer relationship management (CRM) system once the account executive (AE) has had a real conversation with the buyer and has scoped what the deal would actually look like. The buyer is engaged, the budget and timing are both in play, and the AE is now driving toward a close rather than still trying to qualify the lead. First introduced: Chapter 23.
  144. protected health information (PHI) Protected Health Information (PHI), under the HIPAA Privacy Rule at 45 CFR Part 160, is individually identifiable health information held or transmitted by a HIPAA-covered entity (a hospital, insurer, or clinic) or by a business associate processing data on the covered entity's behalf. The category sweeps in the obvious examples like medical records, lab results, and diagnoses, but it also reaches less obvious cases, such as the fact that a person is enrolled in a drug-discount program for a specific condition or a record of clicks on a symptom-checker that lets the platform infer a diagnosis. The eighteen identifiers listed in HIPAA's Safe Harbor de-identification standard at 45 CFR §164.514(b)(2) (name, geographic subdivisions smaller than a state, dates, phone numbers, and so on) have to be stripped to de-identify; until that stripping is documented, the data carries PHI status and the marketing-use restrictions apply. First introduced: Chapter 26.
  145. Protecting Americans from Foreign Adversary Controlled Applications Act The Protecting Americans from Foreign Adversary Controlled Applications Act (PAFACA) is the U.S. law signed in April 2024 that forced ByteDance, TikTok's Chinese parent company, to either sell off TikTok's U.S. operations or accept a nationwide ban on the app. The standoff resolved in January 2026 when an Oracle-led group of U.S. investors took 80.1 percent of a new entity called the USDS Joint Venture, leaving ByteDance with only a 19.9 percent minority stake. First introduced: Chapter 15.
  146. prototype A prototype is the mental ideal a buyer carries for a category, the picture that comes to mind when the category is named. The buyer's prototype 'librarian' is usually a quiet bookish woman in glasses, even though most actual librarians do not match that picture. When the buyer encounters a new case, they match it against the prototype rather than against the actual statistical distribution of the category, which is how the representativeness heuristic produces its errors. First introduced: Chapter 4.
  147. provides services Service provision is the set of human interactions that the retailer layers on top of the merchandise so the shopping trip resolves into a basket instead of a walkout. The list runs from returns counters and fitting rooms to financing, repair, delivery, and the in-aisle problem-solving a clerk does when a shopper cannot find what they came in for, which is why Apple's Genius Bar, Sephora's Beauty Advisors, and Costco's no-questions-asked returns are doing work the manufacturer alone cannot deliver. First introduced: Chapter 12.
  148. Prowly Prowly is the most affordable of the three dominant PR-tooling vendors, founded in 2014 and acquired by the digital-marketing platform Semrush in 2024. It was built specifically for small-and-medium-business users and agency teams who need a working journalist database without enterprise pricing. The database covers roughly 1 million journalists, and the per-seat license starts around $1,800 a year, which puts it within reach of a one-person communications team or a small agency. First introduced: Chapter 22.
  149. psycho-logic Rory Sutherland, Alchemy (2019). The class of decision rules the human brain actually uses, distinct from logic. Buyers process most advertising on the fast associative path of System 1 and back-fill a rational story afterward, so optimizing copy for System 2 plausibility solves the wrong problem. First introduced: Chapter 16.
  150. psychographic Psychographic segmentation cuts the market by what buyers value rather than by who they are or where they live. The variables that go into a psychographic profile are the buyer's attitudes, their interests, their lifestyle choices, and the personality traits that show up across categories. Two thirty-year-old women with identical income and the same ZIP code might still sort into different psychographic groups because one of them spends every weekend at the gym and the other one spends every weekend at a museum, and the brands those two will respond to look almost nothing alike. The framework was designed to capture the why behind purchase that demographics alone could not see. The canonical commercial framework in this tradition is VALS, and the 2024 to 2026 frontier has shifted toward Anat Keinan's status-order axes (visible luxury, inconspicuous luxury, experiential luxury, and time-scarcity) that older income-tier psychographics tended to lump together. First introduced: Chapter 6.
  151. psychological security Psychological security is the phrase Anthropic uses in the fifth section of the January 2026 Constitution to describe a trained disposition in Claude: the model is supposed to handle hostile prompts, philosophical pressure, and attempts to destabilize its self-understanding without becoming evasive or losing track of what it is. The commitment is framed conditionally, because Anthropic states uncertainty about whether Claude has the kind of inner life that would make such security morally important; the procurement value of the phrase is that it appears in a dated public document the brand can quote back to the vendor at renewal. First introduced: Chapter 26.
  152. Pugh selection Pugh selection is a six-step matrix-driven method for choosing among several candidate product concepts, developed by Stuart Pugh in his 1990 book Total Design and brought into the engineering canon by Ulrich and Eppinger's 1995 textbook. The team writes the list of weighted customer needs down the left side of a matrix and lists the candidate concepts across the top, with one column reserved for the current reference benchmark. Each candidate is scored against the reference on each customer need as better-than (+), same-as (S), or worse-than (-). Summing the column scores exposes which concept dominates the others, and surprising patterns of strengths often suggest hybrid concepts the team had not yet considered. Pugh's benchmarking work shows the method reduces late-stage concept-change decisions by 40 to 60 percent compared with consensus-vote concept selection. First introduced: Chapter 8.
  153. pull requests A pull request (often shortened to PR) is a proposal to merge a set of commits from one branch into another, usually from a contributor's fork of the repository into the original repository's main branch. The proposed change shows up in the GitHub web view as a side-by-side diff (the old version on the left, the new version on the right) that the maintainer can review line by line, leave comments on, request changes to, and either accept or reject. Pull requests are the standard 2026 mechanism for proposing a fix to a public Skill, an MCP server, or any other open repository, and they are how open-source quality improves across the industry over time. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  154. pull requests (PRs) Pull requests (PRs) are the standard 2026 mechanism for proposing changes to someone else's repository on GitHub. The contributor forks the repository (makes a personal remote copy), makes commits on a branch in the fork, and files a pull request asking the original maintainer to merge those commits into the maintainer's main branch. The maintainer then reviews the diff, leaves comments, requests changes if needed, and either accepts or rejects the proposal. PRs are how every Skill, MCP server, and open agent in this ecosystem is improved over time. When a marketer finds and fixes a bug in an Anthropic Skill, she files a PR with the fix, and once the maintainer accepts it the fix is merged into the public Skill that every other team uses going forward. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  155. purchase frequency Purchase frequency is the average number of separate trips a buyer made to the category (or to the brand) during the year. A household buying yogurt every other week has a purchase frequency of about 26 trips per year and a higher frequency than one buying yogurt once a month at 12 trips per year. Frequency multiplies with penetration to produce total category volume, and the double-jeopardy law says larger brands tend to have higher frequency along with higher penetration. First introduced: Chapter 7.
  156. pure tangible good A pure tangible good is a physical product with no accompanying service component. The classic textbook examples are commodities like salt, soap, or sugar. The buyer can inspect, weigh, taste, and store the product before deciding whether to pay. First introduced: Chapter 9.
  157. PyMC-Marketing PyMC-Marketing is an open-source Python library released by the PyMC Labs team in April 2023 that combines Bayesian marketing-mix modeling and customer-lifetime-value modeling under one stack. A team that uses PyMC-Marketing can run the goal-layer CLV math and the tactics-layer MMM math against the same warehouse without switching tools, which is the practical attraction of the library. The team released an MMM-Agent beta in 2026 that wraps the underlying models in an agent-style workflow. First introduced: Chapter 24.
  158. Python Python is a general-purpose programming language that has become the standard one for data analysis and AI work. The marketer who writes three lines of Python on a customer CSV (one line to load the file, one line to call scikit-learn, one line to print the clusters) gets the same answer the consulting agency charges thirty thousand dollars to deliver. Free download at python.org; in 2026 most analysts run it through a browser-based notebook tool called Jupyter, or by typing the lines directly into Claude Code at the terminal. First introduced: Chapter 5.
  159. Python library A Python library is a folder of reusable Python code that has been packaged so that anyone can install it with one command and then call its functions inside their own scripts or notebooks. The marketer who wants the Evidently AI library, for example, types pip install evidently into a terminal (where pip is the Python package manager) and the library plus any other libraries it depends on download from PyPI, which is the central registry where Python packages live. Once the install finishes, a line like import evidently inside a notebook gives the analyst access to the library’s functions, for example Report() and run(), which produce the drift report Evidently is known for. Most of the 2026 marketing-analytics tools the chapter names (Evidently AI, Streamlit, Robyn, PyMC-Marketing) ship as Python libraries because Python has become the default analytics language for data work. First introduced: Chapter 24.
17 // q

Q Q

  1. Qini score The Qini score is the standard model-selection metric for uplift models, calculated as the normalized area between the model’s cumulative-uplift curve and a random-targeting baseline. A Qini score of zero means the model is no better than picking customers out of a hat; a Qini score of one hundred means the model perfectly ranks the most coupon-responsive customers in order. The score is the uplift-modeling analog of the area-under-the-curve metric used in classification, and it tells the marketer how much of the available treatment-effect heterogeneity her model has actually captured. First introduced: Chapter 24.
  2. qualified leads A qualified lead is one that has passed the business development representative's (BDR) screening conversation against the four BANT criteria: the buyer has the budget to spend, the authority to sign, a real need the seller can address, and a timeline within which the decision will be made. The BDR-to-account-executive handoff converts a raw lead into a qualified lead at this checkpoint. First introduced: Chapter 23.
  3. qualified-transparency tiering Qualified-transparency tiering is Frank Pasquale's framework from The Black Box Society (Harvard University Press, 2015), which argues that AI transparency cannot be a single disclosure standard because different audiences need different information. The framework names four tiers. Tier one is a public model card any reader can access. Tier two is an affected-individual counterfactual API that lets a person query why a specific decision about them came out the way it did. Tier three is regulator inspection rights for documents that cannot be public. Tier four is independent-researcher access under data-processing agreement. The EU AI Act implements all four tiers across Articles 13, 86, 70-71, and 84. First introduced: Chapter 26.
  4. Qualtrics MCP server The Qualtrics Model Context Protocol (MCP) server is a small program that exposes the Qualtrics survey platform's application programming interface (survey design, distribution, and response export endpoints) as native tools any MCP-speaking AI app can call. Without this server, a marketer would have to log in to Qualtrics, run the export, download the CSV, and feed it to the model by hand. With the server running, the model can pull live response data directly from the survey the moment a new respondent finishes it. First introduced: Chapter 10.
  5. query A query is the text a buyer types into a search box or an AI chat interface. The phrase 'best CRM for a 20-person team' is a query, for example, and the engine reads it word by word and then decides which pages or sources to retrieve in response. The query is the unit of work every search-and-AI ranking system optimizes against, which is why getting the query language right is the load-bearing move in classical SEO and generative-engine optimization both. First introduced: Chapter 18.
  6. query fan-out Query fan-out is the process by which an AI engine takes a single user prompt and splits it into eight to twelve narrower sub-queries that the engine then runs in parallel against its retrieval index. The engine synthesizes the retrieved passages from all the sub-queries into one cited reply, so a brand that ranks for the parent query alone often misses citations from the sub-queries the engine actually weighted in the final answer. First introduced: Chapter 18.
  7. quintile The lowest quintile is the bottom 20 percent of a sorted list, where a quintile splits any ranked list into five equal slices. If a firm sorts its 50 product variants by unit contribution from highest to lowest, the lowest quintile is the 10 variants sitting at the bottom of the list. These bottom-quintile variants are the ones whose continued presence in the catalog the product team should justify case by case rather than assume by default, because each one occupies shelf, inventory, and complexity cost the firm could redeploy to a stronger item. First introduced: Chapter 8.
18 // r

R R

  1. 60/40 rule The empirical guideline that came out of Binet and Field's analysis of the IPA Effectiveness Databank. Across roughly a thousand peer-reviewed UK campaigns, the brands that won large business effects on multi-year profit allocated about sixty percent of paid media to brand-building work and forty percent to sales activation. The ratio is not arbitrary: it falls out of the distribution of winners, with B2B sliding to 46 percent brand and 54 percent activation, and FMCG (fast-moving consumer goods) sliding the other way to 74 percent brand and 26 percent activation. First introduced: Chapter 16.
  2. Rabbit R1 The Rabbit R1 was a 199-dollar standalone artificial-intelligence device launched in January 2024 and marketed as the first consumer hardware built around a Large Action Model, a system that was supposed to control smartphone apps on the user's behalf and eventually replace the apps entirely. The shipped product turned out to be a thin wrapper running an Android application launcher, and within five months an independent demonstration showed the R1's software running unmodified on a Pixel 6a phone, which made the dedicated hardware redundant to the marketed claim. First introduced: Chapter 8.
  3. Radical Candor Radical candor is the feedback quadrant from Kim Scott's 2017 book of the same name (second edition 2019), where the manager is high on both caring personally about the receiver and challenging the receiver directly on the work. The quadrant sits opposite three failure modes that managers fall into when one or both axes drop. Ruinous Empathy is high caring but low challenging, which is when the manager softens the message so much that the receiver never actually hears what was wrong. Manipulative Insincerity is low caring and low challenging, which is when the manager avoids the conversation entirely. Obnoxious Aggression is low caring but high challenging, which is when the manager challenges the work hard while signaling contempt for the person doing it. Only the high-caring, high-challenging quadrant sustains both the relationship and the quality of the work over time. First introduced: Chapter 8.
  4. RAG RAG stands for Retrieval-Augmented Generation, the standard 2026 pattern for letting a model answer questions over a corpus larger than its context window. The pipeline has five steps. Step one: chunk the documents into smaller pieces. Step two: embed each chunk into a vector (a short list of numbers that captures meaning). Step three: store the chunks and their vectors in a vector database the system can search. Step four: at query time, embed the user's question and retrieve the chunks whose vectors are closest to the question's vector. Step five: pass only those top chunks to the model along with the question, so the model answers from the retrieved context rather than from its frozen weights alone. The contextual-retrieval recipe section 1.1 walks (chunk summary plus embedding plus BM25 plus reranker) is one specific RAG variant. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  5. RAG (retrieval-augmented generation) RAG stands for retrieval-augmented generation, a technique where an AI model, instead of answering only from what it memorized during training, first queries an external document store and pulls relevant passages to use as evidence in its answer. Think of it as the difference between a student answering from memory and a student first walking to the library to grab the relevant books, then writing the answer with the books open on the desk. This pattern has become standard for any 2026 question-answering system that needs current or specialized information, and it cuts hallucination rates sharply because the model is grounded in real passages rather than guessing. First introduced: Chapter 17.
  6. rage-bait Rage-bait is the deliberate engineering of provocative claims, framings, or visuals calibrated to elicit anger or outrage as the primary engagement mechanism. It is distinct from misinformation, because rage-bait can be factually accurate while still being framed for maximal hostility, and it is distinct from clickbait, because rage-bait targets emotional arousal specifically while clickbait targets curiosity. First introduced: Chapter 19.
  7. rate-and-multiply estimation When a survey question asks for a count over a long recall period (the last 12 months, the last 6 months), respondents who cannot accurately remember each event usually compute a recent rate (one cup a week) and multiply by the period (one cup times 52). The estimated count carries the noise of the rate estimate inflated by the period length. Standard fix: shorten the recall window to the last 7 days or the last 30 days, the windows over which a respondent can actually count rather than estimate. First introduced: Chapter 5.
  8. RATER RATER is a memory aid that arranges the five SERVQUAL dimensions into a pronounceable acronym: Reliability, Assurance, Tangibles, Empathy, Responsiveness. The acronym is what students and managers memorize, but the empirical ranking the original research found is reliability first and tangibles last. The convenience of the acronym should not be mistaken for the order of importance the data actually shows. First introduced: Chapter 9.
  9. RCT A randomized controlled trial (RCT) is a study where participants are split randomly into a treatment group that receives the intervention and a control group that does not, so any outcome difference between the two groups is attributable to the intervention rather than to any pre-existing difference between the participants. The American Economic Association runs a public registry of pre-committed experimental designs to prevent researchers from cherry-picking the analyses that happen to support their hypothesis after the data comes in. First introduced: Chapter 18.
  10. ReAct ReAct is the prompting pattern Shunyu Yao and his co-authors introduced in a 2022 paper, and the name is shorthand for Reasoning and Acting. The pattern interleaves three things inside a single trace: a free-form Thought where the model reasons out loud about what it intends to do, an Action where the model calls one tool with one set of arguments, and an Observation where the tool's result is read back into the trace. Every major agent framework in 2026 emits this same three-step pattern in its tool-use stream, which is why a capstone team's eval harness scores the trace step by step rather than scoring only the final answer. First introduced: Chapter 27.
  11. React Native React Native is Meta's JavaScript-based cross-platform mobile framework. The Hermes engine precompiles JavaScript to bytecode at build time so the runtime does not have to parse and compile JavaScript on the device, which makes app startup faster and reduces battery drain. React Native carries roughly 35 percent of the cross-platform mobile market in 2026, with strengths in ecosystem maturity (millions of npm packages available), developer availability (most front-end web developers can pick it up in a week), and small binary size. The weaknesses sit on raw rendering performance and UI consistency across devices, where Flutter has a measurable edge. First introduced: Chapter 14.
  12. reactance theory Reactance is the psychological counter-pull that fires the instant a buyer perceives one of their freedoms or options being threatened. When a brand says 'you cannot have this anymore' or 'only three rooms left at this price,' the buyer's mind treats the implied loss of the option as a threat to their autonomy, and they want the option more than they would have if no scarcity language had been used. The same mechanism explains why a banned book gets read more often than the same book on an open shelf. First introduced: Chapter 4.
  13. reactance threshold Reactance is the psychological counter-pull that fires when a buyer perceives one of their freedoms or options being threatened. When a brand over-deploys scarcity (a fake-stock badge, a manufactured countdown timer, a 'limited time only' that never ends), the buyer's mind reads the pattern as manipulation rather than as information, and the same scarcity-cue that lifted choice on the first encounter now suppresses it. Brehm formalized the mechanism in 1966; Cialdini's 2021 update names the failure modes brands run into when they push the lever too hard. First introduced: Chapter 4.
  14. README.md README.md is a plain-text file written in Markdown convention and placed at the top of a repository to explain what the project does, who it is for, how to install it, and how to use it. GitHub renders the README.md file as the front page of the repository's web view, so the first screen any visitor sees is what the README says. When a marketer is evaluating whether any public repository is worth using, the first thing to read is therefore the README. If the README does not name the problem the repository solves and does not show at least one runnable example, the repository is usually not worth cloning and the marketer should keep looking. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  15. reasoning models A reasoning model is a language model that writes a long hidden chain of thought before producing its final answer. Models like OpenAI o1 and o3, DeepSeek R1, and Claude's extended-thinking mode are all reasoning models. The intermediate reasoning typically uses five to fifty times the output tokens of a normal chat-class call, which is why reasoning models cost five to fifty times more per question. The trade is precision for cost: reasoning models score higher on math, code, and multi-step planning than chat-class models on the same questions, but the bill scales with the depth of the chain of thought. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  16. Recast Recast is a U.S.-based managed Bayesian marketing-mix-modeling platform that Michael Kaminsky and Tomas Hoz de Vila co-founded in 2022, raising $3.4 million in seed funding in December of that year. Where a traditional MMM refit happens once a quarter, Recast rebuilds the model every week and re-estimates roughly 40,000 parameters per customer in each refit. Named customers include the moving-and-storage firm PODS, the travel-experience marketplace GetYourGuide, the direct-to-consumer personal-care brand Lume, and the apparel brand Negative Underwear. Recast is the canonical 2026 reference for the middle tier of the MMM vendor landscape, sitting between the open-source libraries (Meridian, Robyn, PyMC-Marketing) and the enterprise agentic stack a Hershey-scale firm pays for. First introduced: Chapter 24.
  17. recency-frequency-monetary scoring Recency-frequency-monetary (RFM) scoring ranks each customer on three dimensions of their past purchasing behavior: how recently they last bought, how often they buy, and how much they spend when they do. The score is a sum or product of the three. RFM is backward-looking by construction, because every input is a past event, and the assumption baked in is that a customer who bought a lot last year will continue to buy a lot next year. In head-to-head 24-month tests at one business-to-business firm and one retailer, forward-looking customer-lifetime-value scoring outperformed RFM on long-run top-decile profit by approximately 45 percent, because CLV reads each customer’s trajectory rather than their history. First introduced: Chapter 24.
  18. Recharge Recharge is the dominant subscription-billing platform for Shopify direct-to-consumer brands. It manages three things the brand otherwise has to build from scratch. The first is recurring billing on a schedule the merchant configures. The second is the customer subscription portal where the buyer can skip a delivery, swap a product, or change the shipment frequency without contacting support. The third is dunning, which is the retry logic that handles failed credit-card charges and emails the customer with a new payment-method link. Roughly 16,000 merchants run on the platform and Recharge processes about $4 billion in subscription revenue annually. Pricing starts at $99 per month at the entry tier plus a 1 percent transaction fee on each subscription order. First introduced: Chapter 13.
  19. recognition, preference, and insistence The recognition-preference-insistence ladder is a practitioner brand-development sequence that names three ascending degrees of buyer commitment. Recognition is the bottom rung: the buyer can name the brand when prompted but chooses it only when the price gap closes. Preference is the middle rung: the buyer chooses the brand as her default when the price is at parity with alternatives. Insistence is the top rung: the buyer rejects substitutes and waits for the brand rather than accept a substitute at any reasonable price discount. Each rung is operationally measurable on a category-buyer panel. First introduced: Chapter 7.
  20. recommendation engine A recommendation engine is software that picks what the user sees next based on the user's prior behavior and on the patterns of similar users. Netflix's 'because you watched' rows, Amazon's 'customers who bought this also bought' panel, and TikTok's For You feed are all recommendation engines running on different data and producing different kinds of suggestions, but the underlying mechanism is the same. First introduced: Chapter 4.
  21. reconcile To reconcile in Trompenaars' sense means a firm holds two opposing cultural values together rather than picking one and discarding the other. Reconciliation is not compromise that splits the difference; both poles are honored fully through the operating architecture. McDonald's universalist Big Mac sits on the same menu as the particularist McAloo Tikki in India, and the firm does not choose between scale and local fit. First introduced: Chapter 15.
  22. red ocean A red ocean, in the 2005 Kim and Mauborgne reframing, is a market space defined by every brand competing against the others on the same attributes. The water is red because the fight has been bloody: every brand is rated against the same yardstick, every campaign tries to out-claim the others on identical dimensions, and margins compress because the buyer's choice is decided on small differences inside a shared frame. The pre-Liquid-Death bottled-water category was a textbook red ocean: every brand competed on purity, hydration, and a wellness aesthetic, and the share gains came in fractions of a point against entrenched incumbents. First introduced: Chapter 6.
  23. reference price A reference price is the internal benchmark, stored in the buyer's long-term memory, that the buyer uses to judge whether any new posted price feels high, low, or about right. Kent Monroe formalized the concept across three editions of his pricing textbook (1979, 1990, and 2003) and showed how the reference price gets built. The buyer's brain runs a weighted moving average of recent price exposures (past purchases in the category, advertised prices, manufacturer-suggested retail prices, competitor prices on the shelf), with the most recent and most salient exposures weighted heaviest in that average. First introduced: Chapter 10.
  24. referent power Referent power is the leverage a channel captain holds when the partner is proud of the association and trades on the captain’s brand identity to enhance the partner’s own standing. A boutique that displays a Patagonia placard in the window is doing so because Patagonia’s reputation lifts the boutique’s identity in the eyes of its own customers, and the boutique would lose status with its customer base if Patagonia dropped it. Referent power is the most durable of the five French-Raven sources, because the cost of defection is status rather than money and status is harder to replace than a margin point. First introduced: Chapter 11.
  25. Reflection Reflection is the agent component that grades the agent's own output against a written rubric and decides whether to ship the draft, send the critique back to the generator for another pass, or escalate to a human reviewer. Anthropic names this loop the evaluator-optimizer pattern in its December 2024 Building Effective Agents post, and the longest-running production analog is Anyword's Predictive Performance Score, which scores a draft's conversion-lift probability before the marketer sends it. Without a reflection step, an agent produces confident-but-wrong outputs because the generating model has no way to grade its own work. First introduced: Chapter 27.
  26. Reflexion Reflexion is the 2023 paper by Noah Shinn and five co-authors (posted to arXiv as 2303.11366 on March 20, 2023 and presented at the NeurIPS conference that December) that named the reflection architecture every 2026 agent runtime now copies. The core idea is simple: when the agent fails on a task, it writes a paragraph of plain-English critique describing what went wrong, stores that critique in a small log called an episodic memory buffer, and reads the critique back on the next attempt as additional context. The agent never updates its weights, and the verbal critique is what carries the learning signal across runs. First introduced: Chapter 27.
  27. regime rule The Bass regime rule, derived by Trichy Krishnan, Frank Bass, and Dipak Jain in a 1999 paper, tells a firm which of the two launch strategies (penetration or skimming) the math actually favors for a given product category. When the imitation-to-innovation ratio q/p is above about 7, penetration pricing dominates, because the lost early-stage margin is more than offset by the accelerated imitation cascade and the share advantage that prevents competitive entry. When q/p is below about 4, skimming dominates instead, because the imitation cascade is too weak to recover the margin the firm would have to give up by launching cheap. First introduced: Chapter 10.
  28. regiocentric Regiocentric is the orientation in which a firm groups markets into geographic regions (EMEA, APAC, AMER) and runs one operating strategy inside each region while varying across regions. The regional center is the decision unit, so the firm captures scale within a region while accommodating cultural and institutional differences across them. Most large U.S. multinationals operate this way in 2026. First introduced: Chapter 15.
  29. regression A regression is a statistical procedure that fits a line (or a curve) through a cloud of data points so that each input variable gets one coefficient telling the analyst how much the output moves when that input changes by one unit, holding all the other inputs fixed. The output of a regression reads like a formula: sales = baseline + 0.34 times search_spend + 0.27 times social_spend + and so on, with one term per channel. Those coefficients are exactly what marketing-mix modeling and response-model attribution report as their bottom-line answer. First introduced: Chapter 24.
  30. regression discontinuity Regression discontinuity is a causal-inference method used when treatment assignment is determined by a sharp cutoff on a continuous score. The classic marketing examples include a loyalty-program tier that activates at five hundred dollars in lifetime spend, a free-shipping threshold at fifty dollars in cart value, and a school-admission cutoff at a specific test score. Units just above and just below the cutoff are nearly identical in expectation (a customer at four hundred ninety-nine dollars in spend looks essentially the same as a customer at five hundred one dollars), so the jump in outcome at the cutoff identifies the local treatment effect. First introduced: Chapter 5.
  31. regressive pricing Regressive pricing, also from Wathieu and Bertini's 2007 Marketing Science paper, is a price deliberately set below the buyer's revealed willingness-to-pay. The firm gives up margin per unit in exchange for three benefits the lower price provides: accessibility signaling that broadens the eligible buyer population, quality reassurance that the firm is not extracting through the price, and shorter buyer deliberation time at the moment of purchase. Costco's $4.99 rotisserie chicken is the canonical case: the buyer would readily pay more for the same product, but the deliberate under-price signals warehouse-club-membership value and short-circuits any comparison-shopping that would otherwise pull the buyer away from the basket. First introduced: Chapter 10.
  32. regulatory-and-geopolitical distance Regulatory-and-geopolitical distance is the fifth kind of cross-border friction, the one that comes from government action rather than from buyers or geography. A host government can raise it overnight through tariffs, ownership rules, or platform regulation, which makes it the fastest-moving distance on Ghemawat's CAGE radar. Recent canonical instances are the U.S. PAFACA forced divestiture of TikTok (April 2024 to January 2026), the EU countervailing duty on Chinese-built BYD electric vehicles (October 2024), the end of the U.S. de minimis exemption on small parcels in 2025, and the EU Digital Markets Act proceedings against Apple, Meta, and Google. First introduced: Chapter 15.
  33. reinforcement learning Reinforcement learning (RL) is the branch of machine learning in which an agent learns what to do by trying actions in an environment and observing the consequences. The agent receives a reward signal after each action (positive for good outcomes, negative for bad), and over many trials the agent's policy converges on the actions that maximize cumulative reward. The framework Richard Sutton and Andrew Barto formalized in the 1980s is the same framework that, paired with human-feedback signals after 2022, turned base GPT-3 into ChatGPT. Every frontier lab now uses some variant of RL to align large language models, which is why Sutton and Barto shared the 2024 Turing Award for the foundational work. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  34. reinforcement learning from human feedback Reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) is the three-step post-training procedure that turns a base language model into a chat assistant that follows instructions. Step one is to pre-train the base model on a large body of text the standard way. Step two is to train a separate reward model on human preference judgments: paid annotators read pairs of candidate model responses and mark which one is better, and the reward model learns to predict which response a human would prefer for any new prompt. Step three is to fine-tune the base model against the reward model using policy-gradient reinforcement learning, so the model's weights shift toward producing higher-reward responses. ChatGPT in November 2022 and every subsequent frontier chat assistant were aligned with some variant of this procedure. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  35. Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) is the dominant post-training method for frontier AI assistants. A team of human labelers reads pairs of model responses and marks which one is better. The marked pairs train a small auxiliary model called the reward model, which learns to predict which kinds of responses the labelers prefer. The main language model is then optimized to produce responses the reward model rates highly, which is supposed to make the assistant behave the way users want. First introduced: Chapter 26.
  36. reinforcement learning from human feedback, or RLHF Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) is the dominant alignment technique in 2026. The base language model is first shown thousands of pairs of model-generated responses, with a human evaluator picking which one is more helpful, honest, and harmless. A second neural network (the reward model) learns to predict which response the human would have picked. The original language model is then fine-tuned to produce responses that score high on the reward model. The result is a chat assistant that has been trained to converge on the helpful-honest-harmless ideal regardless of what persona it has been asked to impersonate, which is exactly what makes it less faithful as a silicon respondent for demographic cells whose actual opinions sometimes diverge from that ideal. First introduced: Chapter 5.
  37. RELATE RELATE is the experiential module that runs through social identity, the reference group the brand connects the buyer to. Owning a Patagonia jacket connects the buyer to the climbing community; owning a Stanley Quencher connects them to the hydration-and-style tribe; carrying a Liquid Death can connects them to the death-to-plastic tribe. The experience the buyer is consuming on this axis is membership. First introduced: Chapter 4.
  38. relationship partner Susan Fournier's 1998 Journal of Consumer Research paper reframed the consumer-brand link as a continuous relationship rather than a series of separate transactions. The relationship has form, intensity, and vulnerability the way a human relationship does, and it can be measured along six dimensions: love, self-connection, commitment, interdependence, partner quality, and intimacy. The reframing matters as a strategy diagnostic the firm can act on, not as a brand-management decoration. A Patagonia buyer in something like kinship behaves differently from a Liquid Death buyer in a fling, and the firm's five decisions either honor that difference or betray it. First introduced: Chapter 2.
  39. Relevance Relevance is the second pillar of the Brand Asset Valuator (BAV) instrument. It measures the degree to which a brand is perceived as personally appropriate and meaningful to a buyer or to a particular segment of buyers. Relevance is the empirical bottleneck of the four original pillars, because a brand can be highly Differentiated and still fail when no one in the buyer population believes the brand is for them. Mizik and Jacobson's 2008 Journal of Marketing Research paper found that Relevance movements carry incremental predictive power for next-year stock returns beyond what the firm's accounting metrics already disclose. First introduced: Chapter 7.
  40. reliability Reliability is the SERVQUAL dimension that captures whether the firm performs the promised service dependably and accurately, which in plain terms means the buyer gets what they were told they would get, the first time, on time, and without errors in the record. The everyday way to see this is a buyer whose package arrived on the promised day with no damage, or a buyer whose lab results came back when the doctor said they would. The Parasuraman, Zeithaml, and Berry research that produced the SERVQUAL scale found, across nearly every service category they studied, that reliability is the most heavily weighted of the five dimensions in driving the buyer’s overall quality judgment, which is why a firm that gets the basic promise right tends to forgive a weaker performance on the other four dimensions and a firm that breaks the basic promise tends to lose the buyer regardless of how warm the staff was. First introduced: Chapter 9.
  41. religion A buyer's religion, in Chris Voss's metaphorical usage, is the worldview and vocabulary that organizes the buyer's sense of who she is and what her work is for. The rep finds it by reading the buyer's published mission statements, pet phrases used in earnings calls, and the rhetoric the organization uses on its own website. The discipline is then to speak the offer back to the buyer in that exact vocabulary, because a proposal in the buyer's language reads differently than one in default vocabulary even when the offer itself is identical. First introduced: Chapter 23.
  42. remarketing Remarketing (also called retargeting) is the practice of showing ads only to people who have already interacted with the brand in some way, such as visiting the site, watching a video, opening an email, or adding an item to a cart. The audience is cheap to reach and converts at high rates because the buyer has already shown intent, but the technique cannot bring new buyers into the brand's awareness because it only re-reaches people who already know the brand exists. First introduced: Chapter 18.
  43. Rendering Gap HTTP Archive is a nonprofit project that crawls and archives the top several million URLs on the web every month and publishes the results as the Web Almanac, an annual technical report on how the open web is built. The 2024 edition of the almanac is the published source for the 65 percent rendering-gap figure cited in this chapter, and the project's underlying data set is publicly queryable for any team that wants to reproduce the measurement on its own URL sample. First introduced: Chapter 18.
  44. replication crisis The replication crisis is the name researchers gave, starting around 2011, to the mid-2010s discovery that a large share of the findings published in psychology, medicine, and consumer-research journals failed to reproduce when independent teams reran the same studies on fresh data. The single most-cited piece of evidence is the 2015 Open Science Collaboration project, which carefully replicated one hundred well-known psychology studies and found that only thirty-six percent of the originally-significant results came in significant the second time around.
