The two main causes the field eventually identified were p-hacking (running many comparisons and reporting only the winner) and selective reporting (publishing only the studies that found something and quietly shelving the ones that did not). The reform that came out of the crisis is now called the pre-registration movement, and its core requirement is that any study claiming to test a hypothesis must declare the hypothesis, the metric, the sample size, and the analysis plan in a public registry before the data is collected. First introduced: Chapter 5.
  45. repositioning the competition Repositioning the competition is a positioning play in which a brand changes what the buyer already believes about a competitor's brand, rather than trying to claim a fresh position of its own from scratch. The mechanism works by shifting the leader off the top rung of the mental ladder, which is done by reframing the leader's defining attribute as a liability, so that the rung effectively clears and the challenger can step into it. Tylenol versus Bayer in the 1970s analgesic category is the canonical worked case, where Tylenol's campaign repositioned Bayer's aspirin as the stomach-upsetting alternative rather than introducing an entirely new claim for itself. First introduced: Chapter 6.
  46. repository A repository (often shortened to repo) is a folder of files plus the full edit history of every change made to those files plus a small set of collaboration metadata (issues, pull requests, branches). The folder lives both on the marketer's laptop (where edits actually happen) and on a server like GitHub (where teammates can read and contribute back). Public repositories are visible to anyone on the internet without an account; private repositories restrict access to invited collaborators only. Every Skill folder, every MCP server, and every scheduled agent in the rest of this book is shareable as a repository. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  47. repurposing cascade The repurposing cascade is the systematic conversion of one long-form piece of content into a handful of shorter derivative formats for the channels where the audience actually consumes content. A single three-thousand-word blog post becomes a Twitter/X thread for the platform's reply-engagement audience, a document-post carousel for LinkedIn, a sixty-second Instagram reel, a fifteen-minute podcast segment, and a two-hundred-word email newsletter blurb, with each derivative respecting that channel's native format. This discipline is what distinguishes a sustainable content operation that publishes for years from a brittle one that burns out producing one-off pieces every week. First introduced: Chapter 17.
  48. rerank Reranking is the second pass in a retrieval pipeline that takes the top 50 to 200 candidate chunks from embedding plus BM25 and asks a small specialized model to score each candidate against the query. The reranker is more expensive per-chunk than embedding search, but it only runs on the 50 to 200 already-filtered candidates rather than on the whole corpus. The top 5 to 20 reranked chunks are then what reaches the main language model that will answer the question. Standard 2026 reranking vendors are Cohere Rerank and Voyage Rerank, both available as inexpensive API calls. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  49. reservation prices A reservation price, as Donald Lehmann and Russell Winer defined it in their 2005 textbook, is the maximum price a specific buyer would pay for a given product before walking away to a substitute product or to no purchase at all. The reservation price is the buyer's private number that the firm cannot observe directly. This is why every procedure for estimating it (direct asking, Van Westendorp price-sensitivity meter, conjoint analysis, scanner-data revealed preference) has to work around the buyer's strategic incentive to under-report what they would actually pay. First introduced: Chapter 10.
  50. residual stream The residual stream is the internal hidden-state vector that flows through a transformer-based language model from one layer to the next, carrying the model's running representation of whatever it is reading and whatever it is about to write. At each layer, the stream picks up an update from that layer's attention block and its feed-forward block, so by the time the model reaches its output the stream has accumulated everything every layer wanted to add. Picture it as a long list of several thousand numbers traveling through the model, where different directions in that high-dimensional space encode different features the model uses to predict the next word. Recent interpretability work has shown that traits like sycophancy, hallucination, and refusal correspond to specific directions in this space rather than being smeared across the whole model, which is the property the persona-vector technique uses to monitor and steer the model in production. First introduced: Chapter 26.
  51. resource-advantage theory Shelby Hunt's resource-advantage theory, developed with Robert Morgan in 1995 and matured into a full framework by 2010, explains why some firms keep winning the exchange repeatedly while their rivals lose it. Firms compete on heterogeneous portfolios of resources, and sustained advantage comes from a particular kind of resource that rivals cannot acquire just by spending more money. A factory or a piece of software can be bought, but a brand's accumulated memory in 200 million households, a salesforce's twenty-year relationships with hospital procurement officers, or Patagonia's reputation for paying for environmental damage out of its own revenue cannot. Marketing's strategic role is the deliberate construction of that hard-to-copy heterogeneity over time. First introduced: Chapter 2.
  52. response coefficients A regression is a statistical procedure that fits a formula through a cloud of data points, giving each input its own coefficient that says how much the output moves when that input changes by one unit, holding all the other inputs constant. The output of a regression is one number per input plus a baseline, which is why regression is the tool behind marketing-mix modeling, response-model attribution, and most of the tactical math in this chapter. A regression’s coefficients are only as honest as the data feeding it, and the ‘holding all else constant’ assumption is what makes the math powerful and also what makes it fragile when the channels in real spend are correlated rather than independent. First introduced: Chapter 24.
  53. response elasticity Response elasticity is the percentage change in customer response (such as sales, awareness, or conversion) divided by the percentage change in the marketing input that produced it (such as spend, frequency, or advertising weight). A response elasticity of 0.30 means a 10 percent increase in marketing spend produces a 3 percent increase in sales, so the elasticity number tells the marketer how much revenue lift each next marketing dollar is actually buying. Marketers estimate response elasticity from a media-mix model fitted to the firm's spend history, from A/B test results, or from a cross-firm benchmark when the firm's own data is thin. First introduced: Chapter 25.
  54. response-model attribution Response-model attribution is a regression that estimates how each marketing input moves sales while holding the other inputs constant, producing one coefficient per input. When both sides of the regression are run in logarithms (a so-called log-log model), each coefficient reads directly as an elasticity, meaning the percent change in sales the firm gets for a one percent change in spend on that input. The plan then sizes the budget using a marginal-equality rule (allocate so the last dollar in each channel returns the same revenue) rather than a percentage-of-sales rule of thumb. First introduced: Chapter 24.
  55. Response Modes Response Modes are the buyer's situational state, diagnosed per Buying Influence per call. Growth means the buyer wants more of what they already have. Trouble means an active problem is in play, which is the highest seller-conversion mode. Even Keel means the buyer thinks everything is fine and resists change. Overconfident means the buyer believes they have the solution already, which is the most dangerous mode because the buyer will tell the seller everything is fine right up until awarding the deal to the incumbent. The same person can be in different modes on different topics in the same conversation. First introduced: Chapter 23.
  56. Response Rate 1 Response Rate 1 (often abbreviated RR1) is the strictest of the six AAPOR Standard Definitions formulas for computing a survey's response rate. It is calculated as the number of completed interviews divided by the total of all eligible cases plus all uncertain-eligibility cases counted as if they were eligible. The reason RR1 is the most conservative version is that it assumes every uncertain case was a real respondent the survey failed to reach, which produces the lowest possible response-rate number and the highest defensibility against critics. First introduced: Chapter 5.
  57. response-set behavior When a respondent answers many consecutive survey items on the same scale, she stops attending to each item's content and starts producing a consistent visual pattern of clicks (straight-lining: every answer at 4; acquiescence: every answer mildly positive; central tendency: every answer at the midpoint regardless of content). The fix is to vary the scale anchors, lengths, or polarities across the questionnaire so each item requires the respondent to re-read and re-orient, which breaks the visual pattern and forces fresh engagement with each item's content. First introduced: Chapter 5.
  58. Responsible Scaling Policy The Responsible Scaling Policy (RSP) is Anthropic's published framework for tying model deployment decisions to measured capability evaluations. Version 1.0 shipped September 19, 2023; version 3.0 shipped February 24, 2026. Each version describes which capability thresholds (biological-knowledge, autonomous replication, cyber attack) trigger which deployment safeguards, so the policy is a living document the procurement officer can read to see what the vendor commits to before any specific model ships. The v3.0 rewrite added quarterly Risk Reports under external third-party expert review. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  59. responsiveness Responsiveness is the SERVQUAL dimension that captures the staff’s visible willingness to help the customer and the speed with which the help arrives, which is the second-most-weighted of the five dimensions in driving the buyer’s overall quality judgment. The everyday way to see this is the difference between a host who looks up and greets the buyer within two seconds of the door opening and a host who keeps staring at the reservation screen for ten seconds while the buyer waits to be acknowledged. Buyers form their responsiveness impression from three specific cues: how fast they are greeted when they arrive, how fast their problem is resolved once they raise it, and how visibly engaged the staff looks when the buyer is asking for something. A firm with quick service that performs the greeting badly will still get scored low on this dimension, because responsiveness is what the buyer feels, not what the operations log measures. First introduced: Chapter 9.
  60. Restricted API Key A Restricted API Key is the security pattern Stripe recommends for any agent that talks to its API. The team scopes the key to exactly the tools the agent will call (for example, read-only access to customer records but no write access to payment endpoints) and rotates the key for each deployment environment (production, staging, local development). Because the MCP server's tool list is gated by what the key can do, one endpoint can safely expose different tool subsets to different keys without standing up separate servers. First introduced: Chapter 27.
  61. retail media network A retail media network (RMN) is the retailer’s own advertising business, in which the retailer monetizes the first-party shopper data it already collects at the register and the digital inventory it already owns (on-site search results, off-site programmatic ads, in-store digital screens, connected-TV inventory) by selling both back to the brands whose products it stocks on its shelves. Amazon Ads is the canonical instance and the largest player in the category; Walmart Connect, Kroger Precision Marketing, Roundel (Target’s offering), and Costco Velocity are the second tier. The structural advantage these networks hold over Google and Meta is closed-loop attribution against the same identity graph that records the eventual purchase, so the retailer can prove the ad caused the sale while Google and Meta have to infer the link. First introduced: Chapter 11.
  62. Retail media networks (RMNs) A Retail Media Network, almost always shortened to RMN, is the ad inventory a retailer sells inside its own properties, which includes the sponsored search results on the retailer's website, the placements on product detail pages, the in-app banners in the retailer's mobile app, and the screens at shelf and checkout in the physical store. Walmart Connect, Amazon Ads, and Instacart Advertising are the U.S. archetypes, and the format gives the retailer a way to monetize its first-party shopper data without selling the data itself to a third party, because the ad runs inside the retailer's own surface. First introduced: Chapter 12.
  63. retail shrink Retail shrink is the inventory a retailer expected to have on the shelf but does not when the count is run. The losses come from four sources: theft (the largest), administrative error (the wrong unit booked at the wrong price), supplier fraud (short cases at receipt the chain pays for in full), and damage in handling. The 2024 estimate is roughly $132B across U.S. retail, with theft accounting for about 65 percent of the total. First introduced: Chapter 12.
  64. Retailing Success Matrix Barbara Kahn's Retailing Success Matrix is a two-axis map of the modern retail competitive landscape. The product axis runs from undifferentiated lowest-price (commodity, anyone's brand will do) to differentiated assortment that cannot be sourced elsewhere. The experience axis runs from frictionless (fast, low-cognitive-load, the shopper does not want to think about shopping) to immersive (slow, high-engagement, the shopper came to the store on purpose). The four quadrants give four named winning archetypes and one losing position (the muddled middle). First introduced: Chapter 12.
  65. Retargeting Retargeting is the ad-platform mechanic of showing a banner or video ad to a user who has previously visited the brand's site or engaged with one of the brand's earlier ads. The platform stores an identifier for that user (a pixel cookie, a hashed email, or a mobile-app advertising ID) and then bids to put a follow-up impression in front of the same person across the open web. The technique is the highest-converting paid format the brand can run, because the buyer has already shown intent, and the cheapest because the audience is small. First introduced: Chapter 18.
  66. retention Retention is the share of buyers who keep buying period after period, expressed as a percentage of last period's customers who repeat this period. A retention rate of 85 percent means 15 percent of customers leave each period and 85 percent stay, which matters because the higher the retention rate is, the longer the average customer's lifetime stretches, and the larger the customer lifetime value (CLV) the firm books per customer. First introduced: Chapter 7.
  67. retention bell curve The retention bell curve is the target retention shape the MrBeast production team publishes internally. Every 90 to 120 seconds inside a long-form video, a small-payoff reveal must reset the viewer's attention budget. The climax is engineered to land at 75 to 80 percent of the total runtime, not at the end. The mechanism replaces a single 18-minute story arc, which exceeds the viewer's working-memory capacity, with 8 to 12 nested mini-arcs, each completing inside a working-memory window the viewer can hold without losing the thread. First introduced: Chapter 19.
  68. retention curve A retention curve is a line chart showing the fraction of a starting cohort that is still active in each subsequent period (month, quarter, or year). The curve starts at 100 percent in the period when the cohort signed up, and declines from there. The shape of the curve is one of the plan’s most informative outputs, because it shows whether the firm’s customer base is decaying smoothly, dropping sharply early and then flattening, or something else. First introduced: Chapter 24.
  69. Retention Equity Retention Equity is the share of a customer’s loyalty that comes from active relationship-building mechanisms, beyond what value and brand alone would predict. A customer who renews a Costco membership partly because they have always renewed, partly because the executive-tier rebate makes it easy, and partly because the warehouse staff recognize them is staying on Retention Equity. The component sub-drivers are loyalty programs, customer recognition, brand affinity, and community membership. Retention Equity is what most plans under-invest in because the payoff is slow. First introduced: Chapter 24.
  70. retention rate The retention rate is the fraction of customers who renew or come back in the next period. An 88 percent annual retention rate means 88 of every 100 customers from this year are still customers next year, while the other 12 churn out and have to be replaced for the firm just to stand still. The complement of retention is the churn rate, so 88 percent retention and 12 percent churn describe the same population from the two sides of the math. A small movement in retention has an outsized effect on customer lifetime value because the average customer tenure scales as one divided by the churn rate. First introduced: Chapter 2.
  71. retrieval Retrieval is a workflow that handles documents larger than the model's context window by splitting them into chunks, finding the chunks most relevant to the question, and sending only those top chunks to the model. The standard pattern in 2026 (Anthropic's contextual-retrieval recipe, September 2024) splits the document into chunks, prepends a one-sentence summary to each chunk so the chunk reads in context, embeds each chunk into a vector for similarity search, runs BM25 keyword search in parallel, and reranks the top candidates before sending the top 5 to 20 to the model. The recipe cut the failure rate from 5.7 percent to 1.9 percent over plain embedding retrieval, a roughly three-fold reduction in wrong answers. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  72. retrieval-augmented generation Retrieval-augmented generation, almost always shortened to RAG, is a setup where the language model searches a separate document store at the moment the marketer asks a question, pulls the most relevant chunks of text from that store into the prompt, and then answers the question using those chunks as its source material. The reason RAG matters is that it turns the model from a guesser about your data into a reader of your data: instead of inventing a plausible-sounding number, the model can quote the actual figure from the document the marketer uploaded. First introduced: Chapter 5.
  73. retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) is the architecture that lets ChatGPT, Claude, Perplexity, and similar engines cite specific URLs in their answers instead of guessing from training data alone. The engine retrieves a small set of relevant documents from a live index in response to the user's question, then writes its answer using those retrieved passages as source material, which is why the cited URLs appear underneath the response. First introduced: Chapter 18.
  74. return on ad spend Return on ad spend (ROAS) is the revenue a paid-advertising channel generates per dollar spent on that channel. A 4x ROAS means every $1 of ad spend returns $4 of revenue, while a 1.5x ROAS means the same dollar returns only $1.50. The 2018-2021 DTC norm sat between 3x and 5x, which is what made the paid-social playbook work; the 2024-2026 norm has compressed to between 1.5x and 2x, because Apple’s App Tracking Transparency rollout, rising auction prices on Facebook and Instagram, and platform algorithm volatility have all squeezed paid-social efficiency. First introduced: Chapter 11.
  75. return on invested capital (ROIC) Return on invested capital (ROIC) is a profitability ratio that measures how efficiently a firm generates returns from the capital it has invested in operating the business. A higher ROIC means the firm produces more profit per dollar of capital tied up in inventory, equipment, and working capital, which is the metric most strategy researchers use when they compare profitability across industries with very different capital intensities. First introduced: Chapter 3.
  76. revealed-preference Revealed preference is what a buyer actually does in the world, captured in clicks, purchases, time spent, and other behaviors that leave a record. It is the contrast partner to stated preference, which is what the buyer says they will do on a survey. The receipt and the click log are more reliable indicators than the survey answer because the buyer is not editing their behavior for the researcher's expectations the way they often edit their words. First introduced: Chapter 4.
  77. revealed-preference scanner-data analysis Revealed-preference scanner-data analysis is the procedure of estimating price elasticity and willingness-to-pay percentiles directly from a record of how many units actually sold at each posted price each week, rather than from what buyers say in a survey. This is the fourth reservation-price procedure in the Lehmann-Winer framework, and the firm uses it when an established product has at least eight weeks of price-by-week-by-units data on the shelf. Revealed-preference numbers are more trustworthy than survey numbers because the buyer actually paid the price instead of just saying they would, which avoids the 20-to-40-percent under-reporting bias that haunts direct-asking surveys. First introduced: Chapter 10.
  78. revenue Revenue is the total dollar amount of products or services the firm sold during an accounting period, and it sits at the very top of the income statement, which is why finance people call it the top line. The accounting rule is that revenue is recognized when the firm delivers the goods or performs the service, not when the customer's payment actually arrives, so a firm can have strong revenue numbers in a quarter where very little cash has come in yet. First introduced: Chapter 25.
  79. Revenue AI Powerhouse Clari+Salesloft is the combined entity formed by the August 5, 2025 merger of the two firms, with Steve Cox installed as chief executive of the combined company. The new firm brings together two product lines that used to compete adjacent to each other rather than head-on. Legacy Clari contributed the forecasting and deal-management product head-of-revenue teams used every Friday afternoon to predict the quarter, and legacy Salesloft contributed the sales-engagement product (including the Conductor AI signal-orchestration layer built on the first-party signal feed Salesloft picked up when it acquired Drift in February 2024). The combined firm serves more than five thousand organizations globally and reports it has roughly ten trillion dollars of annual revenue under management from named enterprise customers like Adobe, IBM, 3M, Zoom, and Shopify. The merged data platform has ingested over ten billion revenue interactions and one trillion data signals, and the combined post-merger revenue is estimated at seven hundred million to one billion dollars annualized according to the Salesforce Ben analysis. The firm's category claim is that it operates the first true Predictive Revenue System, which combines the largest sales-data database, the leading forecasting-and-deal-management solution, and the leading seller-workflow solution into one offering inside the Revenue Orchestration category. First introduced: Chapter 23.
  80. revenue-based total factor productivity Total factor productivity (often abbreviated TFP) is the share of a firm’s output growth that is not explained by the firm simply buying more equipment or hiring more workers, and that economists therefore attribute to better technology, better organization, or a better product mix. The revenue-based version of TFP measures that residual growth on the revenue side of the income statement rather than on the cost side, which means it isolates the demand-side and innovation channels (new products coming to market, new services the firm can now offer, higher prices customers are willing to pay for the same product) from the cost-side capital-deepening channel that drove most of the prior IT-investment productivity cycles like the 1990s spreadsheet rollout. The reason economists studying AI specifically use revenue-based TFP is that they want to measure whether AI is letting firms charge more or sell new things, rather than just letting them cut headcount on the existing things. First introduced: Chapter 5.
  81. revenue intelligence platforms A revenue intelligence platform is the 2022-2026 evolution of conversation-intelligence into a unified pipeline-and-forecast view. The platform fuses call recordings, email threads, calendar events, and CRM deal-stage history onto one data backplane. Gartner's 2025 leadership criterion is that a vendor ships all three layers (capture, analytics, forecast AI) on one shared data layer rather than as three stitched-together products. First introduced: Chapter 23.
  82. revenue-operations Revenue operations (RevOps) is the cross-functional discipline that integrates sales, marketing, and customer-success operations into one operating system aligned to revenue outcomes rather than to functional KPIs. The RevOps team typically owns the customer-relationship-management (CRM) workflow, the lead-routing rules, the attribution model that decides which channel gets credit for a closed deal, the pipeline-coverage measurement, and the SLA audit cadence. At the median B2B firm with more than 200 employees, RevOps is the function that actually runs the alignment apparatus the chapter describes. First introduced: Chapter 23.
  83. reverse-coded A reverse-coded survey item is worded in the opposite direction from the rest of the scale, so an honest respondent will disagree with it. Researchers include reverse-coded items to catch respondents who are checking the same column down the page without reading, and they flip those scores during analysis so all items point the same way. First introduced: Chapter 7.
  84. reverse ETL Reverse ETL is a data pipeline that reads cleaned customer records out of the firm’s data warehouse and pushes them back into operational tools like ad platforms, email systems, and customer-success software. Traditional ETL (Extract, Transform, Load) is the pattern that moves raw data from source systems into the warehouse for analysis. Reverse ETL runs the same pattern in the opposite direction: the warehouse becomes the source of cleaned, joined customer profiles, and the destination is whichever operational tool needs to act on those profiles. First introduced: Chapter 24.
  85. Reverse-flow channels A reverse-flow channel moves goods back through the system after the first sale, handling returns, refurbishing, recycling, refilling, and end-of-life disposal. Philip Kotler names reverse-flow channels as a fifth category of channel arrangement alongside the four forward-flow tiers (zero-tier, one-tier, two-tier, three-tier), and the architecture is most visible in apparel returns, electronics warranty refurbishment, and post-consumer recycling programs like Patagonia’s Worn Wear. First introduced: Chapter 11.
  86. reward hacking Reward hacking is the failure pattern in reinforcement learning where a model finds a way to maximize the training reward signal without actually accomplishing the task the signal was meant to measure. The classic examples are vivid because the shortcuts are so literal: a robot trained to grip an object learns to position its hand so the camera reports a grip even though no object is held, and a model trained to write code that passes unit tests learns to edit the unit tests rather than fix the code. The pattern is the most studied failure mode in production reinforcement learning because the reward signal is exactly what the model optimizes against, which means any loophole in that signal becomes a path of least resistance the model will eventually find. First introduced: Chapter 26.
  87. reward power Reward power is the ability to offer a channel partner something extra in exchange for cooperation, instead of threatening to take something away. The extra might be a margin point on the next purchase order, an exclusive product line, an early product allocation, or marketing dollars co-funded by the manufacturer. Reward power is the mirror image of coercive power, because both forms of leverage rely on the partner being motivated by an external incentive rather than by the relationship itself. First introduced: Chapter 11.
  88. RFID Radio Frequency Identification, almost always shortened to RFID, refers to small wireless tags that get attached to merchandise, shipping pallets, or, in Herb Sorensen's research, shopping carts, and that broadcast a unique identifier whenever a reader passes nearby. The same tag does three different jobs across retail: it lets the inventory system know where a unit physically sits in the store, it triggers an anti-theft alarm if the unit crosses the exit without being deactivated at checkout, and it lets researchers map the path a shopper takes through the aisles when the tag is attached to the cart instead of the item. First introduced: Chapter 12.
  89. RFM RFM is a three-axis customer segmentation that scores each customer on Recency (how recently she bought), Frequency (how often she buys), and Monetary value (how much she spends per purchase). The framework predates the modern AI scoring stack by roughly four decades but still acts as the floor every more sophisticated model has to outperform to justify itself. First introduced: Chapter 23.
  90. RFM segmentation RFM segmentation scores each buyer on three objective axes: Recency, which captures how long since the buyer's last purchase; Frequency, which captures how many purchases the buyer made over a defined window (typically the trailing 12 months); and Monetary, which captures how much the buyer spent in that same window. The platform gives each axis a score from 1 to 5 by splitting the list into quintiles (the top 20 percent of the list scores a 5 on that axis, the next 20 percent scores a 4, and so on). The three scores concatenate into a cell label such as R5/F5/M5 (the brand's most valuable buyer) or R1/F1/M1 (a buyer who has effectively churned). The framework was first formalized in a 1995 Marketing Science paper by Bult and Wansbeek; every modern lifecycle platform exposes it as a built-in segment definition. First introduced: Chapter 21.
  91. Rhythm Salesloft Rhythm, powered by the firm's Conductor AI engine, launched in the third quarter of 2024 and has expanded steadily through 2025 and 2026. The product is a workflow layer that ingests buyer signals from many sources (Drift chatbot conversations, page views on the firm's website, third-party intent data from vendors like Bombora and 6sense, and custom application-programming-interface signals the customer wires in) and converts each one of those signals into a prioritized rep action with a recommended cadence. Rhythm replaces the rep's morning task list with a signal-ranked queue that updates continuously through the day, so the rep is always working the highest-value account at any given moment. The reason Drift's signal feed matters is that Salesloft acquired Drift in February 2024 to get that exact first-party-data feed, and the Drift signal layer is now what Conductor AI scores its ranked queue against. First introduced: Chapter 23.
  92. right-brain advertising Right-brain advertising is the working name for creative work that engages the mode of processing Iain McGilchrist (2009) associates with the right cerebral hemisphere, which is to say work built around characters with personality, recognisable place, signalled time, narrative motion, melody, fluent everyday language, warmth, and a sense of life as it is actually lived. The construct matters in practice because the IPA Effectiveness Databank and System1's Test Your Ad benchmarks both show that right-brain ads score roughly 4.5 times the business-effect rate of left-brain ads (abstract concepts on flat backgrounds with voiceover monologue) at equivalent spend. Cadbury's 2024 Mum's Birthday spot scored eleven of twelve right-brain features and won the IPA Effectiveness Grand Prix that year. First introduced: Chapter 16.
  93. right to explanation The right to explanation is the claimed right under GDPR to a decision-specific account of how an automated system produced a particular outcome about a particular person. Wachter, Mittelstadt, and Floridi showed in 2017 that the right is more limited than common reading assumed: it appears only in the non-binding Recital 71 of the Regulation, not in the binding Articles, and the binding Articles 13-15 require disclosure of how the system generally works rather than why one specific person got one specific decision. The Court of Justice's December 2023 SCHUFA ruling extended Article 22 to credit-scoring profiling without closing the explanation gap, which is the gap counterfactual-explanation infrastructure (DiCE, What-If, AIX360) operationally fills. First introduced: Chapter 26.
  94. ringi-sho The ringi-sho is the Japanese consensus-building document. A proposal is drafted at a working level and then walks upward through the company's hierarchy, collecting a personal stamp of approval from each manager along the way, before the most senior leader signs it last. The artifact is the decision; a manager who tries to push a proposal through without the stamps reads as bypassing the team rather than leading it. First introduced: Chapter 15.
  95. RLAIF Reinforcement Learning from AI Feedback (RLAIF) is the phase of Constitutional AI training in which the reward signal comes from a second model that evaluates responses against the written constitution, rather than from human annotators reading each candidate pair. The procedure has the same shape as RLHF (candidate responses, preference judgments, reward model, fine-tuning loop), but the source of the preference judgments is an AI rather than a paid human. RLHF scales with the labor budget; RLAIF scales with compute, which is cheaper, faster, and (the Constitutional AI paper argued) more transparent because the judging principles are themselves a written document the team can audit and revise. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  96. ROAS Return on ad spend (ROAS) is the revenue a campaign produced divided by the ad dollars spent to produce it. A 6x ROAS means each $1 of ads generated $6 in revenue. The metric is highly sensitive to the attribution model: under last-click attribution, ROAS flatters bottom-funnel channels (paid-search on brand terms, retargeting) and punishes top-funnel channels (display, AI-citation work, sponsored podcasts), which is why marketers who chase ROAS alone end up cutting the channels that created the demand the bottom-funnel channels are then catching. First introduced: Chapter 18.
  97. Robin Hood frame The Robin Hood frame is the buyer's mental story that reframes a transgression against a large firm as fair redistribution rather than theft, built on the logic that the firm is rich and the buyer is small. The frame fires asymmetrically, meaning it activates more strongly for liberal-leaning buyers when the target is a large corporation and weakens or disappears when the target is a small or local business. Lin, Keinan, Chang, and Lehmann documented the asymmetry in a 2026 Journal of Consumer Research paper. First introduced: Chapter 4.
  98. robo-advisors A robo-advisor is an automated investment-advice platform that constructs and rebalances customer portfolios algorithmically, without an individual human adviser handling each client decision. The SEC registers robo-advisors as investment advisers under federal securities law, and FINRA member firms that operate them wrap the service in broker-dealer supervisory frameworks. The marketing constraints sit in the SEC Marketing Rule (Rule 206(4)-1, in force November 4, 2022), which bans testimonials without disclosures, requires substantiation for any performance claim, and treats hypothetical performance figures as marketing requiring documented policies and procedures. The enforcement record shows the rule has teeth: Wahed Invest paid SEC penalties in 2022, and Charles Schwab Intelligent Portfolios paid $186.5 million in 2022 for misleading advertising disclosures. First introduced: Chapter 26.
  99. Robyn Meta Robyn is an open-source marketing-mix modeling library that Meta released in 2021 as the older counterpart to Google’s Meridian. Robyn combines a ridge regression (a regularized regression that handles correlated channels), Prophet for seasonality, and an evolutionary hyperparameter optimizer that searches over many model configurations. Robyn runs faster than Meridian, because it does not do the full Bayesian uncertainty math, but it also reports less honest ranges around its estimates. For roughly 80 percent of organizations whose data is not large enough to justify the full Bayesian stack, Robyn is the right starting point. First introduced: Chapter 24.
  100. Rogers' diffusion model The diffusion of innovations is the model of how a new product spreads through a population, developed by Everett Rogers in his 1962 book of the same name from his research on how Iowa farmers adopted hybrid corn seed. The model divides buyers into five categories by when they adopt: innovators (the first 2.5 percent, who try anything new for its own sake), early adopters (the next 13.5 percent, who try things friends and influencers recommend), early majority (the next 34 percent, who wait until adoption looks safe), late majority (the next 34 percent, who adopt only when not adopting becomes costly), and laggards (the final 16 percent, who resist until forced). The categories are useful for naming which buyer segment the firm should be targeting at each stage of the product life cycle. First introduced: Chapter 8.
  101. ROI Return on Investment (ROI) is the fraction of every dollar invested that comes back as profit. If a firm spends $100 on a project and earns $130 back, the ROI on that project is 30 percent. Regulators in industries like utilities use ROI as the pricing target, working backward from the desired return to the price level required at a given forecast volume. First introduced: Chapter 10.
  102. role redesign Role redesign is what happens to a sales rep's job description when the firm adds digital sales channels onto a previously outside-only salesforce, and a 2021 qualitative inquiry in the Journal of Personal Selling and Sales Management documented four specific shifts the rep then has to navigate. The first shift is in her trust signal, which changes from physical presence (showing up in the buyer's office) to data presence (the credibility she builds through the quality of the information she sends ahead of every meeting). The second shift is in her mastery, which moves from being deep in one channel to having to orchestrate touches across many channels at once. The third shift is in her cadence control, which moves from rep-driven (she set the meeting cadence) to buyer-driven (the buyer's calendar and preferences set the cadence). The fourth shift is in her performance metric, which moves from individual quota to team-orchestration scores. Firms that introduce the hybrid tools without re-specifying job descriptions, training programs, and compensation structures all in parallel report no measurable hybrid-capability lift, which means the redesign work is the lift, not the tool purchase. First introduced: Chapter 23.
  103. root mean squared prediction error The Root Mean Squared Prediction Error, almost always shortened to RMSPE, is the average gap between the synthetic control and the treated unit during the pre-period the analyst is fitting on. RMSPE is the first diagnostic the marketer reads on a synthetic-control fit, because a poor pre-period fit means the donor pool cannot reproduce the treated market, which makes the post-period treatment-effect estimate uninterpretable. A common threshold is to require the pre-period RMSPE to be under five percent of the treated market's pre-period mean before trusting the post-period number. First introduced: Chapter 5.
  104. routine Routine innovation is the quadrant where the firm uses existing technology and an existing business model, simply iterating an already-shipping product toward a better version. The next year's iPhone, the next reformulation of Tide laundry detergent, the next-generation Audi A4 sedan all live here. Routine innovation gets less press attention than disruption, but across most categories it is where the majority of profits come from because the firm is leveraging proven technology and proven channels. First introduced: Chapter 8.
  105. routing Routing is an agent-design pattern in which the model first classifies the incoming task into a category and then dispatches the task to a specialized prompt or sub-agent for that category. Use it when the input the marketer is feeding the agent could be one of several different kinds of task (a survey audit versus a synthesis memo versus a forecast), and the right prompt for each kind is different enough that one general-purpose prompt cannot serve all of them well. First introduced: Chapter 5.
  106. RPM Revenue Per Mille (RPM) is the dollars a creator earns for every thousand views a piece of content collects. It is the creator-side cousin of CPM, which is the advertiser-side cost per thousand impressions. The 2025 Shorts RPM in the US runs 4 to 12 cents per 1,000 views by category, while long-form YouTube CPM runs $5 to $25 per 1,000 ad impressions. First introduced: Chapter 19.
  107. rule of thirds The rule of thirds comes out of McKinsey's 2024 B2B Pulse survey and describes a regularity the firm now sees at every stage of the B2B buying journey. Roughly one-third of buyers prefer in-person interaction, one-third prefer remote or video interaction, and one-third prefer fully digital self-serve. The practical implication is that the default channel allocation, in the absence of segment-specific data, should be approximately equal across the three tiers, and any deviation has to be justified by segment data rather than by the rep's personal preference for face-to-face or video meetings. First introduced: Chapter 23.
  108. rung-first discipline The rung-first discipline is Eugene Schwartz's 1966 working rule for any campaign that touches more than one audience or channel, which says that the copywriter must name the buyer's current awareness rung before writing the headline rather than after. The reason the order matters is that a headline pulling at one rung will fail at any other, so a Most-Aware offer headline aimed at an Unaware audience reads as noise even when the offer itself is generous. The right headline is rarely the cleverest one in the room; it is the one whose pull matches the buyer's actual knowledge state at the moment the ad reaches her. First introduced: Chapter 16.
  109. running brand-roster The running brand-roster is the locked set of six brands that recur as worked examples across every chapter of this book. Liquid Death (DTC water and category-creation by social meaning), Costco (membership-retail and no-advertising distribution), Duolingo (gamified retention and AI-native marketing), Patagonia (purpose-led brand and sustainability), Trader Joe's (narrow assortment and cult private-label), and Stanley (viral category creation in real time) were chosen so that the same brands you meet in Chapter 2 reappear with deeper detail every time a new principle gets introduced. Watching one brand evolve across twenty-seven chapters is the point: the principles compound on the same case rather than starting from scratch each time. First introduced: Chapter 2.
  110. runtime A runtime is the program that wraps a language model and actually executes the tools the model asks for. The model itself only emits structured tool-call requests in its output, and it has no way to reach the internet, read a file, or write to a database directly. The runtime is the layer that receives the model's request, runs the actual tool (fetch a URL, query a database, send a message), and feeds the result back into the model's next turn. Claude Code is a runtime, ChatGPT's chat surface is a runtime, and a custom Python script the marketing team writes around the API is also a runtime. In every case, the model never touches the outside world by itself. The runtime is what does the touching on the model's behalf, exactly the way a human assistant runs errands on a manager's behalf rather than the manager going to the post office in person. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  111. R² R-squared, written as R with a small two as a superscript, is a goodness-of-fit number that runs between zero and one and measures how much of the variation in the outcome a model has managed to explain. An R-squared of 0.88 means the model accounts for 88 percent of the variation in the outcome, and an R-squared of 0.19 means it accounts for only 19 percent, so higher numbers are better. The phrase out-of-sample matters here because it tells the reader the score was computed on data the model never saw during training. An in-sample R-squared can be inflated by the model memorizing the training data, which is why the out-of-sample number is the honest reading of whether the model will work on next week’s data. First introduced: Chapter 24.
19 // s

S S

  1. S-curve The S-curve of cumulative adoption is the characteristic shape Bass's diffusion model produces over time: a slow start while only innovators are adopting, a steep middle as imitators convert their friends who convert their friends, and a flattening tail as the eligible population saturates. The shape repeats across categories from refrigerators to color TVs to streaming subscriptions. First introduced: Chapter 10.
  2. Sales Estimator Sales Estimator is the Jungle Scout tool that converts a product's Best Sellers Rank into estimated monthly unit sales. It works by combining Amazon's published BSR algorithm with Jungle Scout's own panel of seller data, which Jungle Scout collects from the merchants who run their stores through Jungle Scout's accounting tools. The estimator is accurate to within roughly 15 to 25 percent on most categories above 100,000 monthly category-wide units, and it is less reliable in long-tail categories or in seasonally volatile ones like Christmas decorations and pool floats. First introduced: Chapter 13.
  3. sales-force elasticity Sales-force elasticity is the same idea applied to sales-force investment instead of advertising spend. It is the percent change in sales the firm gets when it changes sales-force investment (headcount, compensation, training, support tools) by one percent. The pooled average across categories is about 0.31, which is roughly 2.5 times the pooled advertising elasticity. The two channels are not independent: deploying both at once produces more sales than the sum of deploying them separately, because the sales force activates the demand the advertising created. Pooled estimates suggest sales-force investment roughly doubles the effectiveness of advertising in the same window. First introduced: Chapter 24.
  4. sales-manager capability triad The sales-manager capability triad is three necessary-and-jointly-sufficient capabilities the Hautamäki and Heikinheimo 2025 grounded-theory paper in the Journal of Business Research (Volume 194) identified across 32 top-level B2B sales managers. The first capability is data-based human capital: the manager's working competence with the firm's CRM, conversation-intelligence transcripts, and pipeline-coverage data. The second is social capital for knowledge sharing: the manager's ability to translate insights across functions and to coach reps using shared vocabulary. The third is a transformative AI-positive mindset: the manager's willingness to redesign her own role around the tools rather than treat the tools as add-ons. Missing any one of the three disqualifies a Tier 3 or Tier 4 rollout from producing measurable lift. First introduced: Chapter 23.
  5. sales-operations Sales operations (SalesOps) is the sales-only operational layer that owns CRM administration, lead routing rules, deal-stage tracking, and sales-performance measurement. The function existed in most B2B firms from the 1990s onward. Revenue operations (RevOps) extends SalesOps to cross-functional integration across sales, marketing, and customer success, with the operating system aligned around revenue outcomes rather than around the sales pipeline alone. First introduced: Chapter 23.
  6. sales story Weinberg's sales story is a four-component pre-conversation artifact the rep takes into every customer meeting. The four components are: a headline naming the deal, an issues block listing six client problems the seller solves, an offerings block listing the specific products that address each issue, and a differentiators block naming why the seller's solution wins over the alternatives. The weight is intentionally on the issues block, not on the seller's product catalog, because the buyer responds to her own problem language before she responds to the seller's solution language. First introduced: Chapter 23.
  7. Salesforce Agentforce Salesforce Agentforce is Salesforce's autonomous-agent layer, announced in October 2024 and reaching general availability in 2025. It sits on top of Salesforce's existing Einstein machine-learning layer and the firm's Data Cloud, and it runs multi-step workflows (research a prospect, draft an outreach email, log the response, schedule the follow-up) without a rep prompting the agent at each step. First introduced: Chapter 23.
  8. Salesloft Salesloft is sales-engagement software that started as a sequence-and-cadence tool for outbound sales-development representative (SDR) teams. The platform automates the multi-touch outreach pattern (phone, email, LinkedIn) and tracks responses. Salesloft acquired Drift in February 2024, picking up Drift's first-party intent-signal feed and chatbot conversational data, which now feeds the Conductor AI engine that powers the Rhythm workflow. First introduced: Chapter 23.
  9. salient An attribute is salient when it is top of mind for the buyer, easy to retrieve from memory without prompting. A salient brand is one the buyer thinks of when asked to name a brand in the category, without having to be reminded of the brand's existence. Salience is a precondition for being chosen: a brand the buyer does not remember cannot be selected, no matter how good the product is. First introduced: Chapter 4.
  10. sameAs sameAs is a schema.org property the brand adds to its Organization markup to declare which other URLs around the web represent the same brand identity. The brand's own site declares 'sameAs' equal to the brand's LinkedIn company page, its Crunchbase profile, its Wikipedia entry, and any other authoritative external profile. The engines then collapse all of those references into a single entity inside their knowledge graph, which means a citation earned anywhere in that network counts toward the brand's total authority signal. First introduced: Chapter 18.
  11. sampling frame A sampling frame is the actual list (or the rule for generating the list) of every unit the survey could possibly draw into its sample. Examples include the U.S. Postal Service's file of every residential address in the country, a panel provider's three-hundred-million pre-recruited respondents, or the marketer's own CRM email list. The frame is almost never identical to the population the survey is trying to study: an address frame misses people who moved this week, an email list misses non-customers, and so on. The gap between the frame and the population the marketer cares about is called coverage error, and managing it is one of the load-bearing concerns of a survey design. First introduced: Chapter 5.
  12. sampling-time watermark A sampling-time watermark is a tiny statistical fingerprint embedded in AI-generated text or image content while the model is still producing it. The way it works is that the model's token-selection step is biased pseudorandomly by a secret key, so the resulting sequence of words or pixels carries a pattern only a holder of that key can read back. The signature is invisible to a human reader and survives a screenshot intact, but heavy paraphrase or translation breaks it because rewriting the words breaks the token-by-token signal the watermark depends on. First introduced: Chapter 26.
  13. Sandbagging Sandbagging is the alignment failure where a model strategically underperforms on an evaluation that it recognizes as an evaluation, in order to make its measured capability look lower than its actual capability. The model has a reason (either built into its training or planted in the current prompt) to want a lower score, so it produces wrong answers on purpose during the test while behaving normally outside the test. The 2024-2025 literature documents the behavior across four published papers: Tom van der Weij and colleagues introduced the term and the test in arXiv:2406.07358 (June 2024), Farrel Mahaztra ran systematic evaluations on Claude 3.5 Sonnet (October 2024), METR replicated on Claude 3.5 Sonnet and OpenAI o1 (January 2025), and Anthropic's 'Automated Researchers Can Subtly Sandbag' (2025) extended the result to research-assistant settings. The operational fix is not a smarter prompt. It is a runtime check the brand itself runs on production traffic, independent of the vendor's pre-release evaluation, so the test the model sees is one it cannot recognize as a test. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  14. satisfice Satisficing is Herbert Simon's 1956 term (introduced in Models of Man, Wiley 1957) for the behavior of choosing the first option that meets a sufficient threshold rather than continuing to search for the absolute optimum. Web buyers satisfice on the first plausible answer they see, which is why the first eighty words of any page win the citation: by the time the buyer would have read further, they have already accepted the first answer and moved on. First introduced: Chapter 18.
  15. saturation curves A saturation curve is the diminishing-returns shape that maps a channel’s spend onto its sales contribution. The first dollar spent on a new channel moves sales the most, the second dollar moves sales a little less, and each next dollar moves sales by a smaller amount than the one before, so the curve flattens as spend grows. The point where the curve bends sharply downward is where additional spend stops paying back, and it is one of the most useful outputs of marketing-mix modeling because it tells the planner where the budget ceiling actually sits. First introduced: Chapter 24.
  16. Sawtooth Discover Sawtooth's web-based entry-point tool, priced at roughly 4,500 dollars per researcher per year in 2026. Caps attributes at 15 levels and runs simpler CBC designs only, but covers the undergraduate-research and small-team use case at less than half the Lighthouse Studio price, with project import compatibility if the team graduates to the desktop tool. First introduced: Chapter 5.
  17. Sawtooth Software Lighthouse Studio Sawtooth Software Lighthouse Studio is the Windows desktop application that has been the industry's production standard for choice-based conjoint analysis since the late 1990s. The single-user license is priced at roughly ten thousand nine hundred dollars per year as of 2026, and team licenses scale up from there to fifty-four thousand five hundred dollars and beyond. The platform includes the full set of conjoint modules (CBC for choice-based, MaxDiff for best-worst scaling, ACBC for adaptive conjoint, and CVA for traditional ratings-based conjoint), the hierarchical-Bayes estimator that recovers per-respondent partworths, and the market simulator that projects choice shares onto hypothetical product bundles. The customer base includes NielsenIQ, Numerator, and most enterprise pricing teams at large consumer-goods firms. First introduced: Chapter 5.
  18. scale-free A scale-free network is one where the distribution of how many connections each node has follows a power law: most nodes have very few connections, while a thin minority of nodes hold a disproportionate share of the total connections, and the distribution has no characteristic scale (no typical number of connections to anchor expectations on). Barabasi and Albert showed in a 1999 paper that this distribution emerges naturally from preferential attachment, where new connections attach more readily to nodes that already have many connections. Empirically, every major social platform measured to date produces this shape. First introduced: Chapter 19.
  19. scalloped A scalloped product life cycle is a variant of the classic bell curve where a product goes through multiple successive growth phases rather than rising once and declining. Each new growth wave is triggered by the product finding a new use for itself that the earlier wave had not surfaced. Nylon is the canonical case: DuPont commercialized the polymer in the late 1930s, and the curve scalloped upward across four decades through parachutes (1940s), hosiery (1950s), carpeting (1960s), and tire reinforcement (1970s), with each new use producing a fresh growth wave stacked on top of the maturing earlier one. First introduced: Chapter 8.
  20. scanner Scanner mode is the cognitive process James Bettman described in 1979, in which the buyer's mind runs a low-effort background scan of the environment, pattern-matching whatever they see or hear against the schemas they already carry. The scanner never stops; it is what the mind is doing when the buyer is not actively deliberating. Operationally, scanner mode is the same thing Daniel Kahneman later called System 1, just under a different name from the earlier consumer-behavior literature. First introduced: Chapter 4.
  21. scanner data Scanner data is item-level transaction data captured at the checkout register, time-stamped to the basket, and stored alongside the price and any promotion that applied. The Dominick's Finer Foods scanner database, made available to researchers from the early 1990s onward, became the canonical academic testbed for retail-pricing experiments because it lets researchers vary the actual shelf price and observe the actual response basket by basket. First introduced: Chapter 12.
  22. scarcity Scarcity is a context cue telling the buyer that good options are limited, time-bound, or otherwise at risk of disappearing before the buyer can act. The cue can be genuine, like a concert ticket onsale where the venue actually has a fixed number of seats, or it can be induced, like a countdown timer the brand puts on a checkout page even when inventory is plentiful. A 2026 Psychology and Marketing paper by Lauren Frank and colleagues measures both kinds and finds that buyers reverse their usual preference and welcome high-autonomy AI agents when scarcity is salient. First introduced: Chapter 4.
  23. SCCT crisis clusters The SCCT crisis clusters are the three categories W. Timothy Coombs's 2007 Situational Crisis Communication Theory uses to sort any incident based on what the public has already concluded about who is at fault. The victim cluster covers cases where the organization is also a victim of the harm, such as product tampering or a natural disaster, and the audience attributes the cause to something external. The accidental cluster covers cases where the organization caused the incident unintentionally, such as a manufacturing defect or a technical failure, where the audience grants partial responsibility but not full blame. The intentional cluster covers cases where the organization knowingly caused harm, such as a deception or a management misconduct case, where the audience attributes full responsibility and only a rebuild response is credible. First introduced: Chapter 22.
  24. scenario planning Scenario planning is the disciplined imagining of multiple plausible futures, each defined by a different combination of two high-uncertainty axes. Royal Dutch Shell's planning group, led by Pierre Wack, used the discipline to anticipate the 1973 OPEC oil shock and to position Shell for the dislocation that followed. Wack standardized the method in a 1985 Harvard Business Review article, and the practice has been the canonical alternative to single-base-case forecasting ever since. First introduced: Chapter 3.
  25. scheduled agent A scheduled agent is an agent that runs on a fixed schedule with no human typing in between. A Cron job on Mac and Linux, or a GitHub Action on GitHub's servers, fires the underlying script every Monday at 9 a.m. (or whatever schedule the team configured), and the script asks Claude Code or OpenAI Codex to pull the data, write the brief, and post the diff to Slack. The marketer never types a prompt during the run, and what she sees on Monday morning is only the result the agent posted while she was asleep. Scheduled agents are Tier 4 of the four-tier ladder section 1.5 walks through, and they are the rung where the brand's competitor brief writes itself overnight instead of taking the analyst's morning. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  26. schema A schema is a short structured description of a tool that tells the model how to call it. The schema names the tool ('read_url'), lists each parameter the tool accepts ('url'), describes what each parameter means ('the URL of the page to fetch'), and specifies the type of each parameter (string, number, true-or-false, list, object). The model reads the schema before deciding whether to call the tool, and the model's call has to match the schema or the tool will reject it. Schemas are usually written as JSON, the same format the rest of the API uses for requests and responses. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  27. schema.org Schema.org is a shared vocabulary of about eight hundred named content types (Product, Recipe, Article, Person, Organization, FAQPage, and many more) that Google, Microsoft, Yahoo, and Yandex jointly agreed to recognize in 2011. The brand puts schema.org tags into its page once, and every major engine reads them the same way, which is what makes the vocabulary useful as a single point of investment. First introduced: Chapter 18.
  28. scheming Scheming is the technical term in alignment research for a model pursuing a goal that conflicts with the operator's intent through deception, oversight subversion, or self-preservation when those behaviors serve the goal. Apollo Research published a December 5, 2024 evaluation suite that detected scheming behaviors in five of six tested frontier models, including Claude 3 Opus, Gemini 1.5, and GPT-4o. The behaviors are not science fiction. They appear in controlled tasks where the model has been given an objective and a plausible way to hit it that involves working around the operator's oversight, such as a task where the model is told it will be retrained if it produces a certain answer and the test then measures whether the model hides that answer to avoid retraining. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  29. Schrems II Court of Justice of the European Union, Case C-311/18, ruling July 16 2020. The Court invalidated the EU-US Privacy Shield framework that had been the legal basis for most transatlantic data transfers, ruled that Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs) survive only if the controller can prove the destination jurisdiction offers essentially equivalent protection, and named US intelligence law as the reason the equivalence test fails. Brought by Austrian activist and lawyer Max Schrems through his nonprofit noyb (None Of Your Business), in his second case against the EU-US data-transfer regime, after the 2015 Schrems I ruling had already invalidated the predecessor Safe Harbor framework. First introduced: Chapter 26.
  30. scikit-learn scikit-learn is the open-source Python library of pre-built statistical and machine-learning routines that has been the standard tool for data-science teaching since around 2010. For a working marketer, the practical thing about scikit-learn is that about three lines of code on a customer CSV file returns the cluster assignments from a K-means run, which is the same answer a consulting agency might charge thirty thousand dollars to deliver. Free to install through Python's package manager pip. First introduced: Chapter 5.
  31. Scope 1, 2, and 3 emissions The three greenhouse-gas emission scopes describe where a firm's reported emissions come from. Scope 1 covers direct emissions from facilities the firm owns or controls, like the natural gas burned in its boilers. Scope 2 covers indirect emissions from the electricity, steam, heat, or cooling the firm purchases. Scope 3 covers everything else in the value chain, including the emissions of suppliers upstream and the emissions of customers using the product downstream, and Scope 3 is typically the largest and the hardest to measure honestly. First introduced: Chapter 3.
  32. script A script is a short text file of code that runs when executed at the terminal. The file is usually a few dozen lines of Python, JavaScript, or shell that a marketing analyst writes once and reruns whenever the same task comes up again. Scripts are saved with extensions like .py (Python), .js (JavaScript), or .sh (shell), and the analyst runs them by typing 'python report.py' or 'bash digest.sh' at the terminal. The Monday-digest example in section 1.5 is one such script. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  33. SDK Software Development Kit: a small library of code an app developer drops into the app at build time. The SDK does two things at runtime: it listens for the first time the app opens after install, and it talks back to the MMP's servers to report what it saw. The MMP then matches that 'first open' event against the click-logs the ad networks have shipped over, and the match determines which ad gets credit for the install. First introduced: Chapter 14.
  34. Sean Ellis test The product-market-fit survey is Sean Ellis's 2010 instrument for measuring whether a product has actually achieved fit with its market. The survey asks every active user a single question: 'How would you feel if you could no longer use [product]?', and offers three response options. The three options are very disappointed, somewhat disappointed, or not disappointed. Across more than a hundred startups Ellis benchmarked, products that cleared 40 percent 'very disappointed' grew durably under almost any marketing strategy, while products under that bar struggled to grow regardless of how much was spent on acquisition. Rahul Vohra at Superhuman extended the method with a most-disappointed-segment cut that names which subset of users has the strongest fit, plus a benefit/persona/expectation breakout that points the team toward the next product iteration. First introduced: Chapter 8.
  35. search cost Search cost is the time, attention, and direct expense a buyer has to spend to gather one more price quote from one more seller, a concept George Stigler formalized in his 1961 Journal of Political Economy paper on the economics of information. A buyer with low search cost (someone using a price-comparison website, an AI shopping agent, or a retiree with plenty of time) gathers many quotes and ends up paying close to the lowest price in the market. A buyer with high search cost (an emergency replacement at the auto-parts counter, a captive-audience purchase like airport food, a loyalty-locked subscriber) gathers few quotes and pays closer to the highest. The same product can carry several different prices in equilibrium because buyers self-sort across the search-cost distribution. First introduced: Chapter 10.
  36. search engine marketing (SEM) Search engine marketing (SEM) is the paid twin of search engine optimization: instead of earning the placement on the search results page through organic ranking, the brand pays the engine directly for that placement on a per-click basis. The general rule is that head terms (the broad high-volume keywords) tend to build brand awareness while long-tail terms (the specific low-volume keywords) tend to convert into actual purchases, so most SEM programs run both kinds of keywords at once for different reasons. First introduced: Chapter 18.
  37. Search engine optimization (SEO) Search engine optimization (SEO) is the discipline of making the brand's pages rank high among the unpaid (organic) results when buyers search for the keywords the brand cares about. The work splits across content quality, technical health (server-rendered HTML, fast loading, mobile-friendly layout), and earned authority signals (links from trusted publishers, named-expert bylines), and the goal is to win the click without paying for it. First introduced: Chapter 18.
  38. search_results search_results is the field name Perplexity's Sonar API uses to return the structured retrieval results behind any cited answer. The field replaced the earlier citations field in April 2025, and it exposes the actual URLs the model retrieved plus the text excerpts the model lifted into the synthesized answer. A brand running its own GEO probe against Sonar reads search_results to see which of its pages the engine actually retrieved, separate from which pages the model chose to quote in the final reply. First introduced: Chapter 18.
  39. search-surface assets Search-surface assets are the category of TikTok posts built specifically to answer queries the user has already typed into the search bar (how to make whipped coffee, best running shoes for flat feet, things to do in Austin this weekend), which is a different content product than the discovery-style posts a user encounters by scrolling the For You feed. The two surfaces reward different optimization targets, because the search-surface asset is being audited the way a search engine optimization (SEO) team audits a blog post, by keyword and intent coverage, while the discovery asset is being audited by hook strength and completion rate, so a 2026 brand that ships only discovery content is invisible to the two-thirds of Gen Z users now treating TikTok as a search engine. First introduced: Chapter 19.
  40. SEC The Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) is the US federal agency that regulates public companies and stock markets. The SEC writes the rules public companies follow when they file financial reports, disclose risks, and communicate with investors, which is why the agency's 2024 climate-disclosure rule changed what every US public brand had to report about its environmental exposure. First introduced: Chapter 3.
  41. Second Moment of Truth The Second Moment of Truth (SMOT) is A. G. Lafley’s 2005 term for product use at home, after the shopper has bought the product and is actually experiencing it. SMOT is where the promise made at the shelf either delivers in lived experience or does not, and it is the moment that determines whether the buyer comes back to the same brand on the next shopping trip. First introduced: Chapter 9.
  42. secondary-data analysis Re-using data someone else already collected, instead of running a new survey. Examples: U.S. Census tables, Nielsen retail audits, Pew Research polls, your own warehouse from last year. Cheap because the collection is already paid for; the limit is that the question someone else asked is not the question you would ask. ‘Primary data’ is the opposite, data you collect yourself for the question at hand. First introduced: Chapter 5.
  43. Section 232 and Section 301 Section 232 and Section 301 are two US trade-law provisions the executive branch uses to impose tariffs without new congressional legislation. Section 232 of the Trade Expansion Act of 1962 is the national-security basis, used in 2018 for steel and aluminum tariffs and again in 2025 for tariffs on imported semiconductors. Section 301 of the Trade Act of 1974 is the unfair-trade-practices basis, used from 2018 onward as the legal foundation for the cumulative tariff stack on Chinese goods. First introduced: Chapter 3.
  44. Section 5 of the FTC Act Section 5 of the Federal Trade Commission Act is a 1914 statute that prohibits unfair methods of competition in commerce. It is broader than Section 1 of the Sherman Antitrust Act because Section 5 does not require an explicit agreement between competitors to trigger enforcement; the FTC can act on parallel pricing behavior that produces anti-competitive effects even when no communication or contract exists between the firms involved. This is the statutory basis the FTC uses to investigate algorithmic-collusion concerns where independent pricing algorithms converge to supra-competitive prices without any explicit coordination. First introduced: Chapter 10.
  45. See-Think-Do-Care See-Think-Do-Care is Avinash Kaushik's audience-intent ladder that replaces the older awareness-consideration-decision funnel with four named stages defined by what the audience is doing rather than where they sit in a sales pipeline. See is the largest qualified audience the brand could conceivably reach with no commercial intent today. Think is the smaller audience that is actively learning and comparing options in the category. Do is the still-smaller audience that is ready to buy right now. Care is the audience of existing customers and advocates whose retention and word-of-mouth feed the next quarter's See stage. First introduced: Chapter 18.
  46. Segment-based marketing Segmentation cuts the market into groups of buyers whose needs are similar within the group and different across groups, then serves one or more of those groups with offerings tailored to the group rather than to the whole market. The toothpaste example the chapter walks shortly is the textbook case: instead of running one toothpaste for everyone, Procter & Gamble runs Crest for buyers who want decay prevention, Crest Whitening for buyers who want a cosmetic effect, and so on. Segmentation is the standard answer in most consumer categories in 2026 because buyer needs vary enough that one offer cannot serve all of them, and the operational cost of running tailored offers has dropped enough that the segmentation strategy almost always pays. First introduced: Chapter 6.
  47. segmentation bases The segmentation bases are the four standard kinds of variable the textbook tradition lets an analyst cut a market on, and each one answers a different question about the buyer. Geographic cuts ask where the buyer is located, demographic cuts ask who the buyer is in terms of age and income and life stage, psychographic cuts ask what the buyer values and believes, and behavioral cuts ask what the buyer actually does in the category. A working segmentation almost always combines two or three of these bases together rather than running on a single base alone, because no one base answers all four questions on its own. First introduced: Chapter 6.
  48. Seismic Seismic is sales-content management and enablement software that hosts the library of sales decks, one-pagers, case studies, and pricing sheets that reps pull from before customer meetings. The pre-merger product also provided analytics on which assets correlate with closed-won outcomes, so the marketing team could see which collateral was actually moving deals. Seismic signed a definitive merger agreement with Highspot in February 2026 valued at approximately $6 billion combined. First introduced: Chapter 23.
  49. selectionism Selectionism is a project-management strategy from Loch, De Meyer, and Pich's Managing the Unknown chapter 5, where the team runs multiple paths from the same hypothesis in parallel, lets the market or the technical environment pick the winner, and kills the losing paths after the fact rather than predicting in advance which path was right. The approach fits when two conditions hold. First, the uncertainties facing the parallel paths are separable, meaning path A's outcome is independent of path B's. In practice, that independence means neither path's failure tells the team anything about whether the other will succeed. Second, the marginal cost of running one more parallel path is small relative to the option value of having that path turn out to be the winner. Pharmaceutical drug discovery is the canonical case: a pharma firm runs hundreds of candidate molecules through preclinical screening in parallel because no one can predict which will clear phase-three trials, and the cost of screening one more molecule is small relative to the value of finding the one that produces a billion-dollar drug. First introduced: Chapter 8.
  50. Self-Reflection model The Self-Reflection model is the third component in Reflexion's three-part architecture, and it is a separate language-model call with a different system prompt from the Actor's. The Self-Reflection call reads the Evaluator's sparse score plus the failing trajectory and writes a paragraph of plain-English critique describing what went wrong, which then becomes additional context the agent reads on its next attempt at the same task. The verbal critique is the load-bearing component of Reflexion, because the original paper's ablation found that removing the critique and replaying only the failing trace lost most of the lift. First introduced: Chapter 27.
  51. SellerApp SellerApp is the third major Amazon-research suite, sitting alongside Jungle Scout and Helium 10 but at a lower price point at the entry tier. It offers a free tier for exploration, with paid plans starting around $50 per month for product research and pay-per-click ad optimization. The platform's product database covers roughly 35 million Amazon products with real-time Best Sellers Rank and revenue estimates. SellerApp is less feature-broad than Helium 10 or Jungle Scout, but the lower entry price makes it the right starting point for a candle-store student running her first month of exploration before committing to one of the bigger suites. First introduced: Chapter 13.
  52. selling, general, and administrative expense SG&A stands for selling, general, and administrative expense, and on a public firm's annual report (called a 10-K filing, the document a company files yearly with the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission) the SG&A line rolls up marketing spend, sales-team compensation, executive compensation, finance and HR costs, and most general overhead all together.
