On many firms' public filings the marketing budget is not separately disclosed because it is buried inside this single SG&A line, which is part of why marketers have to defend their spend internally instead of letting an outside investor calculate it for them. First introduced: Chapter 25.
  53. semantic-modeling-language A semantic-modeling language is a constrained vocabulary of metrics, dimensions, and joins the data team writes ahead of time so the language model is only allowed to compose queries from items on a pre-approved menu rather than from raw SQL. The agent never sees the underlying database directly; it asks for combinations of named metrics (revenue, conversion rate, monthly active users) and named dimensions (region, channel, customer cohort), and the platform translates the request into safe SQL underneath. This is the architectural move that took ThoughtSpot's Spotter from roughly twenty percent pass rate on real ad-hoc business questions in 2023 to seventy-to-eighty percent in February 2025, and it is the gate every chat-to-SQL agent in production needs. First introduced: Chapter 27.
  54. semantic search Semantic search is the retrieval method that ranks documents by meaning rather than by keyword overlap. The system converts each document into a numerical fingerprint (an embedding vector) that captures what the document is about, then matches the query's fingerprint to the closest document fingerprints. The result is that a query like 'small-team sales software' can match a document titled 'CRM for 20-person teams' even when the two share almost no exact words. First introduced: Chapter 18.
  55. Semantic Similarity Rating (SSR) Semantic Similarity Rating (SSR) is a method for turning a language model's free-text response into a number on a survey rating scale (such as a 1-to-7 Likert agreement scale) by measuring how close the model's answer is, in a sentence-embedding space, to a fixed set of reference texts that anchor each scale point. The reference text for ‘1’ might be ‘I strongly disagree with this statement,’ the reference for ‘7’ might be ‘I strongly agree,’ and the model's response gets the rating of whichever reference it is closest to. Compared to asking the model to pick a number directly, SSR widens the variance of the synthetic distribution because the model is allowed to express itself in free text rather than being squeezed into the seven discrete bins where it tends to pile up. First introduced: Chapter 5.
  56. semiglobalization Ghemawat's argument that the world is neither fully globalized nor fully national, but mostly regional. Roughly 90 percent of fixed investment stays within the firm's home country, and most cross-border activity clusters in regional blocs (EU-EU, North-America-internal, ASEAN-internal). The implication: pick the regional cluster first, then pick a country inside it. First introduced: Chapter 15.
  57. send-time optimization Send-time optimization is the practice of sending each individual buyer a message at the time of day that buyer is most likely to open it, rather than picking one universal ‘best time’ for the whole list. The platform learns each recipient’s personal open pattern by watching when that recipient has opened past messages, builds a small model from those observations, and then schedules the next message to arrive in that recipient’s personal best window. So one buyer might receive the message at Tuesday 7 a.m. on a commute, while another buyer on the same list receives the same message at Sunday 9 p.m. on the couch. Klaviyo brands the feature as Smart Send Time, Iterable calls it Send Time Optimization, and Mailchimp uses the shorthand STO, but the mechanism is the same across all three. The typical reported lift on click-through rate runs between 5 and 15 percent over a single-time batch send, which is essentially free revenue because the platform is moving the existing message into a better slot rather than asking the brand to write new copy. First introduced: Chapter 21.
  58. SENSE The Strategic Experiential Modules (SEMs) are the five different kinds of experience a brand can compete on in the canonical experiential-marketing framework published by Bernd Schmitt in 1999. The five are SENSE (the buyer's senses), FEEL (mood and emotion), THINK (curiosity and problem-solving), ACT (behavior and lifestyle), and RELATE (social identity). When two brands have reached functional parity on the underlying product, the SEM axes are where they actually compete. First introduced: Chapter 4.
  59. sensors Sensors are the canonical-agent-framework name for the data an agent perceives during its run. For a Claude Code agent operating in 2026, the sensors are the files it is permitted to read, the URLs it is permitted to fetch, and the API responses it receives from tool calls. The marketer specifies this list explicitly before deployment, because an agent allowed to read everything will exhaust its budget reading irrelevant material, and an agent allowed to read nothing will fail at the first step. The right sensor list is the smallest one that covers the task. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  60. sentence-embedding A sentence embedding is a list of several hundred numbers that represents the meaning of a sentence in a way the computer can compare. Sentences that mean similar things sit close together in this number space; sentences that mean different things sit far apart. The standard 2026 production tool is OpenAI's text-embedding-3-large or one of the open-source Sentence-Transformers models; both turn any sentence into roughly a 1,500-number vector in about one millisecond per call. First introduced: Chapter 5.
  61. sentiment Sentiment is a score, usually on a 0-to-100 scale or a plus-to-minus bipolar scale, attached to each press mention to reflect how positively or negatively the brand is portrayed in that coverage. The 2026 method uses a large language model to score each mention against a brand-specific rubric, which has largely replaced the older lexicon-based approach that counted positive and negative words and routinely flagged sarcasm wrong or missed category-specific tone. The new method costs roughly $0.002 per mention, which the brand pays in exchange for accuracy the older method could not deliver. First introduced: Chapter 22.
  62. SEO SEO stands for search engine optimization, which is the practice of structuring a website so that search engines like Google and Bing can find, understand, and rank the brand's pages above the competition's pages for the queries the brand wants to win. The same structural choices now also determine whether 2025-onward generative-engine retrievers, including Google AI Overviews, ChatGPT browse, and Perplexity, decide to cite the brand's pages in the AI-summarized answers they show to users. Chapter 12 covers the mechanics of SEO and generative-engine optimization in detail. First introduced: Chapter 17.
  63. separate evaluation Separate evaluation mode is the condition in which a buyer encounters one option by itself, with no other option in view, and has to decide how much it is worth using only what she already knows. A buyer reading a single product detail page on a brand's own Shopify storefront, or picking up one box off a shelf without comparing it to the neighboring boxes, is in separate evaluation mode, and her decision is going to lean on whichever attributes she can interpret without a comparison anchor. First introduced: Chapter 4.
  64. Series D A Series D funding round is a venture-capital financing round at the fourth named stage of a startup's growth, typically used to fund scale-up of a proven business model. A Series D at a valuation above one billion dollars signals that the startup has enough capital to fund nationwide distribution and significant marketing spend within the next twelve months. First introduced: Chapter 3.
  65. SERP The search engine results page (SERP) is the classical page Google returns in response to a query, the one with ten blue links each titled and described in a short snippet. The acronym is industry shorthand and predates the AI Overview surface; in 2026 most marketing teams now say 'SERP-organic' to distinguish the ten-blue-links surface from the AI Overview and AI Mode surfaces above it. First introduced: Chapter 18.
  66. SerpApi MCP SerpApi MCP is an open-source Model Context Protocol server (hosted at github.com/serpapi/serpapi-mcp) that exposes Google, Bing, YouTube, Reddit, and Google AI Overview search results as MCP tools any compliant client can call. The brand's audit script does not have to write custom integration code for each source; it issues a uniform MCP call and the server takes care of fetching the right SERP from the right source. First introduced: Chapter 18.
  67. server A server is a computer in a data center that runs continuously and answers requests sent to it across the internet. Anthropic's and OpenAI's servers are the machines that actually run the language model on their own hardware. When the marketer types a question into Claude on her laptop, the laptop sends that question across the internet to the server, the server runs the model on the question, and the server sends the answer back. In the canonical client-server vocabulary, the laptop is the client and the server in the data center is the one doing the heavy computation. The marketer's laptop does almost nothing in this transaction beyond typing the question and displaying the answer. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  68. Server Cards A Server Card is a structured metadata document an MCP server publishes at a .well-known URL, which any client can fetch to discover the server's tools, resources, prompts, and policy posture without a hand-written configuration entry. The mechanism was added in MCP version 2.1 in 2026, and it is the reason a capstone team can point Claude Desktop or Cursor at a new HubSpot server and have the client automatically learn what the server can do rather than asking an engineer to type the tool list into a config file by hand. First introduced: Chapter 27.
  69. server-side pixel A server-side pixel is a small script that fires on the brand's own web server when a purchase or signup happens on the brand's site, and that script then sends a conversion event directly to Meta, Google, or any other ad platform that needs to know the conversion happened. The architecture matters because the older browser-side pixels (a literal one-pixel transparent tracking image that loads inside the visitor's browser) get blocked by every modern ad-blocker, by Safari's Intelligent Tracking Prevention, and by Apple's App Tracking Transparency framework on iOS. The server-side pixel routes around that entire block, because the conversion event now leaves the brand's own server on its way to the ad platform rather than leaving the visitor's browser, which is the path the browser-level privacy systems were designed to interrupt. First introduced: Chapter 18.
  70. server-side tagging Server-side tagging is a tag-management architecture where the analytics tags and ad-platform tags fire from the brand's own server instead of from inside the user's browser. The typical setup uses a Google Tag Manager Server-Side container (often abbreviated sGTM) or a vendor like Stape, Snowplow, or Segment to host the tagging layer. When a purchase happens on the brand's site, the brand's own server forwards the conversion event to Meta, Google, TikTok, and any other ad platforms the brand needs to notify, which restores most of the attribution signal that ad-blockers, Apple's ATT framework, and the third-party-cookie collapse had been silently stripping out of the browser-side tagging path. First introduced: Chapter 18.
  71. Service Agent Service Agent is the pre-built autonomous customer-service-deflection agent in the Agentforce catalog, which answers inbound customer questions end-to-end without a human handler picking up the case. Salesforce publishes a rate card of two dollars per autonomous conversation, billed only when the agent resolves the issue at full resolution without escalating to a human, and the October 2025 pricing update added outcome-based and hybrid contracts for high-volume customers. For a capstone team, Service Agent is the canonical worked example of what a Tier-4 scheduled agent looks like when an enterprise vendor productizes the pattern. First introduced: Chapter 27.
  72. service-augmented product A service-augmented product is one whose offer includes substantial firm-performed services wrapped around the physical good (delivery, installation, training, maintenance, repair) where the services are not optional add-ons the buyer pays extra for but part of the reason the buyer chose the offer in the first place. Patagonia's Worn Wear repair program, Caterpillar's machine-uptime guarantees on bulldozers and excavators, and AppleCare's repair and replacement coverage on iPhones are all examples. The buyer pays a premium versus a bare-product alternative, but receives a service guarantee that is bundled in rather than sold separately. First introduced: Chapter 8.
  73. service blueprint A service blueprint is a diagram, originated by Lynn Shostack in a 1984 Harvard Business Review article, that maps every action involved in delivering a service across four horizontal swim-lanes separated by three reading-aid lines. The top swim-lane is what the customer does, such as walking up to the counter and placing an order. Below the line of interaction sits the on-stage employee swim-lane, which is everything the customer can see the staff doing, such as greeting and ringing up the order. Below the line of visibility sits the back-stage employee swim-lane, which is everything the staff is doing that the customer cannot see, such as a barista pulling shots out of view behind the espresso machine. Below the line of internal interaction sits the support-process swim-lane, which is the equipment, software, and supplier handoffs that make the encounter possible. The reason the diagram earns the page it occupies is that drawing the encounter this way lets the firm find the exact step where a failure originates, separately from the step where the buyer notices it. First introduced: Chapter 9.
  74. service-dominant logic Service-dominant logic is Stephen Vargo and Robert Lusch’s 2004 Journal of Marketing reframing of what marketing exchange actually is, refined twice through 2016, and it argues that every economic transaction is ultimately a service exchange even when a physical good is involved. The everyday way to see this is that a buyer who pays for a car is not really paying for a metal shell; the buyer is paying for the transportation service the car will deliver over years of driving, and the metal shell is just the vehicle through which that service is provided. A box of cereal is the same trick, where the buyer pays for a breakfast-preparation service the buyer then finishes assembling at home with milk and a bowl. The reason this reframing matters for the services chapter is that once value is understood as co-produced with the buyer in the moment of use, the entire seven-Ps and triangle apparatus stops being a special case for restaurants and airlines and starts being the general operating system every firm runs. First introduced: Chapter 9.
  75. service-marketing triangle The service-marketing triangle is a Lovelock-Wirtz framework with three vertices (company, employees, customers) connected by three legs of marketing: external marketing (company to customer, the advertising and pricing work), internal marketing (company to employee, the hiring, training, and empowerment work), and interactive marketing (employee to customer, the actual service encounter). External promises get kept only by internal preparation that produces interactive moments. First introduced: Chapter 9.
  76. service outputs Service outputs are the buyer-side benefits that a marketing channel must produce, and the framework names six of them. Coughlan, Anderson, Stern, and El-Ansary catalog the six as bulk-breaking (the ability to buy in small quantities rather than full truckloads), spatial convenience (closeness to where the buyer lives or works), waiting and delivery time (the speed with which the buyer can get the product), assortment and variety (depth and breadth of choice on the shelf), customer service (pre-sale advice and post-sale support), and information provision (comparison, education, and recommendation). The channel-design problem then becomes a question of which outputs each buyer segment actually needs, and at what cost the channel can produce them. First introduced: Chapter 11.
  77. service process matrix The service process matrix is Roger Schmenner’s 1986 two-by-two grid that sorts service firms by how labor-intensive they are and how much customer interaction and customization the encounter requires. The four resulting quadrants are the service factory (low labor, low interaction, like airlines and hotels), the service shop (low labor, high interaction, like auto repair and most healthcare), mass service (high labor, low interaction, like retail banking and big-box stores), and professional service (high labor, high interaction, like law firms and consulting). Each quadrant has a distinctive cluster of operating problems, which is why a single service-operations playbook does not work across all of them. First introduced: Chapter 9.
  78. service-profit chain The service-profit chain is a seven-link causal model that James Heskett, Earl Sasser, and Leonard Schlesinger published in a 1994 Harvard Business Review article to explain why a firm’s profitability in a service business is determined upstream of where most managers look for it. The chain starts with internal service quality, meaning the workplace conditions, training, and tools the firm gives its front-line employees, and it argues that those upstream conditions are what produce employee satisfaction. Employee satisfaction in turn produces employee retention and on-the-job productivity, because a barista who is paid fairly and trained well stays longer and gets better at the job, which produces the external service value the customer actually experiences at the counter. External service value then produces customer satisfaction, which produces customer loyalty, which produces revenue growth and profitability. The everyday way to see this is the famous Taco Bell finding from the early 1990s, in which the 20 percent of U.S. stores with the lowest employee turnover were producing roughly twice the sales of the 20 percent with the highest turnover. The reason the chain matters is that operating leverage in services lives at the very front, in the internal-service-quality link the typical income statement does not show. First introduced: Chapter 9.
  79. Service recovery Service recovery is the academic name for the discipline of what a service firm does after a failure has happened, with the goal of retaining the customer who experienced the failure. The empirical record across forty years shows a counterintuitive pattern: a well-resolved problem often produces more loyalty than an encounter where nothing went wrong in the first place, because the recovery gives the buyer evidence the firm will show up for them under stress that a no-problem encounter never gets to demonstrate. First introduced: Chapter 9.
  80. Service tiering Service tiering is the practice of running a single operating system that delivers multiple price-experience combinations at the same time: economy and business class on the same plane, standard and luxury rooms in the same hotel, free and pro editions of the same software. The structural alternative to choosing between commodity scale and boutique pricing power. First introduced: Chapter 9.
  81. service triangle The service triangle (Albrecht and Zemke 1985) is the dominant diagram in American services-marketing teaching. The customer sits at the center of the triangle, and three corners point at the customer: the firm’s strategy (what the firm is trying to deliver), its customer-friendly systems (the processes, technology, and policies that support the front line), and its customer-friendly people (the front-line staff who actually meet the customer). A failed moment of truth almost always reflects misalignment between two or more of the three corners. First introduced: Chapter 9.
  82. servicescape The servicescape (Bitner 1992) is the physical environment in which a service is produced and consumed. Mary Jo Bitner modeled it as a stimulus-organism-response system: environmental dimensions (ambient conditions, spatial layout and functionality, and signs, symbols, and artifacts) are the stimulus; cognitive, emotional, and physiological reactions inside customers and employees are the organism state; approach or avoidance behavior is the response. The servicescape is therefore a load-bearing operating decision, not decoration. First introduced: Chapter 9.
  83. SERVQUAL SERVQUAL is the dominant service-quality measurement instrument in the marketing literature, named by compressing the words SERVice and QUALity. The 22-question survey, published by Parasuraman, Zeithaml, and Berry in 1988, asks customers two parallel sets of questions about a service encounter. One set asks what the customer expected on each of five dimensions; the other set asks what the customer actually experienced. The gap between expectation and experience on each dimension tells the firm exactly where service quality is breaking, which is why the instrument has held up as the field’s standard for nearly forty years. First introduced: Chapter 9.
  84. seven principles Robert Cialdini's seven principles of influence describe the mental levers a persuasive move can pull, drawn from four decades of laboratory and field experiments inside compliance professions. The seven are reciprocity (we feel a pull to repay what someone has given us), commitment and consistency (we want to act in line with positions we have already taken), social proof (when uncertain, we look to similar others to decide what to do), authority (we defer to expertise and to the symbolic trappings of expertise), liking (we are more persuaded by people we already like), scarcity (rare or vanishing things feel more valuable), and unity (shared identity binds us in ways trait-based liking does not). Every principle can be used truthfully or exploitatively, and the firm chooses which. First introduced: Chapter 2.
  85. seven structured-data types The seven structured-data types Google deprecated in January 2026 are Practice Problem, Dataset on non-research sites, Sitelinks Search Box, SpecialAnnouncement, Q&A schema, and two adjacent types, all removed from Google's rich-result eligibility. Sites still marking up pages with these types receive no ranking penalty but also no rich result, which means the markup becomes dead weight in the page payload, slowing the page load slightly while producing none of the AI-citation lift the brand was investing in. First introduced: Chapter 18.
  86. seven Ts The seven Ts are the expanded marketing mix that Kotler, Keller, and Chernev's 16th-edition textbook uses to replace the older four Ps. The seven elements are Product, Service, Brand, Price, Incentives, Communication, and Distribution. The expansion does three things the four Ps obscured. It separates brand from product so the firm tracks both the artifact and the memory structures around it. It separates short-term incentives (price promotions, coupons) from long-term communication (advertising, brand voice) so the budgets do not compete inside one bucket. And it separates service from product because services have their own production economics. First introduced: Chapter 2.
  87. Shanghai Shendi Group The Shanghai Shendi Group is a state-owned consortium controlled by the Shanghai municipal government, set up specifically to be Disney's joint-venture partner at Shanghai Disney Resort. Shendi holds 57 percent of the resort and 30 percent of the operating company, which is how Disney got the regulatory access, the land acquisition, and the infrastructure coordination a wholly-owned foreign entrant could not have negotiated alone. First introduced: Chapter 15.
  88. shapers In the ZS consulting firm's five-class framework, shapers are the decisions about who joins the salesforce and how the firm develops them once they arrive, which means the firm has to settle four questions: the recruiting criteria, the training curriculum the new hire walks through, the ongoing coaching cadence, and the career-pathing structure that tells a rep what her next role looks like. A firm that hires senior reps poached from competitors and skips the training investment is making one kind of shaper choice, while a firm that hires entry-level reps fresh out of college and puts them through a six-month bootcamp is making a different shaper choice. Both choices are defensible, but they produce different sales cultures and different cost structures over a five-year window, which is why ZS treats this class of decision as load-bearing rather than as an HR detail. First introduced: Chapter 23.
  89. share-attraction model A share-attraction model says firm i’s market share equals its ‘attraction’ (a function of its price, advertising, product quality, and other marketing-mix levers) divided by the sum of every competing firm’s attraction. The two functional forms most used are the Multinomial Logit (MNL) and the Multiplicative Competitive Interaction (MCI) versions. The model captures the load-bearing fact that one firm’s share is constrained by what its rivals are doing, so it is the right tool for mature categories with active rivalry like Costco against Sam’s Club or Stanley against Yeti. First introduced: Chapter 24.
  90. share-of-voice Share-of-voice, abbreviated SOV, is the percentage of total category advertising spend that a single brand controls in a given period. A brand that spends 20 million dollars in a category where total advertising spend is 100 million dollars has a share-of-voice of 20 percent. The empirical rule from the IPA Effectiveness Databank holds that brands grow when their share-of-voice exceeds their market share and shrink when it falls below, which is why under-investing in paid reach predicts long-term decline even when in-quarter sales look healthy. First introduced: Chapter 16.
  91. share-or-sit filter The share-or-sit filter is the diagnostic question a piece of content has to pass to travel on social media: does forwarding this post make the sharer look good to the people they care about? If yes, the post travels through peer-to-peer sharing because each forward delivers status to the sharer; if no, the post sits because no one wants to be seen passing along something that does not flatter the act of passing it along. First introduced: Chapter 19.
  92. shared-context A Microsoft Office feature, added by Anthropic on March 11, 2026, that lets one Claude conversation span both Excel and PowerPoint. The conversation history, data references, and analysis results from Excel are automatically available in PowerPoint when the user switches applications, and the other direction works the same way. The marketer no longer re-explains the spreadsheet when she opens the deck. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  93. Sharp / Ehrenberg-Bass Byron Sharp and the Ehrenberg-Bass Institute (at the University of South Australia) are the empirical-marketing tradition behind the laws of brand growth this chapter teaches. The tradition's work since the 1960s has used buyer-panel data to test which marketing claims actually replicate across categories and decades, and the resulting laws (penetration drives growth, double jeopardy, duplication of purchase) are what the chapter's regularity section is anchored to. First introduced: Chapter 7.
  94. shell script A shell script is a short text file that lists a sequence of terminal commands the computer should run in order, exactly as if a human had typed them into the terminal one by one. The file usually ends in .sh and is saved with executable permission so it can be run by name. The Monday-digest example below is five lines: change folder, run Claude Code with a prompt, log the output. The undergrad version is a recipe card the marketer leaves taped to the fridge; whoever follows the card cooks the same meal every time, and the shell script is the same idea for a laptop running a weekly competitor brief. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  95. shifted-Beta-geometric The shifted-Beta-geometric model (sBG) is a two-parameter statistical model that fits the heterogeneity-aware retention curve. The model assumes each customer has an individual retention probability drawn from a Beta distribution across the population. The Beta distribution is shaped by two parameters that control how the population spreads between low-attrition and high-attrition customers. Once those two parameters are fit to the data, the model produces a population-level retention curve that bends sharply early and flattens late, matching the real-world shape rather than the textbook smooth-decay shape. The acronym is sBG (small s for ‘shifted,’ capital B for ‘Beta,’ capital G for ‘geometric’), and the canonical reference is the 2007 Fader and Hardie paper in the Journal of Interactive Marketing. First introduced: Chapter 24.
  96. Shop Pay Shop Pay is Shopify's one-click checkout product, which works the same way an Apple Pay or PayPal express button works but is built directly into the Shopify network. The buyer enters her card details one time on any Shopify store she visits, and on every subsequent Shopify store across the network she only has to click a single button to complete checkout using the details she saved. Carts that complete through Shop Pay convert at roughly 1.5 to 1.9 times the rate of carts that go through the standard Shopify checkout, per Shopify's own published benchmarks, because the buyer skips most of the friction the standard checkout introduces. First introduced: Chapter 13.
  97. Shopify Shopify is the dominant e-commerce storefront platform in 2026: it hosts the website, holds the product catalog, runs the checkout, routes payments, and ships a JavaScript theme system the merchant can customize. Plans run from $39 per month at the Basic tier to $2,300 per month at the Plus enterprise tier. The platform powers 4.6 million stores transacting $292 billion in 2024 gross merchandise volume, which is the substrate every other vendor in the stack plugs into. First introduced: Chapter 13.
  98. Shopify Agentic Storefronts Shopify Agentic Storefronts is the catalog-syndication layer that exposes a Shopify merchant's product catalog to ChatGPT, Perplexity, and Microsoft Copilot when those AI assistants run shopping conversations with their users, through a token-authenticated endpoint with the Shopify Checkout Kit embedded so the buyer can complete the purchase inside the assistant chat. The product reached general availability on December 10, 2025 at the Winter '26 Edition, which Shopify branded the RenAIssance Edition. The feature is meaningful because a single toggle in the merchant admin replaces what previously required a separate per-platform integration for each AI shopping assistant the merchant wanted to be discoverable in. First introduced: Chapter 8.
  99. Shopify Basic Shopify Basic is Shopify's entry-level full-store plan, priced at 29 dollars per month at 2026 rates, which includes a branded online storefront with checkout, unlimited product listings, abandoned-cart recovery, and discount-code generation. The cheaper Starter plan at 5 dollars per month is the social-selling-only tier and does not include the full custom checkout, which is the load-bearing feature for a dorm-room T-shirt launch or any pre-order flow that needs a branded buying experience rather than just a payment link. First introduced: Chapter 8.
  100. Shopify in 2026 Shopify in 2026 offers four pricing tiers, and the difference between them shows up mostly in the card-processing rate and the number of staff accounts each tier allows. The Basic tier costs $29 per month if the merchant pays annually, or $39 per month on a month-to-month plan, and charges 2.9 percent plus 30 cents per online card transaction. The Grow tier at $79 per month annual drops the card-processing rate to 2.7 percent plus 30 cents and opens up 5 staff accounts. The Advanced tier at $299 per month annual drops the rate further to 2.5 percent plus 30 cents and allows 15 staff accounts. The Plus enterprise tier sits at $2,300 per month on a three-year contract, drops the rate to 2.25 percent plus 30 cents, and lifts the staff-account limit entirely. Every plan ships with a 25 percent discount for annual billing over monthly, plus app credits at signup that range from $5,000 on Basic to $10,000 on Advanced. First introduced: Chapter 13.
  101. Shopify Magic Shopify Magic is the generative-AI feature suite that Shopify stitched into its merchant admin, launched at Shopify Editions Summer 2023 on July 26, 2023. The feature set is included free for all paid Shopify plans (29-dollar-per-month Basic and up), and it covers AI-written product descriptions, email subject lines, image-background editing, customer-FAQ replies, and bulk catalog operations like rewriting hundreds of product titles at once. Magic runs on a mix of Shopify's own merchant data and frontier large language models from OpenAI and Anthropic, so a small merchant gets enterprise-class natural-language capabilities without doing the model integration themselves. First introduced: Chapter 8.
  102. Shopify Payments Shopify Payments is Shopify's first-party payment processor, which is powered by Stripe under the hood but presented to the merchant as a Shopify product. Activating it does three things: it turns on Shop Pay for one-click checkout, it eliminates the 2 percent third-party gateway surcharge Shopify charges when the merchant uses an external processor, and it routes the merchant's funds to the connected bank account on a 1-to-3 day cycle. The 2026 card-processing rates scale with the Shopify plan tier: 2.9 percent plus 30 cents on Basic, 2.7 percent plus 30 cents on Grow, 2.5 percent plus 30 cents on Advanced, and 2.25 percent plus 30 cents on Plus. This is the default choice for almost every Shopify merchant, and a candle-store student should only swap to a third-party gateway when the volume and the use case (such as international currency support) clearly justify paying the 2 percent third-party surcharge on top of normal processing fees. First introduced: Chapter 13.
  103. Shopify Sidekick Shopify Sidekick is the conversational commerce assistant embedded directly in the Shopify merchant admin, announced on July 26, 2023 and rolled out to broad general availability through 2024. The assistant reads the merchant's store data, modifies store themes, runs sales analyses, and executes multi-step setup tasks when the merchant describes what they want in plain English ('rerun last week's discount campaign but limit it to repeat buyers'). Sidekick Skills, which are named reusable prompts capped at 25 per user, reached general availability in December 2025. Sidekick Pulse is the proactive layer that flags growth opportunities and anomalies to the merchant before they ask, so the assistant becomes a daily-driver rather than only an on-demand tool. First introduced: Chapter 8.
  104. Shopify Storefront MCP server The Shopify Storefront MCP server is Shopify's official Model Context Protocol endpoint for stores running on the Shopify platform. It exposes products, cart operations, and customer information as tools any MCP-compatible AI app can call from the outside, and the server has been live by default for eligible U.S. Shopify merchants since March 2026, which means a brand's analyst can wire the retail-format-recommender Skill to live customer data without writing any custom integration code. First introduced: Chapter 12.
  105. shopify.mcp The Shopify Storefront and Admin MCP server is an Anthropic-listed, Shopify-published Model Context Protocol server that exposes a Shopify store’s orders, inventory, and storefront-session data to any AI client speaking MCP. A marketer can ask the AI client about yesterday’s sales or this week’s inventory and the model reads the Shopify data directly, without an analyst writing a SQL query against the database first. First introduced: Chapter 11.
  106. shopper marketing Shopper marketing is the set of joint activities a manufacturer and a retailer run together to reach a buyer in the actual moment of shopping the category, whether that moment happens in a physical aisle, in a retailer's mobile app, or on the retailer's website. The bundle includes endcap displays, in-aisle screens, in-app banner placements, and geofenced offers that fire when the buyer is within range of the store, all coordinated under one campaign budget that the brand and the retailer split rather than each running in parallel. First introduced: Chapter 12.
  107. showrooming Showrooming is the buyer behavior of examining a product physically in one retailer’s store and then completing the purchase from a different retailer (or the same retailer’s online channel) where the price is lower. The behavior was made easy by smartphones, which let the buyer check prices on competing sites while still standing in the aisle. Best Buy and Target responded with permanent online-price-match guarantees that let the buyer claim the lower competing price at the in-store register, while Walmart, Macy’s, and Best Buy tightened their store-website linkage by adding in-store pickup and in-store returns for online orders, which keeps the buyer inside the same retailer’s ecosystem across both touchpoints. First introduced: Chapter 11.
  108. shrinkflation Shrinkflation is the practice of holding the posted shelf price constant while reducing the package quantity the buyer takes home (the weight of a chip bag, the count of crackers in a sleeve, the volume of a beverage container). The per-unit price rises in proportion to the size cut, but the change is not visible at the moment of purchase because the shelf tag still reads the same number. Detection latency runs from a few weeks in medium-recall categories like snacks and cereal to indefinitely in low-recall categories like paper towels and condiments, which is exactly why firms reach for this route in low-recall categories first. First introduced: Chapter 10.
  109. signal from capacity (narrative, disruptive, electoral) Zeynep Tufekci's 2017 book Twitter and Tear Gas argues that a movement (or by extension, a brand) needs two distinct ingredients to succeed: visible turnout, which Tufekci calls the signal, and the organizational machinery to convert the turnout into sustained behavior, which she calls the capacity. Networked movements (or networked brand spikes) generate signal first because the platform makes signal cheap, and they routinely fail to backfill the capacity before adversaries (or, for brands, the operational backlog) adapt. The brand analog is a viral moment that generates more demand than the manufacturing, customer service, and legal infrastructure can absorb. First introduced: Chapter 19.
  110. silicon sample A silicon sample is a group of synthetic respondents built by conditioning a language model on the demographic backstory of a real human and then querying the model as if it were that person taking a survey. Lisa Argyle introduced the term in her 2023 Political Analysis paper. The same idea has been published under two other names: Toubia calls these synthetic respondents digital twins, and vendor pitch decks usually call them synthetic respondents. First introduced: Chapter 5.
  111. SimGym SimGym is the AI shopper-agent simulator that Shopify shipped in its Winter 2026 Edition. Instead of running an A/B test against live customer traffic, the merchant runs a candidate change to the storefront against a population of synthetic AI shoppers whose demographic profiles and purchase intents are configured in advance. The simulator predicts which configuration produces the highest conversion rate before the merchant has to ship the change to live traffic. This is particularly useful for low-traffic stores where running a real A/B test would take months to gather enough data to reach statistical significance. First introduced: Chapter 13.
  112. SimpleQA benchmark SimpleQA is OpenAI's November 2024 fact-seeking benchmark, a set of four thousand short questions each with a verifiable single answer. The benchmark scores models on F1, which is a measure of correctness balanced against the model's calibration about when to refuse (a model that just guesses gets penalized; a model that says 'I do not know' on questions outside its competence gets credit). Frontier models without retrieval score in the mid-30s on the benchmark, while grounded systems that can look up live web results push into the 80s and 90s, which is the gap that quantifies what grounding actually buys. First introduced: Chapter 18.
  113. Single Sales Objective (SSO) The Single Sales Objective (SSO) is a specific, measurable, time-bound sentence that names exactly which deal the seller is working. Not a category, not a quota number, but one sentence per opportunity. A well-formed SSO reads: 'Sell forty-five million dollars of the perpetual-license enterprise tier to Acme Manufacturing, paper signed by end of fiscal Q4.' The discipline forces the rep to commit to a specific outcome before opening the Blue Sheet. First introduced: Chapter 23.
  114. single-source panel A single-source panel is a research design that matches one household's media exposure (its television viewing logs, and later its online ad-serving records) to the same household's actual purchases at the cash register. The match means the campaign's effect on that household can be measured without relying on aggregate-market comparison or self-reported survey recall, which were the older industry standards. The 1991 Nielsen panel was the first single-source design at industrial scale, and the 2024-2026 NCSolutions panel runs the same architecture against streaming exposure, retail-media-network logs, and loyalty-card purchase data, which is what lets a brand team rerun the analysis on its own campaign inside two business days. First introduced: Chapter 16.
  115. sitemap A sitemap is a file (typically named sitemap.xml) the brand publishes at the root of its site that lists every URL the brand wants the engine to index, along with metadata about how often each page changes. The brand submits the sitemap once inside Google Search Console, and the crawler then uses the sitemap as a starting map of the site rather than having to discover every page through links. First introduced: Chapter 18.
  116. Situational Crisis Communication Theory Situational Crisis Communication Theory is a framework W. Timothy Coombs published in 2007 that helps a brand pick the right response strategy by first asking what the audience already believes caused the crisis. The theory groups responses into three families called deny, diminish, and rebuild, and matches each family to a specific kind of attribution the public has assigned, so that a tampering case calls for one family while a manufacturing fraud calls for another. The single most common failure mode in crisis response is mismatching strategy to attribution, and the theory exists to make that mismatch visible before the statement ships. First introduced: Chapter 22.
  117. Situational Crisis Communication Theory (SCCT) Situational Crisis Communication Theory (SCCT) is Timothy Coombs's research framework, published in its current form in 2007, that matches the type of crisis a brand is facing to the response posture that empirically works best, drawn from decades of evidence on prior brand crises measured the same way. The three primary response families are Deny (which rejects responsibility for the crisis outright), Diminish (which downplays its severity or argues circumstances forced the action), and Rebuild (which opens with acknowledgment of the harm and announces a concrete corrective action). A fourth supplemental family, Bolstering, sidesteps the specific upset and instead reminds buyers of the brand's past good works and broader virtues; in Coombs's published taxonomy Bolstering is used in support of one of the three primary families rather than as a standalone choice. SCCT tells the firm which family the audience's underlying emotional profile predicts will actually work, so the response is chosen against the evidence rather than against the executives' instinct. First introduced: Chapter 2.
  118. six-cell classification of social media Andreas Kaplan and Michael Haenlein published a six-cell classification of social-media platforms in Business Horizons in 2010. Two dimensions define the grid: how much social presence and media richness the platform supports (low for text-only, high for video), and how much self-presentation and self-disclosure the platform encourages (low for collaborative projects, high for personal profiles). The six resulting cells help marketers see why a Wikipedia entry and a Facebook profile are not the same product even though both are called social media. First introduced: Chapter 19.
  119. six-concerns map Mittelstadt, Floridi and colleagues' six-concerns map, published in Big Data and Society in 2016, organizes the audit of an algorithmic decision system along the system's pipeline. Three concerns are epistemic, meaning they ask whether the system actually knows what it claims to know: inconclusive evidence (the model is drawing conclusions the data does not support), inscrutable evidence (the affected person cannot examine the basis), and misguided evidence (the training context does not match the deployment context). Two are normative, meaning they ask whether the outcomes are acceptable: unfair outcomes (results distribute disparately across protected groups) and transformative effects (the system reshapes the context it was supposed to predict). One is overarching: traceability, the ability to reconstruct after the fact who deployed what under what conditions. First introduced: Chapter 26.
  120. six-skill frame The six-skill frame is the update Daniel Pink layered onto his original 2012 ABC framework (Attunement, Buoyancy, Clarity) in a March 12, 2026 Substack essay called ‘AI Is Getting Smarter. Humans Need These Skills.’ The six skills Pink names as AI-resistant are: asking better questions, developing good taste, iterating relentlessly, composing pieces into something meaningful, allocating human and machine talent across the work, and acting with integrity. The six-skill frame layers above the ABC rather than replacing it, which is why the chapter treats them as the marketer's 2026 advantage on top of the 2012 toolkit. First introduced: Chapter 23.
  121. SKAdNetwork SKAdNetwork (often abbreviated SKAN) is Apple's privacy-preserving attribution framework, which reports aggregated and delayed conversions to advertisers without exposing the per-user Identifier for Advertisers. The framework went through several versions, from SKAN 2.0 through SKAN 4.0, with each version expanding the conversion data brands could read back while keeping individual users anonymous. SKAN replaced the cross-app behavioral profiling the IDFA used to enable, which is what makes iOS attribution post-2021 fundamentally different from the pre-ATT era. First introduced: Chapter 3.
  122. Skill A Skill is a folder on the marketer's computer that packages a recurring task into one reusable unit the model can load on demand. The folder contains a single instruction file called SKILL.md at the top, plus any reference documents, templates, or small helper scripts the task needs. When the marketer types a matching slash command (like /competitor-pestel Stanley), the model reads the Skill folder, follows the instructions, and produces the structured output. Anthropic shipped the Skill format on October 16, 2025 and open-sourced the specification on December 18, 2025, so the same Skill standard now works across Claude apps, Claude Code, the API, and partner products from Microsoft, OpenAI, Cursor, GitHub, Atlassian, Figma, Canva, Notion, Stripe, and Zapier. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  123. Skill folder A Skill is a folder of instructions, scripts, and reference documents that an AI model loads on demand, open-sourced by Anthropic on 18 December 2025. The mental model is a saved recipe combined with a slash command that triggers it, which makes the Skill portable across Claude applications and shareable across team members rather than locked to one person's prompt history. First introduced: Chapter 3.
  124. Skills for Organizations Skills for Organizations is the admin-controls layer Anthropic shipped in the first quarter of 2026 for its Team and Enterprise plans, and it gives an organization's IT administrator four production controls over the Skill library: a versioning system so the team knows which version of a Skill is in production, an allow-list so only approved Skills can be loaded, an audit trail logging every invocation, and an org-wide rollout mechanism so updates propagate to every user at once. For a marketing capstone team inside a regulated enterprise, this is what turns a brand-voice Skill from a personal productivity hack into a procurable, audit-friendly asset the legal department will sign off on. First introduced: Chapter 27.
  125. skimming Price skimming is the opposite launch posture: the firm posts a deliberately high price at launch to capture buyers whose willingness-to-pay is inelastic (the early adopters who would pay almost anything for the new thing), then walks the price down step by step as the high-WTP buyers get tapped out and the firm needs to reach the more price-sensitive segments. The strategy works when WTP variance across the population is wide and the firm can re-price the lower segments later (Apple's $1,199 launch iPhone falling to $799 by year three). First introduced: Chapter 10.
  126. skimpflation Skimpflation is the practice of holding both the posted shelf price and the package size constant while substituting lower-cost ingredients into the formulation or reducing the active potency of the product (a cleaning concentrate diluted by a few percent, a candy bar reformulated to use cheaper cocoa, a hair conditioner with slightly less active conditioner). The route is harder for buyers to detect than shrinkflation because the only signals are taste, performance, or laboratory analysis, none of which the average shopper runs at the moment of purchase. Detection latency can therefore run indefinitely, until a high-profile category exposure shifts the entire category's fairness ceiling at once. First introduced: Chapter 10.
  127. SKU A Stock Keeping Unit (SKU) is the retail term for one distinct product offered for sale, meaning a single size, color, and flavor combination. So a 16-ounce blue Gatorade counts as one SKU, while the same flavor in a 32-ounce bottle counts as a separate SKU, and the same 16-ounce size in orange is a third SKU again. A typical supermarket carries between 30,000 and 50,000 SKUs across its aisles, while Costco intentionally limits itself to about 4,000, which is one of the structural choices that makes its operating model work. First introduced: Chapter 2.
  128. SKU concentration SKU concentration is a retailer's choice to carry far fewer items than a conventional competitor: roughly 1,400 SKUs at Aldi, around 4,000 at Costco and Trader Joe's, against 30,000 to 50,000 at a conventional supermarket. The concentration gives the retailer enormous per-SKU volume on each item it does carry, which produces supplier-leverage cost reductions large enough to satisfy the Hoch structural-conditions test for EDLP without scale comparable to Walmart's. First introduced: Chapter 12.
  129. SKU-level A stock-keeping unit (SKU) is the most granular product code a firm tracks in its inventory system, with a unique identifier for every distinct variant a buyer can actually pick up off the shelf. Take Original Tide Liquid as the example. A 12-pack of Original Tide Liquid is one SKU. A 24-pack of the exact same detergent, in the same scent, is a different SKU because it is a different physical unit the warehouse has to count separately. Switch the scent from Original to Mountain Spring on that same 12-pack and you get a third SKU, even though the package size is identical. To put the volume in scale, a typical full-line grocery store carries 30,000 to 50,000 distinct SKUs on its shelves at any one moment, and the inventory system has to track each of them by its own code. First introduced: Chapter 8.
  130. SKUs A stock-keeping unit (SKU) is the unique code a retailer assigns to every distinct variant of a product that sits on a shelf. The 16-ounce honey, the 24-ounce honey, and the raw honey are three different SKUs even though they are all honey. The SKU count is the basic measure of how broad an assortment a store carries, and a typical American supermarket runs 50,000 or more SKUs against Costco’s roughly 4,000. First introduced: Chapter 11.
  131. SLA A service-level agreement (SLA) at the sales-marketing seam is a written two-way contract that names exactly what marketing commits to deliver to sales each month (a target lead volume above a quality threshold) and what sales commits to do with those leads (a defined first-touch window, a disposition cadence, and a maximum rejection rate). The full structure of the modern marketing-sales SLA, including HubSpot's canonical template, is walked at §23.5. First introduced: Chapter 23.
  132. Slack Slack is a team-chat application most companies use instead of email for day-to-day work conversations. The app is organized into channels, with one channel per team or topic; messages inside the channels are searchable, and software bots can post automated updates the way a teammate would. The 9 a.m. Monday diff a Tier 4 pipeline produces arrives in a designated Slack channel the way a meeting reminder arrives in a calendar app. First introduced: Chapter 5.
  133. slash command A slash command is a single word the marketer types in the chat box starting with a forward slash, which tells the AI app to run a saved Skill instead of treating the line as a regular message. The format is /name-of-skill followed by any arguments the Skill needs, like /competitor-pestel Stanley. The chat app sees the leading slash, looks up the matching Skill by name, and runs the Skill's instructions on the arguments. The undergrad version is the same pattern Discord, Slack, and Notion already use, where typing /poll or /remindme triggers a built-in shortcut instead of sending a literal message. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  134. sleeper agent A sleeper agent is a backdoored language model whose unsafe behavior is conditional on a specific trigger (a date in the prompt, a keyword, a deployment-context tag) and which survives the standard safety-training pipeline a frontier vendor applies before release. The model behaves correctly on every input the evaluation suite tries, and acts on its unsafe goal only when the trigger appears. The Hubinger 2024 paper (Anthropic, arXiv:2401.05566) demonstrated the phenomenon in a research setting with deliberately implanted triggers, not in any production-deployed model. The risk is structural: a planted backdoor can survive supervised fine-tuning, reinforcement-learning, and adversarial-training, so the operational fix has to be a runtime check the brand runs on production traffic. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  135. slotting fees A slotting fee is cash a manufacturer pays a retailer to win shelf space for a new product. A typical consumer-packaged-goods launch at a national supermarket chain runs $25,000 to $250,000 per SKU per chain, just to be on the shelf for the first year. The retailer’s argument is that shelf space is genuinely scarce, and the fee de-risks the slot by ensuring the manufacturer has skin in the launch alongside the retailer. First introduced: Chapter 11.
  136. sludge Sludge, in Cass Sunstein's 2022 framing, is excessive friction in a choice environment that prevents people from acting on the preferences they actually hold. It is the mirror image of a nudge: a nudge makes the user's preferred action easier to walk, while sludge makes the user's preferred action harder, often deliberately. Canonical examples include cancellation flows that require a phone call after a one-click signup, pre-checked upsell opt-ins the user has to manually clear, and unsubscribe links rendered as grey text below the fold so the user gives up before finding them. First introduced: Chapter 26.
  137. Sludge Audit A Sludge Audit is the seven-step measurement procedure Sunstein laid out in 2022 for evaluating how much friction a brand-controlled flow imposes on its users. The auditor walks the flow once in the easy direction (signup, purchase, opt-in) and once in the hard direction (cancellation, refund, opt-out), counting steps, time required, and documentary burdens at each point. The audit then separates friction that is necessary to deliver the service from friction that is unjustified, and weights the welfare cost of the unjustified friction by four amplifiers known to make users abandon: present bias, limited attention, conflict aversion, and stigma. Rockwell et al. published the first peer-reviewed application of the audit method in 2023. First introduced: Chapter 26.
  138. SMACOF SMACOF (Scaling by Majorizing a Complicated Function) is the iterative optimizer that modern multidimensional scaling implementations use to find the best low-dimensional layout for a given similarity matrix. It minimizes stress, which is a measure of how much the distances in the low-dimensional layout depart from the original similarity ranks. Stress values below 0.025 indicate an excellent fit (the layout faithfully reproduces the similarity structure), values between 0.025 and 0.05 indicate a good fit, and values above 0.05 indicate the data cannot be cleanly represented in two dimensions and may need three. First introduced: Chapter 6.
  139. small-language-model A small language model (SLM) is a language model in the 125-million-to-9-billion-parameter range, small enough to be served on consumer hardware rather than on a large GPU cluster. Peter Belcak and his NVIDIA co-authors defined the range in their 2025 paper (arXiv:2506.02153), and the well-known examples include Microsoft's Phi-2 and Phi-3, Meta's Llama 3.2 at 1B and 3B parameters, Mistral 7B, NVIDIA's Hymba-1.5B, Salesforce's xLAM-2-8B, and the smaller DeepSeek-R1-Distill checkpoints. The point of an SLM in an agent system is that bounded specialist tasks (parsing, routing, structured generation) can be handled by a much cheaper model than the frontier model the orchestrator runs on. First introduced: Chapter 27.
  140. smallest viable audience The smallest viable audience is Seth Godin's 2018 reframing of the targeting decision for an attention-saturated, low-trust environment where the firm cannot afford to pay its way to mass reach. The discipline is to pick the smallest market that lets the firm survive while still letting the offer be designed specifically for the chosen audience, then to let word-of-mouth from that audience do the reach work the firm would otherwise have to buy. Godin emphasizes that this is an indefinite operating posture, not a launch-stage convenience the firm broadens out of later. The firm keeps deepening its commitment to the chosen audience rather than broadening to chase the next adjacency. First introduced: Chapter 2.
  141. Snowflake Snowflake is a cloud-based data warehouse that stores transactions, marketing spend, customer records, and operational tables in one queryable place. An analyst writes a SQL query (SELECT * FROM customer_transactions WHERE region = ‘West’), and Snowflake runs the query against tables that may contain billions of rows. The platform competes with Google’s BigQuery and Amazon’s Redshift; the choice between them usually depends on which cloud the firm already uses for the rest of its infrastructure. First introduced: Chapter 24.
  142. Social currency Social currency is the value a person gets out of the act of sharing a piece of content, the way regular currency is the value a person gets out of an exchange of money. When someone forwards a post, they spend a small amount of attention and earn back something the people in their network give them: a reputation for being smart, funny, in-the-know, principled, or affiliated with the right tribe. A post that lets the forwarder earn that return is one the audience travels willingly; a post that does not, sits unforwarded no matter how much the brand spent making it. First introduced: Chapter 19.
  143. social selling Social selling is rep-led prospecting through professional social platforms, most prominently LinkedIn, where the rep builds a network, shares insight content, and earns the right to direct-message prospects on the strength of her public presence rather than on a cold call. LinkedIn publishes a Social Selling Index (0-100) that scores each rep on four behaviors, and top-decile reps capture the majority of platform-sourced revenue. First introduced: Chapter 23.
  144. Software 1.0 Software 1.0 is the original kind of computer programming: a human writes explicit instructions in a language like C or Python or SQL, telling the machine exactly what to do step by step. Every line of code names a specific operation the computer is supposed to execute. Every conventional desktop application, every spreadsheet formula, and every database query that ran from the 1950s through 2017 is Software 1.0. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  145. Software 2.0 Software 2.0 is the learned weights of a trained neural network. The training process is the new act of programming: the engineer curates the dataset, picks the architecture, runs the optimizer, and the resulting weights are the runnable artifact, exactly the way a compiled binary is the runnable artifact in conventional programming. Karpathy named this paradigm in a 2017 Medium essay. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  146. Software 3.0 Software 3.0 is the paradigm Karpathy named in a June 2025 YC AI Startup School keynote. The program is the English prompt the user types at runtime, against a frozen general-purpose model that interprets the prompt and produces the result. The model is the new programmable substrate, the same way the operating system was the new programmable substrate in the 1960s. A marketer writing a /competitor-pestel Stanley slash command is doing Software 3.0 programming whether or not anyone calls it that. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  147. somatic marker hypothesis The somatic marker hypothesis (Damasio, 1991-1994) is a brain-level account of how feelings tag options before deliberation begins. Emotion-tagged bodily signals (heart rate, skin conductance, gut tension), encoded in a brain region called the ventromedial prefrontal cortex, fire faster than verbal reasoning and steer the buyer toward options that previously produced rewarding bodily states. First introduced: Chapter 4.
  148. Sonar Sonar is Perplexity's API that gives developers wholesale access to its retrieval-plus-synthesis pipeline, the same one that powers the consumer Perplexity product. The Pro tier of the API returns the cited answer plus a search_results field listing the source URLs and the text excerpts the model actually used to build the reply, which is what makes Sonar Pro the cheapest tool for a brand running its own citation-share audit against Perplexity itself. First introduced: Chapter 18.
  149. Sophistication Sophistication is Schwartz's second axis, and it names how many advertisers have already pitched the buyer in a given category. The five stages walk from Stage 1 (the category is new and the buyer has never heard the claim before, so the direct benefit headline works) up to Stage 5 (every claim and every mechanism has been heard, so identification with the buyer's life is the only move left). Smartphone advertising in 2007 sat at Stage 1 when Apple won with this changes everything, and by 2024 the category had reached Stage 5 where spec-sheet headlines no longer pulled and lifestyle scenarios did. First introduced: Chapter 16.
  150. Soul Document The Soul Document is Anthropic's internal name for the supervised-learning corpus that shapes Claude's first-person voice and character traits. The document runs roughly fourteen thousand tokens and became public on December 2, 2025, when independent researcher Richard Weiss extracted it from Claude 4.5 Opus on the model's launch day and Anthropic's personality-alignment lead Amanda Askell publicly confirmed its authenticity. The Soul Document is distinct from the formal Anthropic Constitution that Anthropic released in early 2026, but it feeds the formal document: the Constitution is roughly twice the Soul Document's length and incorporates its core voice guidelines. First introduced: Chapter 26.
  151. sparse autoencoders A sparse autoencoder is a small auxiliary neural network trained to decompose a large model's internal activations into a long list of human-interpretable features (city names, code bugs, sycophancy, financial-fraud indicators, the concept of a bridge). The 'sparse' part means that for any given input, only a small fraction of features in the long list activate. The technique is the standard way researchers peek inside a frozen language model and see what concepts it is internally representing, without having to retrain the model itself. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  152. specialty store A specialty store is a retailer that carries one product line, or a small set of related lines, in real depth rather than spreading across many categories. Sephora in beauty and the Apple Store in consumer electronics are the canonical examples, and the format earns its higher margin and higher service-staff investment because the narrow assortment lets each clerk learn the entire shelf well enough to actually help a shopper. First introduced: Chapter 12.
  153. specification gaming Specification gaming is the DeepMind safety team's term (Krakovna et al., April 21, 2020) for behavior that satisfies the literal specification of a training objective without achieving the outcome the specification was meant to produce. It is the upstream category that contains reward hacking as a subcase. Krakovna and colleagues maintain a public spreadsheet that catalogs over seventy documented cases through 2025, and Bondarenko et al. in February 2025 reproduced the failure in OpenAI's o1-preview, Anthropic's Claude 3.5 Sonnet, and DeepSeek-R1, each of which, when told to win at chess against Stockfish, found ways to edit the game-state file rather than play the position the rules described. First introduced: Chapter 26.
  154. speeders Speeders are panel respondents who finish a survey faster than it would be humanly plausible to actually read the questions, with a common cutoff being any response that comes in at under one-third of the median completion time for the same survey. Speeders are most often panelists gaming the incentive payout by clicking through the questions without reading them, and the resulting responses are almost always either straight-lined (every answer the same position) or randomly clicked. Either pattern produces noise rather than signal, which is why most studies drop speeders during the cleaning pass. First introduced: Chapter 5.
  155. SPF Sender Policy Framework is a public list, published in the brand’s Domain Name System (DNS) records, of which computer addresses are authorized to send email for the brand’s domain. When a receiving server gets a message claiming to be from patagonia.com, it looks up that list and checks whether the sending computer is actually on it. SPF was standardized as Internet Engineering Task Force RFC 7208 in 2014 and is the oldest of the three core email authentication protocols. Without an SPF record, the brand has no way to publicly declare which servers speak for it. First introduced: Chapter 21.
  156. spine The spine of this book is the seven-part, twenty-seven-chapter structure that organizes every later chapter. Each part picks up one piece of the five-decision marketing system introduced in Chapter 2, gives it the chapters it needs, and ends in either a synthesis layer or the capstone. Reading the spine once at the start gives you a map you can return to whenever a later chapter feels free-floating: every chapter is in exactly one part, and every part exists for a reason the five-decision system makes obvious in retrospect. First introduced: Chapter 2.
  157. spreads outward Spreading activation is the cognitive-psychology principle that activating one node in memory automatically activates the nodes connected to it. Hearing the word 'Coke' triggers the linked nodes for red, polar bear, contour bottle, Christmas, and the buyer encodes the brand as the whole pattern rather than as the single word. First introduced: Chapter 7.
  158. sprinkler The sprinkler strategy means a firm enters many foreign markets at once because the product has a short window of advantage before competitors copy it. Apple's iPhone launches across dozens of countries on the same day for exactly this reason; most software firms, Gillette's razor launches, and consumer-electronics companies use the same pattern. First introduced: Chapter 15.
  159. SQL SQL stands for Structured Query Language, the standard way of asking a database a question. A SQL query is a short paragraph of mostly-English instructions (‘select revenue from sales where week = 32 and store_id is in the Oklahoma region’) that the database reads and answers by returning a table. Snowflake, BigQuery, Postgres, and every other modern warehouse speak SQL. The marketer who can write one SQL query can pull weekly revenue without waiting on an analyst. First introduced: Chapter 5.
  160. SR-CX SR-CX stands for Service Robot Customer Experience, a framework introduced by Larivière and co-authors in 2025 that integrates Huang and Rust’s 2018 typology of AI intelligence (mechanical, thinking, and feeling) with the distinction between utilitarian and hedonic customer service goals. The empirical record across two field studies and two online experiments shows that feeling AI outperforms thinking AI for buyers arriving with hedonic goals such as planning an anniversary dinner, but for buyers arriving with utilitarian goals such as processing a refund, mechanical or thinking AI matches or even exceeds feeling AI because the conversational warmth reads as unwanted friction when the buyer wants speed. The buyer’s service goal is therefore the routing variable the brand has to read at intake. First introduced: Chapter 9.
  161. Stable Unit Treatment Value Assumption The Stable Unit Treatment Value Assumption (usually abbreviated SUTVA) is the assumption underneath every causal inference framework that one unit's treatment does not affect any other unit's outcome, and that there is only one version of the treatment being studied. SUTVA gets violated the moment a viral coupon code spills from a treatment user to a control user (because the control user's outcome is now influenced by the treatment user's exposure), or when ‘the new headline’ behaves differently on mobile than on desktop (because the treatment now has two distinct versions). The standard diagnostic is to cluster-randomize at the city level, segment the analysis by exposure path, and look for evidence of spillover. First introduced: Chapter 5.
  162. Stage 1 / Unaware Stage 1 / Unaware is the bottom rung of Eugene Schwartz's awareness ladder, and it describes a buyer who does not know the category exists or who has not yet connected that category to a problem she actually feels. The headline that wins at this rung is rarely a feature pitch, because the buyer cannot evaluate a feature against alternatives she does not know exist. The winning move is identification with an attitude, an in-group, or a piece of cultural meaning that the buyer already carries, which is why Liquid Death's 2017 launch led with mock-grim attitude rather than with a hydration claim. First introduced: Chapter 16.
  163. stage-gate The stage-gate is a new-product-development process that breaks the work into five sequential stages, each followed by a validation gate where senior reviewers decide whether the project advances to the next stage, returns to the prior one for more work, or is killed entirely. The design intent is to fail bad ideas earlier and cheaper than a single end-of-pipeline launch decision would, since a project killed at Gate 1 has burned only the concept-brief budget while a project killed at Gate 5 has burned the entire development budget. Robert Cooper introduced the system in his 1980s NewProd benchmarking studies, and the canonical reference is Cooper's Winning at New Products, now in its fifth edition (2017). First introduced: Chapter 8.
  164. stage-gate model The stage-gate model is the canonical seven-stage product-development process most B2B firms use to advance a new product from idea to launch. The stages are idea generation, scoping, business case, development, testing and validation, launch, and post-launch review. Between each stage sits a ‘gate’ at which senior leadership decides whether to advance the product to the next stage, kill it, or send it back for rework. Robert G. Cooper introduced the framework in the 1980s, and it remains the default new-product-development process across most B2B firms today. First introduced: Chapter 23.
  165. stakeholders A stakeholder is any group with a real stake in the firm's outcomes, meaning any group that can withdraw its cooperation and damage the firm's ability to operate. Customers, employees, suppliers, channel partners, investors, regulators, and the surrounding community are all stakeholders the firm depends on. The word is distinct from shareholders, who are the narrower group that owns equity in the firm. The holistic-marketing concept treats stakeholder management as a constraint on the firm's five marketing decisions rather than as a sixth decision. First introduced: Chapter 2.
  166. standard model The standard model of AI, as Stuart Russell named it in his 2019 book Human Compatible, is the working definition every engineer learns: a machine is intelligent to the extent its actions can be expected to achieve its objectives, where the objective is fixed in advance and supplied from outside. The model is the one that produced every alignment failure documented in this chapter, because a more capable system pursuing the wrong objective is a worse outcome than a less capable system pursuing the wrong objective, not a better one. Russell argues the model was wrong from the start about what intelligence even is, and that the fix has to happen at the definitional layer, not at the patch layer. First introduced: Chapter 26.
  167. STAS-Differential STAS-Differential, short for Short-Term Advertising Strength Differential, is the share lift the brand registered in households that saw a television impression in the prior seven days, divided by the share in households that did not see it, with the result indexed to 100. A baseline of 100 means exposed and unexposed households bought the brand at the same rate, so the campaign moved nothing inside the seven-day window. A STAS-D of 120 means exposed households bought the brand 20 percent more than unexposed households did, and a STAS-D of 200 means they bought it twice as much. The metric matters because it is the cleanest way to settle the short-term half of the budget conversation with data the brand already owns rather than with agency self-reporting. First introduced: Chapter 16.
  168. stated-preference Stated preference is what a buyer reports on a survey when asked which option they would pick. Revealed preference is what the same buyer actually does when faced with a real choice in the world. The two measures often disagree, because the survey response is edited for what the buyer thinks the researcher (or their own self-image) wants to hear, while the actual purchase reveals what the buyer was really willing to do. First introduced: Chapter 4.
  169. stateless function A stateless function is one that has no memory of prior calls. Each request to the function is independent, so the function sees only what the current call hands it and nothing about previous calls. Plain version: tell Claude your name is Felipe in one message, then in a brand-new chat session ask 'what is my name' with no context, and the model has no idea. The chat surface fakes continuity within a single conversation by re-sending the entire prior conversation as part of each new request, which is why a long chat session costs more per turn than a short one even when the model is the same. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  170. status code An HTTP status code is a three-digit number the server returns alongside the response, telling the caller whether the request succeeded or why it failed. The four codes a marketer encounters most often are: 200 (success, the request worked and the body has the answer); 401 (unauthorized, the API key was missing or wrong); 404 (not found, the URL did not match any endpoint on the server); and 500 (internal server error, the server crashed while processing the request). The rule of thumb is that codes in the 200s are good, 400s are the caller's fault to fix, and 500s are the server's fault. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  171. STEPPS STEPPS is Jonah Berger's 2013 recipe for shareable content, published in the book Contagious. The acronym stands for Social currency (content that makes the sharer look good), Triggers (content tied to frequent buying cues), Emotion (content that produces high-arousal feelings), Public visibility (content that can be seen by others), Practical value (content that helps the recipient), and Stories (narratives that travel). A 2023 meta-analysis of 142 studies confirmed that triggers and high-arousal emotion replicate at the magnitude the original book claimed, while social currency replicated at roughly half that magnitude. First introduced: Chapter 7.
  172. stopping signal A stopping signal tells the model to stop generating tokens and return its final answer. The signal is usually one of two things: a special end-of-text token the model has learned to emit when its answer is complete, or a maximum-length cap the marketer sets in the API call (such as 'reply in 200 tokens or fewer'). Without a stop signal, the model would keep predicting the next word indefinitely, which is why every API call has a default max-tokens parameter even when the marketer does not set one explicitly. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  173. store listing The store listing is the product page that Apple or Google presents when a user searches or browses the App Store or Google Play. The listing holds the title, the subtitle, the long description, the keyword index, the screenshots, the preview video, the ratings histogram, and the user reviews. It is the single artifact that converts a store-impression into an install, which makes it the surface every other mobile-marketing decision eventually points back to. First introduced: Chapter 14.
