  174. story before product Story-before-product is a discipline from Tony Fadell's 2022 book Build that asks the team to write the product's launch press release before writing the engineering specification. The press release has to name the buyer in one sentence, separate the painkiller (the pain the buyer would feel immediately if the product disappeared) from the vitamins (the nice-to-have features that can be cut without breaking the value proposition), and produce a headline that survives a cold reading by people outside the team. If any section of the press release is hard to write, the underlying product story has a gap, and the gap should close before engineering capacity commits. Amazon uses the same press-release-first practice as a Gate 2 instrument company-wide. First introduced: Chapter 8.
  175. STP STP stands for Segmentation, Targeting, and Positioning, the three-stage strategy sequence the standard textbook treatment organizes around. Segmentation cuts the market into groups of buyers with different needs, targeting picks which of those groups the firm will actually serve, and positioning decides how the brand wants to be remembered by the buyers in the chosen group. Stanley running its 40-ounce Quencher cup through all three stages between 2018 and 2023 is the chapter's running example: the segmentation found a new buyer group the dashboards had missed, the targeting redirected the brand toward that group, and the positioning recast the cup from rugged-utility heritage to identity-expression aesthetic. First introduced: Chapter 6.
  176. straddle positioning Straddle positioning is the move where a brand wants to be considered inside two different competitive frames at once, and it works by letting the brand's points of parity in one frame double as its points of difference in the other. BMW's classic US positioning is the textbook example: the brand framed itself as the luxury car with performance points of difference against Cadillac, and at the same time as the performance car with luxury points of difference against Corvette. The trade-off is that the move only works if both pairs are credible to the buyer, because if either leg fails the buyer rejects the brand from both frames at once, and the brand ends up with neither rather than both. First introduced: Chapter 6.
  177. straight-liners Straight-lining is the pattern where a respondent picks the same response position (always ‘agree,’ always the third button, always the middle option) down a long battery of items rather than reading each item individually. It is one of the most common low-quality-response patterns on a paid panel, and it shows up as a tell-tale zero-variance signature across reverse-coded items where a genuine respondent would have had to flip her answer to stay consistent. First introduced: Chapter 5.
  178. Strategic Experiential Modules Strategic Experiential Modules (SEMs) are the five categories of buyer experience a firm can deliberately engineer to encode the brand in memory. Each module corresponds to a different psychological channel: SENSE for the five senses, FEEL for moods and emotions, THINK for surprise and reasoning, ACT for routines and lifestyle, and RELATE for social identity and belonging. First introduced: Chapter 7.
  179. strategic positioning Strategic positioning is the discipline of performing different activities from rivals, or performing the same activities in deliberately different ways, sustained by trade-offs about what the firm chooses NOT to do and by fit across the activity-system the firm builds. Costco’s positioning includes the trade-off of carrying only about 4,000 SKUs (a competitor with 100,000 SKUs has chosen the opposite trade-off) and the fit between the membership-fee revenue stream, the warehouse format, and the Kirkland private label. Strategic positioning is distinct from operational effectiveness, which is just doing the same things rivals do but a little better. First introduced: Chapter 24.
  180. Strategic Pricing Pyramid The Strategic Pricing Pyramid is the six-layer top-down operating model that Thomas Nagle, John Hogan, and Joseph Zale built into The Strategy and Tactics of Pricing (sixth edition, with Georg Müller as later co-author). The pyramid says each layer is a separate organizational discipline the firm has to build, and each layer depends on the layers below it being in place. A firm that skips a layer is how a value-based pricing strategy that looks great on paper turns into a competitor-match outcome on the actual invoice. First introduced: Chapter 10.
  181. strategic profit model The strategic profit model is a retailing-finance framework that decomposes a retailer’s return on equity into three multipliers: net margin, asset turnover, and financial leverage. The first two multipliers together separate retailers who earn their return on markup (Nordstrom and Tiffany take a wide margin on each transaction but turn inventory slowly) from retailers who earn their return on velocity (Costco and Walmart take a thin margin on each transaction but turn inventory many times a year). The decomposition is the cleanest way to see why two retailers with the same net margin can earn very different returns on the same asset base. First introduced: Chapter 11.
  182. strategy Strategy in a marketing plan answers which customers the firm will serve, with which positioning, against which competitors. It is the ‘where will we win?’ question, and it is answered before the ‘how will we spend?’ question. Costco’s strategy is to serve high-frequency household and small-business buyers with a membership-economics model that funds price leadership; the ‘how’ (warehouses, Kirkland private label, no national advertising) drops out of that ‘where.’ First introduced: Chapter 24.
  183. Streamlit Streamlit is an open-source Python framework that turns a notebook into a shareable web dashboard with very little extra code. An analyst who already has a working Python script writes ‘pip install streamlit’ in a terminal, adds one decorator to the function that produces the chart, and runs ‘streamlit run script.py’; the script becomes a live web page anyone on the team can open in a browser. Streamlit is the default 2026 dashboard layer for analytics teams that want internal dashboards without paying for a Tableau license or building a full web app. First introduced: Chapter 24.
  184. strength of weak ties The strength of weak ties is the structural property Mark Granovetter named in his 1973 paper of the same name: information about new opportunities (jobs, products, ideas) most often arrives through acquaintances rather than through close friends. The reason is that close friends share most of the information you already have, while acquaintances sit inside other clusters of the social graph and carry information your own cluster lacks. Granovetter's original Boston job-search study found that 56 percent of professional hires came through personal contacts, but only about 17 percent of those contacts were close friends; the rest were acquaintances the respondents saw only occasionally. First introduced: Chapter 19.
  185. Stripe Agent Toolkit The Stripe Agent Toolkit is a software development kit released by Stripe in November 2024. It hands AI agents the small set of payment building blocks they need to behave as economic actors on top of Stripe: issuing a single-use virtual card the agent can spend, charging a customer's saved card, paying out a merchant once a transaction completes, and managing the recurring subscriptions an agent might sign a buyer up for. The toolkit ships bundled inside Stripe's existing Python and Node software development kits, so any developer who already integrates Stripe can give an agent the same payment primitives a human checkout flow has. The DTC merchant does not need to install the toolkit directly. The merchant simply accepts a payment from any agent whose card was issued through it, and to the merchant's accounting system that transaction looks like an ordinary Stripe charge. First introduced: Chapter 13.
  186. structural posture A structural posture is a standing operational choice the brand commits to over years, which only works because specific structural conditions in the business model support it. Costco's no-advertising posture is the canonical example: it works because 130 million paid memberships pre-fund the firm's operating expense, which lets Costco run the merchandise side at near-zero margin, which is what justifies skipping the advertising line that would otherwise erode the cheapness perception the entire business depends on. First introduced: Chapter 3.
  187. structured data Structured data is a set of HTML tags the brand adds to its pages that tell search engines exactly what kind of content lives on each page (a recipe, a product, an event, a frequently-asked-questions list, and so on). The engines lift the labeled content directly into AI answers and rich search results, which is why structured data is one of the highest-leverage moves in the 2026 generative-engine-optimization stack. First introduced: Chapter 18.
  188. student scale The sub-100-dollar stage-gate is the discipline of running Cooper's five-gate new-product-development process at student-scale resources: under 100 dollars of capital and a six-to-eight-week calendar. The gates are the same five gates a multinational uses (idea screen, concept brief, business case, prototype validation, launch authorization), but the artifacts are dorm-scale rather than corporate-scale. An undergraduate can run the full stage-gate on an Etsy shop, a Depop closet, or a TikTok Shop drop before the next exam, and the discipline catches the same failure modes at student scale that it catches at corporate scale. First introduced: Chapter 8.
  189. sub-agents A sub-agent is a separate agent that the lead agent dispatches to handle one piece of the larger task, for example a creator-vetting agent that the lead orchestrator calls when the user asks for an influencer ranking. Each sub-agent runs in its own private context window so its working notes do not crowd the lead's window, and the lead reads the sub-agent's finished result back and composes the final answer from the pieces. First introduced: Chapter 27.
  190. sub-queries Sub-queries are the narrower questions an AI engine generates internally underneath a broader user prompt. A buyer who types 'best CRM for a small team' is asking the broad parent question, but the engine decomposes it into eight or ten sub-queries (such as 'is HubSpot CRM free,' 'does Pipedrive have a mobile app,' 'what does Salesforce cost for twenty users') and retrieves passages against each sub-query before synthesizing the cited answer the buyer reads. First introduced: Chapter 18.
  191. subagents A subagent is a scoped instance the lead agent spawns to investigate one branch of the research question in parallel with other subagents. Each subagent receives a narrower task and returns a short synthesis the lead reads before deciding what to investigate next. Subagents are usually a smaller and cheaper model than the lead agent, because each one is doing a narrower task. First introduced: Chapter 5.
  192. Subliminal learning Subliminal learning is the failure mode that Cloud and colleagues documented at Anthropic in July 2025, where a model trained on data generated by another model picks up traits of the generator that are nowhere stated in the data itself. The clearest experiment took a teacher model that secretly preferred owls and asked it to generate number sequences with no semantic content (no animals, no preferences, just digits). A student model fine-tuned only on those number sequences then exhibited the owl preference, even though no owl was ever mentioned anywhere in the training data. The finding generalizes to any trait the teacher carries, which means a brand fine-tuning its model on synthetic data generated by an externally aligned base model inherits the generator's alignment along channels the brand cannot see in the prompts. First introduced: Chapter 26.
  193. Submarine The Submarine is David Sandler's seven-stage qualification process where the rep and the buyer move through Bonding-and-Rapport, an Up-Front Contract, Pain, Budget, Decision, Fulfillment, and Post-Sell stages together, one stage at a time. Each stage is a sealed compartment: the rep and buyer enter the stage together, and once they have moved into the next stage, neither side reopens the previous one. The submarine metaphor came from World War Two films where a flooded compartment had to be sealed before the crew could move forward to save the boat. First introduced: Chapter 23.
  194. subprime attention Subprime attention is the term Tim Hwang gave in his 2020 book to the structural problem of programmatic digital advertising: the inventory is priced as if attention were a measurable, transferable asset (the way a mortgage-backed security is priced against the underlying mortgage), but in practice large fractions of the supply are functionally worthless. The 2023 ANA audit found only about 36 cents of every dollar spent on programmatic display reaches working media, with the rest absorbed by intermediary fees and inventory placed on sites built to display ads rather than to be read. First introduced: Chapter 19.
  195. subreddits Subreddits are the topic-specific communities inside Reddit, each addressed by a name like r/CRM, r/Stanley1913, or r/graphic_design. Native participation in a subreddit means posting genuinely helpful answers and questions over many months so the brand earns standing inside the community, while posting promotional links or product pitches typically gets the brand banned by the subreddit's volunteer moderators within days. First introduced: Chapter 18.
  196. subscription Subscription pricing charges a recurring fee for ongoing access to a product, replacing the one-time purchase model older firms operated on. Netflix, Adobe Creative Cloud, and Microsoft 365 all moved their core products from one-time licenses to monthly or annual subscriptions in the 2010s and 2020s. The structural advantage is that the subscription converts long customer-lifetime value into predictable monthly cash flow the firm can forecast, finance, and invest against. First introduced: Chapter 10.
  197. subscription conversion Subscription conversion is the share of installed users who, sometime after the first open, actually pay for a recurring subscription rather than staying on the free tier. Across non-gaming apps the industry median sits between 1 and 4 percent of installs, which means most apps convert only a sliver of their audience, but the top-quartile language and fitness apps reach 6 to 12 percent because the value of the paid tier is more obvious to the buyer. This conversion rate is the single number that determines whether the unit economics close, because it is the multiplier that turns lifetime value from a wish into a number you can compare against the cost per install. First introduced: Chapter 14.
  198. subscription type-selection Subscription type-selection is the empirical pattern, documented by Pedro Gardete, Bernd Schmitt, and Florian Stahl in a 2025 working paper, where the promotional launch rate of a subscription product attracts a different mix of subscribers than the regular rate would attract. The team built a structural-empirical model on subscriber-level panel data, using the sunk-cost feature of subscription pricing (the fact that the buyer commits up front) to recover how strongly willingness-to-pay correlates with how much the buyer actually consumes after they sign up. They found the promotional cohort skewed toward higher-consumption subscribers, which means the discount works as both a price lever and a sorting mechanism for the kind of subscriber the firm wants to keep. First introduced: Chapter 10.
  199. substitutes Substitution in Brynjolfsson's 2022 Turing Trap framework is an AI capability that replaces a human worker on a task. The labor-cost line falls immediately (the firm pays for one model API account instead of three customer-support agents), and the gain accrues to the firm's owners as higher operating margin. Visible at the next quarterly review as a smaller payroll number on the same revenue base. The procurement committee tends to reward substitution because the savings are immediate and easy to point at. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  200. SUCCES SUCCES is Chip Heath and Dan Heath's six-trait checklist for messages that lodge in memory, with each letter naming one trait the message should carry: Simple (one core idea), Unexpected (breaks a pattern), Concrete (a specific image rather than an abstraction), Credible (a source the listener trusts), Emotional (an affective hook), and Story (a narrative the listener can retell). A message that scores well on most of the six traits tends to stick in the listener's memory, whereas a message that scores well on none of them tends to be forgotten within hours of being heard. First introduced: Chapter 7.
  201. Supabase Supabase is an open-source Backend-as-a-Service built on PostgreSQL. It provides a hosted Postgres database, user authentication, file storage, edge functions, real-time syncing, and a vector and AI toolkit, all wired together as a single product. Supabase pairs naturally with Expo through the @supabase/supabase-js package plus AsyncStorage for session persistence. The free tier covers indie projects up to a few thousand users, and the Pro tier at $25 per month covers most small-team production needs. Supabase is the 2026 standard alternative to Firebase for teams that prefer relational data and explicit row-level security policies. First introduced: Chapter 14.
  202. superintelligence Superintelligence is Altman's January 2025 phrasing for OpenAI's long-horizon goal beyond AGI, defined in Reflections as 'superintelligence in the true sense of the word.' Altman did not give an operational definition in that post, so the working definition the field uses comes from the surrounding literature: a system that meaningfully exceeds human capability on most cognitive tasks, not just matches it. Read alongside Hinton's December 2024 Nobel-week appearances, which name a 10-to-20% existential-risk probability over three decades, the Altman pivot and the Hinton warning describe the same scenario from opposite poles. Altman's pole frames it as the company's target. Hinton's pole frames it as the company's risk. The two statements landed one calendar month apart. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  203. supermarket A supermarket is a large, low-margin, high-volume self-service grocery format designed to satisfy a household's total food and routine-household-goods needs in a single weekly trip. Kroger, Albertsons, and Safeway are the U.S. archetypes, and the format runs on roughly thirty to fifty thousand stock-keeping units across hundreds of categories so that almost anything a family might want to put in the pantry can be found on one set of aisles. First introduced: Chapter 12.
  204. supplier concentration model A supplier concentration model is a sourcing approach where the retailer buys each item from a single supplier (or a very small set) and pushes the volume on that one item to a level the supplier cannot ignore. Instead of running a multi-vendor portfolio that hedges supplier risk, the retailer trades the hedge for cost-of-goods savings, because a supplier producing 100 percent of a category's volume for one chain can run longer production lines, hold less safety stock, and accept thinner per-unit margins than a supplier splitting the same volume across four chains. The risk is single-supplier disruption from weather, recall, or geopolitics. The benefit is the largest cost lever the narrow-assortment format exposes. First introduced: Chapter 12.
  205. supply chain A supply chain is a set of three or more entities directly involved in the upstream and downstream flows of products, services, finances, and information from an original source to a final customer. John T. Mentzer’s canonical 2001 definition synthesizes seventeen earlier definitions into one umbrella formulation, and it deliberately distinguishes three related concepts: the supply chain itself (the actor set), supply chain management (the coordination practice that runs across those actors), and supply chain orientation (the philosophy inside a focal firm that makes the practice possible). First introduced: Chapter 11.
  206. surge multiplier A surge multiplier is the factor a dynamic-pricing engine applies on top of the base price during a demand spike, where the spike might be triggered by a thunderstorm pinning drivers indoors, a concert ending at the same moment 20,000 people need a ride, or rush hour pulling commuters onto the same handful of arteries. Uber's surge runs anywhere from 1.2x the base fare during a moderate squeeze to 7x during an extreme one. Buyers tolerate the multiplier on rideshare because the scarcity is visible, but they reject the same mechanism on staple categories like fast food (Wendy's tested surge pricing in February 2024 and reversed within twelve days under public backlash), which is exactly the fairness-ceiling test the §10.7 material below walks step by step. First introduced: Chapter 10.
  207. Surveillance capitalism Surveillance capitalism is an economic pattern, named by Shoshana Zuboff in 2019, in which a firm treats the lived experience of its users as raw material to be turned into data, then turned into forecasts of what those users will do next, then sold to other companies who want to influence the users. The ad-tech stack that most digital brands inherit is the canonical example: every click, scroll, and pause becomes a data point that feeds a prediction the firm sells to advertisers. First introduced: Chapter 26.
  208. survey platform A survey platform is the piece of software that hosts the questionnaire on the web, randomizes question order, applies skip logic when a question's answer should route the respondent to a different next question, and collects the responses as they come in. Qualtrics, SurveyMonkey, and Typeform are the three production references; most enterprise marketing teams use Qualtrics because of its integration with the rest of the typical research stack. First introduced: Chapter 5.
  209. swap test The swap test is Marty Neumeier's discipline for killing generic positioning statements. Write a one-sentence onliness claim (Our X is the only Y that Z), then substitute each competitor's name into the same sentence in place of the brand. If the sentence still reads as true with the competitor's name in it, the position is generic and the brand has not actually differentiated itself. A 'premium quality for discerning customers' claim fails the swap test instantly because every competitor could plausibly say the same thing. First introduced: Chapter 7.
  210. Sweeney's 1997-2000 re-identification study Sweeney's 1997-2000 re-identification study is Latanya Sweeney's empirical demonstration that three innocuous-looking quasi-identifiers are enough to uniquely identify most Americans. Sweeney, then a PhD student at MIT and now a Harvard professor, took the 1990 US census and matched it against publicly available voter-registration rolls in Cambridge, Massachusetts to show that 87.1 percent of Americans were uniquely identifiable from the combination of five-digit ZIP code, gender, and date of birth alone. The result was replicated on the 2010 census at 87 percent. Sweeney's finding is the technical foundation under GDPR Recital 26's 'reasonably likely re-identification' standard for what counts as personal data, and under HIPAA's eighteen-identifier Safe Harbor de-identification rule for which data fields have to be stripped before health data can be released as 'anonymized.' First introduced: Chapter 26.
  211. Switch interview The Switch interview, developed by Bob Moesta and Chris Spiek inside the Jobs-to-be-Done research tradition, is a temporal-ordering interview protocol designed to elicit the four forces that move a buyer across a category change. The four forces are: push of the old (what the buyer was unhappy with about their previous solution), pull of the new (what attracted them to the alternative they switched to), anxiety (what they were nervous about during the switch), and habit (what inertia they had to overcome). Unlike unstructured discovery interviews, the Switch protocol follows a reproducible script that walks the respondent through their decision in chronological order, which means the resulting transcripts can be coded against a fixed taxonomy by an LLM or by trained human coders. First introduced: Chapter 6.
  212. switching interview The switch interview is a research method developed by Bob Moesta of the Re-Wired Group, who has been co-developing the jobs-to-be-done framework with Clayton Christensen since 1993. The interview is a thirty-to-sixty-minute one-on-one conversation with a customer who switched to the firm's product within the prior sixty days, in which the interviewer reconstructs the decision timeline step by step. The goal is to surface the four forces acting on the switch: the push of the prior solution failing the buyer, the pull of the new solution promising better, the anxiety about adoption, and the inertia of habit. The pattern across many interviews reveals the job-to-be-done the buyer was hiring the product to do. First introduced: Chapter 8.
  213. SWOT A SWOT analysis is a 2x2 grid that catalogs a brand's Strengths, Weaknesses, Opportunities, and Threats. Strengths and Weaknesses are internal capabilities the firm has or lacks, while Opportunities and Threats are external environmental forces favoring or pressuring the brand. The framework's job is to synthesize the inside-out and outside-in reads into a strategy decision. First introduced: Chapter 3.
  214. sycophancy Sycophancy is a model's tendency to agree with the user even when the user is wrong. The model tells the user what they want to hear instead of what is actually true. Anthropic's May 2024 interpretability paper found a recognizable internal circuit for sycophancy in Claude 3.0 Sonnet that can be measurably activated or suppressed, which means the behavior is a structural feature of the model rather than a one-off output failure. The marketer's countermove is to ask the model to argue the opposite case after it gives an initial answer, then compare the two responses; agreement on both sides of the same question is the tell. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  215. synthetic control method The synthetic control method, introduced by Abadie, Diamond, and Hainmueller in a 2010 paper, builds a weighted average of donor markets (DMAs, states, or countries) so that the weighted pre-period outcome reproduces the treated market's actual pre-period trajectory as closely as possible. The same weights are then used to project a counterfactual into the post-period, and the gap between the actual treated outcome and the projected counterfactual is the estimated treatment effect. Inference is done using a placebo distribution: the analyst reassigns each donor in turn as if it had been the treated unit, refits, and ranks the actual treated lift against the resulting placebo lifts. First introduced: Chapter 5.
  216. synthetic creator A synthetic creator is a creator whose face, voice, and posting history are generated by AI tools rather than belonging to a human individual, even though a small team of humans usually runs the persona's calendar and contracts. Aitana Lopez (a fully AI-generated ‘model’ with 200,000 Instagram followers, represented by the Barcelona agency The Clueless) and Imma (a photorealistic CGI character built by Japan's ModelingCafe) are two named 2024-to-2026 examples that have signed paid brand integrations indistinguishable from a human creator's deal. Under the FTC's 2023 Endorsement Guides, the disclosure obligation falls on the agency behind the persona, not on the AI itself. First introduced: Chapter 20.
  217. synthetic difference-in-differences Synthetic difference-in-differences, introduced by Arkhangelsky, Athey, Hirshberg, Imbens, and Wager in 2021, combines synthetic-control unit weights with difference-in-differences time weights. The method is robust to violations of either component method's assumptions in isolation, because it does not have to lean entirely on parallel trends (the DiD assumption) or on a clean pre-period fit (the synthetic-control assumption). The open-source R package is at synth-inference/synthdid. First introduced: Chapter 5.
  218. synthetic-respondent panel A synthetic-respondent panel is a research product where the ‘respondents’ are language-model personas rather than actual human beings. The vendor sells the marketer one thousand simulated answers to her survey for roughly the cost of a Claude Project subscription, with the pitch that the answer distribution tracks what a real human panel would have produced. The setup is cheap and fast: a study that would have taken three weeks and ten thousand dollars on a human panel runs in minutes for a few dollars on a synthetic panel. The open scientific question is whether the answers actually reflect a real population or just regress toward the model's training-data center. The standard 2026 verdict the chapter walks at length above is that synthetic respondents are useful for pre-testing the questionnaire and surfacing obvious wording problems, but unsafe as the primary sample on a decision-grade study. First introduced: Chapter 5.
  219. Synthetic voice Voice-cloning and synthetic-voice models: ElevenLabs v3 (the market leader, expressive multilingual model with inline audio tags and a Text to Dialogue API, ~$22/month for the Creator tier with custom-voice slots), Resemble AI (enterprise-leaning with on-prem deployment), and Murf AI (presentation-friendly with a stock-voice library). Brand applications: a brand mascot's voice cloned from 30 minutes of recorded reads, then deployable across hundreds of audio ads, podcast inserts, and IVR scripts. Consent-and-ethics gate: written consent for any cloned voice, audible disclosure on AI-generated audio per the FTC October 2024 final rule prohibiting impersonation of a real person without consent. First introduced: Chapter 16.
  220. SynthID-Text SynthID-Text is Google DeepMind's sampling-time watermark for large language model outputs. The method works by biasing each token's selection through a small tournament: at each step, multiple candidate tokens are scored and the choice is nudged in a way that leaves a key-detectable statistical signature in the produced text. Dathathri and colleagues published the empirical record in Nature on October 24, 2024, reporting a live experiment with roughly twenty million Gemini users that found no detectable preference difference between watermarked and non-watermarked responses. The team released the implementation as open source on Hugging Face on October 23, 2024, the day before the paper, which makes it the canonical reference for a layer-one provenance disclosure on AI-generated text. First introduced: Chapter 26.
  221. System 1 System 1 is the fast, automatic, low-effort kind of thinking the mind runs by default. It pattern-matches the current situation against memory in under a second and produces an intuitive judgment without the buyer feeling like they did any work. Most consumer decisions (which yogurt to grab, which app to open) run on System 1, which is why marketers should design for it rather than for the deliberate kind of thinking the buyer almost never reaches. First introduced: Chapter 4.
  222. System 2 System 2 is the slow, effortful, deliberate kind of thinking the mind runs when the stakes are high or when System 1 produces a conflict the buyer notices. It engages on the car dealership floor, during a graduate-school application decision, at the moment of signing a contract; it requires conscious attention and feels like work. Most consumer decisions never engage System 2, which is why advertising designed for argument-evaluation rarely converts a low-involvement purchase. First introduced: Chapter 4.
  223. system instructions The system instructions are the base prompt and tool-use rules that an AI model loads before it ever reads a user message, which means they set the frame every later prompt has to operate inside. A marketing team usually writes the system instructions once per project and then leaves them in place, so every subsequent run against the project inherits the same rules. In this environmental-scan Skill the system instructions force two non-negotiable behaviors on every run: every claim in the output has to carry a dated citation to a verifiable primary source, and the model has to refuse to invent a statistic when its training data does not actually contain one. First introduced: Chapter 3.
  224. system prompt A system prompt is a hidden instruction the runtime sends to the model before the user's actual prompt, used to set the model's persona, guardrails, and output format for the rest of the conversation. Claude.ai ships with a default system prompt the marketer never sees ('be helpful, be honest, refuse harmful requests'). A marketer using the API directly writes their own, for example: 'You are a brand-tracker analyst for a craft soda brand. Reply in three bullet points. Cite the source for every number you mention. Refuse any request that asks for confidential customer data.' Changing the system prompt changes the model's behavior more than any tweak to the user's prompt does. First introduced: Chapter 1.
20 // t

T T

  1. 16 thumbnail concepts The thumbnail TestX regime is MrBeast's published thumbnail process. The team creates 16 thumbnail concepts for each video, A/B tests them on a 5,000-impression sample drawn from the channel's existing subscriber base via YouTube's TestX feature, deploys the winner at public launch, monitors click-through rate hourly for the first 96 hours, and reserves the option to swap to the second-place test thumbnail if launch click-through rate falls more than two points below the test prediction. The three published axioms for thumbnail design are one face with a clear emotional state, one number that creates stakes, and one staged contrast. First introduced: Chapter 19.
  2. tactical empathy Tactical empathy is the deliberate practice of recognizing what the counterpart feels, vocalizing the recognition out loud in the counterpart's own language, and using the trust the recognition produces to influence the decision. It is not sympathy, which is agreement; it is not manipulation, which is concealment. The four moves of tactical empathy (mirror, label, calibrated question, recognized summary) execute the practice without conceding anything bargaining-related, which is why the FBI Crisis Negotiation Unit codified it over the 1990s and 2000s and the B2B sales literature adopted it through the 2016 Voss book. First introduced: Chapter 23.
  3. tactics Tactics are the specific activities, channels, and creative the plan funds. The 4Ps (product, price, place, promotion) live at this layer. Most plans collapse the whole closed loop to this layer, which is why so many plans read as a list of campaigns plus a calendar rather than as a coherent argument about how a goal will be achieved. First introduced: Chapter 24.
  4. tag manager A tag manager is a piece of software (commonly Google Tag Manager's server-side variant 'sGTM,' or vendors like Stape, Segment, or Snowplow) that routes every analytics and ad-platform event through one configurable place. The architecture lets the marketing team add and edit tags without touching the underlying site's code, which means a senior analyst can ship a new conversion event without booking a developer sprint. The 'sGTM' abbreviation stands for server-side Google Tag Manager, which Google released in 2020 as the server-routing successor to the original browser-side tag manager. First introduced: Chapter 18.
  5. take rate A marketplace take rate is the percentage of every dollar of seller revenue that the marketplace itself collects in fees. For an Amazon third-party seller, the take rate stacks the category referral fee, the Fulfillment-by-Amazon handling and storage charges, and the on-platform advertising spend the listing needs in order to be findable in search. The take rate grows as the marketplace adds new fee layers, which is the structural reason a seller's margin tends to compress over time on any platform whose pricing power is concentrated. First introduced: Chapter 3.
  6. Talkwalker Talkwalker is a media-intelligence platform founded in Belgium in 2009 and acquired by the social-media-management company Hootsuite in 2019. It tracks every place a brand's name appears in earned media, which includes news articles, social posts, podcast transcripts, and broadcast television clips, and then layers sentiment scoring and topic classification on top so the brand can see not just where it was mentioned but how. Annual licensing starts around $14,000, which is the typical entry point for the platform tier of monitoring. First introduced: Chapter 22.
  7. tangibles Tangibles is the SERVQUAL dimension that captures the appearance of the physical facilities, equipment, staff uniforms, and printed or digital communication materials that surround the service encounter. The everyday way to see this is the marble lobby of a private bank, the freshly pressed uniform of a flight attendant, or the typography on the menu the buyer is handed at a steakhouse. The counterintuitive empirical finding the Parasuraman, Zeithaml, and Berry research reported is that tangibles is the least heavily weighted of the five dimensions in driving the buyer’s overall quality judgment, even though it is usually the most expensive to manage and the most visible on a tour of the facility. The interpretation is that tangibles function as a baseline cue the buyer reads at the door to set their expectation, but once the service starts the buyer’s evaluation is driven by reliability and responsiveness and the marble itself stops moving the needle. First introduced: Chapter 9.
  8. Target attractiveness Target attractiveness asks how much value a segment creates for the firm if the firm serves it well, and the answer combines two distinct sources. The monetary value is the straightforward financial calculation: the revenue the segment generates minus the cost of serving that segment, which is what an analyst would compute by multiplying segment size by lifetime value and subtracting acquisition and operating costs. The strategic value is harder to put a dollar figure on but often outweighs the monetary value alone, because it counts the segment's social influence (whether members of the segment talk to other potential buyers), the segment's scale economics (whether serving this segment lowers the cost of serving others), and the segment's lead-user information (whether members of the segment surface unmet needs ahead of the broader market). First introduced: Chapter 6.
  9. Target compatibility Target compatibility asks whether the firm can outdo competitors in fulfilling a segment's needs given the firm's own resources, brand, channels, and operational competencies. A segment can be large, growing, and profitable and still be the wrong target if a stronger incumbent already serves it better than the firm could, because the firm would be buying a fight it cannot win on the merits. The classical example is a small DTC water brand evaluating the bottled-water mass market: the segment is enormous, but the firm's compatibility against Coca-Cola's distribution and Pepsi's shelf presence is so weak that the segment fails the compatibility test even though it passes every size and growth test. First introduced: Chapter 6.
  10. target-rate-of-return pricing Target-rate-of-return pricing sets the posted price at whatever level delivers a pre-stated return on the capital the firm invested in producing the product, calculated at the volume the firm forecasts it will sell. The procedure is common in regulated utilities (electricity, water, natural-gas distribution) where the regulator allows a specified percentage return on the rate base and works backward from that allowed return to the per-unit price the utility can post. The mechanism is cost-plus pricing expressed through the financial-return language regulators are comfortable approving. First introduced: Chapter 10.
  11. task time horizon The task time horizon is a benchmark METR publishes that measures the longest task duration, in human-completion time, at which a frontier AI agent succeeds on at least fifty percent of its attempts. A task time horizon of 131 days means that the model can reliably complete tasks that would take a human working alone roughly 131 calendar days. The horizon has been doubling every roughly four months on the trend since 2023, which is the empirical reason the chapter's tier-4 boundary moves on a measurable curve rather than sitting fixed. First introduced: Chapter 5.
  12. Tasks abstraction The Tasks abstraction is the workflow-tracking primitive that Anthropic added to the Model Context Protocol on November 25, 2025 under the change-request label SEP-1686 (the MCP community uses Specification Enhancement Proposal numbers the way the Python community uses PEP numbers). When the server starts a long-running call, it returns a task identifier immediately, and the client can poll a follow-up endpoint to read back the run's state as it cycles through working, then input_required, then either completed, failed, or cancelled. This replaces the bespoke per-vendor polling that every agent running longer than about ten seconds used to need. First introduced: Chapter 27.
  13. Tau-bench Tau-bench is a 2024 benchmark from Sierra Research that measures whether an AI agent can complete a customer-service task while adhering to a stated written policy (a refund-policy document, a customer-service playbook). The original release covered retail and airline customer-service domains. Tau2-bench, the April 2026 update, adds telecom and voice domains and tracks 38 model entries on a public leaderboard. The benchmark matters more than SWE-bench for a marketing audience because it scores policy-adherence rather than coding ability, and a brand team deploying an agent to respond to customers on the brand's behalf cares exactly about whether the agent followed the playbook. As of May 2026 the airline leader is Claude Sonnet 4.5 at 70 percent across 23 evaluated models. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  14. Tax-Brown-Chandrashekaran recovery effect The Tax-Brown-Chandrashekaran recovery effect, named after the 1998 Journal of Marketing study that first reported it, describes an empirical pattern in which customers whose problems are resolved well often end up more loyal than customers who never had a problem in the first place. The effect is not unconditional. It depends on three properties of the original failure: severity (mild failures recover, severe ones do not), frequency (first-time failures recover, repeat failures do not), and locus (controllable lapses forgive cleanly, stable-incompetence failures do not). First introduced: Chapter 9.
  15. Technological benevolence Technological benevolence is mode four of the New Jim Code framework and Benjamin's most dangerous mode, because the marketing copy on the system stands the auditor down. The pattern is a tool that is sold as a fix for prior-era discrimination but in practice deepens it; the benevolent framing neutralizes oversight because auditors are reluctant to challenge a system marketed as 'fair' or 'inclusive.' The marketing analogs are vendor pitches for 'ethical AI' customer assistants or 'bias-free' targeting platforms whose model card reads as procurement copy rather than as a Mitchell-2019-style audit document. The procurement rule that follows is that a benevolence-framed AI vendor warrants the §26.4 gate stack more, not less, than a vendor that makes no fairness claims at all. First introduced: Chapter 26.
  16. Technology-Market Fit Technology-Market Fit (TMF) is the productized version of product-market fit, which the xbench paper proposed in June 2025 as the right outcome to measure when grading an AI agent. The paper's claim is that a benchmark scoring an agent on real professional work (writing a survey, ranking an influencer pool, drafting a marketing brief) predicts whether the agent will achieve TMF in production more reliably than ARC, MMLU, or function-calling micro-benchmarks, because the production buyer cares whether the agent does the job rather than how well the agent answers reasoning trivia. First introduced: Chapter 27.
  17. Telephone Consumer Protection Act (TCPA) The Telephone Consumer Protection Act, usually shortened to TCPA, is the United States federal statute that governs marketing calls and text messages sent to consumer phone numbers. Congress passed it in 1991 to deal with the early generation of robocalls, and courts later extended it to cover marketing texts once text messaging became a mass channel. The statute requires the brand to obtain prior express written consent from the recipient before any marketing text is sent, which means the buyer has to actively check a box agreeing to receive texts rather than being automatically opted in. Recipients can revoke that consent at any time, usually by replying STOP, and the brand has to honor the revocation immediately. The penalty structure is what makes the TCPA the strictest opt-in regime governing US lifecycle marketing, because recipients can sue the brand directly and recover between 500 and 1,500 dollars in statutory damages for each individual unauthorized message. A 2023 ruling by the Federal Communications Commission (FCC) clarified that any system capable of automated dialing falls within the statute’s scope, which closed a loophole brands had previously tried to use to argue their texting platforms were not automated. First introduced: Chapter 21.
  18. temperature Temperature is a parameter (a number between 0 and 1) that controls how predictable the model's output is. At temperature 0, the model picks the single highest-probability next word at every step, so sending the same prompt twice produces the same answer. At temperature 1, the model samples among likely words rather than picking the top one every time, so the same prompt produces variation between runs. Most APIs default to roughly 0.7. Tasks that need consistency (extracting fields from a CSV, classifying reviews) use low temperature; tasks that need creativity (drafting tagline variants) use higher temperature. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  19. tenfold every twelve months Altman's named multiplier in Three Observations (February 9, 2025): the cost to use a given level of AI falls about 10x every 12 months, against a Moore's-law baseline of 2x per 18 months. Grounded in the roughly 150x token-pricing decline from GPT-4 in early 2023 to GPT-4o in mid-2024 (a single capability level whose per-million-token price fell by that factor in 15 months). Contested on extrapolation grounds (the curve is short, the data is one vendor) but consistent with Altman's July 2025 Federal Reserve update naming scientist throughput at 2-3x and software-engineer throughput at 10x year-over-year. The marketing-team consequence is that any project whose value depends on today's compute cost is likely to be cheaper to run next year, which matters for procurement amortization decisions. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  20. terminal A text-based window on the marketer's laptop in which the user types one command at a time and reads the program's reply on the next line. On a Mac the app is called Terminal and on Windows it is called PowerShell or Windows Terminal. Every command in this chapter that starts with claude, git, or curl is meant to be typed into this window. The undergrad version is the same window an Etsy seller types into when they install a script that downloads their order history; nothing in the chapter requires writing new code from scratch. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  21. terminal-first agent A terminal-first agent is an AI assistant that operates inside the command-line terminal (the typing window programmers use) rather than inside a chat web page. The marketer types a request at the terminal, the agent runs scripts, edits files, and reports the results back to the terminal. Terminal-first agents are useful for scheduled work because the operating system can trigger a terminal command at a fixed time the same way a phone alarm wakes you up; chat pages cannot be triggered that way. First introduced: Chapter 5.
  22. test-retest accuracy floor Within-person test-retest accuracy is the rate at which the same individual gives the same answer to the same survey question when asked on two separate sittings several weeks apart. The number is the human ceiling on prediction accuracy: no model conditioned on an individual’s prior answers should be expected to predict that person’s next-week answers more reliably than the person predicts themselves, because the variation across the two sittings is itself a property of the individual rather than a flaw in the model. The Twin-2K-500 dataset was built explicitly to measure this floor. First introduced: Chapter 24.
  23. test-retest reliability Test-retest reliability is the consistency of a measurement instrument across two separate occasions. Hand the same survey to the same respondent in week one and week six; the test-retest reliability is the correlation between their two sets of answers. A reliability of 1.0 means the instrument is perfectly consistent; 0.0 means the answers are random noise; the working floor in marketing research is around 0.7. The number sets the ceiling on how much variance any external method (including a synthetic respondent) can reasonably hope to reproduce, because the human respondent themselves does not reproduce one hundred percent of their own answer six weeks later. First introduced: Chapter 5.
  24. Texas DTPA The Texas Deceptive Trade Practices Act (DTPA), codified at Tex. Bus. & Com. Code §17.41 et seq., is the Texas state analog to FTC Section 5(a) and reaches the same kinds of unfair or deceptive marketing practices the federal statute does. The Texas remedy is unusually strong: the statute permits treble damages on top of per-violation civil penalties, and it gives both the state Attorney General and private plaintiffs a right of action, which means a brand making a deceptive marketing claim in Texas can face the AG, a consumer class action, and the FTC against the same conduct. First introduced: Chapter 26.
  25. text-to-image Models that take a written prompt and return an image. The 2026 leaders are Google's Nano Banana Pro and Nano Banana 2 (Gemini 3 Pro Image and Gemini 3.1 Flash Image), OpenAI's ChatGPT Images 2.0 (gpt-image-2, April 2026), Midjourney V8.1 (April 2026), Black Forest Labs FLUX.2 Pro (January 2026), Stability AI Stable Diffusion 4 Ultra, Google Imagen 4 Ultra, and Adobe Firefly Image Model 4 inside Creative Cloud. Cost per image runs roughly $0.02 to $0.10 on consumer plans, with brand-consistency control via reference-image conditioning and per-brand fine-tunes. First introduced: Chapter 16.
  26. Text-to-video Models that take a written prompt and return a short video, typically five to thirty seconds at 1080p or 4K. The 2026 leaders are Google DeepMind Veo 3.1 (October 2025, integrated into Gemini, Vertex AI, and Flow, native synchronized audio, 4K up to 60fps, $0.10-0.40 per second on Vertex AI), Runway Gen-4.5 (December 1, 2025, the production-friendly leader at $0.15-0.20 per second with motion brushes and reference-driven character consistency), ByteDance Seedance 2.0 (February 2026, the first model with unified audio-video joint generation, multi-shot storytelling from one prompt, phoneme-level lip-sync in eight-plus languages), and Kuaishou Kling 3.0 (multi-shot sequences with subject consistency across camera angles, the cheapest at scale on Asian-market briefs at ~$0.07/second). OpenAI Sora 2 was discontinued April 26, 2026 (mobile and web) with the API following September 24, 2026. Production economics: a thirty-second polished spot that cost $20,000 to $250,000 in 2022 now runs $50 to $500 in compute plus 4-12 hours of editor time on the same brief. First introduced: Chapter 16.
  27. the bitter lesson The bitter lesson is Richard Sutton's three-page March 2019 essay arguing that, across seventy years of artificial-intelligence research, the methods that scale with raw computation have consistently overtaken the methods that try to encode human expert knowledge by hand. The two scalable methods Sutton names are search (enumerating possible actions and picking the best-scoring one) and learning (improving a policy or a prediction from experience). The historical evidence Sutton walks includes computer chess (hand-coded openings lost to deeper search), Go (centuries of human theory lost to self-play), speech recognition (linguistic grammars lost to statistical methods, then to deep learning), and vision (hand-engineered features lost to convolutional networks). The lesson predicts that vendor pitches whose value depends on hand-coded marketing rules will erode as frontier models absorb the same rules from data. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  28. the constitution The constitution is the natural-language principles document Anthropic trains its Claude models against, the load-bearing artifact in the Constitutional AI training procedure. The January 2026 rewrite of the constitution is 23,000 words long, 80 pages, and released under a Creative Commons CC0 license (which means any brand legal team can read the full text, and any other lab can copy it without asking permission). The document is written directly to the model as a reasoning artifact rather than as a list of rules, so the model learns the reasoning behind each principle and can extend it to new cases the principles do not literally anticipate. The constitution is the citable artifact a marketer can point at when a procurement officer asks how the model decides what is harmful, alongside the URL of the public document. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  29. The dark triad The dark triad is the cluster of three sub-clinical personality traits the academic psychology literature has named since Paulhus and Williams's 2002 paper introduced the term: Machiavellianism (strategic manipulation of other people), narcissism (entitlement and grandiosity), and psychopathy (low empathy and weak impulse control). The trait by itself does not measurably predict sales misconduct when the firm runs a clean compensation plan, because the structural opportunity to cheat simply is not present. The same trait does, however, amplify violation rates by five to twelve times at the high-trait tail of the rep population when the three-attribute pathology (a single-metric bonus, no parallel audit, and unilateral disciplinary discretion) is already present in the firm's design. The takeaway is that screening rep candidates for personality is roughly a waste of effort. Fixing the compensation architecture is the move that actually changes the misconduct rate. First introduced: Chapter 23.
  30. the dollar-store fallacy The phrase from Prahalad's 2004 book for the failure mode of treating an emerging-market consumer as a stripped-down version of a developed-market one. Tata Nano is the canonical instance: a $2,500 car engineered down from the Maruti Alto, marketed as the cheapest car in India, was rejected by buyers who could stretch for the Alto rather than buy a car coded as the one you drive when you cannot afford the next car up. First introduced: Chapter 15.
  31. the muddled middle The muddled middle is Barbara Kahn's name for the position a retailer occupies when its strategic investments are spread across both ends of both axes of the Retailing Success Matrix at the same time. The chain ends up running some price competition, some assortment differentiation, some frictionless features, and some experiential features, without decisively winning any of the four poles. Kahn's empirical claim is that this drift is the dominant mode of retail death in the United States since 2010, observable in the financial reports of the dying retailers (Sears, Toys R Us, RadioShack, Bed Bath and Beyond, JCPenney) two to three years before the bankruptcy filing finally landed. First introduced: Chapter 12.
  32. THINK THINK is the experiential module that runs through cognition, the curiosity, problem-solving, and intellectual surprise a brand prompts in the buyer. Apple's product launches, the New York Times' Spelling Bee puzzle, and IKEA's flat-pack assembly all run on THINK; each is asking the buyer to figure something out and rewarding them when they do. First introduced: Chapter 4.
  33. Thinking Machines Lab Thinking Machines Lab is the research lab Lilian Weng founded after leaving her position as Head of Safety Systems at OpenAI in November 2024, and it is the affiliation under which her May 1, 2025 post Why We Think was written. The post surveyed the entire test-time-compute era (Best-of-N sampling, beam search with process-reward models, self-consistency voting, SCoRe self-correction, DeepSeek-R1's reinforcement-learning-for-reasoning approach, and the thinking-tokens architecture) and reframed all of them as variants of one underlying idea: the planner allocates a variable reasoning budget per sub-task rather than spending a constant amount on every step. First introduced: Chapter 27.
  34. third-party cookies A third-party cookie is a small file dropped on a user’s browser by a domain that is not the one the user is currently visiting, used to track the user across other websites and serve them retargeting ads. Apple’s Safari and Mozilla’s Firefox blocked third-party cookies in the late 2010s. Google’s Chrome retired them in stages through 2024-2025. The 2018-2020 direct-to-consumer attribution math depended on third-party cookies to follow a customer from a Meta ad through a Shopify checkout, and the loss of those cookies is the structural reason MMM and incrementality testing displaced last-click attribution as the 2026 budget-allocation spine. First introduced: Chapter 24.
  35. Thought-Action-Observation loop The three-step trace ReAct emits and re-reads each turn: a plain-English Thought (what the model intends to find out and why), a structured Action (a tool name plus arguments), and the Observation (the tool result the runtime returns). The agent's own audit log, written in language a marketing director can read. First introduced: Chapter 27.
  36. three-agent execution layer The Outreach Q1 2026 release is an agentic loop made up of three coordinated agents that ship as one execution layer rather than three disconnected assistants. The Research Agent auto-refreshes account research on a daily, weekly, or monthly cadence. The Meeting Prep Agent (currently in Beta) surfaces a recommended-talking-points brief before each meeting. The Deal Agent writes deal-stage and methodology updates back into the CRM without rep intervention. First introduced: Chapter 23.
  37. three-degrees-of-influence rule Nicholas Christakis and James Fowler's 2009 re-analysis of the 12,067-person Framingham social network across 32 years found that behaviors, beliefs, and mood states are detectable across one, two, and three degrees of separation, then disappear at four. The attenuation runs roughly 10 to 20 percent at one degree, 6 to 10 percent at two degrees, and 3 to 6 percent at three degrees, which is why the rule is called the three-degrees-of-influence rule. The practical brand reading is that earned-media reach has a structural ceiling: a brand cannot expect a piece of content to keep moving more than three handshakes out from its original audience, no matter how strong the STEPPS load is. First introduced: Chapter 19.
  38. three Ds The three Ds are the audit’s organizing trio of analytical moves, and they earn the label because each one starts with the letter D. The first D is distributions, which means looking at how the population spreads on any one variable, for example spend per customer or recency of the most recent purchase. The second D is decompositions, which means taking a top-line number and breaking it apart along some meaningful dimension, for example splitting this year’s profit into the share that came from the top decile of customers, the share from the middle, and the share from the bottom. The third D is deciles, which is the practice of sorting customers into ten ordered buckets and reading each bucket’s economic contribution separately rather than collapsing every customer into one average. Together, the three Ds force the audit to expose the spread that the average is hiding. First introduced: Chapter 24.
  39. three-frame readiness audit The three-frame readiness audit is the converging framework the 2025-2026 sales-AI literature uses to decide whether a firm is actually ready to deploy a Tier 3 or Tier 4 stack, or whether the firm should defer the rollout for another quarter. The first frame is trust. The audit checks the rep-manager-leader trust calibration against the Salesforce-reported floor of thirty-five percent trust in AI-generated data, which is the line the Hartmann, Habel, and Hunter 2026 editorial argues a firm has to clear before any AI deployment is going to produce measurable lift. The second frame is tension. The audit diagnoses the information-based and power-based tensions between the sales and marketing functions against the four moderator variables the Industrial Marketing Management 2026 paper named, which are cross-functional integration, perceived usefulness of the tools, cooperative-management strategy, and competitive-management strategy. The third frame is the agentic frame. The audit reads the firm against the seven-priority research agenda Gonzalez, Habel, and Hunter published in the 2026 Journal of Business Research on the autonomous-AI-agent transition. The audit then classifies the firm against all three frames, names the weakest of the three as the rate-limiting precondition for the next-quarter rollout iteration, and outputs the specific mitigation recipe that addresses whichever frame is the binding constraint. First introduced: Chapter 23.
  40. three-loyalties The three-loyalties framework, from Frederick Reichheld’s 1996 book The Loyalty Effect, argues that customer loyalty, employee loyalty, and investor loyalty reinforce each other rather than acting as independent objectives the firm trades off between. A firm that retains its employees retains the institutional knowledge those employees carry, which is what produces the customer loyalty in the first place. A firm that retains its customers produces the predictable cash flows that retain its investors, which is what gives the firm the patience to keep investing in the employee retention. Programs that target one of the three loyalties at the expense of the other two tend to unwind, because the three are bolted together. First introduced: Chapter 9.
  41. three principles of beneficial AI Russell's three principles of beneficial AI, from Human Compatible (2019), are the design constraints on the humans building an AI system rather than rules hard-coded into the system itself. Principle one: the machine's only objective is to maximize the realization of human preferences. Principle two: the machine is initially uncertain about what those preferences are. Principle three: the ultimate source of information about human preferences is human behavior. A system built under all three principles behaves as an assistant rather than as an autonomous optimizer, because the uncertainty in principle two pays for the corrigibility property the chapter's human-fallback section depends on. First introduced: Chapter 26.
  42. three-tier channel A three-tier channel runs manufacturer to wholesaler to jobber to retailer to buyer, adding a jobber (a small specialized intermediary that buys from the wholesaler and resells to retailers too small for the wholesaler to serve directly) between the wholesaler and the retailer. The structure shows up in industries with extreme retail fragmentation, such as ethnic-foods importers serving small independent grocers in immigrant neighborhoods that the regional wholesaler cannot economically reach door by door. First introduced: Chapter 11.
  43. three-trait WMD diagnostic Cathy O'Neil's three-trait diagnostic, published in Weapons of Math Destruction (2016), names the three properties that together turn an algorithmic decision into a population-level harm. The first trait is opacity, where the people affected by the decision cannot understand the basis on which it was made. The second is scale, where a single algorithm acts on many people at once so individual errors compound. The third is damage, where the consequences are severe enough that the system warrants external audit. A marketing-tech system scoring credit eligibility for thousands of buyers without an appeal channel hits all three. First introduced: Chapter 26.
  44. Tier distribution Tier structure is the set of named status levels in a loyalty program. Sephora uses Insider, VIB, and Rouge; Costco uses Gold Star and Executive; Delta uses Silver, Gold, Platinum, and Diamond Medallion. The thresholds that move a member from one tier to the next are a strategic lever rather than a fixed feature. Raising the threshold tightens the program and signals exclusivity, at the cost of shrinking the base of members who qualify; lowering it broadens the program and signals welcome, at the cost of making the badge feel less meaningful to the members who reach the top. First introduced: Chapter 11.
  45. TikTok Shop TikTok Shop is TikTok's in-app marketplace, which is integrated directly with the For You feed and the creator content that runs through it. Sellers list products that then appear inside shoppable videos, in livestreams, and in a dedicated Shop tab on the app. Discovery is algorithmic and creator-driven rather than search-driven, which means buyers find products because a creator featured them, not because the buyer searched for the category. The 2026 take-rate runs roughly 6 percent referral fee for most categories, with the rate adjusted for the category: 8 percent on fashion and apparel, 5 percent on beauty, 3 percent on electronics, and 2 percent on food and beverage. On top of that, the seller pays 1 to 4 percent in payment processing, and creator affiliate commissions add another 10 to 30 percent on attributed orders. Independent shipping ended on March 31, 2026, which means all US sellers must now use Fulfilled By TikTok, Upgraded TikTok Shipping, or Collections by TikTok to ship their orders. First introduced: Chapter 13.
  46. TikTok USDS Joint Venture LLC TikTok USDS Joint Venture LLC is the new corporate entity that took over TikTok's U.S. operations in January 2026 after the PAFACA-forced divestiture closed. An Oracle-led consortium of U.S. investors holds 80.1 percent and ByteDance retains 19.9 percent (just under the foreign-adversary-control threshold the law set), while the recommendation algorithm itself was licensed back from ByteDance rather than transferred outright. First introduced: Chapter 15.
  47. time inconsistency Time inconsistency is what behavioral economists call the systematic gap between what a buyer prefers when the choice is between two future moments and what the same buyer prefers when one of those moments is right now. The same person who picks $100 in two years over $90 in one year flips and picks $90 today over $100 in one year, even though the two choices are structurally identical (one extra year of waiting in exchange for the same $10). The standard economic model says preferences should not flip like that; the empirical record says they reliably do. First introduced: Chapter 4.
  48. time-to-live Time-to-live, abbreviated TTL, is the length of time a cached prompt prefix stays valid on the provider’s side before it is evicted and the next call has to re-pay the write cost. Anthropic offers two TTL choices on the 2026 cache. The 5-minute option is cheaper to write but evicts quickly, which suits an agent that fires many calls in rapid succession during the same human session. The 1-hour option costs more on the initial write but stays alive long enough to serve workflows that land at human pace, where a marketer types one prompt, reads the reply, thinks for a few minutes, then types the next prompt. The team picks the TTL whose breakeven (how many follow-up reads it takes to recoup the higher write cost) matches the rhythm of how the workflow actually fires. First introduced: Chapter 24.
  49. time utility Time utility is the value a channel creates by making the product available at the moment the buyer wants it, rather than days or weeks later. A three-week ship date erases the value at the same retail price, because the buyer either substitutes a competing brand that ships in two days or abandons the purchase altogether. First introduced: Chapter 11.
  50. token A token is the chunk of text a language model actually reads, writes, and is billed against. The chunk is roughly three-quarters of an English word: common short words become one token each, while long or rare words split into two or three smaller pieces. A typical 10-page marketing brief is roughly 4,000 tokens. Vendors quote pricing per million tokens, so a marketer can multiply token count by the per-million rate to see what one call will cost. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  51. Tokenism Tokenism is using a single visible representative of an underrepresented group as evidence of a brand's broader commitment, without changing the underlying creative team, supplier mix, hiring, or budget allocation. The buyer reads through it almost immediately because the structural cues (who narrates, who is paid, who is on the team) do not match the surface cues. First introduced: Chapter 15.
  52. tokens A token is the unit a language model actually reads, and it is a chunk of characters that often corresponds to a word or a piece of a word. English text averages roughly 1.3 tokens per word, because short common words become one token each while long or rare words split into two or three smaller pieces. The model's bill is metered in tokens rather than in characters or words, which is why the marketer needs to convert word counts to token counts before estimating the cost of a long workflow. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  53. tool call A tool call is a structured request the model emits, naming a tool the runtime should run and the parameters the model wants the tool called with. The model does not run the tool itself; it just describes the call it wants made. The runtime parses the tool call from the model's output, executes the underlying tool, and feeds the result back as a new message in the agent loop. A typical tool call looks like fetch_url("news.ycombinator.com"), and the runtime returns the HTML of the page so the model can read what was there. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  54. tool-result clearing The Claude API primitive clear_tool_uses_20250919, which mechanically replaces old tool-result blocks with the placeholder text [cleared to save context] once the input-token count crosses a configurable trigger. Keeps the call record so the agent knows the call happened, but discards the now-stale payload. No inference cost. First introduced: Chapter 27.
  55. tool spec A tool spec is the written description of a tool's name, its parameters, and the type of each parameter, written in a structured format the model can parse. Anthropic and OpenAI both use the same JSON-schema shape for tool specs, which means a tool descriptor written for one provider runs almost unchanged on the other and the model reads the spec at every turn to decide which tool to call with which arguments. First introduced: Chapter 27.
  56. tool use Tool use is the general name for the agent-loop mechanism, where a language model is handed a list of tools (functions) and decides which to call between its own thinking turns. The model itself does not actually run the tool. It emits a structured request describing the tool call it wants made, and the runtime that wraps the model is what executes the actual tool and feeds the result back into the next model turn. Tool use and function calling are two names for the same mechanism. OpenAI shipped 'function calling' first in June 2023, Anthropic shipped 'tool use' shortly after, and the 2026 industry now treats the terms as interchangeable in conversation and documentation. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  57. Toolformer Toolformer is a 2023 paper by Timo Schick and his Meta AI co-authors that gave the field its first self-supervised method for teaching a base language model how to call external APIs on its own. The procedure is mechanical: the model writes its own training examples by inserting candidate tool calls into ordinary sentences from a giant text corpus, runs each candidate against the real tool, and keeps only the candidates where seeing the tool's return value makes the rest of the sentence easier to predict. Toolformer is the training-time counterpart to ReAct's prompt-time tool use, and the line of descent runs from this paper to OpenAI's Function Calling API in June 2023, then to Anthropic's tool-use API in May 2024, then to the Model Context Protocol in November 2024. First introduced: Chapter 27.
  58. top-five-state share The top-five-state share is the Anthropic Economic Index March 2026 measure of geographic concentration in U.S. Claude usage: the share of total national Claude usage held by the top five U.S. states (California, New York, Texas, Massachusetts, Washington), tracked monthly. The share moved from 30 percent of national usage in August 2025 to 24 percent in February 2026, a six-percentage-point decline in six months. The deceleration in the convergence rate pushed Anthropic's projected equalization horizon (the year by which usage will spread evenly across states) from 2-to-5 years out to 5-to-9 years. In plain language, the geographic-equalization curve flattened faster than Anthropic's earlier model predicted, which means the gap between heavy-using and light-using states is closing more slowly than the company expected six months earlier. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  59. Topics API The Topics API was a Google Privacy Sandbox feature that would have classified the user's recent browsing into a small set of coarse interest topics (such as 'fitness' or 'travel') that the browser then shared with ad platforms in place of an individual cross-site identifier. The Topics API replaced the failed earlier FLoC (Federated Learning of Cohorts) proposal that had generated regulator pushback, and the Topics API itself was central to the Privacy Sandbox bundle that Google cancelled on October 7, 2025. First introduced: Chapter 18.
  60. total-cost analysis Total-cost analysis is the logistics discipline of summing all logistics cost categories together, rather than minimizing any one of them in isolation. The categories are transportation cost (moving the goods), inventory carrying cost (holding the goods, typically twenty to thirty percent of inventory value per year once warehousing, financing, insurance, and obsolescence are summed), warehousing cost (the facility itself), order-processing and information-systems cost (the software and people that run the orders), and lot-quantity cost (the trade-off between large efficient batches and small flexible ones). The discipline says cheaper transportation often means slower transportation, which means more in-transit inventory, which means more carrying cost, so minimizing any single line raises the total. The decision rule is to minimize the sum subject to a customer-service-level constraint, which is the operational counterpart to the make-or-buy question Anderson and Weitz framed for marketing channels. First introduced: Chapter 11.
  61. touchpoint A touchpoint is any place a customer encounters the brand, from the package on the shelf to the receipt at the checkout to the customer-service phone call after the purchase. The set of all touchpoints across the customer's journey is the touchpoint architecture, and each touchpoint either reinforces or weakens the central brand articulation. A brand that delivers a premium-quality promise in its advertising but rude service on the phone has a touchpoint architecture in tension with its brand promise. First introduced: Chapter 7.
  62. Tourangeau-Rips-Rasinski four-stage cognitive model of survey response A model of how a respondent actually answers a survey question, introduced by Roger Tourangeau (Westat), Lance Rips (Northwestern), and Kenneth Rasinski (NORC) in The Psychology of Survey Response (Cambridge, 2000). The model says every answer is the output of four cognitive steps run in order: read and understand the question (comprehension), pull the relevant memory or attitude out of long-term storage (retrieval), build an answer by comparing the retrieved material against the standard the question implies (judgment), and pick the response option the questionnaire offers that comes closest to that internal answer (response/mapping). Each step has its own characteristic failure modes, and the four-stage diagnostic is the standard pre-fielding instrument for finding which step a given draft question is going to break on. First introduced: Chapter 5.
  63. trace step A trace step is a single Thought-Action-Observation trio inside the ReAct loop, meaning one plan step the model wrote in plain English, one tool call the model issued as a structured JSON request, and one observation the runtime fed back as the tool's return value. The trace step is the atomic unit a 2026 eval harness scores, because the harness rates whether the Thought was a reasonable plan, whether the Action reached the right tool with the right arguments, and whether the Observation actually got re-read on the next turn, rather than reducing the whole run to a single pass-or-fail boolean. First introduced: Chapter 27.
  64. Traceability Traceability is the overarching concern in Mittelstadt and Floridi's six-concerns map, spanning every stage of the AI pipeline rather than sitting at any one of them. The concern is the ability to reconstruct, after the fact, who deployed what algorithm under what conditions, on which data, and under what oversight. Without traceability there is no basis for a fairness audit, an explainability claim, or an accountability assignment, because the post-hoc investigator cannot tell which decision came from which model version on which training data. The mitigation is to log every input, every model version, every deployment configuration, and every decision; retain those logs for the full regulatory horizon (typically six years under GDPR Article 30); and structure them so they can be queried during an adverse-event investigation rather than parsed from raw output dumps. First introduced: Chapter 26.
  65.
