Tracer Tracer is a marketing-data-cleanup platform whose unglamorous but load-bearing job is to standardize the fragmented data a large firm holds across its many marketing platforms, retail-partner systems, and finance warehouse, so that the modeling layer above it can read consistent inputs instead of arguing with the data. In Hershey's May 2026 deployment, Tracer sits between the firm's source systems and the Mutinex modeling platform, acting as the data plumbing that lets the agentic media-mix-model workflow (a media-mix model, or MMM, is the statistical model that decomposes revenue across each marketing channel the firm bought) run on a monthly cadence rather than as the annual vendor-engineering project it used to be. First introduced: Chapter 25.
  66. Tracked Capability Levels (TCLs) A Tracked Capability Level (TCL) sits one step below a Critical Capability Level in DeepMind's risk taxonomy. The idea is that a model approaching but not yet crossing a CCL still warrants additional scrutiny, so the lab flags the model at the TCL tier and runs a richer evaluation pipeline as the model gets closer to the threshold. The April 17, 2026 update applied TCLs to a subset of capability domains, with the lab committing to expand the coverage domain by domain over the course of the year. First introduced: Chapter 26.
  67. Tracker firms Tracker firms are market-research companies that run continuous buyer panels and sell the resulting data to brand teams. The four largest in 2026 are Kantar, Ipsos, Nielsen (now NielsenIQ), and YouGov. A brand team subscribes to a panel for several thousand dollars per quarter and receives standardized brand-tracking data the firm cannot generate on its own. First introduced: Chapter 7.
  68. Trade Trade is a free Shopify theme optimized for business-to-business and wholesale catalogs rather than for direct-to-consumer retail. It includes wholesale-pricing tiers that let the merchant set different prices for different customer accounts, customer-specific catalog visibility so the merchant can show or hide products to certain buyer groups, and bulk-order forms designed for retailers placing large orders at once. The candle-store student should pick Trade only if the business model is wholesale-first, meaning the candles are being sold to other retailers for resale rather than to the end consumer directly. First introduced: Chapter 13.
  69. Trade Assurance Trade Assurance is Alibaba's free buyer-protection program, which covers three things: on-time delivery against the agreed timeline, product quality against the specification the seller and buyer agreed to in writing, and refund eligibility if either of the first two conditions fail to be met. The buyer pays the supplier through Alibaba's escrow-style flow, where Alibaba holds the money and only releases funds to the supplier after the buyer confirms receipt and quality. Critically, Trade Assurance is a payment-protection program and not a supplier-verification program. A supplier listed under Trade Assurance can still be a trading company misrepresenting itself as a manufacturer, or a newly registered company with no track record at all. Verification is a separate step. The buyer pays for an on-site inspection from a third party like SGS, Bureau Veritas, or TUV Rheinland before committing capital to a larger production run. First introduced: Chapter 13.
  70. Trader Joe's Trader Joe's is a private-label grocery chain with roughly 580 stores in the United States as of 2025, generating around 16 billion dollars in annual revenue. The brand is famous in marketing circles for choosing the opposite of every default in the grocery industry: it carries about 4,000 SKUs versus the 30,000 a typical supermarket stocks, runs no loyalty program, and spends almost nothing on advertising, yet still produces some of the highest per-square-foot sales in retail. The brand returns as a worked example in chapter 9 on Distribution. First introduced: Chapter 17.
  71. traffic Traffic is the estimated number of times a keyword is searched in the App Store per day. Keyword tools like AppTweak, Sensor Tower, and Mobile Action infer traffic from rank fluctuations they observe after controlled listing changes. A keyword with high traffic plus low difficulty plus high relevance is the asymmetric bet every ASO strategy is hunting for, because such a keyword sends a lot of users to the listing without forcing the listing to compete against the category leader. First introduced: Chapter 14.
  72. training corpora Training corpora are the collections of text an AI model reads during pre-training in order to learn language: open-web pages, digitized books, Reddit threads, YouTube transcripts, and anything else the model's builders chose to include. The marketing relevance is that what the model can quote tomorrow is what it read during training today, so a brand whose advocates post on Reddit and YouTube this year is feeding the corpus that next year's model versions will pre-train on. First introduced: Chapter 18.
  73. training corpus A training corpus is the full pile of text used to train a language model. The word 'corpus' comes from linguistics, where it has long been the standard term for any organized body of texts a research project draws on. In the AI context, 'corpus' and 'training data' refer to the same thing: the books, web pages, articles, code, and conversations that the model read during training and now produces patterns from. First introduced: Chapter 4.
  74. training data Training data is the full pile of text the model was shown while it was being built, drawn from filtered web pages, books, articles, and code. During an ordinary chat the model does not read the live internet at all unless the user explicitly hands it a search tool, which means the answer the buyer reads is reconstructed from the patterns the model picked up across that training pile rather than from anything happening on the web right now. First introduced: Chapter 4.
  75. trajectory basis The trajectory basis is a sixth segmentation basis added to the classical four (geographic, demographic, psychographic, behavioral). Instead of describing the buyer at one moment in time, the trajectory basis describes how the buyer is moving across time on five dimensions: Length of relationship, Recency, Frequency, Monetary value, and Satisfaction (the LRFMS acronym). A customer rising on those five dimensions sorts differently than a customer with identical snapshot values who is falling. The 2024 DCSLM method (Liu et al., Scientific Reports) encodes each customer as a multivariate time series and clusters with dynamic time warping; on a parts-agent validation dataset, static RFM and dynamic LRFMS disagreed on cluster assignment for roughly half the customer base at the one-year horizon. First introduced: Chapter 6.
  76. Transformative effects Transformative effects is the second normative concern in Mittelstadt and Floridi's six-concerns map, sitting at the downstream-consequence stage of the AI pipeline. The concern is that the deployed algorithm changes the social context it operates in, which then invalidates the assumptions the model was trained against and produces harms the model was not designed to anticipate. A recommendation system trained on past behavior changes future behavior in ways that make the original training data no longer descriptive of the population the system now operates on; a credit-scoring model deployed at scale shifts the financial behavior of the scored population in ways the original data could not have measured. The mitigation is to treat the deployment itself as policy, with continuous monitoring for transformative effects and adjustment when those effects diverge from stated intent, rather than running a one-shot pre-launch audit and walking away. First introduced: Chapter 26.
  77. transformer A transformer is the neural-network architecture every modern frontier language model is built on. Introduced in a June 2017 Google paper titled 'Attention Is All You Need,' the transformer replaced the older recurrent-network designs used in 2010s language work with a parallel attention mechanism that lets each token in the input look at every other token directly. Every Claude, GPT, Gemini, and Llama released since 2018 is a transformer under the hood. The differences a marketer notices between them (price, speed, smarts, context window size) come from the training data and the parameter count, not from the underlying recipe, which is the same eight-year-old paper across every vendor. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  78. transgressive pricing Transgressive pricing, which Wathieu and Bertini documented in their 2007 Marketing Science paper, is a price deliberately set above the buyer's initially revealed willingness-to-pay. The firm gives up some immediate sales in exchange for getting the remaining buyers to slow down and reflect on whether the product offers benefits they had not initially weighted. Their experiments in organic-lettuce and fair-trade-coffee categories demonstrated the mechanism: a higher posted price actively triggered deliberation about the underlying quality and values story, which raised post-reflection WTP for the subset of buyers who decided the distinctive benefit applied to them. First introduced: Chapter 10.
  79. transient advantage A transient advantage is a competitive advantage that has a finite and predictable life cycle running through five phases: launch, ramp-up, exploit, reconfigure, and disengage. Rita McGrath's research from 2000 through 2019 measured that advantages now last roughly two to five years before competitors erode them, where in the Porter-era research of the 1980s the same advantages would have lasted ten to twenty years. First introduced: Chapter 3.
  80. transition zone The transition zone is the first five to fifteen feet inside the store entrance, sometimes called the decompression zone because shoppers traverse it while their eyes are still adjusting from outdoor light and their bodies are still slowing from the walk in. Any merchandise or signage placed in that strip is functionally invisible, which is why experienced retailers leave the transition zone deliberately empty and reserve the high-value merchandise for the five-to-fifteen-pace stretch beyond it where the shopper has actually started looking. First introduced: Chapter 12.
  81. Transparency in Frontier Artificial Intelligence Act The Transparency in Frontier Artificial Intelligence Act (TFAIA) is California Senate Bill 53, signed by Governor Newsom on September 29, 2025 and effective January 1, 2026. It is the first US state law in force directly regulating frontier AI development rather than AI deployment. The Act binds any developer that has trained a foundation model above 10^26 cumulative floating-point operations of compute (a threshold roughly an order of magnitude higher than the EU AI Act's GPAI line), with enhanced disclosure obligations for large frontier developers above $500 million in annual gross revenue. The California Attorney General enforces, with civil penalties up to one million dollars per violation. First introduced: Chapter 26.
  82. transparent customization Transparent customization is the quadrant where the product fits each buyer's specific needs without ever asking the buyer to configure anything and without telling the buyer that customization is happening at all. A hotel that quietly assigns a returning guest to her preferred floor based on her past stays is one common example, because the guest never asked for the floor and may not even consciously notice that she got it. A banking app that pre-fills next month's recurring bills from the prior month's pattern is another, since the customer experiences a faster checkout without ever knowing the app inferred a pattern from her history. The buyer experiences the result as the product simply working well, and the firm captures the differentiation benefit without the friction of asking buyers to specify parameters they may not be able to articulate. First introduced: Chapter 8.
  83. treasure-hunt The treasure-hunt assortment is the retailer's choice to vary, rotate, and deliberately make inconsistent the merchandise across stores and across weeks, so that the shopping trip produces discovery rather than fulfillment of a planned list. Costco runs this strategy at the warehouse-club end by rotating limited-time SKUs into the middle of the floor, while off-price chains like TJ Maxx run it across the entire format because the assortment itself changes with whatever inventory the buyers managed to source that month. First introduced: Chapter 12.
  84. Trends-Implications-Advice (TIA) Trends-Implications-Advice (TIA) is the three-step opening sequence Anthony Iannarino puts underneath the Four Levels of Value as the way a seller actually starts a competitive-displacement conversation. Step one names a structural trend in the buyer's industry that the buyer's current vendor has not yet raised, presented with proof from a public document the buyer can verify. Step two walks the buyer through the implications of that trend for her specific company, naming the line items on her profit and loss statement that are at risk. Step three delivers the seller's actual advice on what the buyer should do in the next planning cycle, with the seller's product positioned as one part of the response. The whole sequence runs in a thirty-minute first meeting using a three-page deck. First introduced: Chapter 23.
  85. trial-and-error learning Trial-and-error learning is a project-management strategy from Loch, De Meyer, and Pich's Managing the Unknown chapter 6, where the team runs a single path with a deliberately under-specified plan, names the conditions that would trigger a plan revision in advance, and gives the project sponsor explicit authorization to change the objective itself when one of those triggers fires. The approach fits when two conditions hold. First, the uncertainties are coupled, which means one part of the system affects another in ways the team cannot cleanly decompose into separate sub-problems. Second, the cost of running parallel paths is too high to absorb. Airbus's A380 flutter-mode redesign in 2003 is the canonical case: the wing-flutter problem coupled aerodynamics, structural dynamics, and engine placement in ways no separate-paths approach could untangle, and parallel billion-dollar aircraft programs were not affordable, so Airbus ran a single path with a board-authorized revision trigger that allowed the engineering objective itself to change when the flutter signature emerged in testing. First introduced: Chapter 8.
  86. Triple-A supply chain A Triple-A supply chain is one that is simultaneously agile (responsive to short-term demand and supply shocks within hours rather than weeks), adaptable (capable of evolving the supplier base and network structure as markets shift across years), and aligned (so every member of the chain has incentives that are consistent with the chain’s overall performance rather than just the member’s own). Hau L. Lee introduced the framework in a 2004 Harvard Business Review article drawn from a fifteen-year research program at Stanford, and his core claim was that supply chains optimized only for cost and speed deteriorate over time, while supply chains that hold all three A properties together get ahead of their rivals. First introduced: Chapter 11.
  87. Triple Whale Triple Whale is an analytics platform built specifically for direct-to-consumer brands. It consolidates Shopify orders, paid-media spend across Meta and Google and TikTok and Pinterest, email and SMS revenue from Klaviyo and Postscript, and customer profile data into a single dashboard the operator can read each morning. The platform was built for the world that emerged after Apple's iOS 14.5 privacy update broke the IDFA tracking identifier, which is the world where Meta's and Google's own attribution numbers are no longer reliable. Triple Whale triangulates which channel actually drove each order by combining UTM parameters in the URL, on-site pixel data, and short post-purchase surveys that ask the buyer how she found the brand. Pricing starts at $129 per month for early-stage brands and rises with revenue. First introduced: Chapter 13.
  88. Trompenaars Fons Trompenaars is a Dutch consultant who, with Charles Hampden-Turner, proposed a 1997 cultural framework built around seven dimensions covering how a society handles relationships, time, and the environment. Where Hofstede produces axes to score against, Trompenaars produces dilemmas to reconcile (universalism vs. particularism, sequential vs. synchronic time, and so on). First introduced: Chapter 15.
  89. trunk test The trunk test is Steve Krug's diagnostic from Don't Make Me Think. The analyst opens a random page on the brand's site (as if they had been blindfolded, driven to a strange location, and shown the page) and then has thirty seconds to answer six questions: what site is this, what page am I on, what are the major sections of this site, what are the local navigation options, where am I in the overall hierarchy, and how do I search. Any page that fails to answer any one of the six in thirty seconds is leaking conversion regardless of how high it ranks or how often it gets cited. First introduced: Chapter 18.
  90. trust elasticities An elasticity is the rate at which one thing moves when another thing moves. A trust elasticity in this editorial's usage is the rate at which the copilot's measured productivity lift moves when the rep, manager, leader, or peer's trust in the tool moves. The contrast is to a productivity elasticity, which would measure productivity against tool features or model size. Hartmann, Habel, and Hunter argue the productivity numbers in the AI sales literature are actually trust elasticities in disguise, because the lift evaporates whenever the trust calibration breaks at any tier. First introduced: Chapter 23.
  91. Trust Layer The Trust Layer is Salesforce's content-moderation surface that every Agentforce output passes through before it ships to a customer. It runs content moderation against the operator's policy rules, redacts personally identifiable information from the output, and gates low-confidence answers behind a human approval step. Together with the Agentforce Testing Center (the offline eval harness Salesforce ships for platform-scale rubric scoring), the Trust Layer is the published reflection layer for the entire platform. First introduced: Chapter 27.
  92. TTB The Alcohol and Tobacco Tax and Trade Bureau (TTB) is the US Treasury Department agency that enforces the Federal Alcohol Administration Act (27 USC §201 et seq.), the federal statute governing labeling and advertising of distilled spirits, wine, and malt beverages. The implementing regulations sit at 27 CFR Parts 4, 5, and 7, and they cover three layers: prohibited claims (no curative properties, no unsubstantiated quality guarantees), required disclosures (alcohol content, government warning text), and the rules around brand names and addresses. On top of TTB's federal floor, each state's alcohol-control board adds state-specific advertising rules covering tied-house prohibitions (which retailers a brand may incentivize), sampling limits, hours-of-publication restrictions, and minimum setbacks from schools and youth-serving institutions. First introduced: Chapter 26.
  93. Turing Award The Turing Award is the annual prize the Association for Computing Machinery has awarded since 1966 to recognize lasting contributions of major technical importance to computer science, and is often called computing's Nobel Prize. Donald Knuth received the award in 1974 for fundamental contributions to the analysis of algorithms. Hinton, LeCun, and Bengio shared the 2018 award for the deep-learning lineage section 1.3 traces. Sutton and Barto shared the 2024 award for reinforcement learning, announced March 5, 2025. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  94. Turing Experiment The Turing Experiment, introduced by Aher, Arriaga, and Kalai in their 2023 ICML paper, is a structured protocol for evaluating synthetic respondents. The researcher prompts a language model to simulate participants in a published human-subject study, then compares the population-level distribution of the model's answers to the population-level distribution of the original human answers. The Turing Experiment measures replication of a population-level pattern, not the realism of any single response. Open-source code at github.com/microsoft/turing-experiments. First introduced: Chapter 5.
  95. Twin-2K-500 Twin-2K-500 is a publicly released four-wave Prolific survey dataset of 2,058 U.S. participants, each of whom answered roughly 500 questions across demographics, personality, decision-making style, behavioral-economics replications, and a 40-product pricing study with randomly assigned price points. The fourth wave deliberately re-asks a subset of earlier-wave items on the same people about four to six weeks later, which lets a researcher measure how consistently the same person answers the same question on two separate days. That within-person test-retest floor is the benchmark against which any synthetic or digital-twin panel can be scored. Toubia and five coauthors published the dataset in Marketing Science 44(6):1446-1455 in 2025. First introduced: Chapter 24.
  96. two-part pricing Two-part pricing is a structure where the buyer pays both a fixed access fee and a separate per-unit charge for what they actually consume. A Costco membership at $65 a year unlocks the per-item shelf prices inside the warehouse; a Disney World ticket grants entry plus the per-purchase prices on food, drinks, and merchandise inside the park; a phone plan with a $30 monthly base fee adds $0.10 per text on top. The structure captures more buyer surplus across a mixed-volume population than a single posted price would, because heavy users effectively pay less per unit while light users still cover the firm's fixed costs through the access fee. First introduced: Chapter 10.
  97. two-tailed A two-tailed Gate 3 risk is the recognition that a new-product business case can fail on either side of its demand forecast, not only when actual demand falls short. Over-forecast launches like the Apple Vision Pro and the Humane Ai Pin shipped against optimistic forecasts and arrived at empty showrooms. Under-forecast launches like Wegovy and Zepbound shipped against conservative forecasts and arrived into FDA shortage listings and patient leakage to compounded substitutes. Both failure modes share one root cause: the business case answered with a single point estimate when Loch's decision-under-uncertainty framework actually requires a probability distribution showing how wide the demand uncertainty was. The fix is to forecast a range with explicit upside and downside scenarios rather than a single number. First introduced: Chapter 8.
  98. two-tier channel A two-tier channel runs manufacturer to wholesaler to retailer to final buyer, adding a wholesaler upstream of the retailer to bridge the gap between full-truckload shipments from the factory and the small mixed-case orders a small retailer can actually take in. The arrangement is the standard form for fragmented categories where small retailers cannot economically buy in manufacturer-direct quantities, such as independent neighborhood grocery stores in many regional markets that lack the volume to take a manufacturer’s minimum order alone. First introduced: Chapter 11.
  99. Type I error A Type I error is the mistake of declaring a difference between control and treatment to be real when in fact no real difference exists, which is the same thing as a false positive. The alpha threshold (conventionally set at 0.05) is the long-run rate at which a Type I error happens by construction, because the procedure is designed so that across many hypothetical reruns of the same test on truly-identical groups, the test will still flag a false positive about five percent of the time. The mirror-image mistake is called a Type II error, which is the failure to detect an effect that is genuinely there because the sample was too small or the effect was too subtle to clear the threshold. Bigger samples shrink Type II errors, because more data makes real effects easier to see, while the alpha threshold the analyst chose at the start controls how many Type I errors slip through. First introduced: Chapter 5.
  100. Type M The Type M (magnitude) error is the average factor by which a barely-significant winner overshoots the true effect, among underpowered tests that happen to cross the significance threshold. Andrew Gelman and John Carlin introduced the diagnostic in their 2014 paper. To make the number concrete: at twenty percent statistical power, the average winning estimate is roughly twice the truth, and at ten percent power it is three to four times the truth. The way to use this diagnostic is to run a retrodesign post-mortem on any A/B winner that came in barely above the conventional zero-point-zero-five significance threshold, especially when the test was small. First introduced: Chapter 5.
  101. Type S The Type S (sign) error is the probability that, when an underpowered A/B test crosses the conventional zero-point-zero-five significance threshold, the estimated effect actually has the opposite sign from the true effect (the test reports the treatment as a winner when it was really a loser, or vice versa). The probability is very small in well-powered studies, but it climbs sharply once statistical power falls below thirty percent. Concretely: a study with power above zero-point-eight keeps the Type S rate close to zero, while a study with power at zero-point-one runs the Type S rate up to roughly one in four. The diagnostic comes from Gelman and Carlin's 2014 paper Beyond Power Calculations. First introduced: Chapter 5.
21 // u

U U

  1. unaided brand recall Unaided brand recall is what a periodic survey measures when it asks a sample of buyers in the category whether they can name the brand without being prompted with a list of options. Picture a researcher asking 'when you think of insulated drinkware, which brand comes to mind first?' and recording every brand the buyer names without help: the percentage of respondents who recall a specific brand becomes that brand's unaided recall share, tracked quarter over quarter. This is the only direct measure of mental availability a brand can buy on the open market, because it does not require the brand to own the buyer's transaction record to compute it. First introduced: Chapter 17.
  2. unaided citation share Unaided citation share is the metric you measure when the brand is not named in the prompt at all. The test is to ask ChatGPT (or Claude or Perplexity) something like 'best CRM for a small team' and then count which brands appear in the answer. The unaided variant is the harder metric to win, but it is also the one that maps directly to the brand's organic mental availability inside the AI engine, which is why most serious GEO programs treat it as the primary KPI. First introduced: Chapter 18.
  3. uncanny valley The uncanny valley is a 1970s framing by Japanese roboticist Masahiro Mori, extended into perceptual psychology over the following decades. The empirical claim is that as a synthetic human likeness approaches realism without quite reaching it, viewer affinity drops sharply into a region of discomfort before climbing again at full photorealism. The drop region is the 'valley' in the name. The 2024-2026 generative-video models routinely land in this drop zone when they render close-up faces, which is why holiday and family-occasion campaigns built end-to-end from generative video are unusually likely to fail the warmth test with viewers even when the technical render quality is competent. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  4. uncontrollable The uncontrollable variables are the conditions the retailer has to respond to but cannot actually move, such as shifts in what consumers want, a competitor's pricing decision, the broader economy, and the platforms shoppers are spending their time on. A brand manager who burns budget trying to change TikTok's algorithm is treating an uncontrollable like a controllable, and that is the most expensive mistake in the category. First introduced: Chapter 12.
  5. Undefined Undefined alignment is the lowest rung of the Kotler-Rackham-Krishnaswamy 2006 ladder. The two functions have grown up independently, each is preoccupied with its own objectives, and meetings between them only happen after a conflict has already broken out. There is no shared definition of a qualified lead, no shared scorecard, and no agreed escalation path when the two functions disagree about a customer. First introduced: Chapter 23.
  6. Undifferentiated targeting Undifferentiated targeting is the coverage strategy that ignores segments entirely and offers one product to the whole market, on the bet that the cost advantage of running a single production line at scale more than offsets the buyers who would have preferred something tailored to them. Ford's Model T in the 1910s is the foundational case (one car, one color, one price, one buyer profile), and Coca-Cola Classic ran the same play in soft drinks for decades before extensions arrived. The strategy is rare in 2026 because most categories have enough demand heterogeneity that a segmented competitor will pick off the most profitable slice of buyers and leave the undifferentiated firm with the rest, which is why the textbook calls this approach mass marketing and treats it as the ancestor of the segmented strategies that displaced it. First introduced: Chapter 6.
  7. undirected viewing, conditioned viewing, informal search, formal search Francis Aguilar's 1967 typology names four distinct ways managers scan the environment. Undirected viewing is general exposure to information with no particular target, conditioned viewing is exposure sensitized to a specific topic the manager is already watching for, informal search is a limited unstructured effort to find more on a known concern, and formal search is a deliberate research effort with a stated methodology and a budget. First introduced: Chapter 3.
  8. UNESCO Recommendation The UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence is the global ethics instrument that 193 member states (now 194) adopted at the 41st General Conference on November 23, 2021. The document organizes its substantive content into four values, ten principles, and eleven policy action areas the signatory states commit to translate into national policy. Two operational artifacts come out of the Recommendation that a procurement officer can actually demand: the Ethical Impact Assessment (EIA), which an AI vendor produces before a contract is signed, and the Readiness Assessment Methodology (RAM), which a country's government uses to map its own AI-governance gaps. By 2026 the RAM had been deployed across 60 countries including Argentina, Brazil, Chile, India, Indonesia, Mexico, Nigeria, Saudi Arabia, South Africa, and Vietnam. The Recommendation is the only AI-ethics instrument that both democratic and authoritarian regimes have endorsed, which makes it the de facto baseline in markets the EU AI Act does not reach. First introduced: Chapter 26.
  9. Unfair outcomes Unfair outcomes is the first of the two normative concerns in Mittelstadt and Floridi's six-concerns map, sitting at the decision-output stage of the AI pipeline. The concern is that outcomes distribute unfairly across protected and unprotected groups against legal anti-discrimination baselines (Title VII for employment, ECOA for credit, the Fair Housing Act for tenant decisions, GDPR Article 22 for EU consumer decisions, and EU AI Act Article 5 for prohibited practices). This is the bite the popular press refers to as 'algorithmic bias.' The mitigation is pre-deployment disparate-impact testing across protected slices, paired with the ongoing monitoring §26.5's seven-step architecture instruments. First introduced: Chapter 26.
  10. unforeseeable uncertainty Unforeseeable uncertainty is a concept from Loch, De Meyer, and Pich's 2006 book Managing the Unknown, describing influences on a project that nobody on the team identified in advance because nobody knew to ask the question yet. The gate criteria were written before the relevant information existed, so the gate itself cannot score the project on what actually matters. The concept is distinct from two cousins: variation, where a known parameter wobbles around a known mean (next quarter's sales will be roughly 12 percent give or take), and foreseeable uncertainty, where the team knows the question and is gathering evidence to answer it (will the FDA approve this drug formulation). In an unforeseeable-uncertainty project, the team does not yet know which question is the right one to ask, and the gate process has to allow for that. First introduced: Chapter 8.
  11. ungated content Ungated content is any piece the brand publishes openly on the public web with no email capture, no registration wall, and no other friction between the visitor and the reading experience. The brand earns the click and the read entirely on the merit of the piece, and the buyer can leave without giving the brand anything in return. The trade-off is that ungated pieces reach roughly three times as many readers as the same piece would gated, because most visitors abandon the page the moment a form appears. First introduced: Chapter 17.
  12. unhealthy buying-group conflict An unhealthy buying group is one where stakeholders hold conflicting objectives, openly disagree about the best course of action, or where one stakeholder's position has been overruled rather than reconciled. Gartner's May 2025 sample found 74 percent of B2B buyer teams operate this way, while teams that actively reconcile differences (consensus-reaching teams) are 2.5 times more likely to report a high-quality deal at the end of the cycle. First introduced: Chapter 23.
  13. Uniqueness Uniqueness is the share of those buyers who recognize a cue and attribute it to this specific brand rather than to a competitor or to no brand at all. A cue with high uniqueness retrieves the right brand, whereas a cue with low uniqueness retrieves a category but no specific brand inside it, so the buyer encodes the cue but cannot act on it at shelf. First introduced: Chapter 7.
  14. Up-Front Contract The Up-Front Contract is David Sandler's mutual-agreement framing for the start of every meeting. The seller and the buyer agree out loud at the opening of the call on the meeting's purpose, both sides' agendas, the meeting's duration, and the three acceptable outcomes at the end. Those three outcomes are: a yes that moves to a defined next step, a no that ends the relationship, or a scheduled next meeting with a defined purpose. The soft outcomes professional buyers default to (the ‘let me think about it’ or the ‘send me the deck and I'll get back to you’) are explicitly ruled out as acceptable endings before the meeting even starts. First introduced: Chapter 23.
  15. upper bound The Point of Marginal Expensiveness (PME) is the upper edge of the acceptable price band that the Van Westendorp survey identifies. It sits at the price where the cumulative curve for too expensive intersects the cumulative curve for bargain. Above this point, the share of buyers who reject the price as too expensive begins to exceed the share who still see it as a bargain, so the firm has moved out of the range where the price can sustain demand. First introduced: Chapter 10.
  16. URL A URL (Uniform Resource Locator) is the full web address of a page or a program, like https://api.anthropic.com/v1/messages. The URL has three parts the marketer sees in the bar: the protocol (https://), the domain name (api.anthropic.com), and the path (/v1/messages). The URL tells a request where to go, the way a postal address tells a letter where to be delivered. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  17. URL-mode elicitation URL-mode elicitation is the secure-credential-collection mechanism the Model Context Protocol added on November 25, 2025 under proposal SEP-1036. Instead of asking the agent to handle the user's password directly, the server hands the user a link to an OAuth login flow in the browser, and the user signs in there the same way they would for any normal web app. Because the credential never enters the agent's chat window, it cannot leak through the agent's context even if the agent's prompt is later compromised. First introduced: Chapter 27.
  18. URLs A URL (uniform resource locator) is the web address that appears in the browser's address bar when the buyer is on any specific page. The string https://www.costco.com/treasure-hunt, for example, is one URL, and every distinct page on the open web has its own URL. Search engines and AI engines both index and cite the URL as the unique identifier for a piece of content. First introduced: Chapter 18.
  19. USDS The US Data Security (USDS) joint venture is the Oracle-led entity formed in January 2026 to hold TikTok's US operations, US user data, and US-specific recommendation algorithm tuning. The structure satisfies the divestiture requirement of the Protecting Americans from Foreign Adversary Controlled Applications Act by separating the US-facing TikTok product from ByteDance's control, even though ByteDance retains a stake in the joint venture. First introduced: Chapter 3.
  20. user prompt The user prompt is the run-specific request the marketer (or an upstream agent calling this one as a tool) passes in for this particular turn, for example a request to draft a Liquid Death Instagram caption. It is not cached, because it is fresh on every call, which is why a verbose user prompt costs more than the same instruction packaged into the cached system prompt. First introduced: Chapter 27.
  21. USMCA The United States-Mexico-Canada Agreement (USMCA) is the 2020 trade deal that replaced NAFTA, eliminating most tariffs between the three countries while adding stricter regional auto-content rules and a new labor-rights chapter. For a U.S. consumer-goods firm thinking about Mexico, the USMCA is the single biggest reason the administrative distance between the two countries is low enough that supply chains and same-day truck shipments work without customs friction. First introduced: Chapter 15.
  22. USP A Unique Selling Proposition (USP) is the one specific feature a brand can claim about itself that no competitor in the category can credibly match. The term originated with advertising executive Rosser Reeves at the Ted Bates agency in the 1940s, who argued that every ad should hammer one provable, differentiating claim rather than diluting the message with multiple benefits. The textbook example is Splenda's ‘made from sugar’ USP against Equal and Sweet'N Low: the rival artificial sweeteners were already in market with non-sugar formulations and could not reformulate to claim the same provenance, which made Splenda's claim durable rather than fragile. First introduced: Chapter 6.
  23. USTR The Office of the United States Trade Representative (USTR) is the federal agency that negotiates trade agreements and announces tariffs on behalf of the President. Its press releases and Federal Register filings are usually the first public place a brand learns about a coming tariff, which is why subscribing to the USTR feed is a Monday-morning move for any brand whose supply chain crosses an international border. First introduced: Chapter 3.
  24. UTF-8 UTF-8 is the default text encoding on the modern web and in almost every file created after about 2010. The encoding can represent every character in every written language, so the same UTF-8 file holds English letters, Chinese characters, emoji, and accented Spanish vowels without any of them garbling. If a file opens cleanly in any modern app on any operating system, it is almost certainly UTF-8. Older Windows Excel exports sometimes use a different encoding (Windows-1252), which is why the British pound symbol occasionally shows up as a question mark when a Mac opens the file. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  25. utilitarian value Utilitarian value is the value a buyer gets from a shopping trip in the form of having solved a problem efficiently. The shopper asks whether the trip got the job done, whether the product was the right one, and whether the price was reasonable. A Costco run on a Saturday is the canonical example, and the Babin-Darden-Griffin 1994 shopping-value scale turned this idea into a survey factor that brand teams can measure on their own customers. First introduced: Chapter 4.
22 // v

V V

  1. VALS VALS stands for Values, Attitudes, and Lifestyles. The framework was developed at SRI International in 1978 and classifies US adults into eight named psychographic groups (Innovators, Thinkers, Achievers, Experiencers, Believers, Strivers, Makers, Survivors) along two underlying dimensions: a primary motivation (ideals, achievement, or self-expression) and a resource axis that captures income, education, and energy. A 2026 brand planner uses VALS to answer the why behind a purchase that demographics alone cannot reach. First introduced: Chapter 6.
  2. valuable virality Valuable virality is the standard a brand has to hit on top of plain virality: the content has to travel AND it has to carry the brand with it when it travels. The diagnostic test is whether a listener can retell the story to a friend without naming the brand. If the brand is removable from the retelling, the post is a Trojan Horse with nothing inside, because the audience reached the moment of recommendation without the brand name attached. Liquid Death passes the test (the brand is the joke); a generic celebrity ad rarely does. First introduced: Chapter 19.
  3. valuable-virality test The valuable-virality test asks a simple question about any shared content: can the story be told without mentioning the brand? If yes, the brand is a sponsor sticker on someone else's content, and every share refreshes the content's network rather than the brand's. If no, the brand is integral to the story, and every share refreshes the brand's network. Integral virality is the type that grows brands over time, while sponsor-sticker virality is the type that produces big view counts without leaving any brand memory behind. First introduced: Chapter 7.
  4. Value-based pricing Value-based pricing (also called economic-value-to-customer or EVC pricing) asks what a product is worth to the customer who would otherwise buy the next-best alternative, rather than what it costs the firm to make or what the competition is charging. The ceiling is the buyer's willingness-to-pay; the floor is the alternative's price. First introduced: Chapter 10.
  5. value co-created in use Stephen Vargo and Robert Lusch argued in their 2004 Journal of Marketing paper that value is determined in use by the buyer, not built into the product at the factory. The firm proposes value through what it offers, but actual value only emerges when the buyer integrates the offer with her own skills and her own complementary resources inside her real use-context. A toaster, for instance, is a frozen instance of the firm's competence at making heating elements work cheaply and reliably, but the buyer has to plug it in, slice the bread, push the lever, and butter what comes out before any value happens. What is exchanged is one party's specialized competence for another's, with the artifact as the medium. First introduced: Chapter 2.
  6. Value Equity Value Equity is the share of a customer’s loyalty that comes from their objective assessment of what the firm’s offer delivers, independent of brand feeling. A customer who keeps shopping at Costco because the products genuinely cost less than at competitors is buying on Value Equity. The component sub-drivers are quality, price, and convenience. The framework is from Rust, Lemon, and Zeithaml (2000), who decomposed customer equity into three drivers a firm can invest in separately. First introduced: Chapter 24.
  7. value-now consumer The value-now consumer is McKinsey ConsumerWise's 2025 label for a buyer who oscillates between trade-down and splurge across different categories in the same quarter rather than retreating uniformly across the shopping basket. The pattern is different from the 2008 recession's within-category trade-down because the same household is now cutting spending in one area while spending more in another. First introduced: Chapter 3.
  8. value proposition A value proposition is the buyer-facing benefit a brand offers, and David Aaker's 1996 framework organizes it into three layers the brand team completes separately. Functional benefits describe what the product actually does (Patagonia's jacket keeps you warm and dry on a mountain). Emotional benefits describe how the buyer feels about the brand and the category (the buyer feels part of an environmental community when wearing the jacket). Self-expressive benefits describe what owning the brand signals about who the buyer is (carrying the Patagonia logo says the buyer is the kind of person who values environmental responsibility). First introduced: Chapter 7.
  9. Van Westendorp price-sensitivity meter The Van Westendorp price-sensitivity meter (PSM) is a survey instrument the Dutch market researcher Peter Van Westendorp introduced at the 1976 ESOMAR Congress. It asks every respondent four indirect questions about the same product (so expensive you would not buy, so cheap that quality must be poor, getting expensive but worth considering, a bargain), then plots cumulative curves on the resulting answers. The four curves intersect at four landmark prices that together bound the acceptable range, which is why PSM is the default launch-stage instrument when the firm has no analog data and no comparable category from which to borrow elasticity estimates. First introduced: Chapter 10.
  10. Vanity metrics Vanity metrics are numbers that go up monotonically but do not predict revenue or retention. The canonical examples are total page views, total app downloads, social-media impressions, and cumulative signups since inception. Eric Ries and Sean Ellis converged on the same operational test for whether a metric is vanity: if the metric doubled overnight, would a single management decision change? If the answer is no, the number is vanity and should not appear on the team's scoreboard. First introduced: Chapter 23.
  11. Veblen effect The Veblen effect is the empirical pattern in which higher posted prices lift demand among buyers who use the product to signal status. Thorstein Veblen named the mechanism in 1899 to describe luxury consumption among the leisure class. Cutting the price destroys the signaling value the buyer was paying for, so the demand curve slopes upward across the relevant range rather than downward, until the price rises high enough that the signaling target population can no longer afford the product at all. First introduced: Chapter 10.
  12. Vending-Bench Vending-Bench is a long-horizon agent benchmark Anthropic introduced in 2024 that tests an agent's ability to run an autonomous vending operation for many simulated days. The agent has to order inventory, set prices, handle customer complaints, and balance cash, all without a human operator intervening between turns. The benchmark scores agents on whether they keep the simulated business solvent over time, which is a much harder test than scoring on one-off tasks because the failure modes compound rather than reset. The internal Anthropic worked case named in section 1.6 (the Claudius vending experiment, where Sonnet 3.7 ran a real in-office vending shop for a month) is the real-world cousin of this benchmark. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  13. VentureBots VentureBots is a five-agent entrepreneurship coach built by the AgentLab IlliniHunt research group at the Gies College of Business. The five specialist agents cover the startup journey end to end: market research, brainstorming, validation, product planning, and pitch generation. AgentLab piloted the system with 25 students in summer 2025, and the codebase is open-source on GitHub, which means a capstone team can read the actual prompts and orchestrator wiring rather than rely on a marketing case study. First introduced: Chapter 27.
  14. verification rule The verification rule is Andrej Karpathy's April 30, 2026 Sequoia Ascent reframing of where AI deployment scales and where it does not. The rule has two parts. First: traditional software (Python, SQL) automates what the firm can specify, because a human is writing down the rule the machine will follow. Second: AI automates what the firm can verify, because the machine is producing outputs the firm checks after the fact rather than rules the firm wrote in advance. A workflow with cheap fast verification (a unit test, a structured comparison against a known-good answer, a regex check on the output shape, a single dollar number on a known invoice) is one where agentic engineering returns a usable result across many iterations. A workflow whose verification is expensive (legal compliance review, brand-tone judgment, regulatory submission, taste-based creative judgment) keeps a human reviewer as the bottleneck and degrades into vibe coding because the verifier cannot keep up with the agent. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  15. version control Version control is a category of software that records every change made to a folder of files over time, attaches a name and a short message to each change, and lets the user retrieve any prior version of any file with a single command. Git is the dominant version-control system in 2026, but the category is older: Subversion (SVN, released in 2000) and Mercurial (2005) were the two systems Git largely replaced. The marketing analog the marketer already knows is the version history in a Google Doc, generalized from one document to a whole folder of files with messages attached to each save. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  16. vertical marketing system A vertical marketing system (VMS) is a channel arrangement in which producers, wholesalers, and retailers act as a unified system rather than as independent firms, unified either by single ownership across the tiers, by long-term contracts, or by the dominance of one channel captain that the others follow. VMS arrangements now serve 70 to 80 percent of the U.S. consumer marketplace, displacing the conventional independent-firm channel that dominated through the middle of the twentieth century. First introduced: Chapter 11.
  17. vertical-specialist rep The vertical-specialist (sometimes called the industry-expert rep) is the structural fifth-tier sales role that the Diephuis 2025 framework names as the organizational-design complement to the standard four-role lineup of business-development representative, account executive, customer-success manager, and sales engineer. The role carries deep product-and-customer knowledge in one specific industry vertical (healthcare, manufacturing, financial services), and the vertical specialist bridges the sales-product interface during new-product development by writing the industry-expert content the marketing team distributes, training the sales team on how to sell the new product into her vertical, and feeding the product team the patterns she sees in rep observations. Firms that staff this role report new-product-development success rates that are forty to sixty percent higher than firms without it, but the lift only shows up at firms that actually run active new-product pipelines, which means the role pays for itself only where the firm is genuinely launching new offerings rather than just maintaining the current catalog. First introduced: Chapter 23.
  18. very large business effect A very large business effect is the IPA's coding for the largest level of campaign-attributed gain a submission can claim on any one of six business outcomes, where the six outcomes are customer acquisition, sales volume, sales value, profit, market share, and customer loyalty. A campaign that lifts sales volume by ten percent against a control gets a small-effect code, while a campaign that roughly doubles a brand's market share earns the very-large code. In the 2026 IPA analysis, 93 percent of the 103 trust-gaining campaigns in the Databank reported at least one such effect on at least one of the six outcomes, against 66 percent for the rest of the for-profit set. First introduced: Chapter 16.
  19. vibe coding Vibe coding is the practice of describing a desired output to a large language model in plain English and shipping what the model returns, without writing a specification, a test harness, or an explicit supervision step. Andrej Karpathy named the practice in a June 17, 2025 Y Combinator talk, where he positioned it as the floor of what a non-engineer can now do (a brand manager who has never written Python can ship a working competitor-brief script in an afternoon). Ten months later, in an April 30, 2026 Sequoia Ascent update, Karpathy repositioned it as just one rung in a ladder whose ceiling is a different, more disciplined practice he calls agentic engineering, which adds the specifications and test harnesses vibe coding skips. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  20. vicinity Vicinity is the lever inside David Bell's GRAVITY framework that measures proximity to the physical infrastructure that actually fulfills the order, whether that infrastructure is a fulfillment center, a ship-from-store node, or a same-day-delivery hub. For Buy Online Pickup In Store (BOPIS), ship-from-store, and same-day delivery promises, vicinity is the operational metric that defines which stores can serve which buyer catchment in which delivery window, because the cost-per-delivery curve breaks once the distance between the store and the buyer crosses a threshold the format cannot economically cover. First introduced: Chapter 12.
  21. viewpoint, fact base, analytical tools, and determination plus implementation The four-dimensional power-pricer framework, introduced by Robert Dolan and Hermann Simon in their 1996 book Power Pricing, says a firm has to build four separate organizational capacities to capture pricing leverage rather than just one. The first capacity is the viewpoint that pricing is the primary profit lever, the second is a fact base that actually measures how buyers respond to price, the third is the analytical toolkit (segmentation, bundling, time-based pricing, and similar mechanisms), and the fourth is the cross-functional discipline to hold the recommended price when the sales force pushes for end-of-quarter discounts. The fourth capacity is where most pricing initiatives quietly fall apart. First introduced: Chapter 10.
  22. vigilant organization A vigilant organization is the label George Day and Paul Schoemaker used in their 2008 Sloan Management Review article to describe a firm configured to detect, interpret, and act on weak signals before they sharpen. The condition is auditable on seven attributes including named scanning owners for each peripheral domain and a culture that rewards investigations that do not pan out, which is what keeps the team from punishing the false-positive readings that good peripheral vision necessarily produces. First introduced: Chapter 3.
  23. Visa Intelligent Commerce Visa Intelligent Commerce is the program Visa announced in April 2025 to authorize AI agents to make purchases on behalf of cardholders. The cardholder sets spending rules in advance, things like a per-transaction cap, a list of categories the agent is allowed to buy in, and a monthly budget. Visa then issues the agent a special token tied to those rules. When the agent checks out at a merchant, the merchant's payment processor sees a normal Visa authorization with one extra flag indicating the buyer is an agent acting on a human's behalf. The card network handles the rules-checking automatically, so the merchant does not have to ask the agent to prove anything. Mastercard's Agent Pay, also launched in April 2025, is the parallel program on the Mastercard network. Both programs were rolled out to issuing banks and to merchants throughout 2025 and reached broad availability across the United States by the first quarter of 2026. First introduced: Chapter 13.
  24. Vision Pro The Vision Pro is Apple's 3,499-dollar mixed-reality headset, launched in February 2024 as the company's first new product category since the Apple Watch in 2015. The device combined a high-resolution display, eye and hand tracking, and a custom operating system called visionOS. The first six months of sales (roughly 370,000 units) ran far below the internal forecast of one million, production was cut by mid-2024, and the second-generation model was paused. First introduced: Chapter 8.
  25. voice-of-customer corpus The voice-of-customer corpus is the body of actual language buyers use to describe the product, gathered from product reviews, support tickets, and post-purchase survey responses. A disciplined product-page-audit Skill loads this corpus into the model's context window so the audit is grounded in what this brand's buyers actually say, rather than in what the model's training data assumes buyers in general might say. The candle-store student builds the corpus from the last 200 reviews exported from the Shopify reviews app, the last quarter of Gorgias support tickets, and any post-purchase survey responses Klaviyo has collected. First introduced: Chapter 13.
  26. VP A Vice President of Marketing is a senior marketing executive who reports to the CMO and owns one product line, one region, or one channel. New VPs often want a flagship project to justify the appointment, and rebranding the product line is a visible flagship project that produces immediate evidence of the new VP's work. First introduced: Chapter 7.
23 // w

W W

  1. 5W Model The 5W Model is Cengage's operational version of the customer scan. The model asks Who buys the category, What buyers do with the product, Where they buy and consume, When they buy, Why and how they choose between options, and Why potential customers do not buy at all. The sixth question is the one most teams skip. First introduced: Chapter 3.
  2. 7W audit of category entry points The 7W audit of category entry points (CEPs) is a survey-based diagnostic that measures how many distinct buying occasions a brand's buyers can spontaneously connect back to the brand. The seven Ws are Why (the underlying motivation, such as thirst, energy, or comfort), When (the time of day or year), Where (the location, such as at home, in the car, or at the gym), While doing what (the activity, such as studying, working out, or watching a game), With or for whom (the audience, such as kids, friends, or oneself), With what (other products consumed alongside, such as with pizza or with workout gear), and hoW-feeling (the emotional state, such as tired, celebrating, or stressed). The audit asks buyers to free-recall the brand for each W and then counts the number of dimensions that produced a hit. When a brand spontaneously links to nine or more entry points across the seven dimensions, the audit reads as top-performer territory, whereas the median brand in most consumer categories sits at three to five entry points and has substantial mental-availability room to grow. First introduced: Chapter 6.
  3. warehouse A data warehouse is a single database designed to pull transactions, marketing spend, customer records, and operational tables together into one queryable place. Instead of every team running its own spreadsheet, the warehouse is the authoritative source the audit, the dashboard, and the MMM all read from. Snowflake, BigQuery, and Databricks are the most common warehouse vendors in 2026, and a firm’s warehouse choice usually determines which analytics tools its team can plug in. First introduced: Chapter 24.
  4. warehouse club A warehouse club is a members-only retailer that charges an annual fee at the door and sells household goods in larger mega-pack quantities at low markups on the merchandise itself. Costco, Sam's Club, and BJ's are the U.S. examples, and the actual profit engine of the format is the membership fee rather than the markup on each box of paper towels, which is why the chain can afford to price the rotisserie chicken below cost and still report strong operating income. First introduced: Chapter 12.
  5. Wasserstein distance Wasserstein distance, sometimes called the earth-mover’s distance, measures how different two distributions are by asking how much probability mass would have to be physically moved, and how far, to turn one of them into the other. The intuition is simple: if last week’s spend distribution looks like a pile of sand at one end of a number line and this week’s pile looks similar but shifted halfway down the line, the Wasserstein distance is the work it would take to push the first pile into the shape and location of the second. Inside Evidently AI, the analyst chooses Wasserstein when the goal is to know the magnitude of the shift, not just whether one occurred, because the number is sensitive to both the size and the direction of the drift in a way that simpler tests like Kolmogorov-Smirnov are not. First introduced: Chapter 24.
  6. waterfall The waterfall strategy means a firm enters foreign markets one country at a time, finishes learning each market before opening the next, and uses what it learned in country one to write the playbook for country two. Matsushita, BMW, GE, Benetton, and The Body Shop all expanded this way because their products did not face a closing competitive window. First introduced: Chapter 15.
  7. WcDonald's WcDonald's is the name of a roughly thirty-year-old anime fan tradition in which artists drew the McDonald's restaurant with a flipped W in place of the M, originally as a stand-in to avoid trademark trouble inside their own fan work. In February 2024 the company chose to sanction the long-running fan joke and turned it into a primary creative platform across more than thirty markets, with Studio Pierrot (the studio behind Naruto and Bleach) producing four anime shorts that spanned the mech, fantasy, action, and romance genres of the medium. The case is worth knowing because it shows a heritage brand legitimising an audience-authored asset rather than litigating against it, which is the move Romaniuk's audit instrument scores as a Fame and Uniqueness lift on a near-zero production budget. First introduced: Chapter 16.
  8. weak signals A weak signal is an early, low-magnitude indicator of a force that has not yet shown up in standard market data but is likely to be load-bearing for the category within an eighteen-month horizon. The discipline of weak-signal detection is the practice of separating these early indicators from one-day stories that resolve as noise within a week, and from established trends that competitors have already priced into their plans. First introduced: Chapter 3.
  9. weak ties Weak ties are the acquaintance-strength relationships a person maintains with people they know but do not see often, in contrast to the strong ties they share with close friends and family. Mark Granovetter showed in a 1973 study of Boston job seekers that new information about opportunities (jobs, products, ideas) most often arrives through weak ties rather than strong ones, because close friends already share most of the information you have while acquaintances sit inside other social clusters and carry information your own cluster does not. A 2022 LinkedIn experiment on 20 million users and 600,000 job-finding outcomes confirmed the original finding with causal evidence. First introduced: Chapter 19.
  10. web_search_20260209 web_search_20260209 is the February 9, 2026 release of Anthropic's web-search tool. The release adds dynamic filtering, a feature that lets Claude write and run a short filtering script against retrieved search results before any of those results are loaded into the model's context window. The model keeps only the passages relevant to the query, which both reduces token consumption (so the probe costs less) and improves answer quality (so the citations are more on-point) for any audit script that runs many queries in sequence. First introduced: Chapter 18.
  11. web-search MCP server MCP stands for Model Context Protocol, and it is the open standard that lets a language model talk to external tools and data sources without bespoke integration. Think of MCP as a USB-C port for AI: any MCP-compatible client (Claude Desktop, Claude Code, ChatGPT, Cursor) can plug into any MCP-compatible server (Perplexity, Slack, HubSpot, Notion, and hundreds of others) through a single typed interface. The standard was donated to the Linux Foundation Agentic AI Foundation in December 2025, which is the signal that it is now an industry-wide protocol rather than a single-vendor format. First introduced: Chapter 19.
  12. web-search tool Anthropic's web-search tool is Claude's built-in capability for fetching live pages off the open web and citing them in answers. The tool has been available inside Claude.ai chat since April 2025, and as a paid API endpoint at $10 per thousand calls. The important technical detail is that the default fetch path retrieves the raw HTML the server returned, not the version that would appear after JavaScript runs, which is the structural reason any client-rendered marketing site is invisible to Claude's grounding. First introduced: Chapter 18.
  13. weights Weights are the internal numbers a language model learns during training. A modern frontier model has roughly 100 billion to 1 trillion of them, each one a tiny dial the training process adjusted until the model got next-word prediction right often enough on the training corpus. Together the weights encode everything the model knows, the same way a brain's synapses together encode everything a person knows. After training ends, the weights are frozen and never change for any user; every conversation the marketer has runs against the same frozen weights from the model's release date. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  14. Werther effect The Werther effect is the copycat-suicide pattern in which front-page suicide stories produce demographic-matched spikes in fatal accidents for roughly two weeks after the story ran. A series of studies starting in 1974 documented the regularity that the published victim's age and method tend to match the demographic that goes on to die, which is the social-proof mechanism in its most extreme form. The label comes from Goethe's 1774 novel The Sorrows of Young Werther, whose early readers reportedly imitated the protagonist's death. First introduced: Chapter 4.
  15. wheel of loyalty The wheel of loyalty is a four-spoke operating framework codified by Christopher Lovelock and Jochen Wirtz that turns the abstract goal of customer retention into four sequential pieces of work a brand manager can actually run. The first spoke builds tier-based foundations, meaning the firm gives recovered or returning buyers a structured ladder to climb back into, with membership levels, status programs, and structured onboarding so that staying with the brand earns something visible over time. The second spoke adds social and structural ties, meaning the firm builds community rituals, a shared brand identity buyers want to be seen with, and switching costs the buyer values rather than resents (the everyday way to see this is the Amazon Prime member who stays because the bundled video, music, and shipping all live in one account). The third spoke closes the standard failure modes that produced the defection risk in the first place, often by feeding the SERVQUAL-gap audit results from the prior section back into operating standards. The fourth spoke runs a deliberate win-back program for customers who already left, where the firm reaches out with an offer keyed to the specific reason that buyer left rather than blasting a single discount to everyone. First introduced: Chapter 9.
  16. wheel of retailing The Wheel of Retailing (Malcolm McNair, 1958; Stanley Hollander, Journal of Marketing, 1960) is a four-stage hypothesis about how retail formats evolve over time. A new format enters the market at the lowest possible margin and price, then gradually adds services and broadens its assortment to attract more buyers, which raises the format’s margin and price level over time, which eventually opens the door for a new low-margin entrant to undercut the now-trade-up incumbent from beneath. First introduced: Chapter 11.
  17. whole product The whole product is Geoffrey Moore's term, from Crossing the Chasm (1991, revised 2014) and building on Theodore Levitt's 1980 augmented-product concept, for the minimum set of products and services around the launch item that the target customer needs in order to actually realize the compelling reason to buy. The whole product includes the core item plus integrations with the systems the buyer already runs, training for the people who will use it, documentation that answers the questions the buyer will ask, customer support, complementary products that finish the workflow, and proven implementation paths. Moore's benchmarking shows that pragmatist buyers adopt at roughly 80 percent whole-product completeness and that adoption is near zero below 60 percent completeness. The implication is that the whole-product investment is not optional polish to be added once the core item is selling. It is the entry ticket without which the pragmatist segment will not adopt the offer at all. First introduced: Chapter 8.
  18. willingness-to-pay Willingness-to-pay (WTP) is the maximum price a particular buyer would still choose to buy a product at, given the alternatives she could spend the same money on. If a buyer values a Stanley Quencher cup at thirty-eight dollars in her own mental ledger, her WTP is thirty-eight dollars: she will buy it at any price up to that, and walk away at any price above it. Aggregating WTP across many buyers gives the brand a demand curve it can price against. First introduced: Chapter 5.
  19. willingness-to-pay (WTP) Willingness to pay (WTP) is the maximum price a particular buyer would actually hand over for a product before walking away from the purchase entirely. One buyer might walk away from a $279 Patagonia jacket at $280, while another would have paid up to $350 for the same jacket without flinching. That cross-buyer variation in WTP is exactly what gives the demand curve its downward slope, and §10.3 walks two operational ways to measure WTP empirically: the Van Westendorp price-sensitivity meter and conjoint analysis. First introduced: Chapter 10.
  20. winning-test rates The winning-test rate is the share of all A/B tests a team runs that end up producing a statistically significant lift in the primary metric the team is measuring, such as checkout conversion or revenue per visitor. Across the major experimentation platforms like Optimizely, VWO, and Convert, the industry baseline sits at roughly 10 to 20 percent, because most test ideas are guesses rather than hypotheses grounded in buyer research. A team that screens its test ideas carefully through a framework like Peep Laja's PXL framework typically hits 30 percent, while a team that throws tests at the wall without screening tends to drift down toward 5 percent. First introduced: Chapter 13.
  21. work-in-process limit Work-in-process limits (WIP limits) are a cap on the number of projects or work items a team allows in the pipeline at any one moment, so new work cannot enter until existing work exits. The discipline is counterintuitive because engineers can look idle when the limit is full and a manager would normally hand them another project. But throughput across the whole pipeline rises because what does enter moves through faster, with fewer queues, less context-switching, and faster decisions on the work that is already in flight. Donald Reinertsen develops the case in chapter 6 of The Principles of Product Development Flow (2009), and Kanban boards are the visible implementation in software teams. The six-jam display in Iyengar and Lepper's choice-overload study is the consumer-side cousin: fewer concurrent options moves more buyers through the decision. First introduced: Chapter 8.
  22. workflow In Anthropic’s taxonomy for AI systems, a workflow is one where the large language model follows pre-written code paths that an engineer wrote, rather than a system where the model chooses its own next step. The workflow is predictable, step-by-step debuggable, and the right default for measurement work like marketing-mix-modeling refits because every action the model took is logged and a senior can replay any specific run. The contrast is with an agent, which picks its own next action dynamically from the situation and is harder to audit when something goes wrong. First introduced: Chapter 24.
  23. working-partnership A working partnership in business-to-business channels is a relationship marked by mutual commitment, frequent two-way information flow, joint planning between manufacturer and partner, and explicit dispute-resolution mechanisms written into the contract. It differs from an arm’s-length transactional channel by an order of magnitude in coordination intensity. Erin Anderson and James Narus published the canonical model in a 1990 Journal of Marketing paper, and the framework has anchored B2B distributor-relationship research ever since. First introduced: Chapter 11.
  24. workload units Workload Units (WU) are the metered-cost unit Bubble uses to bill for everything the app actually does in the cloud. Each database query, each workflow action, each page render, each scheduled trigger that fires on its own, and each call out to an external service consumes a small chunk of WU from the monthly allowance. The Starter plan includes a fixed WU budget per month, and once the app exceeds that budget, Bubble bills the overage per 1,000 WUs. The mental model is that the cloud costs of running the app are not absorbed by Bubble the way they are by a flat-rate platform; they are passed back to the developer as a metered bill the developer can watch grow in real time. First introduced: Chapter 14.
  25. World Values Survey World Values Survey: a longitudinal cross-national survey of population-level values, conducted in seven waves from 1981 through 2022, covering more than 120 countries. The Inglehart-Welzel cultural map plots countries on two factor axes (Traditional vs. Secular-Rational, Survival vs. Self-Expression) and updates each wave, which makes it a better instrument than Hofstede for tracking consumer-level cultural change over time. First introduced: Chapter 15.
  26. Worn Wear Worn Wear is Patagonia's repair, resale, and recycling program, set up so that a torn Patagonia jacket can be sent to one of the firm's seventy global repair centers and returned to the buyer fixed, often free of charge if the damage is a standard wear pattern. The program is integrated into the product offer rather than treated as customer-service overhead, which means buyers paying the Patagonia premium are paying partly for the multi-year repair guarantee the program represents. First introduced: Chapter 8.
24 // x

X X

  1. .xlsx XLSX is the Microsoft Excel workbook file format. The format stores cells, formulas, multiple sheets, and formatting as a single binary file the operating system recognizes by the .xlsx extension. A language model can read XLSX content directly, but the model loses some structure compared to what it would see in a clean CSV export of the same sheet. The fix when accuracy matters is to export the sheet to CSV before uploading, so the model receives an unambiguous row-and-column grid. First introduced: Chapter 1.
  2. 12x more profit The 12x more profit figure is the empirical lift in long-term profit return that the top creative quartile of the June 2025 System1 + Effies fusion sample earned over the bottom quartile, across roughly 1,200 paired ads. The multiplier is conditional rather than automatic, because it appears only when three levers hold at the same time. The first lever is emotion, which means the ad has to register warmth, humor, or awe on the System1 scale. The second lever is distinctive-asset density, where the inflection point falls at roughly seven brand codes per thirty-second spot. The third lever is continuous run-time, which is what lets the codes accumulate into memory rather than starting from scratch with each new flight. First introduced: Chapter 16.
  3. X-Ray X-Ray is Helium 10's Chrome extension, and it works by overlaying per-product data directly on top of any Amazon search-results page the seller visits. Each result in the search list shows estimated monthly sales, monthly revenue, Best Sellers Rank, review count, the Amazon fulfillment fee per unit, and price history, all without the seller having to leave the Amazon search page or open another tab. This is the single most-used Helium 10 tool for first-pass niche scanning, because it converts an ordinary Amazon search into a structured intelligence view of the category in roughly five seconds. First introduced: Chapter 13.
  4. xbench Xbench is the profession-aligned agent benchmark suite Chen and his co-authors proposed in June 2025 (arXiv paper 2506.13651), built around the claim that benchmarks tied to real professional work predict whether an agent product will succeed in production better than synthetic tool-use micro-benchmarks do. The xbench Marketing benchmark scores agents on 50 real advertiser briefs against a curated pool of 836 candidate influencers, judging each run by how well its ranking aligns with the advertiser's actual selection rather than by a single-shot accuracy number. A capstone team can replicate the same harness offline by writing twenty briefs with senior-planner-graded golden rankings. First introduced: Chapter 27.
25 // y

Y Y

  1. YAML YAML (originally Yet Another Markup Language, later rebranded as YAML Ain't Markup Language) is a human-readable text format used to store structured configuration data. A YAML frontmatter block at the top of a file is a small section bracketed by three dashes that contains key-value pairs the program reading the file will parse before reading the main content. In a Skill, the YAML frontmatter declares the Skill's name, description, and which tasks it should trigger on. First introduced: Chapter 21.
  2. YAML frontmatter YAML frontmatter is a small block of structured metadata that sits at the very top of a Markdown file, opened and closed by a line of three dashes (---). Inside that block, the author writes one key-value pair per line, where each line names a property and its value: a typical Skill file opens with ‘name: qualitative-coder’ on the first line, ‘description: Code interview transcripts into themes’ on the second, and similar properties for version and model on the next lines. The reason the language model reads this block before the rest of the file is that the frontmatter is what tells the model when the Skill should be loaded into the conversation and what role the Skill is supposed to play, separately from the body of the Markdown file which contains the actual instructions the model follows once it has been activated. First introduced: Chapter 5.
